The following metrics are universally cited in KTU board examinations and aerospace engineering textbooks:

- **Wing Loading** (W/S): typically **10 – 100 N/m²** for small UAVs
- **Aspect Ratio** (AR): typically **5 – 20** depending on endurance requirement
- **Cruise Speed**: **15 – 100 m/s** for tactical UAVs
- **Service Ceiling**: **3,000 – 60,000 ft** depending on class
- **Endurance**: **30 minutes to 30+ hours**
- **Range**: **5 km to 20,000+ km**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A high **aspect ratio** (long, slender wings) directly correlates to **high lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)**, which is the single most important figure of merit for fixed-wing endurance and range performance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Fundamental Forces of Fixed-Wing Flight

A fixed-wing UAV in steady, level, unaccelerated flight is governed by a **closed force polygon** consisting of four vectors. The KTU board frequently asks students to identify and balance these forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Forces in Cruising Flight:**
> 1. **Lift (L)** — generated perpendicular to the relative wind by the wing.
> 2. **Weight (W = mg)** — gravitational pull acting through the center of gravity.
> 3. **Thrust (T)** — generated by the propulsion system (propeller/jet) along the flight path.
> 4. **Drag (D)** — aerodynamic resistance acting opposite to the flight direction.

In **steady level cruise**:
- Vertical equilibrium: $L = W$
- Horizontal equilibrium: $T = D$

### 2.2 Aerodynamic Lift — The Lift Equation

The lift force generated by a fixed wing is given by the canonical lift equation:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{L}
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density [kg/m³] — at sea level, **$\rho \approx 1.225$ kg/m³** under ISA conditions.
- $V$ = true airspeed [m/s]
- $S$ = planform wing area [m²]
- $C_L$ = dimensionless lift coefficient (function of angle of attack $\alpha$ and airfoil shape)

### 2.3 Aerodynamic Drag — The Drag Equation

Total drag is conventionally decomposed into two components:

$$
D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{D}
$$

where the drag coefficient is modeled as:

$$
C_{D} = C_{D,0} + K\,C_{L}^{2}
$$

- $C_{D,0}$ = **parasitic drag coefficient** (form drag + skin friction + interference)
- $K = \dfrac{1}{\pi\,e\,AR}$ = **induced drag factor**
- $e$ = Oswald efficiency factor (typically **0.8 – 0.95** for clean configurations)
- $AR = b^{2}/S$ = **aspect ratio** ($b$ = wingspan)

### 2.4 Lift-to-Drag Ratio — Figure of Merit

The aerodynamic efficiency of a fixed-wing UAV is quantified by the **lift-to-drag ratio**:

$$
E = \dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{C_{L}}{C_{D,0} + K\,C_{L}^{2}}
$$

Maximizing $E$ with respect to $C_L$ gives the **optimal lift coefficient**:

$$
C_{L}^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D,0}}{K}}
$$

Substituting back yields the **maximum aerodynamic efficiency**:

$$
E_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{K\,C_{D,0}}} = \dfrac{\pi\,e\,AR}{2\,C_{D,0}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exam:**
> $E_{\max}$ scales **linearly with aspect ratio (AR)**. This is precisely why surveillance and HALE UAVs use very long, slender wings — to maximize $E_{\max}$ and therefore maximize range and endurance.

### 2.5 Performance Equations — Endurance and Range

For a propeller-driven UAV with constant propeller efficiency $\eta_p$ and fuel-specific energy $c_T$ (or battery specific energy for electric UAVs), the **Breguet range equation** simplifies to:

$$
R = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_T}\,\dfrac{L}{D}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{f}}\right)
$$

and the **endurance equation** is:

$$
E_{end} = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_P}\,\dfrac{L}{D}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{f}}\right)
$$

where:
- $W_i$ = initial weight (at takeoff)
- $W_f$ = final weight (after fuel/battery consumption)
- $c_T$ = **specific fuel consumption** (thrust-based) [1/s] — for electric UAVs, $c_T = 1/E_{bat}$
- $c_P$ = **specific fuel consumption** (power-based) [1/s]

### 2.6 Control Surfaces and Their Functions

| Control Surface | Location | Axis of Rotation | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aileron** | Trailing edge of wing (outboard) | Longitudinal (roll) axis | Roll left/right |
| **Elevator** | Trailing edge of horizontal stabilizer | Lateral (pitch) axis | Pitch up/down |
| **Rudder** | Trailing edge of vertical stabilizer | Vertical (yaw) axis | Yaw left/right |
| **Flaps** | Trailing edge of wing (inboard) | — (not a primary control) | Increase $C_L$ at low speed for takeoff/landing |
| **Spoilers / Airbrakes** | Upper wing surface | — | Reduce lift / increase drag for descent |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L$ | Lift force | N |
| 2 | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D$ | Drag force | N |
| 3 | $C_D = C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2}$ | Drag polar | dimensionless |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{1}{\pi e AR}$ | Induced drag factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $AR = b^{2}/S$ | Aspect ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $WL = W/S$ | Wing loading | N/m² |
| 7 | $E_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi e AR}{2 C_{D,0}}$ | Max aerodynamic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{req} = D = \dfrac{W}{L/D}$ | Thrust required in cruise | N |
| 9 | $P_{req} = D \cdot V$ | Power required in cruise | W |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_T} (L/D) \ln(W_i/W_f)$ | Breguet range | m |
| 11 | $V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,\max}}}$ | Stall speed | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $|V|$ or $|x|$ in a markdown table. Always use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` to avoid table-breaking pipe characters in KTU notes.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agricultural Drones (e.g., senseFly eBee X):** Use high-AR wings for **2-hour endurance** covering 100+ hectares per flight for crop mapping.
- **Military Surveillance (e.g., MQ-1 Predator):** Wing area and AR optimized for **L/D > 18** to achieve 24+ hour loiter time.
- **Civilian Mapping (e.g., WingtraOne VTOL):** Combines multirotor VTOL with fixed-wing cruise for high-accuracy photogrammetry.
- **Atmospheric Research (e.g., Global Hawk):** $AR \approx 25$ yields L/D ≈ 30, supporting cross-continental scientific missions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stall Speed from First Principles

A fixed-wing UAV cannot fly slower than its **stall speed** $V_{stall}$, because below this speed the wing cannot generate enough lift to balance the weight.

**Given:**
- Weight of the UAV: $W$ [N]
- Wing area: $S$ [m²]
- Air density: $\rho$ [kg/m³]
- Maximum lift coefficient: $C_{L,\max}$

**Step 1 — Write the lift equation:**

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-level flight condition ($L = W$):**

$$
W = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{L,\max}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V$ (the stall speed):**

$$
V^{2} = \dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L,\max}}
$$

$$
V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L,\max}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**
- Larger wing area $S$ → lower stall speed (slower, safer landing).
- Higher $C_{L,\max}$ (use of flaps at landing) → lower stall speed.
- Heavier UAV → higher stall speed → longer runway required for landing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example:**
> A small UAV with $W = 80$ N, $S = 0.6$ m², $C_{L,\max} = 1.4$ flying at sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³):
>
> $$
> V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 80}{1.225 \times 0.6 \times 1.4}} = \sqrt{155.4} \approx 12.47 \text{ m/s}
> $$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Lift-to-Drag Ratio

We wish to find the value of $C_L$ that maximizes the aerodynamic efficiency $E = L/D = C_L / C_D$.

**Step 1 — Express efficiency in terms of $C_L$:**

$$
E = \dfrac{C_L}{C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the derivative with respect to $C_L$ and set to zero:**

$$
\dfrac{dE}{dC_L} = \dfrac{(C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2}) - C_L (2 K C_L)}{(C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2})^{2}} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator:**

$$
C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2} - 2K C_L^{2} = 0
$$

$$
C_{D,0} - K C_L^{2} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the optimal $C_L$:**

$$
C_L^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D,0}}{K}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into $E$ expression:**

$$
E_{\max} = \dfrac{\sqrt{C_{D,0}/K}}{C_{D,0} + K (C_{D,0}/K)} = \dfrac{\sqrt{C_{D,0}/K}}{2 C_{D,0}}
$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form expression:**

$$
E_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{K\,C_{D,0}}} = \dfrac{\pi\,e\,AR}{2\,C_{D,0}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **examination critical** — KTU 2024 board papers for PBRAT604 have directly asked students to derive $E_{\max}$ in Part B questions worth 7 marks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thrust Required and Power Required in Cruise

**Step 1 — In steady cruise, $T = D$.**

$$
T_{req} = D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the drag polar $C_D = C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2}$:**

$$
T_{req} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S \left( C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $L = W$ to eliminate $C_L$ using $C_L = \dfrac{2W}{\rho V^{2} S}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{req} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\, C_{D,0} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\, K \!\left(\dfrac{2W}{\rho V^{2} S}\right)^{\!2} \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\, C_{D,0} + \dfrac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho V^{2} S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the **thrust required** as a function of airspeed $V$:**

$$
T_{req}(V) = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D,0}\, V^{2}}_{\text{Parasitic term}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\, \dfrac{1}{V^{2}}}_{\text{Induced term}}
$$

**Step 5 — Power required is $P_{req} = T_{req} \cdot V$:**

$$
P_{req}(V) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D,0}\, V^{3} + \dfrac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\, \dfrac{1}{V}
$$

**Step 6 — Minimize $P_{req}$ with respect to $V$ (set $dP_{req}/dV = 0$):**

$$
\tfrac{3}{2}\rho S C_{D,0}\, V^{2} - \dfrac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\, \dfrac{1}{V^{2}} = 0
$$

$$
V_{min-power}^{4} = \dfrac{4 K W^{2}}{3 \rho^{2} S^{2} C_{D,0}}
$$

$$
V_{min-power} = \left( \dfrac{4 K W^{2}}{3 \rho^{2} S^{2} C_{D,0}} \right)^{1/4}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The speed for **minimum power required** is *not* the same as the speed for **minimum drag**. The minimum-drag speed occurs when $L/D$ is maximum, and is generally higher than $V_{min-power}$. Endurance-maximizing flight is flown at $V_{min-power}$, while range-maximizing flight is flown at the **speed for maximum $L/D$**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fixed-Wing vs. Rotary-Wing UAV

| Parameter | Fixed-Wing UAV | Rotary-Wing (Multirotor) UAV |
|---|---|---|
| Lift generation | Forward motion over fixed wing | Vertical thrust from rotating blades |
| Endurance | **High (hours to days)** | Low (20 – 40 min typical) |
| Range | **Long (km to thousands of km)** | Short (typically < 5 km) |
| Hover capability | **No (except VTOL hybrids)** | **Yes** |
| Speed | **High (30 – 100+ m/s)** | Low (5 – 20 m/s) |
| Mechanical complexity | Moderate (moving control surfaces) | Higher (multi-motor + ESC synchronization) |
| Energy efficiency (L/D or g/W) | **High (L/D ≈ 15 – 30)** | Low |
| Launch requirement | Runway / catapult / hand-launch / VTOL | Vertical takeoff, no runway |
| Best suited for | Surveying, mapping, long-range ISR, pipeline monitoring | Inspection, cinematography, urban delivery |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fixed-Wing UAV

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["MISSION PLANNER GCS"]:::ext -->|TELEMETRY_DATALINK| nodeB["FLIGHT_CONTROLLER"]
    nodeB -->|PWM_SBUS_SIGNALS| nodeC["SERVO_ACTUATORS"]
    nodeC -->|MECHANICAL_MOTION| nodeD["CONTROL_SURFACES"]
    nodeD -->|AERODYNAMIC_FORCES| nodeE["AIRFRAME_WING"]
    nodeB -->|THROTTLE_SIGNAL| nodeF["ESC_UNIT"]
    nodeF -->|3_PHASE_POWER| nodeG["BRUSHLESS_MOTOR"]
    nodeG -->|TORQUE| nodeH["PROPELLER"]
    nodeH -->|THRUST| nodeE
    nodeE -->|FLIGHT_DYNAMICS| nodeI["SENSOR_SUITE"]
    nodeI -->|IMU_GPS_AIRDATA| nodeB
    nodeB -->|TELEMETRY| nodeA

    classDef ext fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef air fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above depicts the closed-loop control and propulsion architecture. Notice the **bidirectional flow** between the Ground Control Station (GCS) and the Flight Controller: commands flow *down* and telemetry flows *up*. The Flight Controller is the heart of the autonomous system — it fuses IMU, GPS, and air-data sensor inputs, runs the flight-control algorithm (e.g., PID, LQR, or model-predictive control), and outputs PWM/SBUS signals to drive the servos and electronic speed controller (ESC).

---

### 4.2 Control Surface Actuation Diagram (Roll-Pitch-Yaw)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["PILOT_INPUT OR AUTOPILOT"] --> node2["FLIGHT_CONTROLLER"]
    node2 -->|AILERON_LEFT_AND_RIGHT_REVERSED| node3["ROLL_AXIS"]
    node2 -->|ELEVATOR_UP_DOWN| node4["PITCH_AXIS"]
    node2 -->|RUDDER_LEFT_RIGHT| node5["YAW_AXIS"]
    node3 --> node6["BANKING_TURN"]
    node4 --> node7["CLIMB_AND_DESCEND"]
    node5 --> node8["COORDINATED_TURN"]
    node6 --> node9["PATH_FOLLOWING"]
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node9
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary vs. Secondary Control Surfaces:**
> - **Aileron** → Primary control for **roll** (lateral axis)
> - **Elevator** → Primary control for **pitch** (lateral axis)
> - **Rudder** → Primary control for **yaw** (vertical axis)
> - In coordinated turns, the **rudder** is used in conjunction with the ailerons to maintain a *coordinated* (slip-free) turn.

---

### 4.3 Aerodynamic Force Diagram in Steady Cruise

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["WEIGHT_W_DOWN"] --> nodeC["UAV_CENTER_OF_GRAVITY"]
    nodeB["LIFT_L_UP"] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD["THRUST_T_FORWARD"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["DRAG_D_BACKWARD"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["IN_STEADY_LEVEL_FLIGHT_L_EQUALS_W_AND_T_EQUALS_D"]

    classDef force1 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef force2 fill:#ffe4e6,stroke:#9f1239,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef body fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> In **climbing flight**, $L = W \cos\gamma$ and $T = D + W \sin\gamma$, where $\gamma$ is the climb angle. In **descending flight**, $\gamma$ becomes negative. In a **coordinated banked turn**, the lift vector tilts inward and a horizontal component provides centripetal force, requiring $L = W/\cos\phi$ where $\phi$ is the bank angle.
