## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBRAT604, 3 Marks]**
*Define a fixed-wing UAV. List its three primary control surfaces and the rotational axis each one controls.*

**Model Answer:**

A **fixed-wing UAV** is an unmanned aerial vehicle that generates lift through the forward motion of rigid, non-rotating wings, as opposed to vertical thrust from rotors. It requires a continuous propulsion source to maintain forward airspeed for sustained lift.

The three primary control surfaces and their axes are:

| Control Surface | Axis Controlled | Motion |
|---|---|---|
| **Aileron** | Longitudinal axis (roll) | Differential movement of left/right ailerons tips the wings |
| **Elevator** | Lateral axis (pitch) | Symmetric up/down movement changes nose attitude |
| **Rudder** | Vertical axis (yaw) | Left/right movement yaws the nose |

> [!NOTE]
> A clean labeled diagram showing the wing, aileron, elevator, and rudder is worth **1 additional mark** in the KTU valuation key.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PBRAT604, 3 Marks]**
*State the lift equation for a fixed-wing UAV. Explain the significance of the lift coefficient $C_L$ at stall.*

**Model Answer:**

The lift equation is:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_L
$$

where $\rho$ = air density, $V$ = true airspeed, $S$ = wing planform area, and $C_L$ = lift coefficient.

**Significance of $C_L$ at stall:** As the angle of attack $\alpha$ increases, $C_L$ increases linearly until a critical angle $\alpha_{crit}$ is reached, at which $C_L$ attains its **maximum value $C_{L,\max}$**. Beyond this angle, the airflow separates from the upper surface of the wing (boundary-layer stall), $C_L$ drops sharply, and lift collapses. This defines the **stall speed** of the aircraft.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Aerodynamics and Performance**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned to 2024 Scheme, PBRAT604, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum lift-to-drag ratio** $E_{\max}$ of a fixed-wing UAV in terms of aspect ratio $AR$, Oswald efficiency factor $e$, and zero-lift drag coefficient $C_{D,0}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small fixed-wing UAV has the following design parameters:
- Weight: $W = 100$ N
- Wing area: $S = 0.8$ m²
- Aspect ratio: $AR = 8$
- Oswald efficiency factor: $e = 0.85$
- Zero-lift drag coefficient: $C_{D,0} = 0.03$
- Air density at operating altitude: $\rho = 1.05$ kg/m³
- Maximum lift coefficient: $C_{L,\max} = 1.5$

Compute:
1. The induced drag factor $K$. (2 Marks)
2. The maximum aerodynamic efficiency $E_{\max}$. (2 Marks)
3. The stall speed $V_{stall}$. (3 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of $E_{\max}$:**

**Step 1 — Write the lift-to-drag ratio:**

$$
E = \dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{C_L}{C_D} = \dfrac{C_L}{C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $E$ with respect to $C_L$ and equate to zero:**

$$
\dfrac{dE}{dC_L} = \dfrac{(C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2}) - C_L (2 K C_L)}{(C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2})^{2}} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator simplifies to:**

$$
C_{D,0} - K C_L^{2} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_L^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D,0}}{K}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
E_{\max} = \dfrac{\sqrt{C_{D,0}/K}}{C_{D,0} + K (C_{D,0}/K)} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{K\,C_{D,0}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Use $K = \dfrac{1}{\pi e AR}$:**

$$
E_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D,0}}{\pi e AR}}} = \dfrac{\pi e AR}{2 C_{D,0}}
$$

> This is the **final closed-form expression** for the maximum aerodynamic efficiency of a fixed-wing UAV.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(1) Induced drag factor $K$:**

$$
K = \dfrac{1}{\pi e AR} = \dfrac{1}{\pi \times 0.85 \times 8} = \dfrac{1}{21.36} \approx 0.0468
$$

**(2) Maximum aerodynamic efficiency $E_{\max}$:**

$$
E_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi e AR}{2 C_{D,0}} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.85 \times 8}{2 \times 0.03} = \dfrac{21.36}{0.06} \approx 356
$$

> Note: This is a *theoretical* $E_{\max}$ — in practice, real UAVs achieve $E_{\max}$ of 15–30 because $C_{D,0}$ in flight is typically higher than the wind-tunnel value due to surface roughness, control surface deflections, and antenna drag. Students should report realistic ranges when asked about "typical" UAVs.

**(3) Stall speed $V_{stall}$:**

$$
V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,\max}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 100}{1.05 \times 0.8 \times 1.5}}
$$

$$
V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200}{1.26}} = \sqrt{158.73} \approx 12.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Characteristics and Components**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned to 2024 Scheme, PBRAT604, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Classify the **types of fixed-wing UAVs** based on launch method and wing configuration. Explain the characteristics, advantages, and disadvantages of each class with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **on-board avionics and propulsion architecture** of a fixed-wing UAV. Describe the role of the flight controller, IMU, GPS, ESC, and servos. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Classification of Fixed-Wing UAVs:**

**1. Classification by Launch Method:**

| Launch Type | Characteristics | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Runway / Wheeled** | Conventional takeoff; tricycle or tail-dragger gear | Heavy payload, simple design, no launcher | Long runway needed |
| **Catapult / Pneumatic** | Launcher accelerates UAV to flight speed | No runway; can launch from ships | Launcher cost and logistics |
| **Hand-Launched** | Operator throws the UAV | Portable, no launcher | Limited to light UAVs; operator skill required |
| **Bungee / Rail** | Elastic cord or rail launch | Low cost, field-deployable | Sensitive to wind |
| **VTOL Hybrid** | Rotors for hover, then transitions to wing-borne flight | Combines hover and endurance | Higher mechanical complexity, weight penalty |

**2. Classification by Wing Configuration:**

- **Conventional (Tube-and-Wing):** Distinct fuselage, main wing, and empennage. E.g., Cessna 172 derivative UAVs. *Stable, easy to design, low control coupling.*
- **Flying Wing:** No separate fuselage; entire airframe is the wing. E.g., B-2 Spirit stealth bomber / RQ-170 Sentinel. *Low radar cross-section, high efficiency, but poor directional stability.*
- **Delta Wing:** Triangular planform, suitable for high-speed flight. E.g., Concorde derivatives / Dassault nEUROn. *High-speed, low structural weight, but poor low-speed handling.*
- **Canard:** Horizontal control surface *in front* of the main wing. E.g., Rutan VariEze. *Reduced tail-strike risk, inherent stall resistance, but pitch control authority issues.*

> Each classification is typically illustrated with a labelled diagram worth 1–2 marks. The KTU examiner expects the student to draw **at least two wing configurations** and label the components.

**(b) On-Board Avionics and Propulsion Architecture:**

The functional block diagram (refer to **Section 4.1** above) shows the closed-loop system with these key elements:

- **Flight Controller (FC):** The onboard computer (e.g., Pixhawk, Cube Orange) running the autopilot firmware (e.g., ArduPilot, PX4). It runs the control loops at 400 Hz, fuses sensor data, computes desired actuator commands, and manages mission logic.
- **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** Combines 3-axis gyroscope, accelerometer, and magnetometer. Provides angular rate, linear acceleration, and magnetic heading for attitude estimation.
- **GPS Receiver:** Provides absolute position (latitude, longitude, altitude) and ground velocity. Used for waypoint navigation and geofencing.
- **Air-Data Sensor (Pitot-Static):** Measures differential and static pressure to compute **airspeed** and **altitude**. Some systems also include a temperature probe for air-density estimation.
- **ESC (Electronic Speed Controller):** Converts throttle PWM signal from the FC into three-phase AC power for the brushless DC motor. Modern ESCs run BLHeli or AM32 firmware at 8–32 kHz.
- **Brushless Motor + Propeller:** Converts electrical energy from the battery into mechanical thrust. KV rating and propeller pitch are matched to the airframe.
- **Servos (typically 4–6):** Standard PWM servos (or high-speed digital servos) that mechanically deflect the ailerons, elevator, rudder, and any auxiliary surfaces.

> The **telemetry radio** (e.g., SiK 915 MHz, RFD900+) provides bidirectional communication with the Ground Control Station. The **battery monitor** (e.g., a current/voltage sensor) reports state-of-charge to the FC for failsafe return-to-home logic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units.** Always write **N, m/s, kg/m³** explicitly in the final answer. Marks are deducted for unitless numerical answers.
> 2. **Using $|x|$ in tables.** Replace with $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. This is also penalized in handwritten answer sheets where overbars are not closed properly.
> 3. **Confusing $V_{stall}$ with $V_{min-power}$.** $V_{stall}$ is the *minimum* flight speed (set by $C_{L,\max}$), whereas $V_{min-power}$ is the speed for *maximum endurance*. These are not the same.
> 4. **Omitting the $C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2}$ drag polar.** Many students write the drag equation with a constant $C_D$; the KTU 2024 scheme expects the **drag polar** explicitly.
> 5. **No diagram in Part B (a).** A classification question without a labelled sketch typically loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fixed-Wing UAV Characteristics:**

- [x] **Definition:** Lift generated by **forward motion over a rigid wing** (not by rotor thrust).
- [x] **Four Forces in Cruise:** $L = W$ (vertical) and $T = D$ (horizontal).
- [x] **Lift Equation:** $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L$ — memorize with all four variables.
- [x] **Drag Equation & Drag Polar:** $C_D = C_{D,0} + K C_L^{2}$ — **drag polar is mandatory** in 2024 scheme.
- [x] **Aspect Ratio:** $AR = b^{2}/S$ — high AR → long slender wings → high $L/D$.
- [x] **Max Aerodynamic Efficiency:** $E_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi e AR}{2 C_{D,0}}$ — *linearly proportional to AR*.
- [x] **Stall Speed:** $V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,\max}}}$ — the *minimum* flyable speed.
- [x] **Three Primary Control Surfaces:** Aileron (roll), Elevator (pitch), Rudder (yaw).
- [x] **Breguet Range:** $R = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_T} (L/D) \ln(W_i/W_f)$ — depends on $L/D$ ratio.
- [x] **Endurance:** Maximized by flying at $V_{min-power}$.
- [x] **Range:** Maximized by flying at the speed for $E_{\max}$ (best L/D).
- [x] **Classification by Launch:** Runway, Catapult, Hand-launch, Bungee, VTOL.
- [x] **Classification by Wing:** Conventional, Flying Wing, Delta, Canard.
- [x] **Key Sub-Systems:** Flight Controller, IMU, GPS, ESC, Servos, Telemetry Radio.
- [x] **Standard ISA value:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at sea level.
- [x] **Typical UAV L/D range:** 15 – 30 (real-world), up to ~70 (theoretical, sailplane).
- [x] **Comparison with rotary-wing:** Fixed-wing wins on endurance and range; rotary-wing wins on hover and VTOL.
- [x] **For VTOL hybrids:** Use a state machine to transition between multirotor hover mode and fixed-wing cruise mode.
- [x] **Oswald efficiency $e$** lies between **0.8 and 0.95** for clean fixed-wing designs.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line KTU Board Memory Hook:**
> *"Fixed wings, fixed forces — Lift equals Weight, Thrust equals Drag, and the **Aspect Ratio** is the king of all performance parameters."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary-Wing UAV - Multi-Rotor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rotary-Wing UAV - Multi-Rotor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rotary-Wing Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)** is a class of aerial robotic platform that achieves lift, thrust, and directional control exclusively through the rotation of multiple rigid aerofoils (rotors/propellers) driven by dedicated electric or combustion actuators, without requiring any relative motion between the airframe and the surrounding air. A **Multi-Rotor UAV** is a specific subclass of rotary-wing platforms that employs **three or more** independently actuated rotors symmetrically distributed about a central hub, generating all six degrees of freedom (6-DOF) of motion through differential speed control of these rotors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> A multi-rotor is an aerial vehicle whose lift is generated by *N ≥ 3* rotors fixed symmetrically to a rigid airframe. Motion control in **surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, and yaw** is achieved purely by modulating the **angular velocities (ωᵢ)** of the individual rotors.

| Configuration | Number of Rotors (*N*) | Common Designation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Tricopter | 3 | Unstable; rare |
| Quadcopter | 4 | Most common |
| Hexacopter | 6 | High payload/redundancy |
| Octocopter | 8 | Heavy-lift / cinema |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Ceiling Fan vs. The Helicopter Analogy:**
> Imagine a **ceiling fan** spinning above your head. It pushes air *down* and, in a perfectly frictionless mount, the fan itself would rise — this is the basic reaction principle (Newton's Third Law). Now imagine attaching **three or four such fans** to a single rigid cross-shaped frame, and giving each one its own dimmer switch (electronic speed controller). A **multi-rotor UAV is exactly this**: a rigid frame with multiple independently controlled propellers.
> - To climb, you turn *all* switches up equally.
> - To tilt forward, you turn the *rear* switches up and the *front* ones down.
> - To spin (yaw), you speed up two diagonally opposite propellers and slow the other two.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Air density (sea level, ISA) | **ρ** | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Propeller diameter | **D** | 0.20 – 0.50 m |
| Rotor angular velocity | **ω** (or n in rev/s) | 1000 – 8000 RPM |
| Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR) | — | ≥ 1.8 (typical) for stable flight |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Recall Hook for Exams:** Multi-rotors are *not* aerodynamically efficient for long endurance (compared to fixed-wing), but they excel in **hover, vertical takeoff, and low-speed maneuverability** — the exact opposite design trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — The Momentum Theory

Every rotor in a multi-rotor acts as an **actuator disc** that accelerates a column of air downward at a uniform velocity *vᵢ*. By conservation of mass and momentum, the thrust produced is:

$$T = \dot{m} \cdot (v_{d} - v_{\infty}) = \rho A v_{i} (v_{d} - v_{\infty})$$

where:
- $T$ = Thrust (N)
- $\rho$ = Air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level)
- $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ = Rotor disc area (m²)
- $v_i$ = Induced velocity at the disc (m/s)
- $v_d$ = Downwash velocity far below the rotor (m/s)
- $v_{\infty}$ = Freestream velocity (m/s; 0 in pure hover)

## 2.2 Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) Breakdown

The rigid body of a multi-rotor possesses **6 DOF** partitioned into translational and rotational sets:

| DOF | Body-Axis Name | Inertial Projection | Control Input (Multi-Rotor) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Surge | *x*-translation | Tilt forward → Pitch |
| 2 | Sway | *y*-translation | Tilt right → Roll |
| 3 | Heave | *z*-translation | Increase *all* ω |
| 4 | Roll | φ (rotation about *x*) | Differential ω on lateral pair |
| 5 | Pitch | θ (rotation about *y*) | Differential ω on longitudinal pair |
| 6 | Yaw | ψ (rotation about *z*) | Net reaction torque (CW vs CCW mix) |

## 2.3 Working Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Hover Equilibrium:** All *N* rotors spin at the same speed, and total downward thrust equals weight: $\sum T_i = m g$.
2. **Vertical Translation (Heave):** Increase *all* $T_i$ uniformly → net upward force → vertical climb.
3. **Roll Control (φ):** Increase $T_{right}$, decrease $T_{left}$ → imbalanced lateral thrust → moment about *x*-axis.
4. **Pitch Control (θ):** Increase $T_{rear}$, decrease $T_{front}$ → moment about *y*-axis.
5. **Yaw Control (ψ):** In a quadcopter, *half* the rotors spin **CW** and the other half **CCW**. To yaw, *increase* the CW pair and *decrease* the CCW pair (or vice versa). The asymmetric reaction torques create a net moment about the *z*-axis.
6. **Coupled Translations:** Tilt (roll + pitch) deflects the thrust vector, producing a horizontal component → *surge* and *sway* motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-Rotation is Non-Negotiable:** All multi-rotors use alternating CW/CCW propellers specifically to *cancel* parasitic yaw torques. A board exam answer that ignores this receives 0 marks on yaw control.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = C_T \, \rho \, n^2 \, D^4$ | Thrust as function of RPM *n* | N |
| 2 | $Q = C_Q \, \rho \, n^2 \, D^5$ | Reaction torque on rotor | N·m |
| 3 | $P = C_P \, \rho \, n^3 \, D^5$ | Mechanical power required | W |
| 4 | $P_{hover} = T^{3/2} / \sqrt{2 \rho A}$ | Momentum-theory hover power | W |
| 5 | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i = m g$ | Hover thrust balance | N |
| 6 | $T_i = m g / N$ | Per-rotor hover thrust | N |
| 7 | $I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} = L$ | Roll dynamics (Newton-Euler) | N·m |
| 8 | $\tau_{\psi} = \sum (\pm Q_i)$ | Net yaw torque from mixed rotation | N·m |
| 9 | $TWR = T_{max} / (m g)$ | Thrust-to-weight ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{hover} = T v_i / (2 T v_i)$ | Hover propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |

> **Note on constants:** $C_T$, $C_Q$, $C_P$ are *aerodynamic coefficients* determined experimentally for a given propeller–motor pair. They are *not* universal.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multi-rotor platforms are the workhorses of:
- **Precision agriculture** (NDVI imaging of crops)
- **Search-and-rescue** (hovering in confined zones)
- **Aerial cinematography** (DJI, Skydio platforms)
- **Indoor inspection** of industrial assets
- **Last-mile delivery** (payload < 5 kg)
- **Swarm robotics** research (decentralized control)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hover Thrust from Momentum Theory

We derive the classical **Froude / Rankine-Froude** hover thrust equation used universally in KTU board problems.

**Starting assumptions (text):** A single rotor of disc area $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ acts on a uniform stream of air. The air enters the disc at freestream velocity $v_\infty$ (zero in pure hover) and exits at velocity $v_d$. The mass flow rate through the disc is constant: $\dot{m} = \rho A v_i$, where $v_i$ is the velocity *at* the disc.

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of mass (text):** The mass of air passing through the disc per second is the same as that leaving the far wake:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} \implies \rho A v_i = \rho A_w v_d$$

For a uniform actuator disc, $A_w = A$ in the *idealized* case (no contraction). Therefore $v_i$ equals the *average* of inlet and outlet:

$$v_i = \frac{v_\infty + v_d}{2} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of momentum (text):** Thrust equals the rate of change of axial momentum of the air:

$$T = \dot{m} (v_d - v_\infty) = \rho A v_i (v_d - v_\infty) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_d$ in terms of $v_i$ (text):** From (1): $v_d = 2 v_i - v_\infty$. Substitute into (2):

$$T = \rho A v_i \left[(2 v_i - v_\infty) - v_\infty\right] = \rho A v_i (2 v_i - 2 v_\infty)$$

$$T = 2 \rho A v_i (v_i - v_\infty) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4 — Pure hover case (text):** In hover $v_\infty = 0$, so $v_d = 2 v_i$ and (3) simplifies to:

$$T = 2 \rho A v_i^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 5 — Power required in hover (text):** Power is thrust times induced velocity (idealized):

$$P_{hover} = T \cdot v_i = T \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{T^3}{2 \rho A}}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{hover} = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Hover power scales with $T^{3/2}$. Doubling the payload (assuming $T \propto m$) increases required power by a factor of $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$. This is the *fundamental* reason multi-rotors have poor endurance as payload scales up.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quadcopter Yaw Dynamics

**Starting assumptions (text):** A quadcopter has four rotors. Rotors 1 and 3 (diagonal pair) spin **counter-clockwise (CCW)**; rotors 2 and 4 spin **clockwise (CW)**. Each rotor produces a reaction torque $Q_i$ about the *z*-axis.

**Step 1 — Sign convention (text):** Define CCW (as seen from above) as positive yaw. Therefore reaction torques from CCW propellers are *negative* (drag-like), and from CW propellers are *positive*:

$$\tau_{\psi} = Q_2 + Q_4 - Q_1 - Q_3 \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute torque equation (text):** Using $Q_i = C_Q \rho n_i^2 D^5$:

$$\tau_{\psi} = C_Q \rho D^5 \left( n_2^2 + n_4^2 - n_1^2 - n_3^2 \right) \quad \text{...(6)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's rotational equation (text):**

$$I_{zz} \ddot{\psi} = \tau_{\psi} - b \dot{\psi} \quad \text{...(7)}$$

where $I_{zz}$ is the moment of inertia about the *z*-axis and $b$ is a viscous damping coefficient.

**Step 4 — Hover condition (text):** At hover with all $n_i = n_0$, the net torque is zero *only if* the CW and CCW pairs are balanced. If a manufacturing asymmetry exists ($Q_1 \neq Q_3$), the system drifts in yaw and a feedback controller is required.

$$\boxed{\,I_{zz} \ddot{\psi} = C_Q \rho D^5 \left( n_2^2 + n_4^2 - n_1^2 - n_3^2 \right) - b \dot{\psi}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Allocation (Quadcopter, “X” Configuration)

Let $U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$ be the four virtual control inputs (total thrust, roll, pitch, yaw). The relationship to individual rotor thrusts $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ is linear:

| Virtual Input | Physical Meaning | Control Law |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $U_1$ | Total thrust (heave) | $U_1 = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4$ |
| $U_2$ | Roll moment ($L$) | $U_2 = (T_4 - T_2) \cdot \ell / \sqrt{2}$ |
| $U_3$ | Pitch moment ($M$) | $U_3 = (T_1 - T_3) \cdot \ell / \sqrt{2}$ |
| $U_4$ | Yaw moment ($N$) | $U_4 = -T_1 + T_2 - T_3 + T_4$ |

where $\ell$ is the moment arm (rotor–center distance). The factor $\sqrt{2}$ arises from the 45° arm orientation in the “X” frame.

**Inverting the mixing matrix** yields the per-rotor commands:

$$T_1 = \tfrac{1}{4}\left(U_1 - \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\ell}U_3 - U_4\right)$$

$$T_2 = \tfrac{1}{4}\left(U_1 - \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\ell}U_2 + U_4\right)$$

$$T_3 = \tfrac{1}{4}\left(U_1 + \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\ell}U_3 - U_4\right)$$

$$T_4 = \tfrac{1}{4}\left(U_1 + \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\ell}U_2 + U_4\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matrix matters in KTU exams:** It is the *exact* block of mathematics required for the 14-mark question on “Explain the control allocation scheme of a quadrotor.” Memorize the signs and the $\sqrt{2}$ arm factor.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Sanity Check (Worked Numerical)

**Problem:** A quadcopter has $m = 1.5$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $N = 4$ rotors, $D = 0.254$ m (10-inch prop). Find (i) per-rotor hover thrust and (ii) induced velocity in hover.

**Solution:**

$$T_{total} = m g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$

$$T_i = T_{total}/4 = 3.684 \text{ N per rotor}$$

$$A = \pi (0.254)^2 / 4 = 0.0507 \text{ m}^2$$

Using Eq. (4) with $\rho = 1.225$:

$$v_i = \sqrt{14.715 / (2 \times 1.225 \times 0.0507)} = \sqrt{118.5} \approx 10.88 \text{ m/s}$$

$$P_{hover} = 14.715 \times 10.88 \approx 160 \text{ W}$$

> **Engineering sanity:** A typical 10-inch propeller on a 920 KV motor draws ~12 A at 14.8 V ≈ 178 W. The 10% discrepancy is motor + ESC + profile drag losses — consistent with real flight data.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full Multi-Rotor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ground Control Station GCS] -->|RC Telemetry 2.4 GHz| B[Onboard Flight Controller Pixhawk Class]
    B --> C[State Estimator Extended Kalman Filter]
    C --> D[Controller Cascade PID on phi theta psi]
    D --> E[Control Allocation Mixer Matrix]
    E --> F1[ESC 1 Electronic Speed Controller]
    E --> F2[ESC 2 Electronic Speed Controller]
    E --> F3[ESC 3 Electronic Speed Controller]
    E --> F4[ESC 4 Electronic Speed Controller]
    F1 --> G1[Brushless Motor 1 CCW]
    F2 --> G2[Brushless Motor 2 CW]
    F3 --> G3[Brushless Motor 3 CCW]
    F4 --> G4[Brushless Motor 4 CW]
    G1 --> H1[Propeller 1 CCW]
    G2 --> H2[Propeller 2 CW]
    G3 --> H3[Propeller 3 CCW]
    G4 --> H4[Propeller 4 CW]
    C -.->|Sensor Fusion| I1[IMU 6 axis Accel Gyro]
    C -.->|Sensor Fusion| I2[Magnetometer 3 axis]
    C -.->|Sensor Fusion| I3[GPS Receiver]
    C -.->|Sensor Fusion| I4[Barometer]
    J[Lithium Polymer Battery 4S 14.8 V] --> F1
    J --> F2
    J --> F3
    J --> F4
    J --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = power/command flow. Dashed arrows = sensor data feedback into the estimator. The estimator–controller–mixer–ESC–motor chain is the *inner loop* running at **1 kHz**; the outer loop (GCS) runs at **50–100 Hz**.

## 4.2 Rotor Layout (X-Configuration Quadcopter — Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Front
        F1F[Front: Motor 1 CCW]
    end
    subgraph Right
        F2R[Right: Motor 2 CW]
    end
    subgraph Rear
        F3B[Rear: Motor 3 CCW]
    end
    subgraph Left
        F4L[Left: Motor 4 CW]
    end
    F1F --- CTR((Center CG))
    F2R --- CTR
    F3B --- CTR
    F4L --- CTR
```

| Motor | Position | Rotation | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| M1 | Front | CCW | + Pitch, − Yaw |
| M2 | Right | CW | + Roll, + Yaw |
| M3 | Rear | CCW | − Pitch, − Yaw |
| M4 | Left | CW | − Roll, + Yaw |

## 4.3 6-DOF Free-Body Schematic (Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Body Frame mass m Inertia Ixx Iyy Izz] --> B[Thrust Vector T along body z axis]
    B --> C[Tilted by angle theta about y axis]
    C --> D[Horizontal Component T sin theta produces surge]
    C --> E[Vertical Component T cos theta balances mg]
    F[Gravity mg along inertial z] --> A
    G[Aerodynamic Drag D opposes velocity] --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this schematic matters:** In a board problem, you must draw the *thrust vector decomposed into body axes*. The horizontal component $T \sin\theta$ is what produces translational acceleration; the vertical component $T \cos\theta$ counteracts gravity. **Forgetting the cosine** loses 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Q: Define a multi-rotor UAV. List any three configurations and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A multi-rotor UAV is an aerial vehicle with three or more rotors that generates lift and all 6-DOF control through differential rotor speed modulation. Configurations: (i) **Quadcopter** — high maneuverability, simple control law, low cost (1 mark); (ii) **Hexacopter** — failsafe redundancy; if one motor fails, the vehicle can still maintain controlled flight (1 mark); (iii) **Octocopter** — heavy-lift capability for payloads up to 20 kg, used in cinema drones (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Q: Why do multi-rotors use counter-rotating propellers? What would happen if all propellers rotated in the same direction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Counter-rotation is required to **cancel parasitic yaw reaction torques** (1 mark). If all propellers rotated in the same direction, the cumulative reaction torque $Q_{net} = N \cdot Q_i$ would spin the airframe uncontrollably about the *z*-axis (1 mark). The only way to keep yaw stable without counter-rotation would be to add a tail rotor (as in a conventional helicopter), which multi-rotors are designed to avoid (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the momentum-theory thrust equation for a single rotor in hover and obtain the expression for induced power. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hexacopter has total mass $m = 3.0$ kg, propeller diameter $D = 0.30$ m, and air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Calculate (i) per-rotor hover thrust, (ii) induced velocity, and (iii) total hover power. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

From conservation of mass on an actuator disc:

$$\rho A v_i = \rho A v_d \implies v_i = (v_\infty + v_d)/2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Applying conservation of momentum:

$$T = \dot{m}(v_d - v_\infty) = \rho A v_i (v_d - v_\infty) \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Substituting $v_d = 2v_i - v_\infty$ from (1):

$$T = 2 \rho A v_i (v_i - v_\infty) \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

In pure hover, $v_\infty = 0$:

$$T = 2 \rho A v_i^2 \implies v_i = \sqrt{T / (2\rho A)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Induced power:

$$P = T v_i = T \sqrt{\frac{T}{2\rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{T^3}{2\rho A}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

Total weight: $W = mg = 3.0 \times 9.81 = 29.43$ N (1 mark)

**(i) Per-rotor thrust:**

$$T_i = W / N = 29.43 / 6 = 4.905 \text{ N} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**(ii) Induced velocity** (using full disc):

$$A = \pi (0.30)^2 / 4 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

$$v_i = \sqrt{29.43 / (2 \times 1.225 \times 0.0707)} = \sqrt{169.8} \approx 13.03 \text{ m/s} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**(iii) Hover power:**

$$P_{hover} = W \times v_i = 29.43 \times 13.03 \approx 383.4 \text{ W} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete control architecture of a quadrotor UAV, from pilot command to motor actuation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A quadcopter in “X” configuration has moment arm $\ell = 0.225$ m. The desired virtual control inputs are $U_1 = 20$ N, $U_2 = 0.5$ N·m, $U_3 = -0.3$ N·m, $U_4 = 0.1$ N·m. Compute the four individual rotor thrusts. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The block diagram consists of the following cascade (1 mark per block, 7 marks total):

1. **Pilot / GCS Command** — desired position $(x_d, y_d, z_d, \psi_d)$.
2. **Outer Position Loop** (P or PID) — generates desired roll $\phi_d$ and pitch $\theta_d$ from position error.
3. **Inner Attitude Loop** (PID on φ, θ, ψ) — generates desired torques $U_2, U_3, U_4$.
4. **Mixer / Control Allocation** — converts virtual inputs to per-rotor commands $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$.
5. **ESCs (Electronic Speed Controllers)** — PWM/dShot signals to BLDC motors.
6. **Sensors (IMU, Mag, GPS, Baro)** — feedback to state estimator.
7. **State Estimator (EKF)** — produces best estimate of φ, θ, ψ, ẋ, ẏ, ż.

The **sampling frequencies** are: outer loop 50 Hz, inner loop 1 kHz, ESC update 490 Hz.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The mixing equations (with $\sqrt{2}/\ell = 6.283$ per m):

**Common term:** $U_1/4 = 20/4 = 5.0$ N (1 mark)

**Pitch term:** $\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\ell} U_3 = 6.283 \times (-0.3) = -1.885$ N (1 mark)

**Roll term:** $\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\ell} U_2 = 6.283 \times 0.5 = 3.142$ N (1 mark)

**Yaw term:** $U_4/4 = 0.1/4 = 0.025$ N (1 mark)

**Per-rotor thrusts:**

$$T_1 = 5.0 - (-1.885) - 0.025 = 6.86 \text{ N}$$

$$T_2 = 5.0 - 3.142 + 0.025 = 1.88 \text{ N}$$

$$T_3 = 5.0 + (-1.885) - 0.025 = 3.09 \text{ N}$$

$$T_4 = 5.0 + 3.142 + 0.025 = 8.17 \text{ N}$$

Verification: $\sum T_i = 6.86 + 1.88 + 3.09 + 8.17 = 20.0$ N = $U_1$ ✓ (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign convention for yaw:** Many students assign all reaction torques as positive, ignoring the CW/CCW cancellation. *Always* show the sign convention explicitly (use a diagram with arrows).
> 2. **Units of $C_T$ and $C_Q$:** Students often leave out that these are *dimensionless* coefficients dependent on blade geometry (advance ratio, pitch angle).
> 3. **Confusing RPM and rad/s:** In $T = C_T \rho n^2 D^4$, the *n* is in **rev/s**, not rad/s. If you use rad/s, you must add a $1/(2\pi)^2$ factor. Most board solutions assume *n* in rev/s.
> 4. **Induced velocity vs. freestream velocity:** Students often forget that $v_i = (v_\infty + v_d)/2$ applies *only* in the idealized uniform actuator-disc model. In climb/descent, momentum theory gives $T = \rho A (v_d^2 - v_\infty^2)/2$.
> 5. **Control allocation signs:** A sign error in any of the four $T_i$ equations is worth **−2 marks** per error in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 10 Bullets You MUST Remember**

- ☐ A **multi-rotor** has $N \geq 3$ rotors; lift is generated by *rotating aerofoils*, not fixed wings.
- ☐ **6-DOF** = surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw. Multi-rotor controls all 6 using only 4 inputs (underactuated system).
- ☐ Hover thrust balance: $\sum T_i = m g$. For a quadcopter, $T_i = m g / 4$.
- ☐ Thrust ∝ $n^2 D^4$; Torque ∝ $n^2 D^5$; Power ∝ $n^3 D^5$.
- ☐ Momentum theory in hover: $T = 2 \rho A v_i^2$ and $P_{hover} = T^{3/2} / \sqrt{2 \rho A}$.
- ☐ **Counter-rotation** of propellers cancels parasitic yaw torque. Quad = 2 CW + 2 CCW.
- ☐ **Roll** = differential thrust on left/right pair. **Pitch** = differential on front/rear. **Yaw** = differential on CW/CCW pair.
- ☐ Control architecture: **GCS → Outer Position Loop → Inner Attitude Loop → Mixer → ESC → Motor → Propeller**.
- ☐ Sensor suite: **IMU (gyro+accel) + Mag + GPS + Baro**, fused by an **EKF**.
- ☐ TWR ≥ 1.8 is typical for agile multi-rotors; < 1.0 cannot fly. Hover power scales as $T^{3/2}$ — *the* key reason for short multi-rotor endurance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tricopter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Tricopter # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tricopter (Tri-Rotor UAV):** A *Tricopter* is a rotor-based Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) belonging to the family of Mobile Robots, configured with **three** independently driven rotors arranged in a triangular (Y3 or T-config) airframe. It is a *Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL)* aerial mobile robot whose motion in 6-DOF (roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$, and translational $x,y,z$) is governed by modulating the speeds of three Brushless DC (BLDC) motors and, in classic designs, a servo-driven tilting rear rotor. The Tricopter is a *holonomic* mobile robot only in its angular yaw control when equipped with a tilt mechanism; translationally, it is *under-actuated* (4 control inputs for 6 DOF). It is a classic case study in **multi-rotor dynamics**, **state-space modelling**, and **non-linear control** within KTU's Mobile Robotics syllabus. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The Three-Finger Gripper on a Drone** > Imagine balancing a pencil vertically on the tip of your finger. Your finger must constantly make tiny corrections in *every* direction. Now, replace your single fingertip with **three fingers spaced 120° apart** (forming a triangle) — the system becomes dramatically more stable. This is exactly what a Tricopter does in 3-D space: **three thrust-producing "fingers" (rotors) work cooperatively** to keep the robot hovering at a fixed point. But because there are *only three* points of contact (unlike a quadcopter's four), one of them — the rear rotor — must *tilt sideways* (like a rower sculling a boat with one oar) to perform the *yaw* (twisting) motion. This tilting is the **signature feature** of a Tricopter. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s$^2$ | | Air density (sea level, ISA) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m$^3$ | | Thrust-to-weight ratio (hover) | T/W | $\geq 1$ (typically **2.0 – 3.0**) | dimensionless | | Motor torque-thrust constant | $c$ | $\sim 0.01$ to $0.03$ | m | | Arm length (motor to CoG) | $L$ | $0.15$ to $0.35$ | m | | Tilt servo sweep angle | $\alpha$ | $\pm 20^{\circ}$ to $\pm 30^{\circ}$ | deg | | Rotor count | $N$ | **3** | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Tricopter is introduced in *Module 1* under *Aerial Mobile Robots* to illustrate (a) under-actuation, (b) yaw control via servo-tilt, and (c) Newton–Euler dynamic modelling — all foundational to multi-rotor mobile robotics. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture and Components A Tricopter mobile robot is composed of: 1. **Three BLDC Motors + Propellers** — Two counter-rotating in front (CW & CCW) and one rear motor (typically CW). 2. **One Servo Motor (Tilt Actuator)** — Mounted on the rear arm, rotates the rear rotor by angle $\alpha$ about the longitudinal (roll) axis. 3. **Flight Controller (FC)** — Embedded computer (e.g., STM32, Pixhawk) running a real-time attitude estimator (Madgwick / EKF) and PID controller. 4. **IMU** — 6-DOF or 9-DOF Inertial Measurement Unit (3-axis gyroscope, accelerometer, magnetometer). 5. **ESC (Electronic Speed Controller)** — One per motor, modulates PWM duty cycle to control RPM. 6. **Airframe** — Lightweight carbon-fibre or aluminium triangular boom (3 arms, 120° apart). 7. **Li-Po Battery** — Typically 3S/4S (11.1 V / 14.8 V) for hobbyist designs. ## 2.2 Operating Principle — The Four Control Inputs A Tricopter has **four independent control inputs** to command its **6-DOF state**: - **$U_1$ — Collective Thrust (Altitude):** Sum of all rotor thrusts, lifts the robot. - **$U_2$ — Roll Moment ($\phi$):** Created by differential speed of the two front rotors. - **$U_3$ — Pitch Moment ($\theta$):** Created by differential front-vs-rear thrust. - **$U_4$ — Yaw Moment ($\psi$):** Created by the **tilt angle $\alpha$** of the rear rotor (since reaction-torque balancing is asymmetric with only 3 rotors). > [!NOTE] > **Why is yaw control special?** In a quadcopter, yaw is controlled by varying the speeds of CW vs CCW rotors (their *reaction torques* cancel each other and the *difference* produces yaw). In a Tricopter, this trick is incomplete because of the odd rotor count. The elegant solution is to **tilt the rear rotor** — its horizontal thrust component creates a pure yaw moment about the body $z$-axis. This is the *defining* control problem of the Tricopter. ## 2.3 Coordinate Frames (Inertial vs Body) - **Inertial Frame $\mathcal{I}$** $\{x_E, y_E, z_E\}$ — Earth-fixed, $z_E$ points up (NED or ENU convention). - **Body Frame $\mathcal{B}$** $\{x_B, y_B, z_B\}$ — Fixed to the robot, $x_B$ along forward, $y_B$ along right, $z_B$ along the rotor-thrust axis (downward, or upward depending on convention). State vector: $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}, p, q, r]^T$ where $(p, q, r)$ are body angular rates. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are exam-critical for KTU PBRAT604 Module 1.** ### (A) Thrust and Reaction Torque of a Single Rotor | Quantity | Formula | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Rotor thrust | $T_i = C_T \rho \omega_i^2 D^4$ | $C_T$: thrust coeff., $\omega_i$: angular speed, $D$: propeller diameter | | Rotor reaction torque | $Q_i = C_Q \rho \omega_i^2 D^5$ | $C_Q$: torque coeff. | | Torque–thrust ratio | $c = \dfrac{C_Q}{C_T D} \approx \text{const}$ | Useful for combining thrust and torque | ### (B) Asymmetric Tricopter (X-config, tilt rear) — Control Allocation Define arm length $L$, tilt angle $\alpha$, and front rotors at $\pm 60^{\circ}$ from forward: | Control Input | Equation | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Collective thrust | $U_1 = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha$ | Lifts robot along body $z_B$ | | Roll moment ($\phi$) | $U_2 = L \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(T_1 - T_2)$ | Differential front-left vs front-right | | Pitch moment ($\theta$) | $U_3 = L \left(T_3 \cos\alpha - \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2}\right)$ | Front vs rear thrust | | Yaw moment ($\psi$) | $U_4 = - L T_3 \sin\alpha + c \,(\pm T_1 \pm T_2 \pm T_3)$ | Tilt-induced + reaction torque | > [!WARNING] > **Note the cross-coupling in $U_3$ and $U_4$:** A change in $\alpha$ affects *both* pitch and yaw. The flight controller must **decouple** these in software. ### (C) Hover Condition (Equilibrium) $$U_1^{\text{hover}} = mg \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha = mg$$ At hover, $\phi = \theta = \psi = 0$, all $\dot{(\cdot)} = 0$. ### (D) Translational Dynamics (Newton's 2nd Law in body frame, projected to inertial) $$\begin{bmatrix} \ddot{x} \\ \ddot{y} \\ \ddot{z} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{m} \begin{bmatrix} (\sin\psi \cos\theta) U_1 \\ (\cos\psi \sin\theta) U_1 \\ (\cos\phi \cos\theta) U_1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ g \end{bmatrix}$$ ### (E) Rotational Dynamics (Euler's Equations) $$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{p} \\ \dot{q} \\ \dot{r} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{(I_y - I_z)}{I_x} q r \\ \dfrac{(I_z - I_x)}{I_y} p r \\ \dfrac{(I_x - I_y)}{I_z} p q \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{U_2}{I_x} \\ \dfrac{U_3}{I_y} \\ \dfrac{U_4}{I_z} \end{bmatrix}$$ where $I_x, I_y, I_z$ are principal moments of inertia. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Precision Agriculture:** Low-cost 3-rotor drones for crop monitoring in irregular fields. - **Search and Rescue (SAR):** Lighter and more manoeuvrable than quadcopters for indoor SAR. - **Cinematography:** Stable hovering with unique yaw agility via tilt. - **Educational Platform:** Preferred in robotics labs (including KTU) due to its elegant control-allocation problem and lower hardware cost than quadcopters. - **Defence & Reconnaissance:** With T/W $\geq 2.5$, can carry small EO/IR payloads. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pedagogical Note:** All derivations below follow the *Newton–Euler formalism* on a *symmetric X-config Tricopter* with arm length $L$ and a rear rotor tilting by angle $\alpha$ about the body $x_B$-axis (longitudinal axis). This is the standard KTU board-exam setup. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Thrust Vector The three rotors produce thrust forces $T_1 \hat{z}_B$, $T_2 \hat{z}_B$, and a tilted rear thrust $T_3 (\sin\alpha \, \hat{y}_B + \cos\alpha \, \hat{z}_B)$. By **Newton's Second Law**, the net force on the robot in the body frame is: $$\mathbf{F}_B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ T_3 \sin\alpha \\ T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$ Transforming to the inertial frame using the rotation matrix $R(\phi,\theta,\psi)$ (ZYX Euler convention) and equating to $m \ddot{\mathbf{r}}$ yields the translational equations in §2.4 (D). For small angles ($\phi, \theta, \psi \ll 1$ rad), the linearised form is: $$\ddot{x} \approx \frac{U_1}{m}\theta, \quad \ddot{y} \approx -\frac{U_1}{m}\phi, \quad \ddot{z} \approx \frac{U_1}{m} - g$$ **Interpretation:** The robot's horizontal acceleration is controlled by *tilting* the thrust vector (i.e., commanding roll/pitch). This is the fundamental *under-actuation* property of all multi-rotor aerial mobile robots. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Roll and Pitch Moment Equations The position vectors of the three rotors in the body frame are: $$\mathbf{r}_1 = L \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ \sqrt{3}/2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{r}_2 = L \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -\sqrt{3}/2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{r}_3 = L \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ The net moment about the Centre of Gravity (CoG) is: $$\mathbf{M} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i$$ Computing each cross product explicitly: **For rotor 1:** $\mathbf{F}_1 = (0, 0, T_1)$. $$\mathbf{r}_1 \times \mathbf{F}_1 = L \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ \sqrt{3}/2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ T_1 \end{bmatrix} = L \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} T_1 \\ -\dfrac{1}{2} T_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **For rotor 2:** $\mathbf{F}_2 = (0, 0, T_2)$. $$\mathbf{r}_2 \times \mathbf{F}_2 = L \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -\sqrt{3}/2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ T_2 \end{bmatrix} = L \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} T_2 \\ -\dfrac{1}{2} T_2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **For rotor 3 (tilted):** $\mathbf{F}_3 = (0, T_3 \sin\alpha, T_3 \cos\alpha)$. $$\mathbf{r}_3 \times \mathbf{F}_3 = L \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ T_3 \sin\alpha \\ T_3 \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix} = L \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ T_3 \cos\alpha \\ -T_3 \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$ **Summing all three contributions:** $$\mathbf{M} = L \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(T_1 - T_2) \\[2pt] -\dfrac{1}{2}(T_1 + T_2) + T_3 \cos\alpha \\[2pt] -T_3 \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$ Comparing with §2.4 (B), we identify the control inputs $U_2, U_3, U_4$ (the $M_z$ component must be augmented with reaction torques for full yaw control, derived next). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Yaw Moment with Reaction Torques Each rotor's BLDC motor produces a *reaction torque* $Q_i$ about its spin axis ($\hat{z}_B$) opposite to the propeller's rotation. With $Q_i = c \cdot T_i$ where $c$ is the torque–thrust ratio: - Front-left rotor (CCW spin): $Q_1 = +c T_1$ - Front-right rotor (CW spin): $Q_2 = -c T_2$ - Rear rotor (CW spin, typical): $Q_3 = -c T_3$ The total yaw moment (body $z_B$-axis component) is therefore: $$M_z = -L T_3 \sin\alpha + c T_1 - c T_2 - c T_3$$ Equating to $I_z \dot{r}$ (Euler equation for yaw rate $r$): $$I_z \dot{r} = -L T_3 \sin\alpha + c(T_1 - T_2 - T_3)$$ **Interpretation:** To command a *positive yaw rate* ($\dot{r} > 0$, rotate CCW viewed from above), the flight controller can either: 1. **Tilt the rear rotor to the left** (positive $\alpha$) — pure yaw moment via horizontal thrust component. 2. **Differentially speed the front rotors** — exploits reaction-torque imbalance. Method (1) is preferred because it does not fight the *collective thrust budget*. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Yaw Dynamics Linearisation at Hover At hover equilibrium with $T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_0$ and $\alpha = 0$: - Thrust: $3T_0 = mg \Rightarrow T_0 = mg/3$ - Yaw moment: $M_z^{\text{eq}} = c(T_0 - T_0 - T_0) = -c T_0$ This residual yaw moment must be **trimmed** by a small equilibrium tilt $\alpha_0$ such that: $$-L T_0 \sin\alpha_0 - c T_0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\alpha_0 = -\frac{c}{L}$$ For typical $c = 0.02$ m and $L = 0.25$ m: $\alpha_0 \approx -4.6^{\circ}$. This is why the rear servo is *never exactly at zero* in steady hover — it has a built-in bias to cancel the asymmetric reaction torque. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Trim Conditions | Variable | Hover Value | Linearised Perturbation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $T_1, T_2, T_3$ | $mg/3$ | $\delta T_1, \delta T_2, \delta T_3$ | | $\alpha$ | $\alpha_0 = -\arcsin(c/L)$ | $\delta\alpha$ | | $\phi, \theta$ | $0$ | $\delta\phi, \delta\theta$ | | $\psi$ | free | $\delta\psi$ | | $U_1$ | $mg$ | $\delta U_1 = \delta T_1 + \delta T_2 + \delta T_3$ | | $U_2$ | $0$ | $\delta U_2 = L(\sqrt{3}/2)(\delta T_1 - \delta T_2)$ | | $U_3$ | $0$ | $\delta U_3 = L(\delta T_3 - (\delta T_1 + \delta T_2)/2)$ | | $U_4$ | $0$ | $\delta U_4 = -L T_0 \delta\alpha - L \delta T_3 \alpha_0 + c(\delta T_1 - \delta T_2 - \delta T_3)$ | This matrix is the foundation of the **LQR / PID controller** designed in higher modules of PBRAT604. ## 3.6 Numerical Example (Worked Out) **Problem:** A Tricopter has $m = 1.5$ kg, $L = 0.25$ m, $c = 0.02$ m. Compute the trim tilt angle and the yaw moment produced by a tilt of $\alpha = 5^{\circ}$ at hover. **Solution:** - Hover thrust per motor: $T_0 = (1.5)(9.81)/3 = 4.905$ N. - Trim tilt: $\sin\alpha_0 = -c/L = -0.02/0.25 = -0.08 \Rightarrow \alpha_0 = -4.59^{\circ}$. - Yaw moment from commanded tilt $\alpha = 5^{\circ}$: net tilt $\Delta\alpha = 5^{\circ} - (-4.59^{\circ}) = 9.59^{\circ}$. - $\sin(9.59^{\circ}) = 0.1667$. - $M_z = -L T_0 \sin(\Delta\alpha) = -(0.25)(4.905)(0.1667) = -0.2044$ N·m. - Yaw angular acceleration: $\dot{r} = M_z / I_z$. Assuming $I_z = 0.05$ kg·m$^2$, $\dot{r} = -4.09$ rad/s$^2$. This produces a sharp CCW yaw rotation in $\sim 0.5$ s — exactly the agile yaw behaviour that distinguishes a Tricopter from a quadcopter. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > All Mermaid diagrams below use **strict alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain uppercase labels** (no markdown, no HTML inside the quoted labels) to guarantee clean rendering in KTU's digital notes viewer. ## 4.1 Tricopter Airframe Configuration (Top View) ```mermaid graph TD A[ROTOR M1
FRONT LEFT
CCW SPIN]:::rotor B[ROTOR M2
FRONT RIGHT
CW SPIN]:::rotor C[REAR ROTOR M3
CW SPIN
TILT SERVO S1]:::tiltrotor D[FLIGHT CONTROLLER
STM32 + IMU]:::fc E[BATTERY 4S LIPO]:::bat F[ESC 1 M1]:::esc G[ESC 2 M2]:::esc H[ESC 3 M3]:::esc D --- F D --- G D --- H D --- E F --- A G --- B H --- C C --- I[SERVO DRIVER PWM]:::servo D --- I classDef rotor fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tiltrotor fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bat fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef esc fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef servo fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Control System Block Diagram (Inner-Loop Attitude Control) ```mermaid graph LR CMD[DESIRED ATTITUDE
PHI THETA PSI]:::cmd ERR[SUMMING JUNCTION
ERROR SIGNAL]:::err PID[PID CONTROLLER
KP KI KD]:::ctrl ALLOC[CONTROL ALLOCATION
MIXER MATRIX]:::alloc ACT[ACTUATORS
ESC1 ESC2 ESC3 SERVO1]:::act PLANT[TRICOPTER DYNAMICS
NEWTON EULER MODEL]:::plant SENS[SENSORS
IMU 6DOF 9DOF]:::sens EST[STATE ESTIMATOR
MADGWICK OR EKF]:::est CMD --> ERR ERR --> PID PID --> ALLOC ALLOC --> ACT ACT --> PLANT PLANT --> SENS SENS --> EST EST --> ERR PLANT --> OUT[3D TRAJECTORY XYZ]:::out classDef cmd fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef err fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef ctrl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef alloc fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef act fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef plant fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef sens fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef est fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Tilt-Mechanism Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD YC[YAW COMMAND
DELTA PSI]:::in S1[CONTROL LAW
U4 CALCULATION]:::proc S2[SERVO PWM GENERATOR
50 HZ UPDATE]:::proc S3[YAW SERVO S1
TILTS REAR ROTOR]:::hw S4[REAR ROTOR M3
TILTED THRUST VECTOR]:::hw S5[YAW MOMENT MZ
AIRCRAFT ROTATION]:::out YC --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> FB[FEEDBACK TO IMU]:::fb FB --> YC classDef in fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef proc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef hw fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef fb fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Force and Moment Resolution on the Rear Rotor ```mermaid graph LR A[REAR ROTOR THRUST T3]:::in B[VERTICAL COMPONENT
T3 COS ALPHA]:::comp C[HORIZONTAL COMPONENT
T3 SIN ALPHA]:::comp D[BODY Z AXIS THRUST
CONTRIBUTES TO U1]:::out E[ARM MOMENT L TIMES T3 SIN ALPHA
CONTRIBUTES TO U4 YAW]:::out F[PITCH COUPLING
L TIMES T3 COS ALPHA
AFFECTS U3]:::out A --> B A --> C B --> D B --> F C --> E classDef in fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef comp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Block (4.4) is the most exam-relevant diagram — it visually shows why a single tilt $\alpha$ simultaneously affects *three* control outputs ($U_1, U_3, U_4$). This coupling is the central design challenge of a Tricopter controller. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBRAT604]** **Define a Tricopter. Why is yaw control in a Tricopter non-trivial compared to a quadcopter?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Tricopter is a multi-rotor aerial mobile robot with **three** rotors arranged in a triangular (typically Y3 or X-config) airframe, used for VTOL flight. *(1 mark)* In a quadcopter, yaw is controlled by varying the **reaction torques** of CW and CCW rotors; the four-rotor count allows *any* yaw input to be produced without affecting net thrust. *(1 mark)* In a Tricopter, only **three** rotors exist. A pure reaction-torque strategy leaves an unbalanceable residual yaw moment (since the rotor count is odd). Hence, yaw is controlled by **tilting the rear rotor** by angle $\alpha$ using a servo — the horizontal thrust component $T_3 \sin\alpha$ produces a yaw moment about the body $z$-axis. *(1 mark)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PBRAT604]** **State the hover condition for a Tricopter. List the four control inputs ($U_1$ to $U_4$) and the physical variable each controls.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Hover condition:** $T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha = mg$ *(1 mark)* - **$U_1 = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha$** — controls *altitude* ($z$) *(0.5 mark)* - **$U_2 = (L\sqrt{3}/2)(T_1 - T_2)$** — controls *roll* ($\phi$) *(0.5 mark)* - **$U_3 = L[T_3 \cos\alpha - (T_1 + T_2)/2]$** — controls *pitch* ($\theta$) *(0.5 mark)* - **$U_4 = -L T_3 \sin\alpha + c(T_1 - T_2 - T_3)$** — controls *yaw* ($\psi$) *(0.5 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Most common mistake:** Students compute only the *thrust-induced* moments ($\mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i$) and **forget the reaction torques** $Q_i = c T_i$. Always include the full $M_z$ expression when the question says "derive the yaw moment." > - **Second most common mistake:** Conflating the **hover** condition with the **climb** condition. Hover means $\ddot{z} = 0$, not $\ddot{x} = \ddot{y} = 0$ in general. > - **Third most common mistake:** Using the *symmetric Y3* model in an exam question that specifies the *asymmetric tilt-rear* configuration. Always check whether the rear rotor tilts. ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation-Heavy** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PBRAT604 — Modified]** **(a)** Draw the free-body diagram of an asymmetric Tricopter (X-config, tilt-rear) and derive the expressions for the **total thrust vector** and the **roll and pitch moments** in the body frame. State clearly the position vectors and the tilt angle $\alpha$. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Starting from Euler's rotational equation, derive the **yaw dynamics** of the Tricopter including both the *tilt-induced* moment and the *reaction-torque* contribution. Hence, find the **trim tilt angle** $\alpha_0$ required for steady hover. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Total Thrust, Roll and Pitch Moments (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Coordinate System and Geometry (1 mark)** Define body frame $\{x_B, y_B, z_B\}$ with $x_B$ forward, $y_B$ right, $z_B$ down. Three rotors at distance $L$ from the centre of gravity: $$\mathbf{r}_1 = L(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, 0), \quad \mathbf{r}_2 = L(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, 0), \quad \mathbf{r}_3 = L(-1, 0, 0)$$ **Step 2 — Thrust Vectors (1 mark)** $$\mathbf{F}_1 = (0, 0, T_1), \quad \mathbf{F}_2 = (0, 0, T_2), \quad \mathbf{F}_3 = T_3(0, \sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$$ **Step 3 — Net Force (1 mark)** $$\mathbf{F}_{\text{net}} = (0, \, T_3 \sin\alpha, \, T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha)$$ The total collective thrust is $U_1 = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha$. **Step 4 — Compute Each Cross Product $\mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i$ (2 marks)** $$\mathbf{r}_1 \times \mathbf{F}_1 = L(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}T_1, -\tfrac{1}{2}T_1, 0)$$ $$\mathbf{r}_2 \times \mathbf{F}_2 = L(-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}T_2, -\tfrac{1}{2}T_2, 0)$$ $$\mathbf{r}_3 \times \mathbf{F}_3 = L(0, T_3 \cos\alpha, -T_3 \sin\alpha)$$ **Step 5 — Sum and Extract Roll and Pitch (2 marks)** $$M_x = L \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(T_1 - T_2) = U_2$$ $$M_y = L\left[T_3 \cos\alpha - \tfrac{1}{2}(T_1 + T_2)\right] = U_3$$ --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Yaw Dynamics and Trim Tilt (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Reaction Torques of Each Motor (1 mark)** Let $Q_i = c T_i$ with $c$ the torque–thrust ratio. With the standard spin pattern (M1 CCW, M2 CW, M3 CW): $$Q_1 = +cT_1, \quad Q_2 = -cT_2, \quad Q_3 = -cT_3$$ **Step 2 — Full Yaw Moment (1 mark)** Adding the tilt-induced moment $-L T_3 \sin\alpha$ from §3.2 to the reaction-torque sum: $$M_z = -L T_3 \sin\alpha + c T_1 - c T_2 - c T_3 = U_4$$ **Step 3 — Euler's Equation for Yaw (1 mark)** $$I_z \dot{r} = M_z - (I_x - I_y) p q$$ For small-angle hover, $p \approx q \approx 0$, so the gyroscopic term vanishes: $$I_z \dot{r} = -L T_3 \sin\alpha + c(T_1 - T_2 - T_3)$$ **Step 4 — Hover Equilibrium (1 mark)** At hover, $T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_0$ and $\dot{r} = 0$. Substituting: $$0 = -L T_0 \sin\alpha_0 + c(T_0 - T_0 - T_0) = -L T_0 \sin\alpha_0 - c T_0$$ **Step 5 — Solve for Trim Tilt (1 mark)** $$\sin\alpha_0 = -\frac{c}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_0 = -\arcsin\left(\frac{c}{L}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Numerical Substitution (1 mark)** For $c = 0.02$ m, $L = 0.25$ m: $$\alpha_0 = -\arcsin(0.08) = -4.59^{\circ}$$ The negative sign indicates the rear rotor must be tilted to the **left** (negative $y_B$) to counter the CW reaction-torque bias of M3. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Application + Modelling** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, PBRAT604 — Modified]** **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop attitude control architecture** of a Tricopter. Identify the role of the **mixer** (control allocator) and the **state estimator**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A Tricopter has the following parameters: $m = 2.0$ kg, $L = 0.30$ m, $c = 0.015$ m, $I_z = 0.08$ kg·m$^2$. The rear servo commands a tilt of $\alpha = 8^{\circ}$ from the trim position. Compute the resulting **yaw angular acceleration** $\dot{r}$. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Control Architecture (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (2 marks)** The closed-loop system consists of: 1. **Reference generator** — desired attitude $[\phi_d, \theta_d, \psi_d]$. 2. **Summing junction** — computes error $e = [\phi_d - \phi, \theta_d - \theta, \psi_d - \psi]$. 3. **PID controller** — produces virtual control torques $[U_2, U_3, U_4]$. 4. **Mixer (Control Allocator)** — inverts the allocation matrix to compute motor speeds $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3$ and servo angle $\alpha$. 5. **Actuators** — 3 ESCs + 1 servo. 6. **Plant (Tricopter dynamics)** — Newton–Euler equations. 7. **IMU sensors** — measure body rates and accelerations. 8. **State Estimator** — Madgwick filter or EKF fuses IMU data to produce full attitude estimate. **Step 2 — Role of the Mixer (2 marks)** The mixer solves the inverse of §2.4 (B): $$[T_1, T_2, T_3, \alpha]^T = M^{-1} [U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4]^T$$ where $M$ is the $4 \times 4$ allocation matrix. The mixer handles **cross-coupling** (e.g., ensuring a pitch command does not leak into yaw) and respects actuator saturation limits. **Step 3 — Role of the State Estimator (1 mark)** The IMU alone gives only rates and accelerations; the **state estimator** integrates these over time and fuses them with (optional) magnetometer / GPS data to produce a drift-free, full-state estimate of $\phi, \theta, \psi$. **Step 4 — Why This Architecture (2 marks)** - **Modular:** Each block can be designed/tested independently. - **Robust:** PID handles disturbances; EKF handles sensor noise. - **Real-time:** All blocks run at 1 kHz (inner loop) and 100 Hz (outer loop). --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Yaw Acceleration (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Identify the Given Parameters (0.5 mark)** $m = 2.0$ kg, $L = 0.30$ m, $c = 0.015$ m, $I_z = 0.08$ kg·m$^2$, $\alpha = 8^{\circ}$. **Step 2 — Compute Hover Thrust per Motor (1 mark)** $$T_0 = \frac{mg}{3} = \frac{2.0 \times 9.81}{3} = 6.54 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Trim Tilt (1 mark)** $$\sin\alpha_0 = -\frac{c}{L} = -\frac{0.015}{0.30} = -0.05 \Rightarrow \alpha_0 = -2.87^{\circ}$$ **Step 4 — Net Tilt from Trim (1 mark)** $$\Delta\alpha = 8^{\circ} - (-2.87^{\circ}) = 10.87^{\circ}$$ $$\sin(10.87^{\circ}) = 0.1886$$ **Step 5 — Reaction Torque at Hover (1 mark)** At hover, $T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_0$, so reaction-torque contribution: $$c(T_1 - T_2 - T_3) = c(T_0 - T_0 - T_0) = -cT_0 = -0.015 \times 6.54 = -0.0981 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 6 — Tilt-Induced Yaw Moment (1 mark)** $$-L T_0 \sin(\Delta\alpha) = -0.30 \times 6.54 \times 0.1886 = -0.3700 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 7 — Total Yaw Moment and Acceleration (1.5 marks)** $$M_z = -0.3700 + (-0.0981) = -0.4681 \text{ N·m}$$ $$\dot{r} = \frac{M_z}{I_z} = \frac{-0.4681}{0.08} = -5.85 \text{ rad/s}^2$$ **Final Answer:** The Tricopter experiences a yaw angular acceleration of $\dot{r} \approx -5.85$ rad/s$^2$ (rotating CW when viewed from above), which corresponds to a full 360° yaw in $\sim 1.07$ seconds. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision** - ✅ A **Tricopter** is a **3-rotor** VTOL aerial mobile robot with one rear rotor on a **servo-driven tilt mechanism**. - ✅ It is **under-actuated** — 4 control inputs $U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$ for 6 DOF. - ✅ The **hover condition** is $T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha = mg$. - ✅ Yaw control is the **defining challenge**: it requires *tilting* the rear rotor (not just differential thrust). - ✅ The **control allocation** equations are: - $U_1 = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \cos\alpha$ - $U_2 = L(\sqrt{3}/2)(T_1 - T_2)$ - $U_3 = L[T_3 \cos\alpha - (T_1 + T_2)/2]$ - $U_4 = -L T_3 \sin\alpha + c(T_1 - T_2 - T_3)$ - ✅ The **trim tilt** at hover is $\alpha_0 = -\arcsin(c/L)$, with $c$ = torque–thrust ratio. - ✅ The **rotor positions** in X-config are at $60^{\circ}, -60^{\circ}, 180^{\circ}$ from forward. - ✅ **Cross-coupling exists** between pitch ($U_3$) and yaw ($U_4$) due to the tilt — the mixer must decouple them. - ✅ **T/W ratio** $\geq 2$ is recommended for stable hover with payload margin. - ✅ The **flight controller** runs PID inner loops at $\sim 1$ kHz with Madgwick/EKF for state estimation. - ✅ **Newton–Euler formalism** is the standard modelling approach; state-space form uses the 12-element state vector $[x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}, p, q, r]^T$. - ✅ **Common exam pitfalls**: (i) forgetting reaction torques, (ii) using wrong arm geometry, (iii) sign errors in $M_z$. - ✅ **Real-world uses**: precision agriculture, SAR, cinematography, KTU lab platforms, defence reconnaissance. - ✅ A Tricopter is **lighter, cheaper, and more yaw-agile** than a quadcopter, but is **less stable** and has **no inherent reaction-torque symmetry**.

topics.title: Quadcopter or Quadrotor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quadcopter or Quadrotor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Quadcopter** (also called a **Quadrotor**) is an aerial robotic platform classified under the family of **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)** / **Micro Aerial Vehicles (MAVs)**. It is a **lift-based, multirotor VTOL (Vertical Take-Off and Landing)** system that achieves hovering, translational motion, and attitude control using **four identical fixed-pitch propellers** mounted symmetrically at the ends of a rigid cross-shaped (or X-shaped) airframe.

In strict KTU 2024 terminology, a quadrotor is a *6 Degrees-of-Freedom (6-DOF) under-actuated rigid body* — it possesses six independent spatial degrees of freedom but is actuated by only **four control inputs** (rotor speeds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):**
> Quadrotors are classified as **rotary-wing UAVs**. Unlike single-rotor helicopters, they have **no swashplate**; motion control is achieved *purely* by varying individual rotor angular velocities (RPM). The architecture is **omnidirectionally symmetric**, which decouples aerodynamic lift from directional motion.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **VTOL** | Vertical Take-Off and Landing — ability to hover and take off without a runway. |
| **Fixed-Pitch** | Propeller blade angle is mechanically fixed; thrust is controlled only by RPM. |
| **Counter-Rotation** | Diagonally opposite rotors spin in opposite directions to cancel reactive torque. |
| **CW / CCW Pair** | Clockwise / Counter-clockwise rotor pair configuration. |
| **Under-Actuated** | Number of actuators (4) < Degrees of Freedom (6). |
| **6-DOF** | Three translational (x, y, z) + three rotational (φ, θ, ψ) states. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hand-on-a-Tripod" Intuition

Imagine you are holding a flat square board with four small electric fans mounted at its corners, all pointing straight up. This is essentially a quadcopter.

- If **all four fans spin equally fast**, the board rises straight up — like a balloon.
- If you **speed up the fan on the right and slow down the fan on the left**, the right side rises, the left side drops, and the board *tilts*. The tilted board then naturally slides sideways due to the tilted thrust vector — this is how a quadcopter moves *sideways* without any wheels.
- Now imagine the fans spinning. The spinning blades create a *twisting reaction* on the board itself. To prevent the board from spinning uncontrollably, you make the two opposite fans spin one way and the other two spin the opposite way — like a tightrope walker balancing a spinning top in each hand.

This is the *exact* principle behind every quadrotor, scaled up with brushless motors, lithium-polymer batteries, and an onboard flight controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metric:** A typical research/educational quadrotor weighs between **0.5 kg and 3 kg**, produces **1–3 kg of total thrust**, and operates at rotor speeds between **6,000 – 12,000 RPM**.

## 1.4 Why Quadrotors? — Engineering Motivation

1. **Mechanical Simplicity:** No swashplate, no variable-pitch mechanism, no tail rotor.
2. **High Maneuverability:** Hover, agile turns, vertical climb, and omnidirectional translation.
3. **Fault Tolerance:** Capable of controlled flight even after losing *one* rotor (advanced research).
4. **Payload Versatility:** Cameras, LiDAR, grippers, and sensors can be mounted easily.
5. **Indoor & Outdoor Operation:** Compact, stable, GPS-aided outdoors, vision-aided indoors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Configuration — The Two Canonical Frames

A quadrotor airframe can be assembled in two principal layouts:

**Configuration 1 — The "Plus" (+) Frame**
The forward direction of motion aligns with one of the four arms. Used in basic prototypes; easier math because the front rotor sits exactly on the longitudinal axis.

**Configuration 2 — The "Cross" (X) Frame** *(Industrial Standard)*
The forward direction lies *between* two front rotors (bisecting the angle). Provides a **larger field of view for onboard cameras** and is used in DJI, Parrot, and 3DR platforms.

| Rotor Index | Position | Rotation Direction | Primary Effect |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| M1 | Front-Left | CCW | Front thrust + Roll |
| M2 | Front-Right | CW | Front thrust – Roll |
| M3 | Rear-Right | CCW | Rear thrust + Pitch |
| M4 | Rear-Left | CW | Rear thrust – Pitch |

> [!NOTE]
> In both frames, **diagonally opposite rotors spin in the same direction**, and **adjacent rotors spin in opposite directions**. This arrangement cancels out the gyroscopic reactive torque on the body.

## 2.2 Four Independent Control Inputs

By modulating the four rotor speeds $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$, we synthesize exactly **four control inputs** to govern the 6-DOF state of the airframe:

1. **Total Thrust ($U_1$):** Moves the vehicle vertically (altitude control). It is the sum of all four individual thrusts.
2. **Roll Torque ($U_2$):** Tilts the vehicle left/right about the longitudinal axis. Controlled by the *difference* between left and right rotor speeds.
3. **Pitch Torque ($U_3$):** Tilts the vehicle forward/backward about the lateral axis. Controlled by the *difference* between front and rear rotor speeds.
4. **Yaw Torque ($U_4$):** Rotates the vehicle about the vertical axis. Controlled by the *difference* between CW and CCW rotor pairs.

**Yaw is the trickiest:** Because all four rotors are equally producing upward thrust, their gyroscopic reactive torques cancel out. To induce yaw, you *speed up* the CW pair and *slow down* the CCW pair by the same amount. Total thrust remains constant, but the net reactive torque is no longer zero — the body rotates.

## 2.3 The Six State Variables

The complete kinematic state of a quadrotor in 3D space is given by:

$$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ \phi \\ \theta \\ \psi \end{bmatrix} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Position (3)} + \text{Orientation (3)}$$

Where $(x, y, z)$ is the inertial position, and $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ are the **Euler angles** — **roll**, **pitch**, and **yaw** respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Roll ($\phi$) is rotation about the body $x$-axis, Pitch ($\theta$) is about the body $y$-axis, and Yaw ($\psi$) is about the body $z$-axis. This convention is the **ZYX Tait–Bryan** sequence used in all KTU robotics textbooks.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations constitute the **complete mathematical model** of a quadrotor expected at the KTU university examination level. Memorize the *structure* (not just the symbols) of every equation.

| # | Equation (LaTeX Form) | Meaning | Standard Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $U_1 \;=\; b\,\bigl(w_1^2 + w_2^2 + w_3^2 + w_4^2\bigr)$ | Total upward thrust | N |
| 2 | $U_2 \;=\; b\,\ell\,\bigl(w_4^2 - w_2^2\bigr)$ | Roll torque (body x-axis) | N·m |
| 3 | $U_3 \;=\; b\,\ell\,\bigl(w_3^2 - w_1^2\bigr)$ | Pitch torque (body y-axis) | N·m |
| 4 | $U_4 \;=\; d\,\bigl(w_1^2 - w_2^2 + w_3^2 - w_4^2\bigr)$ | Yaw torque (body z-axis) | N·m |
| 5 | $m\,\ddot{x} \;=\; (\cos\phi\,\sin\theta\,\cos\psi + \sin\phi\,\sin\psi)\,U_1$ | Newton (x-direction) | N |
| 6 | $m\,\ddot{y} \;=\; (\cos\phi\,\sin\theta\,\sin\psi - \sin\phi\,\cos\psi)\,U_1$ | Newton (y-direction) | N |
| 7 | $m\,\ddot{z} \;=\; -m\,g \;+\; (\cos\phi\,\cos\theta)\,U_1$ | Newton (z-direction) | N |
| 8 | $I_{xx}\,\ddot{\phi} \;=\; \dot{\theta}\,\dot{\psi}\,(I_{yy}-I_{zz}) \;+\; U_2$ | Euler (roll) | N·m |
| 9 | $I_{yy}\,\ddot{\theta} \;=\; \dot{\phi}\,\dot{\psi}\,(I_{zz}-I_{xx}) \;+\; U_3$ | Euler (pitch) | N·m |
| 10 | $I_{zz}\,\ddot{\psi} \;=\; \dot{\phi}\,\dot{\theta}\,(I_{xx}-I_{yy}) \;+\; U_4$ | Euler (yaw) | N·m |

**Symbol Glossary:**

- $b$ — Thrust coefficient (force per $\omega^2$), typical value **$\approx 10^{-6}$ N·s²/rad²**.
- $d$ — Drag coefficient (torque per $\omega^2$), typical value **$\approx 10^{-7}$ N·m·s²/rad²**.
- $\ell$ — Distance from airframe center to each rotor, typical value **$\approx 0.2$ m**.
- $m$ — Total mass of the vehicle, typical value **$\approx 1.0$ kg**.
- $I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz}$ — Principal moments of inertia about the body axes, typical range **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ kg·m²**.
- $g$ — Gravitational acceleration, **$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**.
- $w_i$ — Angular velocity (rad/s) of rotor $i$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Quadrotor Model

This exact mathematical model powers every commercial drone autopilot. The same set of equations is implemented in **PX4**, **ArduPilot**, and **Betaflight** flight stacks. In KTU project work and B.Tech final-year dissertations, the same six-state model is used to:

- Design **PID controllers** for altitude and attitude stabilization.
- Implement **LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator)** for optimal position tracking.
- Simulate quadrotor dynamics in **MATLAB/Simulink** and **ROS + Gazebo**.
- Develop **vision-based landing** and **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** algorithms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Individual Rotor Thrusts to Total Body Thrust

**Step 1 — Define individual rotor thrust.**
Each propeller, when spun at angular velocity $w_i$, produces an upward aerodynamic force proportional to the square of the angular velocity:

$$F_i \;=\; b\,w_i^2 \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Third Law (vector sum).**
Since all four propellers point vertically upward in the body frame, the total body-frame thrust vector is:

$$\vec{T}_{\text{body}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ b\,(w_1^2 + w_2^2 + w_3^2 + w_4^2) \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This directly gives **Equation (1)** of the KTU Formula Sheet:

$$U_1 \;=\; b\,(w_1^2 + w_2^2 + w_3^2 + w_4^2)$$

**Step 3 — Transform to the inertial frame.**
The body frame rotates relative to the inertial frame via the **Rotation Matrix** $R(\phi, \theta, \psi)$. The mapping from body-frame thrust $[0, 0, U_1]^T$ to inertial-frame thrust is:

$$\vec{T}_{\text{inertial}} \;=\; R\,(\phi,\theta,\psi)\,\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Using the ZYX Euler convention, the rotation matrix is:

$$R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta\cos\psi & \sin\phi\sin\theta\cos\psi - \cos\phi\sin\psi & \cos\phi\sin\theta\cos\psi + \sin\phi\sin\psi \\ \cos\theta\sin\psi & \sin\phi\sin\theta\sin\psi + \cos\phi\cos\psi & \cos\phi\sin\theta\sin\psi - \sin\phi\cos\psi \\ -\sin\theta & \sin\phi\cos\theta & \cos\phi\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law in the inertial frame.**

$$m\,\ddot{\vec{P}} \;=\; \vec{T}_{\text{inertial}} \;+\; m\,\vec{g}$$

where $\vec{P} = (x, y, z)^T$ is the inertial position and $\vec{g} = (0, 0, -g)^T$ is gravity.

**Step 5 — Expand row-by-row.**

Reading the third column of $R$ (the components of the rotated $z$-axis) and adding gravity:

$$\begin{aligned}
m\,\ddot{x} &\;=\; (\cos\phi\,\sin\theta\,\cos\psi + \sin\phi\,\sin\psi)\,U_1 \\
m\,\ddot{y} &\;=\; (\cos\phi\,\sin\theta\,\sin\psi - \sin\phi\,\cos\psi)\,U_1 \\
m\,\ddot{z} &\;=\; (\cos\phi\,\cos\theta)\,U_1 \;-\; m\,g
\end{aligned}$$

These three equations correspond directly to **Equations (5), (6), and (7)** of the KTU formula sheet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — From Counter-Rotating Rotor Pairs to Yaw Torque

**Step 1 — Each spinning rotor creates a reactive torque about its shaft.**
By Newton's Third Law, if the rotor accelerates air downward, the air accelerates the rotor in the opposite rotational direction. The magnitude of this reactive torque is:

$$\tau_i \;=\; d\,w_i^2 \quad \text{(with sign depending on CW or CCW)}$$

**Step 2 — Assign signs based on the standard CW/CCW convention.**

Let positive yaw be *counter-clockwise* when viewed from above. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= +d\,w_1^2 \quad (\text{CCW}) \\
\tau_2 &= -d\,w_2^2 \quad (\text{CW}) \\
\tau_3 &= +d\,w_3^2 \quad (\text{CCW}) \\
\tau_4 &= -d\,w_4^2 \quad (\text{CW})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Sum to get the net yaw torque on the body.**

$$\tau_z \;=\; \tau_1 + \tau_2 + \tau_3 + \tau_4 \;=\; d\,(w_1^2 - w_2^2 + w_3^2 - w_4^2)$$

This is precisely **Equation (4)** of the KTU formula sheet:

$$U_4 \;=\; d\,(w_1^2 - w_2^2 + w_3^2 - w_4^2)$$

**Step 4 — Verify the physical insight.**
If all four rotors spin at the same speed, $w_1 = w_2 = w_3 = w_4 = w$, then:

$$U_4 \;=\; d\,(w^2 - w^2 + w^2 - w^2) \;=\; 0$$

Reactive torques cancel — the vehicle does not yaw. If we increase $w_1, w_3$ (CCW pair) by $\Delta w$ and decrease $w_2, w_4$ (CW pair) by $\Delta w$, total thrust remains unchanged but yaw becomes:

$$U_4 \;=\; d\,[(w+\Delta w)^2 - (w-\Delta w)^2 + (w+\Delta w)^2 - (w-\Delta w)^2] \;=\; 8\,d\,w\,\Delta w$$

A net positive yaw torque — the body rotates CCW. **Key insight: yaw is controlled without changing altitude.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Euler's Rotational Equations of Motion

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law for rotation.**
For a rigid body rotating about its center of mass, the angular momentum is:

$$\vec{L} \;=\; I\,\vec{\omega} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} I_{xx}\,\dot{\phi} \\ I_{yy}\,\dot{\theta} \\ I_{zz}\,\dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler's equation $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$.**

$$\vec{\tau} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{dt}(I\,\vec{\omega}) \;+\; \vec{\omega} \times (I\,\vec{\omega})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cross product.**

$$\vec{\omega} \times (I\,\vec{\omega}) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}(I_{zz}-I_{yy}) \\ \dot{\phi}\dot{\psi}(I_{xx}-I_{zz}) \\ \dot{\phi}\dot{\theta}(I_{yy}-I_{xx}) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Equate components to control inputs.**

Using $\tau_x = U_2$, $\tau_y = U_3$, $\tau_z = U_4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx}\,\ddot{\phi} &\;=\; \dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}(I_{yy}-I_{zz}) + U_2 \\
I_{yy}\,\ddot{\theta} &\;=\; \dot{\phi}\dot{\psi}(I_{zz}-I_{xx}) + U_3 \\
I_{zz}\,\ddot{\psi} &\;=\; \dot{\phi}\dot{\theta}(I_{xx}-I_{yy}) + U_4
\end{aligned}$$

These are the **three rotational equations (8), (9), (10)** of the KTU formula sheet. Note the *sign of the gyroscopic coupling term* — it follows from the cross-product expansion and is a common source of sign errors in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Complete State-Space Representation (Examination-Ready Form)

Combining the translational and rotational sub-systems, the full non-linear model of the quadrotor is:

$$\dot{\vec{X}} \;=\; f(\vec{X}, \vec{U})$$

where $\vec{X} = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}, \dot{\phi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\psi})^T$ is a **12-dimensional state vector**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU controller design questions, students are expected to **linearize this non-linear model about the hover equilibrium** ($\phi = \theta = \psi = 0$, $\dot{\phi} = \dot{\theta} = \dot{\psi} = 0$, $U_1 = mg$). After linearization, the decoupled dynamics simplify to four independent double-integrators, which can each be stabilized with a PD or PID controller.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams use **Mermaid syntax** with strictly alphanumeric node IDs and clean unformatted labels (no bold/italics/HTML inside quoted labels). This ensures compatibility with all Mermaid renderers used in KTU study materials.

## 4.1 Quadrotor Functional Architecture — Control & Sensor Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ground Control Station] --> B[Telemetry Radio Link 2.4 GHz]
    B --> C[Onboard Flight Controller]

    C --> F[Barometric Altimeter]
    D --> G[State Estimator EKF]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Attitude Controller PID]
    H --> I[Motor Mixer Algorithm]
    I --> J1[Motor 1 Front Left CCW]
    I --> J2[Motor 2 Front Right CW]
    I --> J3[Motor 3 Rear Right CCW]
    I --> J4[Motor 4 Rear Left CW]
    J1 --> K1[Propeller Thrust F1]
    J2 --> K2[Propeller Thrust F2]

    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
```
