
## 4.2 Quadrotor Degrees of Freedom and Rotor Mapping

```mermaid

    P[Position x y z] --> T1[Translation Control U1 Total Thrust]
    R[Roll phi] --> T2[Rotational Control U2 Roll Torque]

    Y[Yaw psi] --> T4[Rotational Control U4 Yaw Torque]
    T1 --> M1[Sum of all four rotor speeds]
    T2 --> M2[Difference between left and right rotors]
    T3 --> M3[Difference between front and rear rotors]
    T4 --> M4[Difference between CW and CCW pairs]
    M1 --> A1[Altitude Hover Climb Descend]
    M2 --> A2[Sideways Tilt Lateral Slide]
    M3 --> A3[Forward Tilt Longitudinal Slide]
    M4 --> A4[In place Yaw Rotation]
```

```mermaid
    subgraph X_CONFIG[Quadrotor X Configuration Top View]
        N1[Front Left M1 CCW]
```


> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:**
> - Diagram 4.1 traces the *causal* chain from a remote pilot's joystick to the spinning propellers.
> - Diagram 4.2 maps the **four control inputs** to the **four rotor speed differentials** — this is the *exact mapping* required to answer "explain the working of a quadrotor" type questions.
> - Diagram 4.4 illustrates the **classical closed-loop autopilot** structure (the same one implemented in PX4 and ArduPilot) — write this block diagram if asked to "draw and explain the control architecture of a quadrotor."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, PBRAT604]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"Define a quadcopter. Why is it classified as an under-actuated system?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Valuation Key Style):**

A quadcopter is a multirotor UAV that uses four fixed-pitch propellers mounted symmetrically on a rigid frame to achieve VTOL flight. (1 mark)

It is classified as an **under-actuated system** because it has **6 degrees of freedom** (three translations $x, y, z$ and three rotations $\phi, \theta, \psi$) but only **4 independent control inputs** ($U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$). (1 mark)

The mathematical consequence: two translational motions (e.g., $x$ and $y$) cannot be commanded independently — they must be controlled indirectly by *tilting* the airframe via roll and pitch. (1 mark)

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"Why do diagonally opposite rotors of a quadcopter spin in the same direction?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

To **cancel out the gyroscopic reactive torque** generated by each spinning propeller. (1 mark)

If all four rotors spun in the same direction, the cumulative reactive torque would spin the airframe uncontrollably in the opposite direction (Newton's Third Law). (1 mark)

By pairing the rotors as two CW and two CCW diagonals, the reactive torques cancel exactly when all four speeds are equal, and yaw can be induced precisely by unbalancing the CW/CCW pair. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester Examination, PBRAT604 Module 1]**

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — CHOSEN

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a quadrotor UAV. Discuss the role of counter-rotating rotors in attitude control. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
[Draw Diagram 4.4 from SECTION_4 as your answer figure. Label: Reference → PID Controller → Motor Mixer → Motors → Dynamics → Sensors → Estimator → Feedback.]

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The quadrotor operates by varying the angular speeds ($w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$) of its four fixed-pitch propellers. Four independent control inputs $U_1$ (thrust), $U_2$ (roll), $U_3$ (pitch), $U_4$ (yaw) are synthesized. Hover is achieved when $U_1 = mg$ with zero net torque.

**Counter-Rotation Role (2 marks):**
Diagonally opposite rotors spin in the same direction (e.g., M1 and M3 CCW; M2 and M4 CW). This causes the gyroscopic reactive torques to cancel at hover. Yaw is induced by increasing the speed of one diagonal pair relative to the other while keeping total thrust constant.

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the complete dynamic model of a quadrotor in 3D space. Write down the six state equations governing its translational and rotational motion. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Vector (1 mark):**
Define $\xi = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)^T$ and velocity vector $\dot\xi$.

**Step 2 — Translational Equations (3 marks):**
Using Newton's Second Law and the ZYX rotation matrix $R$:

$$\begin{aligned}
m\ddot{x} &\;=\; (\cos\phi\,\sin\theta\,\cos\psi + \sin\phi\,\sin\psi)\,U_1 \\
m\ddot{y} &\;=\; (\cos\phi\,\sin\theta\,\sin\psi - \sin\phi\,\cos\psi)\,U_1 \\
m\ddot{z} &\;=\; (\cos\phi\,\cos\theta)\,U_1 \;-\; m\,g
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Rotational Equations (3 marks):**
Using Euler's equation $\vec{\tau} = I\dot{\vec\omega} + \vec\omega \times I\vec\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} &\;=\; \dot\theta\,\dot\psi\,(I_{yy}-I_{zz}) + U_2 \\
I_{yy}\ddot{\theta} &\;=\; \dot\phi\,\dot\psi\,(I_{zz}-I_{xx}) + U_3 \\
I_{zz}\ddot{\psi} &\;=\; \dot\phi\,\dot{\theta}\,(I_{xx}-I_{yy}) + U_4
\end{aligned}$$

with control inputs $U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$ as defined in the KTU formula sheet.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q. (a)** Explain the four control inputs of a quadrotor in detail. How is yaw control achieved without changing the net thrust? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Four Control Inputs (4 marks):**

| Input | Physical Meaning | Rotor Speed Mapping |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $U_1$ | Total upward thrust (altitude) | $b(w_1^2 + w_2^2 + w_3^2 + w_4^2)$ |
| $U_2$ | Roll torque (lateral tilt) | $b\ell(w_4^2 - w_2^2)$ |
| $U_3$ | Pitch torque (longitudinal tilt) | $b\ell(w_3^2 - w_1^2)$ |
| $U_4$ | Yaw torque (heading) | $d(w_1^2 - w_2^2 + w_3^2 - w_4^2)$ |

**Yaw-without-Thrust-Changing Proof (3 marks):**

Let $w_1 = w_3 = w + \Delta w$ (CCW pair) and $w_2 = w_4 = w - \Delta w$ (CW pair).

Substituting into $U_1$:

$$U_1 \;=\; 2\,b\,(w+\Delta w)^2 + 2\,b\,(w-\Delta w)^2 \;=\; 4\,b\,(w^2 + \Delta w^2)$$

The first-order change in $\Delta w$ vanishes, so $U_1 \approx 4bw^2$ (no change).

Substituting into $U_4$:

$$U_4 \;=\; 2d\,(w+\Delta w)^2 - 2d\,(w-\Delta w)^2 \;=\; 8\,d\,w\,\Delta w$$

A net positive yaw torque is induced. **Yaw has been decoupled from altitude.**

---

**Q. (b)** A quadrotor of mass $m = 1.2$ kg with rotor arm length $\ell = 0.25$ m is in hover. Each rotor produces a thrust of **4 N**. Compute the total upward thrust and the steady-state rotor speed if the thrust coefficient $b = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s²/rad². **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Thrust (2 marks):**
With four rotors, total upward thrust is:

$$U_1 \;=\; 4 \times 4 \;=\; 16 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Hover Condition Check (1 mark):**
For hover: $U_1 = mg = 1.2 \times 9.81 = 11.772$ N.
Since $16 > 11.772$, the vehicle *can* hover with this configuration (with 4.228 N of excess thrust for climb).

**Step 3 — Rotor Speed (4 marks):**
Since all four rotors spin at the same speed $w$:

$$U_1 \;=\; 4\,b\,w^2$$

Solving for $w$:

$$\begin{aligned}
16 &\;=\; 4 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times w^2 \\
16 &\;=\; (4.8 \times 10^{-5}) \times w^2 \\
w^2 &\;=\; \dfrac{16}{4.8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
w^2 &\;=\; 3.333 \times 10^{5} \\
w &\;=\; 577.35 \text{ rad/s} \\
\text{RPM} &\;=\; 577.35 \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \;\approx\; 5514 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

The rotor must spin at approximately **577 rad/s** or **5514 RPM** per motor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors in Euler equations:** The gyroscopic coupling term in $I_{xx}\ddot\phi$ is $(I_{yy} - I_{zz})$, NOT $(I_{zz} - I_{yy})$. Memorize the *order*.
> 2. **Forgetting gravity:** The equation $m\ddot z = (\cos\phi\cos\theta)U_1 - mg$ has gravity in the inertial frame; many students omit the $-mg$ term and lose 1 full mark.
> 3. **Wrong rotation matrix:** Use the **ZYX Euler convention** with $R = R_z(\psi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\phi)$. Mixing Tait–Bryan conventions is a common 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Unit confusion:** Distinguish $w$ in **rad/s** (used in equations) from **RPM** (used in datasheets). Always convert: $\text{RPM} = w \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$.
> 5. **Drawing a helicopter instead of a quadrotor:** In "explain the working" questions, students who draw a single main rotor + tail rotor lose marks. The question is *specifically* about a four-rotor configuration.
> 6. **Omitting the block diagram:** In 7-mark "explain working principle" questions, a labeled block diagram of the control loop is worth **at least 2 marks**. Always include one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **quadrotor** is a **4-rotor, 6-DOF, under-actuated VTOL UAV** with **no swashplate**.
- ✅ **Counter-rotation** of diagonal pairs cancels reactive gyroscopic torque at hover.
- ✅ The **four control inputs** are total thrust $U_1$, roll $U_2$, pitch $U_3$, and yaw $U_4$.
- ✅ Thrust of each rotor: $F_i = b\,w_i^2$. Total thrust: $U_1 = b\sum w_i^2$.
- ✅ Yaw is decoupled from altitude by simultaneously *speeding up* one diagonal pair and *slowing down* the other by the same $\Delta w$.
- ✅ Roll and pitch are induced by *left–right* and *front–rear* speed differentials, respectively — these tilts then translate into lateral/longitudinal motion.
- ✅ The **state vector** is 12-dimensional: $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi, \dot x, \dot y, \dot z, \dot\phi, \dot\theta, \dot\psi)^T$.
- ✅ The **translational equations** use the ZYX rotation matrix and include a gravity term $-mg$ in the $z$-equation.
- ✅ The **rotational equations** are Euler's equations with gyroscopic coupling terms involving products of angular velocities.
- ✅ Standard KTU assumptions: rigid body, symmetric airframe ($I_{xy} = I_{xz} = I_{yz} = 0$), no aerodynamics on the body itself, and fixed-pitch propellers.
- ✅ The model is **non-linear**; for controller design, **linearize about hover** to obtain four decoupled double-integrators.
- ✅ Onboard **IMU + GPS + barometer + Extended Kalman Filter** form the sensor stack.
- ✅ Applications: aerial photography, precision agriculture, surveillance, search and rescue, package delivery, and research platforms for SLAM and swarm robotics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hexacopter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hexacopter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Hexacopter** is a six-rotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) belonging to the class of Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL) multirotor aerial robots. It is a rigid-body aerial platform with **6 DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** controlled by **six independently driven brushless DC motors** mounted on fixed arms arranged at the vertices of a regular hexagon (60° apart). It belongs to the broader family of **Micro Aerial Vehicles (MAVs)** studied extensively in modern mobile robotics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A hexacopter is a six-rotor aerial robotic platform whose motion is governed by a coupled, non-linear, under-actuated dynamic system — 6 DOF controlled through 4 control inputs (Total Thrust, Roll Torque, Pitch Torque, Yaw Torque) distributed across 6 actuators."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are **balancing a hexagonal plate on the tip of your finger** — that's the basic challenge of flying any multirotor. Now imagine six people holding that plate from above with vertical sticks, each able to push it up or down independently. By coordinating how hard each person pushes, you can tilt the plate forward, sideways, or rotate it. A **hexacopter is precisely this**: the six motors are the "people," and the airframe is the "plate." Unlike a quadcopter (which has only 4 such pushers and is "minimally actuated"), the hexacopter has **two extra rotors**, giving it built-in redundancy — much like a six-legged table compared to a four-legged one. If one "pusher" fails, the remaining five can still keep the plate stable.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
Standard parameters used universally in KTU-grade hexacopter modeling:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air density (sea level) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Thrust coefficient | $b$ | $\approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ | N·s²/rad² |
| Drag (torque) coefficient | $d$ | $\approx 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | N·m·s²/rad² |
| Arm length (motor to CG) | $L$ | **0.25 – 0.45** | m |
| Rotor inertia | $J_m$ | $\approx 5 \times 10^{-5}$ | kg·m² |
| Vehicle mass (typical) | $m$ | **1.5 – 3.0** | kg |
| Diagonal wheelbase (motor-to-motor) | $D$ | **0.55 – 0.90** | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Always quote $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ in derivations unless the question explicitly specifies a different altitude or gravitational condition.

### 1.4 Why Choose a Hexacopter Over a Quadcopter?
- **Redundancy (Fault Tolerance):** A hexacopter can hover and land safely even with **one motor failure**, whereas a quadcopter crashes immediately.
- **Higher Payload Capacity:** Six motors can collectively lift ~**50% more** payload than four motors of the same size.
- **Better Stability Margin:** Two extra control variables allow for **smaller individual motor corrections**, leading to smoother flight.
- **Trade-off:** Heavier, more expensive, and slightly less efficient than a quadcopter due to higher mass and power consumption.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6 DOF)
A hexacopter, being a rigid body in 3D space, possesses **six independent DOF**:
- **Translational DOF (3):** Surge ($x$), Sway ($y$), Heave ($z$)
- **Rotational DOF (3):** Roll ($\phi$), Pitch ($\theta$), Yaw ($\psi$)

However, it has only **4 independent control inputs**: $U_1$ (Total Thrust), $U_2$ (Roll Torque), $U_3$ (Pitch Torque), $U_4$ (Yaw Torque). This makes it a **4×6 under-actuated system**.

### 2.2 Rotor Configurations
Two principal arrangements exist:

**(a) X6 (Plus-style with 60° arms) — Most Common in KTU Syllabus:**
Motors are placed at vertices of a hexagon. The X6 is the **KTU-recommended configuration** for analysis.

**(b) Y6 (Coaxial Hex):** Two stacked rotors share each arm — used in heavy-lift industrial drones.

### 2.3 Newton–Euler Dynamic Framework — Step-by-Step Logic
The hexacopter is modeled as a **single rigid body** under the following forces and torques:
1. **Gravity** acts at the Center of Gravity (CG) in the inertial frame.
2. **Thrust forces** from each rotor act along the **local z-axis** of the body frame.
3. **Reaction torques** (drag torques) act about each rotor's spin axis.
4. **Aerodynamic damping** and **gyroscopic effects** oppose motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Logic — "Why four inputs control six motions":** Heave ($z$) is directly mapped to total thrust $U_1$. Roll and pitch are controlled by **differentials** of motor speeds. Yaw is controlled by **differentials** between CW and CCW motor groups. Surge and sway emerge **as a consequence** of tilting the thrust vector.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single-rotor thrust | $T_i = b \cdot \omega_i^2$ | N | $\omega_i$ in rad/s |
| 2 | Single-rotor reaction torque | $\tau_i = d \cdot \omega_i^2$ | N·m | $d$ is drag constant |
| 3 | Total thrust input | $U_1 = b \sum_{i=1}^{6} \omega_i^2$ | N | Acts along body $z_b$ |
| 4 | Roll torque input | $U_2 = \tfrac{bL\sqrt{3}}{2}\bigl(\omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_5^2 - \omega_6^2\bigr)$ | N·m | About body $x_b$ |
| 5 | Pitch torque input | $U_3 = bL\bigl(\omega_1^2 - \omega_4^2\bigr) + \tfrac{bL}{2}\bigl(\omega_2^2 - \omega_3^2 - \omega_5^2 + \omega_6^2\bigr)$ | N·m | About body $y_b$ |
| 6 | Yaw torque input | $U_4 = d\bigl(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2 + \omega_5^2 - \omega_6^2\bigr)$ | N·m | About body $z_b$ |
| 7 | Translational dynamics (x) | $m\ddot{x} = (\cos\phi \sin\theta \cos\psi + \sin\phi \sin\psi)\,U_1$ | N | Inertial frame |
| 8 | Translational dynamics (y) | $m\ddot{y} = (\cos\phi \sin\theta \sin\psi - \sin\phi \cos\psi)\,U_1$ | N | Inertial frame |
| 9 | Translational dynamics (z) | $m\ddot{z} = -mg + (\cos\phi \cos\theta)\,U_1$ | N | Inertial frame |
| 10 | Rotational dynamics (roll) | $I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} = U_2 + \dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}(I_{yy}-I_{zz})$ | N·m | Euler equation |
| 11 | Rotational dynamics (pitch) | $I_{yy}\ddot{\theta} = U_3 + \dot{\phi}\dot{\psi}(I_{zz}-I_{xx})$ | N·m | Euler equation |
| 12 | Rotational dynamics (yaw) | $I_{zz}\ddot{\psi} = U_4 + \dot{\phi}\dot{\theta}(I_{xx}-I_{yy})$ | N·m | Euler equation |
| 13 | Hover condition | $U_1^{hover} = mg$ | N | $\omega_i = \sqrt{mg/6b}$ |
| 14 | Power required (hover) | $P = 6\,T_i^{1.5} \cdot b^{-0.5}$ | W | Momentum-theory limit |

> [!NOTE]
> All angular quantities in the KTU table above are assumed in the **NED (North-East-Down) inertial frame** and the **FRD (Forward-Right-Down) body frame** — the PX4/ArduPilot convention adopted by KTU's ROS-based curriculum.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
The hexacopter dynamic model is the foundation for:
- **Precision agriculture drones** (DJI Agras series) — spraying payloads over crops.
- **Search-and-Rescue (SAR)** platforms — disaster-zone mapping with LiDAR.
- **Cinematography drones** — heavy DSLR + gimbal payload.
- **Indoor SLAM research** — because of stable hovering and high control bandwidth.
- **Last-mile delivery prototypes** — Amazon Prime Air, Wing (Alphabet).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Single-Rotor Thrust to Total Thrust $U_1$

**Step 1 — Thrust Produced by a Single Rotor**
From **momentum theory**, a rotor with disc area $A$ moving air of density $\rho$ downward at induced velocity $v_i$ produces thrust:

$$T_i = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Induced Velocity in Terms of Rotor Angular Speed**
For a rotor with pitch angle $\theta_p$ and diameter $D_p$, the induced velocity scales linearly with $\omega_i$. The **empirically validated** quadratic relation (used in KTU 2024 Scheme) is:

$$T_i = b\,\omega_i^2$$

where $b$ absorbs $\rho$, $A$, and the geometric constants into a single coefficient (units: N·s²/rad²).

**Step 3 — Summation over All Six Rotors**
Since all six rotors produce thrust along the body-frame $z_b$-axis, the total thrust is the **scalar sum** of individual thrusts:

$$U_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{6} T_i = b\sum_{i=1}^{6} \omega_i^2$$

This is the *first control input* $U_1$ — directly used in the equations of motion as the body's thrust magnitude.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Control Allocation Matrix for an X6 Hexacopter

**Step 1 — Define the Motor Positions (in body frame)**
The six motors sit at the vertices of a regular hexagon of circumradius $L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (L,\; 0) \\
M_2 &= \left(\tfrac{L}{2},\; \tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\
M_3 &= \left(-\tfrac{L}{2},\; \tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\
M_4 &= (-L,\; 0) \\
M_5 &= \left(-\tfrac{L}{2},\; -\tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\
M_6 &= \left(\tfrac{L}{2},\; -\tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\
\end{aligned}$$

Rotational sense assignment: **M1, M3, M5 → Counter-Clockwise (CCW)** and **M2, M4, M6 → Clockwise (CW)**.

**Step 2 — Compute the Roll Torque (about body $x_b$-axis)**
A force $T_i$ acting at position $(x_i, y_i)$ creates a roll moment equal to $y_i \cdot T_i$ (moment-arm about x-axis):

$$\begin{aligned}
U_2 &= \sum_{i=1}^{6} y_i \cdot b\,\omega_i^2 \\
&= b\left[0 + \tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_2^2 + \tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_3^2 + 0 - \tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_5^2 - \tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_6^2\right] \\
&= \frac{bL\sqrt{3}}{2}\bigl(\omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_5^2 - \omega_6^2\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Pitch Torque (about body $y_b$-axis)**
The pitch moment-arm is $x_i$, hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_3 &= \sum_{i=1}^{6} x_i \cdot b\,\omega_i^2 \\
&= b\left[L\omega_1^2 + \tfrac{L}{2}\omega_2^2 - \tfrac{L}{2}\omega_3^2 - L\omega_4^2 - \tfrac{L}{2}\omega_5^2 + \tfrac{L}{2}\omega_6^2\right] \\
&= bL\left(\omega_1^2 - \omega_4^2\right) + \frac{bL}{2}\bigl(\omega_2^2 - \omega_3^2 - \omega_5^2 + \omega_6^2\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Yaw Torque (about body $z_b$-axis)**
The yaw reaction torque from motor $i$ is $\tau_i = d\,\omega_i^2$ with sign depending on rotational sense:

$$U_4 = d\bigl(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2 + \omega_5^2 - \omega_6^2\bigr)$$

**Step 5 — Compile the Control Allocation Matrix**
Combining the four input equations, the control allocation is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} U_1 \\ U_2 \\ U_3 \\ U_4 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
b & b & b & b & b & b \\
0 & \tfrac{bL\sqrt{3}}{2} & \tfrac{bL\sqrt{3}}{2} & 0 & -\tfrac{bL\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{bL\sqrt{3}}{2} \\
bL & \tfrac{bL}{2} & -\tfrac{bL}{2} & -bL & -\tfrac{bL}{2} & \tfrac{bL}{2} \\
d & -d & d & -d & d & -d
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_1^2 \\ \omega_2^2 \\ \omega_3^2 \\ \omega_4^2 \\ \omega_5^2 \\ \omega_6^2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **6×4 over-determined system** (6 motors producing 4 commands) is the *hallmark of hexacopter redundancy* — the Pseudo-Inverse $M^\dagger$ is computed onboard the flight controller to obtain $\omega_i^2$ from the desired $(U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4)$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Equations of Motion in Hover (Linearised)

In **hover condition**, $\phi \approx 0$, $\theta \approx 0$, $\psi \approx 0$, and $\dot{\phi},\dot{\theta},\dot{\psi} \approx 0$. The non-linear rotational equations reduce to the **linear decoupled form**:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx}\,\ddot{\phi} &= U_2 \\
I_{yy}\,\ddot{\theta} &= U_3 \\
I_{zz}\,\ddot{\psi} &= U_4
\end{aligned}$$

The translational equations become:

$$\begin{aligned}
m\ddot{x} &= U_1\,\theta \\
m\ddot{y} &= -U_1\,\phi \\
m\ddot{z} &= U_1 - mg
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The small-angle roll $\phi$ **tilts the thrust vector** away from vertical, producing a lateral force in the $-y$ direction. This is precisely **why horizontal motion in a multirotor is a "side-effect" of tilting**, not an independent control input.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example — Hover Motor Speed

**Problem:** A hexacopter of total mass $m = 2.4 \text{ kg}$ has $b = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$. Find the rotor speed at hover.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_1^{hover} &= mg = 2.4 \times 9.81 = 23.544 \text{ N} \\
6\,b\,\omega_{hover}^2 &= 23.544 \\
\omega_{hover}^2 &= \frac{23.544}{6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 261\,600 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2 \\
\omega_{hover} &= 511.47 \text{ rad/s} \approx 4884 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 numerical problems typically give $m$, $b$, $L$ and ask for hover RPM or the control input $U_i$ for a commanded roll rate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 X6 Hexacopter — Rotor Configuration (Block-Level Architecture Flow)
The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the six rotors, their rotational senses, and the four control inputs of an X6 hexacopter. This abstract block representation avoids the complexity of an exact 3-D drawing while remaining fully informative for KTU-level analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef ccw fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#2E86C1,color:#0B2545,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cw fill:#FDECEA,stroke:#C0392B,color:#78281F,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrl fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B,color:#7D6608,stroke-width:2px
    classDef body fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#239B56,color:#0E6251,stroke-width:2px

    fc[Flight Controller + IMU + PID Stack]:::ctrl

    subgraph X6RotorArray [X6 Hexacopter Rotor Array]
        direction LR
        m1[Rot1 Front CCW omega1]:::ccw
        m2[Rot2 Front-Right CW omega2]:::cw
        m3[Rot3 Rear-Right CCW omega3]:::ccw
        m4[Rot4 Rear CW omega4]:::cw
        m5[Rot5 Rear-Left CCW omega5]:::ccw
        m6[Rot6 Front-Left CW omega6]:::cw
    end

    body[(Rigid Hexacopter Airframe)]:::body

    fc -- U1 Total Thrust --> m1
    fc -- U1 Total Thrust --> m2
    fc -- U1 Total Thrust --> m3
    fc -- U1 Total Thrust --> m4
    fc -- U1 Total Thrust --> m5
    fc -- U1 Total Thrust --> m6

    m1 -- b omega1 squared --> body
    m2 -- b omega2 squared --> body
    m3 -- b omega3 squared --> body
    m4 -- b omega4 squared --> body
    m5 -- b omega5 squared --> body
    m6 -- b omega6 squared --> body

    body -- IMU Feedback phi theta psi p q r --> fc
```

### 4.2 Control Loop — Cascaded PID Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef outer fill:#FAD7A0,stroke:#CA6F1E,color:#6E2C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inner fill:#A9CCE3,stroke:#1B4F72,color:#0B2545,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#1E8449,color:#0E6251,stroke-width:2px

    rdes[Desired Setpoint phi d theta d psi d z d]:::outer
    err[Error Computation e1 e2 e3 e4]:::outer
    pidouter[Outer Loop PID Position Controller]:::outer
    pidinner[Inner Loop PID Rate Controller]:::inner
    mix[Control Allocation Pseudo-Inverse Mixer]:::inner
    pwm[PWM Signals to 6 ESCs]:::inner
    moto[Six Brushless Motors omega 1 to omega 6]:::out
    dyn[Hexacopter Nonlinear Dynamics 6 DOF]:::out
    imu[IMU + GPS + Baro Sensor Feedback]:::out

    rdes --> err
    err --> pidouter
    pidouter --> pidinner
    pidinner --> mix
    mix --> pwm
    pwm --> moto
    moto --> dyn
    dyn --> imu
    imu -.feedback.-> err
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The outer PID loop runs at ~**100 Hz** (position control), while the inner loop runs at ~**1 kHz** (rate control). The mixer is computed at 400–500 Hz. This is the standard PX4/ArduPilot inner-outer cascaded structure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define a hexacopter. Mention any two advantages it has over a quadcopter.

**Model Answer:**
A hexacopter is a six-rotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) that achieves vertical take-off and landing using six independently controlled brushless motors fixed at the vertices of a regular hexagon. It has six degrees of freedom (3 translational + 3 rotational) controlled through four input commands (total thrust, roll, pitch, yaw torques).
**Advantages over a quadcopter:**
1. **Redundancy/Fault tolerance:** Can continue stable flight and land safely with one motor failed, whereas a quadcopter cannot.
2. **Higher payload capacity:** Six motors collectively produce ~50% more thrust for the same rotor size, enabling heavier payloads.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two distinct advantages. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is meant by an "under-actuated" system in the context of a hexacopter? Justify with an example.

**Model Answer:**
An under-actuated system is one in which the number of independent control inputs is **less than** the number of Degrees of Freedom (DOF) to be controlled.
**Justification:** A hexacopter has **6 DOF** (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw) but only **4 independent control inputs** ($U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$).
**Example:** To move forward (surge, $+x$), the pilot must tilt the body forward (positive pitch $\theta$). This tilting also produces a small vertical velocity disturbance. The system cannot command forward motion without simultaneously affecting other states.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the **thrust and torque equations** for an X6-configured hexacopter. Assume motors are placed at the vertices of a regular hexagon of circumradius $L$ with alternating CW/CCW rotational senses. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define positions of the six rotors** in the body frame $\{x_b, y_b, z_b\}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (L,\,0) \quad M_2 = (\tfrac{L}{2},\,\tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}) \quad M_3 = (-\tfrac{L}{2},\,\tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}) \\
M_4 &= (-L,\,0) \quad M_5 = (-\tfrac{L}{2},\,-\tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2}) \quad M_6 = (\tfrac{L}{2},\,-\tfrac{L\sqrt{3}}{2})
\end{aligned}$$

Rotational sense: M1, M3, M5 → CCW (positive sign); M2, M4, M6 → CW (negative sign).

**Step 2 — Thrust of individual rotor** (empirically validated momentum-theory model):
$T_i = b\,\omega_i^2$

**Step 3 — Total thrust** $U_1$ is the scalar sum of all six thrusts (all act along $+z_b$):
$$U_1 = b(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2 + \omega_5^2 + \omega_6^2)$$

**Step 4 — Roll torque** $U_2$ about $x_b$-axis: arm is $y_i$ of each rotor.
$$U_2 = \tfrac{bL\sqrt{3}}{2}\bigl(\omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_5^2 - \omega_6^2\bigr)$$

**Step 5 — Pitch torque** $U_3$ about $y_b$-axis: arm is $x_i$ of each rotor.
$$U_3 = bL(\omega_1^2 - \omega_4^2) + \tfrac{bL}{2}(\omega_2^2 - \omega_3^2 - \omega_5^2 + \omega_6^2)$$

**Step 6 — Yaw reaction torque** $U_4$ about $z_b$-axis: drag-torque sum with alternating signs.
$$U_4 = d(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2 + \omega_5^2 - \omega_6^2)$$

> *(Mark split: 1 mark diagram of positions, 2 marks each for $U_1, U_2, U_3$, 1 mark for $U_4$ derivation. Total: 7 marks.)*

**(b)** A hexacopter of mass $m = 1.8 \text{ kg}$ has $b = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$, $d = 2.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$, and $L = 0.22 \text{ m}$. Compute **(i)** the hover motor speed and **(ii)** the motor speeds required to maintain a steady roll rate of $5^\circ/\text{s}$ while hovering. Assume $I_{xx} = 0.025 \text{ kg·m}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Hover Motor Speed:**
$$\begin{aligned}
U_1^{hover} &= mg = 1.8 \times 9.81 = 17.658 \text{ N} \\
6\,b\,\omega_{hover}^2 &= 17.658 \\
\omega_{hover}^2 &= \frac{17.658}{9.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 183\,937.5 \\
\omega_{hover} &= 428.88 \text{ rad/s} \approx 4095 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Motor Speeds for Steady Roll Rate of 5°/s:**

Steady state $\Rightarrow \ddot{\phi} = 0$, but $\dot{\phi} = 5^\circ/\text{s} = 0.0873 \text{ rad/s}$.
Required roll torque:
$$U_2 = I_{xx}\,\ddot{\phi} = 0.025 \times 0 = 0$$

Wait — for **constant** roll rate, $\ddot{\phi} = 0$, but a torque is still required to *maintain* that rate against aerodynamic damping. For a standard KTU problem assuming **no damping**, the additional roll torque needed is $U_2 = 0$, meaning all motors remain at hover speed.

However, interpreting the problem as **angular acceleration** to reach the rate within 0.5 s:
$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{0.0873}{0.5} = 0.1745 \text{ rad/s}^2 \\
U_2 &= I_{xx}\,\alpha = 0.025 \times 0.1745 = 4.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

Differential motor speed to produce this torque (assume only M2 and M5 change symmetrically):
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\omega^2 &= \frac{2\,U_2}{bL\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2 \times 4.36 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.22 \times 1.732} = 1430 \\
\Delta\omega &= 37.8 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

So $M_2, M_3$ increase by $\approx 37.8 \text{ rad/s}$ and $M_5, M_6$ decrease by the same. All motors must also collectively produce $U_1 = 17.658 \text{ N}$.

> *(Mark split: 2 marks for (i), 5 marks for (ii).)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **cascaded PID control architecture** of a hexacopter flight controller. Mention the role of the inner and outer loops. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A hexacopter flight controller employs a **two-loop cascaded PID structure** to decouple the fast rotational dynamics from the slower translational dynamics.

**Outer Loop (Position Controller, ~50–100 Hz):**
- **Inputs:** Desired trajectory $(x_d, y_d, z_d, \psi_d)$ and current state feedback.
- **Outputs:** Desired attitude setpoints $(\phi_d, \theta_d, \psi_d)$ and total thrust $U_{1,d}$.
- **PID gains** are tuned for smooth convergence to waypoints.
- Acts on the **position error** $e_p = \mathbf{p}_d - \mathbf{p}$.

**Inner Loop (Attitude/Rate Controller, ~1 kHz):**
- **Inputs:** Desired attitude from outer loop and current IMU measurements.
- **Outputs:** Body torques $(U_2, U_3, U_4)$ and total thrust $U_1$.
- **PID gains** are tuned for high-bandwidth rejection of disturbances (wind gusts, vibration).
- Acts on the **attitude error** $e_R = \mathbf{R}_d \ominus \mathbf{R}$ and the **angular-rate error** $e_\omega = \boldsymbol{\omega}_d - \boldsymbol{\omega}$.

**Control Allocation Mixer:**
Converts the four commands $(U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4)$ into **six PWM signals** by computing the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse of the $6 \times 4$ allocation matrix.

> *(Mark split: 2 marks block diagram, 3 marks outer loop, 2 marks inner loop.)*

**(b)** Derive the **translational equations of motion** of a hexacopter in the inertial frame using the Newton–Euler formulation. Use the standard Z-X-Y Euler angle convention. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rotation matrix** from body to inertial frame using $\psi \rightarrow \phi \rightarrow \theta$ order:
$$R_{b}^{i} = R_z(\psi)\,R_x(\phi)\,R_y(\theta)$$

Expanding:
$$R_{b}^{i} = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta - s\psi s\phi s\theta & -s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta + s\psi s\phi c\theta \\ s\psi c\theta + c\psi s\phi s\theta & c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta - c\psi s\phi c\theta \\ -c\phi s\theta & s\phi & c\phi c\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $c\alpha = \cos\alpha$ and $s\alpha = \sin\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Total thrust vector** in inertial frame:
The total thrust $U_1$ acts along body $z_b = [0, 0, 1]^T$, transformed into the inertial frame:
$$\mathbf{F}_{thrust}^{i} = R_{b}^{i}\,[0,\,0,\,U_1]^T = U_1\begin{bmatrix} c\psi s\theta + s\psi s\phi c\theta \\ s\psi s\theta - c\psi s\phi c\theta \\ c\phi c\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Gravity vector** in inertial frame:
$$\mathbf{F}_{grav}^{i} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -mg \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Newton's second law** for translation:
$$m\,\ddot{\mathbf{p}} = \mathbf{F}_{thrust}^{i} + \mathbf{F}_{grav}^{i}$$

**Step 5 — Component-wise expansion** gives the three scalar equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
m\ddot{x} &= U_1\,(\cos\psi \sin\theta + \sin\psi \sin\phi \cos\theta) \\
m\ddot{y} &= U_1\,(\sin\psi \sin\theta - \cos\psi \sin\phi \cos\theta) \\
m\ddot{z} &= U_1\cos\phi \cos\theta - mg
\end{aligned}$$

> *(Mark split: 1 mark rotation matrix, 2 marks force vectors, 2 marks Newton's law, 2 marks scalar equations.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by mixing the Z-X-Y and Z-Y-X Euler angle conventions. KTU 2024 uses **Z-X-Y (Tait–Bryan 321)** unless otherwise stated. Always verify the question.
> 2. **CCW vs CW Mistake:** In the yaw torque equation, the sign of $d$ flips depending on which motors you call "positive." Be consistent — adopt **CCW motors (M1, M3, M5) as positive spin** and stick with it.
> 3. **Moment-Arm Error:** Roll arm is the $y$-coordinate, pitch arm is the $x$-coordinate. Confusing these two is the most common deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting $U_1$ in Translational Eqns:** $U_1$ appears in the *lateral* equations as well during tilting — many students write $m\ddot{x} = 0$ for hover, missing the cross-coupling.
> 5. **Numerical Units:** $\omega$ in **rad/s** (not RPM) when plugging into $b\omega^2$. Convert using $\omega_{rad/s} = \omega_{RPM} \times \pi/30$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A **hexacopter** has 6 DOF but only 4 independent control inputs — making it **under-actuated**.
- [ ] The X6 configuration places rotors at **60° intervals** on a regular hexagon of circumradius $L$.
- [ ] Individual rotor thrust follows the quadratic law $T_i = b\,\omega_i^2$, where $b$ absorbs aerodynamic and geometric constants.
- [ ] The four control inputs are **$U_1$ (thrust), $U_2$ (roll), $U_3$ (pitch), $U_4$ (yaw)**, and they are distributed across six motors via a $6 \times 4$ **control allocation matrix**.
- [ ] The pseudo-inverse of the allocation matrix is what the **flight controller mixer** computes onboard at 400–500 Hz.
- [ ] **Hover condition:** $U_1 = mg$, yielding $\omega_{hover} = \sqrt{mg/(6b)}$.
- [ ] **Horizontal motion is NOT directly commanded** — it emerges as a side-effect of tilting the thrust vector.
- [ ] Modern flight controllers use a **cascaded PID architecture**: outer position loop (~100 Hz) and inner attitude loop (~1 kHz).
- [ ] The standard KTU Euler angle convention is **Z-X-Y (yaw–roll–pitch)**; sign conventions: NED inertial frame, FRD body frame.
- [ ] Key advantage over quadcopter: **fault tolerance** — can land safely with one motor out.
- [ ] Key trade-off: **higher mass and lower efficiency** than a quadcopter.
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** Precision agriculture, SAR, cinematography, delivery, indoor SLAM research.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 1
