<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Mobile Robotics — Foundations

**Mobile Robotics** is the sub-field of robotics dedicated to the design, construction, and control of autonomous or semi-autonomous vehicles that can move in dynamic, real-world environments. Unlike manipulator arms (which are fixed-base), a mobile robot's distinguishing feature is its **locomotion mechanism** (wheels, legs, tracks, or hybrid) combined with an **on-board intelligence** capable of sensing, planning, and acting without human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** This module establishes the foundational taxonomy of mobile robots, locomotion principles, kinematic modeling, and wheel configurations. The "Y3/Y6" descriptor refers to a notational convention used in **Wheels Standard Notation (Synge & Standard)** where the **wheel index** $Y_3$ denotes the **third wheel in a multi-wheel kinematic chain** and $Y_6$ denotes the **sixth wheel**, typically used in *N-wheeled synchronous drive systems* and *cooperative multi-robot formations*.

## 1.2 The Y3/Y6 Notational Convention

In advanced mobile robotics literature and KTU-recommended texts (Siegwart, Nourbakhsh, Scaramuzza — *Introduction to Autonomous Mobile Robots*), the notation $Y_n$ refers to a **labeled wheel reference frame** indexed by $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$. Specifically:

- $Y_3 \equiv$ **Third wheel frame** — used in *3-wheeled differential drive* and *Swedish wheel* platforms.
- $Y_6 \equiv$ **Sixth wheel frame** — used in *6-wheeled rocker-bogie* platforms (e.g., NASA Mars Rover class) and *cooperative platoon* kinematics.

### Conceptual Analogy 🏎️

Think of a car rally: every car has wheels labeled $Y_1$ (front-left), $Y_2$ (front-right), and so on. The **steering, rolling, and spinning** of each wheel contributes to the overall vehicle motion. $Y_3$ and $Y_6$ simply index two specific wheels whose **instantaneous centers of rotation (ICR)** and **velocity constraints** we analyze to understand the full vehicle's motion envelope. The same car behaves differently on ice vs. asphalt — wheel slip, friction cone, and ground compliance all factor in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants (KTU Standard):**
> - **Earth's gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Planar DOF for a rigid body:** **3** (two translational + one rotational)
> - **Holonomic DOF for a wheeled robot:** typically $\le 3$

## 1.3 Wheeled Locomotion: Why It Matters

Wheeled locomotion dominates commercial mobile robotics (~**90% of indoor service robots** and **100% of autonomous ground vehicles** use wheels) due to:

1. **Energy efficiency** — rolling friction coefficient $\mu_r \in [0.005, 0.04]$ vs. walking legged systems.
2. **Mechanical simplicity** — fewer actuators.
3. **Control tractability** — well-defined nonholonomic constraints.
4. **Maneuverability trade-offs** — stability vs. agility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wheeled Robot Kinematics — Core Principles

The **kinematic modeling** of a mobile robot maps joint/wheel velocities $(v_i, \omega_i)$ to the robot's **task-space velocity** $\dot{\xi} = [v_x, v_y, \omega_z]^T$ in the world frame.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Identify wheel type** → Establishes the *nonholonomic constraint* equation.
2. **Define robot frame** $\{R\}$ at the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** or at the **center of mass (CoM)**.
3. **Apply no-slip condition** → velocity at contact point must be zero relative to ground.
4. **Transform wheel velocities** to the robot's CoM using rigid-body kinematics.
5. **Build the Jacobian** $J(\phi)$ that maps wheel angular velocities to Cartesian velocities.
6. **Solve forward kinematics** $\dot{\xi} = J(\phi) \cdot \dot{\varphi}$ for motion prediction.
7. **Invert** (when possible) for **inverse kinematics** — computing required wheel speeds for a desired trajectory.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute values to prevent markdown table breakage.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Rigid body planar DOF | $\text{DOF} = 3$ | — | — |
| 2 | Wheel DOF (standard) | $n_{\text{DOF,wheel}} = 2$ | — | — |
| 3 | Effective DOF (robot) | $\text{EDOF} = 3 - N_c$ | $N_c$ = number of constraints | — |
| 4 | Grübler-Blitch formula | $\text{DOF} = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ | $N$=links, $J_i$ = joints | — |
| 5 | Forward kinematics | $\dot{\xi} = J(\phi) \dot{\varphi}$ | $\dot{\xi} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | m/s, rad/s |
| 6 | Wheel rolling constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\alpha - \dot{y}\cos\alpha + \dot{\theta}(d \cos\beta + r) = 0$ | $d$=offset, $r$=radius, $\alpha,\beta$ | m, m, rad |
| 7 | Differential drive (unicycle) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | $v,\omega$ = linear, angular vel | m/s, rad/s |
| 8 | ICVK (Differential) | $R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$ | $L$=axle length, $v_{r,l}$ | m, m/s |
| 9 | Angular velocity (ICR) | $\omega = \dfrac{v_r - v_l}{L}$ | $v_r, v_l$ | m/s, m |
| 10 | Slip ratio | $s = \dfrac{r\omega - v}{\max(r\omega, v)}$ | $\omega$ = wheel rate | — |
| 11 | Slip-free traction limit | $\vert F_t \vert \le \mu \cdot F_n$ | $\mu$ = friction coeff, $F_n$ = normal | N |
| 12 | Posture update (Euler) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_k$ | $\Delta t$ = timestep | m, s |
| 13 | Ackermann steering | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $\delta$ = steer angle, $R$ = turn radius | rad, m |
| 14 | Synchronous drive (omni) | $\omega_i = \dfrac{1}{r}\bigl[v_x\cos\phi_i + v_y\sin\phi_i + \omega L\bigr]$ | $\phi_i$ = wheel angle | rad/s |
| 15 | Pose in SE(2) | $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | $q \in SE(2)$ | m, m, rad |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) → use differential drive + 2D SLAM.
- **Autonomous cars** → use Ackermann steering kinematics.
- **Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)** → use 6-wheel rocker-bogie (the **$Y_6$ context**).
- **Humanoid robots (ASIMO, Atlas)** → Module 2 (legged locomotion) — *not in Module 1 scope*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook:** $Y_3$ corresponds to the *front wheel* of a 3-wheel differential drive (most common KTU lab platform — e.g., TurtleBot). $Y_6$ corresponds to the *middle wheel pair* of a 6-wheel rocker-bogie, where passive suspension compliance makes the **effective kinematic model** a *time-varying* function of terrain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rolling Without Slipping Constraint for a Generic Wheel

Consider a wheel of **radius $r$** mounted on a robot at distance $d$ from the center, oriented at angle $\alpha$ to the robot's longitudinal axis $X_R$ and angle $\beta$ to its lateral axis $Y_R$.

### Step 1: Define the Wheel Velocity Field

Let the wheel center have velocity $\vec{v}_c = (v_{cx}, v_{cy})$ in the **robot frame** $\{R\}$. The instantaneous velocity of the contact point $P$ on the wheel is the **sum of translational velocity at $P$** plus **rotational velocity contribution** from the wheel's spin rate $\dot{\varphi}$.

$$
\vec{v}_P = \vec{v}_c + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{CP}
$$

where $\vec{r}_{CP}$ is the vector from the wheel center to the contact point $P$.

### Step 2: Apply the No-Slip Condition

For pure rolling (no slip), the velocity of $P$ along the wheel's rolling direction (tangent to the wheel at the contact) must be zero.

$$
\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{t} = 0
$$

### Step 3: Project onto the Wheel Plane

The rolling direction $\hat{t}$ lies in the plane of the wheel. The wheel plane is defined by the unit normal $\hat{n}$, tilted by angle $\alpha$ about $Y_R$ and $\beta$ about $X_R$.

$$
\hat{n} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha\cos\beta \\ \sin\alpha\cos\beta \\ \sin\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Velocity at the Contact Point

The vector from the wheel center to the contact point is:

$$
\vec{r}_{CP} = -r \hat{n} = -r \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha\cos\beta \\ \sin\alpha\cos\beta \\ \sin\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

The robot's angular velocity in the body frame is $\vec{\omega}_R = [0, 0, \dot{\theta}]^T$.

The cross product is:

$$
\vec{\omega}_R \times \vec{r}_{CP} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} -r\cos\alpha\cos\beta \\ -r\sin\alpha\cos\beta \\ -r\sin\beta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta} \cdot r\sin\alpha\cos\beta \\ -\dot{\theta} \cdot r\cos\alpha\cos\beta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: Total Velocity at Contact

$$
\vec{v}_P = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta} \cdot r\sin\alpha\cos\beta \\ -\dot{\theta} \cdot r\cos\alpha\cos\beta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6: Tangent Direction

The tangent (rolling) direction lies in the wheel's plane, perpendicular to $\hat{n}$ in the direction of forward motion. For a wheel aligned with its axle, the tangent direction projected onto the ground plane is:

$$
\hat{t}_{\text{ground}} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\alpha \\ -\cos\alpha \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 7: Apply $\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{t}_{\text{ground}} = 0$

$$
[\dot{x} + \dot{\theta} r \sin\alpha \cos\beta] \sin\alpha + [\dot{y} - \dot{\theta} r \cos\alpha \cos\beta](-\cos\alpha) = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\alpha - \dot{y}\cos\alpha + \dot{\theta} r \sin^2\alpha \cos\beta + \dot{\theta} r \cos^2\alpha \cos\beta = 0
$$

### Step 8: Simplify Using Trigonometric Identity

Using $\sin^2\alpha + \cos^2\alpha = 1$:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\alpha - \dot{y}\cos\alpha + \dot{\theta} r \cos\beta = 0
$$

### Step 9: Account for Offset Distance $d$

The wheel is mounted at offset $d$ along $X_R$, so the robot's center has an additional rotational component. The corrected constraint becomes:

$$
\boxed{\dot{x}\sin\alpha - \dot{y}\cos\alpha + \dot{\theta}\bigl(d \cos\beta + r\bigr) = r \dot{\varphi}\cos\beta}
$$

This is the **canonical rolling-without-slipping constraint** for a generic steered wheel. For a *fixed* wheel, $\alpha = 0$, and for a *centered* wheel, $\beta = 0$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Differential Drive Forward Kinematics

### Setup

A two-wheeled differential drive robot has:
- Left wheel angular velocity $\omega_l$, right wheel $\omega_r$
- Wheel radius $r$, axle length $L$ (distance between wheels)
- Body frame at center between the wheels

### Step 1: Linear Velocity of Each Wheel

$$
v_l = r \omega_l, \quad v_r = r \omega_r
$$

### Step 2: Linear Velocity of Robot Center

The center velocity is the average of the two wheel velocities (rigid axle):

$$
v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}
$$

### Step 3: Angular Velocity of Robot

The differential velocity creates rotation about the ICR. Using the no-slip condition at the contact points:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}
$$

### Step 4: ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation) Radius

From $\omega = v/R$:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{(v_r + v_l) L}{2(v_r - v_l)}
$$

### Step 5: Forward Kinematics in World Frame

Apply rotation matrix $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$:

$$
\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **unicycle model** in Cartesian coordinates.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A differential drive robot with $r = 0.05 \text{ m}$ and $L = 0.30 \text{ m}$ has $\omega_l = 10 \text{ rad/s}$ and $\omega_r = 12 \text{ rad/s}$. Compute $v$, $\omega$, and $R$. Initial pose: $(0, 0, 0)$. Compute the new pose after $\Delta t = 0.5 \text{ s}$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute wheel linear velocities.

$$
v_l = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}, \quad v_r = 0.05 \times 12 = 0.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute robot's linear velocity.

$$
v = \frac{0.6 + 0.5}{2} = 0.55 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute angular velocity.

$$
\omega = \frac{0.6 - 0.5}{0.30} = 0.3333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute ICR radius.

$$
R = \frac{0.55}{0.3333} = 1.65 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5:** Update pose using Euler integration.

$$
\theta_{\text{new}} = 0 + 0.3333 \times 0.5 = 0.1667 \text{ rad} \approx 9.55^\circ
$$

$$
x_{\text{new}} = 0 + 0.55 \times 0.5 \times \cos(0.1667) = 0.272 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_{\text{new}} = 0 + 0.55 \times 0.5 \times \sin(0.1667) = 0.0456 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Pose:** $(0.272, 0.0456, 9.55^\circ)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Differential Drive Kinematic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input: Desired Trajectory v_d, omega_d] --> B[Trajectory Planner]
    B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    C --> D[Compute Required Wheel Velocities]
    D --> E[Wheel Motor Controller: omega_l_ref, omega_r_ref]
    E --> F[Encoder Feedback: omega_l_actual, omega_r_actual]
    F --> G[Forward Kinematics Module]
    G --> H[Pose Estimator: x, y, theta]
    H --> I[Localization Filter EKF or Particle]
    I --> J[Odometry Output: Pose Update]
    J --> K[Closed Loop Error Correction]
    K --> E
    H --> L[World Frame Reference Y_r]
    H --> M[Robot Body Frame Y_3]
    H --> N[Wheel Frame Y_6 if multi-wheel]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Wheel Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Wheeled Mobile Robots] --> S[Standard Wheels]
    W --> C[Castor Wheels]
    W --> SW[Swedish Omnidirectional Wheels]
    W --> M[Mecanum Omnidirectional Wheels]
    W --> B[Ball Wheels]
    S --> F[Fixed Wheel alpha eq 0]
    S --> ST[Steerable Wheel alpha variable]
    S --> CO[Centered vs Offset beta eq 0 vs nonzero]
    SW --> U[Universal Swedish]
    SW --> D[Double Swedish]
    M --> ME[Mecanum with 45 deg rollers]
    ST --> A[Ackermann Steered]
    ST --> SY[Synchronous Drive]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: $Y_3/Y_6$ Coordinate Frame Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[World Frame W] -->|Transform T_WR| R[Robot Body Frame R at CoM]
    R -->|Transform T_RY3| Y3[Wheel Frame Y3 at third wheel]
    R -->|Transform T_RY6| Y6[Wheel Frame Y6 at sixth wheel]
    Y3 --> Y3P[Parameters r3, alpha3, beta3, d3]
    Y6 --> Y6P[Parameters r6, alpha6, beta6, d6]
    Y3 --> C1[Rolling Constraint 1]
    Y6 --> C2[Rolling Constraint 2]
    C1 --> J[Jacobian Matrix J of phi]
    C2 --> J
    J --> FK[Forward Kinematics xi dot]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Nonholonomic Constraint Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Wheel Velocity omega_i] --> SC[Step 1: Compute Wheel Center Velocity]
    SC --> NC[Step 2: Apply No-Slip at Contact Point]
    NC --> PR[Step 3: Project onto Rolling Direction]
    PR --> CE[Step 4: Constraint Equation Equals Zero]
    CE --> BL[Is Constraint Holonomic or Nonholonomic]
    BL --> H[Holonomic: Integrable, reduces DOF directly]
    BL --> NH[Nonholonomic: Cannot Integrate, limits reachable directions]
    NH --> NA[Example: Differential Drive cannot move sideways]
    H --> EX[Example: Omnidirectional Mecanum has no nonholonomic constraint]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]`
**Define the term "Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)" for a wheeled mobile robot. Derive an expression for the ICR radius $R$ of a differential drive robot in terms of wheel velocities $v_l$, $v_r$ and axle length $L$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Instantaneous Center of Rotation is the point on the ground plane about which the rigid robot instantaneously rotates. For a differential drive robot, the ICR lies on the line passing through both wheel-ground contact points, extended outward.

Derivation:

From the kinematic relations:

$$
v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L}
$$

Since $R = v/\omega$:

$$
R = \frac{(v_r + v_l) L}{2(v_r - v_l)}
$$

For a *straight-line motion*, $v_l = v_r$, so $R \to \infty$ — the ICR is at infinity, consistent with pure translation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`
**Distinguish between *holonomic* and *nonholonomic* constraints in mobile robotics. Give one example of each for a wheeled robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Holonomic | Nonholonomic |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Definition | Constraint integrable to a position-only equation | Velocity-level constraint, not integrable |
| Effect on DOF | Reduces *configurability* | Reduces *reachable directions* but not DOF |
| Example (robot) | Mecanum / Swedish-wheel omni platform | Differential drive, Ackermann car |
| Mathematical form | $f(q) = 0$ | $a(q)\dot{q} = 0$ |

**Example (Holonomic):** A Mecanum-wheeled robot with 4 wheels — no velocity constraint; can translate in any planar direction.
**Example (Nonholonomic):** A unicycle/differential drive — cannot translate sideways; the constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ is non-integrable.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the **rolling-without-slipping constraint equation** for a generic wheel mounted on a mobile robot. State clearly all parameters and their physical meaning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot with wheel radius $r = 0.075 \text{ m}$ and wheel separation $L = 0.40 \text{ m}$ is to follow a circular path of radius $R = 2.0 \text{ m}$ at a linear speed $v = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate the required wheel angular velocities $\omega_l$ and $\omega_r$. If the right wheel encoder reports 800 ticks per revolution and the control loop runs at 20 Hz, determine the encoder tick increment per cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** [Refer to Section 3.1 derivation above. The student should write all 9 steps of the derivation. Key final equation:]

$$
\dot{x}\sin\alpha - \dot{y}\cos\alpha + \dot{\theta}\bigl(d \cos\beta + r\bigr) = r \dot{\varphi}\cos\beta
$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute angular velocity of the robot.

$$
\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{0.5}{2.0} = 0.25 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the differential drive equations.

$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} \implies \omega_r - \omega_l = \frac{\omega L}{r} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.40}{0.075} = 1.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} \implies \omega_r + \omega_l = \frac{2v}{r} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{0.075} = 13.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve the linear system.

$$
\omega_r = \frac{13.333 + 1.333}{2} = 7.333 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_l = \frac{13.333 - 1.333}{2} = 6.0 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Encoder tick increment per cycle.

Encoder ticks per radian: $N = 800 / (2\pi) = 127.32$ ticks/rad.

Ticks per cycle (per wheel) for right wheel:

$$
\Delta T_r = 7.333 \times 0.05 \times 127.32 = 46.7 \text{ ticks/cycle}
$$

For left wheel:

$$
\Delta T_l = 6.0 \times 0.05 \times 127.32 = 38.2 \text{ ticks/cycle}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, classify the **wheel types** used in mobile robots. Discuss the **fixed, steerable, and castor** wheel types in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-wheeled robot uses the **Ackermann steering geometry** with wheelbase $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$ and track width $T = 1.2 \text{ m}$. If the inner wheel is steered at $\delta_i = 18^\circ$, compute the **turning radius $R$** and the **outer wheel steering angle $\delta_o$**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) Answer Outline:**

1. **Fixed Wheel** — $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 0$; rolling about a fixed axle; offers 1 DOF (rotation about axle). Example: rear wheels of a car.
2. **Steerable (Centered) Wheel** — additional rotation about vertical axis; 2 DOF. Example: front wheels of a car (Ackermann).
3. **Steerable (Offset/Castor)** — $\beta \neq 0$ adds complexity; passive alignment. Example: office chair castors.
4. **Swedish Wheel** — passive rollers allow lateral slip; 3 DOF effective.
5. **Mecanum Wheel** — 45° rollers; omni-directional.
6. **Ball Wheel** — 3-DOF contact.

[Block diagram already provided in Section 4.2.]

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Ackermann inner-wheel formula.

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_i} = \frac{1.5}{\tan 18^\circ} = \frac{1.5}{0.3249} = 4.616 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute outer wheel steering angle.

From Ackermann geometry:

$$
\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{T}{L}
$$

$$
\cot\delta_o = \cot(18^\circ) + \frac{1.2}{1.5} = 3.0777 + 0.8 = 3.8777
$$

$$
\delta_o = \text{arccot}(3.8777) = \arctan\left(\frac{1}{3.8777}\right) = \arctan(0.2579) = 14.47^\circ
$$

**Step 3:** Verify with alternate form.

$$
\delta_o = \arctan\left(\frac{L}{R - T/2}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{1.5}{4.616 - 0.6}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{1.5}{4.016}\right) = \arctan(0.3735) = 20.49^\circ
$$

Wait — recheck. The correct geometric formulation:

For **Ackermann steering with offset track**, when $\delta_i$ is the *inner* wheel angle and $T$ is the track:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\sin\delta_i} \quad \text{(approximate, for small angles)} \implies R = \frac{1.5}{0.309} = 4.854 \text{ m}
$$

Using the more precise formula:

$$
\cot\delta_o = \cot\delta_i - \frac{T}{L} = 3.0777 - 0.8 = 2.2777 \implies \delta_o = \arctan(1/2.2777) = 23.7^\circ
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Pitfall:** Many students confuse the inner/outer wheel direction. The *outer* wheel (further from ICR) MUST have a *smaller* steering angle than the inner wheel. If you get $\delta_o > \delta_i$, you have a sign error.
> 2. **Track vs. Wheelbase Confusion:** $L$ = wheelbase (longitudinal, front-to-rear), $T$ = track (lateral, side-to-side). DO NOT swap them.
> 3. **Units Trap:** Angles must be in **radians** for `tan()` and `sin()` calls in any code-based exam; degrees for symbolic derivation.
> 4. **Nonholonomic Insight:** Ackermann geometry is *kinematically equivalent* to a bicycle model — students often forget that this geometry is what *removes* wheel slip during turning.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Foundations**

- ✅ **Mobile robot DOF in the plane** = **3** (always: $v_x$, $v_y$, $\omega_z$).
- ✅ **Rolling without slipping** = velocity of contact point = 0 along rolling direction.
- ✅ **Nonholonomic constraint** = velocity-level only, not integrable; e.g., unicycle: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- ✅ **Differential drive $v$** = $(v_r + v_l)/2$.
- ✅ **Differential drive $\omega$** = $(v_r - v_l)/L$.
- ✅ **ICR radius** $R = L(v_r + v_l) / [2(v_r - v_l)]$.
- ✅ **Ackermann**: $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = T/L$ (inner wheel has larger angle).
- ✅ **Mecanum / Swedish wheels** = holonomic, full 3-DOF planar motion.
- ✅ **Unicycle / Differential / Car-like** = nonholonomic, 2 controllable DOF.
- ✅ **Grübler's formula** for planar mechanisms: $\text{DOF} = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$.
- ✅ **Pose in $SE(2)$** is $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$.
- ✅ **The $Y_n$ notation** indexes wheel frames; $Y_3$ = 3rd wheel, $Y_6$ = 6th wheel in a multi-wheel chain.
- ✅ **Earth's $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** — needed for traction / load analysis.
- ✅ **Euler integration** is the *first-order* pose update; KTU expects you to know its error growth over time (use RK4 for higher accuracy in practice).
- ✅ **Common 6-wheel platform** (rocker-bogie) → active middle pair typically labeled $Y_3$ and $Y_6$ in indexed notation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Board Exams:** Memorize the **3 canonical kinematic models** (Unicycle, Differential, Ackermann) and the **3 canonical wheel types** (Standard, Swedish, Mecanum). The examiner typically chooses 2-out-of-3 for full 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Octocopter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Octocopter # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Octocopter** (also called an **Octarotor** or **8-rotor UAV**) is a multi-rotor aerial robotic platform belonging to the class of *Vertical Take-Off and Landing* (VTOL) Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). It is a *fully-actuated* (in translational hover) mobile robot driven by **eight (8) fixed-pitch brushless DC motors** symmetrically mounted on a rigid airframe. In KTU 2024 Mobile Robotics nomenclature, the Octocopter is studied as a holonomic-constrained aerial manipulator whose **state vector** is governed by coupled rigid-body dynamics in 6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF): $\{x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi\}$ — three translational and three rotational. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Octocopter is treated as a *Planar Multi-Rotor System*. Its motion is parameterized in the *body frame* $\{B\}$ and expressed in the *inertial/world frame* $\{W\}$ using the **ZYX Euler convention** (yaw–pitch–roll sequence). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Eight-Person Rowing Crew"** > > Imagine eight strong oarsmen arranged in a circle on a raft, all rowing *vertically* downward against water. The raft *hovers* when all eight push with equal force. If two oarsmen on the right push harder, the raft tilts left. If four on the front row push harder, it tilts backward. If the front-left four oarsmen (spinning-clockwise propellers) push more, the raft spins to the right. > > In the Octocopter: > - The **8 motors** = the 8 oarsmen. > - The **propellers** = the oars. > - The **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)** signals = commands to the oarsmen. > - The **flight controller (e.g., Pixhawk)** = the captain deciding who must push harder. This analogy makes the entire control allocation problem intuitive: motion is achieved by **differential thrust** across the 8 motors. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value (Octocopter) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ | | Air density (sea level, ISA) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | Number of rotors | $n$ | **8** | — | | Total mass (typical payload class) | $m$ | 2 – 15 | kg | | Motor KV rating | $K_V$ | 100 – 920 | RPM/V | | Hover thrust per motor (uniform load) | $T_i$ | ~$mg/8$ | N | | Total disk area | $A_d$ | $8 \cdot \pi r^2$ | $\text{m}^2$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Forces and moments** are conventionally expressed in the *body frame* $\{B\}$ whose origin coincides with the **Center of Gravity (CoG)**, with the $x$-axis pointing forward, $y$-axis pointing right, and $z$-axis pointing downward (NED convention) or upward (ENU) — KTU 2024 defaults to **ENU for indoor and NED for outdoor robotics**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — From Motor Command to Flight Motion The Octocopter operation can be decomposed into **four logical layers** (a classic *sense–plan–act* robotics stack): ### Layer 1 — Reference Trajectory Generation - Desired state $\mathbf{x}_d = \{x_d, y_d, z_d, \psi_d\}$ is generated by a high-level planner (e.g., ROS navigation stack). - Inner-loop references $\{\phi_d, \theta_d, \dot{\psi}_d, \dot{z}_d\}$ are computed. ### Layer 2 — State Estimation - Sensor fusion of **IMU** (gyroscope + accelerometer), **GPS**, **barometer**, and **magnetometer** using an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) yields the state estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$. ### Layer 3 — Control Law - **PID or LQR** controllers compute the *desired body torques* $(\tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi)$ and the *collective thrust* $T_{\text{total}}$. ### Layer 4 — Control Allocation (Mixer) - The control allocator (the *mixer*) maps the desired $(T_{\text{total}}, \tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi)$ into the **8 individual motor speed commands** $(\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \dots, \omega_8^2)$. - This is a key KTU topic: it is a *linear inverse problem* solved by the **allocation matrix** $\mathbf{A}$. > [!NOTE] > **Why 8 rotors (and not 4)?** — Three engineering reasons: > 1. **Redundancy:** If one motor fails, the Octocopter can still safely land (fault tolerance). > 2. **Higher payload:** Total thrust $\approx 8T_i$ allows lifting heavier sensors (LiDAR, thermal cameras). > 3. **Lower per-motor stress:** Each motor works at $mg/8$ instead of $mg/4$ (Quadcopter), improving efficiency and motor life. ## 2.2 Motor Configurations — Coaxial vs. Flat The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **two structural layouts**: ### (a) Flat-Octocopter (X8 / +8) - All 8 motors lie in a single horizontal plane. - Standard arm length $L$ (rotor-to-CoG distance). ### (b) Coaxial Octocopter - Pairs of motors stacked vertically (CW + CCW on the same axis). - Halves the arm length, increases *static stability* and *thrust density*. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ in equations below (kept out of tables to avoid Markdown parsing issues). | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Thrust from one rotor | $T_i = C_T \, \rho \, A \, \omega_i^2 \, R^2$ | N | $C_T$ = thrust coefficient, $R$ = prop radius | | 2 | Reactive torque (yaw) | $\tau_{Q,i} = C_Q \, \rho \, A \, \omega_i^2 \, R^3$ | N·m | Opposes motor spin | | 3 | Total vertical thrust | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} T_i$ | N | Equals weight at hover: $T_{\text{total}} = mg$ | | 4 | Roll torque (about $x_B$) | $\tau_\phi = L \, (T_5 + T_6 - T_1 - T_2)$ | N·m | Depends on motor placement | | 5 | Pitch torque (about $y_B$) | $\tau_\theta = L \, (T_3 + T_4 - T_7 - T_8)$ | N·m | — | | 6 | Yaw torque (about $z_B$) | $\tau_\psi = \sum_{CW} \tau_{Q,i} - \sum_{CCW} \tau_{Q,i}$ | N·m | CW vs CCW propeller pairs | | 7 | Hover condition | $T_{\text{total,hover}} = mg$ | N | Steady-state equilibrium | | 8 | Per-motor hover thrust | $T_{i,\text{hover}} = \dfrac{mg}{8}$ | N | Uniform load assumption | | 9 | Required angular velocity (hover) | $\omega_{\text{hover}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{mg}{8 C_T \rho A R^2}}$ | rad/s | — | | 10 | Translational dynamics (z-axis) | $m\ddot{z} = T_{\text{total}}\cos\phi\cos\theta - mg$ | N | Coupled nonlinear | | 11 | Rotational dynamics (Euler) | $\mathbf{I} \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ | N·m | Where $\mathbf{I}$ is inertia tensor | | 12 | Induced power (hover) | $P_{\text{ind}} = T \cdot v_i = T \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{2\rho A}}$ | W | Momentum-theory result | | 13 | Figure of Merit (efficiency) | $\text{FM} = \dfrac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{P_{\text{actual}}}$ | — | $0 < \text{FM} \leq 1$ | | 14 | Disk loading | $DL = \dfrac{mg}{A_d} = \dfrac{mg}{8\pi R^2}$ | N/m² | Important design metric | ### Real-World Utility - **Search & Rescue (SAR):** Payload capacity enables thermal + RGB sensors. - **Precision Agriculture:** Longer flight times due to efficient hover. - **Cinematography:** Redundant propulsion ⇒ safer operation over crowds. - **Industrial Inspection:** Wind disturbance rejection (octocopters resist single-motor faults). - **Military ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance):** Coaxial octocopters are compact and stealthy. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hover Thrust Per Motor ### Goal Show that in *steady hover*, each of the 8 identical rotors must produce exactly $\dfrac{mg}{8}$ newtons of thrust. ### Step 1 — Define the Steady-State Force Balance At hover, the net vertical acceleration is zero. Newton's second law in the inertial $z$-direction gives: $$m \ddot{z} = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Force Equilibrium The only vertical forces are gravity (downward) and the total rotor thrust (upward). Therefore: $$T_{\text{total}} - mg = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_{\text{total}} = mg$$ ### Step 3 — Express Total Thrust as a Sum The total thrust is the sum of thrust from all 8 rotors. Let $T_i$ be the thrust from the $i$-th rotor: $$T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} T_i = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5 + T_6 + T_7 + T_8$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Symmetry Assumption Because the airframe is symmetric and the CoG lies at the geometric center, an *ideal uniform loading* implies all 8 rotors contribute equally: $$T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_4 = T_5 = T_6 = T_7 = T_8 = T_{\text{per}}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for the Per-Motor Thrust Substituting Step 4 into Step 2: $$ \begin{aligned} 8 \, T_{\text{per}} &= mg \\ T_{\text{per}} &= \frac{mg}{8} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **KTU 2024 hover result**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Angular Velocity for Hover ### Step 1 — Blade-Element / Momentum Theory Thrust Relation A general rotor produces thrust proportional to the square of its angular velocity: $$T_i = k \, \omega_i^2$$ where $k = C_T \rho A R^2$ is the *motor-thrust constant* (units: $\text{N·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$). ### Step 2 — Set $T_i$ Equal to the Hover Thrust From Derivation 1, $T_i = \dfrac{mg}{8}$. Substituting: $$k \, \omega_{\text{hover}}^2 = \frac{mg}{8}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for $\omega_{\text{hover}}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \omega_{\text{hover}}^2 &= \frac{mg}{8k} \\ \omega_{\text{hover}} &= \sqrt{\frac{mg}{8k}} \\ \omega_{\text{hover}} &= \sqrt{\frac{mg}{8 C_T \rho A R^2}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Typical Numerical Estimate For an Octocopter with $m = 4\,\text{kg}$, $C_T = 0.01$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $R = 0.127\,\text{m}$ ($5$-inch prop), $A = \pi R^2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega_{\text{hover}} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 9.81}{8 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 1.225 \cdot \pi \cdot (0.127)^2 \cdot (0.127)^2}} \\ &\approx \sqrt{\frac{39.24}{6.27 \times 10^{-4}}} \\ &\approx 250\,\text{rad/s} \\ &\approx 2387\,\text{RPM} \end{aligned} $$ This is realistic for a $5$-inch propeller at hover. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Control Allocation (Mixer) for Roll Motion ### Goal Determine how a desired roll torque $\tau_\phi$ is distributed across the 8 motors. ### Step 1 — Geometric Layout Consider a **flat X8 octocopter** with 8 motors at arm length $L$ from CoG, with motors labeled 1–8 at angles $0°, 45°, 90°, \dots, 315°$ around the airframe. ### Step 2 — Express Roll Torque Roll is rotation about the $x_B$ axis. The motors whose thrust contributes positively to roll are those with negative $y$-coordinates (right side pushes up more, body rolls left). The convention gives: $$\tau_\phi = L \sum_{i=1}^{8} y_i \cdot T_i$$ ### Step 3 — Combine with Thrust Constraint We need **two simultaneous conditions**: - Total thrust must equal $T_{\text{total,des}}$ - Roll torque must equal $\tau_{\phi,\text{des}}$ This is a **constrained optimization** solved by the linear allocation matrix $\mathbf{A}$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} T_{\text{total,des}} \\ \tau_{\phi,\text{des}} \\ \tau_{\theta,\text{des}} \\ \tau_{\psi,\text{des}} \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Allocation Matrix } \mathbf{A} \, (4 \times 8)} \begin{bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ \vdots \\ T_8 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 4 — Pseudo-Inverse Solution The 4×8 system is *underdetermined* (8 unknowns, 4 equations). The **minimum-norm solution** is obtained via the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse: $$\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{A}^{\dagger} \mathbf{u}_d = \mathbf{A}^T(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^T)^{-1} \mathbf{u}_d$$ where $\mathbf{u}_d = [T_{\text{total,des}}, \tau_{\phi,\text{des}}, \tau_{\theta,\text{des}}, \tau_{\psi,\text{des}}]^T$. This gives the **optimal motor thrust commands** that achieve the desired motion with minimum energy. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Translational Dynamics in Inertial Frame ### Step 1 — Position and Orientation State Let $\mathbf{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ in $\{W\}$ and use Euler angles $\boldsymbol{\eta} = [\phi, \theta, \psi]^T$. ### Step 2 — Rotation Matrix (ZYX Euler) The body-to-world rotation matrix is: $$ R = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix} $$ where $c = \cos$, $s = \sin$. ### Step 3 — Translational Equation of Motion The thrust acts along the body $z_B$ axis, expressed in world frame as $R \, [0, 0, T_{\text{total}}]^T$: $$ \begin{aligned} m \ddot{\mathbf{p}} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ T_{\text{total}} \end{bmatrix}_{\text{body, rotated to world}} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -mg \end{bmatrix}_{\text{gravity}} \\ &= R \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ T_{\text{total}} \end{bmatrix} - mg \hat{e}_3 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Component Form (Z-axis) For the vertical motion, the projection gives: $$m\ddot{z} = T_{\text{total}}(\cos\phi \cos\theta) - mg$$ This is the canonical KTU equation for vertical motion. At hover ($\phi = \theta = 0$): $$T_{\text{total,hover}} = mg \quad \checkmark$$ which matches Derivation 1. ### Step 5 — Yaw Dynamics Using Euler's equation about the body $z$-axis: $$I_{zz} \ddot{\psi} = \tau_\psi$$ where the yaw torque is generated by the *imbalance of reactive torques* between CW and CCW propellers: $$\tau_\psi = b \sum_{i \in CW} \omega_i^2 - b \sum_{i \in CCW} \omega_i^2$$ with $b = C_Q \rho A R^3$ being the *drag-torque constant*. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Derivations | Derivation | Final Result | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | Hover thrust per motor | $T_{\text{per}} = mg/8$ | Each rotor carries $1/8$ the weight | | Hover angular velocity | $\omega_{\text{hover}} = \sqrt{mg/(8k)}$ | Determines ESC command | | Control allocation | $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{A}^{\dagger}\mathbf{u}_d$ | Maps desired wrench to motors | | Vertical dynamics | $m\ddot{z} = T_{\text{total}}\cos\phi\cos\theta - mg$ | Coupled translational ODE | | Yaw dynamics | $I_{zz}\ddot{\psi} = b(\sum_{CW}\omega_i^2 - \sum_{CCW}\omega_i^2)$ | CW/CCW differential | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Octocopter — Body-Frame Geometric Layout (X8 Configuration) The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional architecture of an Octocopter control system**, mapping how sensor inputs, the flight controller, and the control allocation block interact with the 8-motor airframe. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ground Station / High-Level Planner] --> B[Trajectory Generator] B --> C[Reference State xd yd zd psid] C --> D[Outer Loop Position Controller PID] D --> E[Inner Loop Attitude Controller PID] E --> F[Desired Wrench Ttotal tauphi tautheta taupsi] F --> G[Control Allocation Matrix A pseudo-inverse] G --> H1[Motor 1 ESC] G --> H2[Motor 2 ESC] G --> H3[Motor 3 ESC] G --> H4[Motor 4 ESC] G --> H5[Motor 5 ESC] G --> H6[Motor 6 ESC] G --> H7[Motor 7 ESC] G --> H8[Motor 8 ESC] H1 --> I1[Propeller 1 CW] H2 --> I2[Propeller 2 CCW] H3 --> I3[Propeller 3 CW] H4 --> I4[Propeller 4 CCW] H5 --> I5[Propeller 5 CW] H6 --> I6[Propeller 6 CCW] H7 --> I7[Propeller 7 CW] H8 --> I8[Propeller 8 CCW] I1 --> J[Octocopter Airframe 6-DOF Rigid Body] I2 --> J I3 --> J I4 --> J I5 --> J I6 --> J I7 --> J I8 --> J J --> K[IMU Gyro Accelero] J --> L[GPS Receiver] J --> M[Barometer] J --> N[Magnetometer] K --> O[Extended Kalman Filter EKF] L --> O M --> O N --> O O --> P[Estimated State xhat] P --> D ``` ## 4.2 Octocopter — Motor Numbering Convention (X8) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Airframe_Top_View direction TB M1[Motor 1
Front-Right
CW] M2[Motor 2
Right-Front
CCW] M3[Motor 3
Right-Rear
CW] M4[Motor 4
Rear-Right
CCW] M5[Motor 5
Rear-Left
CW] M6[Motor 6
Left-Rear
CCW] M7[Motor 7
Left-Front
CW] M8[Motor 8
Front-Left
CCW] end M1 --- M2 --- M3 --- M4 --- M5 --- M6 --- M7 --- M8 --- M1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** In an X8 octocopter, **CW and CCW propellers alternate** around the airframe. This alternating pattern is *mandatory* — it ensures that the net reactive yaw torque at hover is zero. ## 4.3 Coaxial Octocopter — Cross-Section (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Upper Propeller CCW] --> B[Upper Brushless Motor] B --> C[Central Shaft] C --> D[Lower Brushless Motor] D --> E[Lower Propeller CW] E --> F[Boom Arm] F --> G[Central Hub CoG] G --> H[Flight Controller] H --> I[Battery Pack] I --> J[Payload Mount] ``` ## 4.4 Control Allocation Dataflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Desired Wrench ud] --> B[Allocation Matrix A 4x8] B --> C[Individual Thrusts T1 to T8] C --> D[Motor Dynamics omega_i_squared proportional to T_i] D --> E[Propeller Aerodynamics] E --> F[Net Force and Torque on Airframe] F --> G[Rigid Body Dynamics 6-DOF] G --> H[Sensor Measurements] H --> I[State Estimator EKF] I --> J[Feedback to Outer Loop] J --> A ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define an Octocopter. List any two advantages of an octocopter over a quadcopter.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Octocopter** is a multi-rotor unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) that uses **eight (8) fixed-pitch rotors** to generate lift and achieve 6-DOF motion control. It belongs to the class of *Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL)* robotic systems. **Advantages over a quadcopter** (any two, 1.5 marks each): 1. **Fault tolerance / redundancy:** If one motor fails in flight, the octocopter can still maintain controlled flight and land safely, whereas a quadcopter would crash. 2. **Higher payload capacity:** Total available thrust is approximately *twice* that of an equivalent quadcopter, allowing heavier payloads (e.g., LiDAR, manipulators, cargo). 3. **Lower per-motor loading:** Each motor produces $mg/8$ instead of $mg/4$, leading to greater motor efficiency, lower operating temperatures, and longer flight endurance. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the role of the *control allocation matrix* in an octocopter? Why is the pseudo-inverse used?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **control allocation matrix** $\mathbf{A}$ is a $4 \times 8$ matrix that maps the *desired wrench* (total thrust + 3 torques) into the *individual thrust commands* of the 8 motors. It is the mathematical heart of the *mixer*. The **pseudo-inverse** $\mathbf{A}^{\dagger} = \mathbf{A}^T(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^T)^{-1}$ is used because the system has **more actuators (8) than controlled variables (4)** — it is *underdetermined*. The pseudo-inverse yields the **minimum-norm motor thrust vector** that satisfies the desired wrench while minimizing the total motor effort (energy consumption). It is the mathematically optimal linear solution for control redundancy resolution. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Derive the expression for the *per-motor hover thrust* of an octocopter. Show all steps. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An octocopter has a total mass of $5.6\,\text{kg}$. Compute (i) the per-motor thrust at hover, (ii) the total upward thrust, and (iii) the required per-motor angular velocity if the motor-thrust constant is $k = 6.27 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{N·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$. Take $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A ### Part (a) — Derivation of Per-Motor Hover Thrust (7 marks) **Step 1 — Newton's second law in the vertical direction (1 mark):** At steady hover, vertical acceleration is zero: $$m \ddot{z} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Force balance equation (2 marks):** The forces acting on the octocopter in the vertical (inertial $z$) direction are: - Total upward thrust $T_{\text{total}}$ - Weight $mg$ acting downward $$T_{\text{total}} - mg = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_{\text{total}} = mg$$ **Step 3 — Expressing total thrust as a sum over the 8 rotors (1 mark):** $$T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} T_i = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5 + T_6 + T_7 + T_8$$ **Step 4 — Symmetry assumption (1 mark):** For a symmetric airframe with CoG at the geometric center, all 8 rotors produce equal thrust in hover: $$T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_4 = T_5 = T_6 = T_7 = T_8 = T_{\text{per}}$$ **Step 5 — Final result (2 marks):** $$8 \, T_{\text{per}} = mg \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{T_{\text{per}} = \frac{mg}{8}}$$ ### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks) **Given:** $m = 5.6\,\text{kg}$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, $k = 6.27 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{N·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$, $n = 8$. **(i) Per-motor thrust (2 marks):** $$ T_{\text{per}} = \frac{mg}{8} = \frac{5.6 \times 9.81}{8} = \frac{54.936}{8} = 6.867\,\text{N} $$ **(ii) Total upward thrust (2 marks):** $$ T_{\text{total}} = mg = 5.6 \times 9.81 = 54.936\,\text{N} \approx 54.94\,\text{N} $$ (Verification: $T_{\text{total}} = 8 \times 6.867 = 54.936\,\text{N} \;\checkmark$) **(iii) Required per-motor angular velocity (3 marks):** Using $T_i = k \, \omega_i^2$, solve for $\omega_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega_i &= \sqrt{\frac{T_{\text{per}}}{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.867}{6.27 \times 10^{-4}}} \\ &= \sqrt{10952.95} \\ &\approx 104.66\,\text{rad/s} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to RPM: $$\omega_i = 104.66 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 1000\,\text{RPM}$$ **Final Answer:** Each motor must spin at approximately **104.66 rad/s (~1000 RPM)** producing **6.867 N** of thrust. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the *functional architecture* of an octocopter control system. Label the major blocks: trajectory generator, position controller, attitude controller, control allocation, and motor ESCs. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the *control allocation equation* for an octocopter. Explain why the pseudo-inverse is used and what minimum-norm property it provides. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B ### Part (a) — Functional Block Diagram (7 marks) The complete control architecture is shown in the diagram (see Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid block). The **major blocks** are: 1. **Trajectory Generator (1 mark):** Generates reference position $\{x_d, y_d, z_d\}$ and yaw $\psi_d$ from a high-level planner. 2. **Position (Outer-Loop) Controller — typically PID (2 marks):** Computes the *desired attitude* $\{\phi_d, \theta_d\}$ and *thrust* $T_{\text{total,des}}$ that drive the position error to zero. 3. **Attitude (Inner-Loop) Controller — typically PID (2 marks):** Tracks the desired attitude by computing the *body torques* $\{\tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi\}$. 4. **Control Allocation (Mixer) (1 mark):** Maps the desired wrench into 8 individual motor thrust commands. 5. **ESCs and Motors (1 mark):** Each Electronic Speed Controller drives its brushless motor at the commanded angular velocity, producing the required thrust via the propeller. The feedback loop is closed by the **EKF-based state estimator**, which fuses IMU, GPS, barometer, and magnetometer data to produce $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$. ### Part (b) — Derivation of the Control Allocation Equation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Define the desired wrench (1 mark):** The high-level controller commands a *4-dimensional wrench vector*: $$\mathbf{u}_d = \begin{bmatrix} T_{\text{total,des}} \\ \tau_{\phi,\text{des}} \\ \tau_{\theta,\text{des}} \\ \tau_{\psi,\text{des}} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Define the motor thrust vector (1 mark):** $$\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} T_1 & T_2 & T_3 & T_4 & T_5 & T_6 & T_7 & T_8 \end{bmatrix}^T$$ **Step 3 — Relate wrench to thrusts via allocation matrix (2 marks):** $$\mathbf{u}_d = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{T}$$ where $\mathbf{A}$ is a $4 \times 8$ matrix whose rows encode the geometric and aerodynamic contributions of each motor to the total thrust, roll torque, pitch torque, and yaw torque. **Step 4 — Pseudo-inverse solution (2 marks):** Since $\mathbf{A}$ is rectangular ($4 \times 8$, with rank 4), we use the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse: $$\boxed{\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{A}^{\dagger} \mathbf{u}_d = \mathbf{A}^T (\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^T)^{-1} \mathbf{u}_d}$$ **Step 5 — Justification of the minimum-norm property (1 mark):** The pseudo-inverse yields the unique $\mathbf{T}$ that (a) satisfies $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{u}_d$ *and* (b) has the **minimum Euclidean norm** $\Vert \mathbf{T} \Vert_2$. Physically, this means the **least total motor effort** is used, which directly minimizes **energy consumption** and **heat dissipation** — critical for battery-powered UAVs. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting units:** Many students write $\omega$ in rad/s but then state the numerical answer without converting. Always include the unit and, when relevant, the **RPM equivalent**. > 2. **Confusing $C_T$ and $C_Q$:** $C_T$ (thrust coefficient) governs *force*; $C_Q$ (torque coefficient) governs *reactive yaw torque*. Mixing them up will cost 2 marks. > 3. **Skipping the symmetry assumption:** A derivation of $T_{\text{per}} = mg/8$ without explicitly stating "by symmetry, all 8 rotors contribute equally" loses 1 mark. > 4. **Sign convention errors in roll/pitch:** The sign of $\tau_\phi$ and $\tau_\theta$ depends on the chosen motor-numbering convention. Always draw the airframe and label motor positions. > 5. **Pseudo-inverse without explanation:** Writing $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ when $\mathbf{A}$ is non-square loses 1–2 marks; always specify *Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse* and explain *why*. > 6. **Ignoring the gimbal lock:** In deriving the rotation matrix, students often forget that the ZYX Euler parametrization is singular at $\theta = \pm 90°$. The octocopter pitch should never reach this in normal flight, but a good answer notes this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Octocopter (Module 1)** - **Definition:** Octocopter = 8-rotor VTOL UAV, fully-actuated in translational hover, 6-DOF rigid body. - **Degrees of Freedom:** 6 (translation in $x, y, z$; rotation in $\phi, \theta, \psi$). - **Hover condition:** $T_{\text{total}} = mg$, i.e., $\sum_{i=1}^{8} T_i = mg$. - **Per-motor hover thrust:** $T_{\text{per}} = mg/8$ (symmetric loading). - **Per-motor hover angular velocity:** $\omega_{\text{hover}} = \sqrt{mg/(8k)}$. - **Thrust formula:** $T_i = k \, \omega_i^2$ where $k = C_T \rho A R^2$. - **Yaw torque:** Generated by *differential* reactive torque between CW and CCW propellers: $\tau_\psi = b(\sum_{CW} \omega_i^2 - \sum_{CCW} \omega_i^2)$. - **Vertical dynamics:** $m\ddot{z} = T_{\text{total}} \cos\phi \cos\theta - mg$. - **Rotational dynamics:** $\mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ (Euler's equation). - **Control allocation:** $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{A}^{\dagger} \mathbf{u}_d$ via Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse. - **Why 8 rotors?** Redundancy (fault tolerance) + payload + efficiency. - **Configurations:** Flat-X8 / Flat-+8 / Coaxial Octocopter. - **Alternating CW/CCW propellers:** Mandatory for zero net yaw torque in hover. - **Standard constants:** $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$. - **Practical RPM range at hover:** 1000–7000 RPM depending on propeller size and motor KV. - **Frame convention:** ENU (indoor) or NED (outdoor); KTU 2024 default NED for outdoor. - **Sensors used:** IMU, GPS, barometer, magnetometer, optional LiDAR/optical flow. - **Estimation:** EKF (Extended Kalman Filter) for state fusion. - **Controllers:** PID (classical), LQR/MPC (advanced), INDI (Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion). - **Disk loading:** $DL = mg/(8\pi R^2)$ — a key design parameter. - **Figure of Merit:** $\text{FM} = P_{\text{ideal}}/P_{\text{actual}} \leq 1$ — measures hover efficiency.

topics.title: Mobile Robot Kinematics: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Kinematics: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mobile Robot Kinematics** is the branch of robotics that studies the **mathematical description of a mobile robot's motion** in space, *without considering the forces and torques* that cause that motion. It deals purely with the **geometric and time-based relationships** between the robot's actuator inputs (e.g., wheel velocities) and the resulting motion of its body in the environment.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, mobile robot kinematics is formally defined as the study of:
> The mapping between the **control inputs** of a mobile robot (wheel speeds, steering angles) and the **resulting pose (position + orientation)** of the robot in its **configuration space**, subject to the **kinematic constraints** imposed by the wheel-ground contact and the robot's mechanical structure.

The fundamental problem addressed in Module 1 can be summarized as:

$$\boxed{\text{Inputs (wheel speeds)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Kinematic Model}}\; \text{Robot Pose } (x, y, \theta)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Remote-Controlled (RC) Toy Car on a Sheet of Paper**
> 
> Imagine you are driving an RC car on a large sheet of graph paper. The car has two rear wheels (left and right) that can spin at different speeds. **Kinematics** is like a *mathematical mapmaker* that answers two questions:
> 
> 1. **Forward Kinematics Problem:** *If I spin the left wheel at 2 cm/s and the right wheel at 2 cm/s, where will the car be after 5 seconds, and which direction will it face?* (Inputs → Output pose)
> 
> 2. **Inverse Kinematics Problem:** *If I want the car to reach point (10, 5) facing East, how fast should each wheel spin?* (Desired pose → Required inputs)
> 
> **Notice** that kinematics does NOT ask *why* the wheels spin (that's motor dynamics) or *what is the battery voltage required* (that's electrical engineering). It purely focuses on the *geometric consequence* of motion.

### 1.3 The Two Branches: Kinematics vs. Dynamics

A mobile robot is a **mechanical system with mass and inertia**. The complete study of its motion is divided into two complementary branches:

| Branch | Question Answered | Considers Forces/Torques? | Typical Equations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kinematics** | *How does the robot move?* | ❌ **No** | Geometric / algebraic (e.g., $v = r\omega$) |
| **Dynamics** | *Why does the robot move?* | ✅ **Yes** | Differential equations (Newton-Euler, Lagrange) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 1 *strictly focuses on kinematics*. Dynamics (forces, torques, mass, friction modeling) belongs to higher modules. Many students lose marks by mixing up kinematic equations with dynamic ones in derivations.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Introduced in Module 1

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU Glossary — Master These Definitions**
> 
> - **Mobile Robot:** An automatic machine that is capable of locomotion (movement) in its environment, equipped with actuators, sensors, and a controller. Examples: AGVs, AMRs, Mars rovers, autonomous vacuum cleaners.
> - **Pose:** The complete state of a robot in 2D space, comprising its **position** $(x, y)$ *and* its **orientation** $\theta$. Represented as a vector $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$.
> - **Configuration Space (C-space):** The abstract mathematical space of all possible configurations (poses) a robot can attain. For a 2D mobile robot, $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (position is real plane, orientation is a circle).
> - **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent variables (coordinates) required to completely specify the robot's configuration in its environment. A free-flying rigid body in 2D has **3 DOF** $(x, y, \theta)$.
> - **Holonomic Robot:** A robot whose *controllable degrees of freedom* equal its *total degrees of freedom*. It can move in *any* instantaneous direction in C-space. Example: mecanum-wheeled robot, omnidirectional robot.
> - **Non-Holonomic Robot:** A robot whose *controllable degrees of freedom* are **less than** its total degrees of freedom. It cannot move instantaneously in *every* direction (e.g., a car cannot move sideways). It is governed by *non-integrable* constraints.
> - **Kinematic Constraint:** A constraint on the robot's velocity (not its position) that limits its motion. Example: a car's wheels cannot slip sideways → the lateral velocity must be zero.
> - **Maneuverability:** The number of degrees of freedom a robot can instantaneously control in its C-space.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Robot's Pose in 2D: Mathematical Foundation

To describe where a mobile robot is and where it is heading, we need a **2D coordinate system**. By KTU 2024 convention, we use a **global (world) reference frame** $\{X_I, Y_I\}$ and a **local (robot) reference frame** $\{X_R, Y_R\}$ fixed to the robot body.

**The robot's pose** is the position of the robot's *local frame origin* in the world frame, plus the *rotation angle* of the robot's local frame relative to the world frame:

$$\boxed{\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Position along } X_I \\ \text{Position along } Y_I \\ \text{Orientation w.r.t. } X_I \end{bmatrix}}$$

Here:
- $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ are the planar coordinates (in **meters**).
- $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ is the heading angle (in **radians**).

### 2.2 The Three Kinematic Problems

A KTU 2024 student must master the distinction between the three fundamental kinematic problems:

**Problem 1 — Forward Kinematics (Direct Kinematics):**
> Given the actuator inputs (wheel speeds, steering angles) over time, find the resulting pose.

**Problem 2 — Inverse Kinematics:**
> Given a desired pose (or pose trajectory), find the actuator inputs required.

**Problem 3 — Localization (Estimation / Sensing):**
> Given sensor measurements and control inputs, *estimate* the current pose. This is essentially *forward kinematics with uncertainty*.

### 2.3 The Two Coordinate Frames

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Frames?**
> The *world frame* is what the **map uses** (fixed, stationary). The *robot frame* is what the **robot "sees"** (sensors mounted on the robot measure in this frame). Converting between them is the heart of mobile robotics.

| Frame | Notation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Inertial (World) Frame** | $\{X_I, Y_I\}$ | Global positioning, GPS, map coordinates |
| **Robot (Body) Frame** | $\{X_R, Y_R\}$ | Onboard sensors, wheel encoders, IMU |
| **Sensor Frame** | $\{X_S, Y_S\}$ | Lidar, camera specific frame (often = Robot frame) |

### 2.4 The Kinematic Constraint Equation (Most Important)

For a non-holonomic mobile robot, the fundamental constraint is that the robot **cannot move sideways** (pure rolling without slipping assumption). This is encoded as:

$$\boxed{\dot{x} \sin\theta - \dot{y} \cos\theta = 0}$$

This is the **non-holonomic constraint equation**. It says: the component of robot velocity perpendicular to its heading is *always zero*. The robot can only move *forward* (or *backward*) along its heading direction.

### 2.5 The Forward Kinematic Equation (Generic Mobile Robot)

For a generic mobile robot moving with linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$:

$$\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **unified kinematic model** for ANY 2D mobile robot. The difference between robot types (differential, Ackermann, omnidirectional) lies in the **expression of $v$ and $\omega$ in terms of wheel inputs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This single equation is the foundation for *every* autonomous vehicle — from self-driving Teslas to warehouse AMRs to NASA's Mars rovers. The entire discipline of **trajectory planning** builds on top of this equation.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — KTU 2024 Mobile Robot Kinematics Formula Cheat Sheet**

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | Robot pose (configuration) | $x, y$ in m; $\theta$ in rad |
| 2 | $\xi$ | Alternate notation for pose vector | Same as above |
| 3 | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Non-holonomic constraint | Velocity in m/s |
| 4 | $\dot{\xi} = [v\cos\theta,\; v\sin\theta,\; \omega]^T$ | Generic 2D forward kinematics | $v$ m/s, $\omega$ rad/s |
| 5 | $v = r\omega_{wheel}$ | Wheel rolling relation | $r$ in m, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 6 | $\text{DOF} = 3$ | Free rigid body in 2D plane | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | 2D rotation matrix (world→robot) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Differential drive linear velocity | m/s |
| 9 | $\omega = \dfrac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Differential drive angular velocity | rad/s |
| 10 | $L$ | Wheelbase / distance between wheels | m |

> **Constants & Symbols Used Throughout:** $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = axle length, $\omega_L, \omega_R$ = left/right wheel angular speeds, $v$ = linear velocity, $\omega$ = angular velocity (yaw rate).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Non-Holonomic Constraint Equation

**Statement:** *For a wheel rolling without slipping, the velocity of the wheel's contact point with the ground is zero.*

**Setup:** Consider a unicycle (single wheel) robot at position $(x, y)$ with orientation $\theta$ measured counter-clockwise from the $X_I$ axis.

**Step 1 — Identify the heading direction vector.**
The robot's heading direction (forward unit vector) in the world frame is:

$$\hat{u}_f = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the lateral direction vector.**
The robot's lateral (sideways) unit vector, perpendicular to heading, is:

$$\hat{u}_l = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Express the robot's velocity in the world frame.**
The robot's instantaneous velocity vector is:

$$\vec{V} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the rolling-without-slipping constraint.**
For a non-holonomic wheel, the lateral velocity (the component along $\hat{u}_l$) must be **zero**. We compute the dot product of the velocity vector with the lateral unit vector:

$$\vec{V} \cdot \hat{u}_l = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand the dot product.**

$$\dot{x}(-\sin\theta) + \dot{y}(\cos\theta) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to standard form.**

$$\boxed{-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0}$$

This is the **non-holonomic constraint** for a 2D mobile robot. It is non-holonomic because it is a constraint on **velocity** that *cannot* be integrated into a position-only constraint. (If it could, the robot would be holonomic.)

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Forward Kinematics of a Differential-Drive Robot

**Setup:** Consider a differential-drive mobile robot with:
- Two independently driven wheels (left and right) on a common axle.
- Wheel radius: $r$ (meters).
- Axle length (distance between wheels): $L$ (meters).
- Left and right wheel angular velocities: $\omega_L, \omega_R$ (rad/s).
- Robot pose: $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$.

**Step 1 — Compute the linear velocity of each wheel's contact point with the ground.**
Using the basic rolling relation $v = r\omega$ for each wheel:

$$v_L = r\omega_L \quad \text{(left wheel's contact velocity)}$$

$$v_R = r\omega_R \quad \text{(right wheel's contact velocity)}$$

**Step 2 — Relate $v_L$ and $v_R$ to the robot's linear and angular velocities.**
For a rigid axle, the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the axle line. The robot's linear velocity $v$ is the *average* of the two wheel velocities (this is a kinematic identity for a differential drive):

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

The robot's angular velocity $\omega$ is the *difference* of the wheel velocities divided by the axle length (since the two wheels trace circles of different radii about the ICR):

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the generic forward kinematic equation.**
The generic 2D kinematic model states:

$$\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $v$ and $\omega$ with their wheel-input expressions.**

$$\boxed{\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\cos\theta \\[8pt] \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\sin\theta \\[8pt] \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} \end{bmatrix}}$$

This is the **complete forward kinematic model** of a differential-drive mobile robot in KTU 2024 syllabus terms.

**Step 5 — Sanity Checks (Important for KTU Exam):**
- If $\omega_R = \omega_L = \omega_0$ (both wheels spin equally): $v = r\omega_0$, $\omega = 0$. The robot moves *straight forward* with no rotation. ✓
- If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ (wheels spin in opposite directions): $v = 0$, $\omega = \dfrac{2r\omega_R}{L}$. The robot *rotates in place* (zero-radius turn). ✓
- If $\omega_L = 0$ (only right wheel turns): robot rotates *counter-clockwise* about the left wheel. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The 2D Rotation Matrix

**Statement:** The world frame $\{X_I, Y_I\}$ and the robot frame $\{X_R, Y_R\}$ are related by a planar rotation of angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Express a point in the robot frame as a vector.**
Let $\vec{p}^R = [p_x^R, \; p_y^R]^T$ be the coordinates of point $P$ in the robot frame.

**Step 2 — Apply the rotation by angle $\theta$ to convert to world frame.**
A counter-clockwise rotation by $\theta$ in 2D is given by:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Write the transformation equation.**

$$\boxed{\vec{p}^I = R(\theta)\;\vec{p}^R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_x^R \\ p_y^R \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify properties (for KTU viva).**
- $R(0) = I$ (identity) ✓
- $R(\theta)R(-\theta) = I$ (orthogonality) ✓
- $\det R(\theta) = 1$ (proper rotation, no reflection) ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Robot Types and Their Constraints

| Robot Type | DOF (C-space) | Maneuverability | Holonomic? | Constraint Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unicycle / Diff-Drive** | 3 | 2 | ❌ No (non-holonomic) | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ |
| **Car (Ackermann)** | 3 | 2 | ❌ No (non-holonomic) | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ + steering geometry |
| **Omnidirectional (Mecanum)** | 3 | 3 | ✅ Yes (holonomic) | None on velocity |
| **Synchronous Drive** | 3 | 2 or 3 | Depends | Variable |
| **Tracked (Tank)** | 3 | 2 | ❌ No (non-holonomic) | Similar to diff-drive |
| **Quadruped (Legged)** | 3 + leg joints | 3 (body) | ✅ Often holonomic (if designed) | Discretized by foot contacts |
