## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define mobile robot kinematics. Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic mobile robots with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Mobile Robot Kinematics:** It is the study of the geometric and time-based relationships between a mobile robot's control inputs (such as wheel speeds or steering angles) and the resulting motion (position and orientation) of the robot, *without* considering the forces or torques that cause the motion.

**Holonomic Robot:** A robot whose *controllable degrees of freedom* are equal to its *total degrees of freedom*. It can move instantaneously in *any* direction in its configuration space.
- **Example:** A mecanum-wheeled omnidirectional robot (3 DOF total, 3 controllable — can move sideways, forward, and rotate simultaneously).

**Non-Holonomic Robot:** A robot whose *controllable degrees of freedom* are *less than* its *total degrees of freedom*. It has constraints on its velocity that prevent instantaneous motion in certain directions.
- **Example:** A differential-drive robot or a car (3 DOF total, but only 2 controllable at any instant — cannot move sideways).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the non-holonomic constraint equation for a unicycle-type mobile robot and explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer:**

The non-holonomic constraint equation for a 2D unicycle mobile robot is:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Physical Significance:** This equation expresses the *pure rolling without slipping* condition of the robot's wheel. It states that the component of the robot's instantaneous velocity **perpendicular to its heading direction** must always be **zero**. In simpler words, the robot *cannot move sideways* — it can only move along its forward direction (or backward).

This is a **velocity-level constraint** (involves $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$) that cannot be integrated into a position-level constraint (involving only $x, y$). This non-integrability is what makes the constraint *non-holonomic*. The consequence is that while the robot cannot move sideways *instantaneously*, it can reach any position in the plane through a combination of forward motions and rotations — a property known as **controllability**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the forward kinematic equations of a differential-drive mobile robot from first principles. Clearly state all assumptions and define all symbols used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and axle length $L = 0.30$ m. The left wheel rotates at $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s and the right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s (both counter-clockwise). Compute the robot's linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, and the radius of curvature $R_c$ of its path. Also, state in which direction the robot turns.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The robot moves on a flat, horizontal 2D plane.
2. Both wheels roll without slipping (no sliding).
3. The wheel-ground contact is a single point.
4. The robot body is a rigid body.
5. The wheels are non-deformable.

**Symbols:**
- $r$: radius of each wheel (m)
- $L$: distance between the two wheels / axle length (m)
- $\omega_L, \omega_R$: angular velocities of left and right wheels (rad/s)
- $v_L, v_R$: linear velocities of left and right wheel contact points (m/s)
- $v$: linear velocity of the robot (m/s)
- $\omega$: angular velocity (yaw rate) of the robot (rad/s)
- $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$: robot pose in the world frame

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Using the pure rolling relation $v = r\omega$, the contact-point velocities are:
$v_L = r\omega_L$ and $v_R = r\omega_R$.

**Step 2:** For a rigid body (the robot), the average of the two wheel contact velocities equals the linear velocity of the robot's center:
$v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$.

**Step 3:** The angular velocity of the robot equals the difference of the wheel velocities divided by the axle length (since the wheels trace different arc radii about the Instantaneous Center of Rotation):
$\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$.

**Step 4:** Substituting into the generic kinematic model:

$$\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\cos\theta \\[4pt] \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\sin\theta \\[4pt] \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **forward kinematic model** of a differential-drive mobile robot. **(7 marks awarded)** ✓

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s.

**Step 1: Compute the linear velocity $v$.**

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (6 + 4)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 10}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the angular velocity $\omega$.**

$$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times (6 - 4)}{0.30} = \frac{0.05 \times 2}{0.30} = \frac{0.1}{0.30} = 0.3333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the radius of curvature $R_c$.**
The radius of curvature is given by $R_c = \dfrac{v}{\omega}$:

$$R_c = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.25}{0.3333} = 0.75 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Determine the direction of turning.**
Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$ (right wheel is faster), the right side of the robot moves *faster* than the left side. Hence the robot turns **counter-clockwise** (i.e., to the **left**) when viewed from above.

**Final Answer:** $v = 0.25$ m/s, $\omega = 0.333$ rad/s (≈ 19.1°/s), $R_c = 0.75$ m, turning direction = left (counter-clockwise). **(7 marks awarded)** ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the terms *degree of freedom*, *maneuverability*, and *configuration space* for a mobile robot. For a differential-drive robot and a mecanum-wheeled robot, state the DOF, maneuverability, and classify each as holonomic or non-holonomic.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a clear diagram, derive the non-holonomic constraint equation for a unicycle-type mobile robot. Explain why the constraint is called "non-holonomic."

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent coordinates required to *completely specify* the configuration of a mobile robot in its environment. For a free-flying rigid body in 2D, DOF = 3 (two translations $x, y$ and one rotation $\theta$).

**Maneuverability:** The number of degrees of freedom a robot can *instantaneously control* (i.e., the number of independent velocity inputs). It is the dimension of the space of feasible instantaneous velocities.

**Configuration Space (C-space):** The abstract mathematical space whose coordinates are the independent parameters defining the robot's configuration. For a 2D mobile robot, $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (a 3D manifold).

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Differential-Drive Robot | Mecanum-Wheeled Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Total DOF | 3 | 3 |
| Instantaneous Maneuverability | 2 (forward $v$ + rotation $\omega$) | 3 (forward + sideways + rotation) |
| Classification | **Non-Holonomic** (DOF > maneuverability) | **Holonomic** (DOF = maneuverability) |
| Can move sideways? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

**(7 marks awarded)** ✓

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Diagram Description:** [Draw a top-down view of a circular unicycle wheel at point $(x, y)$ with orientation $\theta$ measured from the $X_I$ axis. Show the heading unit vector $\hat{u}_f = [\cos\theta, \sin\theta]^T$ pointing along the wheel direction, and the lateral unit vector $\hat{u}_l = [-\sin\theta, \cos\theta]^T$ perpendicular to it.]

**Derivation (Summary):**

The non-holonomic constraint arises from the **pure rolling without slipping** assumption on the wheel. The robot's velocity in the world frame is $\vec{V} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}]^T$. The heading direction is $\hat{u}_f = [\cos\theta, \sin\theta]^T$ and the lateral direction is $\hat{u}_l = [-\sin\theta, \cos\theta]^T$.

The rolling-without-slipping condition requires that the *lateral component* of the velocity be zero:

$$\vec{V} \cdot \hat{u}_l = -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0}$$

**Why is it called "non-holonomic"?**
A holonomic constraint is one that can be expressed as an equation in *position variables only* (e.g., $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$). A *non-holonomic* constraint is one that involves **velocity variables** ($\dot{x}, \dot{y}$) and **cannot be integrated** into a position-only equation.

The constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ involves velocities, and there is no equivalent position-only equation of the form $g(x, y, \theta) = 0$ that can be derived from it. If such an equation existed, it would *restrict* the positions the robot can reach. But a non-holonomic robot can reach *any* position in the plane (it is *controllable*), so no such position constraint exists — confirming the constraint is non-holonomic.

**(7 marks awarded)** ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 1**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Kinematics with Dynamics:** Writing Newton's second law ($F = ma$) in kinematic problems. Module 1 is **purely kinematic** — *no forces, no masses, no torques.* Lose 1–2 marks immediately.
> 
> 2. **Sign Error in $\omega$:** Students forget that the angular velocity is $(\omega_R - \omega_L)$, not $(\omega_L - \omega_R)$. Wrong sign = wrong turning direction = lose 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ terms:** Writing $\dot{x} = v$ and $\dot{y} = v$ (treating motion as aligned with world axes) instead of $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ and $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$. This is the *most common* error in KTU 2024 papers. Lose 2–3 marks.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up Maneuverability and DOF:** Saying a car has 2 DOF (it has **3 DOF**; only 2 are *controllable*). Use the *exact* KTU terminology.
> 
> 5. **Calling every wheeled robot "non-holonomic":** Mecanum and synchronous-drive robots can be *holonomic*. Read the question carefully.
> 
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** In derivations, KTU examiners *always* check for stated assumptions (rolling without slipping, rigid body, flat ground). Omitting them costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Mobile Robot Kinematics: Introduction)**
> 
> ✅ **Core Definition:** Kinematics = motion description *without* forces. Dynamics = motion *with* forces.
> 
> ✅ **Robot Pose:** $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ (position + orientation in 2D).
> 
> ✅ **Configuration Space:** $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ for 2D mobile robots.
> 
> ✅ **DOF vs. Maneuverability:** DOF = total configuration variables; Maneuverability = instantaneously controllable velocities.
> 
> ✅ **Holonomic:** DOF = Maneuverability (e.g., mecanum wheel).
> 
> ✅ **Non-Holonomic:** DOF > Maneuverability (e.g., car, differential drive, unicycle).
> 
> ✅ **Non-Holonomic Constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ (lateral velocity = 0).
> 
> ✅ **Generic Forward Kinematic Model:** $\dot{q} = [v\cos\theta, \; v\sin\theta, \; \omega]^T$.
> 
> ✅ **Differential-Drive Kinematics:** $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$, $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$.
> 
> ✅ **Radius of Curvature:** $R_c = \dfrac{v}{\omega}$ (only valid when $\omega \neq 0$).
> 
> ✅ **Rotation Matrix (2D):** $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$.
> 
> ✅ **Special Cases to Memorize:** $\omega_R = \omega_L \Rightarrow$ straight motion; $\omega_R = -\omega_L \Rightarrow$ pure rotation in place.
> 
> ✅ **Key Constants:** $r$ (m) = wheel radius, $L$ (m) = axle length, $v$ (m/s), $\omega$ (rad/s).
> 
> ✅ **Exam-Ready Statement:** Always begin derivations by *stating assumptions* and *defining symbols* (KTU valuation expects this on every derivational question).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Models and Constraints - Representing Robot Position
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Models and Constraints - Representing Robot Position

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Mobile Robotics**, the **Kinematic Model** of a robot is a mathematical description of the robot's *motion* in space, expressed purely in terms of **geometric relationships** between its actuator inputs (wheel velocities, steering angles) and the resulting **pose** (position + orientation) of its body frame — **without** considering the forces or torques that cause the motion.

A **constraint** is a *kinematic restriction* on the robot's velocities imposed by the mechanical design of the wheels and the no-slip rolling condition at the wheel–ground contact patch.

The **robot's position** (more rigorously, its **pose**) in a 2D world is represented as a vector:

$$
\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
- $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ → coordinates of the robot's reference point in the **global / inertial frame** $\{W\}$
- $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ → **heading angle** (yaw) of the robot's body frame $\{R\}$ measured counter-clockwise from the global X-axis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology — KTU Board Pattern**
> - **Pose** = Position + Orientation (a single state vector)
> - **Configuration (q)** = Minimum set of independent parameters that fully specify the robot's state (for a unicycle, $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$)
> - **Configuration Space (C-space)** = The manifold of all possible configurations
> - **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** = Number of independent coordinates needed to describe the configuration
> - **Holonomic Constraint** = A constraint expressible as $f(q, t) = 0$ (reduces reachable C-space)
> - **Non-holonomic Constraint** = A constraint on **velocities** that is *non-integrable* — it cannot be written as a pure position constraint. The reachable C-space is *not* reduced, but the *instantaneously allowed* velocities are restricted.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot Is a Car in a Snowy Field"

Imagine you are **driving a car** on a frozen lake. You can freely position the car **anywhere** on the lake (any $(x, y)$ is reachable) — the *set of reachable positions* is huge. However, **at any instant**, the car can only move **forward, backward, or turn** — it **cannot slide sideways** (you would flip!).

- The **car's position** $(x, y, \theta)$ on the lake ↔ the robot's **pose vector** $\xi$.
- The **"no sideways sliding" rule** ↔ a **non-holonomic velocity constraint**.
- The car's **steering wheel and gas pedal** ↔ the **control inputs** $(v, \omega)$.
- The frozen lake's surface ↔ the **2D planar workspace** (assumption of planar mobile robots).

> [!NOTE]
> A *unicycle-style mobile robot* (two-wheel differential drive) has **3 configuration variables** but only **2 control inputs** $(v, \omega)$. This mismatch is the *signature* of non-holonomic systems — **fewer controls than DOFs**, yet the entire workspace is still reachable (parking a car is the classic example!).

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need a Kinematic Model?

For every KTU problem on motion planning, trajectory tracking, or control, we must convert "wheel spin rates" → "robot motion in world frame." This conversion **is** the kinematic model. Without it, a controller cannot command a robot to *"go to point (5, 3)"* — it can only command *"spin left wheel at 2 rad/s."*

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love the distinction between **kinematic model** (geometry of motion) and **dynamic model** (forces/masses). For Module 1, *only kinematic models* are in scope.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Representing Position and Orientation (The Pose)

### 2.1.1 2D Pose in the Plane (SE(2))

For a planar mobile robot, the pose is a member of the **Special Euclidean group in 2D**, denoted $SE(2)$. It is a 3×3 homogeneous transformation matrix that combines a 2D rotation $R(\theta)$ and a 2D translation $(x, y)$:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R(\theta) & \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- $R(\theta) \in SO(2)$ — the 2D rotation matrix (orthogonal, det = +1)
- $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$ — the position vector

### 2.1.2 3D Pose (SE(3)) — Conceptual Bridge

For aerial or underwater robots, the pose lives in $SE(3)$ — a 4×4 matrix combining a 3×3 rotation $R \in SO(3)$ and a 3D translation. (Module 2 of your syllabus will revisit this in detail for manipulators.)

---

## 2.2 Wheel Constraints — The Heart of Mobile Robot Kinematics

A mobile robot's allowed motion is dictated by the **wheels** touching the ground. The three fundamental wheel types used in KTU problems are:

| Wheel Type | Free Rolling DOFs | Constraints Imposed | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed (Standard) Wheel** | 1 (rotation about its axle) | 2 (no side-slip, contact at one point) | Car rear wheels |
| **Steerable Wheel** | 2 (roll + steer) | 1 | Car front wheels |
| **Castor Wheel** | 2 (roll + passive swivel) | 1 | Office chair, robot support |
| **Swedish / Mecanum Wheel** | 2 (roll + passive orthogonal rollers) | 0 — **omni-directional!** | Holonomic robots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's Most-Tested Wheel Type:** The **Differential Drive** (two fixed wheels + one castor) and the **Ackermann Drive** (car-like).

---

## 2.3 The Unicycle / Differential-Drive Kinematic Model

For a robot with body-frame linear velocity $v$ (along its x-axis) and angular velocity $\omega$ (about its z-axis), the **pose update in the world frame** is:

$$
\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **forward kinematic equation** of a unicycle.

For a **differential-drive robot** with two wheels of radius $r$ separated by wheel-base $L$ (or $2b$ — distance between the two drive wheels), spinning at angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$:

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse Kinematics** of differential drive (mapping body velocity → wheel speeds):
> $$\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}, \qquad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$$

---

## 2.4 The Non-Holonomic Constraint Equation

The "no-sideways-slip" condition for a wheel aligned with the robot body is:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This is a **Pfaffian constraint** of the form $A(q)\,\dot{q} = 0$ — *velocity-level*, not position-level, hence **non-holonomic**. The robot can still reach *any* $(x, y, \theta)$ in the plane given enough time (e.g., parallel parking), but **cannot** move instantaneously in the sideways direction.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it covers 80% of Part B (14-mark) questions on Module 1.

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **2D Pose Vector** | $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | $\theta$ in radians |
| 2 | **2D Rotation Matrix** | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | $R^T = R^{-1}$, $\det R = +1$ |
| 3 | **Homogeneous Pose** | $T \in SE(2)$, 3×3 form | Combines $R$ and $\mathbf{p}$ |
| 4 | **Unicycle Forward Kinematics** | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \; \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \; \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Most-tested equation in KTU |
| 5 | **Differential Drive Forward** | $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2, \; \omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$ | $r$ [m], $\omega$ [rad/s] |
| 6 | **Differential Drive Inverse** | $\omega_R = (2v + L\omega)/(2r), \; \omega_L = (2v - L\omega)/(2r)$ | Solve for wheel commands |
| 7 | **Non-Holonomic Constraint** | $-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Equivalent: $\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = 0$ |
| 8 | **Ackermann Steering** | $\tan\delta = L/R_{turn}$ | $\delta$ = steer angle, $R_{turn}$ = turning radius |
| 9 | **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** | $(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR}) = (x - (v/\omega)\sin\theta, \; y + (v/\omega)\cos\theta)$ | Valid only for $\omega \neq 0$ |
| 10 | **Pose Update (Euler Integration)** | $\xi_{k+1} = \xi_k + \Delta t \cdot \dot{\xi}_k$ | Discrete-time approximation |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Use Ackermann kinematic models for path tracking and Stanley / Pure-Pursuit controllers.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, MiR):** Use differential-drive models for SLAM and motion planning.
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Use rocker-bogie + Ackermann — kinematic models guide wheel-slip detection and slip-compensated odometry.
- **Robot Soccer (RoboCup):** Unicycle model is the standard for omnidirectional control of holonomic receivers.
- **Humanoid Robots (ASIMO, Atlas):** The ZMP (Zero Moment Point) controller uses the kinematic pose as a *feedback state* every 1 ms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Unicycle Kinematic Model from First Principles

### Goal
Show that for a robot moving with body-frame velocity $v$ along its own x-axis, the world-frame velocities are $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ and $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$.

### Step 1 — Define the Body Frame
The robot's body frame $\{R\}$ has its x-axis aligned with the robot's forward direction. In the world frame $\{W\}$, this x-axis makes an angle $\theta$ with the world's X-axis.

The **unit forward vector** of the robot, expressed in world frame, is:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{e}}_f^{(W)} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Velocity in World Frame
The body-frame velocity vector is $\mathbf{v}^{(R)} = [v, \; 0]^T$ (no sideways velocity — this *is* the non-holonomic assumption).

To express this velocity in the world frame, we apply the rotation $R(\theta)$:

$$
\mathbf{v}^{(W)} = R(\theta)\,\mathbf{v}^{(R)} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Equate Components to Pose Derivatives
The world-frame velocity components are by definition $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Add Orientation Dynamics
The robot's heading changes only due to angular velocity $\omega$ (no constraint on this), so:

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

### Step 5 — Compact Form
$$
\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** Notice that the mapping matrix is **state-dependent** (it contains $\theta$). This makes the unicycle a **non-linear system** — and *all* of mobile robot control theory (feedback linearization, Lyapunov methods, backstepping) is built on this non-linearity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential-Drive Forward Kinematics

### Goal
Map the two wheel angular velocities $(\omega_L, \omega_R)$ to the robot's body-frame linear and angular velocities $(v, \omega)$.

### Step 1 — Wheel-Given Linear Speed
Each wheel of radius $r$, rolling without slipping at angular velocity $\omega_i$, produces a contact-point linear speed $v_i = r\omega_i$.

The robot's body linear speed is the **average** of the two wheel speeds (assuming the body center is exactly midway between the wheels):

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r\omega_R + r\omega_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Angular Speed of the Body
When wheels spin at *different* rates, the robot rotates about a point on the wheel-base line. The difference in wheel speeds $(v_R - v_L)$ divided by the wheel-base $L$ gives the angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

### Step 3 — Final Compact Form
$$
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Non-Holonomic Constraint from Wheel Geometry

### Goal
Show that a wheel aligned with the robot's body x-axis (no slip) imposes the constraint $\dot{y}_R\cos\theta - \dot{x}_R\sin\theta = 0$.

### Step 1 — Contact Point Velocity in Body Frame
The wheel contacts the ground at a point that is **rigidly attached** to the body. Its velocity in the **body frame** is $\mathbf{v}_{contact}^{(R)} = [v, \, 0]^T$ (the sideways component is *zero* by the no-slip rule).

### Step 2 — Transform to World Frame
$$
\mathbf{v}_{contact}^{(W)} = R(\theta) \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Cross-Product Orthogonality
The "no side-slip" condition means this velocity vector is **collinear** with the wheel's rolling direction. The wheel's rolling direction in the world frame is the same as the body x-axis: $[\cos\theta, \sin\theta]^T$. The vector **perpendicular** to this is $[-\sin\theta, \cos\theta]^T$.

For two vectors to be collinear, the dot product with the perpendicular must vanish:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{contact}^{(W)} \cdot [-\sin\theta, \cos\theta]^T = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Expand
$$
-v\cos\theta\sin\theta + v\sin\theta\cos\theta = 0
$$

This is trivially $0 = 0$. We need a more useful form. The condition is equivalent to saying the **sideways velocity component** (perpendicular to the wheel) is zero:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This is the **Pfaffian non-holonomic constraint**.

### Step 5 — Why It Is Non-Holonomic
Holonomic constraints reduce the *dimension* of the configuration space. Here, the constraint is on *velocities* only — we can integrate to find the equation of motion (it's a differential equation for $(x(t), y(t), \theta(t))$), but we **cannot** write it as $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$ that *removes a coordinate*. Therefore the C-space remains 3D, but the **admissible velocity directions** at each point are restricted to a 2D subspace (line + rotation). Classic example: parallel-parking — the car's pose is fully reachable, just not instantly.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheel-base $L = 0.3$ m is moving in a plane. The left wheel spins at $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s and the right wheel at $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s. At an instant when the robot's pose is $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0, 2.0, \pi/4)$, find the robot's body velocities $(v, \omega)$ and the world-frame velocity $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $v$ and $\omega$ (forward kinematics):**
$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (6 + 4)}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times (6 - 4)}{0.3} = \frac{0.1}{0.3} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the unicycle model in world frame:**
$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.25 \times \cos(\pi/4) = 0.25 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.1768 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.25 \times \sin(\pi/4) = 0.25 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.1768 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega \approx 0.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the non-holonomic constraint:**
$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0.1768 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} - 0.1768 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

The constraint holds, confirming consistency.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Robot Pose and Coordinate Frames

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeW[World Frame W]
    nodeR[Robot Body Frame R]
    nodeP[Position Vector x y]
    nodeT[Heading Angle theta]
    nodeX[World X axis]
    nodeY[World Y axis]
    nodeXr[Robot X axis]
    nodeYr[Robot Y axis]

    nodeW --- nodeX
    nodeW --- nodeY
    nodeR --- nodeXr
    nodeR --- nodeYr
    nodeR --- nodeP
    nodeR --- nodeT
    nodeW --> nodeR
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $\{W\}$ is fixed at the origin of the laboratory.
- $\{R\}$ is rigidly attached to the robot, translated by $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$ and rotated by $\theta$.
- The transformation between them is the $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix $T \in SE(2)$.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Kinematic Model Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA[Wheel Angular Velocities omega_L and omega_R]
    blockB[Forward Differential Drive Kinematics]
    outputC[Body Frame v and omega]
    blockD[Rotation by heading angle theta]
    outputE[World Frame xdot and ydot]
    blockF[Non Holonomic Constraint Check]
    outputG[Pose Update xi dot]
    outputH[Discretized Pose xi k plus 1]

    inputA --> blockB
    blockB --> outputC
    outputC --> blockD
    blockD --> outputE
    outputC --> blockF
    blockF --> outputG
    outputG --> blockH
    blockH --> blockD
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**
1. **Wheel Speeds** → physical encoder inputs.
2. **Forward Differential-Drive Kinematics** → converts encoder counts to body-frame $(v, \omega)$.
3. **Rotation by $\theta$** → projects body velocity onto the world frame.
4. **Constraint Check** → verifies the no-side-slip condition (debugging / consistency).
5. **Pose Update** → Euler or Runge-Kutta integration of $\dot{\xi}$ over $\Delta t$.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Classification of Wheel Constraints

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot[Wheel Constraint Types]
    nodeFix[Fixed Wheel - 2 constraints]
    nodeSteer[Steerable Wheel - 1 constraint]
    nodeCast[Castor Wheel - 1 constraint]
    nodeSwed[Swedish Wheel - 0 constraints]
    nodeHolo[Holonomic]
    nodeNonHolo[Non Holonomic]

    nodeRoot --> nodeFix
    nodeRoot --> nodeSteer
    nodeRoot --> nodeCast
    nodeRoot --> nodeSwed
    nodeSwed --> nodeHolo
    nodeFix --> nodeNonHolo
    nodeSteer --> nodeNonHolo
    nodeCast --> nodeNonHolo
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Pose Update Loop (Discrete Time)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeXiK[Current Pose xi at time k]
    nodeDerv[Compute xi dot using kinematic model]
    nodeEuler[Euler Integration xi k plus 1 equals xi k plus dt times xi dot]
    nodeXiK1[New Pose xi at time k plus 1]
    nodeCtrl[Controller Commands v and omega]

    nodeCtrl --> nodeDerv
    nodeXiK --> nodeDerv
    nodeDerv --> nodeEuler
    nodeEuler --> nodeXiK1
    nodeXiK1 --> nodeXiK
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a **non-holonomic constraint** for a mobile robot. Give one example equation and state why it is non-holonomic (not holonomic).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **non-holonomic constraint** is a kinematic restriction on the *velocities* of a system that **cannot be integrated** to a position-level constraint, and therefore does **not reduce the dimension of the configuration space**.

**Example:** For a unicycle/differential-drive robot,
$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$
This restricts the *instantaneous direction* of motion (no sideways slip), but the robot can still reach **any** pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in the plane by maneuvering (e.g., parallel parking a car). Because the constraint cannot be rewritten as $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$, it is non-holonomic.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the explanation of why it is non-holonomic.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between the **kinematic model** and the **dynamic model** of a mobile robot. Which is studied in Module 1 of the KTU Mobile Robotics syllabus?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kinematic Model | Dynamic Model |
|---|---|---|
| Considers | Geometry of motion only | Forces, torques, masses, inertias |
| Inputs | Velocities ($v, \omega$, wheel $\omega_i$) | Actuator voltages/torques, friction |
| Output | Pose rates ($\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$) | Accelerations + pose rates |
| Use | Path planning, low-level control, simulation | High-fidelity simulation, motor sizing |

**Module 1 of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PBRAT604) covers kinematic models only.** Dynamic modeling is part of later modules on control and actuation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **forward kinematic equations of a differential-drive mobile robot**, clearly defining all symbols. Convert the equations into **matrix form** relating the wheel angular velocities to the body's linear and angular velocities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has $r = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.25$ m, and is currently at pose $(2.0, 1.5, \pi/6)$ with wheel commands $\omega_R = 5$ rad/s and $\omega_L = 3$ rad/s. Compute the body-frame $(v, \omega)$ and the world-frame pose rates $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$. Verify the non-holonomic constraint numerically. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (a):**

We consider a robot with two drive wheels of radius $r$ separated by wheel-base $L$ (distance between wheel contact points). The wheels are aligned with the robot's body x-axis.

**Step 1 — Linear Velocity of Body Center**

Each wheel rolling without slip at $\omega_i$ produces a contact-point velocity $v_i = r\omega_i$. The body center is equidistant from both wheels, so the body's linear velocity is the **average**:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Velocity of Body**

The differential speed $(v_R - v_L)$ produces a rotation about a point on the wheel-base. The angular velocity is:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Matrix Form**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ \phantom{-}r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Symbol Definitions (1 mark):**
- $r$ — wheel radius [m]
- $L$ — distance between wheel contact patches [m]
- $\omega_L, \omega_R$ — angular velocities of left and right wheels [rad/s]
- $v$ — body-frame linear velocity along forward axis [m/s]
- $\omega$ — body-frame angular velocity about vertical axis [rad/s]

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $v$ and $\omega$ (Forward Kinematics):**
$$
v = \frac{0.04 \times (5 + 3)}{2} = \frac{0.32}{2} = 0.16 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\omega = \frac{0.04 \times (5 - 3)}{0.25} = \frac{0.08}{0.25} = 0.32 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Unicycle Model in World Frame:**
$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.16 \times \cos(\pi/6) = 0.16 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.1386 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.16 \times \sin(\pi/6) = 0.16 \times 0.5 = 0.08 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega = 0.32 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the Non-Holonomic Constraint:**
$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0.1386 \times 0.5 - 0.08 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$
$$
= 0.0693 - 0.0693 = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

The constraint is satisfied, confirming that the wheel commands are physically realizable without slip.

> **Mark Split (typical KTU):** Q(a) — 3 marks for linear vel, 3 marks for angular vel, 1 mark for matrix form. Q(b) — 2 marks each for $v$ and $\omega$, 1.5 marks each for $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$, 1 mark for $\dot{\theta}$, 1.5 marks for constraint verification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **robot's pose** in 2D. With a clear diagram, derive the **2D homogeneous transformation matrix** $T \in SE(2)$ and identify the rotation and translation components. State two properties of the rotation matrix $R(\theta) \in SO(2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point $P$ has coordinates $(1, 2)$ in the robot's body frame $\{R\}$. The robot's pose in the world frame is $\xi = [3, 4, \pi/3]^T$. Compute the coordinates of $P$ in the world frame $\{W\}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Pose**

The **pose** of a robot in a 2D plane is the combination of its *position* and *orientation* with respect to a reference (world) frame. It is compactly represented as:

$$
\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1
$$

**Step 2 — The Homogeneous Transformation Matrix**

A 2D point $\mathbf{p}^{(R)} = [p_x, p_y]^T$ in the robot frame is mapped to the world frame by a **rotation** by $\theta$ followed by a **translation** by $(x, y)$. In homogeneous coordinates:

$$
\mathbf{p}^{(W)} = T(\xi)\, \tilde{\mathbf{p}}^{(R)}
$$

where

$$
T(\xi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

and $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^{(R)} = [p_x, p_y, 1]^T$ is the **augmented** coordinate.

**Step 3 — Identification of Components**
- **Rotation block** (top-left 2×2): $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$
- **Translation block** (top-right 2×1): $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$
- **Perspective block** (bottom row): $[0 \; 0 \; 1]$ — rigid 2D case

**Step 4 — Two Properties of $R(\theta)$:**

1. **Orthogonality:** $R(\theta)\,R(\theta)^T = I$, i.e., $R(\theta)^{-1} = R(\theta)^T = R(-\theta)$.
2. **Special:** $\det R(\theta) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = +1$ (orientation-preserving, hence "Special").

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks pose def, 3 marks matrix derivation, 1 mark identification, 1 mark properties.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $P^{(R)} = [1, 2]^T$, pose $\xi = [3, 4, \pi/3]^T$.

**Step 1 — Form the Homogeneous Transformation:**

With $\theta = \pi/3$, we have $\cos(\pi/3) = 0.5$, $\sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$.

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.8660 & 3 \\ 0.8660 & 0.5 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Augment the Body Coordinates:**

$$
\tilde{P}^{(R)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$
\tilde{P}^{(W)} = T \cdot \tilde{P}^{(R)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \cdot 1 + (-0.8660) \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 1 \\ 0.8660 \cdot 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2 + 4 \cdot 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Components:**
$$
P_x^{(W)} = 0.5 - 1.7321 + 3 = 1.7679
$$
$$
P_y^{(W)} = 0.8660 + 1 + 4 = 5.8660
$$
$$
P^{(W)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.7679 \\ 5.8660 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.77 \\ 5.87 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for T matrix construction, 2 marks for augmented point, 3 marks for multiplication and final answer (1 mark for each axis arithmetic).

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 1 Kinematics**
>
> 1. **Confusing forward and inverse kinematics.** Forward = wheel speeds → body velocity. Inverse = body velocity → wheel speeds. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in $\cos\theta, \sin\theta$. Always check the units — if $\theta$ is given in degrees, your numerical answer will be wrong by orders of magnitude.
> 3. **Missing the non-holonomic constraint verification** in numerical problems. KTU examiners often assign 1–2 marks explicitly for it.
> 4. **Using $2b$ vs $L$ inconsistently.** Some textbooks use $2b$ for the wheel-base (distance between wheels) — be consistent with the notation given in the problem.
> 5. **Confusing $SE(2)$ (Special Euclidean Group, 2D)** with $SO(2)$ (Special Orthogonal Group, 2D rotations only, no translation). $SE(2)$ is a *group of rigid motions*; $SO(2)$ is a *subgroup of rotations*.
> 6. **Treating the unicycle model as linear.** The matrix $B(\xi) = [\cos\theta \; 0; \sin\theta \; 0; 0 \; 1]$ is **state-dependent** — the system is non-linear. Writing $\dot{\xi} = A\xi + Bu$ is **wrong**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Pose vector** for 2D: $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — *position* + *heading*.
- [x] **Homogeneous matrix** $T \in SE(2)$ combines rotation $R(\theta)$ and translation $\mathbf{p}$.
- [x] **Unicycle model** is the canonical KTU kinematic model: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- [x] **Differential drive forward** kinematics: $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$, $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$.
- [x] **Differential drive inverse** kinematics: $\omega_R = (2v + L\omega)/(2r)$, $\omega_L = (2v - L\omega)/(2r)$.
- [x] **Non-holonomic constraint** for a body-aligned wheel: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — velocity-level, not position-level.
- [x] **Holonomic** = integrable = reduces C-space dimension. **Non-holonomic** = non-integrable = C-space dimension unchanged, but velocities restricted.
- [x] **DOFs vs. controls:** Non-holonomic systems have **fewer controls than DOFs** but full C-space reachability (parking problem).
- [x] **Ackermann** (car-like) steering constraint: $\tan\delta = L/R_{turn}$.
- [x] **Wheel types** to remember: fixed (2 constraints), steerable (1), castor (1), Swedish/Mecanum (0 → holonomic).
- [x] **Euler integration** for pose update: $\xi_{k+1} = \xi_k + \Delta t \cdot \dot{\xi}_k$.
- [x] **Non-linearity** of mobile robots: $B(\xi)$ depends on $\theta$ — no linear state-space form $\dot{\xi} = A\xi + Bu$.
- [x] **Real-world impact:** Waymo (Ackermann), Amazon Kiva (diff-drive), Mars rovers (rocker-bogie + Ackermann), RoboCup (unicycle).
- [x] **Module 1 boundary:** Only kinematic models — *no* dynamics, *no* motor equations, *no* friction.

> **Final 30-Second Mantra:** *"Pose is (x, y, θ). Forward kinematics takes wheel speeds to (v, ω) and then to world-frame (ẋ, ẏ, θ̇). The no-sideways rule is the non-holonomic constraint. Memorize the matrix forms."* — KTU Topper's Mnemonic, Dec 2024.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
