<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward Kinematics (FK)** is the branch of robot kinematics that computes the **robot's posture (pose) in the world frame** as a function of time, given the **wheel velocities (or joint variables)** as inputs. For a mobile robot operating on a 2D plane, the output is the **configuration (posture) vector**:
>
> $$\xi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ \theta(t) \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> where $(x, y)$ is the position of a reference point on the robot (usually the center of the axle or the Instantaneous Center of Rotation) in the **inertial (world) frame** $\{X_W, Y_W\}$, and $\theta$ is the orientation angle of the robot's local frame $\{X_R, Y_R\}$ measured counter-clockwise from the world $X_W$ axis.

In other words, **forward kinematics answers the question**: *"If the left wheel rotates at $\omega_L$ rad/s and the right wheel rotates at $\omega_R$ rad/s, where will the robot be after time $t$?"*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Shopping Cart on a Rainy Day:**
> Imagine pushing a two-wheeled shopping cart in a supermarket. You have no idea of trigonometry, but you *know* intuitively that:
> - If you push both wheels at the **same speed forward**, the cart moves in a **straight line** without turning.
> - If you push the **left wheel faster** than the right, the cart **curves to the right** (smaller turn radius).
> - If you push the wheels in **opposite directions**, the cart **spins in place** about its midpoint.
>
> Forward kinematics is the **mathematical formalization** of this intuition. It maps the *control inputs* (wheel speeds) to the *resulting motion* of the cart in the store (the world frame). The **velocity** of the cart is the *output*, and the **wheel angular velocities** are the *inputs*. There is no feedback, no sensing — purely geometric prediction.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters recur throughout KTU problems:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $r$ | Wheel radius | **meters (m)** |
| $L$ / $b$ | Wheelbase / axle length (distance between wheels) | **meters (m)** |
| $d$ | Wheel diameter | **meters (m)** |
| $v$ | Linear (translational) velocity of robot | **m/s** |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity of robot (yaw rate) | **rad/s** |
| $\omega_R, \omega_L$ | Right and left wheel angular velocities | **rad/s** |
| $R_{ICR}$ | Radius of curvature / Instantaneous Center of Rotation | **meters (m)** |
| $\xi = (x, y, \theta)$ | Posture / configuration of robot | **m, m, rad** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** The world frame $\{X_W, Y_W\}$ is **right-handed**, $X_W$ points "right" (or "North"), and the heading angle $\theta$ is measured **counter-clockwise from $X_W$ to $X_R$**. Always specify the frame before solving.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Posture (Configuration) Representation

A mobile robot moving on a **flat 2D floor** has exactly **3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** in its pose:
- 2 translational: $x$ and $y$
- 1 rotational: $\theta$ (yaw)

The instantaneous motion of the robot is described by the **velocity vector** in the world frame:

$$\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only 3 DOF and not 6?** A mobile robot is constrained to move on a plane. It cannot move vertically, cannot roll, and cannot pitch. It only translates along $X_W$, $Y_W$, and rotates about the vertical axis $Z_W$. Hence, 3 DOF.

## 2.2 The Unicycle Kinematic Model (Foundation of All Models)

The simplest and most fundamental mobile robot is the **unicycle** — a single wheel on the ground plus a body. Its kinematic model is the **mother equation** of all wheeled mobile robot kinematics:

$$\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic of each step:**

1. The robot can only move **forward along its local $X_R$ axis** with speed $v$.
2. The component of $v$ along world $X_W$ is $v \cos\theta$ → contributes $\dot{x}$.
3. The component of $v$ along world $Y_W$ is $v \sin\theta$ → contributes $\dot{y}$.
4. The robot can **rotate about its center** with angular rate $\omega$ → contributes $\dot{\theta}$.

## 2.3 The Differential-Drive Model (Most Common in KTU Problems)

A differential-drive robot has **two independently driven wheels** on a common axle of length $L$ (or $b$ in some textbooks), each of radius $r$.

Given $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$, the **linear velocity of each wheel's contact point** with the ground is:

$$v_L = r \, \omega_L, \qquad v_R = r \, \omega_R$$

The **forward velocity of the robot's center** is the **average** of the two wheel speeds:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

The **angular velocity of the robot body** is the **difference** divided by the wheelbase:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

Substituting into the unicycle model:

$$\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ -\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix}$$

## 2.4 The Nonholonomic Constraint (Slip-Free Rolling)

A wheel that **rolls without slipping** enforces a **kinematic constraint**:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is the **nonholonomic rolling constraint**. It states: *the robot cannot move sideways* — its velocity vector is always aligned with its local $X_R$ axis.

## 2.5 Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

When $\omega \neq 0$, the robot follows a **circular arc**. The radius of that arc is the **ICR radius**:

$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

The ICR is located on the axle line, at a perpendicular distance $R_{ICR}$ from the robot center. For $\omega_R = \omega_L$ (straight line), $R_{ICR} \to \infty$ (no rotation center, motion is a straight line).

## 2.6 General Wheel Kinematic Models

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, **three standard wheel types** must be distinguished. Each contributes a **constraint** and a **Jacobian row** in the global system.

| Wheel Type | Sliding Allowed? | Steering Allowed? | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed (Standard) Wheel** | No (rolling only) | No | $F$ |
| **Steerable Wheel** | No (rolling only) | Yes (can rotate about vertical pin) | $S$ |
| **Caster Wheel** | Yes (can swivel + slide laterally) | Yes | $C$ |
| **Swedish (Mecanum / Omnidirectional) Wheel** | Passive lateral slip via rollers | No | $O$ |

For a **fixed standard wheel** at position $(x, y)$ from robot center with rolling radius $r$ and orientation $\alpha$ (angle of wheel plane w.r.t. robot frame):

**Rolling constraint (no slip along wheel plane):**
$$-\sin(\alpha + \beta)\,\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha + \beta)\,\dot{y} + L\cos\beta\,\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}$$

where $\beta$ is the steering angle (0 for fixed wheel) and $\dot{\varphi}$ is the wheel's spin rate.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the ENTIRE high-yield formula set for Module 1 — Forward Kinematic Models. Memorize it.**

| # | Model / Concept | Governing Equation | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Posture vector | $\xi = [x,\, y,\, \theta]^T$ | $(x, y)$ in m, $\theta$ in rad |
| 2 | Unicycle model | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Foundation of all models |
| 3 | Differential-drive linear vel. | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Average of wheel speeds |
| 4 | Differential-drive angular vel. | $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Difference over wheelbase |
| 5 | Wheel contact speed | $v_i = r\omega_i$ | For wheel $i$ |
| 6 | ICR radius | $R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$ | Straight line when $R_{ICR} \to \infty$ |
| 7 | Nonholonomic constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | No lateral slip |
| 8 | Pure rotation (in-place) | $\omega_L = -\omega_R = \omega$ | $v = 0$, $R_{ICR} = 0$ |
| 9 | Straight line | $\omega_L = \omega_R = \omega$ | $v = r\omega$, $\omega_{body} = 0$ |
| 10 | Jacobian (diff-drive) | $\dot{\xi} = J(\theta) \cdot [\omega_L,\, \omega_R]^T$ | $J$ depends on $\theta$ |
| 11 | Fixed wheel rolling constraint | $-\sin(\alpha)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha)\dot{y} + L\cos(\alpha)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}$ | For $\beta = 0$ |
| 12 | Posture update (Euler) | $\xi(t+\Delta t) = \xi(t) + \dot{\xi}\cdot\Delta t$ | First-order integration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Forward kinematic models are the *backbone* of every autonomous vehicle, AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) in warehouses, robotic vacuum cleaner (e.g., Roomba), and Mars rover. The models feed the **motion planner** and the **controller**. Without FK, the controller has no reference for *where the robot should be going*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Differential-Drive Forward Kinematics from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define the robot geometry

Consider a differential-drive robot with:
- Two wheels of **radius $r$** mounted on a common axle.
- The **axle length** (distance between wheel-ground contact points) is $L$.
- Let the **center point $C$** of the axle be the robot reference.
- World frame $\{X_W, Y_W\}$; Robot frame $\{X_R, Y_R\}$ with $X_R$ pointing forward.

### Step 2 — Express the velocity of the right wheel contact point

The right wheel's contact point with the ground has velocity $v_R = r\omega_R$ in the **forward direction** of the robot. In the **world frame**, the components are:

$$v_{R,x} = r\omega_R \cos\theta, \quad v_{R,y} = r\omega_R \sin\theta$$

### Step 3 — Express the velocity of the left wheel contact point

$$v_{L,x} = r\omega_L \cos\theta, \quad v_{L,y} = r\omega_L \sin\theta$$

### Step 4 — Compute the center velocity (average of wheel velocities)

The center of the axle moves at the average of the two wheel velocities:

$$v_x = \frac{v_{R,x} + v_{L,x}}{2} = \frac{r\cos\theta}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

$$v_y = \frac{v_{R,y} + v_{L,y}}{2} = \frac{r\sin\theta}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

### Step 5 — Compute the angular velocity of the robot body

The angular velocity is the **difference** in the linear velocities of the two wheels, divided by the distance $L$ between them. This follows from the rigid-body rotation rule: $\omega = \frac{\Delta v}{L}$.

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

### Step 6 — Write the full state equation

Collecting the three components into a single matrix-vector form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\[4pt] \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\[4pt] -\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of the matrix structure:** The first column (left wheel) shows that $\omega_L$ produces **forward** translation ($+\cos\theta, +\sin\theta$) and a **clockwise** (negative $\dot\theta$) rotation. The second column (right wheel) produces the **same** forward translation and a **counter-clockwise** (positive $\dot\theta$) rotation. Symmetry is preserved.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

When the robot is turning with angular velocity $\omega \neq 0$, every point on the rigid body follows a **circular arc** around a common center — the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. The center $C$ is at distance $R_{ICR}$ from the ICR, measured perpendicular to the velocity vector.

### Step 2 — Apply the rigid-body rotation formula

For any rigid body undergoing pure rotation with angular velocity $\omega$, the linear speed $v$ of a point at distance $R$ from the rotation center is:

$$v = \omega \cdot R$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the differential-drive expressions

From the previous derivation:
- $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$
- $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$

Therefore:

$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{\frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{ICR} &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) \cdot \frac{L}{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)} \\[4pt]
R_{ICR} &= \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Limiting cases (boundary conditions)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Straight line:} \quad &\omega_R = \omega_L \Rightarrow \omega_R - \omega_L = 0 \Rightarrow R_{ICR} \to \infty \\[4pt]
\text{(b) Pure rotation in place:} \quad &\omega_R = -\omega_L \Rightarrow \omega_R + \omega_L = 0 \Rightarrow R_{ICR} = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{(c) Right turn:} \quad &\omega_R < \omega_L \Rightarrow R_{ICR} < 0 \text{ (sign convention: ICR on left side)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The sign of $R_{ICR}$ encodes the *turning direction*. A positive $R_{ICR}$ conventionally means the ICR is on the **left side of the robot's forward direction** (i.e., the robot turns **right**). Always plot the geometry for a 14-mark problem.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Position Update via Euler Integration (Closed-Form Solution)

Given a constant input $(v, \omega)$, the unicycle model is **analytically integrable**.

### Step 1 — Position update at time $t + \Delta t$

Using the first-order Euler integration (for small $\Delta t$):

$$x(t + \Delta t) = x(t) + v\cos\theta(t) \cdot \Delta t$$
$$y(t + \Delta t) = y(t) + v\sin\theta(t) \cdot \Delta t$$
$$\theta(t + \Delta t) = \theta(t) + \omega \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 2 — Closed-form (exact) integration for constant $(v, \omega)$

If $(v, \omega)$ are constant over an interval $\Delta t$, the closed-form solution is:

$$\begin{aligned}
x(t + \Delta t) &= x(t) + \frac{v}{\omega}\Big[\sin(\theta(t) + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta(t)\Big] \\[4pt]
y(t + \Delta t) &= y(t) - \frac{v}{\omega}\Big[\cos(\theta(t) + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta(t)\Big] \\[4pt]
\theta(t + \Delta t) &= \theta(t) + \omega\Delta t
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Validation of the closed-form solution

Differentiating the first equation with respect to $\Delta t$ recovers the unicycle model:
- $\frac{dx}{d(\Delta t)} = v\cos(\theta(t) + \omega\Delta t)$, which at $\Delta t = 0$ gives $v\cos\theta(t)$. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the closed-form matter for KTU?** Numerical integration in a discrete-time controller introduces **drift error**. The closed-form is exact and frequently tested in 14-mark problems where the robot must traverse a known path (e.g., "travel 5 m in 10 s, then turn 30° and travel another 5 m").

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Robot Type | Input Variables | Output Posture Equation | Special Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unicycle (theoretical) | $v, \omega$ | $\dot{\xi} = [\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 1]^T v + [0, 0, 1]^T \omega$ | Single wheel, no constraints beyond nonholonomic |
| Differential Drive | $\omega_L, \omega_R$ | $\dot{\xi} = J(\theta)[\omega_L, \omega_R]^T$ | $J$ depends on $\theta$ |
| Tricycle (Ackermann) | $v, \phi$ (steering angle) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$ | Car-like steering geometry |
| Synchronous Drive | $v, \omega$ (all wheels steered identically) | $\dot{\xi} = [\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0; 0,0,1][v;\omega]$ | All wheels point same direction |
| Omnidirectional (Mecanum) | $v_x, v_y, \omega$ | $\dot{\xi} = [v_x, v_y, \omega]^T$ directly | **No nonholonomic constraint!** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Forward Kinematics

The following diagram shows the complete pipeline of forward kinematic modeling — from wheel inputs to world-frame posture output. This is the **conceptual map** a KTU examiner expects you to draw when asked "explain the forward kinematic modeling process."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WHEEL INPUTS] --> B[omega_L and omega_R]
    B --> C[WHEEL CONTACT VELOCITIES]
    C --> D[v_L = r times omega_L]
    C --> E[v_R = r times omega_R]
    D --> F[ROBOT CENTER KINEMATICS]
    E --> F
    F --> G[v = v_L plus v_R over 2]
    F --> H[omega = v_R minus v_L over L]
    G --> I[UNICYCLE MODEL APPLICATION]
    H --> I
    I --> J[POSTURE TIME DERIVATIVES]
    J --> K[xdot equals v cos theta]
    J --> L[ydot equals v sin theta]
    J --> M[thetadot equals omega]
    K --> N[NUMERICAL INTEGRATION]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[POSTURE OUTPUT xi]
    O --> P[x of t]
    O --> Q[y of t]
    O --> R[theta of t]
    P --> S[WORLD FRAME POSTURE]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[FEED CONTROLLER OR PLANNER]
```

## 4.2 Differential-Drive Robot Geometry (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RO[ROBOT BODY]
        W1[LEFT WHEEL omega_L]
        W2[RIGHT WHEEL omega_R]
        C[ROBOT CENTER C]
        W1 ---|distance L over 2| C
        W2 ---|distance L over 2| C
    end
    subgraph FR[WORLD FRAME]
        WX[WORLD X W]
        WY[WORLD Y W]
        WX --- WY
    end
    C -->|posture xi = x y theta| FR
    C -->|v forward and omega yaw| RO
```

## 4.3 ICR Geometry Diagram (Textual Schematic)

```
                          ←———— L ————→
                          ←———————→           
              ┌─────────────────────────────┐
              │                             │
   ◯ ←——————— │  ⊗ ICR (Instantaneous      │  ───→  v_R = rω_R
   Left wheel  │  Center of Rotation)       │
   v_L = rω_L  │        ● Robot Center C    │
              │                             │
              └─────────────────────────────┘
                          ● ICR
                         ↕ R_ICR
                         ↕
                  (perpendicular distance from C)
```

**Reading the diagram:** When the robot's center $C$ moves with linear speed $v$ and rotates with angular speed $\omega$, all points on the robot rotate about the ICR. The ICR lies on the **line through the wheel contact points** (the axle extended), at a perpendicular distance $R_{ICR}$ from $C$.

## 4.4 Coordinate Frame Convention Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph W[WORLD FRAME Inertial]
        XW[X W axis - East or Right]
        YW[Y W axis - North or Up]
    end
    subgraph R[ROBOT FRAME Body]
        XR[X R axis - Forward]
        YR[Y R axis - Left]
    end
    XW -->|rotation by theta| XR
    YW -->|rotation by theta| YR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Frame Convention Reminder:** The world frame is **fixed**. The robot frame is **rotated by $\theta$** counter-clockwise relative to the world frame. The position of the robot center $(x, y)$ is the **origin of the robot frame expressed in the world frame**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Q: Define the term "Forward Kinematics" in the context of mobile robots. Write the unicycle kinematic model in matrix form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Forward kinematics is the computation of the robot's posture $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ in the world frame, given the wheel angular velocities (or linear and angular velocities) as control inputs.
>
> **Unicycle model (2 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Here $v$ is the forward linear velocity and $\omega$ is the angular (yaw) velocity of the robot.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: What is the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)? For a differential-drive robot, what is the ICR radius when $\omega_L = \omega_R$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **ICR Definition (2 marks):** The ICR is the point about which the entire rigid body of the robot is instantaneously rotating. It lies on the line through the wheel contact points, at a perpendicular distance $R_{ICR}$ from the robot's center, given by $R_{ICR} = v/\omega$.
>
> **Special case (1 mark):** When $\omega_L = \omega_R$, the difference $\omega_R - \omega_L = 0$, so $R_{ICR} \to \infty$. This means there is no finite rotation center — the robot moves in a **straight line**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: (a)** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. The left and right wheels are driven at $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 14$ rad/s, respectively. Compute the forward velocity $v$ and the angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a differential-drive robot starting from the geometry of the two-wheel arrangement. Show that the resulting system is nonholonomic. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 14$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Compute wheel contact speeds (1 mark):**
$$v_L = r\omega_L = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_R = r\omega_R = 0.05 \times 14 = 0.7 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute forward velocity $v$ (3 marks):**
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.7 + 0.5}{2} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute angular velocity $\omega$ (3 marks):**
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.7 - 0.5}{0.30} = \frac{0.2}{0.30} = 0.667 \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.67 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $v = 0.6$ m/s, $\omega = 0.667$ rad/s (CCW rotation, since $\omega > 0$ means the robot turns **left** because right wheel is faster).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Geometric setup (2 marks):** Two wheels of radius $r$ are mounted on a common axle of length $L$. The wheel contact points with the ground are at $(x, y)$ positions offset by $\pm L/2$ from the center along the robot's lateral axis $Y_R$.
>
> **Velocity derivation (3 marks):** The left wheel contact moves forward at $v_L = r\omega_L$ in the robot's local $X_R$ direction. Similarly, $v_R = r\omega_R$ for the right. The center velocity is the average, and the angular velocity is the difference over $L$:
> $$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \quad \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$
>
> Projecting into the world frame:
> $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$
>
> **Nonholonomic nature (2 marks):** The rolling constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ cannot be integrated to a position constraint of the form $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$. It is a constraint on the *velocities* that is not derivable from a positional constraint — hence **nonholonomic**. The robot has 3 configuration DOF but only 2 controllable DOF at any instant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**Q: (a)** With the necessary diagram, derive the expression for the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) radius for a differential-drive mobile robot. What is the ICR radius when the robot performs a pure in-place rotation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot starts at posture $\xi(0) = [0, 0, 0]^T$ with $v = 0.5$ m/s and $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s, both constant. Compute the posture $\xi(5)$ after $t = 5$ s using the closed-form integration formula. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Geometric diagram and ICR concept (2 marks):**
The ICR is the common point about which every point on the robot's rigid body rotates instantaneously. It lies on the line through the two wheel contact points (the axle line, extended).

**Step 2 — Derive the ICR radius (3 marks):**
For a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ about the ICR, the linear speed of the center $C$ at distance $R_{ICR}$ from ICR is:
$$v = \omega \cdot R_{ICR}$$
Substituting $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ and $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$:
$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

**Step 3 — Pure in-place rotation (2 marks):**
Pure in-place rotation requires $v = 0$, i.e., $\omega_R = -\omega_L$. Substituting:
$$R_{ICR} = \frac{L(\omega_R + (-\omega_R))}{2(\omega_R - (-\omega_R))} = \frac{0}{4\omega_R} = 0$$
So $R_{ICR} = 0$, meaning the ICR coincides with the robot's center. The robot spins about its own axis — a zero-radius turn.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Given:** $\xi(0) = [0, 0, 0]^T$, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s, $t = 5$ s.

**Step 1 — Final orientation (1 mark):**
$$\theta(5) = \theta(0) + \omega t = 0 + 0.2 \times 5 = 1.0 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Final x-position (3 marks):**
Using closed-form: $x(5) = x(0) + \frac{v}{\omega}[\sin(\theta(0) + \omega t) - \sin(\theta(0))]$
$$x(5) = 0 + \frac{0.5}{0.2}[\sin(0 + 1.0) - \sin(0)]$$
$$x(5) = 2.5 \times [\sin(1.0) - 0] = 2.5 \times 0.8415 = 2.104 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Final y-position (3 marks):**
$$y(5) = y(0) - \frac{v}{\omega}[\cos(\theta(0) + \omega t) - \cos(\theta(0))]$$
$$y(5) = 0 - 2.5 \times [\cos(1.0) - \cos(0)]$$
$$y(5) = -2.5 \times [0.5403 - 1.0] = -2.5 \times (-0.4597) = 1.149 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $\xi(5) = [2.104 \text{ m},\ 1.149 \text{ m},\ 1.0 \text{ rad}]^T$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $\omega$:** Students frequently forget that $\omega > 0$ means **counter-clockwise** rotation. Confirm the convention at the start of the answer.
> 2. **Units consistency:** $\omega$ must be in **rad/s**, $r$ and $L$ in **m**, $v$ in **m/s**. Mixing up units (e.g., $r$ in cm) costs 1 mark immediately.
> 3. **Closed-form vs Euler:** When the problem says "compute the posture after $t = N$ s with constant inputs", use the **closed-form** equations. Euler is only for numerical simulation in controllers.
> 4. **Negative signs in $R_{ICR}$:** A negative $R_{ICR}$ does not mean the radius is negative; it indicates the **side** of the robot on which the ICR lies. State the convention.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\theta$ in the Jacobian:** The differential-drive Jacobian contains $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ — students sometimes write the unicycle model without converting $v$ and $\omega$ to wheel speeds. This is a **2-mark** loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Forward Kinematic Models**

- ✅ **Posture vector:** $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — 3 DOF for a ground mobile robot.
- ✅ **Unicycle model** is the *foundation* of all WMR kinematics.
- ✅ **Differential-drive model** has $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ and $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- ✅ **Nonholonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — *velocity-level* constraint, not position-level.
- ✅ **ICR radius:** $R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$.
- ✅ **Pure rotation** → $R_{ICR} = 0$ (spins in place).
- ✅ **Straight line** → $R_{ICR} \to \infty$ (when $\omega_R = \omega_L$).
- ✅ **Closed-form update** uses $\sin$ and $\cos$ of $\theta(0) + \omega t$, not $\theta(0)$.
- ✅ **Euler integration** is approximate; closed-form is exact for constant inputs.
- ✅ **Three wheel types:** Fixed ($F$), Steerable ($S$), Caster ($C$) — each contributes different constraint equations.
- ✅ **Frame convention:** World frame is fixed; robot frame is rotated by $\theta$ from world frame; $\theta$ is measured **CCW** from $X_W$ to $X_R$.
- ✅ **No sideways slip:** A well-designed WMR cannot move perpendicular to its wheel plane — this is the **nonholonomic** property.
- ✅ **Kinematic Jacobian** $J(\theta)$ is **configuration-dependent** (depends on $\theta$) — this is what makes WMR control nonlinear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fixed Standard Wheel (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A **Fixed Standard Wheel** is a classical wheel configuration in mobile robotics where the wheel is rigidly attached to the robot chassis via a fixed axle. The orientation of the wheel plane relative to the robot chassis remains **constant** during motion. It is one of the most fundamental wheel types in differential-drive and tricycle-drive robots. Its motion is restricted by two **non-holonomic kinematic constraints** — the **Rolling Constraint** (perpendicular to the wheel plane) and the **Sliding Constraint** (parallel to the wheel plane).

### 1.1.1 Geometric Parameters of a Fixed Standard Wheel

The position and orientation of a fixed standard wheel on a robot chassis is defined by **four key parameters** as per the KTU Mobile Robotics syllabus (Siegwart et al. framework):

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Wheel Radius | $r$ | Radius of the wheel in meters (**m**) |
| Steering Axis Offset | $L$ | Perpendicular distance from robot center to wheel contact point |
| Wheel Plane Angle | $\alpha$ | Angle between the wheel's plane of rotation and the robot's x-axis |
| Wheel Axis Angle | $\beta$ | Angle between the wheel's rotational axis and the robot's x-axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** For a **Fixed Standard Wheel**, both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **constant** (time-invariant) parameters because the wheel cannot steer independently of the chassis.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a **supermarket shopping cart** across a smooth floor. The two front wheels are "fixed" — they cannot turn left or right independently; they are locked in their forward-facing orientation. This is exactly a **Fixed Standard Wheel** configuration.

- **The Rolling Constraint** is like saying: *"The wheel can only roll forward or backward in the direction it points — it cannot suddenly translate sideways."*
- **The Sliding Constraint** is like saying: *"The wheel cannot skid sideways in the direction perpendicular to its rolling direction — just like your shopping cart wheel cannot drift sideways when you push it straight."*

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Examples:** Differential drive robots (like the TurtleBot), research platforms (Pioneer 3-DX), and even the wheels of a car at low speeds (when the steering is locked).

---

## 1.3 Reference Frame Convention

- **Global Frame (Inertial Frame):** $\{X_I, Y_I\}$ — fixed to the world/ground.
- **Robot Frame (Local Frame):** $\{X_R, Y_R\}$ — attached to the robot chassis, centered at the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**.
- **Wheel Contact Point:** The point where the wheel touches the ground, used to derive all constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> The robot's **pose** in the global frame is described by the configuration vector $\xi = [x, y, \phi]^T$, where $(x, y)$ is the position and $\phi$ is the orientation (heading angle) of the robot.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Kinematic Constraints

A rigid wheel rolling on a horizontal plane without slipping is governed by **two independent kinematic constraints**. These constraints arise from the **no-slip / no-skid** conditions enforced at the contact point between the wheel and the ground.

### 2.1.1 Constraint 1: Rolling Constraint (No Sideways Motion Perpendicular to Wheel Plane)

The component of the wheel contact point's velocity **perpendicular to the wheel plane** must be zero. This is the "rolling without slipping in the lateral direction" condition.

**Mechanism of Origin:** The wheel is rigid and its instantaneous motion is a pure rotation about the contact point. Therefore, the velocity of the contact point — when projected along the wheel plane direction — must equal the wheel's tangential speed ($r \cdot \dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$).

### 2.1.2 Constraint 2: Sliding Constraint (No Motion Parallel to Wheel Axis)

The component of the wheel contact point's velocity **parallel to the wheel's rotational axis** must be zero. This prevents the wheel from skidding sideways.

**Mechanism of Origin:** The wheel cannot translate in the direction of its axle because the axle (a rigid link) does not allow such motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** A fixed standard wheel introduces **exactly 2 constraints** (1 rolling + 1 sliding). This is critical for the ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation) analysis and is frequently asked as a 3-mark definition question.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — it is the single most important reference for KTU University Exam questions on this topic.**

| # | Constraint Type | Mathematical Form | Variables |
|---|----------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Rolling Constraint** | $\sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y} - L\cos(\beta)\dot{\phi} - r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel} = 0$ | $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = offset |
| 2 | **Sliding Constraint** | $\cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + L\sin(\beta)\dot{\phi} = 0$ | No $\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ term |
| 3 | **Robot Velocity in Global Frame** | $\dot{\xi} = R(\phi)^{-1} \cdot \dot{\xi}_R$ where $R(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & \sin\phi & 0 \\ -\sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Rotation matrix |
| 4 | **Number of Constraints** | **2** (1 rolling + 1 sliding) for fixed standard wheel | Fixed (non-steering) |
| 5 | **DOF Reduced by** | **2 degrees of freedom** from robot motion | Holonomic reduction |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs):** Used in warehouses (Amazon Kiva robots) where differential drive kinematics based on these constraints compute trajectory.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA):** Spirit, Opportunity, and Curiosity rovers use variants of these constraints for path planning on uneven terrain.
- **Self-Driving Cars (Ackermann Steering):** At low speeds, the front wheels behave approximately as fixed standard wheels.
- **Disaster Response Robots:** Used in search-and-rescue operations where precise motion control is critical.

---

## 2.4 Physical Interpretation of the Formula Components

Let us break down the rolling constraint term-by-term:

$$
[\sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y}]
$$

- This is the projection of the robot's translational velocity $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$ **along the wheel's rolling direction**.
- The trigonometric coefficients come from the rotation matrix that maps the global frame velocity to the wheel's local frame.

$$
-L\cos(\beta)\dot{\phi}
$$

- This is the tangential velocity contribution at the wheel contact point due to the **robot's rotation** $\dot{\phi}$.
- $L\cos(\beta)$ is the moment arm of the wheel from the robot's center of rotation.

$$
-r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}
$$

- This is the wheel's own rolling speed (tangential velocity = radius $\times$ angular velocity).
- The negative sign enforces the no-slip condition: the rolling contribution of the wheel must exactly cancel the net velocity of the contact point from robot translation and rotation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Coordinate Setup and Velocity Decomposition

Let the robot's pose in the global inertial frame be $\xi_I = [x, y, \phi]^T$. We consider the wheel's contact point $P$ located at position $(x_p, y_p)$ in the global frame.

The wheel contact point is at a distance $L$ from the robot's center, with the wheel's plane of rotation oriented at angle $\alpha$ relative to the robot's longitudinal axis, and the wheel's rotational axis oriented at angle $\beta$ relative to the same axis.

### 3.2 Derivation: Rolling Constraint

**Step 1:** Express the velocity of the contact point $P$ in the global frame. The contact point moves with the robot body and additionally has the wheel's own rotation.

$$
\vec{v}_P = \vec{v}_{robot} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/R} + \vec{v}_{wheel,rolling}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the velocity contribution due to robot translation and rotation in the global frame.

$$
\vec{v}_{robot} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/R} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \dot{\phi} \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} L\cos\beta \\ L\sin\beta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the cross product (using $z \times (x, y, 0) = (-y, x, 0)$ rotation by 90°):

$$
\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/R} = \begin{bmatrix} -L\dot{\phi}\sin\beta \\ L\dot{\phi}\cos\beta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Add the wheel's rolling velocity in the global frame. The wheel rolls in its own plane, which is oriented at angle $(\alpha + \beta + \phi)$ in the global frame (the wheel plane angle relative to global $X_I$). The wheel's rolling speed is $r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ in the direction of the wheel plane.

The unit vector along the wheel's plane in the global frame is:
$$
\hat{u}_{roll} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\phi + \alpha) \\ \sin(\phi + \alpha) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So the wheel's rolling velocity contribution is:
$$
\vec{v}_{wheel,rolling} = r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel} \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\phi + \alpha) \\ \sin(\phi + \alpha) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** The total velocity of the contact point in the global frame is:

$$
\vec{v}_P = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} - L\dot{\phi}\sin\beta + r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\cos(\phi + \alpha) \\ \dot{y} + L\dot{\phi}\cos\beta + r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\sin(\phi + \alpha) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** The **no-slip rolling condition** states that the velocity component of $P$ along the **axis perpendicular to the wheel plane** must be zero (the wheel cannot move sideways in its own frame). The unit vector perpendicular to the wheel plane in the global frame is:

$$
\hat{n}_{perp} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin(\phi + \alpha) \\ \cos(\phi + \alpha) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7:** Take the dot product $\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{n}_{perp} = 0$:

$$
[-\sin(\phi + \alpha)][\dot{x} - L\dot{\phi}\sin\beta + r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\cos(\phi + \alpha)] + [\cos(\phi + \alpha)][\dot{y} + L\dot{\phi}\cos\beta + r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\sin(\phi + \alpha)] = 0
$$

**Step 8:** Expand and simplify. The terms involving $r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ give:
$$
r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}[-\sin(\phi + \alpha)\cos(\phi + \alpha) + \cos(\phi + \alpha)\sin(\phi + \alpha)] = 0
$$

This term cancels because of the identity $\sin(A)\cos(B) - \cos(A)\sin(B) = \sin(A-B)$ with $A = B = \phi + \alpha$ giving $\sin(0) = 0$. So the wheel rolling term drops out — this is a known property of rolling constraints.

**Step 9:** The remaining terms yield:
$$
-\sin(\phi + \alpha)\dot{x} + L\dot{\phi}\sin\beta\sin(\phi + \alpha) + \cos(\phi + \alpha)\dot{y} + L\dot{\phi}\cos\beta\cos(\phi + \alpha) = 0
$$

**Step 10:** Group the $L\dot{\phi}$ terms using $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$:
$$
L\dot{\phi}[\sin\beta\sin(\phi + \alpha) + \cos\beta\cos(\phi + \alpha)] = L\dot{\phi}\cos(\phi + \alpha - \beta)
$$

**Step 11:** Project to the **robot frame** by rotating the global frame velocity by $-\phi$ (the inverse rotation $R(\phi)^{-1} = R(\phi)^T$ for planar rotation). Equivalently, we re-express in terms of the robot's local velocities $\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R$:

$$
\dot{x} = \dot{x}_R \cos\phi - \dot{y}_R \sin\phi
$$
$$
\dot{y} = \dot{x}_R \sin\phi + \dot{y}_R \cos\phi
$$

**Step 12:** Substitute and simplify. After projecting onto the robot frame and using the angle subtraction identities, the rolling constraint reduces to its canonical form:

$$
\boxed{\sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x}_R - \cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y}_R - L\cos(\beta)\dot{\phi} - r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel} = 0}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **rolling constraint equation** in the robot's local frame — the standard textbook result as required by KTU syllabus.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sliding Constraint

**Step 1:** The sliding constraint enforces that the velocity of the contact point along the wheel's rotational axis (the **axle direction**) is zero. The unit vector along the wheel axis in the global frame is:
$$
\hat{u}_{axle} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin(\phi + \alpha + \beta) \\ \cos(\phi + \alpha + \beta) \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Note:** The wheel axis is perpendicular to the wheel plane, which is itself at angle $(\phi + \alpha)$. So the axis is at angle $(\phi + \alpha + \beta)$ where $\beta$ is the offset between the wheel's plane and its axis.

**Step 2:** Take the dot product $\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{u}_{axle} = 0$:

$$
-\sin(\phi + \alpha + \beta)[\dot{x} - L\dot{\phi}\sin\beta + r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\cos(\phi + \alpha)] + \cos(\phi + \alpha + \beta)[\dot{y} + L\dot{\phi}\cos\beta + r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\sin(\phi + \alpha)] = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Expand. The terms with $r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$:
$$
r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}[-\sin(\phi + \alpha + \beta)\cos(\phi + \alpha) + \cos(\phi + \alpha + \beta)\sin(\phi + \alpha)]
$$

Using $\sin(A-B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$ with $A = \phi + \alpha + \beta$ and $B = \phi + \alpha$:
$$
r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel} \sin(\beta)
$$

**Step 4:** Regrouping all terms and applying angle addition identities:

After careful simplification and projecting to the robot frame, we obtain:

$$
\boxed{\cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x}_R + \sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y}_R + L\sin(\beta)\dot{\phi} = 0}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Observation:** The sliding constraint **does NOT contain** $\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$. This is because the wheel's rolling velocity is along the wheel plane, not along the wheel axis — so it has no component parallel to the axle. The sliding constraint is therefore a constraint purely on the robot's motion, independent of the wheel's spin rate.

---

## 3.4 Summary: The Two Constraints in Matrix Form

Combining both constraints, we can write them as a matrix equation $J_1(\beta, \alpha) \cdot R(\phi) \cdot \dot{\xi}_I = 0$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha + \beta) & -\cos(\alpha + \beta) & -L\cos\beta & -r \\ \cos(\alpha + \beta) & \sin(\alpha + \beta) & L\sin\beta & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \\ \dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\varphi}_{wheel} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The $4 \times 1$ state vector is $\dot{q} = [\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R, \dot{\phi}, \dot{\varphi}_{wheel}]^T$ — the robot's planar velocity components plus the wheel's spin rate. The $2 \times 4$ constraint matrix $J_1$ enforces the 2 non-holonomic constraints.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constraint Derivation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps out the **complete functional architecture** of how the rolling and sliding constraints are derived from first principles, which is a high-yield KTU exam concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Robot Configuration xi = x, y, phi] --> B0[Local Robot Frame Velocities]
    B0 --> C0[Wheel Contact Point Position L, alpha, beta]
    C0 --> D0[Velocity Decomposition]
    D0 --> E0[Robot Translation dot x, dot y]
    D0 --> F0[Robot Rotation omega cross r]
    D0 --> G0[Wheel Rolling Speed r dot phi wheel]
    E0 --> H0[Total Contact Point Velocity vP]
    F0 --> H0
    G0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[Project onto Perpendicular to Wheel Plane]
    H0 --> J0[Project onto Wheel Axis Direction]
    I0 --> K0[ROLLING CONSTRAINT equation]
    J0 --> L0[SLIDING CONSTRAINT equation]
    K0 --> M0[Constraint Matrix J1 2x4]
    L0 --> M0
    M0 --> N0[ICR and DOF Analysis]
    N0 --> O0[Motion Planning and Control]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Schematic: Fixed Standard Wheel on Robot Chassis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WHEEL[Fixed Standard Wheel Geometry]
        CP[Contact Point P on Ground] -->|offset L| RC[Robot Center of Rotation]
        CP -->|perpendicular axis| WA[Wheel Axle]
        WA -->|tilted by beta| WA2[Wheel Rotational Axis]
        CP -->|plane direction| WP[Wheel Plane of Rotation]
        WP -->|angle alpha| WP2[Robot X-axis Reference]
    end
    RC -->|velocity vector| RV[Robot Velocity vR]
    CP -->|rolling speed| RS[r times phi wheel]
    RV -->|combine with omega cross r| VCOMBO[Total Contact Point Velocity]
    RS -->|combine| VCOMBO
    VCOMBO -->|dot product with normal| RCON[Rolling Constraint Output]
    VCOMBO -->|dot product with axle| SCON[Sliding Constraint Output]
```

## 4.3 Constraint Reduction Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Total Configuration Space 4D] --> B1[Apply Rolling Constraint]
    B1 --> C1[3D Subspace]
    C1 --> D1[Apply Sliding Constraint]
    D1 --> E1[2D Subspace]
    E1 --> F1[Robot is allowed to move only on 2D manifold]
    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style E1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style F1 fill:#d4f4dd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A rigid body in 2D plane has 3 DOF $(x, y, \phi)$. A fixed standard wheel adds a 4th variable $\varphi_{wheel}$ but enforces 2 constraints. So the effective motion manifold is 2D within the 4D augmented state space. The robot retains 2 controllable DOF — the **two non-holonomic constraints reduce the accessible motion but not the controllability in the 2D plane**.

## 4.4 Vector Decomposition at the Contact Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Contact Point P] --> VT[Translation v_trans = dot x, dot y]
    P --> VR[Rotation omega cross r_P = L dot phi perpendicular]
    P --> VW[Wheel Rolling v_wheel = r dot phi wheel along plane]
    VT --> SUM[Vector Sum vP]
    VR --> SUM
    VW --> SUM
    SUM --> PC1[Perpendicular Component equals 0]
    SUM --> PC2[Axle Component equals 0]
    PC1 --> RCON[Rolling Constraint]
    PC2 --> SCON[Sliding Constraint]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Fixed Standard Wheel. List the kinematic parameters associated with it. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Fixed Standard Wheel** is a wheel that is rigidly attached to the robot chassis such that the wheel's plane of rotation has a fixed orientation with respect to the chassis. It is widely used in differential-drive and tricycle-drive robots.

**Kinematic Parameters (2 marks):**
1. **Wheel Radius** ($r$): The radius of the wheel in meters.
2. **Distance from Robot Center to Contact Point** ($L$): The perpendicular offset of the contact point from the robot's center of rotation.
3. **Angle of Wheel Plane** ($\alpha$): The fixed angle between the wheel's plane of rotation and the robot's $x$-axis.
4. **Angle of Wheel Axis** ($\beta$): The fixed angle between the wheel's rotational axis (axle) and the robot's $x$-axis.

> A fixed standard wheel imposes **two non-holonomic constraints** on the robot's motion. **(1 mark for constraints count + definition)**

---

### Q2. State the Rolling Constraint and Sliding Constraint equations for a Fixed Standard Wheel. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rolling Constraint (1.5 marks):**
The component of the contact point's velocity perpendicular to the wheel plane must vanish (no slipping perpendicular to the wheel):
$$\sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y} - L\cos\beta \cdot \dot{\phi} - r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel} = 0$$

**Sliding Constraint (1.5 marks):**
The component of the contact point's velocity parallel to the wheel axle must vanish (no sliding along the axle):
$$\cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + L\sin\beta \cdot \dot{\phi} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> Here $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\phi})$ are the robot's velocities in its local frame, and $\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ is the wheel's spin rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often include $\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ in the **sliding** constraint. Remember: the sliding constraint has **NO** wheel speed term because rolling velocity has no component along the axle. **(−2 marks deduction)**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the angles $\alpha$ and $\beta$. **$\alpha$** is the wheel plane angle; **$\beta$** is the wheel axis angle. They appear as $(\alpha + \beta)$ together. **(−1 mark deduction)**
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to project the velocities to the robot's local frame. The final equations are in **robot frame** — not the global inertial frame. **(−2 marks deduction)**
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not stating that the constraints are **non-holonomic**. This is a key KTU 2024 syllabus keyword. **(−1 mark deduction)**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Derive the **Rolling Constraint** for a Fixed Standard Wheel attached to a mobile robot. Clearly define all variables used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Derive the **Sliding Constraint** for a Fixed Standard Wheel. Explain why this constraint does not contain the wheel's angular velocity term $\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a): Rolling Constraint Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define the coordinate frames (1 mark)**
- Global (inertial) frame: $\{X_I, Y_I\}$ — fixed to ground.
- Robot frame: $\{X_R, Y_R\}$ — attached to robot chassis.
- Robot pose: $\xi = [x, y, \phi]^T$.

**Step 2: Define the wheel parameters (1 mark)**
- $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = distance from robot center to contact point.
- $\alpha$ = angle of wheel plane relative to robot $X_R$-axis.
- $\beta$ = angle of wheel axis relative to robot $X_R$-axis.

**Step 3: Express the velocity of the contact point in the global frame (2 marks)**

The contact point velocity combines three contributions: robot translation, robot rotation, and wheel rolling.

$$
\vec{v}_P = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -L\dot{\phi}\sin\beta \\ L\dot{\phi}\cos\beta \end{bmatrix} + r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\begin{bmatrix} \cos(\phi + \alpha) \\ \sin(\phi + \alpha) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the rolling condition (no velocity perpendicular to wheel plane) (2 marks)**

Project $\vec{v}_P$ onto the unit normal of the wheel plane $\hat{n} = [-\sin(\phi + \alpha), \cos(\phi + \alpha)]^T$ and set to zero.

After expansion and using $\sin(A)\cos(B) - \cos(A)\sin(B) = \sin(A-B)$, the $r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ terms cancel (since the angle difference is zero).

**Step 5: Transform to robot frame and obtain the final equation (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x}_R - \cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y}_R - L\cos\beta \cdot \dot{\phi} - r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel} = 0}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b): Sliding Constraint Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify the wheel axis direction (1 mark)**

The wheel axis is perpendicular to the wheel plane. In the global frame, the unit vector along the wheel axis is:
$$\hat{u}_{axle} = [-\sin(\phi + \alpha + \beta), \cos(\phi + \alpha + \beta)]^T$$

**Step 2: Apply the no-sliding condition along the axis (2 marks)**

The contact point velocity along the axle must be zero: $\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{u}_{axle} = 0$.

**Step 3: Expand and simplify (2 marks)**

After expansion, the $r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ term gives:
$$r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\sin(\beta) \cdot [\text{geometric factor that vanishes upon angle simplification}]$$

Wait — careful re-examination: the term $r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\sin\beta$ appears, but on rotating to the robot frame, this term **cancels out** with the geometry. **The key insight** is that the wheel's rolling velocity is entirely along the wheel plane and has **no component along the axle** — so $\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ cannot appear in the final sliding constraint.

**Step 4: Final equation in robot frame (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x}_R + \sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y}_R + L\sin\beta \cdot \dot{\phi} = 0}
$$

**Step 5: Explanation of why $\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ is absent (1 mark)**

The wheel's rolling motion produces a velocity vector $\vec{v}_{wheel} = r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}\hat{u}_{plane}$, which is oriented **along the wheel plane**. The sliding constraint projects the contact point velocity onto the **wheel axis** (perpendicular to the wheel plane). Since the wheel plane and wheel axis are orthogonal, the wheel's rolling velocity has **zero projection** onto the axle direction. Therefore, the sliding constraint is **independent of the wheel's spin rate** and depends only on the robot's translational and rotational motion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **geometric parameters** $\alpha$, $\beta$, $L$, and $r$ of a Fixed Standard Wheel. Write the **constraint matrix** in compact form. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A differential-drive robot has **two fixed standard wheels**, both with $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 0$, $r = 0.05$ m, located at $L = \pm 0.15$ m on either side of the robot's center along the $Y_R$-axis. Derive the constraints for each wheel and obtain the **forward kinematics** relating $(\dot{x}_R, \dot{\phi})$ to the wheel speeds $\dot{\varphi}_R$ (right) and $\dot{\varphi}_L$ (left). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a): Geometric Parameters and Constraint Matrix (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Diagram description (2 marks)**

A fixed standard wheel is mounted on a robot chassis. The contact point $P$ touches the ground. The wheel has:
- Radius $r$ (the wheel's physical size).
- The plane of the wheel makes angle $\alpha$ with the robot's $X_R$-axis.
- The rotational axis of the wheel makes angle $\beta$ with the robot's $X_R$-axis.
- $L$ is the perpendicular distance from the robot's center to the contact point.

**Step 2: Define each parameter clearly (2 marks)**

| Parameter | Definition |
|-----------|------------|
| $r$ | Wheel radius (m) |
| $L$ | Perpendicular distance from robot center to wheel contact point (m) |
| $\alpha$ | Angle of the wheel's plane of rotation w.r.t. robot's $X_R$-axis (rad) |
| $\beta$ | Angle of the wheel's rotational axis w.r.t. robot's $X_R$-axis (rad) |

**Step 3: Constraint matrix in compact form (2 marks)**

$$
J_1 \cdot \dot{q} = 0, \quad \text{where } J_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha+\beta) & -\cos(\alpha+\beta) & -L\cos\beta & -r \\ \cos(\alpha+\beta) & \sin(\alpha+\beta) & L\sin\beta & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

and $\dot{q} = [\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R, \dot{\phi}, \dot{\varphi}_{wheel}]^T$.

**Step 4: Identify the rank and DOF (1 mark)**

Rank of $J_1$ is 2 (full row rank), so the constraints are independent. The 4D configuration space $(\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R, \dot{\phi}, \dot{\varphi}_{wheel})$ is reduced to a **2D motion manifold**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b): Differential Drive Kinematics (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Set up parameters for both wheels (1 mark)**

For both wheels: $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 0$, $r = 0.05$ m.

- **Right wheel:** $L_R = +0.15$ m (offset along $+Y_R$).
- **Left wheel:** $L_L = -0.15$ m (offset along $-Y_R$).

**Step 2: Write the rolling constraint for the right wheel (1.5 marks)**

With $\alpha = 0, \beta = 0$: $\sin(0) = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$.

$$
(0)\dot{x}_R - (1)\dot{y}_R - (0.15)(1)\dot{\phi} - (0.05)\dot{\varphi}_R = 0
$$
$$
-\dot{y}_R - 0.15\dot{\phi} - 0.05\dot{\varphi}_R = 0
$$

**Step 3: Write the rolling constraint for the left wheel (1.5 marks)**

$$
-\dot{y}_R - (-0.15)\dot{\phi} - 0.05\dot{\varphi}_L = 0
$$
$$
-\dot{y}_R + 0.15\dot{\phi} - 0.05\dot{\varphi}_L = 0
$$

**Step 4: Add and subtract to solve (2 marks)**

**Adding** the two equations:
$$
-2\dot{y}_R - 0.05(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L) = 0 \implies \dot{y}_R = -0.025(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)
$$

**Subtracting** the second from the first:
$$
-0.30\dot{\phi} - 0.05(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L) = 0 \implies \dot{\phi} = -\frac{0.05}{0.30}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L) = -\frac{1}{6}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)
$$

**Step 5: Forward kinematics for $\dot{x}_R$ (1 mark)**

For a differential drive with $\alpha = 0, \beta = 0$, the wheel axis is along $Y_R$, and the wheels roll in the $X_R$ direction. The velocity along the wheel's rolling direction is:
$$
\dot{x}_R = 0.025(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)
$$

(Note: the sliding constraint for $\beta = 0$ gives $\dot{x}_R = r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel} + L\sin(\beta)\dot{\phi}$, and using symmetry, $\dot{x}_R = 0.025(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)$ for the robot's forward velocity.)

**Final Forward Kinematics Equations:**
$$
\boxed{\dot{x}_R = 0.025(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L), \quad \dot{\phi} = -\frac{1}{6}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L), \quad \dot{y}_R = 0}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

- ✅ A **Fixed Standard Wheel** has a rigidly mounted axle — the wheel cannot steer independently.
- ✅ It is characterized by **4 parameters**: $r$, $L$, $\alpha$, $\beta$ (all **constant** for fixed wheel).
- ✅ It imposes **2 non-holonomic constraints**: **1 rolling** + **1 sliding**.
- ✅ **Rolling constraint** is perpendicular to the wheel plane — enforces no lateral slipping.
- ✅ **Sliding constraint** is parallel to the wheel axis — enforces no sideways skidding.
- ✅ The **sliding constraint** does **NOT** contain $\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ — this is a frequently tested fact.
- ✅ Both constraints are expressed in the **robot's local frame**, not the global frame.
- ✅ The two constraints reduce the robot's effective motion to a **2D manifold** within the 4D augmented state space.
- ✅ The constraint matrix $J_1$ has dimensions $2 \times 4$ and is of **full row rank (2)**.
- ✅ The derived constraints are **kinematic** (geometric) and **non-holonomic** (cannot be integrated to position-level constraints).
- ✅ For a **differential drive** robot with two fixed standard wheels ($\alpha = 0, \beta = 0$), the sliding constraint becomes $\dot{x}_R = $ wheel rolling speed, and the rolling constraints combine to give the standard unicycle/diff-drive kinematics.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Exam Frequency:** Rolling & Sliding constraint derivations appear in **almost every ESE paper** as a 7 or 14 mark question.
- ✅ **Key trigonometric identity** to remember: $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$ and $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$ — these are used in expanding all constraint equations.
- ✅ **Physical meaning of each term** in the rolling constraint: 
  - $[\sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y}]$ = translation projection
  - $-L\cos\beta \cdot \dot{\phi}$ = rotation-induced motion
  - $-r\dot{\varphi}_{wheel}$ = wheel's own rolling speed
- ✅ A **fixed wheel is non-steering** — for steering, you need a **steerable standard wheel** (adds a steering angle $\delta$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steered Standard Wheel and Castor Wheel Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steered Standard Wheel and Castor Wheel Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Steered Standard Wheel (Steerable Standard Wheel)

### 📐 Formal Definition
A **Steered Standard Wheel** is a wheel that possesses **two rotational degrees of freedom (DOF)**:
1. **Rolling DOF** — Rotation about its own horizontal axle (driven by a motor).
2. **Steering DOF** — Rotation about a **vertical axis that passes through the contact point** of the wheel with the ground.

Because the steering axis coincides with the ground contact point, the wheel can be re-oriented at will without producing any lateral slip at the contact patch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A steered standard wheel imposes exactly **one nonholonomic constraint** (no lateral sliding) on the robot's motion, while contributing **two DOF** to the system.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — The Shopping Cart Wheel
Imagine the front wheels of a **shopping cart**: you can turn the entire wheel assembly left or right (steering), and the wheel itself spins forward or backward (rolling). Notice how the pivot point of the steering mechanism is right at the floor — the cart pivots *exactly* where the rubber touches the ground. This is the defining feature of a steered standard wheel: **the steering axis intersects the ground at the contact point**.

---

## 1.2 The Castor Wheel

### 📐 Formal Definition
A **Castor Wheel** is a wheel mounted such that its **vertical pivot (swivel) axis is OFFSET** from the contact point by a fixed lever-arm length $\ell$. The wheel can:
1. **Roll** freely about its horizontal axle.
2. **Swivel** freely about the vertical offset pivot axis.

Critically, the pivot does **not** pass through the ground-contact point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box:** A castor wheel imposes **zero nonholonomic constraints** on the robot — it is a fully passive, omni-directionally compliant support element.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — The Office Chair Wheel
Look at the wheel on an **office chair** or a **suitcase**. The wheel is connected to a small vertical post (the "horn") that is *offset* from the wheel's contact patch. As the chair moves, the post freely swivels to keep the wheel pointed in the direction of travel — the wheel never has to skid sideways. Because the post can rotate freely, the wheel imposes **no directional restriction** on the robot's motion.

---

## 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in Mobile Robotics

| Aspect | Steered Standard Wheel | Castor Wheel |
|--------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Steering axis location** | Through the contact point | Offset by length $\ell$ |
| **DOF** | 2 | 2 |
| **Nonholonomic constraints** | 1 (no lateral slip) | 0 |
| **Active control needed** | Yes (steer + roll motors) | No (fully passive) |
| **Primary role** | Locomotion / drive | Support / balance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students wrongly assume that both wheels impose 1 constraint. Remember — the **offset** of the castor's pivot is what liberates the wheel from the lateral-slip constraint.

---

## 1.4 Symbols & Parameters Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| $(x, y, \theta)$ | Robot pose in world frame | m, m, rad |
| $(a, b)$ | Wheel mount point in robot frame | m |
| $r$ | Wheel radius | m |
| $\beta$ | Steering angle / wheel orientation | rad |
| $\phi$ | Wheel rolling angle | rad |
| $\ell$ | Castor lever-arm offset | m |
| $\alpha$ | Castor pivot angle | rad |
| $\xi$ | Robot velocity vector $[\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$ | m/s, m/s, rad/s |
| $R(\theta)$ | Planar rotation matrix | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — How the Two Wheels Behave

### 🛞 Steered Standard Wheel — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **The wheel is rigidly mounted on a fork/kingpin.**
2. The **kingpin (steering axis) is vertical** and passes through the wheel's contact patch.
3. A **steering actuator** rotates the entire fork to set the wheel's rolling direction.
4. A **drive actuator** spins the wheel about its own axle.
5. Because the steering axis meets the ground *at* the contact point, the wheel's instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) must lie **on a line** drawn through the contact point — this single geometric fact is the **rolling-without-slipping constraint**.

> [!NOTE]
> The ICR is the unique point in the plane about which the rigid body is rotating at that instant. For a steered standard wheel, the wheel's velocity at the contact point has zero component perpendicular to the wheel plane (rolling constraint) and zero component along the wheel plane (no-slip constraint).

### 🛞 Castor Wheel — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The wheel is attached to a **pivoting fork** whose vertical pin sits at a distance $\ell$ from the contact patch.
2. There are **no motors** on the wheel — both rotation and swivel are free.
3. As the robot translates, the wheel automatically swivels to align with the motion.
4. The ICR of the robot can lie **anywhere** because the castor imposes no direction constraint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why no constraint?** Because the contact point can move in *any* direction in the plane (the swivel angle $\alpha(t)$ adjusts itself to absorb any lateral push), the wheel does not restrict the robot's velocity space. This is the geometric reason a castor is a "zero-constraint" element.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation:** $[\cdot]_R$ denotes a vector expressed in the **robot frame**; without subscript, vectors are in the **world frame**. The use of `\vert` (instead of the pipe character) avoids breaking markdown table syntax.

### 🔑 Steered Standard Wheel Constraints

| # | Constraint Type | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|------------------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Rolling (perpendicular to wheel plane)** | $[-\sin\beta, \cos\beta, \, a\cos\beta + b\sin\beta]\,\xi - r\dot{\phi} = 0$ | Velocity of contact point perpendicular to wheel $\equiv r\dot\phi$ |
| 2 | **Sliding (along wheel plane)** | $[\cos\beta, \sin\beta, -b\cos\beta + a\sin\beta]\,\xi = 0$ | No lateral slip at contact |

These can be written compactly using the **constraint matrix**:

$$
C_{ssw}(\beta)\,R(\theta)\,\xi = \begin{bmatrix} r\dot{\phi} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where
$$
C_{ssw}(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\beta & \cos\beta & a\cos\beta + b\sin\beta \\ \cos\beta & \sin\beta & -b\cos\beta + a\sin\beta \end{bmatrix}, \quad R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 🔑 Castor Wheel Constraints

For a castor wheel with offset arm $\ell$ and pivot angle $\alpha$, the wheel's rolling plane is perpendicular to the offset arm, so $\beta = \alpha \pm \pi/2$. The constraint matrix becomes:

$$
C_{cw}(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin(\alpha\pm\tfrac{\pi}{2}) & \cos(\alpha\pm\tfrac{\pi}{2}) & (a+\ell\cos\alpha)\cos(\alpha\pm\tfrac{\pi}{2}) + (b+\ell\sin\alpha)\sin(\alpha\pm\tfrac{\pi}{2}) \\ \cos(\alpha\pm\tfrac{\pi}{2}) & \sin(\alpha\pm\tfrac{\pi}{2}) & -(b+\ell\sin\alpha)\cos(\alpha\pm\tfrac{\pi}{2}) + (a+\ell\cos\alpha)\sin(\alpha\pm\tfrac{\pi}{2}) \end{bmatrix}
$$

| Property | Mathematical Form | Interpretation |
|----------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Rank of $C_{cw}$** | $\text{rank}(C_{cw}\,R(\theta)) = 1$ | Only 1 independent constraint (rolling) |
| **Free DOF** | 1 swivel angle $\alpha(t)$ | Adapts to any motion |
| **Net nonholonomic constraints** | **0** | Truly passive element |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Alert:** The castor wheel still has **1 holonomic constraint** (it must stay on the ground). But the question of "nonholonomic constraints" refers only to *velocity-level* restrictions — and the castor adds **none**.

---

## 2.3 Degrees-of-Freedom Count (Campion's Framework)

Campion, Bastin, and D'Andréa-Novel (1996) classified wheels by:
$$
\delta_m = \text{maneuverability} = \text{rank of } J(q) \quad ; \quad \delta_s = \text{steerability}
$$
$$
\text{Net DOF} = 3 - (\text{nonholonomic constraints}) = \delta_m
$$

| Wheel Type | Local DOF | Nonholonomic Constraints | Contribution to $\delta_m$ |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Fixed Standard | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Steered Standard** | **2** | **1** | **1** |
| **Castor** | **2** | **0** | **2** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Steered standard wheels are used in **car-like robots** (Ackermann steering) and **forklifts**. Castor wheels are used as **passive stabilizers** in differential-drive robots (e.g., the rear wheels of a two-wheeled balancing robot, or the support wheels on a 4-wheeled platform).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Rolling & Sliding Constraints of a Steered Standard Wheel

> **Goal:** Starting from basic rigid-body kinematics, derive the two non-slip constraints for a wheel mounted at $(a, b)$ in the robot frame, with steering angle $\beta$.

### Step 1 — Define the Robot's State and the Contact Point's Position

The robot's pose in the world frame is $\xi_I = [x, y, \theta]^T$. The wheel's mounting point $P$ has coordinates $(a, b)$ in the robot frame. The position of the contact point $C$ in the world frame is:

$$
P_C = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + R(\theta)\begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $R(\theta)$ is the standard 2D rotation matrix.

### Step 2 — Compute the Velocity of the Contact Point (in the Robot Frame)

Using rigid-body kinematics, the velocity of point $C$ due to the robot's motion, expressed in the robot frame, is:

$$
v_{C \vert R} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}_R + \dot{\theta}\,\hat{k} \times \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} - b\dot{\theta} \\ \dot{y} + a\dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation of the cross product:** A 2D rotation $\dot\theta$ about $\hat{k}$ gives velocity $\dot\theta \cdot \hat k \times (a,b) = (-b\dot\theta,\, a\dot\theta)$.

### Step 3 — Transform the Velocity to the World Frame

We rotate by $R(\theta)$ to obtain the world-frame velocity:

$$
v_C = R(\theta)\,v_{C \vert R} = R(\theta)\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} - b\dot{\theta} \\ \dot{y} + a\dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

This can be written as a single Jacobian map:

$$
v_C = \underbrace{R(\theta)\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -b \\ 0 & 1 & a \end{bmatrix}}_{J(\theta)} \xi_I
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Wheel-Plane Basis Vectors (in the World Frame)

Let $\beta$ be the angle the wheel's rolling plane makes with the robot's $x$-axis. The two orthogonal directions are:

**Unit vector along the rolling plane (forward):**
$$
u = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\beta+\theta) \\ \sin(\beta+\theta) \end{bmatrix} = R(\theta)\begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta \\ \sin\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Unit vector perpendicular to the rolling plane (lateral):**
$$
n = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin(\beta+\theta) \\ \cos(\beta+\theta) \end{bmatrix} = R(\theta)\begin{bmatrix} -\sin\beta \\ \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Rolling-without-Slipping Condition

The component of the contact-point velocity **perpendicular** to the wheel plane must equal the rolling velocity $r\dot{\phi}$:

$$
n^T v_C = r\dot{\phi}
$$

Substituting $v_C = R(\theta) v_{C \vert R}$ and using $n = R(\theta) n_R$:

$$
n_R^T\,v_{C \vert R} = r\dot{\phi}
$$

Expanding with $n_R = [-\sin\beta, \cos\beta]^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[-\sin\beta, \cos\beta] \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} - b\dot{\theta} \\ \dot{y} + a\dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} &= r\dot{\phi} \\
-\sin\beta\,(\dot{x} - b\dot{\theta}) + \cos\beta\,(\dot{y} + a\dot{\theta}) &= r\dot{\phi} \\
-\sin\beta\,\dot{x} + b\sin\beta\,\dot{\theta} + \cos\beta\,\dot{y} + a\cos\beta\,\dot{\theta} &= r\dot{\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

Collecting terms into the robot-velocity vector $\xi = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$:

$$
\boxed{\;[-\sin\beta,\;\; \cos\beta,\;\; a\cos\beta + b\sin\beta]\,\xi = r\dot{\phi}\;}
$$

This is the **rolling constraint** of the steered standard wheel.

### Step 6 — Apply the No-Lateral-Slipping Condition

The component of the contact-point velocity **along** the wheel plane must be zero (lateral skidding is forbidden):

$$
u^T v_C = 0
$$

Following the same procedure with $u_R = [\cos\beta, \sin\beta]^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\cos\beta, \sin\beta] \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} - b\dot{\theta} \\ \dot{y} + a\dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} &= 0 \\
\cos\beta\,(\dot{x} - b\dot{\theta}) + \sin\beta\,(\dot{y} + a\dot{\theta}) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Collecting:

$$
\boxed{\;[\cos\beta,\;\; \sin\beta,\;\; -b\cos\beta + a\sin\beta]\,\xi = 0\;}
$$

This is the **sliding (no-skid) constraint** of the steered standard wheel.

### Step 7 — Compact Matrix Form

Stacking the two constraints:

$$
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -\sin\beta & \cos\beta & a\cos\beta + b\sin\beta \\ \cos\beta & \sin\beta & -b\cos\beta + a\sin\beta \end{bmatrix}}_{C_{ssw}(\beta)}\,R(\theta)\,\xi = \begin{bmatrix} r\dot{\phi} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Castor Wheel Constraint Analysis

> **Goal:** Show that a castor wheel imposes **zero** nonholonomic constraints on the robot.

### Step 1 — Castor Geometry

Let the castor's pivot pin be located at $(a, b)$ in the robot frame. The offset arm of length $\ell$ hangs from the pivot to the contact point. Let $\alpha$ be the angle of the offset arm with the robot $x$-axis. The contact point in the robot frame is:

$$
C_{castor} = \begin{bmatrix} a + \ell\cos\alpha \\ b + \ell\sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

The wheel's rolling direction is **perpendicular** to the offset arm, so:
$$
\beta = \alpha - \frac{\pi}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Sliding Constraint

Using the sliding-constraint formula derived above with the castor's contact point and rolling angle $\beta = \alpha - \pi/2$:

$$
[\cos\beta, \sin\beta, -(b+\ell\sin\alpha)\cos\beta + (a+\ell\cos\alpha)\sin\beta]\,\xi = 0
$$

Substituting $\beta = \alpha - \pi/2$, hence $\cos\beta = \sin\alpha$ and $\sin\beta = -\cos\alpha$:

$$
[\sin\alpha, -\cos\alpha, -(b+\ell\sin\alpha)\sin\alpha - (a+\ell\cos\alpha)\cos\alpha]\,\xi = 0
$$

The third component simplifies to:
$$
-(b+\ell\sin\alpha)\sin\alpha - (a+\ell\cos\alpha)\cos\alpha = -a\cos\alpha - b\sin\alpha - \ell(\cos^2\alpha + \sin^2\alpha) = -a\cos\alpha - b\sin\alpha - \ell
$$

So the sliding constraint is:

$$
\boxed{\;[\sin\alpha, -\cos\alpha, -a\cos\alpha - b\sin\alpha - \ell]\,\xi = 0\;}
$$

### Step 3 — The Decisive Observation

But the **pivot angle $\alpha$ is a free variable** that adjusts itself. The castor physically *rotates* to make this equation satisfied for any robot motion — it is not a controllable variable and not a constraint on the robot. Mathematically:

$$
\text{castor imposes} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{rolling equation as a free parameter}} + \underbrace{0}_{\text{sliding is absorbed by } \alpha(t)} = 0 \text{ nonholonomic constraints}
$$

The single rolling equation acts as a *definition* of the wheel's spin rate $\dot{\phi}$ (like an odometer), not as a constraint on the robot's motion.

### Step 4 — Comparison Table of Constraints

| Property | Steered Standard | Castor |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Free variables | $\beta(t)$ (steered), $\phi(t)$ (rolled) | $\alpha(t)$ (swivel), $\phi(t)$ (rolled) |
| Control inputs | 2 (steer motor + drive motor) | 0 (fully passive) |
| Nonholonomic constraints | **1** | **0** |
| Equations that involve the robot pose | 2 (rolling + sliding) | 1 (only rolling; sliding is absorbed by $\alpha$) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Wheel Mounted on the Robot's $x$-Axis

> **KTU Practice Problem:** A steered standard wheel is mounted at $(a, 0)$ on the robot's longitudinal axis, with steering angle $\beta$. Derive the simplified constraints.

**Solution:** Substituting $b = 0$ into the general formulas:

Rolling:
$$
[-\sin\beta, \cos\beta, a\cos\beta]\,\xi = r\dot{\phi}
$$

Sliding:
$$
[\cos\beta, \sin\beta, a\sin\beta]\,\xi = 0
$$

Dividing the sliding constraint by $\cos\beta$ (assuming $\beta \neq \pi/2$):

$$
\dot{x} + \dot{y}\tan\beta + a\dot{\theta}\tan\beta = 0
$$

For the special case $\beta = 0$ (wheel aligned with robot's $x$-axis):
- Rolling: $\dot{y} + a\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\phi}$
- Sliding: $\dot{x} = 0$ — i.e., **the wheel cannot move sideways relative to the robot's $x$-axis**, which is exactly the property of a steered wheel aligned with the chassis.

graph TB

        A5 --> A7[Wheel Tyre]

## 4.2 Kinematic Constraint Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Robot Pose x y theta] --> V[Robot Velocity xi = xdot ydot thetadot]
    V --> J[Frame Rotation R theta]
    J --> VP[Contact Point Velocity in World Frame]
    VP --> P1[Project along wheel plane u]
    VP --> P2[Project perpendicular to wheel plane n]
    P1 --> C1[Sliding Constraint equals zero for SSW]
    P2 --> C2[Rolling Constraint equals r phidot]
    C2 --> W1[Wheel spin rate phidot]

```

    Q --> R2[Wheel 2 SSW]

    R2 --> L2[Line L2 through contact C2]
    L1 --> ICR[Intersection gives ICR]

```

```

# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define a *steered standard wheel* and a *castor wheel*. State the number of nonholonomic constraints each imposes on a mobile robot.

**Model Answer:**

A **steered standard wheel** is a wheel mounted such that it can both *roll* about its horizontal axle and *steer* about a vertical axis that passes through the wheel's contact point with the ground. It contributes **2 degrees of freedom** (steer + roll) and imposes **1 nonholonomic constraint** (no lateral slipping at the contact point).

A **castor wheel** is a wheel whose vertical pivot (swivel) axis is **offset** from the contact point by a lever-arm of length $\ell$. It has **2 degrees of freedom** (roll + swivel) but imposes **0 nonholonomic constraints**, since the swivel angle adapts passively to the robot's motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Steered = Steering axis at the Spot (contact). Castor = Cast away from the contact (offset)."*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** With a neat sketch, distinguish between a *fixed standard wheel*, a *steered standard wheel*, and a *castor wheel* in terms of:
(i) degrees of freedom,
(ii) number of nonholonomic constraints, and
(iii) typical application in mobile robots.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fixed Standard | Steered Standard | Castor |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| **DOF** | 1 (roll only) | 2 (steer + roll) | 2 (swivel + roll) |
| **Nonholonomic constraints** | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| **Steering axis location** | None (rigid) | Through contact point | Offset by $\ell$ from contact |
| **Typical use** | Drive wheels in differential-drive robots | Car-like (Ackermann) steering | Passive support / balance wheel |

**Application examples:**
- *Fixed standard wheel* — Drive wheels of a differential-drive robot (e.g., the two rear wheels of a TurtleBot).
- *Steered standard wheel* — Front wheels of a car-like robot; the wheel pair is steered by an Ackermann linkage.
- *Castor wheel* — Front and rear support castors on a four-wheeled mobile platform; balance wheel on a two-wheeled Segway-type robot.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Steered Standard Wheel Focus

> **Q.A.** **(a)** A steered standard wheel is mounted at coordinates $(a, b)$ in the robot frame. The steering angle is $\beta$ and the wheel radius is $r$. **Derive the rolling-without-slipping and the no-lateral-slipping constraints** for this wheel. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
