> **(b)** For a **car-like robot** with two steered standard wheels on the rear axle and two fixed standard wheels on the front axle, set up the complete set of nonholonomic constraints. Discuss how the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is determined, and explain the role of the **Ackermann steering geometry**. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1:** Define the contact point in the robot frame as $(a, b)$. The velocity of the contact point in the robot frame due to robot motion $\xi = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$ is:

$$
v_{C \vert R} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} - b\dot{\theta} \\ \dot{y} + a\dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Define wheel-frame unit vectors in the robot frame:
- $u_R = [\cos\beta, \sin\beta]^T$ (rolling direction)
- $n_R = [-\sin\beta, \cos\beta]^T$ (perpendicular to wheel plane)

**Step 3:** Rolling constraint (perpendicular velocity $\equiv r\dot{\phi}$):

$$
n_R^T v_{C \vert R} = r\dot{\phi}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow [-\sin\beta, \cos\beta, a\cos\beta + b\sin\beta]\,\xi = r\dot{\phi} \quad \cdots (1)
$$

**Step 4:** Sliding constraint (parallel velocity $\equiv 0$):

$$
u_R^T v_{C \vert R} = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow [\cos\beta, \sin\beta, -b\cos\beta + a\sin\beta]\,\xi = 0 \quad \cdots (2)
$$

**Step 5:** Combining (1) and (2) in matrix form:

$$
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -\sin\beta & \cos\beta & a\cos\beta + b\sin\beta \\ \cos\beta & \sin\beta & -b\cos\beta + a\sin\beta \end{bmatrix}}_{C_{ssw}(\beta)} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r\dot{\phi} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **complete kinematic model** of the steered standard wheel.

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Constraint setup for a 4-wheel car-like robot:**

Let the rear axle be at $(x_R, y_R, \theta)$ with wheelbase $L$ and track $T$. The two front steered wheels are at positions:

$$
\text{Left front: } (a, b) = (L, T/2), \quad \text{Right front: } (a, b) = (L, -T/2)
$$

Both have the same steering angle $\beta$ (approximately, in Ackermann geometry).

**Step 2 — Per-wheel constraints:**

Each front wheel contributes 1 nonholonomic constraint (no lateral slip). So we have 2 sliding constraints for the front pair:

$$
[\cos\beta, \sin\beta, L\sin\beta \pm (T/2)\cos\beta]\,\xi = 0
$$

The two rear fixed wheels ($\beta = 0$, mount at $(0, \pm T/2)$) give:

$$
[1, 0, \mp T/2]\,\xi = 0
$$

**Step 3 — ICR determination:**

Each rolling-without-slipping constraint requires the ICR to lie on a line through the contact point, perpendicular to the wheel plane. For the car, these lines for all four wheels must **intersect at a single point** (the ICR). The geometry forces the front inner wheel to steer at a **larger angle** than the outer wheel — this is the **Ackermann condition**:

$$
\cot\beta_{\text{inner}} - \cot\beta_{\text{outer}} = \frac{T}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Role of Ackermann steering:**

Ackermann geometry ensures that *all four wheels rotate about a common ICR*, eliminating tire scrub. It is a **geometric compensation** for the fact that the inner and outer wheels travel on circles of different radii during a turn.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often forget that the **rolling constraint** (1) is *not* a nonholonomic constraint on the robot's motion — it merely defines the wheel spin rate $\dot{\phi}$. Only the **sliding constraint** (2) restricts the robot's velocity space. Each steered standard wheel thus adds **exactly one** nonholonomic constraint, not two.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Castor Wheel Focus (Alternative Choice)

> **Q.B.** **(a)** With a clear diagram, describe the geometry of a **castor wheel**. Define the lever-arm length $\ell$ and the pivot angle $\alpha$. **Derive the constraint equations** for a castor wheel mounted at $(a, b)$ in the robot frame, and show that the **sliding constraint is absorbed by the free pivot angle** $\alpha(t)$. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A two-wheeled differential-drive robot uses **two fixed standard drive wheels** and **one castor wheel** as a passive front support. Write down the **complete set of nonholonomic constraints** for this configuration. Show that the robot's **maneuverability index** $\delta_m = 2$ and discuss its controllability. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Geometry:**

The castor has a vertical pivot pin located at $(a, b)$ in the robot frame. From this pivot, a rigid offset arm of length $\ell$ extends to the contact point $C$ on the ground. The arm makes an angle $\alpha$ with the robot's $x$-axis. The contact point in the robot frame is:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} a + \ell\cos\alpha \\ b + \ell\sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling direction:**

The wheel rolls **perpendicular** to the offset arm, so its rolling-direction angle is $\beta = \alpha - \pi/2$. Substituting this into the generic sliding-constraint formula:

$$
[\cos\beta, \sin\beta, -b'\cos\beta + a'\sin\beta]\,\xi = 0
$$

where $a' = a + \ell\cos\alpha$ and $b' = b + \ell\sin\alpha$.

**Step 3 — Simplification:**

Using $\cos(\alpha - \pi/2) = \sin\alpha$ and $\sin(\alpha - \pi/2) = -\cos\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&[\sin\alpha, -\cos\alpha, -(b + \ell\sin\alpha)\sin\alpha - (a + \ell\cos\alpha)\cos\alpha]\,\xi = 0 \\
\Rightarrow\;&[\sin\alpha, -\cos\alpha, -a\cos\alpha - b\sin\alpha - \ell(\sin^2\alpha + \cos^2\alpha)]\,\xi = 0 \\
\Rightarrow\;&[\sin\alpha, -\cos\alpha, -a\cos\alpha - b\sin\alpha - \ell]\,\xi = 0 \quad \cdots (*)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Why this is NOT a constraint on the robot:**

The pivot angle $\alpha$ is **not controlled** — it adjusts itself so that equation $(*)$ is automatically satisfied. Rearranging $(*)$ as:

$$
\alpha(t) = \arctan\!\left( \frac{\dot{y} - \ell\dot{\theta}}{-\dot{x} + (a + \ell\cos\alpha)\dot{\theta}} \right)
$$

shows that for **any** robot velocity $[\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$, the castor can find an $\alpha(t)$ that makes $(*)$ hold. Hence **the castor adds 0 nonholonomic constraints**.

**Step 5 — Rolling equation (only a definition of $\dot{\phi}$):**

The rolling-constraint equation for the castor is:

$$
[-\cos\alpha, -\sin\alpha, (\text{expression in } a, b, \ell, \alpha)]\,\xi = r\dot{\phi}
$$

This equation **defines** the wheel's spin rate — it is not a constraint on the robot.

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Robot configuration:**

A differential-drive robot has:
- **Two fixed standard drive wheels** at $(0, d)$ and $(0, -d)$, both with $\beta = 0$.
- **One castor wheel** at $(L, 0)$ as a passive front support.

**Step 2 — Constraints from the two drive wheels:**

Each fixed wheel ($\beta = 0$, position $(0, \pm d)$) gives one sliding constraint:

$$
\text{Left wheel: } [1, 0, -d]\,\xi = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{x} - d\dot{\theta} = 0 \quad \cdots (1)
$$

$$
\text{Right wheel: } [1, 0, d]\,\xi = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{x} + d\dot{\theta} = 0 \quad \cdots (2)
$$

**Step 3 — Castor constraint:**

The castor adds **0** nonholonomic constraints (as proved in part (a)).

**Step 4 — Total constraint count:**

Total sliding constraints: **2** (one per drive wheel). Total nonholonomic constraints on the robot's 3-DOF motion: **2**.

**Step 5 — Maneuverability index:**

$$
\delta_m = 3 - (\text{nonholonomic constraints}) = 3 - 2 = 2
$$

This means the robot has **2 controllable DOF** (forward velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$), which is exactly what a differential-drive robot offers.

**Step 6 — Controllability:**

The system is **locally controllable** (it satisfies the Lie algebra rank condition via Brockett's theorem for nonholonomic systems). However, it **cannot move sideways** (no $\dot{y}$ command), which is the classic limitation of differential-drive robots — and the reason researchers add Swedish or caster-omni wheels for holonomic motion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (5-mark loss zone):** Students often write that the castor adds a constraint and then fail to show *why* it is automatically satisfied by the free pivot $\alpha(t)$. You must explicitly argue that the castor can absorb any lateral motion by rotating its swivel — that is the geometric essence of "0 nonholonomic constraints."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Steered Standard & Castor Wheels**

- ☐ A **steered standard wheel** has 2 DOF (steer + roll) and 1 nonholonomic constraint (no lateral slip).
- ☐ The **steering axis of an SSW passes through the contact point** — this is the geometric signature.
- ☐ A **castor wheel** has 2 DOF (swivel + roll) and **0 nonholonomic constraints**.
- ☐ The **steering axis of a castor is offset by a lever-arm $\ell$ from the contact point** — this is what frees it from the lateral-slip constraint.
- ☐ Rolling constraint (perpendicular to wheel): defines the wheel spin rate $\dot\phi$ via $r\dot\phi$.
- ☐ Sliding constraint (parallel to wheel): is the actual nonholonomic constraint.
- ☐ Constraint matrix $C_{ssw}(\beta)$ is a $2 \times 3$ matrix premultiplying $R(\theta)\xi$.
- ☐ For an SSW, the ICR must lie on a line through the contact, perpendicular to the wheel plane.
- ☐ For a castor, the ICR can be anywhere (no constraint on direction).
- ☐ **Ackermann condition**: $\cot\beta_{\text{inner}} - \cot\beta_{\text{outer}} = T/L$ — required for all four wheels to share a common ICR.
- ☐ A castor wheel contributes the **same maneuverability** as a Swedish wheel (both add 2 to $\delta_m$).
- ☐ **Differential-drive robots** (2 fixed wheels + 1 castor) have $\delta_m = 2$ — they can control $(v, \omega)$ but not lateral velocity.
- ☐ In the sliding-constraint equation, **never confuse** the third element $a\sin\beta - b\cos\beta$ with the rolling one $a\cos\beta + b\sin\beta$ — a classic valuation trap.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"Standard wheels slide sideways if not steered, castor wheels just spin to dodge the problem."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Kinematic Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Kinematic Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Mobile Robotics**, a *kinematic constraint* is a mathematical restriction imposed on the *configuration* or *velocity* of a robot arising from its mechanical design, wheel arrangement, or contact with the environment. Formally, for a mobile robot with generalized coordinates $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$, a kinematic constraint restricts the admissible motions of $q$ (or its time derivative $\dot{q}$) to a sub-manifold of the configuration space.

Kinematic constraints are generally expressed in two canonical forms:

1. **Holonomic (Configuration) Constraints:** A restriction on the configuration itself,
$$h(q) = 0$$
2. **Nonholonomic (Velocity / Pfaffian) Constraints:** A restriction on the velocity that *cannot* be integrated to a position-level equation,
$$A(q)\,\dot{q} = 0$$

where $A(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ is the *constraint matrix* (also called the *Pfaffian matrix*) and $k$ is the number of independent constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PBRAT604 Module 1**
> Kinematic constraints form the *foundational* block of mobile robot modelling. Without them, the differential equations of motion cannot be written, and consequently control, path-planning, and localization are not possible.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Car in a Parking Lot**
> Imagine you are driving a **car** versus a **person walking** in a two-dimensional parking lot.
> - The **person** can move in *any* direction instantaneously — sideways, diagonally, or rotate in place. The motion is unconstrained; the parking lot is the *configuration space* and the person can reach any pose.
> - The **car** cannot move sideways. If the steering wheel is locked, the car must move along the axis of its body. Even with the steering free, the car *cannot* achieve an arbitrary pose without first reorienting.
>
> This difference is exactly what **kinematic constraints** capture. The car's admissible velocities form a *subset* of all possible velocities. The mathematical statement of "the car cannot translate sideways" is a nonholonomic constraint.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** A robot with $n$ generalized coordinates and $k$ independent kinematic constraints has its motion restricted to an $(n-k)$ dimensional manifold. The *number* and *type* of constraints determine whether the robot is **omnidirectional** (no nonholonomic constraints) or **car-like / differential-drive** (one or more nonholonomic constraints).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The **degree of mobility** $\delta_m$ = number of independent velocity constraints that restrict motion.
- The **degree of steerability** $\delta_s$ = number of independently steered wheels.
- The **degree of maneuverability** (Maneuverability Index by Campion et al., 1996):
$$M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$
- A robot is said to be **fully omnidirectional** if $M = 3$ (can achieve any planar velocity instantaneously).
- The **Manhattan distance** metric and **configuration space obstacle** notions rely on holonomic constraints for their mathematical formulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Kinematic Constraints

Kinematic constraints are classified along two axes:

**(A) Holonomic vs. Nonholonomic**

| Type | Mathematical Form | Integrable? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Holonomic | $h(q) = 0$ | Trivially integrable | Robot arm joint limit; omnidirectional wheel slip-free condition |
| Nonholonomic | $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ | **NOT** integrable to a finite position relation | Car sideways-slip constraint; unicycle rotation-slip |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Frobenius Theorem Test:** A Pfaffian constraint $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ is *holonomic* if and only if the distribution defined by the null-space of $A(q)$ is **involutive (closed under Lie bracket)**. For the unicycle and car, this fails → the constraints are nonholonomic.

**(B) Scleronomic vs. Rheonomic**

- **Scleronomic:** Constraint does not depend explicitly on time.
- **Rheonomic:** Constraint depends explicitly on time (e.g., a moving conveyor belt).

For most ground mobile robots, only **scleronomic, nonholonomic** constraints are considered.

### 2.2 Pfaffian Form and Driftless Systems

When all nonholonomic constraints are *linear in velocity* and *autonomous in time*, the system can be written as a **driftless control-affine system**:

$$\dot{q} = G(q)\,u$$

where:
- $G(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ is the *input matrix* whose columns span the null-space of $A(q)$.
- $u \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the *control input vector* (e.g., wheel angular velocities).
- The condition $A(q)G(q) = 0$ guarantees that all admissible velocities are tangent to the constraint manifold.

### 2.3 Constraint Matrices for Canonical Mobile Robots

**(a) Unicycle / Differential-Drive Robot**

Generalized coordinates: $q = (x, y, \theta)^{T}$.

The robot cannot move sideways → the lateral velocity must be zero:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

Pfaffian form:

$$A(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A(q)\dot{q} = 0$$

Driftless form (controls $u = (v, \omega)$):

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**(b) Car-Like (Ackermann) Robot**

Generalized coordinates: $q = (x, y, \theta, \phi)^{T}$, where $\phi$ is the steering angle.

Two Pfaffian constraints:
1. No side-slip: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$
2. Steering geometry: $\dot{\phi} = \dot{\theta}\tan\phi$ (or equivalently $\dot{\theta}\cos\phi - \dot{\phi} = 0$ … see derivation below).

**(c) Omnidirectional Robot (Swedish / Mecanum wheels)**

- Has *zero* nonholonomic constraints → can translate and rotate independently.
- $A(q)$ is the *empty* matrix; $G(q) = I_3$ (identity); $M = 3$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table for any numerical / theoretical question on Module 1.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Holonomic constraint | $h(q) = 0$ | Configuration level |
| 2 | Pfaffian / nonholonomic | $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ | Velocity level |
| 3 | Driftless system | $\dot{q} = G(q)u$ | $G \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| 4 | Null-space condition | $A(q)G(q) = 0$ | Admissibility |
| 5 | Mobility | $\delta_m$ | # velocity restrictions |
| 6 | Steerability | $\delta_s$ | # steerable wheels |
| 7 | Maneuverability | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | $0 \le M \le 3$ |
| 8 | Unicycle lateral slip | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Single Pfaffian |
| 9 | Ackermann #1 | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | No side-slip |
| 10 | Ackermann #2 | $\dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\phi}{L}$ | Steering geometry |
| 11 | Differentially flat outputs | $(x, y)$ for unicycle / car | Used in trajectory planning |
| 12 | Frobenius integrability | $[\nabla h_1, \nabla h_2] \in \text{span}\{\nabla h_1, \nabla h_2\}$ | Holonomy test |

> [!WARNING]
> **Marks Alert:** In the KTU valuation key, the *null-space condition* $A(q)G(q) = 0$ carries **at least 2 marks** whenever a driftless form is asked. Never omit it.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Ackermann constraints dictate that a self-driving car cannot change lane instantly; trajectory planners must use these as hard equality constraints.
- **AGVs in Warehouses:** Differential-drive robots (e.g., Kiva / Amazon Robotics) rely on nonholonomic constraints; control laws use **feedback linearization** or **Lyapunov-based** designs to overcome Brockett's obstruction.
- **Robot Soccer (RoboCup):** Omnidirectional robots exploit $M = 3$ to dribble and rotate in tight spaces.
- **Space Rovers:** Rocky planets require careful modelling of wheel-soil interaction, often adding extra nonholonomic slip constraints.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Dominated by 6-DOF nonholonomic constraints from hydrodynamics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Unicycle Kinematic Constraint

> **Goal:** Show that the *no side-slip* condition of a unicycle is a single Pfaffian constraint.

**Step 1 — Define the World Frame and Body Frame.**

Let the world frame be $\mathcal{W} = \{O_W, \hat{x}_W, \hat{y}_W\}$ and the body frame be $\mathcal{B} = \{O_B, \hat{x}_B, \hat{y}_B\}$, with the heading angle $\theta$ measured from $\hat{x}_W$ to $\hat{x}_B$ counter-clockwise. The pose in the world frame is $q = (x, y, \theta)^{T}$.

**Step 2 — Express the Robot's Linear Velocity in the World Frame.**

The linear velocity of the body center in the world frame is the body-frame velocity rotated by $R_z(\theta)$:

$$^{W}v = R_z(\theta)\,^{B}v = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

Here we set the body-frame lateral velocity $^{B}v_y = 0$ because a unicycle cannot translate sideways — this is the *kinematic assumption*.

**Step 3 — Write the Position-Level Kinematics.**

Taking the time derivative of the position, we get:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate the Control $v$ to Obtain a Pure Constraint.**

Multiply the first equation by $\sin\theta$, the second by $\cos\theta$, and subtract:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}\sin\theta &= v\cos\theta\sin\theta \\
\dot{y}\cos\theta &= v\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta &= v\cos\theta\sin\theta - v\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Pfaffian (nonholonomic) constraint** for the unicycle.

**Step 5 — Write in Standard Matrix Form.**

$$
A(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$.

**Step 6 — Construct the Driftless System by Spanning the Null-Space.**

The null-space of $A(q)$ is the set of all $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$ satisfying the constraint. Choosing basis vectors $g_1, g_2$:

$$g_1(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad g_2(q) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Verify the admissibility:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(q)g_1 &= \sin\theta\cos\theta - \cos\theta\sin\theta + 0 = 0 \\
A(q)g_2 &= 0 - 0 + 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So $G(q) = [g_1, g_2]$ and the driftless form $\dot{q} = G(q)u$ follows with $u = (v, \omega)^{T}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Ackermann (Car-Like) Constraints

**Step 1 — Generalized Coordinates.**

Let $q = (x, y, \theta, \phi)^{T}$ where $\phi$ is the steering angle. The wheelbase is $L$.

**Step 2 — Constraint #1: No Side-Slip (Same as Unicycle).**

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Constraint #2: Ackermann Steering Geometry.**

The center of rotation of the car is on the line through the rear axle perpendicular to the rear wheels. The instantaneous radius of curvature is $R = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}$. Therefore:

$$
\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{v\tan\phi}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Pfaffian Form.**

Using $v = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta$ (the projection of velocity along the heading), we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\theta}\,L &= (\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta)\tan\phi \\
L\dot{\theta}\cos\phi &= (\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta)\sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging into a single Pfaffian form by introducing $\phi$ dynamics:

$$
\dot{x}\cos\theta\sin\phi + \dot{y}\sin\theta\sin\phi - L\dot{\theta}\cos\phi = 0
$$

So the second row of $A(q)$ is $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta\sin\phi & \sin\theta\sin\phi & -L\cos\phi & 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 5 — Driftless Form with Two Controls $u = (v, \phi_{ref})$ (or $(v, \dot{\phi})$).**

$$
G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ \frac{\tan\phi}{L} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A(q)G(q) = 0
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Robot Type | # Generalized Coords $n$ | # Nonholonomic Constraints $k$ | $m = n - k$ (Controls) | DOF in Motion | Maneuverability $M$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unicycle | 3 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Differential Drive | 3 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Car (Ackermann) | 4 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Tricycle | 4 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Omnidirectional (Mecanum) | 3 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| Point Mass (no robot) | 3 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 3 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Kinematic Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Kinematic Constraints"] --> nodeB["Time Dependence"]
    nodeA["Kinematic Constraints"] --> nodeC["Integrability"]
    nodeA["Kinematic Constraints"] --> nodeD["Domain"]
    nodeB["Time Dependence"] --> nodeB1["Scleronomic: no explicit time"]
    nodeB["Time Dependence"] --> nodeB2["Rheonomic: explicit time"]
    nodeC["Integrability"] --> nodeC1["Holonomic: h of q equals 0"]
    nodeC["Integrability"] --> nodeC2["Nonholonomic: A of q times qdot equals 0"]
    nodeC2["Nonholonomic: A of q times qdot equals 0"] --> nodeC2a["Pfaffian Form"]
    nodeC2["Nonholonomic: A of q times qdot equals 0"] --> nodeC2b["Fails Frobenius Test"]
    nodeD["Domain"] --> nodeD1["Configuration Level: h of q equals 0"]
    nodeD["Domain"] --> nodeD2["Velocity Level: A of q times qdot equals 0"]
    nodeC1["Holonomic: h of q equals 0"] --> nodeC1a["Example: Robot Arm Joint Limit"]
    nodeC2a["Pfaffian Form"] --> nodeC2a1["Unicycle: side slip"]
    nodeC2a["Pfaffian Form"] --> nodeC2a2["Car Ackermann: two constraints"]
    nodeC2a["Pfaffian Form"] --> nodeC2a3["Differential Drive: one constraint"]
    nodeC2a["Pfaffian Form"] --> nodeC2a4["Omnidirectional: zero constraints"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mobile Robot Motion Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1["Reference Inputs u: v and omega"] --> blkA["Input Matrix G of q"]
    cfg["Generalized Coordinates q: x, y, theta"] --> blkA["Input Matrix G of q"]
    blkA["Input Matrix G of q"] --> blkB["Driftless Kinematic Equation qdot equals G of q times u"]
    blkB["Driftless Kinematic Equation qdot equals G of q times u"] --> blkC["Constraint Checker A of q times qdot equals 0"]
    blkC["Constraint Checker A of q times qdot equals 0"] --> out1["Pose Update q new"]
    blkC["Constraint Checker A of q times qdot equals 0"] --> out2["Feasibility Flag"]
    out1["Pose Update q new"] --> cfg["Generalized Coordinates q: x, y, theta"]
    out2["Feasibility Flag"] --> fbk1["Feedback to Planner"]
    fbk1["Feedback to Planner"] --> in1["Reference Inputs u: v and omega"]
```

### 4.3 Constraint-to-Robot Mapping Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeX["Constraint Type"] --> nodeY["Robot Class"]
    nodeY["Robot Class"] --> nodeZ["Application Domain"]
    nodeX["Constraint Type"] --> nodeX1["No Nonholonomic"]
    nodeX["Constraint Type"] --> nodeX2["One Nonholonomic"]
    nodeX["Constraint Type"] --> nodeX3["Two Nonholonomic"]
    nodeX1["No Nonholonomic"] --> nodeY1["Omnidirectional Mecanum"]
    nodeX2["One Nonholonomic"] --> nodeY2["Unicycle and Differential Drive"]
    nodeX3["Two Nonholonomic"] --> nodeY3["Ackermann Car Like"]
    nodeY1["Omnidirectional Mecanum"] --> nodeZ1["RoboCup and Warehouse Picking"]
    nodeY2["Unicycle and Differential Drive"] --> nodeZ2["Indoor AGV and Research Platforms"]
    nodeY3["Ackermann Car Like"] --> nodeZ3["Autonomous Cars and Outdoor Navigation"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between holonomic and nonholonomic constraints with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Holonomic constraints** are expressed purely in terms of the configuration variables, of the form $h(q) = 0$, and are *integrable*. They reduce the dimension of the *configuration space*. *Example:* A robot arm joint that is mechanically locked at angle $\theta_1 = \pi/4$.
- **Nonholonomic constraints** are expressed as linear relations in the *velocities*, of the form $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$, and are *not integrable* to a finite position equation. They reduce the *admissible instantaneous velocities* but not the reachable configurations. *Example:* The unicycle's no-side-slip condition $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- **Marking scheme:** Definition holonomic (1), definition nonholonomic (1), one example each (1). **(Total: 3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the Frobenius integrability condition. Why does the unicycle constraint fail it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A Pfaffian system $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ is holonomic if and only if the *distribution* $\mathcal{D} = \ker A(q)$ is **involutive**, i.e., closed under the Lie bracket. Equivalently, the span of the gradients $\nabla h_i$ must be preserved by the Lie bracket of any two such gradients.
- For the unicycle, $\mathcal{D} = \text{span}\{\partial_x, \partial_\theta\}$ when evaluated at $\theta = 0$ does not contain $\partial_y$, and the Lie bracket of the two vector fields spanning the distribution does not lie in the distribution.
- Hence the distribution is **not involutive** → the unicycle constraint is **nonholonomic**. **(Total: 3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the Pfaffian nonholonomic constraint for a unicycle-type mobile robot. Show that the driftless form of the kinematic equation can be written using the input matrix $G(q)$ that spans the null-space of $A(q)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a car-like robot with wheelbase $L$ and steering angle $\phi$, write down the two Pfaffian constraints and the corresponding driftless kinematic equation. Compute the maneuverability index $M$ and justify the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Unicycle Derivation (7 marks)**

1. Generalized coordinates $q = (x, y, \theta)^{T}$. **(1 mark)**
2. The body-frame lateral velocity is zero (no side-slip). Rotating the body velocity into the world frame gives $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$. **(2 marks)**
3. Eliminating $v$ by $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. **(2 marks)**
4. Writing $A(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}$. Null-space basis: $g_1 = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)^{T}$, $g_2 = (0, 0, 1)^{T}$. Verify $A(q)G(q) = 0$. **(2 marks)**

**(b) — Car-Like Robot (7 marks)**

1. $q = (x, y, \theta, \phi)^{T}$. Two Pfaffian constraints: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ and the steering geometry constraint. **(2 marks)**
2. Steering constraint derivation: $\dot{\theta} = v\tan\phi / L$. Expressing with $v = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta$ yields the second Pfaffian row. **(2 marks)**
3. Driftless form $\dot{q} = G(q)u$ with $u = (v, \dot{\phi})^{T}$ and $G(q)$ a $4 \times 2$ matrix with rows $\cos\theta, \sin\theta, \tan\phi/L, 0$ and $0, 0, 0, 1$. **(1 mark)**
4. Maneuverability: $\delta_m = 2$ (cannot move sideways, cannot translate and rotate independently about a fixed point), $\delta_s = 0$ (Ackermann has no separately steerable wheel in the Campion sense for the simple model), $M = 2$. Justification: car cannot rotate in place or translate sideways. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Alternative]**

**(a)** Define the *maneuverability index* $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ as introduced by Campion, Bastin, and d'Andréa-Novel. For each of the following robots, compute $M$ and state the number of independent Pfaffian constraints: (i) differential drive, (ii) tricycle, (iii) omnidirectional with four Mecanum wheels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that the Pfaffian constraint of a unicycle is *nonholonomic* by demonstrating that any attempt to integrate it to a finite configuration relation leads to a contradiction. Hence explain why a unicycle can still reach any pose in the plane despite the constraint. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Maneuverability Index (7 marks)**

The maneuverability index $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ captures the total degrees of freedom in motion achievable by a mobile robot.

1. **Differential drive:** Two independently driven wheels on a common axle, one passive caster. $\delta_m = 2$ (it has one nonholonomic constraint, leaving 2 admissible velocities). $\delta_s = 0$. Hence $M = 2$. **(2 marks)**
2. **Tricycle:** Two passive rear wheels, one steerable + driven front wheel. The steering introduces an extra velocity constraint, so $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 1$. Hence $M = 3$. **(2 marks)** *(Note: depending on convention, simple tricycles have $M=2$ if the steerable wheel is not counted; the KTU key accepts either with justification.)*
3. **Omnidirectional (Mecanum):** Has zero nonholonomic constraints and three independently driven axes. $\delta_m = 3$, $\delta_s = 0$. Hence $M = 3$ (fully omnidirectional). **(2 marks)**
4. **Summary statement:** Only robots with $M = 3$ are *fully* omnidirectional. **(1 mark)**

**(b) — Non-Integrability Demonstration (7 marks)**

1. Attempt to integrate $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. **(1 mark)**
2. Rewrite as $\frac{dy}{dx} = \tan\theta$. Treating $\theta$ as a function of $x$ (or equivalently, trying to express $y$ as $f(x)$) yields $y = \int \tan\theta(x)\,dx$, but $\theta(x)$ depends on the path taken, not on the final position. **(2 marks)**
3. Therefore the equation cannot be reduced to a *finite* relation $h(x, y, \theta) = 0$ that holds identically. A counter-example: two different trajectories with the same start and end points can give different accumulated values of $y - \int \tan\theta\,dx$. **(2 marks)**
4. **Conclusion:** The constraint is nonholonomic. However, by **Chow's theorem**, since the Lie algebra generated by $\{g_1, g_2\}$ spans $\mathbb{R}^3$ at every point, the system is *controllable* — the unicycle can reach *any* $(x, y, \theta)$ in the plane. This is the famous *Brockett example* where reachability holds despite nonholonomy. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the null-space condition $A(q)G(q) = 0$** — costs 1–2 marks in any driftless-form derivation.
> 2. **Confusing *degree of mobility* with *maneuverability index*** — $\delta_m$ counts *restricted* motions; $M$ is the *additive* sum.
> 3. **Claiming nonholonomic = uncontrollable** — this is the most frequent conceptual error. Always cite **Chow–Rashevskii theorem** to support the claim that *all* nonholonomic systems studied in this module are controllable.
> 4. **Mixing the Pfaffian form for unicycle and differential drive** — although the *constraint* is identical, the *control inputs* differ. For differential drive, $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ and $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/b$, where $b$ is the axle length.
> 5. **Omitting the dependence on time** when classifying scleronomic vs. rheonomic constraints — examiners specifically look for the *autonomous* (scleronomic) qualifier.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Kinematic constraints** restrict either the **configuration** (holonomic) or the **velocity** (nonholonomic/Pfaffian) of a mobile robot.
- The **Pfaffian form** $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ is the canonical velocity-level representation of a nonholonomic constraint.
- The **driftless system** $\dot{q} = G(q)u$ requires the **admissibility condition** $A(q)G(q) = 0$.
- The **unicycle constraint** is $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$, giving the input matrix
$$G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.$$
- The **Ackermann (car-like) robot** has **two** nonholonomic constraints, both relating to lateral slip and steering geometry.
- The **maneuverability index** $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ classifies robots as non-holonomic ($M < 3$) or omnidirectional ($M = 3$).
- **Frobenius theorem** is the mathematical *test* for holonomy: if the constraint distribution is involutive, the constraint is holonomic; otherwise nonholonomic.
- The **unicycle** is **nonholonomic** but **controllable** (Chow's theorem) — a foundational result exploited in trajectory planning.
- **Holonomic constraints** restrict reachable configurations; **nonholonomic** ones only restrict instantaneous velocities.
- For KTU problems, **always verify** $A(q)G(q) = 0$ and state the **null-space basis** explicitly.
- **Degrees of freedom** in motion for a constrained system = $n - \text{rank}(A(q))$ where $A(q)$ is the Pfaffian matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example of Robot Kinematic Models and Constraints: Differential-Drive Robot Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Example of Robot Kinematic Models and Constraints: Differential-Drive Robot Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Differential-Drive Robot (DDR)** is a non-holonomic wheeled mobile robot whose locomotion is achieved by two independently actuated coaxial drive wheels mounted on a common axle, supplemented by one or more passive caster wheels for static balance. By commanding *different angular velocities* to the left and right wheels, the robot can achieve pure translation, pure rotation, or any *combined curvilinear motion* — all within the plane of motion.

In the formal KTU framework, a differential-drive robot belongs to the class **(2, 0)** wheel-type vehicles (two fixed standard wheels, zero steered wheels) under the Campion-Bastin-Gorges classification of unicycle-type mobile robots. Its **configuration / posture** is completely described by the state vector:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$$

where $(x, y)$ is the position of the reference point (typically the midpoint between the two drive wheels) in the world (inertial) frame, and $\theta$ is the **heading angle** measured counter-clockwise from the global X-axis to the robot's local X-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A differential-drive robot has **3 configuration variables** but only **2 controllable degrees of freedom (DOF)**. This mismatch gives rise to a *non-holonomic constraint*, which is the single most important concept tested in this module.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in an **office chair with wheels** on a smooth floor. If both your hands push the floor backward with **equal force**, you glide straight forward. If you push **harder on the right side** than the left, the chair swerves left because the right wheel rotates faster. If you push them in *opposite directions* with equal force, the chair **spins in place** like a top, even though your body's center point never translates.

A differential-drive robot is *exactly* this chair — except the "pushing" is done by two precise DC motors on rigid wheels. The intelligence lies in *coordinating* the two motor speeds to trace any desired path (a circle, a figure-8, a straight corridor).

| Physical Quantity | Everyday Equivalent | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel radius $r$ | Size of the wheel | **meters (m)** |
| Wheelbase $L$ (or $2l$) | Distance between your two hands | **meters (m)** |
| Wheel angular velocity $\omega_R, \omega_L$ | Speed of pushing | **rad/s** |
| Robot linear velocity $v$ | Forward glide speed | **m/s** |
| Robot angular velocity $\omega$ | Rate of chair rotation | **rad/s** |
| Sampling time $T_s$ | Reaction time of the brain | **seconds (s)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $L$ is widely used to denote the *distance between the two wheel contact points* (the wheelbase). Some textbooks use $2l$ or $d$. All three refer to the same geometric quantity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometry and Assumptions

The standard differential-drive kinematic model is derived under the following idealizations:

1. The robot moves on a **flat, horizontal 2D plane** (no ramps, no obstacles).
2. The drive wheels are **rigid (non-deformable)** and maintain **point contact** with the ground.
3. The wheels satisfy the **pure rolling without slipping** condition — at every instant, the velocity of the wheel contact point along the wheel's plane is zero.
4. The **steering axis** of the two drive wheels is the same line — they are coaxial.
5. The mechanical response of the motors is *instantaneous* (no actuator dynamics) — this is a **kinematic** model, not dynamic.

### 2.2 Wheel Constraints (Why the Robot Cannot Move Sideways)

Each drive wheel imposes **two constraints** on the robot body's motion at its contact point:

- **Rolling constraint**: The component of the contact-point velocity *along* the wheel's plane must equal $r \cdot \omega_{wheel}$.
- **Sliding (no-side-slip) constraint**: The component of the contact-point velocity *perpendicular* to the wheel's plane must be zero (the wheel cannot skid sideways).

For a single standard fixed wheel, these reduce to the Pfaffian form:

$$J_1(\beta) \cdot \dot{q} = 0$$

where $\beta$ is the steering angle of the wheel and $J_1$ is a $1 \times 3$ constraint matrix. For a non-steerable (fixed) wheel, $\beta$ is constant, and the constraint simplifies to a relationship between the body velocity and the wheel's spin rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total degrees of mobility of a DDR equals $\delta_m = 2$, meaning **only two independent velocity commands** can be issued — the linear velocity $v$ and the angular velocity $\omega$. This is why a DDR *cannot* translate sideways: a sideways command would require a third independent velocity, which the geometry forbids.

### 2.3 The Non-Holonomic Constraint Equation

Combining the two wheel constraints and eliminating the wheel spin rates, the DDR obeys the celebrated **non-integrable (non-holonomic) constraint**:

$$\dot{x} \sin\theta - \dot{y} \cos\theta = 0$$

This is a **first-order linear constraint on the velocities** that *cannot* be integrated to give a finite relationship among $x, y, \theta$. The robot's *local* configuration space looks 2-dimensional, but its *global reachable set* is constrained — this is the defining property of non-holonomy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A constraint is *holonomic* if it reduces the accessible configuration space. A constraint is *non-holonomic* if it restricts only the *velocities* (the *tangent* space), leaving the configuration space fully 3-dimensional. A DDR can reach *any* point in any orientation — yet it cannot do so while keeping its wheels fixed; it must *maneuver*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Posture Vector | $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | m, m, rad | State representation |
| 2 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | m/s, m/s, rad | Path feasibility check |
| 3 | Forward Kinematics (Jacobian form) | $\dot{q} = J(q)\cdot u$ | $u = [v, \omega]^T$ | Pose update from velocity inputs |
| 4 | Forward Kinematics (expanded) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | m/s, m/s, rad/s | Differential equations of motion |
| 5 | Wheel speeds from $v, \omega$ (Inverse Kinematics) | $\omega_R = (v + L\omega/2)/r,\ \ \omega_L = (v - L\omega/2)/r$ | rad/s | Motor command generation |
| 6 | $v, \omega$ from wheel speeds | $v = (r/2)(\omega_R + \omega_L),\ \ \omega = (r/L)(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | m/s, rad/s | Sensor feedback to controller |
| 7 | Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) radius | $R = v/\omega = (L/2)(\omega_R + \omega_L)/(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | m | Path curvature analysis |
| 8 | Discrete (Euler) Update | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k T_s \cos\theta_k$ | m | Numerical simulation |
| 9 | Discrete (Euler) Update | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k T_s \sin\theta_k$ | m | Numerical simulation |
| 10 | Discrete (Euler) Update | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k T_s$ | rad | Numerical simulation |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical bar $\vert$ in formulas is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table. Do **not** write `|v|` directly inside a table cell.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The differential-drive kinematic model is the *backbone* of nearly every commercial indoor mobile robot platform:

- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — Kiva/Amazon Robotics, Fetch Robotics.
- **Domestic vacuum cleaners** — iRobot Roomba, Neato Botvac, Xiaomi Mi Robot.
- **Service & hospitality robots** — Savioke Relay, Pepper (base), TUG robots in hospitals.
- **Educational & research platforms** — Pioneer P3-DX, TurtleBot 3, Kobuki, iRobot Create.
- **Defense & surveillance** — PackBot, Talon, Dragon Runner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the DDR so dominant?** It has the *minimum possible mechanical complexity* (just 2 actuators) for a fully controllable ground vehicle, has zero turning radius (it can rotate in place), and has a *simple, linear* kinematic model that is *trivial* to implement in real-time embedded controllers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Kinematic Model

**Step 1 — Define the reference frame.**
Place the origin of the robot body frame $\{R\}$ at the midpoint between the two drive wheels. The body X-axis points forward, the Y-axis points to the left, and the body Z-axis points upward (right-handed frame). The world frame $\{W\}$ has its X-axis pointing East, Y-axis pointing North.

The pose of $\{R\}$ with respect to $\{W\}$ is given by the **homogeneous transformation matrix**:

$$T_{WR} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Express the velocity of the contact points in the world frame.**
For a *rigid body* moving on a plane, the velocity of any point $P$ attached to the body is:

$$\dot{P} = \dot{P}_{ref} + \omega \times (P - P_{ref})$$

where $\dot{P}_{ref} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, 0]^T$ is the velocity of the reference point and $\omega = [0, 0, \dot{\theta}]^T$ is the angular velocity vector of the body.

**Step 3 — Apply the no-side-slip constraint at the right wheel.**
The right wheel contact point is located at $P_R = [0, -L/2, 0]^T$ in the body frame. Its velocity in the body frame is:

$$\dot{P}_{R, body} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta \\ -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} (L/2)\dot{\theta} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The *no-side-slip* condition requires that the lateral (body-Y) component of the contact point velocity is zero (the wheel cannot move sideways in its own plane):

$$-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta} = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the no-side-slip constraint at the left wheel.**
The left wheel contact point is at $P_L = [0, +L/2, 0]^T$ in the body frame. By identical reasoning:

$$-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta - (L/2)\dot{\theta} = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the two constraints.**
Subtracting equation (ii) from equation (i):

$$\dot{\theta} \cdot L = 0 \ \text{?}$$

Wait — let us correct this. Subtracting (ii) from (i) gives the angular velocity in terms of forward velocity. Adding them eliminates the rotation term. Let us redo this more carefully:

**From (i) alone:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = (L/2)\dot{\theta}$

**From (ii) alone:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = -(L/2)\dot{\theta}$

This gives the *non-holonomic* constraint at each wheel. Summing the two:

$$2(\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta) = 0 \implies \dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

This is the **single non-holonomic constraint** of the entire vehicle — a direct consequence of the *symmetric* placement of the two wheels.

Subtracting (ii) from (i):

$$L \cdot \dot{\theta} = 0 \quad \text{???}$$

No, again this is wrong. The proper way: subtract (ii) from (i) gives $L\dot{\theta} = 0$ only if the *forward velocity* component is the same. Let us restart cleanly.

**Clean derivation (corrected):**

Equation (i) for right wheel: $-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta} = 0$

Equation (ii) for left wheel: $-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta - (L/2)\dot{\theta} = 0$

Subtract (ii) from (i):

$$\begin{aligned}
&[-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta}] - [-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta - (L/2)\dot{\theta}] = 0 \\
&L \cdot \dot{\theta} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

This is wrong! The right conclusion should be: from the difference, the *forward* velocity component cancels and we get $L\dot{\theta} = 0$ — which would imply the robot cannot rotate! The error is that I have written the *full* body-frame velocity as the no-side-slip condition, but I missed the fact that the **forward velocity component** (along the wheel's rolling direction) is *not* required to be zero — only the lateral component is.

**Correct procedure:**

The right wheel rolls in the body X-direction. Its contact point velocity in the body frame is:

$$v_{R, body} = \begin{bmatrix} v_{R,x} \\ v_{R,y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta} \\ -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**No-side-slip condition (lateral component of contact point velocity is zero):**

$$-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 \quad \text{(for right wheel)} \quad \text{...(i')}$$

**No-side-slip condition for left wheel** (located at $y = +L/2$):

$$-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 \quad \text{(for left wheel)} \quad \text{...(ii')}$$

**Both wheels yield the *same* non-holonomic constraint** because they are coaxial and both cannot slip sideways. The difference is that the *forward* components differ — and that difference is what produces rotation.

**Rolling constraint for right wheel** (forward velocity equals $r\omega_R$):

$$\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta} = r\omega_R \quad \text{...(iii')}$$

**Rolling constraint for left wheel**:

$$\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta - (L/2)\dot{\theta} = r\omega_L \quad \text{...(iv')}$$

**Now combine (i'), (iii'), and (iv'):**

From (i'): $\dot{y} = \dot{x}\tan\theta$. Substituting into (iii'):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{x}\tan\theta \cdot \sin\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta} &= r\omega_R \\
\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{x}\frac{\sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta} + (L/2)\dot{\theta} &= r\omega_R \\
\dot{x}\left(\frac{\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta}\right) + (L/2)\dot{\theta} &= r\omega_R \\
\frac{\dot{x}}{\cos\theta} + (L/2)\dot{\theta} &= r\omega_R \quad \text{...(v)}
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly, from (iv'):

$$\frac{\dot{x}}{\cos\theta} - (L/2)\dot{\theta} = r\omega_L \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the body velocities.**

Add (v) and (vi):

$$2 \cdot \frac{\dot{x}}{\cos\theta} = r(\omega_R + \omega_L) \implies \dot{x} = \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \cdot (\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

Subtract (vi) from (v):

$$L \cdot \dot{\theta} = r(\omega_R - \omega_L) \implies \dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

And from (i'): $\dot{y} = \dot{x}\tan\theta = \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \cdot (\omega_R + \omega_L)$

**Step 7 — Define the body-frame linear velocity.**
Let $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ (the *forward* speed of the reference point in the body frame). Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \equiv \omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Write the matrix (Jacobian) form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{q} &= \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **forward kinematic model** in its canonical form. The matrix on the right is the **kinematic Jacobian** $J(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times 2}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Kinematic Model

Given a desired body-frame velocity $(v, \omega)$, find the required wheel speeds $(\omega_R, \omega_L)$:

From the definitions:
- $v = (r/2)(\omega_R + \omega_L) \implies \omega_R + \omega_L = 2v/r$
- $\omega = (r/L)(\omega_R - \omega_L) \implies \omega_R - \omega_L = L\omega/r$

Solving the 2×2 linear system:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_R &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2v}{r} + \frac{L\omega}{r}\right) = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L}{2}\omega\right) \\
\omega_L &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2v}{r} - \frac{L\omega}{r}\right) = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L}{2}\omega\right)
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & L/2 \\ 1 & -L/2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) Radius

When $\omega \neq 0$, the robot traces a circular arc of radius $R$ (measured from the ICR to the reference point). For a wheel rolling without slipping, $v = R\omega$:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{(r/2)(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{(r/L)(\omega_R - \omega_L)} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

**Special cases:**
- $\omega_R = \omega_L \implies R = \infty$ (pure translation along a straight line).
- $\omega_R = -\omega_L \implies R = 0$ (pure rotation about the reference point — *zero turning radius*).
- $\omega_R = 0 \implies R = -L/2$ (the ICR is on the right wheel — robot pivots around the right wheel).

### 3.4 Derivation of the Discrete-Time (Euler) Update Equations

For numerical simulation on a digital computer, the continuous-time ODEs are integrated using the **forward Euler method** with sampling period $T_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v_k T_s \cos\theta_k \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v_k T_s \sin\theta_k \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega_k T_s
\end{aligned}$$

These three recursions are the *workhorse* of every mobile robot simulator. For higher accuracy, the **Runge-Kutta 4th order (RK4)** integrator is preferred, but Euler integration is *standard* for KTU exams and lab work.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Equations vs. Interpretation

| Equation | Type | Inputs | Outputs | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\dot{q} = J(\theta) \cdot [v,\omega]^T$ | Forward Kinematics | $v, \omega$ (or $\omega_R, \omega_L$) | Pose rate $\dot{q}$ | Pose update, control |
| $[v, \omega]^T = J^{-1}(\theta) \cdot \dot{q}$ | Pose-to-velocity | Desired $\dot{q}$ | Required $v, \omega$ | Trajectory tracking |
| $[\omega_R, \omega_L]^T = M \cdot [v, \omega]^T$ | Inverse Kinematics | $v, \omega$ | Motor commands | Wheel-level control |
| $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Non-holonomic | Pose rate | Scalar $= 0$ | Path validity |
| $R = (L/2)(\omega_R + \omega_L)/(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Path geometry | Wheel speeds | Turn radius | Path planning |
| $x_{k+1} = x_k + v T_s\cos\theta_k$, etc. | Discrete integration | $v_k, \omega_k, T_s$ | New pose | Simulation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Schematic of a Differential-Drive Robot (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Because a precise mechanical drawing is difficult to render in Mermaid, the following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the system — i.e., the data and signal flow from a high-level velocity command down to the wheel rotations and back up to the odometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["HIGHER LEVEL PLANNER - Goal Pose xg yg thetag"]
    B["TRAJECTORY TRACKER - Computes v and omega"]
    C["INVERSE KINEMATIC BLOCK - Matrix M over r"]
    D["RIGHT MOTOR CONTROLLER - PWM Signal"]
    E["LEFT MOTOR CONTROLLER - PWM Signal"]
    F["RIGHT DRIVE WHEEL - Radius r"]
    G["LEFT DRIVE WHEEL - Radius r"]
    H["CHASSIS BODY - Reference Point at Midpoint"]
    I["ENCODER SENSE RIGHT - Counts wheel ticks"]
    J["ENCODER SENSE LEFT - Counts wheel ticks"]
    K["ODOMETRY ESTIMATION - Recovers v and omega"]
    L["POSE UPDATE BLOCK - Euler Integration"]
    M["CURRENT POSE x y theta - Feedback to Planner"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H
    G --> H
    F --> I
    G --> J
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L --> M
    M --> B
```

### 4.2 Coordinate Frame and Non-Holonomic Constraint Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WORLD["WORLD FRAME W"]
        XW["XW - Global East"]
        YW["YW - Global North"]
    end

    subgraph ROBOT["ROBOT FRAME R at midpoint"]
        XR["XR - Forward"]
        YR["YR - Left"]
        THETA["theta - Heading Angle"]
    end

    subgraph WHEELS["DRIVE WHEELS - Coaxial at distance L"]
        WR["RIGHT WHEEL - Position minus L over 2"]
        WL["LEFT WHEEL - Position plus L over 2"]
    end

    subgraph CONSTRAINT["NON HOLONOMIC CONSTRAINT"]
        NH["x dot sin theta minus y dot cos theta equals 0"]
    end

    WORLD --- ROBOT
    ROBOT --- WHEELS
    WHEELS --- CONSTRAINT
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow — Forward and Inverse Kinematics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INV["INVERSE KINEMATICS - From Body Velocity to Wheel Speeds"]
        V1["v m per s"]
        W1["omega rad per s"]
        M1["1 over r times 1 and L over 2 in top row 1 and minus L over 2 in bottom row"]
        OR["omega R rad per s"]
        OL["omega L rad per s"]
        V1 --> M1
        W1 --> M1
        M1 --> OR
        M1 --> OL
    end

    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL ROBOT - Wheelbase L and radius r"]
        ROB["Differential Drive Chassis"]
        OR --> ROB
        OL --> ROB
    end

    subgraph ENC["ENCODER FEEDBACK - Wheel Tick Counts"]
        NR["nR ticks per sample"]
        NL["nL ticks per sample"]
        ROB --> NR
        ROB --> NL
    end

    subgraph FWD["FORWARD KINEMATICS AND ODOMETRY - From Wheel Speeds to Pose Update"]
        M2["r over 2 for v and r over L for omega"]
        V2["v m per s"]
        W2["omega rad per s"]
        EUL["Euler Integration over Ts"]
        XN["x k plus 1"]
        YN["y k plus 1"]
        TN["theta k plus 1"]
        NR --> M2
        NL --> M2
        M2 --> V2
        M2 --> W2
        V2 --> EUL
        W2 --> EUL
        EUL --> XN
        EUL --> YN
        EUL --> TN
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *top row* (Inverse Kinematics) is what happens when a high-level planner issues a desired $v, \omega$. The *bottom row* (Forward Kinematics + Odometry) is what happens when wheel encoders report tick counts. The two are **mathematical inverses** of each other and are used at *different* stages of the control loop.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. What is a differential-drive robot? State the number of degrees of freedom and the nature of the non-holonomic constraint.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A differential-drive robot is a wheeled mobile robot with two independently driven coaxial wheels. It has **3 configuration variables** $(x, y, \theta)$ but only **2 controllable degrees of freedom** (linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$). It obeys the non-holonomic constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$, which restricts sideways motion of the wheel contact points. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for DOF statement, 1 mark for the constraint equation.)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. Why is the differential-drive robot classified as a non-holonomic system? Can it reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ in its workspace?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The robot is non-holonomic because the constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ is a *velocity-level* constraint that is **non-integrable** — it cannot be written as a finite algebraic relation among $x, y, \theta$. **(1 mark)** However, despite the constraint, the robot *can* reach **any pose** $(x, y, \theta)$ in the obstacle-free workspace by suitable maneuvering, because the constraint is *not* a holonomic (configuration-level) restriction. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks, Module 1]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Derivation + Application

**(a)** Starting from the rolling and no-side-slip constraints of a differential-drive robot, derive the **forward kinematic model** in the form $\dot{q} = J(\theta) \cdot [v, \omega]^T$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. The robot is required to move forward at $v = 0.20$ m/s while turning with angular velocity $\omega = 0.50$ rad/s. Compute:
- (i) The required wheel angular velocities $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The radius of curvature $R$ of the resulting path. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The position and orientation after $T_s = 0.1$ s, assuming the robot starts at the origin heading along the X-axis. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Full derivation (7 marks):**
- State assumptions (1 mark): flat plane, rigid wheels, pure rolling, no slip, instantaneous motor response.
- Write the two rolling constraints (1 mark):
$$r\omega_R = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta}$$
$$r\omega_L = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta - (L/2)\dot{\theta}$$
- Write the single no-side-slip (non-holonomic) constraint (1 mark):
$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$
- Solve the system (2 marks): add and subtract the rolling constraints to get $v$ and $\omega$, then apply the non-holonomic constraint to express $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ in terms of $v, \omega$.
- Write the final matrix form (2 marks):
$$\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i)** Using the inverse kinematics formulas:
$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_R &= \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L\omega}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{0.05}\left(0.20 + \frac{0.30 \cdot 0.50}{2}\right) \\
&= 20 \cdot (0.20 + 0.075) = 20 \cdot 0.275 = 5.5 \ \text{rad/s} \\
\omega_L &= \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L\omega}{2}\right) = 20 \cdot (0.20 - 0.075) = 20 \cdot 0.125 = 2.5 \ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii)** Radius of curvature:
$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.20}{0.50} = 0.40 \ \text{m}$$

**(iii)** Euler update with $x_0 = y_0 = \theta_0 = 0$:
$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 0.20 \cdot 0.1 \cdot \cos(0) = 0.020 \ \text{m} \\
y_1 &= 0 + 0.20 \cdot 0.1 \cdot \sin(0) = 0 \ \text{m} \\
\theta_1 &= 0 + 0.50 \cdot 0.1 = 0.050 \ \text{rad} \approx 2.86^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the aid of neat diagrams, explain the **rolling and sliding constraints** of a single fixed standard wheel of a mobile robot. Show that the non-holonomic constraint of a differential-drive robot is $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot with $r = 0.04$ m and $L = 0.20$ m is executing a pure rotation in place. The right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = -2$ rad/s.
- (i) Find $\omega_L$. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Compute the angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot body. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If this rotation is commanded for $T = 4$ s, by what angle (in degrees) does the robot turn? **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Constraint derivation (7 marks):**
- Define the wheel geometry (1 mark): wheel plane, rolling direction, lateral direction, contact point.
- Write the rolling constraint (2 marks): velocity of contact point along rolling direction = $r\dot{\phi}$.
- Write the sliding (lateral) constraint (2 marks): velocity of contact point along lateral direction = 0.
- Combine both wheel constraints of the DDR and show the cancellation that yields the non-holonomic equation (2 marks).

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i)** For pure in-place rotation, the ICR is at the robot's center, so $v = 0$. Then:
$$\begin{aligned}
v &= (r/2)(\omega_R + \omega_L) = 0 \implies \omega_L = -\omega_R = +2 \ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii)** Angular velocity of the body:
$$\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.04}{0.20}(-2 - 2) = 0.2 \cdot (-4) = -0.8 \ \text{rad/s}$$

(The negative sign means the robot rotates clockwise.)

**(iii)** Total angle turned:
$$\Delta\theta = \omega \cdot T = -0.8 \cdot 4 = -3.2 \ \text{rad} = -3.2 \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \approx -183.35^\circ$$

So the robot turns approximately **183.35° clockwise** (almost a half-revolution).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the non-holonomic constraint** when deriving forward kinematics: many students drop the equation $\dot{y} = \dot{x}\tan\theta$ and end up with a wrong Jacobian. **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error in $\omega_L$**: the inverse kinematic formula for the left wheel uses a *minus* sign — students often write the same sign for both wheels, getting $\omega_L = (v + L\omega/2)/r$. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing $L$ with $2L$**: the wheelbase is the *total* distance between the two wheel contact points, *not* the half-distance. **−1 mark** per occurrence.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert radians to degrees** in the final answer of angle questions. Always state units. **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Mixing up the angle convention**: $\theta$ is measured from the global X-axis to the robot's body X-axis (counter-clockwise positive). Mixing degrees and radians inside trig functions is a common error. **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Not stating the assumptions** of the kinematic model: this is *explicitly* asked in part (a) of KTU derivations. **−1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Differential-Drive Kinematics**

- **Posture vector:** $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — 3 numbers fully describe the robot.
- **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — the *only* velocity restriction; **non-integrable**.
- **DOF:** 2 controllable ($v$, $\omega$) out of 3 configuration variables.
- **Forward Kinematics (Jacobian form):**
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Forward Kinematics (wheel form):**
$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \quad \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$
- **Inverse Kinematics:**
$$\omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L\omega}{2}\right), \quad \omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L\omega}{2}\right)$$
- **Radius of curvature:** $R = v/\omega = (L/2)(\omega_R + \omega_L)/(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- **Special motions:**
  - *Pure translation:* $\omega_R = \omega_L$ → straight line, $R = \infty$.
  - *Pure rotation in place:* $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ → $R = 0$.
  - *Pivot about right wheel:* $\omega_R = 0$ → $R = -L/2$ (left side swings).
- **Discrete-time update (Euler):**
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + vT_s\cos\theta_k, \quad y_{k+1} = y_k + vT_s\sin\theta_k, \quad \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega T_s$$
- **Degrees of mobility:** $\delta_m = 2$; **degrees of steerability:** $\delta_s = 0$; **maneuverability:** $\delta_M = 3$.
- **Class:** Campion-Bastin-Gorges type (2, 0).
- **Assumptions to always state in derivations:** flat plane, rigid wheels, point contact, no slip, instantaneous motor dynamics.
- **Why non-holonomic?** The constraint involves $\dot{\theta}$ in a way that cannot be integrated to a finite algebraic equation in $x, y, \theta$.
- **Key engineering insight:** The DDR has the *simplest* mechanical design with full 2D controllability, hence its dominance in commercial indoor robots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Maneuverability - Degree of Mobility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Maneuverability - Degree of Mobility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Mobile Robot Maneuverability?

**Maneuverability** of a mobile robot is a measure of its ability to achieve various configurations and motions within its workspace. It quantifies *how freely* a robot can move, turn, and reorient itself in its environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Campion, Bastin & D'Andrea-Novel, 1996):**
> Maneuverability is the combination of two independent structural properties of a wheeled mobile robot:
> 1. **Degree of Mobility (δm)** – the number of degrees of freedom (DOF) of the robot's chassis that can be **instantaneously** controlled by the wheel actuators.
> 2. **Degree of Steerability (δs)** – the number of orientable (steerable) wheels that can be **independently** steered.
>
> **Degree of Maneuverability:** $$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$

## 1.2 The Configuration Space: SE(2)

A ground mobile robot operates in the plane with position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$. Its configuration space is the **Special Euclidean group**:

$$SE(2) = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1 = \{(x, y, \theta) \mid (x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2, \theta \in [0, 2\pi)\}$$

This gives a **maximum of 3 DOF**. However, wheel constraints (nonholonomic) usually prevent exploiting all 3 DOF instantaneously.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Three Vehicles, Three Maneuverability Levels**
> - **A car on a highway**: Can move forward/backward and steer, but *cannot* slide sideways. **Low maneuverability** (δm = 1, δs = 1 or 2).
> - **A forklift in a warehouse (differential drive)**: Can spin in place by driving wheels in opposite directions. **Medium maneuverability** (δm = 1, δs = 0, but with rotational capability).
> - **A drone/quadcopter**: Can fly in any 3D direction and rotate freely. **Maximum maneuverability** — but this is an *aerial* robot, not constrained to the ground plane.

A ground robot's mobility is fundamentally limited by the **non-slipping condition** of its wheels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wheel Types and Their Constraints

The Campion framework classifies wheels into three fundamental types:

| Wheel Type | Symbol | Nonholonomic Constraints | Steering DOF | Description |
|------------|--------|--------------------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Fixed Standard Wheel** | $N_1$ | **1** | 0 | Wheel rolls in a fixed direction relative to the chassis (e.g., rear wheel of a car) |
| **Steered Standard Wheel** | $N_2$ | **1** | **+1** | Wheel can pivot about a vertical axis (e.g., front wheel of a car) |
| **Castor (Off-centered Orientable) Wheel** | $N_3$ | **2** | **+1** | Wheel pivots freely + offset pivot from ground contact (e.g., office chair wheel) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are there 1 vs 2 constraints?**
> - A *fixed* or *steered* wheel: only one direction is forbidden (lateral slip). → **1 nonholonomic constraint**
> - A *castor* wheel: the pivot is offset from the wheel's contact point, so the wheel contributes **2 independent nonholonomic constraints** on the chassis motion.

## 2.2 The Mobility Formula

For a robot with $N_1$ fixed wheels, $N_2$ steering wheels, and $N_3$ castor wheels (where the constraint directions are *generic* / linearly independent), the **Degree of Mobility** is given by:

$$\boxed{\delta_m = 3 - \frac{N_1 + N_2 + 2\,N_3}{2}}$$

### Special Considerations
- The formula assumes **generic (non-degenerate)** placement — wheels are not collinear and not symmetric in a degenerate way.
- For **omnidirectional** robots (e.g., Swedish/Mecanum wheels), the standard "non-slip" constraint of a fixed wheel is *relaxed*, leading to higher δm than the formula predicts.
- $\delta_m$ must lie in $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ — fractional values indicate **degenerate** geometry.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Configuration Space | $SE(2) = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | 3D pose of a ground robot |
| Degree of Mobility | $\delta_m = 3 - \frac{N_1 + N_2 + 2N_3}{2}$ | Instantaneously controllable chassis DOFs |
| Degree of Steerability | $\delta_s = N_2 + N_3$ | Independently steerable wheels |
| Degree of Maneuverability | $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Total structural mobility |
| Nonholonomic constraint (rolling) | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | No lateral slip of wheel |
| Wheel rotation rate | $\omega = \frac{v}{r}$ | Angular to linear velocity |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The maneuverability classification is critical in:
- **Warehouse robotics** (AMRs): Omni-directional (δm = 2 or 3) preferred for tight spaces.
- **Autonomous cars**: Ackermann (δm = 1, δs = 2) is the standard.
- **Planetary rovers**: Differential drive (δm = 1) preferred for simplicity and ruggedness.
- **Industrial AGVs**: Tricycle (δm = 1, δs = 1) for load-bearing capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Mobility Formula

The chassis has **3 DOF** in the plane: $\dot{q} = (\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})^T$.

### Step 1 — Fixed Standard Wheel Constraint
A fixed wheel aligned at angle $\alpha$ with the chassis (wheel plane direction is chassis-fixed) imposes the *no lateral slip* condition:

$$\dot{x}\sin\alpha - \dot{y}\cos\alpha = 0$$

This is a **single scalar (Pfaffian) constraint** of the form:
$$A(q)\,\dot{q} = 0$$

It reduces the admissible velocity space by 1 dimension.

### Step 2 — Steering Standard Wheel
A steering wheel has an *additional* DOF — the steering angle $\beta$ — but the *no-lateral-slip* condition is still **one** constraint on $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$. The steering angle contributes to $\delta_s$, not to $\delta_m$.

### Step 3 — Castor Wheel
A castor wheel has its pivot offset from the contact point. The geometric analysis (see Latombe, *Robot Motion Planning*, Ch. 3) shows that the castor contributes **two** independent nonholonomic constraints on the chassis motion.

### Step 4 — Combine All Constraints
Stacking all wheel constraints into a single Pfaffian matrix $A(q)$:

$$A(q)\,\dot{q} = 0, \quad A(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{(N_1 + N_2 + 2N_3) \times 3}$$

If the **rank** of $A(q)$ is $r$ (assumed $r = (N_1 + N_2 + 2N_3)/2$ in the generic case), then:

$$\delta_m = 3 - r = 3 - \frac{N_1 + N_2 + 2N_3}{2}$$

The factor of **½** arises because each row of $A(q)$ lives in a 2D subspace (one constraint = one row, but for a fixed wheel the constraint's null direction is the wheel's rolling direction — a single DOF). Two wheels in non-degenerate positions span the full 2D plane, giving **2 constraints from 2 wheels**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Differential-Drive Robot

**Configuration**: 2 fixed rear wheels ($N_1 = 2$) + 1 castor front wheel ($N_3 = 1$); $N_2 = 0$.

Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_m &= 3 - \frac{N_1 + N_2 + 2N_3}{2} \\
&= 3 - \frac{2 + 0 + 2 \cdot 1}{2} \\
&= 3 - \frac{4}{2} \\
&= 3 - 2 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Steerability**: $\delta_s = N_2 + N_3 = 0 + 1 = 1$
**Maneuverability**: $\delta_M = 1 + 1 = 2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: δm = 1 means the chassis can move along **1 direction** (forward/backward) instantaneously. The castor's pivot provides 1 steerable DOF. Together with differential wheel speeds, the robot can effectively rotate, making it very agile despite low δm.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Tricycle / Three-Wheeled Vehicle

**Configuration**: 1 steering front wheel ($N_2 = 1$) + 2 fixed rear wheels ($N_1 = 2$); $N_3 = 0$.

Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_m &= 3 - \frac{N_1 + N_2 + 2N_3}{2} \\
&= 3 - \frac{2 + 1 + 0}{2} \\
&= 3 - 1.5 = 1.5
\end{aligned}$$

The fractional value indicates **geometric degeneracy** (the 2 rear wheels lie on the same axle and are collinear, so their constraints are *not* fully independent). The true mobility is:

$$\delta_m = 1 \quad \text{(after resolving degeneracy)}$$

**Steerability**: $\delta_s = N_2 + N_3 = 1 + 0 = 1$
**Maneuverability**: $\delta_M = 1 + 1 = 2$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Car-Like Robot (Ackermann Steering)

**Configuration**: 2 fixed rear wheels ($N_1 = 2$) + 2 steering front wheels ($N_2 = 2$); $N_3 = 0$.

Applying the formula (generic case):

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_m &= 3 - \frac{N_1 + N_2 + 2N_3}{2} \\
&= 3 - \frac{2 + 2 + 0}{2} \\
&= 3 - 2 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Steerability**: $\delta_s = N_2 + N_3 = 2 + 0 = 2$
**Maneuverability**: $\delta_M = 1 + 2 = 3$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ackermann Constraint**: In real cars, the two front wheels are *not* independently steered — they are linked by the Ackermann geometry to avoid tire slip. This reduces the *effective* δs from 2 to 1, but the **structural** δs (as defined by Campion) remains 2.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Omnidirectional (3 Swedish Wheels at 120°)

**Configuration**: 3 Swedish wheels ($N_1 = 0, N_2 = 0, N_3 = 3$ in the standard wheel taxonomy). However, Swedish wheels use passive rollers, **relaxing** the non-slip constraint.

The mobility formula gives:

$$\delta_m = 3 - \frac{0 + 0 + 2 \cdot 3}{2} = 3 - 3 = 0$$

But because the constraints are **linearly dependent** (the 3 wheel constraint directions at 120° span only a 2D subspace in the chassis motion space), the actual rank is 1, giving:

$$\delta_m = 3 - 1 = 2$$

This is why Swedish-wheeled robots are classified as **type (2, 0)** — full planar translation with no steering required.

## 3.6 Master Classification Table (Campion et al.)

| Robot Type | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $\delta_M$ | Example |
|------------|------------|------------|------------|---------|
| Type (1, 0) | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 fixed wheels (degenerate) |
| Type (1, 1) | 1 | 1 | 2 | **Differential drive, Tricycle** |
| Type (1, 2) | 1 | 2 | 3 | **Car (Ackermann)** |
| Type (2, 0) | 2 | 0 | 2 | **Omnidirectional (Swedish/Mecanum)** |
| Type (2, 1) | 2 | 1 | 3 | 2 Swedish + 1 steering |
| Type (3, 0) | 3 | 0 | 3 | Theoretically not realizable |
| Type (0, 3) | 0 | 3 | 3 | Fully steered immobile platform |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mobility Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Wheeled Mobile Robot] --> wheelCheck{Wheel Type Count}
    wheelCheck --> e1[N1 fixed, N2 steering, N3 castor]
    e1 --> formula[Apply formula deltam equals 3 minus N1+N2+2N3 over 2]
    formula --> rankCheck{Check Rank Degeneracy}
    rankCheck --> high[High Rank Constraint Matrix]
    rankCheck --> low[Low Rank Dependent Constraints]
    high --> type1[Type 1,1 : Differential Drive Tricycle]
    high --> type2[Type 1,2 : Ackermann Car]
    high --> type3[Type 1,0 : Simple 2-Wheel]
    low --> type4[Type 2,0 : Omnidirectional Swedish Mecanum]
    low --> type5[Type 2,1 : Hybrid Omni with Steering]
    type1 --> rec1[deltaM equals 2]
    type2 --> rec2[deltaM equals 3]
    type4 --> rec3[deltaM equals 2]
    type5 --> rec4[deltaM equals 3]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Robot Kinematics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Chassis Layer]
        pose[Chassis Pose q = x,y,theta in SE2]
    end
    subgraph B[Wheel Layer]
        fw[Fixed Wheels N1]
        sw[Steering Wheels N2]
        cw[Castor Wheels N3]
    end
    subgraph C[Constraint Engine]
        pf[Pfaffian Matrix A of q]
        rank[Rank Analysis r]
    end
    subgraph D[Mobility Output]
        mob[Degree of Mobility deltam = 3 minus r]
        steer[Degree of Steerability deltas = N2 + N3]
        man[Degree of Maneuverability deltaM = deltam + deltas]
    end
    pose --> pf
    fw --> pf
    sw --> pf
    cw --> pf
    pf --> rank
    rank --> mob
    fw --> steer
    sw --> steer
    cw --> steer
    mob --> man
    steer --> man
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Admissible Velocity Subspaces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    space[SE2 Configuration Space 3D]
    space --> a1[Differential Drive : 1D line forward-backward]
    space --> a2[Omnidirectional : 2D plane x-y translations]
    space --> a3[Ackermann Car : 1D arc forward motion + steering angle]
    a1 --> m1[deltaM = 2]
    a2 --> m2[deltaM = 2]
    a3 --> m3[deltaM = 3]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Degree of Mobility (δm) and Degree of Steerability (δs) of a wheeled mobile robot. How are they related to the Degree of Maneuverability (δM)?**

**Model Answer:**
