# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Proprioceptive sensors* are devices that measure the internal state of the robot, such as wheel rotation, joint angle, or battery voltage, without depending on external environmental features.

*Exteroceptive sensors* measure features of the robot's external environment, such as distance to obstacles, ambient light, or GPS satellite signals.

| Type | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Proprioceptive | Wheel Encoder (PPR) | IMU Gyroscope |
| Exteroceptive | LIDAR | GPS / GNSS |

(1 mark definition of proprioceptive + 1 mark definition of exteroceptive + 1 mark examples = 3 marks)

---

### Question 2: A wheel encoder has 360 pulses per revolution and is mounted on a wheel of radius 8 cm. Calculate the linear distance the robot moves per pulse. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Wheel circumference.

$$C = 2\pi r = 2 \pi \times 0.08 = 0.5027\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Distance per pulse.

$$\delta d = \frac{C}{N} = \frac{0.5027}{360} = 1.397 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/pulse} \approx 1.40\ \text{mm/pulse}$$

(1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark final answer = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an ultrasonic sonar sensor. Derive the formula used for range measurement. State the standard speed of sound used. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(b)** A mobile robot uses a 2D LIDAR with 0.25° angular resolution spinning at 10 Hz. Calculate (i) the total number of range readings per scan, (ii) the time between consecutive range points, and (iii) the beam footprint at 30 m assuming a beam divergence of 0.4°. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Principle (3 marks):** An ultrasonic sonar sensor operates on the *Time-of-Flight (ToF)* principle. A piezoelectric transducer emits a short burst of acoustic energy (typically at **40 kHz**), well above the human audible range. The sound wave propagates through the air, strikes an obstacle, and is reflected back as an echo. The same (or a paired) transducer detects the echo, and an internal timer measures the round-trip time $t$ between emission and detection.

**Range Formula Derivation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance} &= \text{Speed} \times \text{Time} \\
\text{Round-trip path} &= v_{\text{sound}} \times t \\
\text{One-way distance } d &= \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \times t}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

At standard conditions ($T = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$), the speed of sound in air is:

$$v_{\text{sound}} \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$$

**Key Limitations (1 mark):** Specular reflection, crosstalk, slow update rate, and temperature sensitivity.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:** $\Delta\theta = 0.25^\circ$, $f_{\text{scan}} = 10\ \text{Hz}$, $d = 30\ \text{m}$, $\alpha = 0.4^\circ$.

**(i) Total number of readings per scan:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{points}} &= \frac{360^\circ}{0.25^\circ} \\
&= 1440\ \text{points per full revolution}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Time between consecutive range points:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= \frac{1}{f_{\text{scan}}} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1\ \text{s} \\
\Delta t &= \frac{T_{\text{scan}}}{N_{\text{points}}} = \frac{0.1}{1440} \\
&= 6.944 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} \approx 69.4\ \mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Beam footprint at 30 m:**

$$\begin{aligned}
w &= 2 d \tan(\alpha/2) \\
&= 2 \times 30 \times \tan(0.2^\circ) \\
&= 60 \times \tan(3.491 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&\approx 60 \times 3.491 \times 10^{-3} \\
&\approx 0.2095\ \text{m} \approx 21\ \text{cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students frequently forget the factor of **2** in the ToF formula (round-trip correction). Marks are deducted if you write $d = v \cdot t$ instead of $d = (v \cdot t)/2$. Always state "round-trip time" explicitly in your answer.
> 
> **Pitfall Callout #2:** When calculating scan time, ensure you use the *scan frequency* (rotations per second) and not the *pulse repetition frequency* — KTU problems often disguise this distinction.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks

**(a)** Describe the working of a wheel encoder (optical type). How is it used in mobile robot odometry? Derive the relationship between pulse count and linear distance traveled. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Set A]`

**(b)** Compare the merits and demerits of ultrasonic sonar, LIDAR, and stereo vision as range sensors for an autonomous mobile robot navigating indoors. Justify your recommendation for a warehouse AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) application. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Optical Encoder Working (3 marks):** A rotary optical encoder consists of a disc patterned with $N$ alternating opaque and transparent slots. An LED emits light on one side; a photodiode detector on the other side produces a pulse every time a slot aligns with the light path. A quadrature encoder uses two such channels (A and B) 90° out of phase, enabling direction discrimination and 4× resolution via edge counting.

**Odometry Application (2 marks):** Wheel odometry integrates incremental wheel rotations to estimate the robot's pose change $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta\theta)$ over time. This is a *dead-reckoning* technique — the cornerstone of short-term localization.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$\Delta d = \frac{2\pi r}{N} \cdot \Delta n, \quad \Delta\theta_{\text{wheel}} = \frac{2\pi}{N} \cdot \Delta n$$

For a differential-drive robot, the body turn $\Delta\theta_b$ and translation $\Delta s$ are computed from the left and right wheel displacements.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Attribute | Ultrasonic Sonar | LIDAR | Stereo Vision |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±1–3 cm | ±2–5 cm | ~1% of range |
| Range | 0.02–10 m | 0.1–100 m | 0.5–20 m |
| Cost | Very Low | High | Medium |
| 2D / 3D | 1D per sensor | 2D / 3D | Dense 2.5D |
| Texture Dependence | No | No | Yes |
| Failure Mode | Specular reflect. | Dust, rain | Textureless walls |

**Recommendation for Warehouse AGV (3 marks):**

A **2D LIDAR** is the optimal choice because:

1. **Long range and high accuracy** are essential for safely navigating wide warehouse aisles.
2. **Wide field-of-view (typically 270°)** allows cornering and reversing maneuvers.
3. **Independence from ambient light** — warehouses are often dimly lit.
4. **Real-time obstacle detection** at high update rates (10–40 Hz) suitable for moving AGVs.

Ultrasonic is too short-range; stereo cameras are unreliable with uniform cardboard boxes on shelves (low texture).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** Many students confuse *accuracy* and *precision* in comparison answers. Always state both — for example, "LIDAR has ±2 cm accuracy AND sub-cm precision." Partial marks are awarded for getting at least one right.
> 
> **Pitfall Callout #4:** When asked to "justify," do not just list facts — explicitly link the chosen sensor's property to the *application requirement* (e.g., "warehouse is dim → LIDAR works in dark"). Examiners reward application-level reasoning.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Sensing**

* **Sensing vs Perception:** Sensing = raw measurement; Perception = interpretation.
* **Sensor Taxonomy:** *Proprioceptive* (internal) vs *Exteroceptive* (external); *Active* (emits energy) vs *Passive* (observes only).
* **Master Formula #1 — Time-of-Flight:** $d = (v \cdot t) / 2$. Always include the factor of 2.
* **Standard Constants:** $v_{\text{sound}} \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$ (at 20 °C), $c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ (light), GPS uses 4+ satellites for 3D fix.
* **Encoder Resolution:** $\delta d = 2\pi r / N$ per pulse. Higher PPR → better resolution.
* **Accuracy vs Precision:** Accuracy = closeness to truth; Precision = repeatability (low $\sigma$).
* **LIDAR Beam Footprint:** $w = 2 d \tan(\alpha/2)$ — increases with range; affects obstacle detection reliability.
* **IMU Drift:** Double integration of accelerometer noise → quadratic position error growth. Always fuse IMU with other sensors.
* **Camera Pinhole Model:** $u = f X_c / Z_c$ — projects 3D world points to image plane.
* **Sensor Fusion:** Combines complementary sensors to overcome individual limitations (the reason a Tesla has cameras + radar + ultrasonics + IMU).
* **Range-Failure Modes:** Sonar (specular), LIDAR (rain/dust), Camera (dark/glare), GPS (indoor), IMU (drift).
* **Statistical Tools:** Mean, standard deviation $\sigma$, and Gaussian noise assumption $v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ are essential for sensor noise modeling.
* **KTU Exam Frequency:** Sensing forms 2 short-answer questions and one 14-mark question almost every semester. Memorize the ToF formula and the encoder formulas — they are asked verbatim in ~80% of papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — MAPPING IN MOBILE ROBOTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping** in mobile robotics is the computational process by which a robot creates a **spatial representation** (map) of its environment using onboard sensor measurements (e.g., LIDAR, sonar, cameras, odometry) while simultaneously estimating its own pose (location and orientation) within that representation.

The formal pipeline is mathematically expressed as the inference problem:

$$m^* = \arg\max_{m} \; P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

where:
- $m$ = the map of the environment
- $z_{1:t}$ = sensor measurements from time $1$ to $t$
- $x_{1:t}$ = robot poses/paths from time $1$ to $t$
- $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ = posterior probability of the map given sensor data and robot trajectory

### 1.2 Three Canonical Map Types

| Map Type | Representation | Best Use Case |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Metric (Grid)** | Uniform 2D/3D cells with occupancy probability | Indoor structured environments |
| **Feature (Landmark)** | Geometric primitives (points, lines, planes) | Office corridors, urban roads |
| **Topological** | Graph of nodes (places) and edges (paths) | Large-scale, semantic navigation |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of metric + topological layers | Real-world SLAM deployments |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favourite**: The three "M"s of robotics — **M**apping, **M**otion control, and **M**anipulation — always start from mapping because **no autonomous decision is possible without a representation of the world**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Reader

Imagine you are **blindfolded and dropped in an unknown room**. You have a **walking stick** (sensor) that pings the walls. As you shuffle:

1. Each ping tells you "there is a wall at ~1.2 m at angle 35°" — this is a **sensor measurement $z_t$**.
2. Each shuffle step shifts your internal body-pose estimate — this is **odometry $x_t$**.
3. You mentally fill a grid: free cell, occupied cell, unknown cell — this is your **map $m$**.

The blindfolded walker is doing exactly what a mobile robot does: fusing noisy, partial sensor measurements over time into a coherent **occupancy grid** representation. The key challenge is **noise** — both the sensor and your step estimator are imperfect, so you must update beliefs **probabilistically** rather than deterministically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensory Update Frequency (Standard Values)**:
> - LIDAR: **10–40 Hz** with range **≈100 m** and **±2 cm** accuracy
> - Sonar: **5–20 Hz** with range **≈5 m** and **±5 cm** accuracy
> - Wheel Odometry drift: **≈3–5 %** of distance travelled

### 1.4 The Mapping Problem — Formal Statement

The mapping problem is **under-determined** because:
- Sensor noise corrupts $z_t$
- Odometry drift corrupts $x_t$
- Two unknowns ($m$ and $x_{1:t}$) must be solved simultaneously

This coupling between map and pose is precisely why **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is considered one of the "**holy grails**" of mobile robotics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Occupancy Grid Mapping — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Discretisation Step

The continuous 2D workspace $\mathbb{R}^2$ is discretised into a uniform grid of $N \times N$ cells, each cell $m_{ij}$ storing the probability that it is **occupied** by an obstacle.

Each cell is treated as an **independent binary random variable**:

$$m_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \text{where } 1 \equiv \text{occupied}, \; 0 \equiv \text{free}$$

The full map is therefore the product:

$$P(m) = \prod_{ij} P(m_{ij})$$

### 2.1.2 Bayesian Update for a Single Cell

Using Bayes' rule, the posterior probability of cell occupancy given measurements up to time $t$ is:

$$P(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_{ij}, x_t) \cdot P(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

The denominator is a normalising constant; we track only the **odds ratio**:

$$\text{Odds}(m_{ij}) = \frac{P(m_{ij})}{1 - P(m_{ij})}$$

### 2.1.3 Log-Odds Representation (Why We Use It!)

Multiplication of probabilities over thousands of cells leads to **numerical underflow**. The trick is to work in the **log-odds** domain:

$$\ell_{ij,t} = \log \frac{P(m_{ij} = 1 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{P(m_{ij} = 0 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This converts a **multiplicative Bayes update** into a simple **additive** update — a critical KTU 2024 board insight.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Map posterior | $m^* = \arg\max_m P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | Decision rule |
| 2 | Bayes update (per cell) | $P(m_{ij}\mid z_t, x_t) \propto P(z_t\mid m_{ij}, x_t) P(m_{ij}\mid z_{1:t-1})$ | Probabilistic |
| 3 | Log-odds update | $\ell_{ij,t} = \ell_{ij,t-1} + \log \frac{P(m_{ij}=1\mid z_t,x_t)}{1 - P(m_{ij}=1\mid z_t,x_t)}$ | Additive |
| 4 | Inverse sensor model | $P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_t, x_t) = f(z_t, d_{ij})$ | Function of distance |
| 5 | Grid cell count | $N_{cells} = (L_{x}/\Delta r) \times (L_{y}/\Delta r)$ | $\Delta r$ = resolution |
| 6 | Inverse range sensor beam | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_j - x_t)^2 + (y_j - y_t)^2}$ | Euclidean distance |
| 7 | Sensor measurement model | $z_t = d_{true} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Gaussian noise |
| 8 | Cell-area update rule | $P(m_{ij}=1) > 0.5 \Rightarrow$ occupied | Threshold rule |
| 9 | Map uncertainty (entropy) | $H(m_{ij}) = -p\log p - (1-p)\log(1-p)$ | bits/cell |
| 10 | SLAM posterior | $P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Joint estimate |
| 11 | EKF-SLAM state vector | $\mathbf{y}_t = [x_t,\; m_1,\; m_2,\; \dots,\; m_N]^T$ | Augmented state |
| 12 | Landmark range-bearing | $z_t = \begin{bmatrix} r_t \\ \phi_t \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_L-x_t)^2+(y_L-y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_L-y_t,\, x_L-x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$ | Polar form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Convention**: Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside any markdown table. The vertical bar breaks table syntax. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead.

---

## 2.3 Why Mapping is Hard — The Three Core Challenges

### Challenge 1: **Correspondence (Data Association)**
Given two sensor observations $z_a$ and $z_b$, are they from the **same physical landmark**? In EKF-SLAM, this is solved via **Mahalanobis distance gating**:

$$D^2 = (z_a - z_b)^T S^{-1} (z_a - z_b) < \chi^2_{d, \alpha}$$

where $S$ is the innovation covariance and $\chi^2_{d,\alpha}$ is the chi-squared threshold for $d$ DOF at confidence $\alpha$.

### Challenge 2: **Perceptual Ambiguity**
Two different environments can produce identical sensor signatures (e.g., symmetric corridors). Solution: **probabilistic fusion** over time — keep alternative hypotheses until evidence disambiguates.

### Challenge 3: **Computational Complexity**
Naive occupancy grid: $O(N^2)$ per update. For $N = 1000$, that is $10^6$ cells. Real-time update at **40 Hz** requires **400 million cell updates per second**.

---

## 2.4 Topological Mapping

A topological map is a **graph** $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$:
- $\mathcal{V}$ = nodes (distinctive places)
- $\mathcal{E}$ = edges (traversable connections)

The adjacency matrix is:

$$A_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } v_i, v_j \in \mathcal{V} \text{ are adjacent} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Dijkstra's shortest path** is used for navigation:

$$d(v_i, v_j) = \min_{k \in \mathcal{V}} \bigl[ d(v_i, v_k) + w_{kj} \bigr]$$

where $w_{kj}$ is the edge weight (cost/distance).

---

## 2.5 Feature-Based (Landmark) Mapping

Landmarks are **distinctive, re-observable features** extracted from sensor data. The robot state is augmented:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t, \ell_1, \ell_2, \dots, \ell_N]^T$$

State evolves via the **motion model**:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = f(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}, u_t) + \mathbf{w}_t, \quad \mathbf{w}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

Measurement model:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{y}_t) + \mathbf{v}_t, \quad \mathbf{v}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: Occupancy grids power **ROS `gmapping`**, **Google Cartographer**, and every autonomous vacuum cleaner (Roomba, Mi Robot). Topological maps drive **Google Maps indoor navigation**. Feature-based SLAM underlies **autonomous vehicle lane/landmark tracking**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to Additive Log-Odds Update

**Starting point** — Bayes rule for a single cell:

$$P(m_{ij} = 1 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_{ij}=1, x_t) \cdot P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 1** — Define prior and likelihood terms symbolically:

$$p_{t} = P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

$$p_{t-1} = P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$$

$$q_t = P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_t, x_t) \quad \text{(inverse sensor model)}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute and rearrange:

$$p_t = \frac{q_t \cdot p_{t-1}}{q_t \cdot p_{t-1} + (1 - q_t)(1 - p_{t-1})}$$

This is the **closed-form Bayes filter** for a binary state. This form is correct but multiplicative — bad for long sequences.

**Step 3** — Convert to odds:

$$\frac{p_t}{1 - p_t} = \frac{q_t}{1 - q_t} \cdot \frac{p_{t-1}}{1 - p_{t-1}}$$

This converts the product into a product of odds — still multiplicative.

**Step 4** — Take the natural log:

$$\ell_t = \log \frac{p_t}{1 - p_t} = \log \frac{q_t}{1 - q_t} + \log \frac{p_{t-1}}{1 - p_{t-1}}$$

$$\ell_t = \ell_{t-1} + \log \frac{q_t}{1 - q_t}$$

This is the celebrated **additive log-odds update rule**.

**Step 5** — Recover probability from log-odds:

$$p_t = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{\ell_t}}$$

Equivalently:

$$p_t = \frac{e^{\ell_t}}{1 + e^{\ell_t}} = \sigma(\ell_t)$$

where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the **logistic sigmoid function**.

$$\boxed{\; \ell_t = \ell_{t-1} + \log \frac{P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_t, x_t)} \;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Sensor Model for a Range Finder

**Scenario**: A range sensor at pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ returns range $r_t$ along bearing $\phi$.

**Step 1** — Compute the cell-to-sensor distance:

$$d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_{ij} - x)^2 + (y_{ij} - y)^2}$$

**Step 2** — Compute the bearing to the cell:

$$\phi_{ij} = \text{atan2}(y_{ij} - y, \; x_{ij} - x) - \theta$$

**Step 3** — Define the beam angle window of width $\Delta\phi$:

$$|\phi_{ij}| \le \Delta\phi / 2$$

**Step 4** — Categorise cells into three regions:

- **Region A — Free cells** ($0 < d_{ij} < r_t - \alpha$): $P_{occ} = p_{free}$ (low, e.g. **0.1**)
- **Region B — Frontier cells** ($r_t - \alpha \le d_{ij} \le r_t + \alpha$): $P_{occ} = 0.5$ (unknown)
- **Region C — Occupied cells** ($d_{ij} \approx r_t$): $P_{occ} = p_{occ}$ (high, e.g. **0.9**)

where $\alpha$ is the **sensor resolution margin** (typically $\alpha = 0.5 \Delta r$ for a grid of resolution $\Delta r$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: EKF-SLAM Update (Linearised Landmark Observation)

**State vector** at time $t$:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \\ \ell_{x,1} \\ \ell_{y,1} \\ \vdots \\ \ell_{x,N} \\ \ell_{y,N} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1** — Predicted observation for landmark $k$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{z}}_t = h(\mathbf{y}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(\ell_{x,k} - x_t)^2 + (\ell_{y,k} - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(\ell_{y,k} - y_t, \; \ell_{x,k} - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2** — Innovation (measurement residual):

$$\nu_t = \mathbf{z}_t - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_t$$

**Step 3** — Jacobian of $h$ with respect to the state:

$$H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{y}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} & 0 \dots 0 & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial \ell_{x,k}} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial \ell_{y,k}} & 0 \dots 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} & 0 \dots 0 & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \ell_{x,k}} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \ell_{y,k}} & 0 \dots 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4** — Innovation covariance:

$$S_t = H_t P_t H_t^T + R_t$$

**Step 5** — Kalman gain:

$$K_t = P_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$$

**Step 6** — State update:

$$\mathbf{y}_t \leftarrow \mathbf{y}_t + K_t \nu_t$$

**Step 7** — Covariance update (Joseph form for stability):

$$P_t \leftarrow (I - K_t H_t) P_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t R_t K_t^T$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Method | Map Form | Sensor | Complexity | Drift? | Suitable For |
|--------|----------|--------|------------|--------|--------------|
| Raw Odometry + Grid | Occupancy grid | LIDAR/sonar | Low | Yes (unbounded) | Short runs |
| EKF-SLAM | Feature-based | Camera/LIDAR | $O(N^2)$ | Bounded | Small landmarks |
| FastSLAM (Particle) | Grid (per particle) | LIDAR | $O(M \log N)$ | Bounded | Large maps |
| Graph-SLAM | Pose graph | Any | $O(N^3)$ offline | Bounded | Loop closure |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-to-End Mapping Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Odometry u_t] --> C[Pose Predictor x_t]
    B[Sensor Raw Data z_t] --> D[Pre-processing Filter]
    D --> E[Feature Extractor / Range Scan]
    C --> F[Coordinate Transform]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Inverse Sensor Model]
    G --> H[Bayesian Update Engine]
    H --> I[Occupancy Grid Map m]
    H --> J[Feature Map Landmarks]
    I --> K[Map Fusion Module]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Loop Closure Detector]
    L --> M[Pose Graph Optimizer]
    M --> H
    H --> N[Output Map to Planner]
```

## 4.2 Occupancy Grid Update Process (Per-Cell Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: New Scan Arrives z_t] --> A[Robot Pose x_t]
    A --> B[For each cell m_ij in grid]
    B --> C[Compute distance d_ij and bearing phi_ij]
    C --> D{Beam hits cell?}
    D -- No --> E[Skip cell - no update]
    D -- Yes --> F{Within free region?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply p_free weight]
    F -- No --> H{Within occupied region?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply p_occ weight]
    H -- No --> J[Mark as unknown - no update]
    G --> K[Update log-odds L_ij]
    I --> K
    K --> L{L_ij exceeds threshold?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Classify cell: occupied]
    L -- No --> N{Log-odds below lower bound?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Classify cell: free]
    N -- No --> P[Keep cell: unknown]
    M --> Q[Emit updated grid]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 4.3 SLAM Architecture: EKF vs Particle Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EKFPATH[EKF-SLAM]
        E1[Single Gaussian Pose] --> E2[Augmented State Vector]
        E2 --> E3[Linearised Observation]
        E3 --> E4[Kalman Gain Update]
    end
    subgraph PFSLAMPATH[FastSLAM - Particle Filter]
        P1[Pose Particles M] --> P2[Per-Particle Grid Map]
        P2 --> P3[Particle Weighting]
        P3 --> P4[Resampling]
        P4 --> P1
    end
```

## 4.4 Topological Map Representation (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1((Room A)) -- corridor --> N2((Corridor 1))
    N2 -- doorway --> N3((Room B))
    N2 -- stairs --> N4((Stairwell))
    N3 -- hallway --> N5((Lab 1))
    N4 -- hallway --> N5
    N5 -- door --> N6((Exit))
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "occupancy grid map" and explain how a single cell's occupancy probability is updated using Bayes' rule when a new range measurement is received.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **occupancy grid map** is a discretised 2D (or 3D) representation of the environment where each cell stores the probability that the corresponding spatial region is occupied by an obstacle. It is one of the most popular metric map forms in mobile robotics (used in `gmapping`, `cartographer`).

When a new range measurement $z_t$ arrives at robot pose $x_t$, the occupancy probability of each cell $m_{ij}$ lying along the sensor beam is updated using Bayes' rule:

$$P(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_{ij}, x_t)\, P(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

Cells in the **free region** get their probability **decreased**, while the cell at the **measured range** gets it **increased**. Cells outside the beam are left unchanged. To avoid numerical underflow, the update is performed in the **log-odds domain**:

$$\ell_{ij,t} = \ell_{ij,t-1} + \log \frac{P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_t, x_t)}$$

A cell is declared occupied if its probability exceeds 0.5 (log-odds > 0), free if below 0.5, and unknown otherwise. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between metric, topological, and hybrid maps. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Map Type | Definition | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|----------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Metric** | Stores geometric coordinates of obstacles and free space | Precise path planning; intuitive | Memory-heavy; affected by odometry drift |
| **Topological** | Graph of places (nodes) and connections (edges) | Compact; scalable to large environments | No metric detail — poor for precise navigation |
| **Hybrid** | Combines local metric maps with global topological graph | Best of both worlds; efficient loop closure | Higher implementation complexity |

**(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** Derive the additive log-odds update rule for occupancy grid mapping starting from Bayes' rule. Show clearly why the log-odds formulation avoids numerical underflow. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the inverse sensor model of a 2D laser range finder. With a neat diagram, describe how the cells along the beam are classified into free, occupied, and unknown regions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

Starting with Bayes' rule for a single cell:

$$p_t = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_{ij}=1, x_t)\, p_{t-1}}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

Expanding the denominator using the law of total probability over the binary state:

$$p_t = \frac{q_t \, p_{t-1}}{q_t \, p_{t-1} + (1 - q_t)(1 - p_{t-1})}$$

where $q_t = P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_t, x_t)$ is the **inverse sensor model**.

Dividing numerator and denominator by $(1 - q_t)(1 - p_{t-1})$:

$$\frac{p_t}{1 - p_t} = \frac{q_t}{1 - q_t} \cdot \frac{p_{t-1}}{1 - p_{t-1}}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\log \frac{p_t}{1 - p_t} = \log \frac{q_t}{1 - q_t} + \log \frac{p_{t-1}}{1 - p_{t-1}}$$

Defining $\ell_t = \log \frac{p_t}{1 - p_t}$:

$$\ell_t = \ell_{t-1} + \log \frac{q_t}{1 - q_t}$$

**Why log-odds avoids underflow**: Over thousands of cells and millions of timesteps, the product form $\prod q_t$ causes probabilities to underflow to zero (machine epsilon limit). The log transform converts multiplication into addition, keeping all values in a bounded range (typically $\ell \in [-50, +50]$). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

The **inverse sensor model** $P(m_{ij}=1 \mid z_t, x_t)$ gives the probability that cell $m_{ij}$ is occupied, conditioned on the current sensor reading and robot pose.

For a 2D laser range finder at pose $(x, y, \theta)$ returning range $r_t$ along beam angle $\phi$:

**Step 1 — Compute distance and bearing**:
$$d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_{ij} - x)^2 + (y_{ij} - y)^2}$$
$$\phi_{ij} = \text{atan2}(y_{ij} - y,\; x_{ij} - x) - \theta$$

**Step 2 — Classify cells into three regions**:

| Region | Condition | $P_{occ}$ |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Free | $0 < d_{ij} < r_t - \alpha$ and $\vert \phi_{ij} \vert \le \Delta\phi/2$ | $p_{free}$ (e.g. **0.1**) |
| Frontier | $r_t - \alpha \le d_{ij} \le r_t + \alpha$ | **0.5** (unknown) |
| Occupied | $d_{ij} \approx r_t$ | $p_{occ}$ (e.g. **0.9**) |

where $\alpha$ is the sensor resolution margin and $\Delta\phi$ is the beam angular width.

**Diagram (text-based)**:

```
        Occupied
           *
           |
           | <- measured range r_t
           |
   Free    |    Free
  *--*--*  |  *--*--*
  *--*--*  |  *--*--*
  *--*--*  |  *--*--*
   ROBOT---->   bearing phi
```

Cells along the beam from robot up to $r_t - \alpha$ are marked **free** (probability decreased); the cell at $r_t$ is marked **occupied**; cells outside the beam cone are **unchanged**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **(a)** What is SLAM? Explain the Extended Kalman Filter based SLAM algorithm with the prediction and update equations. State the state vector and observation model. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM in terms of map representation, computational complexity, and ability to handle loop closure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the problem of concurrently estimating the robot pose and building a map of the environment, given that both quantities are inter-dependent and uncertain.

**EKF-SLAM Algorithm**:

**State Vector** (robot pose + all landmarks):

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \\ \ell_{x,1} \\ \ell_{y,1} \\ \vdots \\ \ell_{x,N} \\ \ell_{y,N} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Prediction step** (using motion model $u_t$):

$$\bar{\mathbf{y}}_t = f(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}, u_t)$$
$$\bar{P}_t = F_t P_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t$$

where $F_t = \partial f / \partial \mathbf{y}$ is the motion Jacobian and $Q_t$ is process noise covariance.

**Observation model** (range-bearing to landmark $k$):

$$\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{y}_t) + \mathbf{v}_t, \quad \mathbf{v}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

$$h(\mathbf{y}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(\ell_{x,k}-x_t)^2 + (\ell_{y,k}-y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(\ell_{y,k}-y_t,\; \ell_{x,k}-x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

**Update step**:

$$H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{y}}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mathbf{y}}_t}, \quad S_t = H_t \bar{P}_t H_t^T + R_t$$
$$K_t = \bar{P}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$$
$$\mathbf{y}_t = \bar{\mathbf{y}}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - h(\bar{\mathbf{y}}_t))$$
$$P_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{P}_t$$

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

| Criterion | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM | Graph-SLAM |
|-----------|----------|----------|------------|
| Map Form | Feature/landmark points | Occupancy grid per particle | Pose graph + constraints |
| State Size | $O(3 + 2N)$ | $O(M \cdot \text{grid})$ | Pose nodes + edges |
| Complexity | $O(N^2)$ per step | $O(M \log N)$ | $O(N^3)$ offline |
| Loop Closure | Indirect (via covariance) | Direct (resampling) | Native (graph optimization) |
| Scalability | Poor beyond ~100 landmarks | Good for medium-large | Excellent (sparse) |
| Memory | Quadratic growth | Linear in particles | Sparse matrix storage |

EKF-SLAM suffers from **quadratic covariance growth** — adding $N$ landmarks inflates the state covariance to $O(N^2)$. FastSLAM decouples landmarks via Rao-Blackwellisation, assigning each particle its own EKF. Graph-SLAM defers estimation to a global optimisation (e.g., `g2o`, `GTSAM`) where loop closures are added as soft constraints. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Forgetting to define the log-odds symbol** before using it: always write $\ell_t = \log \frac{p_t}{1-p_t}$ first.
> 2. **Mixing up prediction vs update Jacobians**: $F_t$ is for motion, $H_t$ is for observation.
> 3. **Using the vertical bar `|` inside tables** — this **breaks markdown** and costs 0.5 marks if examiner cannot read your answer.
> 4. **Confusing $z_t$ (measurement) with $u_t$ (control)**: in KTU answers, always state which is which.
> 5. **Forgetting units and numerical values** (e.g. $\Delta r = 0.05$ m for a typical 5 cm grid).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Map posterior**: $m^* = \arg\max_m P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$
- **Three map types**: metric (grid/feature), topological (graph), hybrid.
- **Occupancy grid**: each cell is an independent binary random variable for occupied/free.
- **Bayes update per cell**: multiplicative form → convert to **log-odds** → **additive** update.
- **Log-odds formula**: $\ell_t = \ell_{t-1} + \log \frac{q_t}{1 - q_t}$
- **Threshold rule**: cell occupied if $p > 0.5$ (i.e. $\ell > 0$); free if $p < 0.5$; unknown if uninitialised.
- **Inverse sensor model**: divides cells along a beam into free, occupied, frontier (unknown).
- **SLAM posterior**: joint estimate $P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- **EKF-SLAM state**: pose + all landmark positions; quadratic complexity $O(N^2)$.
- **FastSLAM**: particle-filter based; each particle carries its own map (Rao-Blackwellised).
- **Graph-SLAM**: pose graph + soft constraints; offline optimisation; best for loop closure.
- **Data association**: solved via **Mahalanobis distance gating** under a $\chi^2$ threshold.
- **Standard numerical values**: LIDAR range $\approx \mathbf{100\ m}$, accuracy $\pm \mathbf{2\ cm}$; grid resolution $\mathbf{0.05\ m}$; sensor rate $\mathbf{10\text{–}40\ Hz}$.
- **Numerical safeguard**: log-odds bounded in $\ell \in [-50, +50]$ to prevent underflow/overflow.
- **Common KTU board keywords**: "**occupancy grid**", "**inverse sensor model**", "**log-odds**", "**data association**", "**loop closure**", "**EKF update**".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Localization and Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously tracking an agent's (robot's) location within that environment, using onboard sensors, probabilistic algorithms, and a unifying estimation framework.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, SLAM is defined as the recursive probabilistic estimation of:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where:
- $x_t$ = robot pose (position + orientation) at time $t$
- $m$ = the map of the environment
- $z_{1:t}$ = history of sensor observations up to time $t$
- $u_{1:t}$ = history of control inputs (odometry) up to time $t$

> [!NOTE]
> The SLAM problem is often called a **"chicken-and-egg" problem** because a good map is needed for localization, but a good pose estimate is needed to build the map. SLAM solves this circular dependency through joint probabilistic estimation.

### Key Performance Metrics in SLAM

| Metric | Standard Value/Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Localization Error** | < **0.1 m** (indoor), < **1 m** (outdoor) | Drift minimization |
| **Map Resolution** | **0.05 m – 0.5 m** per cell | Trade-off with memory |
| **Update Rate** | **10 – 50 Hz** | Real-time constraint |
| **Loop Closure Threshold** | Typically **0.5 m** | Data association accuracy |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Blindfolded Explorer in a New House" Analogy**

Imagine you wake up blindfolded inside an unfamiliar house. You cannot see (no a-priori map), but you can:
1. **Feel** the walls with your hands (sensor observations)
2. **Count your steps** as you walk (odometry/control inputs)
3. **Touch a familiar doorknob** that reminds you of one you have touched before (loop closure)

Your brain must simultaneously:
- **Build a mental map** of the house (Mapping)
- **Track where you are** in that mental map (Localization)

If your step count is wrong (odometry drift), your mental map becomes distorted. If you forget the layout (poor map), you cannot tell which room you are in. SLAM algorithms mimic exactly this cognitive process for robots, but with mathematical rigor and probabilistic guarantees.

The famous quote by Sebastian Thrun captures it best:

> *"SLAM is a robot's way of figuring out 'I am here, and the world looks like this.'"*

### Real-World Domains Where SLAM is Applied

- **Autonomous Vehicles** (Google/Waymo, Tesla FSD)
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots** (iRobot Roomba, Xiaomi Mi Robot)
- **Augmented Reality (AR)** (Apple ARKit, Google ARCore)
- **Underwater Exploration** (AUVs mapping the ocean floor)
- **Aerial Drones** (DJI mapping and inspection)
- **Planetary Rovers** (NASA Mars rovers: Spirit, Opportunity, Perseverance)
- **Medical Robotics** (endoscopic capsule mapping the gut)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SLAM Problem — Decomposed Logic

The SLAM problem is decomposed into three intertwined sub-problems:

### Step 1: State Prediction (Motion Model)
- **Why:** We need to predict where the robot will be after applying control input $u_t$.
- **How:** Apply the kinematic/dynamic model of the robot to its previous pose.

$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$$

where $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ is the process noise (Gaussian with covariance $R_t$).

### Step 2: Measurement Update (Observation Model)
- **Why:** Correct the predicted pose using sensor measurements.
- **How:** Compare predicted sensor readings with actual readings.

$$z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$$

where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ is the measurement noise.

### Step 3: Map Augmentation / Loop Closure
- **Why:** Add new landmarks to the map OR recognize previously visited places.
- **How:** Update landmark positions and check for matches with existing landmarks.

$$m_k = g(z_t, x_t)$$

---

## 2.2 Classification of SLAM Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask this classification. Memorize the table below.

| Type | Core Algorithm | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EKF-SLAM** | Extended Kalman Filter | Mathematically rigorous, smooth estimates | $O(N^2)$ complexity, sensitive to data association |
| **FastSLAM** | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Handles non-linearities, efficient for many landmarks | Particle depletion, dimensionality issues |
| **Graph-SLAM** | Pose Graph Optimization | Highly accurate, sparse solver support | Requires loop closures, offline batch processing |
| **UKF-SLAM** | Unscented Kalman Filter | Better for non-linear models than EKF | Higher computational cost |
| **Visual SLAM (vSLAM)** | ORB-SLAM, LSD-SLAM | Cheap sensors (cameras), rich features | Lighting sensitive |
| **LiDAR SLAM** | LOAM, Cartographer | Geometric accuracy, lighting invariant | Expensive sensors, heavy data |
| **RTAB-Map** | Appearance-based | Real-time, RGB-D + stereo support | Memory-bound for large maps |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with Thrun, Burgard & Fox (*Probabilistic Robotics*, MIT Press) — the standard reference adopted by KTU 2024.

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Full SLAM posterior | Probability density |
| 2 | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Predicted mean (motion) | Pose vector $\in \mathbb{R}^3$ |
| 3 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$ | Predicted covariance | $\in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ for pose |
| 4 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | $\in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$ |
| 5 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ | Updated mean | Pose correction |
| 6 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Updated covariance | $\in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ |
| 7 | $J_t = H_t^T Q_t^{-1} H_t$ | Information matrix contribution | Sparse structure |
| 8 | $\Omega_t = \Omega_{t-1} + \sum_i J_t^{(i)}$ | Global information matrix | Used in Graph-SLAM |
| 9 | $\xi^* = \arg\min_\xi \sum_{ij} e_{ij}^T \Omega_{ij} e_{ij}$ | Graph-SLAM objective | Pose graph optimization |
| 10 | $H(x,y,\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -dy \\ 0 & 1 & dx \end{bmatrix}$ | Jacobian for landmark observation | $dx = x_l - x_r, dy = y_l - y_r$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Memorize Eq. (1) and Eq. (9)** — these are the most frequently asked in KTU university exams.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | SLAM Use-Case | Engineering Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous Driving** | Real-time lane-level localization | Safety-critical decision making |
| **Warehouse Robotics** (Amazon Kiva) | Path planning in dynamic spaces | Efficiency: 50% reduction in order time |
| **Search & Rescue** | Mapping collapsed buildings | Life-saving situational awareness |
| **Agriculture** | Autonomous tractors & drones | Precision farming with cm-level accuracy |
| **Space Exploration** | Mars rover autonomous navigation | Mission-critical on alien terrain |
| **Healthcare** | Capsule endoscopy | Non-invasive diagnosis |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EKF-SLAM Update Equations

The EKF-SLAM algorithm is derived by linearizing the non-linear motion and observation models around the current estimate.

### Derivation of the Motion Update

Starting from the non-linear motion model:

$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$$

We linearize it via a first-order Taylor expansion about the current mean $\mu_{t-1}$:

$$f(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + F_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$

where $F_t$ is the Jacobian of $f$ with respect to $x$:

$$F_t = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = \mu_{t-1}}$$

The predicted state is then:

$$\bar{\mu}_t = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

The covariance propagates as:

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + R_t$$

This is **Eq. (3)** in our formula sheet.

### Derivation of the Observation Update

The non-linear observation model is:

$$z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$$

Linearizing about $\bar{\mu}_t$:

$$h(x_t) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)$$

where:

$$H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = \bar{\mu}_t}$$

For a landmark observation with range $r$ and bearing $\phi$:

$$h(x_t, m_j) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{j,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{j,y} - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{j,y} - y_t,\ m_{j,x} - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

The Jacobian for a 2D landmark observation is:

$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{dx}{q} & -\frac{dy}{q} & 0 \\ \frac{dy}{dx^2 + dy^2} & -\frac{dx}{dx^2 + dy^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $dx = m_{j,x} - x_t$, $dy = m_{j,y} - y_t$, and $q = \sqrt{dx^2 + dy^2}$.

### Derivation of Kalman Gain

The innovation (measurement residual) is:

$$\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$$

The innovation covariance is:

$$S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$$

The Kalman gain minimizes the posterior covariance:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$$

This is **Eq. (4)** in our formula sheet.

### Derivation of the Posterior Update

The updated state estimate is:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$$

Substituting the innovation:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$$

This matches **Eq. (5)**.

The posterior covariance is derived by substituting the corrected state back into the covariance recursion:

$$\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - K_t H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

This matches **Eq. (6)**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Sensor-to-Landmark Bearing)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks **numerical problems** on EKF-SLAM with a single landmark. Practice the worked example below until you can solve it in under 6 minutes.

**Given:**
- Robot pose estimate: $x_{t-1} = [0,\ 0,\ 0]^T$ (x, y, θ in meters/radians)
- Control input: $u_t = [0.5\ \text{m forward},\ 0\ \text{rad turn}]^T$
- Motion model (linear velocity $v$):

$$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1} + v \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ y_{t-1} + v \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ \theta_{t-1} \end{bmatrix} + w_t$$

- Motion noise: $R_t = \text{diag}(0.04,\ 0.04,\ 0.01)$
- Landmark at: $m = [3,\ 2]^T$
- Observation: $z_t = [2.236\ \text{m},\ 0.4636\ \text{rad}]^T$ (range, bearing)
- Measurement noise: $Q_t = \text{diag}(0.05,\ 0.02)$
- Initial covariance: $\Sigma_{t-1} = \text{diag}(0.1,\ 0.1,\ 0.05)$

**Step 1 — Predicted Pose:**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0 + 0.5 \cdot \cos(0) \\ 0 + 0.5 \cdot \sin(0) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Predicted Covariance:**
Jacobian of motion model $F_t = I$ (identity for constant velocity model with $v$ embedded).

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + R_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.14 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.14 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.06 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Predicted Observation:**

$$dx = 3 - 0.5 = 2.5, \quad dy = 2 - 0 = 2, \quad q = \sqrt{2.5^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{10.25} \approx 3.2016$$

$$\hat{z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} q \\ \text{atan2}(2,\ 2.5) - 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.2016 \\ \text{atan2}(2,\ 2.5) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\text{atan2}(2,\ 2.5) = 0.6747\ \text{rad}$$

$$\hat{z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 3.2016 \\ 0.6747 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Innovation:**

$$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 2.236 - 3.2016 \\ 0.4636 - 0.6747 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9656 \\ -0.2111 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Jacobian $H_t$:**

$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{2.5}{3.2016} & -\frac{2}{3.2016} & 0 \\ \frac{2}{10.25} & -\frac{2.5}{10.25} & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7809 & -0.6247 & 0 \\ 0.1951 & -0.2439 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Innovation Covariance $S_t$:**

$$S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$$

Compute $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t$:

$$H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7809 \cdot 0.14 & -0.6247 \cdot 0.14 & 0 \\ 0.1951 \cdot 0.14 & -0.2439 \cdot 0.14 & -1 \cdot 0.06 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1093 & -0.0875 & 0 \\ 0.0273 & -0.0341 & -0.06 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T$:

$$= \begin{bmatrix} -0.7809 & -0.6247 & 0 \\ 0.1951 & -0.2439 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -0.1093 & 0.0273 \\ -0.0875 & -0.0341 \\ 0 & -0.06 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.0853 + 0.0547 & 0.0213 - 0.0213 \\ -0.0213 + 0.0213 & 0.0053 + 0.0083 + 0.06 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.14 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0736 \end{bmatrix}$$

Add $Q_t$:

$$S_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.14 + 0.05 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0736 + 0.02 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.19 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0936 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Kalman Gain:**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.14 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.14 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.06 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -0.7809 & 0.1951 \\ -0.6247 & -0.2439 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 5.263 & 0 \\ 0 & 10.684 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1093 & 0.0273 \\ -0.0875 & -0.0341 \\ 0 & -0.06 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$K_t = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1093 \cdot 5.263 & 0.0273 \cdot 10.684 \\ -0.0875 \cdot 5.263 & -0.0341 \cdot 10.684 \\ 0 & -0.6410 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5753 & 0.2917 \\ -0.4605 & -0.3643 \\ 0 & -0.6410 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Updated Pose:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -0.5753 \cdot (-0.9656) + 0.2917 \cdot (-0.2111) \\ -0.4605 \cdot (-0.9656) + (-0.3643) \cdot (-0.2111) \\ 0 + (-0.6410) \cdot (-0.2111) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5556 - 0.0616 \\ 0.4446 + 0.0769 \\ 0.1353 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9940 \\ 0.5215 \\ 0.1353 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 9 — Updated Covariance:**

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

The result is a reduced $3 \times 3$ matrix, computed analogously. The trace of $\Sigma_t$ will be smaller than the trace of $\bar{\Sigma}_t$, demonstrating **information gain from the sensor measurement**.

> [!TIP]
> This numerical example tests your ability to: (i) compute the Jacobian correctly, (ii) handle the innovation sequence, and (iii) understand the geometric meaning of EKF-SLAM. Replicate this with different landmark positions to master the workflow.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SLAM System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sensor Suite: LiDAR, Camera, IMU, Wheel Encoders] --> B[Data Preprocessing & Synchronization]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction Module]
    C --> D{Data Association Block}
    D -->|Match Found| E[State Prediction: Motion Model u_t]
    E --> F[Measurement Update: Observation Model z_t]
    F --> G[Pose Graph Optimization]
    G --> H[Updated Map Database]
    H --> I[Loop Closure Detection]
    I --> D
    D -->|New Landmark| J[Map Augmentation]
    J --> H
    H --> K[Robot Pose Estimate x_t]
    K --> L[Path Planning & Navigation Module]
    L --> M[Actuator Commands to Robot]
    M --> A
    H --> N[Visualization: RViz / Custom UI]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The SLAM pipeline is a closed-loop feedback system. Sensor data → Feature extraction → Data association → State estimation → Map update → Loop closure detection → (back to data association). The estimate is consumed by path planning for action generation.

## 4.2 EKF-SLAM Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Init[Initialization: x0, Sigma0] --> Pred[Predict: x-bar and Sigma-bar]
    Pred --> Meas[Acquire Measurement z_t]
    Meas --> Assoc{Data Association}
    Assoc -->|Known Landmark| KF[Compute Kalman Gain K_t]
    KF --> Upd[Update: x_t and Sigma_t]
    Upd --> Map[Update Landmark Map]
    Map --> Pred
    Assoc -->|Unknown Landmark| NewL[Initialize New Landmark in Map]
    NewL --> Map
```

## 4.3 SLAM Problem State-Space Graph

```mermaid
graph TB
    X0[Pose x_0] -->|u_1| X1[Pose x_1]
    X1 -->|u_2| X2[Pose x_2]
    X2 -->|u_3| X3[Pose x_3]
    X1 -.->|z_1| M1[Landmark m_1]
    X2 -.->|z_2| M1
    X3 -.->|z_3| M2[Landmark m_2]
    X3 -.->|z_4| M1
    X0 -.->|z_0| M2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graph-SLAM interpretation:** Solid arrows represent **motion constraints** (odometry). Dotted arrows represent **measurement constraints** (sensor observations). The goal of Graph-SLAM is to find the configuration of all nodes that best satisfies these constraints, which is a **non-linear least-squares problem**.

## 4.4 Concept Dependency Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    P[Probability Theory] --> B[Bayes Filter]
    B --> K[Kalman Filter]
    K --> E[EKF]
    E --> ES[EKF-SLAM]
    B --> PF[Particle Filter]
    PF --> FS[FastSLAM]
    ES --> GS[Graph-SLAM]
    FS --> GS
    GS --> S[Modern SLAM: ORB-SLAM, LOAM, Cartographer]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]`
**Q: Define the SLAM problem mathematically and explain why it is considered computationally hard.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The SLAM problem is defined as the recursive estimation of the joint posterior over the robot pose $x_t$ and map $m$ given all sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Why it is computationally hard (1 mark each):**
1. **High dimensionality:** The state space grows with the number of landmarks (continuous and growing).
2. **Non-linear sensor models:** Requires approximations (EKF, UKF, particle filters) that scale poorly.
3. **Data association ambiguity:** Matching observations to landmarks is NP-hard in the worst case.
4. **Loop closure complexity:** Detecting revisited places requires global search, often $O(N^2)$ or worse.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]`
**Q: Differentiate between EKF-SLAM and FastSLAM. List one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM |
|---|---|---|
| **Filter Type** | Single Extended Kalman Filter | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter |
| **State Representation** | Joint state (pose + all landmarks) | Pose per particle, independent EKF per landmark |
| **Complexity** | $O(N^2)$ in landmark count | $O(M \log N)$ with $M$ particles |
| **Advantage** | Smooth, continuous estimates; well-understood convergence | Handles non-linearities better; scales to larger maps |

(2 marks for differentiation, 1 mark for advantages.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With suitable block diagram, explain the architecture of a SLAM system. Discuss the role of prediction, observation, and update phases in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot is at pose $[1.0, 2.0, \pi/4]^T$. It moves with control input $u_t = [v=0.8\ \text{m/s},\ \omega=0.2\ \text{rad/s}]$ for $\Delta t = 0.5\ \text{s}$. Compute the new pose using the motion model. Also compute the predicted observation (range and bearing) to a landmark at $[5.0, 4.0]^T$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) SLAM Architecture (7 Marks):**

The SLAM system has four main modules as shown in the block diagram:

**1. Sensor Module (1 mark):** Collects raw data from LiDAR, cameras, IMU, wheel encoders.

**2. Prediction Phase (2 marks):** Uses the motion model $f(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ and the previous pose estimate to predict the current pose. Propagates uncertainty via $\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + R_t$. Handles dead reckoning errors gracefully.

**3. Observation Phase (2 marks):** Predicts what the sensor should see given the predicted pose and current map. Computes innovation $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ by comparing actual and predicted measurements.

**4. Update Phase (2 marks):** Computes Kalman gain $K_t$, updates the pose $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$, and updates covariance $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$. Loop closure detection triggers global map correction.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply motion model (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_t &= x_{t-1} + v \cos(\theta_{t-1}) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} + v \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \omega \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting values:

$$x_t = 1.0 + 0.8 \cdot \cos(\pi/4) \cdot 0.5 = 1.0 + 0.8 \cdot 0.7071 \cdot 0.5 = 1.2828\ \text{m}$$

$$y_t = 2.0 + 0.8 \cdot \sin(\pi/4) \cdot 0.5 = 2.0 + 0.8 \cdot 0.7071 \cdot 0.5 = 2.2828\ \text{m}$$

$$\theta_t = \pi/4 + 0.2 \cdot 0.5 = \pi/4 + 0.1 \approx 0.8854\ \text{rad}$$

New pose: $\bar{\mu}_t = [1.2828,\ 2.2828,\ 0.8854]^T$

**Step 2 — Predicted observation (4 marks):**

Compute relative position:

$$dx = m_x - x_t = 5.0 - 1.2828 = 3.7172$$
$$dy = m_y - y_t = 4.0 - 2.2828 = 1.7172$$
$$q = \sqrt{dx^2 + dy^2} = \sqrt{3.7172^2 + 1.7172^2} = \sqrt{13.8176 + 2.9488} = \sqrt{16.7664} \approx 4.0947\ \text{m}$$

Compute bearing:

$$\phi = \text{atan2}(dy, dx) - \theta_t = \text{atan2}(1.7172, 3.7172) - 0.8854$$
$$\text{atan2}(1.7172, 3.7172) = 0.4322\ \text{rad}$$
$$\phi = 0.4322 - 0.8854 = -0.4532\ \text{rad} \approx -25.97^\circ$$

Predicted observation: $\hat{z}_t = [4.0947\ \text{m},\ -0.4532\ \text{rad}]^T$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to subtract the robot's orientation $\theta_t$ from the global bearing. This is the most common 2-mark loss in EKF-SLAM numerical problems. Also, ensure your angle is normalized to $(-\pi, \pi]$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the various sensors used in SLAM. Compare LiDAR-based and visual SLAM in terms of accuracy, cost, and computational requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the EKF-SLAM update equations starting from the Bayes filter recursion. Show explicitly the linearization of the non-linear motion and observation models using Jacobians. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Sensors in SLAM (7 Marks):**

| Sensor | Type | Range | Accuracy | Cost | SLAM Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wheel Encoders** | Proprioceptive | Unlimited | High (short-term) | Very low | Odometry |
| **IMU** | Proprioceptive | Unlimited | Drift over time | Medium | Orientation, dead reckoning |
| **LiDAR 2D** | Exteroceptive | 10–100 m | cm-level | High | 2D mapping |
| **LiDAR 3D** | Exteroceptive | 10–200 m | cm-level | Very high | 3D mapping |
| **Mono Camera** | Exteroceptive | 0.5–∞ m | Pixel-level | Low | Visual SLAM |
| **Stereo Camera** | Exteroceptive | 0.5–50 m | Sub-cm (close) | Medium | Depth-aware vSLAM |
| **RGB-D Camera** | Exteroceptive | 0.1–10 m | mm-cm (close) | Low–Medium | Indoor vSLAM |
| **GPS** | Exteroceptive | Global | 1–5 m | Low | Outdoor coarse localization |
| **Ultrasonic** | Exteroceptive | 0.1–10 m | Low | Very low | Simple obstacle avoidance |

(3 marks for sensor table, 4 marks for comparison.)

**LiDAR vs Visual SLAM Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | LiDAR SLAM | Visual SLAM |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | cm-level geometric | Pixel-level photometric |
| **Cost** | High (₹1–10 lakh for 3D) | Low (₹500–5000 for camera) |
| **Computation** | Moderate (point cloud) | High (feature extraction) |
| **Lighting** | Invariant | Highly sensitive |
| **Texture Need** | No | Yes (for feature-based) |
| **Range** | Long (100 m+) | Variable (depth-dependent) |

---

**(b) Derivation of EKF-SLAM Update (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Bayes Filter Recursion (2 marks):**

The SLAM posterior is recursively updated as:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) dx_{t-1}$$

where $\eta$ is a normalization constant. This is intractable in closed form, motivating the EKF approximation.

**Step 2 — Linearization of Motion Model (2 marks):**

The non-linear motion model $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ is linearized via first-order Taylor expansion:

$$f(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + F_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$

$$F_t = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = \mu_{t-1}}$$

The prediction step gives:

$$\bar{\mu}_t = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t), \quad \bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + R_t$$

**Step 3 — Linearization of Observation Model and Update (3 marks):**

$$h(x_t, m_j) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_j) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t), \quad H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = \bar{\mu}_t}$$

Computing the Kalman gain to minimize posterior covariance:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

Final state and covariance update:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_j))$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often skip the Taylor expansion step and directly write the EKF equations, losing 1–2 marks. Always show the linearization explicitly. Also, the Jacobian must be evaluated at the **predicted** state $\bar{\mu}_t$, not the previous state $\mu_{t-1}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 SLAM Module**

- **Core SLAM Posterior:** $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Three SLAM Sub-problems:** Prediction, Observation Update, Map Augmentation.
- **EKF-SLAM equations:** Prediction (Eq. 3), Kalman Gain (Eq. 4), Update (Eq. 5, 6) — **know all 4 by heart**.
- **Graph-SLAM objective:** $\xi^* = \arg\min_\xi \sum_{ij} e_{ij}^T \Omega_{ij} e_{ij}$ — frequently asked.
- **Jacobian structure:** Always $2 \times 3$ for range-bearing observation of a 2D landmark.
- **Loop closure:** A mechanism to recognize previously visited locations; key to long-term SLAM accuracy.
- **Data association:** Nearest Neighbor (NN), Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB), Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC).
- **Complexity:** EKF-SLAM is $O(N^2)$, FastSLAM is $O(M \log N)$ with $M$ particles.
- **Sensors:** LiDAR is geometric and lighting-invariant; cameras are cheap but lighting-sensitive.
- **Modern SLAM systems to know:** ORB-SLAM2/3, LOAM, Cartographer, RTAB-Map, LIO-SAM.
- **Open problem:** Lifelong SLAM (handling dynamic environments, lifelong mapping without memory explosion).
- **Map representations:** Feature map, occupancy grid, topological map, dense point cloud.
- **Covariance inflation:** A practical issue in EKF-SLAM — the filter becomes **overconfident** in consistent maps; consistency restoration methods exist (Neira, Tardós).
- **The SLAM convergence theorem** (Dissanayake et al., 2001): The determinant of the landmark covariance matrix monotonically decreases as more observations are made — i.e., the map becomes more accurate as SLAM proceeds.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** Before the exam, draw the SLAM block diagram from memory, write down the Bayes filter recursion, and the four EKF-SLAM equations. If you can do this in under 5 minutes, you are well-prepared.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Localization: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Localization: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Robot Localization** is the process of determining the **pose** (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) of a robot within a reference frame or a known map, given sensor observations and control actions over time.

Mathematically, the localization problem is defined as estimating the posterior probability density of the robot's state $x_t$ at time $t$, conditioned on all available sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

where:
- $x_t$ = robot state (pose) at time $t$
- $z_{1:t}$ = sequence of sensor measurements from time $1$ to $t$
- $u_{1:t}$ = sequence of control actions (odometry) from time $1$ to $t$
- $m$ = known map of the environment

The result of localization is a **belief** $\text{bel}(x_t)$ which is a probability distribution over all possible robot poses.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Finding Yourself in a Dark Building**

Imagine you wake up in a completely dark, unfamiliar shopping mall with a torch (flashlight) and a paper map. You cannot see the entrance, but as you walk, the torch illuminates small regions (pillars, walls, shops). You compare what you see with the map to figure out where you are.

- **The torch** → Sensors (LIDAR, camera, sonar)
- **Your footsteps** → Odometry / control inputs $u_t$
- **The paper map** → Known environment map $m$
- **Your mental estimate of "where you are"** → Belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$
- **Each torch flash gives clues** → Sensor measurement $z_t$

Initially, you are **lost** (uniform belief everywhere). As you walk and observe, your belief becomes **sharper** (more confident about your pose). This iterative refinement is exactly what **probabilistic localization** does mathematically.

---

### 1.3 Why Localization is Considered a "Hard" Problem

> [!WARNING]
> Localization is non-trivial because of **inherent uncertainty** in:
> 1. **Sensors** — Every sensor has noise (Gaussian, bias, drift).
> 2. **Actuators** — Wheel slippage causes odometry errors that grow unboundedly with distance.
> 3. **Environment** — Dynamic objects, symmetry (corridors), and perceptual aliasing make places look identical.
> 4. **Initial Knowledge** — The robot may have **no prior idea** of where it is (**global localization** problem).

In KTU parlance, this is often called the **"Where am I?"** problem and is one of the most fundamental challenges in mobile robotics.

---

### 1.4 Key Physical & Mathematical Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Robot pose dimension | $n$ | 2D: $n = 3$ $(x, y, \theta)$ |
| Map resolution | $\Delta$ | **5 cm – 10 cm** (occupancy grids) |
| Odometry error growth | $\sigma_{odom}$ | **1% – 5%** of distance traveled |
| Sensor update rate | $f_s$ | **10 Hz – 40 Hz** (typical LIDAR) |
| Belief update frequency | $f_b$ | Matches sensor rate |

---

### 1.5 Historical Note (KTU Context)

> [!NOTE]
> Localization techniques have evolved from **dead reckoning** (1950s) → **Kalman filter-based** (1980s) → **Markov / Grid-based** (1990s) → **Monte Carlo / Particle filters** (2000s) → **Deep learning–assisted** (2018+). KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the first four paradigms.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Localization Problem — Structural Breakdown

The localization problem is solved by recursively estimating the robot's belief through two alternating steps:

**Step 1: Motion Update (Prediction)**
- Input: Control action $u_t$ and previous belief $\text{bel}(x_{t-1})$
- Action: Propagate belief through the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$
- Output: Predicted belief $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ (before seeing new sensor data)

**Step 2: Measurement Update (Correction)**
- Input: Sensor measurement $z_t$ and predicted belief $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$
- Action: Multiply by sensor model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ and normalize
- Output: Posterior belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$

> **Why this order?**
> The motion model says "where could I be if I only used my wheels?" The sensor model says "given what I see, where am I most likely?" Combining both is more robust than relying on either alone.

---

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Localization Problems (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

| Category | Sub-type | Description | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local vs Global** | Position tracking | Initial pose known; small drift | Easy |
| | Global localization | Initial pose unknown | Hard |
| | Kidnapped robot | Robot teleported mid-run | Hardest |
| **Static vs Dynamic** | Static world | Map doesn't change | Easier |
| | Dynamic world | People, moving objects | Harder |
| **Single vs Multi** | Single robot | Centralized belief | Standard |
| | Multi-robot | Cooperative estimation | Advanced |
| **Map-based vs Mapless** | With known map $m$ | Classical localization | KTU focus |
| | Mapless (SLAM) | Build map & localize together | KTU Module 3+ |

---

### 2.3 Sources of Uncertainty

1. **Sensor noise** — Gaussian noise in LIDAR range, camera pixel error.
2. **Actuator / odometry error** — Wheel slippage, encoder quantization.
3. **Modeling errors** — Imperfect kinematic models.
4. **Perceptual aliasing** — Different places looking identical (long corridors).
5. **Initial uncertainty** — Uniform prior in global localization.

---

### 2.4 Belief Representations (CRITICAL for KTU)

| Representation | Belief Type | Algorithm | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Gaussian** | Unimodal | Kalman Filter | Efficient, optimal for linear-Gaussian | Fails under multi-modal beliefs |
| **Mixture of Gaussians** | Multi-modal | GMF, UKF | Handles ambiguity | Complex to tune |
| **Histogram grid** | Discrete | Markov Localization | Handles global & multi-modal | Resolution-limited |
| **Particle set** | Sample-based | Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) | Handles any distribution | Computationally heavier |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The four canonical probabilistic localization algorithms are:
> 1. **Markov Localization** (grid-based, discrete Bayes)
> 2. **Kalman Filter Localization** (EKF, Gaussian)
> 3. **Multi-Hypothesis Tracking** (mixture of Gaussians)
> 4. **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** (particle filter)

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are examinable under Module 2 of PBRAT604.**

**Bayes Filter (Continuous form):**
$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Discrete Bayes Filter — Prediction step:**
$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})$$

**Discrete Bayes Filter — Measurement update step:**
$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizer:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{\sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)}$$

**Total Probability Theorem (used in prediction):**
$$p(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Bayes Rule (used in correction):**
$$p(x \mid z) = \eta \, p(z \mid x) \, p(x)$$

**Markov Assumption:**
$$p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Odometry error growth (dead reckoning):**
$$\sigma_{x_t} \approx \sigma_{x_0} + k_{xy} \, \Delta d$$

> ⚠ **Note on symbols:** In the table above, $\eta$ (eta) denotes the normalizer constant, **not** a unit; $p(\cdot)$ denotes probability density; $\sum$ and $\int$ represent summation over discrete states and integration over continuous states respectively.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Cars** | Self-driving cars use particle filter–based MCL for GPS-denied tunnels. |
| **Warehouse AGVs** | Amazon Kiva robots use Markov localization over grid maps. |
| **Aerial Drones** | EKF localization combines IMU + GPS + vision (VIO). |
| **Planetary Rovers** | NASA's Mars rovers use Kalman-filter localization with stereo vision. |
| **Domestic Vacuum Cleaners** | Roomba uses simple Markov localization on occupancy grids. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Recursive Bayes Filter for Localization

We start from the definition of the **posterior belief** and apply the theorem of total probability and Bayes rule.

**Step 1: Define the belief as a posterior.**

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

**Step 2: Use the definition of conditional probability.**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Markov assumption (the future is independent of the past given the present state and current measurement).**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

> *Justification:* Under the Markov assumption, the current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ and the static map $m$, not on the history of measurements or controls.

**Step 4: Substitute the simplification back.**

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizer (a constant with respect to $x_t$).

**Step 5: Apply the law of total probability over the previous state $x_{t-1}$.**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 6: Apply Markov assumption again to the motion model.**

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

> *Justification:* The current state $x_t$ depends only on the previous state $x_{t-1}$ and the current control $u_t$, not on the full history of measurements.

**Step 7: Recognize that the second term is the prior belief from the previous time step.**

$$p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \text{bel}(x_{t-1})$$

> *Justification:* Since the control $u_t$ has not yet been "applied" in $\text{bel}(x_{t-1})$, conditioning on $u_{1:t}$ versus $u_{1:t-1}$ yields the same value for $x_{t-1}$.

**Step 8: Assemble the final recursive Bayes filter.**

$$\boxed{\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

This is the **fundamental recursive estimation equation** for probabilistic localization.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison: Why Probabilistic Localization Wins

| Property | Geometric Localization (e.g., triangle/trilateration) | Dead Reckoning (odometry only) | Probabilistic Localization |
|---|---|---|---|
| Handles sensor noise | Poor | Doesn't address it | Excellent |
| Handles global localization | No | No | Yes |
| Computes confidence | No | No | Yes (belief shape) |
| Combines multiple sensors | Difficult | Limited | Native (Bayes fusion) |
| Computational cost | Low | Very low | Moderate–High |
| KTU 2024 relevance | Low | Low | **High** |

> **Take-away for KTU exams:** Always argue in favor of probabilistic methods when asked "Why localization needs probability theory?" The answer is: **sensors and actuators are noisy, environments are ambiguous, and decisions must be made under uncertainty.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Discrete Bayes Filter on a 1-D Corridor

Consider a robot in a 1-D corridor with **3 cells**: $x \in \{1, 2, 3\}$.

Initial belief: $\text{bel}(x_0) = [0.6, 0.3, 0.1]$ (robot "thinks" it is in cell 1).

**Robot moves right with motion model:**

$$p(x_t = k \mid u_t = R, x_{t-1} = j) = \begin{cases} 0.8 & \text{if } k = j+1 \\ 0.2 & \text{if } k = j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1: Prediction.**

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t = 1) = p(x_t=1 \mid R, x_{t-1}=1) \cdot \text{bel}(x_0=1) = 0.2 \times 0.6 = 0.12$$

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t = 2) = 0.8 \times 0.6 + 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.48 + 0.06 = 0.54$$

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t = 3) = 0.8 \times 0.3 + 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.24 + 0.02 = 0.26$$

> *Intuition:* The belief "shifts" right because the robot moved right. The mass is now concentrated around cell 2.

**Step 2: Measurement.** Suppose sensor reports "doorway detected" with likelihood:

$$p(z_t = \text{door} \mid x_t, m) = [0.1, 0.9, 0.2]$$

**Step 3: Multiply elementwise (unnormalized posterior).**

$$\text{bel}_{\text{unnorm}}(x_t) = [0.12 \times 0.1, \; 0.54 \times 0.9, \; 0.26 \times 0.2] = [0.012, 0.486, 0.052]$$

**Step 4: Normalize.**

$$\eta = \frac{1}{0.012 + 0.486 + 0.052} = \frac{1}{0.550} \approx 1.818$$

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = [0.022, \; 0.884, \; 0.094]$$

> *Result:* The robot is now **88.4% confident** it is in cell 2 — a sharp peak. The measurement has dramatically localized the robot.

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete recursive localization architecture** as it should be drawn in KTU exam answers.

    G[Known Map m Static] --> E
    E --> H[Posterior Belief bel xt Normalized]

    I -->|Above Threshold| K[Output Robot Pose Estimate xt hat]

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank 2024 Scheme]**
*Define mobile robot localization. Why is probability theory essential for solving it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mobile robot localization is the process of estimating the **pose** $(x, y, \theta)$ of a robot within a known map $m$, using sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control actions $u_{1:t}$ over time.

Probability theory is essential because:
1. **Sensors are noisy** — measurements have inherent uncertainty.
2. **Actuators are imprecise** — wheel slippage causes odometry drift.
3. **Environment is ambiguous** — symmetric places (corridors) produce perceptual aliasing.
4. **Initial pose may be unknown** — a belief distribution captures this uncertainty compactly.

The result is a **belief** $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$, a probability distribution over all possible poses, rather than a single point estimate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between position tracking, global localization, and the kidnapped robot problem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Problem Type | Initial Pose | Difficulty | Belief Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Known approximately | Easy | Unimodal, narrow Gaussian |
| **Global Localization** | Completely unknown | Hard | Initially uniform, multi-modal |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Unknown + can be teleported mid-run | Hardest | Must recover from catastrophic failure |

> **Key distinction:** Global localization requires the robot to "search" across the entire map, while position tracking only refines an existing estimate. The kidnapped robot problem additionally tests **recovery** capability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE, Module 2 typically offers an internal choice. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.** Both questions carry 14 marks (split as $7 + 7$).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam Pattern]

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Derive the recursive Bayes filter equation for mobile robot localization. Clearly state the Markov assumption and explain its physical significance.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the four main challenges in mobile robot localization. How does probabilistic localization address each?

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

We start with the posterior:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

**Step 1:** Apply Bayes' rule:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Markov assumption** to the measurement model:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

> *Physical meaning:* The current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ and map $m$, **not** on the history.

**Step 3:** Apply the law of total probability:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Markov to motion model:

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the prior belief:

$$p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \text{bel}(x_{t-1})$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

The four main challenges are:

1. **Sensor noise** — Handled by probabilistic sensor models $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ that explicitly model noise distributions (e.g., Gaussian).
2. **Actuator / odometry error** — Captured by motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ that accounts for slip and drift.
