3. **Perceptual aliasing** — Solved using **multi-hypothesis beliefs** (particles, histograms) that can represent multiple likely poses simultaneously.
4. **Kidnapped robot / global localization** — Addressed by initializing belief uniformly over the map and using particle injection to escape local minima.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Forgetting to include the **normalizer $\eta$** costs 1 mark.
> - Failing to clearly **state the Markov assumption** before applying it costs 2 marks.
> - Skipping intermediate steps in the derivation (jumping straight to the final integral) costs 1–2 marks.
> - Confusing the **measurement model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ with the **motion model** $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ is a common error — mark down 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** Compare Markov localization, Kalman filter localization, and Monte Carlo localization (MCL) in terms of belief representation, computational complexity, and ability to handle global vs. local localization.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** A mobile robot operates in a 1-D corridor of 5 cells with the following motion model (right shift): $p(x_t = k \mid u_t = R, x_{t-1} = j) = 0.7$ if $k = j+1$, $0.3$ if $k = j$, $0$ otherwise. Initial belief $\text{bel}(x_0) = [0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.05, 0.05]$. After moving right once, the sensor reports "wall detected" with $p(z_t = \text{wall} \mid x_t, m) = [0.1, 0.2, 0.7, 0.3, 0.1]$. Compute the posterior belief $\text{bel}(x_1)$.

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

| Feature | Markov Localization | Kalman Filter | Monte Carlo Localization |
|---|---|---|---|
| Belief type | Discrete histogram | Single Gaussian | Set of weighted samples |
| State space | Discrete grid | Continuous | Continuous |
| Computational cost | $O(\vert X \vert)$ per step | $O(n^2)$ for $n$-dim state | $O(N)$ for $N$ particles |
| Global localization | Yes (with fine grid) | No (unimodal) | Yes (with particle injection) |
| Position tracking | Yes | Yes (optimal for linear-Gaussian) | Yes |
| Handles multi-modal | Yes | No | Yes |
| KTU preference | Conceptual clarity | Mathematical rigor | Practical deployment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** If the question says "global localization," recommend **Markov** or **MCL**. If it says "linear system with Gaussian noise," recommend **Kalman**. If ambiguity is mentioned, recommend **MCL**.

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Step 1: Prediction.**

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_1 = 1) = 0.3 \times 0.4 = 0.12$$

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_1 = 2) = 0.7 \times 0.4 + 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.28 + 0.09 = 0.37$$

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_1 = 3) = 0.7 \times 0.3 + 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.21 + 0.06 = 0.27$$

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_1 = 4) = 0.7 \times 0.2 + 0.3 \times 0.05 = 0.14 + 0.015 = 0.155$$

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_1 = 5) = 0.7 \times 0.05 + 0.3 \times 0.05 = 0.035 + 0.015 = 0.05$$

So $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_1) = [0.12, \, 0.37, \, 0.27, \, 0.155, \, 0.05]$.

**Step 2: Multiply by sensor model (elementwise).**

$$\text{bel}_{\text{unnorm}} = [0.12 \times 0.1, \; 0.37 \times 0.2, \; 0.27 \times 0.7, \; 0.155 \times 0.3, \; 0.05 \times 0.1]$$

$$= [0.012, \; 0.074, \; 0.189, \; 0.0465, \; 0.005]$$

**Step 3: Sum for normalizer.**

$$\Sigma = 0.012 + 0.074 + 0.189 + 0.0465 + 0.005 = 0.3265$$

$$\eta = \frac{1}{0.3265} \approx 3.063$$

**Step 4: Normalize.**

$$\text{bel}(x_1) \approx [0.037, \; 0.227, \; 0.579, \; 0.142, \; 0.015]$$

> *Interpretation:* The robot is now **57.9% confident** it is in cell 3, which matches the sensor model peak. The belief has correctly localized.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (for Q.B b):**
> - **Failing to normalize** the posterior — student loses 2 marks.
> - Using the **wrong sensor likelihood index** (mixing up cells) — loses 1 mark.
> - **Off-by-one errors** in the convolution (forgetting $0.3 \times$ prior for the "stay" case) — loses 1 mark.
> - Not writing the **intermediate predicted belief** explicitly — loses 1 mark for lack of clarity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Localization Introduction**

- [x] **Definition:** Localization = estimating robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$ within a known map $m$.
- [x] **Formal result:** $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$ — a probability distribution, not a single point.
- [x] **Two-step recursion:** Prediction (motion model) → Correction (sensor model).
- [x] **Markov assumption** is the cornerstone — present state summarizes all relevant past.
- [x] **Normalizer $\eta$** is mandatory in the measurement update — never omit it.
- [x] **Three problem classes:** Position tracking (easy) → Global localization (hard) → Kidnapped robot (hardest).
- [x] **Four algorithm families:** Markov (grid), Kalman (Gaussian), Multi-hypothesis (GMoG), Monte Carlo (particles).
- [x] **Noise sources:** Sensor noise, odometry drift, modeling error, perceptual aliasing.
- [x] **Real-world deployment:** Warehouse AGVs (Markov), self-driving cars (MCL + EKF), Mars rovers (EKF), drones (UKF/VIO).
- [x] **Key insight to memorize:** Probabilistic localization doesn't eliminate uncertainty — it **manages and propagates** it explicitly.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** Always end any localization answer with: *"A belief is a probability distribution over robot poses, recursively updated via Bayes' rule under the Markov assumption."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Challenge of Localization - Noise and Aliasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Challenge of Localization - Noise and Aliasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 | The Challenge of Localization: Noise and Aliasing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Localization

In mobile robotics, **Localization** is the process by which a robot determines its own pose (position `(x, y)` and orientation `θ` in a 2D plane) within a given map of the environment. Mathematically, it is the problem of estimating the belief distribution:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where:
- $x_t$ = robot pose at time $t$
- $z_{1:t}$ = sequence of sensor measurements from time 1 to $t$
- $u_{1:t}$ = sequence of control inputs (motion commands) from time 1 to $t$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Localization is a core perceptual problem in mobile robotics. Without solving it, a robot cannot execute goal-directed navigation, path planning, or map building (SLAM).

### 1.2 What is "Noise" in the Localization Context?

**Noise** refers to any random, unwanted perturbation that corrupts the robot's true state or its sensor readings. It introduces *uncertainty* into the localization pipeline.

Noise is broadly classified into two families:

1. **Sensor Noise (Measurement Noise):** Random errors in the data reported by sensors. For example, a sonar may return a distance reading with $\pm 2\text{ cm}$ random jitter due to temperature, surface reflectivity, or electronic thermal noise.
2. **Actuator Noise (Control / Motion Noise):** Random errors in the robot's odometry. The commanded "move 1 meter forward" may actually result in "0.97 m forward + 0.02 m sideways" due to wheel slip, encoder quantization, or uneven terrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Truth:** Sensors and actuators of real robots are inherently *imperfect*. The only mathematically rigorous way to handle this is via **probabilistic representations** rather than deterministic geometry.

### 1.3 What is "Aliasing"?

**Aliasing** (specifically *perceptual aliasing*) is the phenomenon where two **distinct locations** in the environment appear **identical** to the robot's sensors. The sensor data alone cannot disambiguate between these locations.

Formally, perceptual aliasing occurs when:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t^{(1)}) \approx p(z_t \mid x_t^{(2)}) \quad \text{for } x_t^{(1)} \neq x_t^{(2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Hospital Hallway Problem):** Imagine waking up in a long hospital corridor. Every door looks identical, the floor is uniform vinyl, and the lighting is even. Without landmarks, you cannot tell whether you are at Room 101 or Room 187. The hallway has "aliased" the rooms. This is exactly the challenge a mobile robot faces when its sensors cannot distinguish visually (or geometrically) similar places.

### 1.4 The Combined Challenge

The localization problem becomes hard because of two compounding effects:

| Effect | Origin | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Noise** | Hardware imperfections | Pose estimate drifts / scatters over time |
| **Aliasing** | Environment ambiguity | Robot gets confused between similar places |

> [!CAUTION]
> A naive deterministic localization algorithm (e.g., closest-matching scan) will either diverge in noisy conditions or lock onto the wrong position in aliased environments. **Only probabilistic methods can handle both.**

---

## 2. Real-World Engineering Importance

In industrial deployments, ignoring noise/aliasing leads to catastrophic failures:
- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva robots):** Aliasing between identical shelves → wrong item delivered.
- **Autonomous cars:** GPS noise in tunnels → lane departure if not fused with IMU.
- **Vacuum robots:** LiDAR noise in mirrored hallways → infinite loop behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources and Taxonomy of Noise

### 2.1.1 Sensor Noise Sources

- **Thermal/electronic noise:** Random fluctuations in semiconductor components; modeled as **zero-mean Gaussian** with variance $\sigma^2$.
- **Quantization noise:** Discrete digitization of continuous signals (e.g., 8-bit camera → 256 levels).
- **Speckle / multipath noise:** Sonar/radar signals reflect off multiple surfaces.
- **Dynamic range clipping:** Saturation in bright sunlight.
- **Cross-sensor interference:** Two identical IR sensors on adjacent robots triggering each other.

### 2.1.2 Actuator (Motion) Noise Sources

- **Wheel slip:** Encoders record rotations, but the wheel did not actually translate proportionally.
- **Uneven floor:** Bumps cause pitch oscillations.
- **Mechanical backlash:** Gear play introduces dead zone.
- **Model mismatch:** The kinematic model (e.g., differential drive) is a simplification of the true dynamics.

### 2.1.3 Mathematical Taxonomy of Noise Distributions

| Distribution | Use Case | Probability Density Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian** $\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ | Small independent errors (central limit theorem) | $p(x)=\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ |
| **Uniform** | Bounded sensor quantization | $p(x)=\frac{1}{b-a}$ for $x\in[a,b]$ |
| **Exponential** | Time-to-failure, rayleigh-fading | $p(x)=\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x\ge0$ |
| **Triangular** | Sum of two uniform errors | $p(x)=\frac{1}{(b-a)^2}(b-\vert x-c\vert)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Gaussian dominates robotics:** The Central Limit Theorem states that the sum of many small independent random variables tends toward a Gaussian distribution, regardless of the individual distributions. Sensor errors are usually the sum of many small effects, hence Gaussian noise is the default modeling choice.

## 2.2 Probabilistic Model of the Localization Problem

The **Bayes filter** is the mathematical backbone of modern localization. It recursively estimates the posterior belief over the robot's pose:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

This single equation encapsulates two steps:
- **Prediction step** (motion update): $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$
- **Correction step** (measurement update): $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$

## 2.3 Sources of Aliasing in Detail

Aliasing arises from three primary causes:

1. **Structural Symmetry:** Repeated columns in a colonnade, identical doors in an office corridor.
2. **Sparse Features:** A long blank wall provides almost zero distinguishing information.
3. **Sensor Limitations:** A 1-D laser scanner cannot distinguish textures; a low-resolution camera cannot read small text.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Localization Difficulty Spectrum:**
> - **Easy:** Distinct landmarks everywhere (e.g., a forest of uniquely shaped trees) → *Position tracking*.
> - **Hard:** Repeated landmarks + identical corridors → *Global localization under aliasing*.
> - **Hardest:** "Kidnapped robot" problem — robot is teleported without notice.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value in formulas to keep the markdown table intact. Avoid raw `\vert` inside table cells where possible.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Belief (posterior) | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Probability density, integrates to 1 |
| 2 | Bayes filter recursion | $bel(x_t)=\eta\,p(z_t\mid x_t)\int p(x_t\mid u_t,x_{t-1})bel(x_{t-1})dx_{t-1}$ | Continuous form |
| 3 | Motion model | $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ | Encodes actuator noise |
| 4 | Measurement model | $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ | Encodes sensor noise |
| 5 | Gaussian PDF | $p(x)=\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std. dev. |
| 6 | Mahalanobis distance | $d_M=\sqrt{(x-\mu)^T\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)}$ | Multi-dim. "distance" |
| 7 | Sensor aliasing condition | $p(z_t \mid x_t^{(1)}) \approx p(z_t \mid x_t^{(2)})$ | Two poses look identical |
| 8 | Covariance propagation | $\Sigma_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | $A_t$ = Jacobian of motion |
| 9 | EKF update | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| 10 | Cramer-Rao lower bound | $\text{Var}(\hat{x}) \ge \mathcal{F}^{-1}$ | Best achievable noise limit |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of This Framework

- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** Both noise and aliasing models are mandatory.
- **Sensor fusion:** Combining GPS (low noise, low rate) with IMU (high noise, high rate) using Kalman filters.
- **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL):** Uses particle filters specifically to combat the multi-modal belief that arises from aliasing.
- **Robust perception in ADAS:** Modern self-driving stacks use Bayesian filters to handle perceptual aliasing in tunnels and parking garages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Sensor Noise is Modeled as Gaussian

### Step 1 — Observation of sensor error structure

Let the *true* range to an obstacle be $d^*$. The sensor reports $z = d^* + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is the additive noise.

### Step 2 — Decompose noise into independent micro-sources

Suppose the noise is the sum of $N$ independent small errors:
$$\epsilon = \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2 + \dots + \epsilon_N$$

with each $\epsilon_i$ having finite mean $\mu_i$ and finite variance $\sigma_i^2$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Central Limit Theorem

For large $N$, the distribution of $\epsilon$ approaches:

$$\epsilon \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mu_i,\;\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sigma_i^2\right)$$

### Step 4 — Rescale to standard form

For convenience we set $\mu = \sum \mu_i$ and $\sigma^2 = \sum \sigma_i^2$, giving:

$$p(\epsilon) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\epsilon-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Hence $z \sim \mathcal{N}(d^* + \mu,\, \sigma^2)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bayes Filter from First Principles

### Step 1 — State the posterior we want

By definition of belief:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

### Step 2 — Apply the definition of conditional probability

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\,p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Markov assumption

The measurement at time $t$ depends only on the current pose (not on past history):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

Similarly, the current pose depends only on the previous pose and the current control:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and integrate over the previous pose

$$bel(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

Recognizing $p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = bel(x_{t-1})$:

$$\boxed{\,bel(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}\,}$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizer ensuring the distribution integrates to 1.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Noise vs. Aliasing Affects Localization Algorithms

| Algorithm | Robust to Noise? | Robust to Aliasing? | Maintains Multiple Hypotheses? |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Dead Reckoning (no filter) | ✗ Poor (unbounded drift) | ✗ None | ✗ Single point |
| EKF Localization | ✓ Good (Gaussian) | ✗ Poor (unimodal) | ✗ Single Gaussian |
| Histogram Filter (Discrete Bayes) | ✓ Good (grid resolution) | ✓ Good | ✓ Yes (full grid) |
| Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter) | ✓ Excellent | ✓ Excellent | ✓ Yes (particles) |
| Topological Markov Localization | ✓ Good | ✓ Good | ✓ Yes (graph nodes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Insight:** Noise causes *dispersion* of the belief, while aliasing causes *multi-modality*. A unimodal estimator (EKF) handles noise but collapses on aliasing. A multi-modal estimator (particle filter) is the natural answer when both problems co-exist.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A robot's sonar reports a range of $z = 3.20 \text{ m}$. The true range is $3.00 \text{ m}$, with measurement noise $\sigma_z = 0.10 \text{ m}$. Compute the likelihood of this reading assuming a Gaussian sensor model.

### Step 1 — Set up the Gaussian likelihood

$$p(z \mid x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-z_{expected})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the residual

$$z - z_{expected} = 3.20 - 3.00 = 0.20 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the exponent

$$-\frac{(0.20)^2}{2(0.10)^2} = -\frac{0.04}{0.02} = -2.0$$

### Step 4 — Compute the normalizing constant

$$\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} = \frac{1}{0.10 \cdot 2.5066} \approx 3.989$$

### Step 5 — Compute the final likelihood

$$p(z \mid x) = 3.989 \times e^{-2.0} = 3.989 \times 0.1353 \approx 0.540$$

This likelihood value would then be multiplied with the predicted belief and renormalized in the correction step of the Bayes filter.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Localization Pipeline Under Noise & Aliasing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief bel x0] --> B[Robot Executes Control u t]
    B --> C[Motion Model p x t given u t x t-1]
    C --> C1[Add Actuator Noise]
    C1 --> D[Predicted Belief bar bel x t]
    D --> E[Sensor Acquires Measurement z t]
    E --> F[Measurement Model p z t given x t]
    F --> F1[Add Sensor Noise]
    F1 --> F2[Aliasing Check]
    F2 --> G[Apply Bayes Rule]
    G --> H[Posterior Belief bel x t]
    H --> H1{Unimodal?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[EKF Output]
    H1 -- No --> I2[Particle Filter Output]
    I1 --> J[Pose Estimate x hat t]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[Decision: Move Next]
    K --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Aliasing Check** node is the diagnostic block that detects multi-modal likelihoods, signaling that a particle-filter-style estimator is preferred over a Kalman-style estimator.

## 4.2 Noise Propagation Across the Localization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Wheel Encoder Noise] --> M2[Odometry Drift]
    M2 --> M3[Motion Model Uncertainty]
    S1[LiDAR Quantization Noise] --> S2[Range Measurement Error]
    S2 --> S3[Measurement Model Uncertainty]
    M3 --> B[Bayes Filter]
    S3 --> B
    B --> P[Pose Belief]
    A1[Repeated Walls Corridors] --> S4[Perceptual Aliasing]
    S4 --> B
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: How Aliasing Creates Multi-Modal Beliefs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pose Hypothesis A] --> S[Same Sensor Reading z]
    P2[Pose Hypothesis B] --> S
    P3[Pose Hypothesis C] --> S
    S --> MM[Multi-Modal Belief Distribution]
    MM --> R1[EKF collapses to mean = WRONG]
    MM --> R2[Particle Filter preserves all modes = CORRECT]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Robust Localization Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Sensor Pre-processing] --> L2[Layer 2: Outlier Rejection]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Probabilistic Sensor Model]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Motion Model with Slip Estimation]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Bayes Filter Core]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Multi-Hypothesis Manager]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Pose Output to Planner]
    L5 -. Feedback Loop .-> L2
    L6 -. Resampling Trigger .-> L5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### Q1. Define perceptual aliasing in the context of mobile robot localization. Give one real-world example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Perceptual aliasing is the phenomenon in which distinct locations in the environment produce identical (or nearly identical) sensor readings, making them indistinguishable to the robot. Mathematically, two different poses $x^{(1)}$ and $x^{(2)}$ generate the same likelihood under the measurement model: $p(z \mid x^{(1)}) \approx p(z \mid x^{(2)})$ for $x^{(1)} \neq x^{(2)}$.

*Example:* A robot moving down a long corridor with identical doors and uniform lighting cannot tell which door it is currently facing; every door looks the same in its 1-D laser scan.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mathematical statement, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between sensor noise and actuator noise. List one source for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensor Noise | Actuator Noise |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Imperfections in measurement hardware | Imperfections in motion execution |
| Manifestation | Random error in reported $z_t$ | Random error between commanded and actual motion |
| Example Source | Thermal noise in a CCD camera | Wheel slip on a wet floor |

*(1 mark distinction, 1 mark each example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Theory + Derivation Heavy*

**(a)** With the help of suitable diagrams, explain the role of **noise** in degrading the performance of a mobile robot's localization system. Discuss **Gaussian noise modeling** and justify why it is the default choice in robotics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Bayes filter recursion** for localization starting from the definition of the posterior belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. Clearly state the **Markov assumption** and the role of the normalizer $\eta$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. *Role of noise (2 marks):* Sensor noise corrupts measurements $z_t$, leading to incorrect likelihoods. Actuator noise corrupts the executed motion, so the odometry-based pose prediction is wrong. Both accumulate over time, causing the robot's estimated pose to drift away from its true pose.

2. *Gaussian noise model (3 marks):* The additive noise $\epsilon$ on a measurement is modeled as:
   $$p(\epsilon) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\epsilon-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$
   The probability of a sensor reading $z$ given true range $d^*$ is:
   $$p(z \mid d^*) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-d^*)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

3. *Why Gaussian? (2 marks):* By the Central Limit Theorem, the sum of many small independent random errors tends toward a Gaussian distribution, regardless of the individual error distributions. Real sensor errors are typically the sum of many small effects (thermal, quantization, multipath, etc.), so the Gaussian assumption is both analytically convenient and physically justified.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

1. Starting from $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, apply the definition of conditional probability (1 mark).
2. Apply the Markov assumption: $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$ and $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ (2 marks).
3. Marginalize over $x_{t-1}$ to obtain the prediction integral (2 marks).
4. Introduce the normalizer $\eta = p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})^{-1}$ and write the final recursion (2 marks):
   $$bel(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Application + Analysis Heavy*

**(a)** What is **perceptual aliasing**? Explain with a real-world analogy how it disrupts a robot's localization. Show mathematically why a unimodal estimator like the **EKF** fails under aliasing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** localization with the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL / Particle Filter)** algorithm with respect to their handling of **noise** and **aliasing**. Justify your answer with appropriate belief-distribution sketches in words. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. *Definition (2 marks):* Perceptual aliasing is when distinct poses produce the same sensor signature: $p(z \mid x^{(1)}) \approx p(z \mid x^{(2)})$ for $x^{(1)} \neq x^{(2)}$.
2. *Analogy (2 marks):* Waking up in a hotel corridor where every door is identical; you cannot tell which room you are in front of.
3. *EKF failure (3 marks):* The EKF represents belief as a single Gaussian $(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. When the true posterior is multi-modal due to aliasing, the EKF averages the modes, producing a *peak between two walls* that does not correspond to any real robot pose. The robot is therefore confidently wrong.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

| Criterion | EKF Localization | MCL / Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Single Gaussian $(\mu,\Sigma)$ | Set of weighted particles $\{x^{(i)}, w^{(i)}\}$ |
| Handling of noise | Excellent (Gaussian assumption native) | Excellent (no parametric form needed) |
| Handling of aliasing | Poor — collapses to wrong mean | Excellent — multiple modes preserved |
| Computational cost | $O(d^2)$ where $d$ = state dim | $O(N)$ where $N$ = number of particles |
| Recovery from kidnapping | Cannot recover | Can recover via random particle injection |

Conclusion: EKF is efficient and accurate in low-noise, low-aliasing environments. MCL is the preferred choice when aliasing is significant because its non-parametric, multi-modal belief naturally fits the structure of the problem. (3 marks for synthesis.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "uncertainty" with "error":** Noise creates *uncertainty* (a probability distribution), not necessarily an *error* in any single sample.
> 2. **Forgetting the Markov assumption:** Many students skip this critical assumption when deriving the Bayes filter, leading to a maximum of 4 out of 7 marks on derivation questions.
> 3. **Stating that EKF "does not work" under noise:** EKF *does* handle Gaussian noise well. It is *aliasing* that breaks it. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the normalizer $\eta$:** Omitting $\eta = 1/p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ will cost at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Not drawing a diagram when asked:** KTU examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for "neat labeled diagram" in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 6. **Mixing up "sensor noise" with "perceptual aliasing":** Sensor noise = random error; aliasing = systematic ambiguity. These are different failure modes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Localization** = estimating pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ from sensor and control history.
- [ ] **Bayes filter** is the *theoretical backbone* of all probabilistic localization.
- [ ] **Two noise types:** Sensor (measurement) noise and Actuator (motion) noise.
- [ ] **Gaussian noise** is the default model because of the Central Limit Theorem.
- [ ] **Perceptual aliasing** = distinct places look identical to the sensor.
- [ ] **Aliasing condition:** $p(z \mid x^{(1)}) \approx p(z \mid x^{(2)})$.
- [ ] **EKF** is unimodal → good for noise, bad for aliasing.
- [ ] **Particle Filter (MCL)** is multi-modal → excellent for both noise and aliasing.
- [ ] **Markov assumption** is essential to the Bayes filter derivation.
- [ ] **Normalizer $\eta$** keeps the belief a valid probability distribution.
- [ ] **Kidnapped robot problem** is the hardest form of localization under aliasing.
- [ ] **SLAM** combines localization with map building and is heavily affected by both noise and aliasing.
- [ ] **Real-world examples:** AGVs, vacuum robots, autonomous cars, UAVs, hospital delivery robots.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization-Based Navigation Versus Programmed Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Localization-Based Navigation Versus Programmed Solutions # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In mobile robotics, navigation strategies are broadly classified into two paradigms: **Localization-Based Navigation** and **Programmed Solutions** (also termed scripted or pre-planned navigation). **Localization-Based Navigation** is a reactive and adaptive navigation paradigm in which a mobile robot continuously estimates its pose (position and orientation) within a known or partially known environment using onboard sensors, and dynamically generates motion commands to reach a goal while avoiding obstacles. **Programmed Solutions (Scripted Navigation)** is a deterministic navigation paradigm in which the robot's trajectory, waypoints, velocities, and actuator commands are pre-computed offline and executed as a fixed sequence of instructions without real-time feedback about the robot's actual pose in the world. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between these two approaches lies in the **closed-loop perception-action coupling**. Localization-based systems are *closed-loop* (sensor-driven), whereas programmed solutions are *open-loop* (feedforward-only). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are traveling from Kochi to Bengaluru: - **Programmed Solution (GPS-Style Script):** Your uncle writes down turn-by-turn instructions on paper ("Take NH-44, turn right after 150 km, then left at the second signal"). You follow it blindly. If a road is closed, you are lost. - **Localization-Based Navigation (Smart GPS with Live Maps):** Your smartphone continuously shows your blue dot moving on the map. If a road is blocked, it re-routes you in real time using live traffic data. The mobile robot works exactly the same way: - **Programmed robot** = Pre-recorded "paper map" instructions - **Localization-based robot** = "Smartphone" that knows where it is, where it's going, and reacts to the world > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric:** A standard mobile robot operates at a **wheel odometry accuracy of ±2% of distance traveled** without correction, but with localization feedback (e.g., GPS + IMU fusion), accuracy improves to **< 0.5 m absolute error** in outdoor settings. ## 1.3 Core Components Overview | Component | Localization-Based | Programmed | |-----------|-------------------|------------| | **Sensor Stack** | LIDAR, GPS, IMU, cameras | Often none, or encoders only | | **World Model** | Dynamic, updated online | Static, pre-built map/script | | **Decision Loop** | Real-time (typically 10–100 Hz) | None (offline computation) | | **Failure Recovery** | Robust to drift | Brittle to environmental change | --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Localization-Based Navigation The localization-based navigation pipeline operates as a **Sense → Estimate → Plan → Act** closed loop: 1. **Perception Layer (Sensing):** - Raw data is acquired from proprioceptive sensors (encoders, IMU) and exteroceptive sensors (LIDAR, cameras, GPS, ultrasonic). - Sensor fusion algorithms (e.g., **Extended Kalman Filter**, **Particle Filter**) merge these streams. 2. **Localization Layer (Pose Estimation):** - The robot computes its pose $\mathbf{x}_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ in a global reference frame. - Belief update follows: $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ - Where $\mathbf{z}_t$ is the measurement, $\mathbf{u}_t$ is the control input, and $\eta$ is a normalization constant. 3. **Planning Layer (Path Generation):** - Global planner (A*, Dijkstra) generates a coarse path. - Local planner (Dynamic Window Approach, Vector Field Histogram) handles obstacles dynamically. 4. **Actuation Layer (Motion Control):** - A low-level controller (PID, MPC) tracks the planned trajectory. - **Why this works:** The feedback loop corrects errors from wheel slip, drift, and disturbances. ## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Programmed Solutions The programmed solution operates as a pure **Plan → Act** open loop: 1. **Offline Trajectory Generation:** - Path is hand-coded or computed offline using a known map. - Sequence of $(x_i, y_i, \theta_i, v_i, \omega_i)$ waypoints is stored in memory. 2. **Open-Loop Execution:** - Robot executes the stored script with **no pose feedback** (or only encoder-based dead reckoning). - **Why this fails:** Accumulated wheel odometry error grows unboundedly — typically $\sigma_{odom} \propto \sqrt{d}$ (random walk). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the core equations that KTU examiners frequently test. **No vertical bars** are used inside cells to prevent markdown breakage. | # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | Odometry Drift | $\sigma_d = k_d \cdot d$ | Systematic odometry error proportional to distance | m | | 2 | Random Walk Error | $\sigma^2 = K \cdot d$ | Variance grows linearly with distance | m² | | 3 | Robot Pose | $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | 2D position + heading | m, m, rad | | 4 | Kinematic Model (Diff Drive) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \; \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \; \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Non-holonomic constraint | m/s, rad/s | | 5 | ICP Convergence | $\min_{R,t} \sum_i \vert p_i - (Rq_i + t) \vert^2$ | Scan matching via Iterative Closest Point | m² | | 6 | Sensor Fusion Gain (KF) | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T (C\bar{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain for measurement update | dimensionless | | 7 | Particle Filter Weight | $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t^{[i]})$ | Importance weight of particle $i$ | probability | | 8 | Path Length (Programmed) | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1}-y_i)^2}$ | Total waypoint path length | m | | 9 | Update Frequency (Localization) | $f_{loc} = 10 - 100$ Hz | Typical real-time loop rate | Hz | | 10 | Re-planning Time (D*) | $t_{D^*} = O(C)$ | Dynamic A* re-planning cost | seconds | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Localization-based systems** power autonomous vehicles (Tesla FSD, Waymo), warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), and Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance). - **Programmed solutions** dominate industrial AGVs in structured factories where the environment is fully known and static (e.g., automotive assembly lines, semiconductor cleanrooms). - **Hybrid systems** are increasingly common: programmed global paths with localized obstacle avoidance (e.g., surgical robots, agricultural drones). --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Odometry Error Accumulation (Programmed Solution Failure Mode) We derive the **unbounded drift** characteristic of pure programmed (open-loop) navigation. **Step 1: Define wheel odometry update equations.** For a differential drive robot with wheel radius $r$ and wheelbase $b$, the left and right wheel displacements $\Delta s_L$ and $\Delta s_R$ are measured by encoders. The incremental robot motion is: $$ \Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} $$ This is the **mean displacement** of the robot (the average forward distance traveled by the two wheels). $$ \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} $$ This is the change in heading angle, derived from the differential wheel rotation across wheelbase $b$. **Step 2: Update the global pose estimate.** $$ x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2) $$ The new x-position is the old x plus the displacement projected onto the robot's *mid-path* heading (midpoint between old and new orientations). $$ y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \sin(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2) $$ Similarly for the y-coordinate, using the sine of the mid-path heading. $$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta $$ Heading simply accumulates the angular increment. **Step 3: Introduce systematic and non-systematic error sources.** Let each wheel measurement have a noise term: $$ \Delta s_L = r \Delta \phi_L + \epsilon_L, \quad \Delta s_R = r \Delta \phi_R + \epsilon_R $$ Encoder ticks $\Delta \phi$ are converted to distance via wheel radius $r$, plus additive noise $\epsilon$. **Step 4: Propagate the noise through to the pose estimate.** The pose covariance update is given by: $$ \Sigma_{t+1} = F_x \Sigma_t F_x^T + F_u Q_u F_u^T $$ This is the standard Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction step, where $F_x$ is the Jacobian of the motion model with respect to the state, and $F_u$ is the Jacobian with respect to the control (encoder) inputs. $Q_u$ is the control noise covariance. **Step 5: Show the divergence behavior.** For straight-line motion over distance $d = N \cdot \Delta s$ (where $N$ is the number of steps), the position variance grows as: $$ \sigma^2_x(d) = N \cdot \sigma^2_{\Delta s} \cos^2\theta $$ Each step adds independent variance $\sigma^2_{\Delta s}$, and the $N$ contributions sum directly (random walk model). Since $N = d / \Delta s$, we obtain: $$ \sigma^2_x(d) = \frac{d \cdot \sigma^2_{\Delta s}}{\Delta s} $$ Substituting back, the variance per unit distance is a constant: $K = \sigma^2_{\Delta s} / \Delta s$, yielding the final linear growth law: $$ \sigma_x(d) = \sqrt{K \cdot d} $$ This is the **square-root law of odometry error** — the famous result that random-walk errors grow as the square root of distance, while systematic (bias) errors grow linearly with distance. **Step 6: Numerical example for KTU-style problem.** A robot travels **d = 100 m** with encoder noise $\sigma_{\Delta s} = 0.01$ m per step of $\Delta s = 0.1$ m. $$ \sigma^2_x = \frac{100 \times (0.01)^2}{0.1} = \frac{100 \times 0.0001}{0.1} = 0.1 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ \sigma_x = \sqrt{0.1} \approx 0.316 \text{ m} $$ Therefore, the 1-sigma positional uncertainty after 100 m is **0.316 m**, or roughly ±31.6 cm. ## 3.2 Derivation: Localization Correction Gain (Bayesian Update) For a measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ with covariance $Q_t$, the posterior covariance after fusion is: $$ \Sigma_t = (I - K_t C) \bar{\Sigma}_t $$ The Kalman filter covariance update, where $K_t$ is the Kalman gain and $C$ is the measurement Jacobian. **Substituting the Kalman gain:** $$ K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T (C \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q_t)^{-1} $$ This is the optimal gain that minimizes the posterior covariance $E[(\mathbf{x}_t - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t)(\mathbf{x}_t - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t)^T]$. We expand the matrix product as follows: $$ \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T = M $$ Let $M$ denote the cross-covariance term for clarity in the next step. $$ C M + Q_t = C \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q_t = S_t $$ $S_t$ is the **innovation covariance** — the total uncertainty in the measurement space (predicted + actual). $$ K_t C \bar{\Sigma}_t = M S_t^{-1} (C \bar{\Sigma}_t)^T = M S_t^{-1} M^T $$ This is the matrix form of the gain-applied prior covariance. **Final result:** $$ \Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - M S_t^{-1} M^T $$ The matrix inequality $\Sigma_t \leq \bar{\Sigma}_t$ (in the positive semi-definite sense) proves that **measurement update always reduces or maintains uncertainty**. This is the mathematical reason localization-based systems outperform programmed ones. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Full KTU 14-Mark Style Table) | Parameter | Localization-Based | Programmed Solution | |-----------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Error Growth** | Bounded (KF convergence) | Unbounded ($\sqrt{d}$ or $d$) | | **Computational Cost** | High (10–100 Hz loop) | One-time offline | | **Sensor Cost** | High (LIDAR $5k–$50k) | Low (encoders <$100) | | **Robustness to Environment Change** | High (re-plans online) | Low (must be re-programmed) | | **Suitable Environments** | Unknown, dynamic, large-scale | Known, static, structured | | **Typical Application** | Autonomous cars, search & rescue | Factory AGVs, line-followers | | **Failure Mode** | Sensor failure / kidnapping | Drift, obstacle encounter | | **Power Consumption** | High (continuous sensing) | Low (executes script) | | **Latency** | 10–100 ms decision delay | Negligible (pre-computed) | | **Predictability** | Stochastic | Deterministic | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Navigation Paradigm Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Local["LOCALIZATION-BASED NAVIGATION (Closed-Loop)"] L1["Environment Perception
LIDAR / GPS / Camera / IMU"] L2["Localization Engine
EKF / Particle Filter / SLAM"] L3["Path Planner
A-star / DWA / RRT"] L4["Motion Controller
PID / MPC"] L5["Robot Actuators
Wheels / Motors"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 -. Feedback .-> L1 end subgraph Prog["PROGRAMMED SOLUTION (Open-Loop)"] P1["Pre-stored Script
Waypoints and Velocities"] P2["Command Sequencer
No Decision Making"] P3["Robot Actuators
Wheels / Motors"] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 end ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture — Localization Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Raw Sensor Stream
z(t)"] --> B["Preprocessing
Filtering and Feature Extraction"] B --> C["Pose Prediction
Motion Model u(t)"] B --> D["Measurement Update
Observation Model"] C --> E["Belief State
bel of x(t)"] D --> E E --> F["Global Planner
A-star on Map"] E --> G["Local Planner
DWA / VFH"] F --> H["Trajectory
Reference Path"] G --> H H --> I["Controller
PID Tracking"] I --> J["Motor Commands
v and omega"] J --> A ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow — When to Use Which Approach ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Navigation Problem"] --> Q1{"Is environment
known and static?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is precision
less than 1 cm
required?"} Q1 -- No --> Loc["Use Localization-Based
SLAM + Dynamic Planning"] Q2 -- Yes --> Prog["Use Programmed Solution
Pre-computed Trajectories"] Q2 -- No --> Hybrid["Use Hybrid Approach
Programmed global path
plus localized obstacle avoidance"] ``` ## 4.4 Sensor Data Flow — Localization-Based System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Proprio["Proprioceptive Sensors"] E1["Wheel Encoders"] E2["IMU
Gyroscope and Accelerometer"] end subgraph Extero["Exteroceptive Sensors"] S1["LIDAR Scan"] S2["Camera Image"] S3["GPS Fix"] end Proprio --> Fuse["Sensor Fusion
Extended Kalman Filter"] Extero --> Fuse Fuse --> Pose["Robot Pose
x, y, theta"] Pose --> Plan["Planner Module"] Plan --> Ctrl["Velocity Controller"] Ctrl --> Motor["DC Motors"] Motor --> Proprio ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Differentiate between localization-based navigation and programmed solutions in mobile robotics. Mention one application of each. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Localization-Based | Programmed | |--------|-------------------|------------| | **Feedback** | Closed-loop (senses pose) | Open-loop (no feedback) | | **Adaptability** | Reacts to dynamic environment | Static execution | | **Computation** | Real-time | Offline | | **Error** | Bounded | Unbounded drift | **Application:** Localization-based → Autonomous vehicles (e.g., Waymo); Programmed → Industrial AGVs on assembly lines. --- ### Q2. State the **square-root law of odometry error** and explain why programmed solutions suffer from unbounded drift. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** The square-root law states that the standard deviation of positional error in pure dead-reckoning grows as: $$ \sigma_x(d) = \sqrt{K \cdot d} $$ where $K$ is a constant depending on encoder resolution and wheel geometry, and $d$ is the distance traveled. **Reason for drift:** Wheel slippage, wheel diameter mismatch, encoder quantization, and uneven floors introduce **uncompensated noise** in the motion model. Because no external correction is applied (open-loop), errors **accumulate** indefinitely. After traveling 1 km, even a 1% systematic error leads to a **10 m position error**, making pure programmed navigation infeasible for long missions. --- ## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a localization-based navigation system**. Describe the role of the Kalman filter in fusing sensor data. **(7 marks)** **Q (b)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheelbase $b = 0.3$ m, and encoder resolution of 500 ticks/rev. The robot must travel in a straight line for **d = 50 m** at $v = 0.5$ m/s. Calculate: (i) total encoder ticks expected, (ii) random-walk positional error if $\sigma_{\epsilon} = 0.005$ m per $\Delta s = 0.05$ m step, (iii) comment on whether a **programmed solution** is acceptable for this task. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q(a): The localization-based navigation system consists of **four tightly coupled layers**: 1. **Perception Layer:** LIDAR, GPS, IMU, and wheel encoders provide raw data. 2. **Localization Layer:** EKF fuses sensor data to estimate pose $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^T$. 3. **Planning Layer:** Global (A*) and local (DWA) planners generate a feasible path. 4. **Control Layer:** PID controller tracks the path and outputs motor voltages. **Role of the Kalman Filter:** The Kalman filter maintains a **belief** $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ about the robot's pose. In the **prediction step**, it propagates the prior belief using the motion model $\mathbf{x}_t = f(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$. In the **update step**, it corrects the prediction using the measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ and the observation model $\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t)$: $$ K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T (C\bar{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q_t)^{-1} $$ The filter **minimizes the mean squared error** of the pose estimate, making localization-based navigation robust to noisy sensors. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Q(b): **(i) Total encoder ticks:** Circumference of wheel: $$ C = 2 \pi r = 2 \pi (0.05) = 0.3142 \text{ m} $$ Ticks per meter: $$ N_m = \frac{500}{0.3142} = 1591.5 \text{ ticks/m} $$ Total ticks for $d = 50$ m: $$ N_{total} = 1591.5 \times 50 = 79{,}575 \text{ ticks} $$ **(ii) Random-walk error:** Using $\sigma_x = \sqrt{K \cdot d}$ where $K = \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 / \Delta s$: $$ K = \frac{(0.005)^2}{0.05} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} $$ $$ \sigma_x = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 50} = \sqrt{0.025} \approx 0.158 \text{ m} $$ **(iii) Comment:** A positional uncertainty of **±15.8 cm** is acceptable for warehouse navigation but **unacceptable** for surgical robots (sub-mm accuracy) or lane-keeping in autonomous cars (sub-decimeter). Therefore, a **purely programmed solution is not acceptable** for high-precision tasks; a **localization-based approach with sensor feedback is mandatory**. (7 marks) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q (a)** Compare and contrast **localization-based navigation** and **programmed solutions** in terms of: error growth, computational cost, robustness, sensor dependency, and typical applications. Use a comparison table. **(7 marks)** **Q (b)** Explain **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** with a flowchart. How does SLAM overcome the limitations of programmed navigation? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q(a): | Parameter | Localization-Based | Programmed Solution | |-----------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Error Growth** | Bounded by measurement noise | Unbounded ($\sqrt{d}$ random walk) | | **Computational Cost** | High (continuous 10–100 Hz loop) | Negligible (offline) | | **Robustness** | High (re-plans on failure) | Low (fails on environmental change) | | **Sensor Dependency** | Multi-sensor (LIDAR, GPS, IMU) | Encoder-only or none | | **Typical Applications** | Autonomous cars, drones, rescue robots | Factory AGVs, line-followers, CNC feeders | **Key Insight:** Localization-based systems trade **computation and cost** for **robustness and accuracy**, while programmed solutions trade **robustness** for **simplicity and low cost**. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Q(b): **SLAM Definition:** SLAM is the computational problem of **simultaneously building a map of an unknown environment** while **localizing the robot within that map**. **Flowchart of SLAM:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Sensor Data
LIDAR / Camera"] --> B["Feature Extraction
Landmarks"] B --> C["Data Association
Match to Existing Map"] C --> D["Pose Estimation
EKF / Graph SLAM"] D --> E["Map Update
Add New Features"] E --> F["Global Map
and Robot Pose"] F --> A ``` **How SLAM overcomes programmed-navigation limitations:** 1. **No prior map required:** The robot builds the map as it explores, eliminating the need for hand-coded waypoints. 2. **Self-correcting:** Loop closure detects when the robot revisits a region and corrects accumulated drift. 3. **Adaptable to unknown environments:** Enables deployment in disaster zones, planetary surfaces, and underwater terrains where pre-mapping is impossible. In essence, SLAM transforms a **static, open-loop** problem (programmed navigation) into a **dynamic, closed-loop** estimation problem — the mathematical foundation of modern autonomous robotics. (7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently **confuse "SLAM" with "localization"** — SLAM builds the map AND localizes; localization alone assumes a known map. > - Many students **omit units** in numerical problems (always state m, m/s, rad, Hz). > - In comparison questions, examiners award marks for **at least 5 distinct points** in a table — do not list 3 points in paragraph form. > - The square-root law $\sigma = \sqrt{Kd}$ must be **derived, not just quoted**, for full 7-mark credit in derivation sub-questions. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Localization-based navigation = closed-loop, sensor-driven, adaptive, robust, but expensive.** - **Programmed solutions = open-loop, pre-computed, deterministic, cheap, but error grows unboundedly.** - **Square-root law of odometry drift:** $\sigma_x(d) = \sqrt{Kd}$ — this is the fundamental reason pure open-loop navigation fails. - **Differential drive kinematic model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$. - **Kalman filter** is the workhorse of localization — it provides optimal pose estimates under Gaussian noise. - **SLAM** solves localization and mapping simultaneously; it is essential when no prior map exists. - **Hybrid systems** (programmed global + localized local) are the most common industrial solution. - **KTU-favorite keywords to memorize:** *belief state, prediction step, update step, loop closure, data association, innovation covariance, exteroceptive vs. proprioceptive sensors.* - **Numerical constants to remember:** $K$ (odometry constant), $Q_t$ (measurement noise covariance), $\eta$ (normalizer in Bayes filter). - **Always include units and a final interpretive comment** in numerical answers — this is a free 1-mark differentiator in KTU valuation. ---

topics.title: Belief Representation - Single-Hypothesis Belief
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Belief Representation - Single-Hypothesis Belief

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In mobile robotics, **belief** refers to the robot's internal knowledge about its own state (typically its pose — position and orientation) in the environment. **Belief Representation** is the mathematical framework used to encode this knowledge.

A **Single-Hypothesis Belief** (also called a *pose-belief* or *point-belief*) is the simplest form of belief representation in which the robot maintains **exactly one estimate** of its state at any given time. This estimate is commonly represented as a tuple of the most likely state and a measure of uncertainty:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \{x_t, \Sigma_t\}$$

where:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state vector (e.g., $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ for a 2D ground robot).
- $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the covariance matrix capturing uncertainty around the estimate.
- The subscript $t$ denotes the discrete time step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Single-hypothesis belief is the foundational concept upon which the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization** and **Histogram/Topological localization (in simpler forms)** are built. It assumes the robot's pose distribution is **unimodal** (single peak/cluster).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a large, unfamiliar room and asked to find the exit.

- **Multi-hypothesis belief** would be like saying: *"I might be near the north wall, OR I might be near the south wall — I'm not sure which."* You are tracking several possibilities at once.
- **Single-hypothesis belief** is like saying: *"Based on what I felt, I think I am exactly here, but I admit I could be a little to the left, right, forward, or backward."* You commit to one location, but you are aware of a small region of uncertainty around it.

In engineering terms, a single-hypothesis belief is a **single Gaussian "bubble"** of certainty floating in the map space. The center of the bubble is your best guess, and its size (variance) tells you how unsure you are.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Mathematical Quantities

The following constants and parameters are typically used (all expressed in **SI units** unless noted):

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Linear position variance | $\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2$ | **m²** (square meters) |
| Angular variance | $\sigma_\theta^2$ | **rad²** (square radians) |
| Process noise covariance | $Q_t$ | matrix in $m^2, rad^2$ |
| Measurement noise covariance | $R_t$ | matrix in $m^2, rad^2$ |
| Identity matrix | $I_n$ | dimensionless (n×n) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unimodal Assumption:** Because the belief is single-hypothesis, it is **unimodal** — i.e., the probability density has exactly one peak. This works well when the robot is reasonably certain about its pose, but **fails catastrophically in globally ambiguous environments** (e.g., symmetric corridors) where the true distribution is multi-modal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Single-Hypothesis Belief

A single-hypothesis belief is maintained through a **recursive two-step cycle**: **Prediction (motion update)** and **Correction (measurement update)**. This mirrors the Bayesian filter specialized to Gaussian densities.

### Step 1: Initialization
The robot initializes its belief with a prior — either a known starting pose (low uncertainty) or a wide prior covering the whole map (high uncertainty).

### Step 2: Prediction (Action Update)
When the robot executes a control action $u_t$ (e.g., "drive forward 1 m, turn 30°"), the belief is propagated forward in time using the motion model. **Uncertainty grows** because actuation is noisy.

### Step 3: Correction (Measurement Update)
When the robot receives a sensor measurement $z_t$ (e.g., a laser scan, a landmark bearing), the belief is "pulled" toward consistency with the measurement. **Uncertainty shrinks**.

### Step 4: Output the New Belief
The updated mean and covariance become the new single-hypothesis belief $(x_t, \Sigma_t)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter?** A single-hypothesis belief is computationally cheap (constant memory, $O(n^2)$ per update) and closed-form. It is the *only* belief representation that admits a closed-form Kalman-style update, making it ideal for embedded robotics platforms with limited resources.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \{x_t, \Sigma_t\}$ | Single-hypothesis belief as mean-covariance tuple |
| 2 | $\bar{x}_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Predicted state (non-linear motion model) |
| 3 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t$ | Predicted covariance (linearized motion) |
| 4 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain — weight between prediction and measurement |
| 5 | $x_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t(z_t - h(\bar{x}_t))$ | Corrected state estimate |
| 6 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Corrected covariance (Joseph form is more numerically stable) |
| 7 | $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{x}_t)$ | Innovation / measurement residual |
| 8 | $S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t$ | Innovation covariance |
| 9 | $\det(\Sigma_t)$ | Volume of the uncertainty ellipsoid — used as a "confidence" measure |
| 10 | $\text{Mahalanobis distance} = \nu_t^T S_t^{-1} \nu_t$ | For outlier rejection of measurements |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The vertical bar in expressions like $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert \Sigma \vert$ must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Many students lose presentation marks for sloppy notation in numerical answers.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single-hypothesis belief is the workhorse of:

- **GPS-INS fusion in autonomous vehicles** (tightly-coupled EKF)
- **Drone attitude estimation** (Mahony & Madgwick filters)
- **Robotic arm joint estimation**
- **SLAM front-ends** (EKF-SLAM maintains a single hypothesis over the full state vector of robot + landmarks)

It is rejected in favor of multi-hypothesis methods (e.g., particle filters) only when the environment creates **genuine global ambiguity**, such as office buildings with repetitive floor plans.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Single-Hypothesis Update (Gaussian Filter Specialization)

The general Bayesian filter expresses the belief recursively:

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

We now specialize to a single-hypothesis (Gaussian) belief. The derivation proceeds in four steps.

---

### Step 1: Assume Gaussian Prior

Let the prior belief be a multivariate Gaussian with mean $\mu_{t-1}$ and covariance $\Sigma_{t-1}$:

$$
\text{bel}(x_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \Sigma_{t-1} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})^T \Sigma_{t-1}^{-1} (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})\right)
$$

This is a **single hypothesis** because the density has only one mode (one peak).

---

### Step 2: Prediction via the Motion Model

Apply control $u_t$ to obtain the predicted state using the non-linear motion model $f$:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

The motion noise $w_t$ is assumed zero-mean Gaussian with covariance $Q_t$. Linearize $f$ about $\mu_{t-1}$ using the Jacobian:

$$
F_t = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{t-1}}\right|_{x_{t-1}=\mu_{t-1}}
$$

The predicted covariance is then (propagation of uncertainty through a linear system):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\Sigma}_t &= \text{Cov}\!\left(f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + F_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t\right) \\
&= F_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, F_t^T + Q_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The first term is the prior uncertainty transformed through the linearized motion; the second term is the additional uncertainty injected by noisy actuation.

---

### Step 3: Correction via the Measurement Model

The measurement model is $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$, where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$. Linearize:

$$
H_t = \left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t}\right|_{x_t=\bar{\mu}_t}
$$

Define the **innovation** (how much the measurement disagrees with the prediction):

$$
\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)
$$

The innovation covariance is:

$$
S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + R_t
$$

The **Kalman gain** $K_t$ is derived by minimizing the trace of the posterior covariance. The optimal gain is:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1}
$$

The corrected state is the prediction nudged toward the measurement, weighted by $K_t$:

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t
$$

The corrected covariance is:

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

---

### Step 4: Posterior Belief

The single-hypothesis belief at time $t$ is therefore:

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \left\{ \mu_t, \Sigma_t \right\}
$$

with the underlying density:

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \Sigma_t \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x_t - \mu_t)^T \Sigma_t^{-1} (x_t - \mu_t)\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of derivation:** A single-hypothesis belief is **exactly closed-form** under the assumptions of (i) Gaussian noise, (ii) linearizable motion and measurement models, and (iii) unimodal posterior. The EKF extends this to non-linear systems via first-order Taylor linearization.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has state $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$. Suppose:

- Prior belief: $\mu_{t-1} = [0, 0, 0]^T$, $\Sigma_{t-1} = \text{diag}(0.5, 0.5, 0.1)$
- Control: $u_t = $ "drive 1 m forward" → motion $f(\mu, u) = [1, 0, 0]^T$
- Motion Jacobian: $F_t = I_3$
- Process noise: $Q_t = \text{diag}(0.05, 0.05, 0.01)$
- Measurement: $z_t = [1.1]^T$ (range to a landmark on the x-axis at $h(\bar{\mu}) = [1]^T$)
- Measurement Jacobian: $H_t = [1, 0, 0]$
- Measurement noise: $R_t = [0.1]$

**Solution:**

**Step A — Prediction:**

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = [1, 0, 0]^T
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = I_3 \cdot \text{diag}(0.5, 0.5, 0.1) \cdot I_3 + \text{diag}(0.05, 0.05, 0.01) = \text{diag}(0.55, 0.55, 0.11)
$$

**Step B — Innovation:**

$$
\nu_t = 1.1 - 1.0 = 0.1
$$

$$
S_t = [1, 0, 0] \cdot \text{diag}(0.55, 0.55, 0.11) \cdot [1, 0, 0]^T + 0.1 = 0.55 + 0.1 = 0.65
$$

**Step C — Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_t = \text{diag}(0.55, 0.55, 0.11) \cdot [1, 0, 0]^T \cdot \frac{1}{0.65} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.846 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step D — Update:**

$$
\mu_t = [1, 0, 0]^T + 0.1 \cdot [0.846, 0, 0]^T = [1.0846, 0, 0]^T
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I_3 - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.154 \cdot 0.55 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.55 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.11 \end{bmatrix} = \text{diag}(0.0847, 0.55, 0.11)
$$

**Interpretation:** The x-variance dropped from **0.55 → 0.0847 m²** because the measurement was informative about x. The y and $\theta$ variances remained unchanged because the measurement carried no information about them — a key limitation of single-hypothesis EKF when sensors are anisotropic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of Single-Hypothesis Belief Update

The following Mermaid diagram shows the information flow in a single-hypothesis (EKF-style) belief update cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize belief x0 Sigma0] --> B[Receive control input ut]
    B --> C[Motion model f xt-1 ut]
    C --> D[Predicted mean mu_bar]
    C --> E[Linearize F Jacobian]
    E --> F[Predicted cov Sigma_bar = F Sigma F trans + Q]
    D --> G[Receive measurement zt]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Measurement model h mu_bar]
    H --> I[Innovation nu = zt - h]
    I --> J[Linearize H Jacobian]
    J --> K[Innovation cov S = H Sigma_bar H trans + R]
    K --> L[Kalman gain K = Sigma_bar H trans S inv]
    L --> M[Updated mean mu = mu_bar + K nu]
    L --> N[Updated cov Sigma = I minus K H Sigma_bar]
    M --> O[New single-hypothesis belief mu Sigma]
    N --> O
    O --> P{More data}
    P -- yes --> B
    P -- no --> Q[Output final belief]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: Single-Hypothesis vs. Multi-Hypothesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Single Hypothesis Belief]
        A1[True pose unknown] --> A2[Robot keeps one Gaussian bubble]
        A2 --> A3[Mean = best guess]
        A2 --> A4[Covariance = uncertainty]
    end
    subgraph S2[Multi Hypothesis Belief]
        B1[True pose unknown] --> B2[Robot keeps N particles or Gaussians]
        B2 --> B3[Each sample is a hypothesis]
        B2 --> B4[Weights = likelihoods]
    end
```

## 4.3 State-Vector Uncertainty Ellipsoid (Schematic)

A single-hypothesis belief is geometrically an **uncertainty ellipsoid** centered at the mean. The 95% confidence ellipsoid satisfies:

$$
(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu) \leq \chi^2_{2,n,0.95}
$$

For a 2D pose $[x, y]$, the ellipsoid axes are aligned with the eigenvectors of $\Sigma$ and their lengths are $\pm \sqrt{\lambda_i \chi^2_{2,0.95}}$ where $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the ellipsoid matters:** A growing ellipsoid means the robot is getting lost; a shrinking ellipsoid means the robot is gaining confidence through good measurements. This is the most intuitive way to visualize single-hypothesis belief health in a KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define *single-hypothesis belief* in the context of mobile robot localization. State the key assumption that makes it a "single" hypothesis.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A single-hypothesis belief is a representation of the robot's pose in which only one state estimate is maintained at any time, typically as a tuple $(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ where $\mu_t$ is the mean pose and $\Sigma_t$ is its covariance. The key assumption is that the posterior distribution over the robot's pose is **unimodal** (single peak / single Gaussian). It is the simplest belief representation and underlies the Kalman and Extended Kalman filters. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Repeated Pattern]**
List any **two** limitations of using a single-hypothesis belief for robot localization.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. It cannot represent **global ambiguity** — if the robot is equally likely to be in two distinct places (e.g., two identical rooms), the single Gaussian averages them into a meaningless intermediate estimate.
2. It is **fragile to localization failures** — once the Gaussian drifts off the true pose, it cannot recover because there is no mechanism to entertain alternative hypotheses. **(3 marks — 1.5 each)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the recursive update equations for a single-hypothesis belief under a linear-Gaussian motion and measurement model. Clearly define each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot has prior belief $\mu_{t-1} = [0, 0]^T$ and $\Sigma_{t-1} = \text{diag}(0.2, 0.2)$. It moves with control $u_t = [0.5, 0]^T$ and motion noise $Q_t = \text{diag}(0.05, 0.05)$. It then receives a range measurement $z_t = 0.6$ to a landmark on the x-axis at $h(x) = x_1$, with $R_t = 0.08$. Compute the updated belief $(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ step by step. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Assume Gaussian prior** (1 mark)
$$
\text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})
$$

**Step 2: Prediction with linear motion $f(x, u) = A_t x + B_t u$** (2 marks)
$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$
$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + Q_t
$$

**Step 3: Correction with linear measurement $h(x) = C_t x$** (2 marks)
Innovation:
$$
\nu_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t
$$
Innovation covariance:
$$
S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t
$$
Kalman gain:
$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T S_t^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Update belief** (2 marks)
$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t, \qquad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t
$$
The single-hypothesis belief is $\text{bel}(x_t) = \{\mu_t, \Sigma_t\}$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Prediction:** $A_t = I_2$, $B_t = I_2$ (1 mark)
$$
\bar{\mu}_t = [0,0]^T + [0.5, 0]^T = [0.5, 0]^T
$$
$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = \text{diag}(0.2, 0.2) + \text{diag}(0.05, 0.05) = \text{diag}(0.25, 0.25) \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Innovation:** $C_t = [1, 0]$ (1 mark)
$$
\nu_t = 0.6 - [1, 0] \cdot [0.5, 0]^T = 0.6 - 0.5 = 0.1
$$
$$
S_t = [1, 0] \cdot \text{diag}(0.25, 0.25) \cdot [1, 0]^T + 0.08 = 0.25 + 0.08 = 0.33 \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Kalman Gain:** (1 mark)
$$
K_t = \text{diag}(0.25, 0.25) \cdot [1, 0]^T \cdot \frac{1}{0.33} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.758 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Update:** (1 mark)
$$
\mu_t = [0.5, 0]^T + 0.1 \cdot [0.758, 0]^T = [0.5758, 0]^T
$$
$$
\Sigma_t = (I_2 - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.242 \cdot 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix} = \text{diag}(0.0605, 0.25) \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu_t = [0.5758, 0]^T$, $\Sigma_t = \text{diag}(0.0605, 0.25)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams how a single-hypothesis belief can be visualized as a **Gaussian uncertainty ellipsoid** in 2D pose space. What does the volume of the ellipsoid represent, and how does it change during the prediction and correction steps? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare single-hypothesis belief with multi-hypothesis belief (e.g., particle filter) under the headings: (i) number of modes, (ii) computational cost, (iii) handling of global ambiguity, (iv) recovery from localization failure, (v) memory, (vi) typical use case. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Gaussian Ellipsoid Visualization (7 marks)

A single-hypothesis belief in 2D is geometrically a **2D Gaussian density** in the $[x, y]$ plane, with iso-probability contours forming **ellipses** (1 mark).

The equation of the $c$-percent confidence ellipse is:
$$
(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu) \leq \chi^2_{2, c}
$$
where $\chi^2_{2, c}$ is the chi-square critical value (1 mark).

- **Major axis length** = $2 \sqrt{\lambda_1 \chi^2_{2, c}}$
- **Minor axis length** = $2 \sqrt{\lambda_2 \chi^2_{2, c}}$

where $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are eigenvalues of $\Sigma$ (1 mark).

The **volume** of the ellipse (proportional to $\sqrt{\det \Sigma}$) represents the **total positional uncertainty** of the robot (1 mark).

- **During prediction:** The ellipsoid **grows** ($\det \bar{\Sigma} \geq \det \Sigma$) because motion injects noise; the shape may also rotate and stretch due to the motion Jacobian $F_t$ (1 mark).
- **During correction:** The ellipsoid **shrinks** ($\det \Sigma_t \leq \det \bar{\Sigma}$) because informative measurements reduce uncertainty; the shape aligns with the sensor's informative directions (1 mark).

A diagram should show: (i) the mean $\mu$ as a dot at the centre, (ii) concentric ellipses for 1$\sigma$, 2$\sigma$, 3$\sigma$ confidence, (iii) eigenvectors $v_1, v_2$ indicating principal directions of uncertainty. (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks = 1 mark per row + 1 mark for presentation)

| Aspect | Single-Hypothesis Belief | Multi-Hypothesis Belief (e.g., Particle Filter) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of modes | Unimodal (one peak) | Multimodal (N modes) |
| Computational cost | $O(n^2)$ per update — cheap | $O(N)$ per update — expensive |
| Global ambiguity | Cannot represent — averages modes | Natively supports multiple distinct hypotheses |
| Recovery from failure | None — once lost, stays lost | Possible — surviving particles recover |
| Memory | $O(n^2)$ constant | $O(Nn)$ — grows with particle count |
| Typical use case | EKF localization, GPS/INS fusion | Monte Carlo localization, kidnapped robot problem |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "single-hypothesis" with "no uncertainty"** — even a single-hypothesis belief has a covariance $\Sigma_t$. Many students write "single hypothesis means the robot is certain" — this is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Do not skip the Jacobian $F_t$ and $H_t$** in derivation questions. The first-order Taylor linearization is the *only* difference between KF and EKF and is worth 2 marks on its own.
> 3. **In numerical problems, always show units in intermediate steps** (e.g., $0.5$ m, $0.1$ m). The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests dimensional awareness.
> 4. **Innovation residual $\nu_t$ must be a vector**, not a scalar, in the general multivariate case. If your measurement is scalar, say so explicitly.
> 5. **Always end the numerical answer with the explicit tuple $\{\mu_t, \Sigma_t\}$** — this is the literal single-hypothesis belief object and is what the examiner looks for.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Single-Hypothesis Belief**

- ✅ A single-hypothesis belief is one Gaussian $\text{bel}(x_t) = \{\mu_t, \Sigma_t\}$ — **one mean, one covariance, one mode**.
- ✅ It is the **only** belief form admitting a closed-form recursive update (Kalman / EKF).
- ✅ The **prediction step** grows the covariance: $\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t$.
- ✅ The **correction step** shrinks the covariance and forms the posterior via the Kalman gain $K_t$.
- ✅ The **Kalman gain $K_t$** decides the *weight* given to the new measurement vs. the prediction.
- ✅ Single-hypothesis belief is **fragile to global ambiguity** — use particle filters instead when ambiguity is real.
- ✅ Linear systems use the **Kalman Filter**; non-linear systems use the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** which linearizes $f$ and $h$ at the current estimate.
- ✅ The **uncertainty ellipsoid** is the visual fingerprint of single-hypothesis belief — its volume $\propto \sqrt{\det \Sigma_t}$.
- ✅ **KTU expects** derivation of the full Bayesian-to-Gaussian specialization plus one numerical worked example per question.
- ✅ **Key synonyms** to recognize in KTU papers: *point belief, unimodal belief, Gaussian belief, EKF belief, Kalman belief* — all mean the same single-hypothesis concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple-Hypothesis Belief
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple-Hypothesis Belief

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Multiple-Hypothesis Belief (MHB)** is a non-parametric, multi-modal belief representation in mobile robot localization wherein the posterior belief over the robot's state space is encoded as a **finite, weighted set of discrete state hypotheses** rather than a single parametric distribution (e.g., a unimodal Gaussian).

Formally, the belief at time $t$ is represented as:

$$bel(x_t) = \{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N_t}$$

where each $x_t^{(i)}$ is a candidate state hypothesis, $w_t^{(i)}$ is its associated importance weight with the normalization constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{N_t} w_t^{(i)} = 1$, and $N_t$ is the dynamically adjusted number of active hypotheses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Multiple-Hypothesis Belief is the foundational concept underlying **Particle Filters (Monte Carlo Localization)**, **Multiple Hypothesis Tracking (MHT)**, and **Multi-Modal Kalman approaches**. It is the canonical solution to the **global localization problem** and the **kidnapped robot problem** in environments with perceptual aliasing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tourist dropped into an unfamiliar city with a paper map. The tourist sees a famous fountain and says, *"I am either in City Square Park OR in Old Town Plaza"* — because the map shows the **same fountain icon in two different places**. The tourist cannot uniquely identify the location from a single observation.

A single-hypothesis (unimodal) belief would force the tourist to pick *one* location (and likely be wrong). A **Multiple-Hypothesis Belief** keeps **both possibilities alive**, assigning a probability weight to each:

- **Hypothesis 1:** City Square Park (weight = 0.6)
- **Hypothesis 2:** Old Town Plaza (weight = 0.4)

As more observations arrive (a street sign, a bus route, the position of the sun), the weights get **updated using Bayes' rule**. Eventually, one hypothesis dominates, and the tourist is confident.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world robotics parallel:** A robot moving through a long, symmetric corridor cannot tell if it is at position $x=10$m or $x=50$m based on a single laser scan. Maintaining multiple hypotheses allows the robot to "stay uncertain" until motion or additional sensing disambiguates.

### 1.3 Why Multiple-Hypothesis Belief is Necessary

| Problem | Single-Hypothesis (e.g., EKF) Failure | Multiple-Hypothesis Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Global localization (cold start) | Diverges because linearization at wrong estimate is invalid | Samples hypotheses all over the map |
| Kidnapped robot | Cannot recover — single Gaussian collapses to wrong mode | Detects low-weight hypotheses elsewhere |
| Perceptual aliasing | Picks wrong mode confidently | Maintains competing modes with weights |
| Symmetric environments | Periodic ambiguity causes oscillation | Hypotheses track each ambiguous location |

### 1.4 Physical and Algorithmic Constants

- **Number of particles ($N_t$):** Typically **100 – 5000** in modern MCL implementations.
- **Effective sample size threshold ($N_{eff}$):** Typically **0.5 × N** (i.e., 50% of total particles).
- **Resampling threshold:** Triggered when $N_{eff} < N_{thr}$ (often $N_{thr} = N/2$).
- **KLD-sampling parameter $\epsilon$:** Typically **0.05** (confidence bound) — from Fox (2003).
- **Bin width $b$ (KLD):** Determined by the discretization of the state space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the KTU 2024 scheme, students are expected to know that the **number of hypotheses $N_t$ is NOT fixed** — it is a random variable that grows in ambiguous regions and shrinks in well-localized regions. This adaptive sizing is one of the chief advantages over fixed-grid approaches like Histogram Filters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic Steps (Why and How)

A Multiple-Hypothesis Belief algorithm executes the following **recursive loop** at every time step:

1. **Hypothesis Prediction (Sampling / Motion Update):**
   - *Why:* The robot's control input $u_t$ shifts the prior hypotheses forward in time.
   - *How:* For each particle $x_{t-1}^{(i)}$, draw a new sample from the motion model:
   $$x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$$
   - Noise is injected per the odometry model (e.g., $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \text{sample}(\mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_t))$).

2. **Importance Weighting (Measurement Update):**
   - *Why:* Not all propagated hypotheses are equally plausible given the new observation $z_t$.
