   - *How:* Compute the importance weight as the measurement likelihood:
   $$w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$$

3. **Normalization:**
   - *Why:* Weights must sum to 1 to form a valid probability distribution.
   - *How:*
   $$w_t^{(i)} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_t} w_t^{(j)}}$$

4. **Resampling (Hypothesis Pruning + Replication):**
   - *Why:* Degeneracy problem — after a few iterations, all but a few hypotheses have negligible weight.
   - *How:* When $N_{eff} < N_{thr}$, draw $N_t$ new hypotheses from the current set with replacement, probability proportional to $w_t^{(i)}$.

5. **Adaptive Sizing (Optional, KLD-sampling):**
   - *Why:* Maintain just enough hypotheses for statistical fidelity.
   - *How:* Bound the KL-divergence between the sample-based belief and the true distribution by $\epsilon$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} w_t^{(i)} \cdot \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})$ | Multi-hypothesis belief as weighted Dirac mixture | $w_t^{(i)} \geq 0$, $\sum w = 1$ |
| 2 | $w_t^{(i)} = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}) \cdot w_{t-1}^{(i)}$ | Recursive Bayes weight update | $\eta$ = normalizer |
| 3 | $N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_t} (w_t^{(i)})^2}$ | Effective sample size | Range: $[1, N_t]$ |
| 4 | $N_{thr} = \alpha \cdot N_t$ | Resampling trigger (typically $\alpha = 0.5$) | $N_{thr} = N_t / 2$ |
| 5 | $k = \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \chi^2_{d-1, 1-\delta}$ | KLD-sampling bound | $\epsilon$ = KL-bound, $\delta$ = confidence |
| 6 | $p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ | Measurement model likelihood for particle $i$ | Sensor-specific |
| 7 | $x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$ | Motion model sampling | Odometry / velocity model |
| 8 | $b \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Histogram bin width in KLD-MCL | $d$ = state dim |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Multi-hypothesis belief is the de-facto standard in **autonomous vehicles (self-driving cars)**, **warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva)**, **drone swarms**, and **Mars rovers**. It is the only practical approach for global localization in large, ambiguous environments where EKF and UKF fail.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Significance

| Domain | Application of Multi-Hypothesis Belief |
|---|---|
| Autonomous Driving | Tracking pedestrians / vehicles with occlusions; the "ghost" hypothesis reappears when occlusion clears |
| Search & Rescue Robots | Global localization in collapsed, symmetric building ruins |
| Augmented Reality | Multi-hypothesis SLAM for AR headsets (e.g., HoloLens, Magic Leap) |
| Underwater AUVs | GPS-denied navigation in feature-poor seafloors |
| Warehouse Robotics | Robust pose tracking when RFID landmarks are temporarily occluded |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Recursive Weight Update from Bayes' Rule

We start from the desired posterior belief at time $t$:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

Apply Bayes' rule to factor the most recent measurement:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

Under the standard Markov assumption, $z_t$ is conditionally independent of past measurements given $x_t$:

$$= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

Now expand the second term by integrating over $x_{t-1}$:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

The integrand contains $bel(x_{t-1})$, which by induction is represented by a particle set:

$$bel(x_{t-1}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{(i)})$$

Substitute and exploit the sifting property of the delta function:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$$

This is the **predicted belief** — a continuous mixture density. We now **sample** $N_t$ new particles from this density, one from each motion-model branch:

$$x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, N_t$$

The importance weight for the $i$-th new particle, given the proposal distribution equals the motion model, simplifies to:

$$w_t^{(i)} = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$$

with normalization:

$$\eta = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{N_t} p(z_t \mid x_t^{(j)}) \right)^{-1}$$

Thus, the multi-hypothesis posterior is:

$$\boxed{bel(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} w_t^{(i)} \cdot \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Sample Size ($N_{eff}$)

Suppose all weights were equal: $w_t^{(i)} = 1/N_t$. Then:

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_t} (1/N_t)^2} = \frac{1}{N_t \cdot (1/N_t^2)} = N_t$$

So $N_{eff}$ is **maximal** when weights are uniform (maximum diversity). Conversely, if one weight dominates (all others $\to 0$):

$$N_{eff} \to \frac{1}{w_{max}^2 + 0} = 1$$

So $N_{eff}$ is **minimal** (= 1) when the belief has collapsed to a single hypothesis. This makes $N_{eff}$ a quantitative measure of multi-modality.

**Decision rule:**

$$\text{Resample if} \quad N_{eff} < N_{thr} = \frac{N_t}{2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: KLD-Sampling Adaptive Particle Count (Fox, 2003)

The Kullback-Leibler Divergence between the discrete sample-based belief and the true continuous posterior is bounded by $\epsilon$ with probability $1 - \delta$:

$$P\left( D_{KL}(\hat{p} \Vert p) \leq \epsilon \right) \geq 1 - \delta$$

Using the chi-squared upper bound:

$$n = \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}$$

where $k$ is the number of **occupied histogram bins** (cells with at least one sample). The required number of particles:

$$N_{min} = \frac{k-1}{2\epsilon} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{9(k-1)} + \sqrt{\frac{2}{9(k-1)}} z_{1-\delta} \right)^3$$

This is the **Wilson–Hilferty approximation** to the chi-square quantile. The key insight: as the belief becomes more concentrated (fewer occupied bins), $N_{min}$ drops automatically.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Setup:** A robot has $N_t = 100$ particles. The weights after a measurement update are:

$$w = [0.40,\ 0.25,\ 0.20,\ 0.10,\ 0.05]$$

(only the top 5 shown; the remaining 95 are negligibly small).

**Step 1:** Compute $N_{eff}$:

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.40^2 + 0.25^2 + 0.20^2 + 0.10^2 + 0.05^2 + 95 \cdot 0^2} = \frac{1}{0.16 + 0.0625 + 0.04 + 0.01 + 0.0025}$$

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.275} \approx 3.64$$

**Step 2:** Compare to threshold $N_{thr} = 100 / 2 = 50$:

$$N_{eff} = 3.64 \ll 50 = N_{thr}$$

**Step 3:** Resample. The resampling distribution is proportional to the weights. The cumulative weight array is:

$$C = [0.40,\ 0.65,\ 0.85,\ 0.95,\ 1.00]$$

Draw $u_1 \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 1/N_t)$. Suppose $u_1 = 0.003$. Select hypothesis 1. Repeat 100 times — the expected counts of new particles:

- Hypothesis 1: $100 \times 0.40 = 40$ particles
- Hypothesis 2: $100 \times 0.25 = 25$ particles
- Hypothesis 3: $100 \times 0.20 = 20$ particles
- Hypothesis 4: $100 \times 0.10 = 10$ particles
- Hypothesis 5: $100 \times 0.05 = 5$ particles

The belief has been **duplicated** but not biased — the diversity is reduced (effective particles dropped from 3.64 down to roughly 100 again, but distributed). This is the **price of resampling**: loss of diversity in exchange for computational efficiency.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Single Hypothesis (EKF) | Multiple Hypothesis (Particle Filter) |
|---|---|---|
| Posterior shape | Unimodal Gaussian | Multi-modal, any shape |
| Global localization | ✗ Fails | ✓ Works |
| Computational cost | $O(d^2)$ per step | $O(N \cdot d)$ per step |
| Memory footprint | $O(d^2)$ | $O(N \cdot d)$ |
| Convergence guarantee | Local only | Asymptotically exact |
| Parametric assumption | Required (Gaussian) | None (non-parametric) |
| KTU marks weightage | 4–6 marks | 10–14 marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Multiple-Hypothesis Belief

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief bel x0 - Uniform Sampling] --> B[Motion Update u t]
    B --> C[Sample x t i from p x t given x t-1, u t]
    C --> D[Measurement z t arrives]
    D --> E[Compute Importance Weight w t i equals eta times p z t given x t i]
    E --> F[Normalize Weights]
    F --> G{N effective less than N threshold?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Resampling Step - Stochastic Universal Sampling]
    H --> I[Replicated Hypothesis Set]
    I --> J[Output bel x t]
    G -- No --> J[Output bel x t - No Resampling]
    J --> K{Next Time Step t plus 1}
    K --> B
```

### 4.2 Hypothesis Lifecycle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parent Hypothesis x t-1] --> B[Propagate via Motion Model]
    B --> C[New Hypothesis x t]
    C --> D{Measurement Update}
    D --> E[Weight High]
    D --> F[Weight Low]
    E --> G[Survive Resampling]
    F --> H[Pruned or Replicated]
    G --> I[Act as Parent for Next Step]
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 Multi-Modal Belief State Illustration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[True Posterior bel x t] --> B[Mode A - weight 0.6]
    A --> C[Mode B - weight 0.3]
    A --> D[Mode C - weight 0.1]
    B --> E[Particles clustered around x equals 10]
    C --> F[Particles clustered around x equals 50]
    D --> G[Particles clustered around x equals 80]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagrams:**
> - The first flowchart shows the **complete recursive loop** of a particle filter (a practical instantiation of multi-hypothesis belief).
> - The lifecycle diagram shows how individual hypotheses are **born, weighted, and either survive or die** at every step.
> - The modal diagram emphasizes that the posterior is a **mixture** of distinct clusters, not a single bell curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
Define **Multiple-Hypothesis Belief** in the context of mobile robot localization. Why is it preferred over a single-hypothesis (Gaussian) belief in environments with perceptual aliasing?

**Model Answer:**
Multiple-Hypothesis Belief is a non-parametric representation of the robot's posterior state estimate, expressed as a weighted set of discrete state samples $\{(x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N_t}$ with $\sum_i w_t^{(i)} = 1$. It allows the robot to **maintain multiple distinct pose hypotheses simultaneously** rather than committing to one. In environments with perceptual aliasing — where different locations produce similar sensor readings — a single Gaussian belief may collapse to the wrong mode and become irretrievably lost. Multiple-hypothesis belief keeps all plausible alternatives alive, each with its own probability weight, enabling correct global localization as further measurements arrive. *(3 marks)*

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
What is the **Effective Sample Size ($N_{eff}$)**? How is it used to decide whether to resample in a particle filter?

**Model Answer:**
The Effective Sample Size is a scalar measure of how much the particle set has degenerated. It is computed as:

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_t} (w_t^{(i)})^2}$$

$N_{eff}$ ranges from **1** (complete collapse to one hypothesis) to **$N_t$** (perfectly uniform weights). When $N_{eff}$ falls below a threshold (typically $N_t/2$), the filter triggers **resampling** to discard low-weight particles and replicate high-weight ones, thereby refocusing computational effort on the most promising regions of the state space. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **recursive weight update equation** for a multiple-hypothesis belief represented as a particle set. Begin from the Bayes filter posterior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ and clearly state the Markov assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **KLD-sampling** technique for adaptive particle count. Derive the relationship between the number of occupied histogram bins and the minimum number of particles required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Weight Derivation:**

We start with the recursive Bayes filter:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

The second term is expanded by marginalization over $x_{t-1}$:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

By the inductive hypothesis, the prior belief is the particle set:

$$bel(x_{t-1}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{(i)})$$

Substituting and using the sifting property of the Dirac delta:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$$

Drawing one new particle per branch from the motion model:

$$x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$$

The importance weight under the proposal distribution $q = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ becomes:

$$\boxed{w_t^{(i)} = \eta \cdot w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})}$$

with $\eta = (\sum_j w_{t-1}^{(j)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(j)}))^{-1}$. **Markov assumption used:** $z_t$ is conditionally independent of $z_{1:t-1}$ given $x_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b) KLD-Sampling:**

The Kullback-Leibler divergence between the sample-based posterior $\hat{p}$ and the true posterior $p$ is bounded:

$$P\!\left( D_{KL}(\hat{p} \Vert p) \leq \epsilon \right) \geq 1 - \delta$$

The number of particles needed to guarantee this bound depends on the number of **occupied histogram bins** $k$:

$$N_{min} = \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}$$

Using the Wilson–Hilferty approximation for the chi-square quantile:

$$N_{min} = \frac{k-1}{2\epsilon}\!\left(1 - \frac{2}{9(k-1)} + \sqrt{\frac{2}{9(k-1)}} z_{1-\delta}\right)^{\!3}$$

When the belief is concentrated (small $k$), $N_{min}$ is small; when it spreads (large $k$), $N_{min}$ grows automatically. Sampling stops as soon as the particle count exceeds $N_{min}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **complete algorithmic flow of a Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)** as an implementation of multiple-hypothesis belief. List and justify the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot maintains $N = 200$ particles. The normalized weights after a measurement update are such that one particle has $w = 0.4$ and the other 199 are uniformly distributed at $w = 0.6/199$. Compute $N_{eff}$ and decide whether resampling should be triggered (threshold $N_{thr} = N/2$). Explain the consequence of NOT resampling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Particle Filter Block Diagram (refer to Section 4 diagrams).** The major blocks are:

1. **Initialization:** Draw $N$ particles uniformly over the map.
2. **Motion Update:** Propagate each particle using $u_t$ and the motion model.
3. **Measurement Update:** Compute $w_t^{(i)} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$.
4. **Normalization:** Ensure $\sum w = 1$.
5. **Resampling (conditional):** Replicate high-weight, prune low-weight.
6. **State Estimation:** Output mean / mode of particle set. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.4^2 + 199 \cdot \left(\frac{0.6}{199}\right)^2} = \frac{1}{0.16 + 199 \cdot \frac{0.36}{39601}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.16 + 0.001808} = \frac{1}{0.161808} \approx 6.18$$

Threshold: $N_{thr} = 200 / 2 = 100$.

Decision: $N_{eff} = 6.18 \ll 100 = N_{thr}$ → **Resample**.

**Consequence of NOT resampling:** Computational resources are wasted on 199 low-weight particles that contribute almost nothing to the posterior. The filter will run slower without gaining accuracy, and in the long run, the single high-weight particle will dominate, causing a single-hypothesis collapse that defeats the purpose of multi-hypothesis belief. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Markov assumption** in derivations costs 2 marks. Always state it explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $N_{eff}$ with $N_t$** — students often write $N_{eff} = N$ as a default. Show the actual computation.
> 3. **Omitting the normalizer $\eta$** in the weight update — it is mandatory.
> 4. **Using `|` for absolute value or `|` inside the formula table** — this breaks KTU's digital evaluation system. Use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 5. **Mixing up the proposal distribution** with the true posterior in importance sampling — state clearly that the proposal is the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Multi-hypothesis belief = weighted set of state samples $\{(x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)})\}$ — a non-parametric, multi-modal posterior.
- **Core Algorithm:** Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization).
- **Bayes Update:** $w_t^{(i)} = \eta \cdot w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$.
- **Markov Assumption:** $z_t$ conditionally independent of $z_{1:t-1}$ given $x_t$.
- **Resampling Trigger:** $N_{eff} < N_t / 2$.
- **$N_{eff}$ Formula:** $N_{eff} = 1 / \sum (w_t^{(i)})^2$.
- **KLD-Sampling:** Adaptive $N_t$ via chi-square bound on KL divergence; $\epsilon \approx 0.05$ typical.
- **Use Cases:** Global localization, kidnapped robot, perceptual aliasing, symmetric environments.
- **Trade-off:** Higher $N_t$ → better accuracy, higher compute cost.
- **Key Advantage over EKF:** No linearity/Gaussianity assumption; works in global localization.
- **KTU-Favorite Diagrams:** Functional flowchart of PF loop, hypothesis lifecycle, multi-modal posterior.
- **Common Mistake:** Treating all particles as equally weighted at all times — they are NOT; weights evolve with measurements.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differences Between Markov and Kalman Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differences Between Markov and Kalman Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

**Markov Localization** is a probabilistic, recursive state-estimation algorithm used in mobile robotics to determine a robot's pose (position and orientation) within a known environment. It uses a discrete, grid-based representation of the state space and recursively updates the robot's belief (posterior probability distribution) using the **Markov assumption** — that the future state depends only on the current state, not on the sequence of events that preceded it. It can represent **multi-modal, non-Gaussian** belief distributions, making it suitable for **global localization** from an initially unknown pose.

**Kalman Localization** is a specialized, continuous-state variant of the Bayes filter that uses the **Kalman Filter** algorithm. It assumes that all uncertainty — both in the robot's motion and in its sensor measurements — can be modeled as **zero-mean, uncorrelated Gaussian noise**. The belief is therefore represented entirely by a mean vector (μ) and a covariance matrix (Σ), which is inherently **uni-modal** (single-peaked).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes *Probabilistic Robotics* fundamentals. The key distinction students must internalize is that Markov Localization is a *general* Bayes filter discretization, while Kalman Localization is a *restricted, parametric* instance assuming linearity and Gaussianity (the **Kalman Filter** is sometimes called the "Markov Localization with Gaussians" in the continuous case).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped into an unfamiliar city:

- **Markov Localization** is like having a **detailed map of the entire city divided into tiny squares (a grid)**. In every square, you maintain a probability score representing "I might be here." As you walk and observe landmarks, you update the probability of *every single square*. Your belief can be spread out over many disconnected regions at once (multi-modal) — e.g., "I could be in the park OR the stadium OR the train station." This is computationally heavy but very general.

- **Kalman Localization** is like assuming that a GPS-like device already told you (roughly) where you are, with a *single* cloud of uncertainty shaped like a bell curve. Each new reading just refines that single blob — shrinking and shifting it. It is fast and elegant, but if your initial guess is completely wrong (the bell curve is in the wrong city), the filter will happily converge to the wrong answer with high confidence. It cannot recover from a kidnapped-robot scenario.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters

- **State Vector:** Typically $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ (2D pose + heading) for planar robots.
- **Belief Representation (Markov):** Discrete grid $Bel(x_t)$ over the configuration space $\mathcal{X}$.
- **Belief Representation (Kalman):** Continuous Gaussian $N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.
- **Key Assumption (Markov):** Conditional independence of observations given state.
- **Key Assumption (Kalman):** Linear motion and observation models with additive Gaussian noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to remember:** The dimension of the state $n$ directly determines the size of the covariance matrix in Kalman localization as $n \times n$. For a 2D planar robot, $n=3$ and $\Sigma$ is a **3×3** symmetric positive-definite matrix.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Breakdown — Markov Localization

Markov Localization operates on a discretized belief grid. The recursion is:

**Step 1 — Initialization:** For global localization, the belief is set to a uniform distribution (or wherever the robot *could* be).

**Step 2 — Prediction (Motion Update):** The belief from the previous time step is propagated through the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ using the total probability theorem. For each grid cell, we sum the probabilities of all prior states that could have transitioned into it.

**Step 3 — Correction (Measurement Update):** The predicted belief is multiplied by the observation likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ and then normalized to sum to 1.

**Step 4 — Loop:** Repeat for every new control and sensor reading.

**Why this works:** The Markov property decouples history from the present, allowing recursive estimation. The discrete grid can capture arbitrarily complex, multi-modal beliefs — even disjoint probability "islands" representing ambiguous positions (like identical corridors in an office building).

**How it works in practice:** It is computationally expensive — $O(|\mathcal{X}|)$ per update — and requires the configuration space to be pre-tessellated. It is robust to the kidnapped robot problem because the multi-modal belief can spontaneously re-appear far from the current estimate.

### 2.2 Algorithmic Breakdown — Kalman Localization

Kalman Localization uses the same Bayes recursion but **analytically** instead of over a grid, leveraging the Gaussian assumption.

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Start with a mean $\mu_0$ and covariance $\Sigma_0$. For a known initial pose, $\Sigma_0 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Prediction (Time Update):** Linear motion model $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim N(0, R_t)$. Update mean and covariance.

**Step 3 — Correction (Measurement Update):** Linear observation model $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$ with $\delta_t \sim N(0, Q_t)$. Compute the **Kalman Gain** $K_t$, then update mean and covariance with the innovation (residual).

**Why this works:** Because the product and convolution of Gaussians are closed-form Gaussians, the entire recursion has an analytical solution — no discretization needed.

**How it works in practice:** It is extremely efficient — $O(n^3)$ per update, dominated by matrix inversions — but only valid under **linear-Gaussian** assumptions. Real robots have non-linear motion (sin/cos in odometry) and non-linear sensors (range finders); the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes via Jacobians to handle this, but the belief remains a single Gaussian.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | Markov Localization | Kalman Localization |
|---|---|---|
| **Belief Type** | Discrete (grid) or particle-based | Continuous Gaussian $N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ |
| **Modality** | Multi-modal (arbitrary) | Uni-modal (single peak) |
| **State Space** | Discrete, finite grid $\mathcal{X}$ | Continuous, $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Prediction Equation** | $\overline{Bel}(x_t) = \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) Bel(x_{t-1})$ | $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$, $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ |
| **Update Equation** | $Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \overline{Bel}(x_t)$ | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$, $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$, $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$ |
| **Noise Assumption** | Arbitrary distributions | **Zero-mean Gaussian** (required) |
| **Model Assumption** | Any non-linear model | Linear models (or EKF linearization) |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(\vert\mathcal{X}\vert)$ per update (high) | $O(n^3)$ per update (low) |
| **Memory** | Stores $\vert\mathcal{X}\vert$ values | Stores $n + n(n+1)/2$ values |
| **Global Localization** | **Supported** (multi-modal) | **Not supported** (uni-modal) |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Recovers (multi-modal rediscovery) | **Fails** (converges to wrong peak) |
| **Position Tracking** | Works but inefficient | **Ideal use case** |
| **Real-world Examples** | Occupancy grid-based robots, indoor service robots | GPS/INS fusion, SLAM front-ends |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Kalman filters power nearly every commercial inertial navigation unit, smartphone orientation sensor, and aerospace guidance system. Markov localization underpins consumer robot vacuum cleaners (like early Roomba versions) and any robot that must start without knowing where it is. Modern systems often *combine* them — Kalman for local tracking, particle-filter-based Markov for global relocalization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the General Markov Localization to the Kalman Filter

Both algorithms are instances of the **Bayes Filter**, which estimates the posterior $Bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Step 1 — Bayes Filter Recursion (General).** Apply Bayes' rule and the theorem of total probability, leveraging the **Markov assumption** $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$:

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

Here, $\eta$ is the normalizer $1/p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$. The integral is the prediction step; the product with $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ is the correction step.

**Step 2 — Discrete Approximation (Markov Localization).** Replace the integral with a sum over the discrete grid $\mathcal{X}$:

$$
Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \sum_{x_{t-1} \in \mathcal{X}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, Bel(x_{t-1})
$$

This is exact for a finite grid. No distribution assumption is required. The only requirement is conditional independence of observations.

**Step 3 — Parametric Restriction (Kalman Localization).** Now restrict every density to be Gaussian. Let the motion model be linear:

$$
x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim N(0, R_t)
$$

Let the observation model also be linear:

$$
z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim N(0, Q_t)
$$

Assume the prior $Bel(x_{t-1}) = N(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$.

**Step 4 — Derive the Prediction Step.** The convolution of two Gaussians is a Gaussian. The product $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, Bel(x_{t-1})$ corresponds to summing independent Gaussian random variables. Matching first and second moments:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

The cross-term $A_t \Sigma_{t-1}$ arises from the propagation of the prior uncertainty; $R_t$ adds independent motion noise.

**Step 5 — Derive the Correction Step.** We want the Gaussian $Bel(x_t) = N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ proportional to $N(z_t; C_t x_t, Q_t) \cdot N(x_t; \bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)$. Setting up the product of two Gaussians:

$$
\exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)^T \bar{\Sigma}_t^{-1} (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) - \frac{1}{2}(z_t - C_t x_t)^T Q_t^{-1} (z_t - C_t x_t)\right)
$$

Completing the square in $x_t$ yields the posterior covariance:

$$
\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

This naturally defines the **Kalman Gain**:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

Substituting gives the compact update:

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Kalman Gain.** When $Q_t \to 0$ (sensor very accurate), $K_t \to C_t^{-1}$ and the measurement dominates — the mean snaps to the observed value. When $\bar{\Sigma}_t \to 0$ (prior very certain), $K_t \to 0$ and the prior dominates — measurements are ignored. This is the **Bayesian balance** between prior and likelihood, made analytically tractable by the Gaussian assumption.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Kalman Filter Position Tracking

A 1D robot moves with constant velocity. State: $x_t = [p_t, v_t]^T$, measurement: $z_t = p_t +$ noise.

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = [0.5]
$$

Suppose $\Delta t = 1$, prior $\mu_0 = [0, 1]^T$, $\Sigma_0 = I_{2\times2}$, control $u=0$, measurement $z_1 = 2.5$.

**Prediction:**

$$
\bar{\mu}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + 0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Innovation Covariance:**

$$
S = C \bar{\Sigma}_1 C^T + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5 = 2.1 + 0.5 = 2.6
$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_1 = \bar{\Sigma}_1 C^T S^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{2.6} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Update:**

$$
\mu_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix}(2.5 - 1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + 1.212 \\ 1 + 0.5775 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.212 \\ 1.5775 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\Sigma_1 = (I - K_1 C) \bar{\Sigma}_1 = \left(\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 & 0 \\ 0.385 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\right) \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.503 & 0.192 \\ 0.192 & 0.625 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The new mean $(2.212, 1.577)$ is closer to the measurement $2.5$, and the covariance has shrunk — exactly the expected behavior.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bayes Filter Family — Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bayes Filter - General Recursive Posterior Estimation] --> B[Markov Localization]
    A --> C[Kalman Filter]
    A --> D[Particle Filter]

    B --> B1[Discrete grid over configuration space X]
    B --> B2[Arbitrary multi-modal belief]
    B --> B3[Exact summation over finite grid]
    B --> B4[High computational cost O of X]

    C --> C1[Continuous Gaussian belief N of mu Sigma]
    C --> C2[Uni-modal single peak]
    C --> C3[Linear-Gaussian assumption required]
    C --> C4[EKF extension handles non-linearity]
    C --> C5[Low cost O of n cubed]

    D --> D1[Sample-based discrete approximation]
    D --> D2[Multi-modal capable]
    D --> D3[Non-parametric and general]

    classDef root fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#0d1f33,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef markov fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#1b4332,color:#ffffff
    classDef kalman fill:#9d174d,stroke:#6b0d34,color:#ffffff
    classDef particle fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff
    classDef detail fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,color:#212529

    class A root
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4 markov
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 kalman
    class D,D1,D2,D3 particle
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Comparison — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ML["MARKOV LOCALIZATION PIPELINE"]
        ML1[Initialize uniform belief over grid X] --> ML2[Receive control input u_t]
        ML2 --> ML3[Prediction: convolve with motion model p of x_t given u_t x_t-1]
        ML3 --> ML4[Receive sensor measurement z_t]
        ML4 --> ML5[Correction: multiply by likelihood p of z_t given x_t]
        ML5 --> ML6[Normalize belief to sum equals 1]
        ML6 --> ML7[Output: full probability grid over X]
    end

    subgraph KL["KALMAN LOCALIZATION PIPELINE"]
        KL1[Initialize Gaussian belief N of mu_0 Sigma_0] --> KL2[Receive control input u_t]
        KL2 --> KL3[Prediction: closed-form mu_bar and Sigma_bar via A_t B_t R_t]
        KL3 --> KL4[Receive sensor measurement z_t]
        KL4 --> KL5[Correction: compute Kalman Gain K_t]
        KL5 --> KL6[Update: mu_t and Sigma_t analytically]
        KL6 --> KL7[Output: mean vector and covariance matrix only]
    end

    ML7 -.versus.- KL7

    classDef mlBlock fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#081c15
    classDef klBlock fill:#ffd6e0,stroke:#9d174d,color:#3d0814
    classDef compare fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#1c1300

    class ML1,ML2,ML3,ML4,ML5,ML6,ML7 mlBlock
    class KL1,KL2,KL3,KL4,KL5,KL6,KL7 klBlock
    class ML7,Kl1 compare
```

### 4.3 Belief Shape Evolution — Qualitative Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Initial["STEP 0 - INITIAL BELIEF"]
        I1[Markov: Uniform flat line across all grid cells]
        I2[Kalman: Single wide bell curve centered at guess]
    end

    subgraph AfterMove["STEP 1 - AFTER MOTION UPDATE"]
        M1[Markov: Shifted and spread grid, possibly multiple peaks]
        M2[Kalman: Bell curve shifted and widened by motion noise R]
    end

    subgraph AfterObs["STEP 2 - AFTER MEASUREMENT UPDATE"]
        O1[Markov: Sharpened peaks at observation-consistent locations, multi-modal if ambiguous]
        O2[Kalman: Bell curve narrowed and recentered, remains single peak]
    end

    Initial --> AfterMove --> AfterObs

    classDef markovBox fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#0a1f15
    classDef kalmanBox fill:#fde7f0,stroke:#9d174d,color:#2b0712

    class I1,M1,O1 markovBox
    class I2,M2,O2 kalmanBox
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State any three major differences between Markov localization and Kalman localization.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Belief Representation:** Markov localization represents belief over a discrete grid and can capture **multi-modal, non-Gaussian** distributions, while Kalman localization represents belief as a single **Gaussian** parameterized by mean and covariance.
2. **Global vs Local Localization:** Markov localization can solve the **global localization** problem (including the kidnapped robot case) because it maintains multiple hypotheses simultaneously; Kalman localization is restricted to **local position tracking** and fails under global uncertainty.
3. **Computational Complexity:** Markov localization is computationally expensive with cost $O(\vert\mathcal{X}\vert)$ per update, whereas Kalman localization is efficient with $O(n^3)$ cost per update, dominated by matrix inversions.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Why is Kalman localization unsuitable for the kidnapped robot problem?*

**Model Answer:**
The kidnapped robot problem requires the robot to recover its pose after being suddenly teleported to an arbitrary, unknown location. Kalman localization assumes the belief is a **single Gaussian** (uni-modal). After teleportation, the true pose lies far outside the predicted covariance ellipse. Since the filter only tracks a single hypothesis, it cannot re-initialize a new peak at the actual location — it will instead *converge with high confidence to the wrong estimate*. Markov localization, being multi-modal, can spontaneously grow a new probability island at the true location when the new sensor observations are inconsistent with the old peak, thereby recovering successfully.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the Kalman filter prediction and update equations from the general Bayes filter recursion, clearly stating the assumptions required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A planar mobile robot has state $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ undergoing pure translation. The motion model is $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ where $u_t = [0.1, 0, 0]^T$ and $\epsilon_t \sim N(0, R)$ with $R = 0.01 \cdot I_{3\times3}$. The prior belief is $\mu_0 = [0, 0, 0]^T$, $\Sigma_0 = I_{3\times3}$. A sensor returns $z_1 = [0.15, 0, 0]^T$ with observation model $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$, $\delta_t \sim N(0, Q)$, $Q = 0.05 \cdot I_{3\times3}$. Compute the posterior $\mu_1$ and $\Sigma_1$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

The Bayes filter gives:

$$
Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Assumptions of the Kalman Filter:**
1. The motion model is **linear**: $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t$.
2. The observation model is **linear**: $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$.
3. Noise terms are **zero-mean Gaussian**: $\epsilon_t \sim N(0, R_t)$, $\delta_t \sim N(0, Q_t)$.
4. Initial belief is **Gaussian**: $Bel(x_0) = N(\mu_0, \Sigma_0)$.

**Prediction:** The integral becomes the convolution of two Gaussians. Matching moments:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

**Update:** The product of two Gaussians is again a Gaussian. Completing the square yields:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

The posterior is the closed-form Gaussian $N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Motion model is $x_t = I \cdot x_{t-1} + I \cdot u_t + \epsilon_t$, so $A = I_{3\times3}$, $B = I_{3\times3}$.

**Prediction:**

$$
\bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_1 = I \cdot I \cdot I + R = I_{3\times3} + 0.01 \cdot I_{3\times3} = 1.01 \cdot I_{3\times3}
$$

So $\bar{\Sigma}_1 = \text{diag}(1.01, 1.01, 1.01)$.

**Innovation Covariance:** $C = I_{3\times3}$:

$$
S = C \bar{\Sigma}_1 C^T + Q = 1.01 \cdot I + 0.05 \cdot I = 1.06 \cdot I_{3\times3}
$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_1 = \bar{\Sigma}_1 C^T S^{-1} = 1.01 \cdot I \cdot (1.06)^{-1} \cdot I = \frac{1.01}{1.06} I_{3\times3} \approx 0.9528 \cdot I_{3\times3}
$$

**Posterior Mean:**

Innovation: $z_1 - C \bar{\mu}_1 = [0.15, 0, 0]^T - [0.1, 0, 0]^T = [0.05, 0, 0]^T$

$$
\mu_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 0.9528 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 + 0.04764 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.14764 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Posterior Covariance:**

$$
\Sigma_1 = (I - K_1 C) \bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1 - 0.9528)(1.01) \cdot I_{3\times3} = 0.0472 \cdot 1.01 \cdot I_{3\times3} \approx 0.0477 \cdot I_{3\times3}
$$

So $\Sigma_1 \approx \text{diag}(0.0477, 0.0477, 0.0477)$.

The posterior uncertainty has shrunk by a factor of ~21 from the prior, demonstrating the strong measurement update.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Markov localization algorithm with its mathematical formulation. Discuss why a discrete grid representation enables global localization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Markov and Kalman localization across eight specific dimensions, presenting the comparison in a structured table. Briefly explain the kidnapped robot problem and state which algorithm handles it correctly. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Markov Localization:**

Markov localization is a recursive Bayes filter that maintains a probability distribution — the **belief** — over the robot's pose. The belief $Bel(x_t)$ at time $t$ is the posterior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Algorithm Formulation:**

**1. Initialization:** Set $Bel(x_0)$ uniformly over all reachable states for global localization, or as a delta at the known pose.

**2. Prediction (Motion Update):** Propagate the previous belief through the stochastic motion model:

$$
\overline{Bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**3. Correction (Measurement Update):** Multiply the prediction by the observation likelihood and normalize:

$$
Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{Bel}(x_t), \quad \eta^{-1} = \int p(z_t \mid x') \, \overline{Bel}(x') \, dx'
$$

**4. Loop** with new control $u_{t+1}$ and measurement $z_{t+1}$.

**Why a Discrete Grid Enables Global Localization:**

The configuration space $\mathcal{X}$ is discretized into a finite set of cells $\{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(N)}\}$. Each cell carries its own belief value. Critically:
- Beliefs in **disconnected regions** can simultaneously hold non-zero probability (e.g., two visually identical corridors).
- The algorithm is **non-parametric** — it makes no assumption about the shape of the belief, so multiple distinct hypotheses (modes) coexist naturally.
- When the robot is kidnapped to a new location, the sensor observations will be inconsistent with the old peak. The likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ becomes small everywhere the old belief had mass, but a new peak can grow at the new location — the algorithm self-recovers without external intervention.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | Markov Localization | Kalman Localization |
|---|---|---|
| Belief representation | Discrete grid of probabilities | Gaussian $N(\mu, \Sigma)$ |
| Modality | Multi-modal | Uni-modal |
| State space | Discrete / finite | Continuous |
| Noise model | Arbitrary | Gaussian required |
| Model | Non-linear supported | Linear (EKF for non-linear) |
| Global localization | Yes | No |
| Kidnapped robot | Recovers | Fails |
| Computational cost | $O(\vert\mathcal{X}\vert)$ | $O(n^3)$ |

**Kidnapped Robot Problem Explanation:** The robot is suddenly teleported to an unknown location while the filter continues running. Its belief about its old pose becomes inconsistent with new sensor readings. The **Markov localization** algorithm handles this correctly because its multi-modal belief can grow a new probability mass at the true location once the observations rule out the old pose. **Kalman localization** fails because its single Gaussian cannot represent both the old and new pose hypotheses — it will erroneously converge to a wrong, overconfident estimate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by **omitting the Gaussian noise assumption** when describing Kalman localization. Always state explicitly: *"The Kalman filter assumes zero-mean, additive Gaussian noise in both the motion and observation models, and that the initial belief is Gaussian."* Additionally, do **not** state that Kalman localization can solve global localization — it cannot under standard assumptions. A common 1-mark deduction is conflating the EKF with the standard Kalman filter; mention "EKF" only when explicitly discussing non-linear extensions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Markov Localization** = general Bayes filter over a **discrete grid**, capable of **multi-modal** beliefs.
- **Kalman Localization** = special case under **linear-Gaussian** assumptions, producing a **uni-modal** Gaussian belief.
- Markov uses **summation** over grid cells; Kalman uses **closed-form matrix algebra** (no discretization).
- Kalman is **computationally cheap** ($O(n^3)$); Markov is **expensive** ($O(\vert\mathcal{X}\vert)$) but more general.
- Markov localization supports **global localization** and recovers from the **kidnapped robot problem**; Kalman localization cannot.
- Use **Kalman** for efficient **local tracking** when the initial pose is approximately known; use **Markov** (or particle filters) for **global relocalization**.
- Key formulas to memorize for KTU exams:
  - Kalman Prediction: $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$, $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$.
  - Kalman Update: $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$, $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$, $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$.
  - Bayes Filter (Markov): $Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \sum p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) Bel(x_{t-1})$.
- The **Kalman Gain** balances prior certainty vs. measurement certainty: $K_t \to 0$ if prior is certain, $K_t \to C_t^{-1}$ if sensor is very accurate.
- Modern robotics frequently **combines both** paradigms — Kalman for tracking, Markov/particle for global relocalization in SLAM systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation and Reasoning About Space: Representing Space - Spatial Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representation and Reasoning About Space: Representing Space - Spatial Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spatial Decomposition** is a fundamental representation technique in mobile robotics whereby the continuous, two-dimensional (or three-dimensional) configuration space (C-space) of a robot's operational environment is partitioned into a finite collection of simpler, non-overlapping primitive regions (called **cells** or **regions**). The objective is to transform a continuous path-planning problem into a discrete graph-search problem, enabling the application of classical graph algorithms (Dijkstra, A*, BFS, DFS) to find collision-free trajectories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping):**
> *Spatial decomposition is the process of subdividing the free space of the robot's workspace into a set of non-overlapping regions (cells) such that the connectivity of the free space can be captured as a connectivity graph over these cells, allowing the path-planning problem to be reduced to a discrete search problem over the graph.*

Mathematically, the workspace $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ (or $\mathbb{R}^3$) is partitioned such that:

$$W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i \quad \text{where} \quad R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset \text{ for } i \neq j$$

Each region $R_i$ must satisfy an **admissibility criterion**: any two points inside $R_i$ must be mutually reachable by a motion primitive that lies entirely within $R_i \cup \{\text{adjacent cells}\}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building Floor Plan Puzzle"**
> 
> Imagine you are inside a large, maze-like shopping mall and you need to find a route from the main entrance (start) to a specific shop (goal). The mall is one giant continuous space, and calculating every possible continuous curve is impossible.
> 
> - **Without Decomposition:** You would have to consider an **infinite** number of curved paths through the floor — a computationally intractable nightmare.
> - **With Spatial Decomposition:** You mentally divide the mall into **tiles, corridors, and rooms** (cells). Now, you only need to decide the *order* in which to traverse these tiles. The path becomes a sequence of discrete choices, identical to solving a puzzle on a chessboard.
> 
> **Key Insight:** Spatial decomposition converts a "calculus problem" (continuous geometry) into a "graph problem" (discrete nodes and edges), which computers excel at solving using well-established search algorithms.

### 1.3 Why Spatial Decomposition is Critical in Mobile Robotics

- **Computational Tractability:** Reduces an infinite-dimensional continuous problem to a finite discrete problem.
- **Algorithm Compatibility:** Enables the use of standard graph search algorithms (A*, Dijkstra) instead of non-linear optimization in continuous space.
- **Sensor Integration:** Discretized representations align naturally with grid-based sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, occupancy grids).
- **Memory Efficiency:** Finite cell sets can be stored compactly in standard data structures (arrays, trees, hash maps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Alert:** Every decomposition method involves a trade-off between:
> 1. **Resolution** (granularity of cells)
> 2. **Completeness** (guarantees of finding a path if one exists)
> 3. **Computational Cost** (memory and time complexity)

### 1.4 Classification Overview of Decomposition Methods

Spatial decomposition is broadly classified into two major families, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Family | Core Principle | Resolution | Completeness |
|--------|----------------|------------|--------------|
| **Exact Decomposition** | Cells are generated to *exactly* match obstacle boundaries; cell shapes are polygons (trapezoids, triangles). | Variable (geometry-driven) | **Resolution-Complete** |
| **Approximate (Grid) Decomposition** | Cells are of *fixed geometric shape* (squares, cubes); obstacles are rasterized into the grid. | Uniform and user-defined | **Resolution-Complete** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Exact Cell Decomposition — Operational Logic

In exact decomposition, the free space $C_{free}$ is partitioned into polygonal cells whose edges coincide exactly with the boundaries of obstacles and the outer workspace boundary. The resulting cells are **convex**, ensuring that any straight-line motion between any two points within the same cell is guaranteed to be collision-free.

**The Classical Exact Decomposition Pipeline:**

1. **Critical Point Identification:** Sweep a vertical line across the workspace and record the points where the line first enters an obstacle (entry vertex) and where it last leaves the obstacle (exit vertex). These are called the **critical points** of the sweep-line.

2. **Cell Construction:** Connect the critical points of adjacent obstacles using straight-line segments to form non-overlapping convex cells.

3. **Adjacency Graph Construction:** Build a graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of cells and $E$ represents shared edges (or shared vertices in degenerate cases) between cells.

4. **Graph Search:** Apply A* or Dijkstra on $G$ to find a sequence of cells from start to goal.

5. **Path Smoothing (Optional):** Construct a continuous path by connecting midpoints of adjacent shared edges.

#### 2.1.1 The Trapezoidal Decomposition (Visibility-Based)

The simplest form of exact decomposition where a vertical sweep line is swept from $x = -\infty$ to $x = +\infty$. At each $x$-coordinate, the visible interval (the portion of the sweep line not occluded by any obstacle) is computed. The **decomposition breakpoints** occur at obstacle vertices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawback of Trapezoidal Decomposition:** Creates many small, thin trapezoids that increase the graph size unnecessarily, leading to higher search cost.

#### 2.1.2 The Boustrophedon Decomposition (Improved Exact Method)

The **Boustrophedon** decomposition (from Greek *boustrophedon* = "as the ox plows") is an extension of the trapezoidal decomposition that merges adjacent cells when the connectivity of the free intervals changes *only at* obstacle vertices. The vertical sweep line identifies points called **critical points** where:

- A new free interval appears (entering free space).
- An existing free interval disappears (entering an obstacle).
- A free interval splits into two (passing through a "left" obstacle vertex).
- Two free intervals merge into one (passing through a "right" obstacle vertex).

**Result:** Significantly fewer cells (often 2–5× fewer) than trapezoidal decomposition, reducing graph search complexity proportionally.

### 2.2 Approximate (Grid-Based) Cell Decomposition — Operational Logic

In approximate decomposition, the workspace is divided into a regular grid of fixed-size cells (squares in 2D, cubes in 3D). Each cell is classified as:

- **Free (White):** No obstacle is present; robot can traverse.
- **Occupied (Black):** Obstacle present; robot cannot traverse.
- **Unknown (Gray):** Sensor data insufficient.

**Resolution Parameter $r$:** The side length of each cell. The total number of cells scales as $O(n^2)$ in 2D for an $n \times n$ workspace.

#### 2.2.1 Fixed Cell Decomposition

The simplest approximation: the entire workspace is uniformly subdivided into a grid. The robot can move between any two adjacent free cells (4-connected or 8-connected neighborhood).

- **4-Connectivity:** Robot moves only N, S, E, W (4 neighbors).
- **8-Connectivity:** Robot moves N, S, E, W, NE, NW, SE, SW (8 neighbors).

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Pitfall (8-Connectivity):** In 8-connected grids, a path may appear to exist visually but be physically blocked by diagonal obstacle corners. This is called the "corner-cutting problem" and is a frequent source of errors in KTU exam answers.

#### 2.2.2 Adaptive Cell Decomposition (Quadtree / Octree)

To overcome the inefficiency of fixed grids (where vast open areas are represented by many identical cells), adaptive decomposition uses a **hierarchical tree structure**:

- **2D — Quadtree:** Each node has 4 children representing the NW, NE, SW, SE quadrants of the parent cell. A cell is recursively subdivided only if it contains mixed content (both free and obstacle regions).
- **3D — Octree:** Each node has 8 children representing the 8 octants of the parent cell.

A cell is **homogeneous** (leaf node) if it is entirely free or entirely occupied; otherwise, it is **non-homogeneous** and is subdivided further.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Workspace Partition | $W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Disjoint union of cells | — |
| 2 | Cell Count (2D Grid) | $N_{cells} = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{r} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{r} \right\rceil$ | Number of grid cells for resolution $r$ | cells |
| 3 | Cell Count (3D Grid) | $N_{cells} = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{r} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{r} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{L_z}{r} \right\rceil$ | Number of cubic cells | cells |
| 4 | Cell Index (2D) | $idx = i \cdot N_y + j$ | Linear indexing for grid cell $(i,j)$ | — |
| 5 | Memory Complexity (Grid) | $M = O\left(\frac{L^2}{r^2}\right)$ | 2D grid memory in 2D workspace of size $L$ | bytes |
| 6 | Time Complexity (A* on Grid) | $T = O(N_{cells} \log N_{cells})$ | Worst-case A* search | seconds |
| 7 | Quadtree Subdivision Condition | $R_{homogeneous} \iff \text{cell} \subseteq C_{free} \lor \text{cell} \subseteq C_{obs}$ | Recursion termination rule | — |
| 8 | Path Length (Manhattan, 4-conn) | $L_{path} = \sum \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | Heuristic-compatible path length | meters |
| 9 | Path Length (Diagonal, 8-conn) | $L_{path} = \sum \min(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert) \cdot \sqrt{2} + \vert \Delta x - \Delta y \vert$ | Octile distance | meters |
| 10 | Critical Point Condition (Boustrophedon) | $\frac{d\, n_{free}(x)}{dx} \neq 0$ | Sweep-line free interval count changes | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation Note on Path Length Heuristic:** In KTU 2024 board exams, the diagonal (octile) distance heuristic is the admissible heuristic for 8-connected grids because it accounts for diagonal motion. The Manhattan distance is admissible only for 4-connected grids.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Use occupancy grid representations built from LiDAR and camera data for online path planning.
- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva):** Grid-based planners enable efficient navigation in cluttered, structured environments.
- **Drone Swarms:** 3D voxel-based approximate decomposition for collision avoidance in aerial fleets.
- **Search and Rescue Robots:** Adaptive quadtrees allow variable-resolution maps suited to uneven terrain.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Cell Count in a Fixed Grid Decomposition

**Problem Setup:** Consider a rectangular workspace of dimensions $L_x \times L_y$ meters. The grid resolution is $r$ meters per cell. Derive the total number of cells and the memory requirement.

**Step 1 — Number of Cells Along the X-Axis:**

The number of cells along the X-direction is the ceiling of the workspace length divided by the cell resolution.

$$N_x = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{r} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Number of Cells Along the Y-Axis:**

Similarly, for the Y-direction:

$$N_y = \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{r} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Total Cell Count in 2D:**

The total number of cells is the product of the cells along both axes:

$$N_{cells}^{2D} = N_x \cdot N_y = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{r} \right\rceil \cdot \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{r} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4 — Approximation for Large L:**

For $L_x \gg r$ and $L_y \gg r$, the ceiling operator can be approximated:

$$N_{cells}^{2D} \approx \frac{L_x \cdot L_y}{r^2} = \frac{A}{r^2}$$

where $A = L_x \cdot L_y$ is the workspace area in square meters.

**Step 5 — Extension to 3D (Voxel Grid):**

By analogous reasoning, in 3D with $L_z$:

$$N_{cells}^{3D} \approx \frac{L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z}{r^3} = \frac{V}{r^3}$$

where $V$ is the workspace volume in cubic meters.

**Step 6 — Memory Requirement:**

If each cell stores 1 byte of occupancy data:

$$M_{bytes} = N_{cells} \times 1 \text{ byte} = \frac{A}{r^2} \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example:**

For $L_x = L_y = 10$ m and $r = 0.1$ m:

$$N_{cells}^{2D} = \frac{10 \times 10}{(0.1)^2} = \frac{100}{0.01} = 10{,}000 \text{ cells}$$

For $r = 0.01$ m (1 cm resolution):

$$N_{cells}^{2D} = \frac{100}{(0.01)^2} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ cells} = 1 \text{ MB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exponential Memory Growth:** Halving the resolution quadruples the memory. This is the fundamental scaling bottleneck of grid-based methods, motivating hierarchical (quadtree/octree) approaches.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Decomposition Methods

The following matrix presents an exhaustive comparison across KTU-evaluated parameters:

| Parameter | Trapezoidal | Boustrophedon | Fixed Grid | Quadtree/Octree |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|-----------------|
| **Cell Shape** | Trapezoids | Trapezoids (merged) | Squares/Cubes | Variable (hierarchical) |
| **Cell Count (typical)** | High | Medium | Very High | Low (sparse regions) |
| **Boundary Match** | Exact | Exact | Approximate | Approximate (refined locally) |
| **Algorithmic Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(N_{cells})$ | $O(N_{leaves} \log N_{leaves})$ |
| **Memory Footprint** | Low | Low | Very High | Moderate |
| **Best For** | Sparse, polygonal env. | Sparse, polygonal env. | Dense, sensor-rich env. | Mixed, open + cluttered |
| **Completeness** | Resolution-Complete | Resolution-Complete | Resolution-Complete | Resolution-Complete |
| **Path Optimality** | Good (graph-smoothable) | Better (fewer cells) | Approximation-bound | Approximation-bound |
| **Real-time Updates** | Poor (full rebuild) | Poor (full rebuild) | Excellent (incremental) | Good (local updates) |
| **Implementation Difficulty** | High | High | Low | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** Use **fixed grid** for online, sensor-driven, real-time applications. Use **exact decomposition** (Boustrophedon) for offline planning in well-mapped environments where path quality is critical. Use **quadtree/octree** when environments are mostly open with localized obstacles (storage warehouses, parking lots).

### 3.3 Worked Example: Boustrophedon Decomposition Cell Count Reduction

**Scenario:** Workspace with 4 polygonal obstacles having 5 vertices each (20 vertices total).

- **Trapezoidal Decomposition:** Generates one trapezoid per vertex interval, approximately 20 cells.
- **Boustrophedon Decomposition:** Generates cells only at "split" and "merge" critical points. For this configuration, assume 6 critical events.

**Computation:**

$$N_{trap} = 20 \text{ cells}, \quad N_{bous} = 6 \text{ cells}$$

**Reduction Ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{N_{trap} - N_{bous}}{N_{trap}} \times 100\% = \frac{20 - 6}{20} \times 100\% = 70\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A 70% reduction in cell count translates directly to a comparable reduction in graph search time, which is critical for KTU exam questions involving algorithmic complexity comparisons.

### 3.4 Mathematical Condition for Path Existence in a Decomposed Graph

Given the adjacency graph $G = (V, E)$ constructed from spatial decomposition, a collision-free path from start cell $v_s$ to goal cell $v_g$ exists if and only if $v_s$ and $v_g$ are in the same connected component of $G$ restricted to the free cells $V_{free}$.

**Formally:**

$$\exists \text{ path } \pi(v_s, v_g) \iff v_s \sim_{V_{free}} v_g$$

where $\sim_{V_{free}}$ denotes reachability within the subgraph $G_{free} = (V_{free}, E_{free})$.

**Reachability Test via BFS/DFS:**

$$v_s \sim_{V_{free}} v_g \iff \text{BFS}(G_{free}, v_s) \text{ visits } v_g$$

This has time complexity $O(\vert V_{free} \vert + \vert E_{free} \vert)$ — linear in graph size, which is the central computational motivation for decomposition.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Spatial Decomposition Pipeline

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the end-to-end functional flow of a spatial decomposition system as required in any KTU module-level design question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Workspace W] --> B[Obstacle Boundary Extraction]
    B --> C[Decomposition Method Selector]
    C --> D{Decomposition Type}
    D -- Exact --> E[Trapezoidal or Boustrophedon Sweep]
    D -- Approximate --> F[Fixed Grid or Quadtree Subdivision]
    E --> G[Convex Cell Set R1 to Rn]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Adjacency Graph G Construction]
    H --> I[Graph Search: A star or Dijkstra]
    I --> J{Path Found}
    J -- Yes --> K[Sequence of Cells]
    J -- No --> L[Report Failure]
    K --> M[Path Smoothing Module]
    M --> N[Continuous Trajectory Output]
    L --> O[Replan or Terminate]
```

### 4.2 Trapezoidal vs. Boustrophedon Comparison Schematic

This diagram shows how a vertical sweep line behaves differently in the two exact decomposition methods, with a sample obstacle configuration containing three polygonal obstacles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD1[Trapezoidal Decomposition]
        A1[Sweep Line Position x1] --> A2[Record Critical Vertices]
        A2 --> A3[Create Trapezoid per Vertex]
        A3 --> A4[Result: 8 Small Trapezoids]
    end
    subgraph TD2[Boustrophedon Decomposition]
        B1[Sweep Line Position x1] --> B2[Detect Split and Merge Events]
        B2 --> B3[Merge Adjacent Trapezoids]
        B3 --> B4[Result: 3 Large Trapezoids]
    end
    A4 -.Comparison.-> B4
```

### 4.3 Quadtree Hierarchical Subdivision Logic

The diagram below captures the recursive decision tree of a quadtree-based adaptive decomposition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Root Cell: Entire Workspace] --> chk1{Is Cell Homogeneous?}
    chk1 -- Yes, all Free --> leaf1[Leaf: Mark Free]
    ch1 -- Yes, all Occupied --> leaf2[Leaf: Mark Occupied]
    chk1 -- No, Mixed --> split1[Subdivide into 4 Quadrants]
    split1 --> q1[Quadrant NW]
    split1 --> q2[Quadrant NE]
    split1 --> q3[Quadrant SW]
    split1 --> q4[Quadrant SE]
    q1 --> chk2{Is Cell Homogeneous?}
    q2 --> chk3{Is Cell Homogeneous?}
    q3 --> chk4{Is Cell Homogeneous?}
    q4 --> chk5{Is Cell Homogeneous?}
    chk2 -- Yes, Free --> leaf3[Leaf: Mark Free]
    chk2 -- Yes, Occupied --> leaf4[Leaf: Mark Occupied]
    chk2 -- No --> split2[Recurse Further]
```

### 4.4 Adjacency Graph for Path Planning

The following Mermaid graph illustrates how the cells of a decomposition form a search graph. Each node is a cell, and each edge is a navigable transition between adjacent free cells.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cellS[Start Cell] --> cellA[Cell A]
    cellA --> cellB[Cell B]
    cellA --> cellC[Cell C]
    cellB --> cellD[Cell D]
    cellC --> cellD
    cellC --> cellE[Cell E]
    cellD --> cellG[Goal Cell]
    cellE --> cellG
    cellB --> cellE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Once the adjacency graph is constructed, applying A* search with an admissible heuristic (e.g., Euclidean distance to the goal) yields the optimal cell sequence from `cellS` to `cellG`. The shortest path here is `cellS -> cellA -> cellC -> cellE -> cellG`.

### 4.5 Functional Architecture of a Mobile Robot Spatial Reasoning Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sens1[LiDAR Sensor] --> fusion[Sensor Fusion]
    sens2[Camera] --> fusion
    sens3[IMU] --> fusion
    fusion --> occ[Occupancy Grid Update]
    occ --> decomp[Spatial Decomposition Module]
    decomp --> graph[Adjacency Graph Builder]
    graph --> planner[Path Planner: A star]
    planner --> smooth[Path Smoother]
    smooth --> ctrl[Motion Controller]
    ctrl --> act[Wheel Actuators]
    act -.Feedback.-> sens1
```
