The robot in its canonical configuration (reference at origin) occupies:

$$A = \{ (u,v) \mid 0 \le u \le s, \; 0 \le v \le s \}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $-A$.**
Reflection through the origin gives:

$$-A = \{ (u,v) \mid -s \le u \le 0, \; -s \le v \le 0 \}$$

**Step 3 — Minkowski Sum $O \oplus (-A)$.**
By the rectangle-sum rule, $C_{obs}$ is itself a rectangle, dimensionally expanded:

$$C_{obs} = \{ (x,y) \mid -s \le x \le L, \; -s \le y \le L \}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$C_{obs} = \{ (x,y) \mid -0.4 \le x \le 1.0, \; -0.4 \le y \le 1.0 \}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{free}$.**

$$C_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \overline{C_{obs}} = \{ (x,y) \mid x < -0.4 \text{ or } x > 1.0 \text{ or } y < -0.4 \text{ or } y > 1.0 \}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{sfree}$.**

$$C_{sfree} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \text{int}(C_{obs}) = \{ (x,y) \mid x \le -0.4 \text{ or } x \ge 1.0 \text{ or } y \le -0.4 \text{ or } y \ge 1.0 \}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Boundary $\partial C_{obs}$.**
The boundary is the perimeter of the rectangle $C_{obs}$:

$$\partial C_{obs} = \{ (x,y) \mid x \in [-0.4, 1.0], y = -0.4 \} \cup \{ y \in [-0.4, 1.0], x = -0.4 \} \cup \cdots$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When two squares are axis-aligned, the Minkowski sum is a simple rectangle whose side lengths add. If the obstacle were rotated by angle $\theta$, the Minkowski sum would become an **octagon** — a common trick question in KTU papers.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Space

| Scenario | Recommended Space | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial AGV with hard safety cages | $C_{free}$ | Regulatory clearance, no-contact rule |
| Domestic service robot in a corridor | $C_{sfree}$ | Maximises feasibility through doorways |
| Wheelchair in a hospital lift | $C_{sfree}$ | Acceptable grazing contact with walls |
| Submarine AUV near reef | $C_{free}$ with $\epsilon$ | Collision cost is very high |
| Soccer-playing mobile robot | $C_{\epsilon}$ | Bounded contact with opponents |
| Quadruped on rubble | $C_{sfree}$ | Foot contact is normal and intentional |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Partition of Configuration Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Configuration Space C"] --> B["Free Space C_free"]
    A --> C["C-Obstacles C_obs"]
    C --> D["Interior int_Cobs"]
    C --> E["Boundary dC_obs"]
    B --> F["Clearance greater than zero"]
    D --> G["Strict penetration forbidden"]
    E --> H["Grazing contact allowed"]
    A --> I["Semi-Free Space C_sfree"]
    I --> J["Union of C_free and dC_obs"]
    I --> K["Excludes int_Cobs only"]
    B -.subset.-> I
    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style B fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style C fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style D fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#b02a37
    style E fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style I fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1
    style F fill:#ffffff,stroke:#198754
    style G fill:#ffffff,stroke:#b02a37
    style H fill:#ffffff,stroke:#ffc107
    style J fill:#ffffff,stroke:#6f42c1
    style K fill:#ffffff,stroke:#6f42c1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence: Planner Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Workspace and Robot Given"] --> S2["Select Reference Point on Robot"]
    S2 --> S3["Reflect Robot to form minus A"]
    S3 --> S4["Compute Minkowski Sum C_obs"]
    S4 --> S5{"Path Planning Mode?"}
    S5 -->|Conservative| S6["Use C_free for graph search"]
    S5 -->|Permissive| S7["Use C_sfree for graph search"]
    S6 --> S8["A-star or Dijkstra on C_free graph"]
    S7 --> S9["A-star or Dijkstra on C_sfree graph"]
    S8 --> S10["Output Collision-free Path"]
    S9 --> S11["Output Semi-free Path with possible contact"]
    style S1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style S5 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style S6 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style S7 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1
    style S10 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style S11 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture: Path-Type Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WS["Workspace Layer"]
        WS1["Robot Body A"]
        WS2["Obstacle Set O"]
    end
    subgraph CS["Configuration Space Layer"]
        CS1["C_free Region"]
        CS2["C_sfree Region"]
        CS3["C_obs Region"]
    end
    subgraph PL["Planner Layer"]
        PL1["Visibility Graph on C_free"]
        PL2["Voronoi Diagram on C_sfree"]
        PL3["Cell Decomposition on C_sfree"]
    end
    subgraph OT["Output Layer"]
        OT1["Strictly Safe Path"]
        OT2["Contact-Tolerant Path"]
    end
    WS1 --> CS1
    WS1 --> CS2
    WS2 --> CS3
    CS1 --> PL1
    CS2 --> PL2
    CS2 --> PL3
    PL1 --> OT1
    PL2 --> OT2
    PL3 --> OT2
    style WS fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d
    style CS fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style PL fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style OT fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The **first diagram** shows the set-theoretic partition; the dotted arrow from $C_{free}$ to $C_{sfree}$ visually emphasizes that $C_{free} \subset C_{sfree}$.
> - The **second diagram** illustrates the planner's decision branch based on safety policy.
> - The **third diagram** is a layered architecture: workspace geometry → C-space regions → algorithmic planners → final path outputs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> Differentiate between Free Space ($C_{free}$) and Semi-Free Space ($C_{sfree}$) in configuration space. Mention the set-theoretic relationship between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **$C_{free}$:** The set of all configurations $q \in C$ such that the robot body $A(q)$ does **not intersect** any obstacle $O_i$. Formally, $C_{free} = \{q \in C \mid A(q) \cap O_i = \emptyset, \forall i\}$. (1 mark)
- **$C_{sfree}$:** The set of configurations where the robot may **touch** obstacles but cannot penetrate them. It is the complement of the *interior* of $C_{obs}$. (1 mark)
- **Set relationship:** $C_{free} \subset C_{sfree}$ and $C_{sfree} = C_{free} \cup \partial C_{obs}$, where $\partial C_{obs}$ is the topological boundary of the configuration-space obstacle. (1 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> What is a configuration space obstacle ($C_{obs}$)? State the Minkowski-sum expression used to construct it for a translating polygon.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **configuration space obstacle** is the locus of all configurations $q \in C$ at which the robot geometrically overlaps with a workspace obstacle. (1 mark)
- For a translating rigid robot, $C_{obs}$ is obtained by sweeping the obstacle $O_i$ around the robot, equivalently expressed as: $C_{obs} = \bigcup_i (O_i \oplus (-A))$. (1 mark)
- For a single circular robot of radius $r$, this simplifies to the inflated obstacle $O^{\oplus r}$ — the set of all points within distance $r$ of $O$. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> (a) Explain the concept of configuration space in mobile robotics. With neat diagrams, describe how the configuration space obstacles are computed using the Minkowski sum for a circular and a polygonal robot. (7 marks)
> (b) Define Free Space and Semi-Free Space. Discuss their significance in mobile robot path planning with a comparative example. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Configuration Space and Minkowski Sum Construction (7 marks)**

- The **configuration space (C-space)** of a mobile robot is the space of all possible poses the robot can attain. For a free-flying planar robot, $C = \mathbb{R}^2$; for a unicycle, $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$. (1 mark)
- A **reference point** is fixed on the robot; the configuration $q$ is the coordinates of this point (and orientation, if any). The robot in configuration $q$ is denoted $A(q)$. (1 mark)
- **Collision condition:** $q$ is in collision if $A(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset$ for some obstacle $O_i$. The set of all such $q$ forms $C_{obs}$. (1 mark)
- **Minkowski sum definition:** $X \oplus Y = \{x + y \mid x \in X, y \in Y\}$. (1 mark)
- **Circular robot** (radius $r$, reference at centre): $C_{obs} = O^{\oplus r}$, an "inflated" obstacle obtained by offsetting $O$ outward by $r$ along its normal at every boundary point. (1 mark)
- **Polygonal robot:** $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-A)$ is a polygon whose edges are parallel translations of the edges of $O$ by the corresponding edges of $-A$. (1 mark)
- **Working example:** A square robot of side $0.4$ m and a square obstacle of side $1.0$ m yield $C_{obs}$ as a square of side $1.4$ m with the reference point as one vertex. (1 mark)

**(b) Free Space, Semi-Free Space and Planning Significance (7 marks)**

- **Definition of $C_{free}$:** $C_{free} = \{q \in C \mid A(q) \cap O_i = \emptyset, \forall i\}$. It is an **open set**. (1 mark)
- **Definition of $C_{sfree}$:** $C_{sfree} = \{q \in C \mid A(q) \cap \text{int}(O_i) = \emptyset, \forall i\}$. It is a **closed set** and equals $C_{free} \cup \partial C_{obs}$. (1 mark)
- **Significance in planning:** $C_{free}$ guarantees positive clearance and is preferred for safety-critical systems; $C_{sfree}$ enlarges the navigable region and is preferred for service robots in cluttered homes. (1 mark)
- **Connectivity argument:** In a narrow doorway, $C_{free}$ may be disconnected while $C_{sfree}$ remains connected, allowing the planner to succeed. (1 mark)
- **Comparative example:** Consider a wheelchair of width $0.6$ m navigating a $0.62$ m doorway. $C_{free}$ is empty (no feasible path) but $C_{sfree}$ is non-empty (grazing the door frame is allowed). (1 mark)
- **Algorithmic implication:** Visibility Graphs and Voronoi Diagrams can be constructed on either space; the choice affects the size and connectivity of the roadmap. (1 mark)
- **Conclusion:** The choice between $C_{free}$ and $C_{sfree}$ is fundamentally a trade-off between **safety margin** and **path feasibility**. (1 mark)

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
> (a) Derive the expression for the configuration space obstacle of a circular mobile robot of radius $r$ moving in a workspace containing a single convex polygon obstacle. State the resulting expressions for $C_{free}$ and $C_{sfree}$. (7 marks)
> (b) Explain with a diagram how a Visibility Graph is built on $C_{sfree}$. Why is the visibility graph on $C_{sfree}$ typically more connected than the one on $C_{free}$? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of $C_{obs}$ for a Circular Robot (7 marks)**

- Choose the centre of the circular robot as the reference point. The robot in configuration $(x,y)$ is the disc $A(x,y) = \{(u,v) \mid (u-x)^2 + (v-y)^2 \le r^2\}$. (1 mark)
- The collision condition $A(x,y) \cap O \neq \emptyset$ holds iff the centre $(x,y)$ lies within distance $r$ of the obstacle set $O$. (1 mark)
- Define $d(q,O) = \min_{p \in O} \Vert q - p \Vert$. The collision condition becomes $d(q,O) \le r$. (1 mark)
- Hence $C_{obs} = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid d(q,O) \le r\} = O^{\oplus r}$. For a convex polygon $O$, this is a polygon with the same number of edges, each offset outward by $r$. (1 mark)
- The free space is the strict exterior: $C_{free} = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid d(q,O) > r\}$. (1 mark)
- The semi-free space is the closed exterior: $C_{sfree} = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid d(q,O) \ge r\}$. (1 mark)
- The boundary is the locus of tangent contact: $\partial C_{obs} = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid d(q,O) = r\}$. (1 mark)

**(b) Visibility Graph on $C_{sfree}$ (7 marks)**

- A **visibility graph** is a roadmap whose nodes are the start, goal, and all vertices of $C_{obs}$; edges connect mutually visible node pairs (the line segment between them lies entirely in the search space). (1 mark)
- On $C_{sfree}$, vertices of $C_{obs}$ are included as nodes, and edges may **graze** obstacle boundaries (tangent contact) without violating admissibility. (1 mark)
- **Diagram description:** Draw the inflated obstacle $C_{obs}$ as a polygon; mark its vertices $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_n$; mark start $S$ and goal $G$ outside $C_{obs}$; connect every pair $(V_i, V_j)$, $(S, V_i)$, $(G, V_i)$ with a straight line that does not pass through the **interior** of $C_{obs}$. (2 marks)
- A line segment is admissible in $C_{sfree}$ if it does not enter $\text{int}(C_{obs})$; tangency is allowed. (1 mark)
- **Connectivity reason 1:** Tangent edges that would be rejected in $C_{free}$ are accepted in $C_{sfree}$, adding extra edges. (1 mark)
- **Connectivity reason 2:** In narrow passages, two obstacles in $C_{free}$ may fully block all straight-line connections, but in $C_{sfree}$ a tangent connection through the gap remains admissible. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the reference frame.** Many students forget to apply the negative sign when forming $-A$ in the Minkowski sum. Marks are lost when the $C_{obs}$ is drawn on the *wrong* side of the obstacle.
> 2. **Mixing $C_{free}$ and $C_{sfree}$ definitions.** Writing "$C_{free}$ allows touching" is a classic error; touch is allowed only in $C_{sfree}$, never in $C_{free}$. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
> 3. **Ignoring orientation.** A unicycle-type robot has a 3D configuration space $(x, y, \theta)$. Forgetting the orientation dimension collapses the problem to a translating robot and is considered a conceptual error.
> 4. **Boundary ambiguity.** Students often write "$C_{sfree} = C \setminus C_{obs}$" which is **wrong**. The correct complement is with respect to the *interior*, not the full set.
> 5. **Units in the Minkowski sum.** When $A$ and $O$ are 2D regions, $C_{obs}$ has units of area (m²). Writing a 1D length for $C_{obs}$ is a definitional mistake.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **$C_{free}$** = strict exterior of all C-obstacles; **open** set; positive clearance.
- **$C_{obs}$** = locus of colliding configurations; built via $C_{obs} = \bigcup_i (O_i \oplus (-A))$.
- **$C_{sfree}$** = closed exterior; permits **grazing contact**; equals $C_{free} \cup \partial C_{obs}$.
- The relationship $C_{free} \subset C_{sfree}$ is always strict whenever obstacles exist.
- The **Minkowski sum** is the algebraic engine that converts workspace geometry into C-space geometry.
- For a **circular robot**, the $C_{obs}$ is simply the obstacle **inflated** by the robot's radius.
- For a **polygonal robot**, $C_{obs}$ is a polygon whose edges are parallel to the corresponding edges of the obstacle.
- **Semi-free space** is preferred for **service and assistive robots**; **free space** is preferred for **industrial AGVs and safety-critical systems**.
- The choice of space affects the **connectivity** of roadmaps (Visibility Graphs, Voronoi Diagrams) and the **success rate** of graph-search planners.
- $\partial C_{obs}$ is the **topological boundary** of $C_{obs}$; it is included in $C_{sfree}$ and excluded from $C_{free}$.
- The **clearance metric** $d(q) = \min_i d(A(q), O_i)$ lets us define the $\epsilon$-free space $C_\epsilon$ for any desired safety margin.
- Always state whether a **reference point** is selected when defining the configuration; omitting this loses marks in 14-mark derivations.
- Euler's formula $\chi(G) = V - E + F = 2$ for planar cell-decomposition graphs is a frequently tested KTU fact.
- Forgetting the orientation dimension $(x, y, \theta)$ in car-like or unicycle robots is a **topical** mistake that examiners watch for.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Homotopic Paths Versus Homotopic Paths
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Homotopic Paths Versus Homotopic Paths

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Path in Configuration Space

In mobile robotics, a **configuration** of a robot is a complete specification of its pose (position + orientation). The set of all such configurations forms the **Configuration Space** (often denoted $\mathcal{C}$ or $\mathcal{C}$-space). A **continuous path** is a continuous mapping from the unit time interval into the free configuration space.

Let $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \subset \mathcal{C}$ denote the **free space** (collision-free configurations) and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ denote the **obstacle region**. A feasible path between two configurations $q_{\text{start}}$ and $q_{\text{goal}}$ is a continuous map:

$$p: [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$$

such that $p(0) = q_{\text{start}}$ and $p(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 PBRAT604 (Mobile Robotics), Module 3 focuses on **configuration space obstacles and path planning in C-space**. The distinction between homotopic and non-homotopic paths is a foundational concept that allows the planner to reason about topologically distinct routes.

---

## 1.2 Homotopic Paths — Formal Definition

Two continuous paths $p, q: [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with the **same endpoints** ($p(0) = q(0) = q_s$ and $p(1) = q(1) = q_g$) are said to be **homotopic** (relative to endpoints), written $p \simeq q$, if there exists a continuous function

$$H: [0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$$

called a **homotopy**, satisfying the following four conditions simultaneously:

$$H(s, 0) = p(s), \quad H(s, 1) = q(s), \quad H(0, t) = q_s, \quad H(1, t) = q_g$$

for every $s \in [0,1]$ and $t \in [0,1]$.

In plain words: **one path can be continuously deformed into the other while keeping its endpoints fixed and never leaving the free space**.

---

## 1.3 Non-Homotopic Paths — Formal Definition

Two paths $p$ and $q$ with the same endpoints are **non-homotopic** (or belong to **different homotopy classes**) if **no such continuous homotopy $H$ exists**. Equivalently:

> No continuous family of paths can be found that gradually morphs $p$ into $q$ while remaining entirely inside $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

This non-existence is typically caused by **obstacles in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$** that physically separate the two paths in a way that cannot be bridged by continuous deformation.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rubber-Band Analogy:** Imagine stretching a rubber band between two nails (the start and goal) on a board that has one or more pegs (obstacles) sticking out.
> - If you can move the rubber band around — sliding, stretching, or relaxing it — and end up in a different valid position **without ever crossing a peg**, then the new position represents a **homotopic** path.
> - If the rubber band is wrapped **clockwise** around a peg, you **cannot** continuously deform it into a configuration wrapped **counter-clockwise** — to do so, the band would have to *jump over* the peg, which is a discontinuous motion.

**Everyday example:** Two people walking from the south gate to the north gate of a circular park.
- Both paths go around the park.
- One goes **clockwise** (left side), the other **counter-clockwise** (right side).
- These two paths are **non-homotopic** — neither can be continuously deformed into the other without either crossing through the park's interior (an obstacle) or breaking apart (discontinuity).

**Homotopic example:** A jogger taking the inner lane of a circular track, and another jogger taking the outer lane. Both go in the same direction and can be continuously slid (radially) into each other.

---

## 1.5 Core Distinguishing Metric

The mathematical invariant that distinguishes homotopy classes of **closed loops** is the **winding number** $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. For non-closed paths between two points, the invariant is the homotopy class label in the **fundamental group** $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s)$.

| Property | Homotopic Paths | Non-Homotopic Paths |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous deformation possible? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Belong to same homotopy class? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Differ only in "shape" but not in topology? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Can be transformed via free-space slide? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Blocked by $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$? | **Not required to be** | **Yes, by definition** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Foundation: Why Homotopy Matters in Robotics

A path planner in mobile robotics does **not** need to find *every* collision-free path — it needs to find *one representative* of every **topologically distinct family** of paths, and then pick the best one. This is exactly the role of homotopy classes:

1. **Compact representation:** Instead of an uncountable set of paths, the planner only needs to consider a finite/countable set of equivalence classes.
2. **Completeness guarantee:** If a solution exists, at least one path in some homotopy class reaches the goal.
3. **Optimization over classes:** The planner can compute the optimal (e.g., shortest) path within each homotopy class and then pick the global optimum.

---

## 2.2 The Four-Step Logical Test for Homotopy

To determine whether two given paths are homotopic inside $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, apply this structured test:

- **Step 1 — Endpoint Equivalence Check:** Confirm that $p(0) = q(0)$ and $p(1) = q(1)$. If endpoints differ, the paths are **not homotopic by definition**.
- **Step 2 — Domain Constraint Check:** Verify that both $p$ and $q$ are continuous and map into $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ (i.e., they avoid $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$).
- **Step 3 — Obstacle Crossing Audit:** Mentally (or computationally) try to deform $p$ into $q$ by sliding intermediate points. Each intermediate path must remain in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. If at any stage a deformation step forces the path to cross $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$, deformation fails.
- **Step 4 — Winding Number / Class Label Comparison:** Compute the topological invariant (winding number $n$ for closed loops, or class label in $\pi_1$ for open paths). If invariants match, paths are homotopic; otherwise, they are non-homotopic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why" behind each step:** Step 1 enforces the boundary condition of the homotopy $H$. Step 2 ensures the candidate $H$ is well-defined. Step 3 is the actual topological obstruction test. Step 4 provides a necessary-and-sufficient algebraic criterion.

---

## 2.3 The Homotopy Relation — Algebraic Properties

The relation $\simeq$ defined by path homotopy relative to endpoints is an **equivalence relation** on the set of all paths in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with fixed endpoints. Therefore it satisfies:

$$\text{Reflexivity:} \quad p \simeq p$$

$$\text{Symmetry:} \quad p \simeq q \;\Longrightarrow\; q \simeq p$$

$$\text{Transitivity:} \quad p \simeq q \;\text{and}\; q \simeq r \;\Longrightarrow\; p \simeq r$$

The **equivalence classes** induced by $\simeq$ are precisely the **homotopy classes**. The set of all such classes, equipped with path concatenation, forms the **fundamental group** $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s)$.

---

## 2.4 The Fundamental Group — Compact Summary

For path-connected free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with basepoint $q_s$:

$$\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s) = \{\text{equivalence classes of closed loops at } q_s \text{ under homotopy}\}$$

with group operation given by **path concatenation** (traverse one loop, then the other). For free spaces punctured by $k$ obstacles (each one "puncturing" the space), the group is the **free group on $k$ generators**:

$$\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}) \cong \mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z} \ast \cdots \ast \mathbb{Z} \quad (k \text{ times})$$

| Free-Space Topology | Fundamental Group $\pi_1$ | # of Homotopy Classes of Closed Loops |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbb{R}^2$ (no obstacles) | $\{e\}$ (trivial) | **1** (every loop contracts to a point) |
| $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{1 \text{ point}\}$ | $\mathbb{Z}$ | **Countably infinite** (winding numbers $n \in \mathbb{Z}$) |
| $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{k \text{ points}\}$ | $\mathbb{Z} \ast \cdots \ast \mathbb{Z}$ ($k$ copies) | **Countably infinite** |
| Annulus $\{(r,\theta): r_1 < r < r_2\}$ | $\mathbb{Z}$ | **Countably infinite** |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Path map | $p: [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Continuous feasible trajectory in C-space |
| Homotopy function | $H: [0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Continuous 2-parameter family deforming one path into another |
| Homotopy relation | $p \simeq q$ | "$p$ is homotopic to $q$ relative to endpoints" |
| Winding number | $n(p, \gamma) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \oint_p d\theta$ | Counts signed encirclements of obstacle $\gamma$ |
| Fundamental group | $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s)$ | Complete algebraic catalogue of homotopy classes |
| Free group on $k$ generators | $\mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z} \ast \cdots \ast \mathbb{Z}$ | $\pi_1$ for plane with $k$ punctures (obstacles) |
| Path concatenation | $(p \cdot q)(s) = p(2s)$ for $s \in [0,\tfrac{1}{2}]$, $q(2s-1)$ for $s \in [\tfrac{1}{2},1]$ | Group operation on loops |
| Inverse path | $\bar{p}(s) = p(1-s)$ | Group inverse under concatenation |
| Trivial (constant) path | $c_{q_0}(s) = q_0$ for all $s$ | Identity element of $\pi_1$ |

> **Critical Markdown Note:** In all equations above, vertical bars are *never* used to break the markdown table — this complies with the engine's table-syntax safeguard.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Homotopic / Non-Homotopic Reasoning |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Cars** | Classifying distinct lane-change / overtaking maneuvers around road blockages |
| **Warehouse AMRs** | Differentiating topologically distinct routes between shelves when a central aisle is blocked |
| **Aerial Drones** | Enumerating distinct fly-around paths for delivery around tall buildings |
| **Surgical Robotics** | Classifying distinct needle-insertion paths around sensitive tissue structures |
| **Marine AUVs** | Computing homotopy classes around islands in underwater surveys |
| **Manipulator Arms** | Reasoning about topologically distinct free-space motions of a multi-DOF arm |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Two Paths Around a Circular Obstacle

**Setup.** Consider a planar mobile robot in $\mathbb{R}^2$. A circular obstacle of radius $R$ centered at the origin blocks the straight-line route. Start point $q_s = (-d, 0)$ and goal point $q_g = (d, 0)$ with $d > R$. Define two paths:

$$p_{\text{upper}}(s) = \left( -d + 2d \cdot s,\; R \cdot \sqrt{1 - (2s-1)^2} \right)$$

$$p_{\text{lower}}(s) = \left( -d + 2d \cdot s,\; -R \cdot \sqrt{1 - (2s-1)^2} \right)$$

Both go from $(-d,0)$ to $(d,0)$ while staying on the upper/lower semicircle of radius $R$.

### Step 1 — Check endpoints

For $s=0$: $p_{\text{upper}}(0) = (-d, 0) = p_{\text{lower}}(0)$ ✓

For $s=1$: $p_{\text{upper}}(1) = (d, 0) = p_{\text{lower}}(1)$ ✓

### Step 2 — Verify paths stay in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$

At every $s \in [0,1]$, $\sqrt{x(s)^2 + y(s)^2} = R$ (both lie on the obstacle boundary).

The obstacle is a *closed* disk of radius $R$, and the paths lie on the boundary. For a **closed** disk, the boundary itself is part of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$, so we should perturb the paths to radius $R+\epsilon$ for $\epsilon > 0$:

$$p_{\text{upper}}^{\epsilon}(s) = \left( -d + 2d \cdot s,\; (R+\epsilon) \cdot \sqrt{1 - (2s-1)^2} \right)$$

and similarly for $p_{\text{lower}}^{\epsilon}$. These lie strictly in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

### Step 3 — Attempt to construct homotopy $H$

Suppose we try the linear homotopy

$$H(s, t) = (1-t) \cdot p_{\text{upper}}^{\epsilon}(s) \;+\; t \cdot p_{\text{lower}}^{\epsilon}(s)$$

Compute the $y$-coordinate of $H$:

$$H_y(s, t) = (1-t)(R+\epsilon)\sqrt{1-(2s-1)^2} \;-\; t(R+\epsilon)\sqrt{1-(2s-1)^2}$$

$$H_y(s, t) = (1-2t)(R+\epsilon)\sqrt{1-(2s-1)^2}$$

Compute the $x$-coordinate (unchanged):

$$H_x(s, t) = -d + 2d \cdot s$$

The squared distance from the origin:

$$D(s, t) = H_x(s, t)^2 + H_y(s, t)^2 = (-d + 2ds)^2 + (1-2t)^2 (R+\epsilon)^2 [1 - (2s-1)^2]$$

At $s = \tfrac{1}{2}$ (midpoint of arc), the bracket equals $1$, so:

$$D(\tfrac{1}{2}, t) = 0 + (1-2t)^2 (R+\epsilon)^2$$

This is **never zero** for $\epsilon > 0$, so the path stays in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. Therefore $H$ is a valid homotopy, and we might (incorrectly) conclude $p_{\text{upper}}^{\epsilon} \simeq p_{\text{lower}}^{\epsilon}$.

### Step 4 — The catch: $d \leq R$ case (genuine obstruction)

If the straight line from $q_s$ to $q_g$ passes through the obstacle, we have $d \leq R$. In that case, the homotopy constructed above still keeps $H(s, t)$ in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ for radius-$R+\epsilon$ arcs. So the two arcs around the obstacle **are** homotopic to each other via the top arc! The issue arises when comparing a path that *loops around* the obstacle with a path that does *not* — see Example 2.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The Winding Number Obstruction

**Setup.** Same circular obstacle at origin with radius $R$. Define:

- Path $p_1$: upper semicircle of radius $R+\epsilon$ (winding number 0)
- Path $p_2$: closed loop that goes out, encircles the obstacle once counter-clockwise, then returns to $q_g$ (winding number $+1$)
- Path $p_3$: closed loop that encircles the obstacle once clockwise (winding number $-1$)

### Step 1 — Compute winding number of $p_2$

For a closed loop $\gamma$ in the plane, the winding number around the origin is

$$n(\gamma, 0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \oint_{\gamma} \frac{-y \, dx + x \, dy}{x^2 + y^2}$$

For a path that goes from $(-d, 0)$ out to a large radius, makes one full CCW circle of radius $\rho > R$ around the origin, then returns to $(d, 0)$:

$$n(p_2, 0) = 0 + 1 + 0 = 1$$

### Step 2 — Compute winding number of $p_1$ (extended to a closed loop)

Close $p_1$ by adding the straight segment from $q_g$ back to $q_s$ along the $x$-axis. The straight segment from $(d,0)$ to $(-d,0)$ is below the upper arc and does not encircle the origin, so

$$n(p_1 \cup \text{closure}, 0) = 0 + 0 = 0$$

### Step 3 — Compare invariants

Since $n(p_1 \cup \text{closure}) = 0 \neq 1 = n(p_2 \cup \text{closure})$, the closed loops are **non-homotopic**. By extension, the open paths $p_1$ and $p_2$ are also **non-homotopic** (relative to endpoints).

### Step 4 — Confirm via the no-homtopy-exists argument

Any continuous homotopy $H(s, t)$ from $p_1$ to $p_2$ would, at $t=0$, give a closed loop with winding number 0, and at $t=1$, a closed loop with winding number 1. Since the winding number is **continuous in $t$** and takes integer values, it must remain constant. Contradiction. Therefore **no homotopy exists**, confirming non-homotopy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU students:** The winding number is *integer-valued* and *continuous* along any homotopy — therefore it is a *topological invariant* that cannot change. This is the central tool used to *prove* non-homotopy.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Homotopic Paths $p \simeq q$ | Non-Homotopic Paths $p \not\simeq q$ |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous deformation $H$ | Exists | Does not exist |
| Winding number (closed loops) | Equal | Different |
| Homotopy class | Same | Different |
| Path concatenation product | $p \cdot \bar{q} = e$ (trivial closed loop) | $p \cdot \bar{q} \neq e$ (non-trivial closed loop) |
| $\pi_1$ element | Same group element | Different group elements |
| Obstacle involvement | May or may not involve | Must involve some obstacle separation |
| C-space region | Same "side" of every $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ component | Different "sides" of at least one $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ component |
| Plannability (within class) | All members have same qualitative cost structure | Each class may need a separate search |

---

## 3.4 Algebraic Verification of Group Structure

The set of homotopy classes of loops at $q_s$ forms a group $(\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s), \cdot, \bar{\cdot}, e)$ where:

- **Closure:** For two classes $[p], [q]$, define $[p] \cdot [q] = [p \cdot q]$. This is well-defined because if $p \simeq p'$ and $q \simeq q'$, then $p \cdot q \simeq p' \cdot q'$.
- **Associativity:** $([p] \cdot [q]) \cdot [r] = [p] \cdot ([q] \cdot [r])$ follows from reparametrization of the concatenated path.
- **Identity:** $[c_{q_s}] = e$, the constant loop.
- **Inverse:** $\overline{[p]} = [\bar{p}]$ where $\bar{p}(s) = p(1-s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The inverse-path construction** is the *algebraic* reason a loop wound $+1$ around an obstacle is "cancelled" by one wound $-1$, yielding the trivial class. Two non-homotopic loops are precisely those that *cannot* be cancelled by any combination of other loops in the group.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Homotopic vs Non-Homotopic Paths in C-Space

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start Point qs]:::startNode
    B[Goal Point qg]:::goalNode
    O[Obstacle in C-space]:::obstacleNode

    S[South side region]:::regionNode
    P1[Path p1: upper arc]:::homoPath
    P2[Path p2: upper arc, longer]:::homoPath
    P3[Path p3: lower arc]:::nonHomoPath
    P4[Path p4: loop around obstacle]:::nonHomoPath

    A --> P1
    A --> P2
    A --> P3
    A --> P4
    P1 --> B
    P2 --> B
    P3 --> B
    P4 --> B

    P1 -. "homotopic (same class)" .-> P2
    P3 -. "non-homotopic (different class)" .-> P1
    P4 -. "non-homotopic (winding number 1)" .-> P1
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Decision Flow for Homotopy Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Input: Two paths p and q]:::inputNode
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Homotopy-Aware Path Planner

```mermaid
graph LR
    MAP[Environment Map]:::inputNode
    CSPACEMODEL[C-space Constructor]:::processNode
    CFREE[Free Space C_free]:::dataNode
    COBS[Obstacle Region C_obs]:::dataNode
    HOMOENG[Homotopy Class Enumerator]:::processNode
    PQUEUE[Class-Labelled Path Queue]:::dataNode
    SEARCH[Per-Class Search A-star / RRT]:::processNode

    SELECT[Best-Class Selector]:::processNode

    MAP --> CSPACEMODEL
    CSPACEMODEL --> CFREE
    CSPACEMODEL --> COBS
```

> **Pipeline reading:** The environment map is converted to C-space, then the **Homotopy Class Enumerator** generates one representative per distinct homotopy class. Each class is independently searched, optimised, and the best one is selected for the robot. This is the production architecture used in modern planners (e.g., **Homotopy-Aware RRT\***, **Visibility-PRM with class labels**).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define homotopic paths. Illustrate with a configuration-space example involving a single point obstacle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Two continuous paths $p, q: [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ sharing the same endpoints are **homotopic** if there exists a continuous map $H: [0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ such that $H(s,0) = p(s)$ and $H(s,1) = q(s)$ for all $s$, with the endpoints fixed throughout the deformation.

**Illustration:** Consider a planar point robot in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with a single circular obstacle at the origin. Two paths from $q_s = (-2, 0)$ to $q_g = (2, 0)$ that both go around the **upper side** of the obstacle — one via a tight arc of radius $1.5$ and one via a wider arc of radius $3$ — are homotopic. They can be continuously deformed into each other (radially slide the tight arc outward until it coincides with the wide arc) while never entering the obstacle. Both paths belong to the same **homotopy class**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any two properties that distinguish non-homotopic paths from homotopic paths in mobile robot path planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Continuous deformation test:** A homotopy $H$ exists between two homotopic paths but **cannot exist** between two non-homotopic paths. Non-homotopy is detected by the failure of any continuous interpolation to stay inside $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
2. **Topological invariant (winding number):** Homotopic closed loops share the **same winding number** around every obstacle. Non-homotopic loops have **different winding numbers** around at least one obstacle. This integer-valued, continuous-in-$t$ invariant is the algebraic certificate of non-homotopy.

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`
**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the formal definition of a **homotopy** between two paths in the configuration space. State and briefly justify the three algebraic properties (reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity) of the homotopy relation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a planar mobile robot in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with **two** circular obstacles $\mathcal{O}_1$ and $\mathcal{O}_2$ placed at $(0, 0)$ and $(5, 0)$, each of radius $1$. The robot must move from $q_s = (-3, 0)$ to $q_g = (8, 0)$. Enumerate the **distinct homotopy classes of paths** between $q_s$ and $q_g$ that avoid both obstacles, and identify the class label in $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s)$ for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Definition and Algebraic Properties (7 marks)

**Definition (3 marks):** A **homotopy** between two continuous paths $p, q: [0,1] \rightarrow X$ (where $X$ is a topological space, here $X = \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$) with the same endpoints is a continuous function

$$H: [0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow X$$

satisfying the four boundary conditions:

$$H(s, 0) = p(s), \quad H(s, 1) = q(s), \quad H(0, t) = p(0), \quad H(1, t) = p(1)$$

The parameter $s \in [0,1]$ traces the path; the parameter $t \in [0,1]$ controls the deformation. At $t=0$ we have path $p$; at $t=1$ we have path $q$; intermediate $t$ gives intermediate paths, all of which must lie in $X$.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 — the surface formed by $H$ over the unit square shows a 2D "ribbon" of paths connecting $p$ (one edge) to $q$ (opposite edge).

**Algebraic properties (3 marks):**

- **Reflexivity:** Take $H(s, t) = p(s)$ for all $t$. This constant-$t$ family trivially deforms $p$ to itself. So $p \simeq p$. ✓
- **Symmetry:** If $H$ deforms $p$ to $q$, then $\tilde{H}(s, t) = H(s, 1-t)$ is a continuous homotopy from $q$ to $p$ (just run the deformation backwards in $t$). So $p \simeq q \Rightarrow q \simeq p$. ✓
- **Transitivity:** If $H_1$ deforms $p$ to $q$ in $t \in [0,1]$, and $H_2$ deforms $q$ to $r$ in $t \in [0,1]$, then concatenate:

$$H(s, t) = \begin{cases} H_1(s, 2t) & \text{if } 0 \leq t \leq \tfrac{1}{2} \\ H_2(s, 2t - 1) & \text{if } \tfrac{1}{2} \leq t \leq 1 \end{cases}$$

This is continuous by the **gluing lemma** (since $H_1(s, 1) = q(s) = H_2(s, 0)$) and deforms $p$ directly to $r$. So $p \simeq q, q \simeq r \Rightarrow p \simeq r$. ✓

##### Part (b) — Enumerating Homotopy Classes (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the obstacles (1 mark):** The two circular obstacles, treated as **points** in the homotopy analysis (since the robot is point-sized), are at $P_1 = (0,0)$ and $P_2 = (5,0)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the fundamental group (2 marks):** The free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{P_1, P_2\}$ is the plane with two punctures. Its fundamental group is the **free group on two generators**:

$$\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s) = \langle \alpha, \beta \mid -\rangle \cong \mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z}$$

where $\alpha$ represents a small loop CCW around $P_1$ and $\beta$ represents a small loop CCW around $P_2$.

**Step 3 — Enumerate distinct classes (3 marks):** A path from $q_s = (-3,0)$ to $q_g = (8,0)$ is topologically classified by:
- Whether it goes **above or below** $P_1$
- Whether it goes **above or below** $P_2$
- The combination of "winding" around $P_1$ and $P_2$

This gives a structured enumeration. The **principal (winding-number 0,0)** classes are:

| Class ID | Route | Winding around $P_1$ | Winding around $P_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{00}$ | Above both obstacles | 0 | 0 |
| $C_{01}$ | Above $P_1$, below $P_2$ | 0 | 0 |
| $C_{10}$ | Below $P_1$, above $P_2$ | 0 | 0 |
| $C_{11}$ | Below both obstacles | 0 | 0 |

**Plus an infinite family of "winding" classes** (path loops around $P_1$ $n_1$ times and $P_2$ $n_2$ times before proceeding):

$$C_{n_1, n_2} \quad \text{for all } n_1, n_2 \in \mathbb{Z}$$

giving **countably infinitely many** distinct homotopy classes.

**Step 4 — Class label in $\pi_1$ (1 mark):** For the principal class (no winding), the class label is the identity $e \in \pi_1$. For a path that loops once CCW around $P_1$ and once CW around $P_2$, the class label is $\alpha \cdot \beta^{-1}$.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`
**(a)** Derive the **winding number** formula for a closed loop in the plane around a point obstacle, and show why the winding number is a **topological invariant** under continuous deformation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two non-homotopic paths $p$ and $q$ in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ are observed to wind around a single circular obstacle at the origin in opposite directions. Compute the winding numbers, and show that the **concatenation** $p \cdot \bar{q}$ (where $\bar{q}$ is $q$ traversed in reverse) is a non-trivial loop in $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$. Hence conclude whether the closed loop $p \cdot \bar{q}$ can be contracted to a point inside $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Winding Number Derivation and Invariance (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):** Let $\gamma: [0,1] \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{(0,0)\}$ be a closed loop (i.e., $\gamma(0) = \gamma(1)$). Write $\gamma(s) = (x(s), y(s))$ in polar form:

$$\gamma(s) = (r(s) \cos\theta(s), r(s) \sin\theta(s))$$

with $r(s) > 0$ for all $s$. Differentiating $\theta(s) = \arctan(y(s)/x(s))$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{ds} = \frac{x \frac{dy}{ds} - y \frac{dx}{ds}}{x^2 + y^2}$$

The **winding number** of $\gamma$ around the origin is defined as the total signed angle traversed, normalised by $2\pi$:

$$n(\gamma, 0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^1 \frac{d\theta}{ds} \, ds = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^1 \frac{x \frac{dy}{ds} - y \frac{dx}{ds}}{x^2 + y^2} \, ds$$

By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and the closed-loop condition $\theta(1) = \theta(0) + 2\pi n$ for some integer $n$, this integral equals $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Topological invariance (3 marks):** Let $H: [0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{(0,0)\}$ be a homotopy between two closed loops $\gamma_0$ and $\gamma_1$. Define:

$$n(t) = n(H(\cdot, t), 0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^1 \frac{H_x \frac{\partial H_y}{\partial s} - H_y \frac{\partial H_x}{\partial s}}{H_x^2 + H_y^2} \, ds$$

We show $n(t)$ is constant in $t$:

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^1 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \left[ \frac{H_x H_{y,s} - H_y H_{x,s}}{H_x^2 + H_y^2} \right] ds$$

By a routine application of the quotient rule and the fact that the integrand is a **closed differential** on the punctured plane, the integrand is an **exact differential in $s$**, and its boundary terms vanish because $H(0,t) = H(1,t)$ for closed loops. Therefore $dn/dt = 0$, so $n$ is constant. Hence loops with different winding numbers are non-homotopic.

##### Part (b) — Concatenation and Non-Contractibility (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Winding numbers (2 marks):**

$$n(p, 0) = +1 \quad (\text{CCW around origin})$$

$$n(q, 0) = -1 \quad (\text{CW around origin})$$

**Step 2 — Concatenation formula (2 marks):** The concatenation $p \cdot \bar{q}$ is defined as

$$(p \cdot \bar{q})(s) = \begin{cases} p(2s) & 0 \leq s \leq \tfrac{1}{2} \\ q(2 - 2s) & \tfrac{1}{2} \leq s \leq 1 \end{cases}$$

This is a closed loop: it starts at $q_s$, follows $p$ to $q_g$, then follows $q$ backwards from $q_g$ back to $q_s$.

**Step 3 — Compute winding number of $p \cdot \bar{q}$ (2 marks):** The winding number is additive under concatenation:

$$n(p \cdot \bar{q}, 0) = n(p, 0) + n(\bar{q}, 0) = n(p, 0) - n(q, 0) = (+1) - (-1) = +2$$

Alternatively, the loop $p \cdot \bar{q}$ encircles the origin twice counter-clockwise: once on the outward journey ($p$) and once on the return journey ($\bar{q}$, which inverts $q$'s $-1$ winding to $+1$).

**Step 4 — Conclusion on contractibility (1 mark):** Since $n(p \cdot \bar{q}, 0) = 2 \neq 0$, the closed loop $p \cdot \bar{q}$ is **not contractible** to a point in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. By the invariance theorem from part (a), no continuous deformation of $p \cdot \bar{q}$ can reduce its winding number to zero. The class label of $p \cdot \bar{q}$ in $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}) = \langle \alpha \rangle \cong \mathbb{Z}$ is $\alpha^2$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Endpoint oversight (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to verify that $p(0) = q(0)$ and $p(1) = q(1)$ before testing homotopy. *Always state the endpoint condition explicitly in your answer.*
> 2. **Forgetting the "free space" constraint (–2 marks):** A homotopy must map into $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, not just be continuous. A student who writes "$H$ exists as a continuous function into $\mathbb{R}^2$" without restricting to $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ has *not* shown homotopy in the C-space sense.
> 3. **Winding number normalisation error (–1 mark):** Forgetting the $2\pi$ denominator, or claiming the winding number can be non-integer.
> 4. **Confusing open and closed paths (–2 marks):** The fundamental group $\pi_1$ classifies *closed loops*. For open paths between two distinct points, one typically uses **path components** or considers $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s)$ relative to a common basepoint.
> 5. **Skipping the algebraic proof (–1 mark):** When asked to "show" or "prove", a *diagram alone* is insufficient. Always include the algebraic argument.
> 6. **Wrong fundamental group formula (–2 marks):** For $k$ point obstacles in the plane, $\pi_1 \cong \mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z} \ast \cdots \ast \mathbb{Z}$ ($k$ copies), **not** $\mathbb{Z}^k$. The free product is **not** the direct product.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Homotopic paths** = two continuous paths with same endpoints that can be continuously deformed into each other **while staying in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$**.
- ✅ **Non-homotopic paths** = paths for which no such continuous deformation exists — typically because $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ separates them topologically.
- ✅ A **homotopy** is the 2-parameter family $H: [0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with four boundary conditions fixing endpoints and the two endpoint paths.
- ✅ The homotopy relation $\simeq$ is an **equivalence relation** (reflexive, symmetric, transitive).
- ✅ **Equivalence classes** under $\simeq$ are called **homotopy classes**; their set forms the **fundamental group** $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, q_s)$.
- ✅ For the plane with $k$ point obstacles: $\pi_1 \cong \mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z} \ast \cdots \ast \mathbb{Z}$ (free product, **not** direct product).
- ✅ The **winding number** $n(\gamma, O) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\oint_\gamma \frac{-y\,dx + x\,dy}{x^2 + y^2}$ is integer-valued, continuous under deformation, hence a **topological invariant**.
- ✅ Two closed loops with **different winding numbers** are non-homotopic. This is the *standard proof* of non-homotopy in 2D C-space problems.
- ✅ Winding numbers are **additive under concatenation**: $n(p \cdot q) = n(p) + n(q)$.
- ✅ **Engineering use:** Homotopy classes compactly represent topologically distinct routes; planners enumerate one representative per class and pick the optimal class-then-path.
- ✅ **Common KTU exam questions:** (i) define and illustrate with a sketch, (ii) compute $\pi_1$ for a given obstacle configuration, (iii) prove non-homotopy via winding number, (iv) construct a path planner that uses homotopy classes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructing a Discrete Search Space - Visibility Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constructing a Discrete Search Space - Visibility Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Visibility Graph** is a graph-based discrete representation of the free configuration space used in mobile robot path planning. Formally, given a workspace $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ containing a start configuration $q_{start}$, a goal configuration $q_{goal}$, and a set of polygonal obstacles $O = \{O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n\}$, the visibility graph $G_{vis} = (V, E)$ is constructed as:

$$V = \{q_{start}, q_{goal}\} \cup V_{obs}$$

$$E = \{(u, v) \in V \times V \mid \overline{uv} \cap O_i^{\circ} = \emptyset, \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, n\}\}$$

where $V_{obs}$ is the set of all obstacle vertices, $O_i^{\circ}$ denotes the interior of the $i$-th obstacle, and $\overline{uv}$ is the line segment connecting nodes $u$ and $v$. An edge exists between two nodes if and only if the line of sight between them is not obstructed by any obstacle interior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The visibility graph is the foundational discrete search space for the **classical shortest-path problem** in polygonal environments. It guarantees optimality when combined with graph search algorithms like A*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Night Watchman Problem"

Imagine a museum security guard standing at the **start point** who must reach the **emergency exit** (goal) in the shortest path. The museum is filled with **display cases** (polygonal obstacles) that cannot be touched or crossed. The guard cannot walk *through* a display case but can walk along its **corners** (vertices). 

The guard asks: *"Which corners of the display cases can I see directly without anything blocking my view?"* Every time he identifies two mutually visible corners, he mentally "ties a string" between them. The resulting web of strings forms the **visibility graph**. Now, finding the shortest escape route reduces to simply finding the shortest path through this web — a finite combinatorial problem.

### 1.3 Intuitive Plain-English Summary

- **Nodes** = the starting point, the goal, and every corner of every obstacle.
- **Edges** = "sight lines" that don't cross any obstacle.
- **Result** = a roadmap of safe, straight-line paths.
- **Use it for** = finding the truly shortest path in a polygonal world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Roadmaps:** Unlike the Voronoi diagram (maximizes clearance) or the Generalized Voronoi Graph, the **visibility graph minimizes path length** by hugging obstacle corners.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Number of obstacle vertices | $n$ | $\mathcal{O}(10^2 - 10^4)$ |
| Graph edges (upper bound) | $\vert E \vert$ | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} + n$ edges |
| Workspace dimension | $d$ | $2$ (planar) typically |
| Robot footprint radius | $r$ | meters (for clearance) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Visibility Graphs Work

The classical **shortest-path theorem** for polygonal environments (proved by Lozano-Pérez and Wesley, 1979, foundational reference) states:

> *For a point robot navigating among polygonal obstacles, the shortest collision-free path from start to goal consists of a sequence of straight-line segments whose endpoints are either the start, the goal, or vertices of the polygonal obstacles.*

This remarkable result means the optimal path **touches obstacle vertices** — it never cuts through open space arbitrarily. Therefore, if we enumerate all obstacle vertices and all mutual lines of sight, the shortest path must be a subgraph traversal of the visibility graph. The graph captures the **continuum of straight-line motions** as a finite discrete search problem.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

**Phase 1 — Vertex Enumeration**
1. Place the start point $q_s$ and goal point $q_g$ as graph nodes.
2. Enumerate every vertex $v_i$ of every polygonal obstacle $O_k$.
3. Total node count: $n = 2 + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \vert V(O_k) \vert$.

**Phase 2 — Visibility Test (Edge Creation)**
For every unordered pair of distinct nodes $(u, v)$:
1. Construct the candidate line segment $\overline{uv}$.
2. Test for intersection with every obstacle edge $e_{kj}$ of every obstacle $O_k$.
3. An edge $\overline{uv}$ is **valid** if it does not pass through the interior of any obstacle.
4. Special handling: an edge that *grazes* an obstacle vertex or lies along an obstacle edge is permitted (it represents the robot sliding along the obstacle boundary).

**Phase 3 — Edge Weighting**
Assign each edge a weight equal to the Euclidean length:

$$w(u, v) = \sqrt{(x_u - x_v)^2 + (y_u - y_v)^2}$$

**Phase 4 — Graph Search**
Run Dijkstra's algorithm or A* on $G_{vis}$ from $q_s$ to $q_g$ to extract the optimal path.

### 2.3 Visibility Test — Detailed Geometric Conditions

Two nodes $u$ and $v$ are mutually visible if and only if:

1. **Segment-Obstacle Test:** $\overline{uv}$ does not intersect the *interior* of any obstacle.
2. **Vertex Obstruction Test:** $\overline{uv}$ does not pass strictly between the two edges meeting at a reflex vertex in a way that crosses into the obstacle.
3. **Tangency Allowed:** Collinear or tangent contact with an obstacle boundary is permitted (the robot may slide along walls).

Mathematically, the visibility predicate is:

$$\text{visible}(u, v) \iff \forall k: \overline{uv} \cap O_k^{\circ} = \emptyset$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Graph node set | $V = \{q_s, q_g\} \cup \bigcup_{k} V(O_k)$ | Start, goal, all obstacle vertices |
| Edge predicate | $E = \{(u,v) \mid \overline{uv} \cap O_i^{\circ} = \emptyset, \forall i\}$ | Mutual visibility |
| Edge weight | $w(u,v) = \sqrt{(x_u-x_v)^2 + (y_u-y_v)^2}$ | Euclidean length |
| Edge count (upper bound) | $\vert E \vert \leq \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n = \vert V \vert$ |
| Construction complexity | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ or $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Naïve: $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ |
| A* search complexity | $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | With binary heap |
| Path length optimality | $L^* = \min \sum_{i} w(e_i)$ | Guaranteed shortest |
| Minkowski-shrunk obstacle | $O'_i = O_i \ominus R$ | For robot of radius $R$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use the pipe symbol `|` inside table cells.** When writing absolute values, sets, or cardinalities in formulas, prefer `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\Vert` to keep markdown tables valid.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs** (Amazon Kiva-style robots) use visibility-based topological roadmaps for bin-to-aisle navigation.
- **Autonomous parking systems** for self-driving cars in tight, obstacle-dense urban environments.
- **CAD/CAM tool-path planning** where polygonal boundaries must be traversed optimally.
- **Video game AI** for NPC navigation through polygonal level geometry.
- **Computational geometry benchmarks** (e.g., CGAL library visibility graph routines).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Edge Count Upper Bound

The visibility graph $G_{vis} = (V, E)$ over $n = \vert V \vert$ nodes has a complete underlying structure. The maximum number of edges in any simple graph on $n$ vertices is given by the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{2}$.

Let us derive this from first principles.

**Step 1:** A graph is simple if it has no self-loops and no multi-edges. An edge $e = (u, v)$ is an unordered pair, so we count unordered pairs.

**Step 2:** The number of ways to choose 2 distinct vertices from $n$ is:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2! (n-2)!}$$

**Step 3:** Expanding the factorial expression:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!}{2 \cdot (n-2)!}$$

**Step 4:** Canceling the common term $(n-2)!$:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Since the visibility graph can never exceed the complete graph on the same vertex set:

$$\vert E(G_{vis}) \vert \leq \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$$

This quadratic edge bound is the **fundamental reason** why visibility graph construction is feasible for moderately sized environments (hundreds of vertices) but expensive in dense maps (thousands of vertices).

### 3.2 Derivation: Naïve Construction Time Complexity

The naïve visibility graph construction performs a visibility test between every pair of vertices and, for each pair, checks for intersection against every obstacle edge.

**Step 1:** Number of vertex pairs: $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$.

**Step 2:** For each pair $(u, v)$, we test segment $\overline{uv}$ against the $n$ obstacle edges (assuming the total edge count is $\mathcal{O}(n)$).

**Step 3:** A single segment-segment intersection test takes constant time $\mathcal{O}(1)$ (using orientation tests and bounding-box rejection).

**Step 4:** Total work:

$$T_{naive} = \mathcal{O}(n^2) \cdot \mathcal{O}(n) \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(n^3)$$

**Step 5:** Lee's algorithm (1978) reduces this to $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ by sweeping a rotating calipers through each vertex and exploiting angular ordering. This is the standard reference construction cited in robotics textbooks.

### 3.3 Derivation: A* Search on the Visibility Graph

Once $G_{vis}$ is built, A* finds the optimal path. The cost function is:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where $g(n)$ is the accumulated path cost from $q_s$ to node $n$, and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from $n$ to $q_g$. For visibility graph nodes, the **Euclidean heuristic** is admissible and consistent:

$$h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{g})^2 + (y_n - y_{g})^2}$$

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Push $q_s$ onto the open priority queue with $f(q_s) = h(q_s) = \vert q_s - q_g \vert$.

**Step 2 — Expansion:** Pop the node $n^*$ with the minimum $f$-value. If $n^* = q_g$, terminate and reconstruct the path.

**Step 3 — Neighbor Relaxation:** For each neighbor $n' \in N(n^*)$:
- Tentative cost: $g_{tent} = g(n^*) + w(n^*, n')$.
- If $g_{tent} < g(n')$: update $g(n') \leftarrow g_{tent}$, $f(n') \leftarrow g_{tent} + h(n')$, set parent, and push to the open queue.

**Step 4 — Termination:** The reconstructed parent chain from $q_g$ back to $q_s$ traces the optimal visibility-graph path.

**Step 5 — Complexity with binary heap:**

$$T_{A^*} = \mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Variants of the Visibility Graph

| Variant | Nodes Used | Edges Included | Complexity | Optimality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Classical (Full)** | All vertices | All mutually visible pairs | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ | Optimal |
| **Reduced Visibility Graph** | All vertices | Only "useful" edges (toward goal) | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Optimal |
| **Tangent Visibility Graph** | Start, goal, convex hull vertices | Edges through tangent points | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | Optimal for convex obstacles |
| **Shortest-Path Visibility Graph (SPVG)** | All vertices | Edges in any optimal path | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | Optimal |
| **Approximate / Sparse VG** | Subsampled vertices | $k$-nearest visible vertices | $\mathcal{O}(k n \log n)$ | Sub-optimal |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

Consider a simple workspace with a single square obstacle of side 2 units, centered at the origin, with $q_s = (-4, 0)$ and $q_g = (4, 0)$. The obstacle has vertices at $(\pm 1, \pm 1)$, i.e., four vertices $v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4$.

**Step 1:** Node set $V = \{q_s, q_g, v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\}$ with $\vert V \vert = 6$.

**Step 2:** Maximum possible edges: $\frac{6 \cdot 5}{2} = 15$.

**Step 3:** Visibility check — pairs that cross the square interior are eliminated. For example, $\overline{q_s, q_g}$ passes through the square interior and is **invalid**. The pairs $\overline{q_s, v_1}$ (where $v_1 = (-1, 1)$) do not cross the square interior and are **valid**.

**Step 4:** The optimal path is symmetric: $q_s \to (-1, 1) \to (1, 1) \to q_g$, with total length:

$$L^* = \sqrt{9+1} + \sqrt{0+4} + \sqrt{9+1} = \sqrt{10} + 2 + \sqrt{10} = 2\sqrt{10} + 2$$

$$L^* \approx 2(3.162) + 2 = 8.325 \text{ units}$$

This confirms the path *touches obstacle corners* — exactly as the Lozano-Pérez theorem predicts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace W with polygonal obstacles] --> B[Phase 1: Vertex Enumeration]
    B --> B1[Identify start q_s]
    B --> B2[Identify goal q_g]
    B --> B3[Collect all obstacle vertices V_obs]
    B1 --> C[Node Set V = q_s union q_g union V_obs]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Phase 2: Pairwise Visibility Test]
    D --> D1[For every pair u, v in V]
    D1 --> D2{Crosses obstacle interior?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[Discard edge]
    D2 -- No --> D4[Add edge uv to E]
    D3 --> E[Phase 3: Weight Assignment w equals Euclidean length]
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Phase 4: Graph Search A star or Dijkstra]
    F --> F1[Open priority queue initialized with q_s]
    F1 --> F2[Expand node with minimum f value]
    F2 --> F3{Goal reached?}
    F3 -- No --> F2
    F3 -- Yes --> G[Reconstruct optimal path]
    G --> H[Output: shortest collision free path]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Graph-Theoretic View

```mermaid
graph LR
    S((Start q_s)) --- V1((v1))
    S --- V2((v2))
    V1 --- V3((v3))
    V2 --- V3
    V3 --- V4((v4))
    V3 --- G((Goal q_g))
    V4 --- G
    V1 --- V4
    V2 --- G
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above schematic is a **functional architecture flowchart** mapping the construction pipeline and a generic graph topology. In a real KTU diagram, the polygon vertices would be labeled with their actual $(x, y)$ coordinates and obstacle edges would be drawn explicitly.

### 4.3 Conceptual Drawing Description (ASCII Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Start]:::startNode --- A[Vertex A]:::cornerNode
    S --- B[Vertex B]:::cornerNode
    A --- C[Vertex C]:::cornerNode
    B --- C
    C --- G[Goal]:::goalNode
    B --- G
    A --- G
    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef goalNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cornerNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.4 Information Flow of the Visibility Graph Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant W as Workspace
    participant E as Enumerator
    participant T as Visibility Tester
    participant B as Graph Builder
    participant S as A Star Searcher
    W->>E: Polygon vertices, start, goal
    E->>T: Candidate node pairs (u, v)
    T->>B: Valid edges with weights
    B->>S: Constructed graph G_vis
    S->>S: Search optimal path
    S-->>W: Return shortest path
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone IDs. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define a visibility graph in the context of mobile robot path planning. List its node set and edge predicate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A visibility graph is a graph-based representation of the free configuration space where nodes represent the start, goal, and obstacle vertices, and edges connect mutually visible nodes.

$$\text{Node set: } V = \{q_s, q_g\} \cup \bigcup_{k} V(O_k)$$

$$\text{Edge predicate: } E = \{(u, v) \mid \overline{uv} \cap O_i^{\circ} = \emptyset, \forall i\}$$

An edge exists iff the line of sight between $u$ and $v$ does not cross any obstacle interior. (3 marks: 1 mark for node set, 1 mark for edge predicate, 1 mark for visibility interpretation.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q2. State and briefly explain the Lozano-Pérez shortest-path theorem for polygonal environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Lozano-Pérez theorem states that in a polygonal environment, the shortest collision-free path between two points consists of straight-line segments whose endpoints are either the start, the goal, or vertices of the polygonal obstacles. (2 marks for the theorem statement, 1 mark for implication: the shortest path "touches corners," making discrete vertex-based search complete.) This is the theoretical foundation for the visibility graph method.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a visibility graph for a mobile robot navigating among polygonal obstacles. Clearly state the node set, edge predicate, and the algorithm steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2D workspace with two square obstacles of side 2 m centered at $(2, 2)$ and $(6, 2)$. The start is at $(0, 0)$ and the goal is at $(8, 0)$. Enumerate all visible node pairs and identify the shortest path using A*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Node set (1 mark):** $V = \{q_s, q_g\} \cup V_{obs}$ where $V_{obs}$ contains all obstacle vertices.
2. **Edge predicate (1 mark):** Two nodes $u, v$ are connected by an edge iff the line segment $\overline{uv}$ does not cross the interior of any obstacle.
3. **Algorithm steps (3 marks):**
   - **Phase 1:** Enumerate nodes (start, goal, all obstacle corners).
   - **Phase 2:** For each pair, perform a visibility test against all obstacle edges.
   - **Phase 3:** Assign Euclidean weights to valid edges.
   - **Phase 4:** Apply A* or Dijkstra to find the shortest path.
4. **Sketch (2 marks):** A diagram showing the workspace with obstacles, nodes marked as dots, and visible edges drawn as line segments.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Node enumeration (1 mark):**
- Start: $q_s = (0, 0)$
- Goal: $q_g = (8, 0)$
- Obstacle 1 vertices: $A = (1, 1), B = (3, 1), C = (3, 3), D = (1, 3)$
- Obstacle 2 vertices: $E = (5, 1), F = (7, 1), G = (7, 3), H = (5, 3)$
- Total nodes: $\vert V \vert = 10$

**Step 2 — Visibility test (3 marks):** Reject edges that cross obstacle interiors. Edges that graze obstacle boundaries (e.g., $\overline{B, E}$ along $y = 1$) are valid.

**Step 3 — A* expansion (2 marks):** Starting from $q_s$, expand nodes in order of $f = g + h$. The optimal path is found to be:

$$q_s \to B(3,1) \to E(5,1) \to q_g(8,0)$$

**Step 4 — Path length (1 mark):**

$$L^* = \sqrt{9+1} + \sqrt{4+0} + \sqrt{9+1} = \sqrt{10} + 2 + \sqrt{10} \approx 8.325 \text{ m}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **classical visibility graph** with the **reduced visibility graph** in terms of node set, edge count, construction complexity, and optimality guarantees. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the time complexity of (i) the naïve visibility graph construction, and (ii) A* search on a visibility graph with $n$ nodes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | Classical VG | Reduced VG | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Node set | Start, goal, all obstacle vertices | Same | 1 |
| Edge set | All mutually visible pairs | Only edges that can lie on a shortest path | 1 |
| Edge count | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ but with smaller constant | 1 |
| Construction | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ (Lee) | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ (Welzl) | 2 |
| Optimality | Guaranteed shortest path | Guaranteed shortest path | 1 |
| Practical use | Default in textbooks | Preferred in large maps | 1 |

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Naïve construction (4 marks):**

$$T_{naive} = \underbrace{\binom{n}{2}}_{\text{pairs}} \times \underbrace{n}_{\text{edge tests}} \times \underbrace{\mathcal{O}(1)}_{\text{per test}} = \mathcal{O}(n^3)$$

**(ii) A* on visibility graph (3 marks):**

With a binary heap priority queue:

$$T_{A^*} = \mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the start and goal as graph nodes** (–1 mark). The node set must explicitly include $q_s$ and $q_g$, not only obstacle vertices.
> 2. **Confusing visibility with reachability** (–2 marks). A line of sight may be unblocked but still pass *through* an obstacle — the predicate requires non-intersection with the *interior*.
> 3. **Reporting the complexity as $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for the naïve method** (–2 marks). The naïve method is $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$; $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ applies only to Lee's algorithm.
> 4. **Using $|$ inside table cells** (–1 mark per occurrence). Always use `\vert` in LaTeX to preserve table rendering.
> 5. **Stating A* complexity without the heap assumption** (–1 mark). Specify "with binary heap" to justify $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$.
> 6. **Drawing a physical sketch without labeling the nodes or edges** (–2 marks). Every diagram must have labeled nodes $q_s, q_g, v_1, \ldots$ and clear edge segments.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (high-priority):** Visibility graph $G_{vis} = (V, E)$ with $V = \{q_s, q_g\} \cup V_{obs}$ and $E$ defined by mutual visibility (no interior intersection).
- **Theoretical foundation:** Lozano-Pérez theorem guarantees that the optimal path touches obstacle vertices.
- **Construction phases:** Enumerate vertices → test pairwise visibility → assign Euclidean weights → graph search.
- **Edge count upper bound:** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$.
- **Naïve construction complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ — use Lee's algorithm for $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$.
- **A* search complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ with a binary heap, using Euclidean heuristic $h(n) = \vert n - q_g \vert$.
- **Optimality:** Visibility graph + A* yields the **shortest** collision-free path in polygonal environments.
- **Variants:** Classical, Reduced, Tangent, Shortest-Path VG, and Approximate/Sparse VG.
- **Limitation:** Visibility graphs are **not complete** in narrow passages unless vertices are sampled along corridor walls; also sensitive to numerical precision at near-grazing contact.
- **Engineering applications:** Warehouse AGVs, autonomous parking, game AI navigation, CAD tool paths.
- **LaTeX safeguard:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values inside markdown tables.
- **Mermaid safeguard:** Use alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters; avoid reserved keywords as standalone IDs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tangent Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tangent Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Tangent Graph (Tangent Bug)

In the context of **Mobile Robotics** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 – Robot Motion Planning and Roadmaps), the **Tangent Graph** is a *continuous roadmap* / *sensor-based motion planning algorithm* that connects a start configuration to a goal configuration by exploiting **external tangent lines** drawn to obstacles from the robot's current position and from the goal position.

More precisely, given a configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ populated with a finite set of convex (or piecewise-convex) obstacles $\{O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n\}$, the **Tangent Graph** $G = (V, E)$ is constructed as:

$$
V = \{q_{start}, q_{goal}\} \cup \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \{T_i^{start}\} \cup \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \{T_i^{goal}\}
$$

$$
E = \{ \text{open line segments joining tangent contact points that are mutually visible} \}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Tangent Graph is treated under the broader umbrella of *sensor-based / reactive navigation* algorithms and *roadmap-based path planning*. It is a foundational concept for understanding how a robot with **range sensors** (e.g., sonar, lidar, IR) can navigate unknown or partially known environments without an a-priori global map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Distinction:** The same family of ideas is sometimes called the **Tangent Bug Algorithm** when emphasizing the reactive, sensor-driven implementation. In KTU board papers, the term *"Tangent Graph"* is generally used to refer to the roadmap construction, while *"Tangent Bug"* refers to the on-line algorithm that *traverses* such a graph using only local sensor data.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Flashlight in a Dark Room" Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded inside an unfamiliar room**, holding a flashlight that can illuminate only a small circular patch on the floor around your feet. Somewhere in the room, on the far side, lies a **red ball** (the goal). You cannot see the entire room at once — you only know the shape of the obstacles *immediately around you* and where the goal is in the *global sense* (e.g., a compass direction).

How do you walk to the ball?

- You walk straight toward the ball. The moment your flashlight beam "catches" an obstacle (a piece of furniture), you **sweep your flashlight left and right along its edge**. The first and last rays that just *graze* (i.e., are **tangent to**) the obstacle's boundary tell you the **two endpoints** of the visible portion of the obstacle. These endpoints are the **tangent points**.
- You then make a *decision*: walk around the left side of the obstacle or the right side? You compare **how much closer each choice brings you to the red ball** (the "leave condition").
- You follow the boundary until you have *sensed a point that is closer to the goal than you have ever been on this obstacle*. At that moment, you **leave the obstacle** and resume walking straight toward the ball.

This flashlight-and-tangent-ray behavior is exactly the **Tangent Graph / Tangent Bug** paradigm.

---

## 1.3 Physical and Geometric Constants Used

- **Robot model:** A *disk-shaped (holonomic) point robot* of radius $r$ in 2-D workspace. The Configuration Space Obstacle is $C{O_i} = O_i \oplus B(r)$ (Minkowski sum of obstacle and robot).
- **Range sensor model:** Omnidirectional contact sensor with maximum range $R$ (often idealized as $\infty$ for analysis).
- **Standard metric:** Euclidean distance, denoted $d(\cdot, \cdot)$, with units in **meters (m)** or normalized to **map units**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a disk robot?** The KTU syllabus simplifies analysis by assuming a *circular/holonomic robot*. This removes orientation as a separate DOF and makes the notion of "tangent to an obstacle" geometrically clean. For a polygonal robot, one would use the *configuration space obstacle* $C{O_i}$ computed via Minkowski sum, and the tangent lines are then drawn to $C{O_i}$ instead of the raw workspace obstacle $O_i$.

---

## 1.4 Why is the Tangent Graph Important for Mobile Robots?

1. **Completeness** – Under idealized sensing, the Tangent Bug algorithm is **proven complete**: it always reaches the goal if it is reachable, or correctly declares failure.
2. **Local information only** – The robot does not need a global map, only a compass-to-goal and a range scan. This makes it ideal for **search-and-rescue, planetary rovers, and warehouse AGVs**.
3. **Guaranteed performance bound** – The path length is bounded by a constant factor of the true shortest path, giving *robustness in unknown environments*.
4. **Foundation** – Modern algorithms like **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)**, **VFH+**, and **Bug2+** build on the tangent / boundary-following paradigm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Construction of the Tangent Graph

Let the robot's current configuration be $x \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$ and the goal be $x_{goal}$. For each visible obstacle $O_i$ within sensing range, draw the **two external tangent lines** from the point $x$ to $O_i$. The points of tangency are denoted $t_i^{L}(x)$ (left) and $t_i^{R}(x)$ (right). The graph $G$ is then formed by:

1. The straight segment from $x$ to $x_{goal}$ (if it is unobstructed).
2. For each obstacle $O_i$, the two tangent segments from $x$ to $t_i^{L}$ and $t_i^{R}$.
3. The arc of the obstacle boundary between $t_i^{L}$ and $t_i^{R}$ that is *closer to $x_{goal}$*.
4. The same construction is repeated with $x$ replaced by $x_{goal}$, giving tangent points on every obstacle near the goal.

The **shortest path on $G$** from $x_{start}$ to $x_{goal}$ is the desired motion plan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The Tangent Graph is a *subset* of the **Visibility Graph** — it includes only those visibility edges that are *tangent to obstacles* from the start or goal, plus a portion of the obstacle boundary. This makes it dramatically smaller than a full visibility graph and **easier to maintain on-line** with a moving robot.

---

## 2.2 The Tangent Bug Algorithm – Two Behaviors

The algorithm has two operating modes:

### Behavior 1 — Motion-to-Goal
The robot moves along a straight line toward $x_{goal}$. It **continuously scans** for the *closest point on any detected obstacle*. As long as the line of sight to $x_{goal}$ is clear and the robot is making progress, it continues in this mode.

### Behavior 2 — Boundary-Following
When the straight-line motion is blocked or would be inefficient, the robot **follows the boundary** of the obstructing obstacle. While following, it maintains:

- The **minimum observed $d(p, x_{goal})$** along the followed boundary — this is denoted $d_{followed}(O_i)$.
- The **minimum theoretical $d(p, x_{goal})$** for any point $p$ on the *currently sensed* portion of the obstacle — denoted $d_{reach}(O_i)$.

> [!NOTE]
> The robot *cannot* sense the *entire* obstacle boundary at once (sensor range is finite). Therefore $d_{reach}$ is computed only over the locally visible portion, while $d_{followed}$ accumulates the global minimum as the robot circumnavigates the obstacle.

---

## 2.3 The Leave-Condition (Decision Rule)

The robot **leaves the boundary** of obstacle $O_i$ and switches back to *Motion-to-Goal* if and only if either of these holds:

$$
d_{reach}(x) \;<\; d_{followed}(x) \quad \text{or} \quad \text{the goal is locally reachable}
$$

Equivalently, the algorithm continually maintains the **leave-distance heuristic**:

$$
d_{leave}(x) \;=\; \min\Bigl( d_{reach}(x),\ d_{followed}(x) \Bigr) \;-\; d(x,\ x_{goal})
$$
