topics.title: Breadth-First Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Breadth-First Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Breadth-First Search (BFS)** is a uniform-cost, uninformed graph traversal algorithm that systematically explores a graph or grid by expanding all nodes (states) at the present depth level before proceeding to nodes at the next depth level. In the context of **Mobile Robotics**, BFS is one of the foundational discrete search algorithms used for **path planning** in **occupancy grid maps**, **configuration space (C-space)** discretization, and **topological map** traversal.

Formally, given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with a start node $s \in V$ and a goal node $g \in V$, BFS computes the shortest path (in terms of number of edges/steps) from $s$ to $g$ when all edges have equal weight (typically **unit cost = 1**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Search Algorithms):**
> Breadth-First Search is a **Level-by-Level / FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** exploration strategy. It guarantees **completeness** (finds a solution if one exists) and **optimality** (shortest path in unweighted graphs) — two properties heavily tested in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ripple in a Pond"

Imagine dropping a stone into a perfectly still pond. The ripple does not shoot directly across to the other side; instead, it expands outward as a perfect circle, touching every point at distance 1 meter first, then every point at distance 2 meters, and so on. BFS behaves identically:

- The **stone** = the robot's start position.
- The **pond surface** = the robot's free configuration space (mapped grid).
- Each **ripple ring** = one depth layer of exploration.
- The **first ripple to reach the goal** = the shortest path (fewest moves).

A robot never "jumps ahead" — it must thoroughly inspect every reachable neighboring cell at the current distance ring before venturing further. This guarantees the **first time** the goal is dequeued, it is via the **shortest possible path** in terms of moves/steps.

## 1.3 Core Data Structures Used

> [!NOTE]
> BFS fundamentally relies on **two synchronized data structures**:

| Structure | Type | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Frontier (Open List)** | **Queue** (FIFO) | Holds nodes discovered but not yet expanded. |
| **Explored (Closed List)** | Set / Boolean Grid | Holds nodes already expanded (visited). |
| **Parent Map** | Dictionary / 2D Array | Stores each node's predecessor for path reconstruction. |

## 1.4 Key Properties (Board-Exam Favorites)

1. **Complete?** ✅ Yes — if a path exists in a finite graph, BFS will find it.
2. **Optimal?** ✅ Yes — but **only for unweighted graphs** (or uniform edge costs).
3. **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — linear in graph size.
4. **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ — must store the frontier and explored set.
5. **Branching Factor:** Denoted as $b$. Memory grows as $O(b^d)$ where $d$ is the depth of the shallowest goal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanics

BFS operates on a strict **FIFO queue discipline**. The algorithm proceeds as follows for a mobile robot traversing a 2D occupancy grid:

1. **Initialization:** Enqueue the start node $s$ into the **FIFO queue** $Q$. Mark $s$ as visited. Set $\text{parent}[s] = \text{NULL}$ and $\text{g-cost}[s] = 0$.
2. **Termination Check:** If $Q$ is empty → return **FAILURE** (goal unreachable).
3. **Dequeue:** Pop the **front** node $u$ from $Q$ (oldest entry — this is the FIFO property).
4. **Goal Test:** If $u = g$ → reconstruct path via `parent` pointers and return **SUCCESS**.
5. **Expansion (Successor Generation):** For every valid neighbor $v$ of $u$ (in 4-connected or 8-connected grids):
    - If $v$ is **not visited** AND $v$ is **not an obstacle**:
        - Mark $v$ as visited.
        - Set $\text{parent}[v] = u$.
        - Set $\text{g-cost}[v] = \text{g-cost}[u] + 1$.
        - Enqueue $v$ at the **rear** of $Q$.
6. **Loop** back to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does BFS guarantee the shortest path?**
> Because the queue enforces that nodes are processed in **non-decreasing order of path cost**. The first time the goal is removed from $Q$, every other path to it in the queue is **at least as long**, since it was discovered at the same or a later depth layer.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table for direct lookup during KTU University Examination (ESE) preparation.

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Graph representation | $G = (V, E)$ | $V$ = nodes, $E$ = edges |
| Edge cost (BFS) | $c(u, v) = 1$ | Uniform, dimensionless |
| Path cost to node $n$ | $g(n) = g(\text{parent}) + c$ | Number of edges traversed |
| Time complexity | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | BFS visits every node once |
| Space complexity | $S(n) = O(\vert V \vert)$ | Worst case: queue holds $\vert V \vert$ nodes |
| Memory growth (tree) | $M = O(b^d)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = solution depth |
| Branching factor (4-conn.) | $b = 4$ | Cardinal moves: N, S, E, W |
| Branching factor (8-conn.) | $b = 8$ | Adds diagonals: NE, NW, SE, SW |
| Number of expanded nodes | $N_{\text{exp}} = 1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^d$ | Geometric series sum |
| Optimal path length | $L^{*} = d_{\min}$ | Shortest in unweighted grid |

## 2.3 Closed-Form Solution for Expanded Nodes

When BFS expands a grid uniformly with branching factor $b$ and reaches the goal at depth $d$, the total number of nodes expanded is given by the geometric series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{exp}} &= 1 + b + b^{2} + b^{3} + \dots + b^{d} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{exp}} &= \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^{k} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} \quad \text{for } b > 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 4-connected grid: $b = 4$, so $N_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{4^{d+1} - 1}{3}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Mobile Robotics

| Application Domain | Role of BFS |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** | Finds shortest pick-route in known floor grid. |
| **Autonomous Vacuum Cleaners** | Covers rooms via systematic frontier expansion. |
| **Disaster Rescue Robots** | Plans shortest evacuation paths in collapsed-building grids. |
| **Multi-Robot Coordination** | Computes minimal collision-free reassignment routes. |
| **ROS Navigation Stack** | Acts as a global planner baseline for `move_base`. |
| **Voronoi Roadmap Search** | Traverses topological skeleton of free space. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** BFS assumes a **known, static environment**. In real-world dynamic settings, **D***, **D\*-Lite**, or **A\*** (with a heuristic) are preferred — a frequent KTU follow-up question in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why BFS Produces the Optimal Path

We derive the **optimality guarantee** of BFS formally so that you can reproduce it in the KTU exam for full marks.

**Theorem:** *If a goal node $g$ is reachable from start node $s$ in an unweighted graph, BFS finds a path of minimum length.*

**Proof:**

Let $d(v)$ denote the true shortest distance (number of edges) from $s$ to any node $v$ in the graph.

**Step 1 — Base Case:**
At initialization, only $s$ is in the queue. $d(s) = 0$, and $s$ is dequeued first. The claim holds trivially.

**Step 2 — Inductive Hypothesis:**
Assume that when a node $u$ is dequeued, $g\text{-cost}[u] = d(u)$ (i.e., the path recorded is the shortest).

**Step 3 — Inductive Step:**
When $u$ is expanded, each unvisited neighbor $v$ is assigned:
$$
g\text{-cost}[v] = g\text{-cost}[u] + 1 = d(u) + 1
$$
Since $v$ is adjacent to $u$, the shortest path to $v$ must satisfy $d(v) \le d(u) + 1$. Also, because BFS marks $v$ as visited the first time it is discovered, and BFS explores nodes layer-by-layer, $v$ cannot later be reached via a shorter path. Hence:
$$
d(v) = d(u) + 1 = g\text{-cost}[v]
$$

**Step 4 — Queue Invariant:**
By induction, nodes enter the queue in **non-decreasing** order of $g$-cost. Therefore the first time $g$ is dequeued, $g\text{-cost}[g] = d(g)$, the global minimum. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — BFS Trace on a 5×5 Mobile Robot Grid

Consider a robot in a 5×5 grid where **S** = start (0,0), **G** = goal (4,4), **#** = obstacle. Trajectory steps enumerated with each BFS frontier layer.

**Initial Grid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grid} &=
\begin{bmatrix}
S & . & . & . & \# \\
. & \# & . & . & . \\
. & . & . & \# & . \\
. & \# & \# & . & . \\
. & . & . & . & G
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> `.` = free cell, `#` = obstacle, 4-connected motion assumed.

### BFS Expansion Trace Table

| Step | Dequeue $u$ | Enqueued Neighbors | Frontier $Q$ (front → rear) | Goal Found? |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $(0,0)$ S | $(1,0)$, $(0,1)$ | $(1,0), (0,1)$ | No |
| 2 | $(1,0)$ | $(2,0)$, $(1,1)$ is # | $(0,1), (2,0)$ | No |
| 3 | $(0,1)$ | $(0,2)$ | $(2,0), (0,2)$ | No |
| 4 | $(2,0)$ | $(3,0)$, $(2,1)$ | $(0,2), (3,0), (2,1)$ | No |
| 5 | $(0,2)$ | $(0,3)$, $(1,2)$ | $(3,0), (2,1), (0,3), (1,2)$ | No |
| 6 | $(3,0)$ | $(4,0)$ | $(2,1), (0,3), (1,2), (4,0)$ | No |
| 7 | $(2,1)$ | $(2,2)$, $(3,1)$ is # | $(0,3), (1,2), (4,0), (2,2), (3,1)$ | No |
| 8 | $(0,3)$ | $(0,4)$ | $(1,2), (4,0), (2,2), (3,1), (0,4)$ | No |
| 9 | $(1,2)$ | $(1,3)$ | $(4,0), (2,2), (3,1), (0,4), (1,3)$ | No |
| 10 | $(4,0)$ | $(4,1)$ | $(2,2), (3,1), (0,4), (1,3), (4,1)$ | No |
| 11 | $(2,2)$ | — (neighbors visited/obstacle) | $(3,1), (0,4), (1,3), (4,1)$ | No |
| 12 | $(3,1)$ | — | $(0,4), (1,3), (4,1)$ | No |
| 13 | $(0,4)$ | — | $(1,3), (4,1)$ | No |
| 14 | $(1,3)$ | — | $(4,1)$ | No |
| 15 | $(4,1)$ | $(4,2)$, $(3,1)$ visited | $(4,2)$ | No |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| 25 | $(4,4)$ G | — | — | **YES ✅** |

### Path Reconstruction via `parent` Pointers

Backtracking from goal gives the shortest path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path} &= (0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (2,1) \to (2,2) \\
&\quad \to (3,2)\text{(blocked, alternative)} \\
&\quad \to (4,0) \to (4,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to (4,4)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Path length:** $L = 8$ steps (optimal in 4-connected grid).

## 3.3 Complexity Derivation for an $N \times N$ Grid

For an $N \times N$ mobile robot grid (assuming full connectivity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert V \vert &= N^{2} \\
\vert E \vert &\le 4N^{2} \quad \text{(each cell has ≤ 4 neighbors)} \\
T(n) &= O(N^{2} + 4N^{2}) = O(N^{2}) \\
S(n) &= O(N^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

For an 8-connected grid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert E \vert &\le 8N^{2} \\
T(n) &= O(N^{2}) \quad \text{(still linear in grid size)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms BFS scales **quadratically with grid resolution** — a major drawback for high-resolution maps, motivating the use of heuristic search (A\*) in real robotic deployments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BFS Algorithm — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **complete functional flow** of the BFS algorithm as deployed in a mobile robot's path planner. All node IDs use the **alphanumeric-prefix rule** to avoid Mermaid parser conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Robot Power-On] --> B0[Load Occupancy Grid Map]
    B0 --> C0[Initialize FIFO Queue Q]
    C0 --> D0[Enqueue Start Node s]
    D0 --> E0[Mark s as Visited]
    E0 --> F0{Is Queue Q Empty?}
    F0 -- Yes --> G0[Return FAILURE: Goal Unreachable]
    F0 -- No --> H0[Dequeue Front Node u]
    H0 --> I0{Is u equal to Goal g?}
    I0 -- Yes --> J0[Reconstruct Path via Parent Map]
    J0 --> K0[Return SUCCESS and Path]
    I0 -- No --> L0[Generate Successor Neighbors of u]
    L0 --> M0{For Each Neighbor v}
    M0 --> N0{Is v Free AND Unvisited?}
    N0 -- No --> M0
    N0 -- Yes --> O0[Mark v as Visited]
    O0 --> P0[Set parent of v equal to u]
    P0 --> Q0[Set g-cost of v equal to g-cost of u plus 1]
    Q0 --> R0[Enqueue v at Rear of Q]
    R0 --> M0
    M0 -- All neighbors processed --> F0
```

## 4.2 BFS Search Tree — Layer-Wise Expansion (Mobile Robot Grid)

This diagram shows how BFS expands the search tree **layer by layer** (ripple-by-ripple) from the robot's start position. Each ring represents one depth layer $d = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0((Start s, depth 0)) --> L1a((depth 1))
    L0 --> L1b((depth 1))
    L0 --> L1c((depth 1))
    L0 --> L1d((depth 1))
    L1a --> L2a((depth 2))
    L1a --> L2b((depth 2))
    L1b --> L2c((depth 2))
    L1b --> L2d((depth 2))
    L1c --> L2e((depth 2))
    L1c --> L2f((depth 2))
    L1d --> L2g((depth 2))
    L1d --> L2h((depth 2))
    L2a --> L3a(((depth 3)))
    L2b --> L3b(((depth 3)))
    L2c --> L3c(((depth 3)))
    L2e --> L3d(((depth 3)))
    L3a --> GoalNode(((Goal G reached)))
    L3d --> GoalNode
    GoalNode --> Terminate[TERMINATE BFS]
```

## 4.3 Robot Workspace — BFS Frontier Wavefront Visualization

This block diagram maps the **state-machine interaction** between the robot's perception module, the BFS planner, and the actuation layer — the canonical pipeline in any **ROS-based mobile robot**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PerceptionLayer
        sensor1[Lidar Sensor]
        sensor2[Camera Module]
        mapBuilder[Occupancy Grid Map Builder]
    end
    subgraph PlanningLayer
        bfsCore[BFS Path Planner]
        queue1[FIFO Frontier Queue]
        visited1[Visited Set]
        parentMap[Parent Pointer Map]
    end
    subgraph ActuationLayer
        controller[Mobile Robot Controller]
        motors[Drive Motors]
        wheels[Wheel Encoders]
    end
    sensor1 --> mapBuilder
    sensor2 --> mapBuilder
    mapBuilder --> bfsCore
    bfsCore --> queue1
    bfsCore --> visited1
    bfsCore --> parentMap
    bfsCore --> controller
    controller --> motors
    motors --> wheels
    wheels --> controller
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently test whether students can relate BFS to the **perception–planning–action** loop of mobile robots. Be ready to draw or describe this pipeline in 2-mark short notes.

## 4.4 BFS vs. Other Search Algorithms — Comparative Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartAlg[Graph Search Algorithm Family] --> BlindSearch[Uninformed Blind Search]
    StartAlg --> HeuristicSearch[Informed Heuristic Search]
    BlindSearch --> BFSAlgo[BFS: FIFO Queue, Uniform Cost]
    BlindSearch --> DFSAlgo[DFS: LIFO Stack, Deepest First]
    BlindSearch --> UCSAlgo[Uniform Cost: Priority Queue]
    HeuristicSearch --> AStarAlgo[A*: g-cost plus h-cost]
    HeuristicSearch --> GreedyAlgo[Greedy Best-First: h-cost only]
    BFSAlgo --> CompleteYes[Complete: YES]
    BFSAlgo --> OptimalYes[Optimal: YES for unit costs]
    AStarAlgo --> OptimalYes2[Optimal: YES with admissible heuristic]
    DFSAlgo --> CompleteNo[Complete: NO in infinite graphs]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
Define **Breadth-First Search (BFS)**. List any **two properties** of BFS with respect to mobile robot path planning.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition:** BFS is an uninformed graph search algorithm that explores a robot's configuration space level by level, expanding all cells at the current depth before moving deeper. It uses a **FIFO queue** to manage the frontier.
>
> **Property 1 — Completeness:** BFS is *complete*. It will always find a path from the robot's start cell to the goal cell if one exists in a finite grid, because it never abandons unexpanded frontier nodes.
>
> **Property 2 — Optimality:** BFS is *optimal* in graphs with uniform edge weights (e.g., grids where each move costs 1). The first time the goal is dequeued, the path recorded has the minimum number of steps.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** and **Depth-First Search (DFS)** based on **data structure used** and **optimality**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | BFS | DFS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Structure** | FIFO **Queue** | LIFO **Stack** (or recursion) |
| **Optimality** | Optimal for unit-cost graphs | Not optimal; may find a long path first |
| **Space** | $O(\vert V \vert)$ — large frontier | $O(d)$ — only current path stored |
| **Use in Robotics** | Shortest-path grid planning | Maze exploration, topological sorting |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse BFS optimality conditions — BFS is optimal **only for uniform-cost graphs**. Writing "BFS is always optimal" costs 2 marks.
> - **Always draw** the data-structure (queue) snapshot at each step in trace questions — partial traces without state are penalized.
> - **Specify** whether the grid is 4-connected or 8-connected — branch factor changes the complexity.
> - Show **parent-pointer backtracking** explicitly when reconstructing the final path.

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Breadth-First Search algorithm** as applied to mobile robot path planning on a 2D occupancy grid. State its **time and space complexity**, and discuss its **completeness** and **optimality** properties. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot operates in a **6×6 grid**. The start cell **S** is at $(0,0)$ and the goal **G** is at $(5,5)$. Obstacles are located at: $(1,2), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2), (4,4), (4,1)$. Assuming **4-connected motion** (N, S, E, W), apply **BFS** to find the **shortest path**. Show the **frontier queue** at each step and the final **reconstructed path** with its length. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Algorithm Definition (2 Marks):**
BFS is a level-by-level graph traversal algorithm. For a mobile robot, the grid cells are treated as graph nodes; free cells (non-obstacles) form the navigable space. BFS uses a **FIFO queue** to maintain the frontier and a **visited set** to avoid revisits.

**2. Algorithmic Steps (2 Marks):**
- Enqueue start $s$; mark visited.
- While queue non-empty: dequeue $u$, test if $u = g$.
- If not goal, expand $u$: enqueue all free, unvisited neighbors of $u$.
- Record each neighbor's parent for backtracking.

**3. Complexity (2 Marks):**
For a grid of size $N \times N$:
- $\vert V \vert = N^2$, $\vert E \vert \le 4N^2$
- Time: $O(N^2)$
- Space: $O(N^2)$

**4. Completeness & Optimality (1 Mark):**
- **Complete** ✅ — finite graph, exhaustive expansion.
- **Optimal** ✅ — uniform edge costs guarantee first-found goal is shortest.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Grid Setup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grid} &=
\begin{bmatrix}
S & . & . & . & . & . \\
. & . & \# & . & . & . \\
. & \# & . & \# & . & . \\
. & . & \# & . & . & . \\
. & \# & . & . & \# & . \\
. & . & . & . & . & G
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**BFS Trace:**

| Step | Dequeue $u$ | Enqueued Neighbors | Queue $Q$ |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $(0,0)$ | $(1,0), (0,1)$ | $(1,0), (0,1)$ |
| 2 | $(1,0)$ | $(2,0)$ | $(0,1), (2,0)$ |
| 3 | $(0,1)$ | $(0,2)$ | $(2,0), (0,2)$ |
| 4 | $(2,0)$ | $(3,0)$ | $(0,2), (3,0)$ |
| 5 | $(0,2)$ | $(1,2)$ blocked, $(0,3)$ | $(3,0), (0,3)$ |
| 6 | $(3,0)$ | $(4,0)$ | $(0,3), (4,0)$ |
| 7 | $(0,3)$ | $(0,4)$ | $(4,0), (0,4)$ |
| 8 | $(4,0)$ | $(4,1)$ blocked | $(0,4)$ |
| 9 | $(0,4)$ | $(0,5)$ | $(0,5)$ |
| 10 | $(0,5)$ | $(1,5)$ | $(1,5)$ |
| 11 | $(1,5)$ | $(2,5)$ | $(2,5)$ |
| 12 | $(2,5)$ | $(3,5)$ | $(3,5)$ |
| 13 | $(3,5)$ | $(4,5)$ | $(4,5)$ |
| 14 | $(4,5)$ | $(5,5)$ Goal! | $(5,5)$ ✅ |
| 15 | $(5,5)$ | — | **GOAL FOUND** |

**Path Reconstruction (backtrack via `parent` pointers):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(5,5) \leftarrow (4,5) \leftarrow (3,5) \leftarrow (2,5) \leftarrow (1,5) \\
\leftarrow (0,5) \leftarrow (0,4) \leftarrow (0,3) \leftarrow (0,2) \\
\leftarrow (0,1) \leftarrow (0,0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Path:**
$$
S \to (0,1) \to (0,2) \to (0,3) \to (0,4) \to (0,5) \to (1,5) \to (2,5) \to (3,5) \to (4,5) \to G
$$

**Path Length:** $L = 10$ steps. ✅ (Optimal in 4-connected grid.)

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **concept of configuration space (C-space)** and how BFS operates over a discretized C-space for a **circular mobile robot**. Mention the role of the **Minkowski sum** in obstacle inflation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **5×5 grid** with start $S = (0,0)$, goal $G = (4,4)$, and obstacles at $(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)$, compute the **total number of nodes expanded by BFS** using **4-connected motion**, assuming the goal is at depth $d$. Also compute the **memory requirement** in terms of branching factor $b = 4$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Configuration Space Definition (2 Marks):**
The **Configuration Space (C-space)** is the set of all possible configurations (poses) a robot can attain. For a **2D mobile robot** with position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$, the C-space is $\mathbb{R}^2 \times [0, 2\pi)$. For a point robot in a 2D plane, C-space $\equiv$ workspace.

**2. Discretization (2 Marks):**
The continuous C-space is discretized into a uniform grid of resolution $\Delta$. Each grid cell becomes a node in the search graph. Free cells $\in C_{\text{free}}$, obstacle cells $\in C_{\text{obs}}$.

**3. Obstacle Inflation via Minkowski Sum (2 Marks):**
For a circular robot of radius $r$, obstacles must be **inflated** by $r$ so the robot is treated as a point. Mathematically:

$$
C_{\text{obs,robot}} = C_{\text{obs}} \oplus R(-r)
$$

where $R(-r)$ is the robot reflected about its origin, and $\oplus$ denotes the **Minkowski sum**. This ensures the robot's center never enters the inflated obstacle region.

**4. BFS over Discretized C-space (1 Mark):**
BFS then expands nodes (grid cells) in $C_{\text{free}}$ layer by layer until the goal cell is reached, producing the shortest collision-free path.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Grid: $5 \times 5$, $S = (0,0)$, $G = (4,4)$.
- Obstacles: $(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)$.
- 4-connected motion → $b = 4$.

**Step 1 — Determine Depth $d$:**
The shortest 4-connected path from $(0,0)$ to $(4,4)$ has length $d = 8$ (Manhattan distance):

$$
d = \vert 4 - 0 \vert + \vert 4 - 0 \vert = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Number of Expanded Nodes (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{exp}} &= \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^{k} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{exp}} &= \frac{4^{9} - 1}{4 - 1} = \frac{262144 - 1}{3} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{exp}} &= \frac{262143}{3} = 87381 \text{ nodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: In practice, obstacles prune the tree, so actual expansion is less. This is the theoretical worst-case.)*

**Step 3 — Compute Memory Requirement (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= O(b^{d}) \quad \text{(frontier size at goal depth)} \\[4pt]
M &= O(4^{8}) = O(65536) \text{ nodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

In a $5 \times 5$ grid there are only $25$ cells; hence the **actual** memory needed is $O(25)$. The $O(b^d)$ expression represents the **worst-case theoretical bound** for a sparse infinite grid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** For small grids, BFS is computationally cheap and optimal. For large real-world maps (e.g., $1000 \times 1000$), BFS's $O(b^d)$ memory growth becomes prohibitive — motivating heuristic methods like **A\***.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute KTU exam revision sheet.**

- ✅ **BFS** = Level-by-level, FIFO queue-based, **uninformed** graph search.
- ✅ Primary data structures: **FIFO Queue** (frontier), **Visited Set** (closed), **Parent Map** (backtracking).
- ✅ **Completeness:** Guaranteed ✅ (in finite graphs).
- ✅ **Optimality:** Guaranteed ✅ **only** for uniform / unit edge costs.
- ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — linear.
- ✅ **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ — bottleneck for large grids.
- ✅ Branching factor: $b = 4$ (4-connected), $b = 8$ (8-connected).
- ✅ Geometric expansion: $N_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$.
- ✅ BFS is the **theoretical foundation** for shortest-path planning in mobile robotics.
- ✅ Real-world limitation: **poor scaling** → use **A\***, **D\***, **D\*-Lite** for large dynamic environments.
- ✅ Application: warehouse AMRs, ROS `move_base` global planner, occupancy grid navigation.
- ✅ **Key exam trick:** Always trace the **queue snapshot** at every step — examiners award marks for visible state transitions.
- ✅ **Key exam trick:** Specify connectivity (4 vs. 8) and **inflate obstacles** by robot radius when applying BFS to real robots.
- ✅ BFS optimality **fails** if edge weights vary — switch to **Uniform Cost Search (UCS)** or **Dijkstra's algorithm**.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a classical **greedy, graph-based search algorithm** used to find the **shortest-path tree** from a designated **source node** to every other reachable node in a weighted graph whose edge weights are **non-negative**.

In the context of **mobile robotics**, Dijkstra's algorithm is widely deployed as a **global path planner** in known, static environments where the workspace has been discretized into a graph or occupancy grid. The robot's configuration space is reduced to nodes (waypoints, grid cells, or roadmap vertices), and edge weights represent traversal cost (Euclidean distance, energy, time, or risk).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBRAT604 - Module 3):**
> Dijkstra's algorithm is grouped under *"Classical Path Planning"* alongside A*, D*, and visibility graphs. It must be studied with reference to **graph discretization**, **completeness**, **optimality**, and **computational complexity**.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights $w(u,v) \geq 0$, and a source node $s \in V$, Dijkstra's algorithm computes for every vertex $v \in V$:

$$d(s, v) = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s \to v}} \sum_{e \in P} w(e)$$

where $\mathcal{P}_{s \to v}$ is the set of all paths from $s$ to $v$, and $d(s,v)$ is the shortest-path distance.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: The "Spreading Water" or "GPS Navigation" model.**
>
> Imagine you drop a single drop of ink at the source city on a map. The ink begins to spread outward through all roads. Roads have lengths. At every instant, the ink expands fastest along the **shortest known route to the next unvisited city**. Once a city has been "touched" by the ink at the **minimum possible distance**, it is permanently marked. The ink never revisits a marked city because any alternative path would necessarily be longer (since weights are non-negative).
>
> The *order* in which cities get marked is the **priority of exploration**, and the *distance* recorded at the moment of marking is the **shortest-path distance** from the source.

In robotics:
- **Nodes** = discrete robot poses (grid cells, waypoints, Voronoi vertices).
- **Edges** = admissible motions between poses with associated cost.
- **Source** = current robot pose $q_{start}$.
- **Goal** = target pose $q_{goal}$.

The algorithm therefore behaves like an **omniscient navigator** that pre-computes the optimal roadmap before the robot executes a single motor command.

---

## 1.3 Key Properties (For Board Examinations)

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Strategy Class** | Greedy (single-source shortest path) |
| **Optimality** | Guaranteed for **non-negative** weights |
| **Completeness** | Complete in finite graphs |
| **Time Complexity (Array)** | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ |
| **Time Complexity (Binary Heap)** | $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ |
| **Time Complexity (Fibonacci Heap)** | $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| **Edge Weight Restriction** | $w(u,v) \geq 0$ (cannot handle negative cycles) |
| **Typical Robotic Use** | Global path planning on **known static maps** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Decomposition

Dijkstra's algorithm maintains **three core data structures** during execution:

1. **Distance Array** $d[v]$ — current best known shortest distance from source $s$ to node $v$.
2. **Predecessor Array** $\pi[v]$ — stores the parent node on the tentative shortest path (used for path reconstruction).
3. **Priority Queue (Min-Heap)** $Q$ — orders all unvisited nodes by their current distance $d[v]$ in ascending order.
4. **Visited Set** $S$ — the set of nodes whose final shortest distance has been confirmed.

### Operational Logic Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind every step:**

- **Initialization** — Set the source's distance to **0** (it costs nothing to reach itself from itself) and all others to **$\infty$** (unknown/unbounded). Without this, the algorithm cannot bootstrap.

- **Relaxation** — When an edge $(u, v)$ is examined, we check whether going through $u$ offers a shorter route to $v$ than what we currently know. If yes, update $d[v]$ and $\pi[v]$. This is the *greedy improvement* step.

- **Greedy Selection** — At every iteration, extract the unvisited node with the **minimum** $d[v]$. Because weights are non-negative, once a node is extracted from $Q$, its distance **cannot be improved further**, so we permanently commit it to $S$.

- **Termination** — The algorithm halts when $Q$ becomes empty (all reachable nodes finalized) OR when the target node $q_{goal}$ is added to $S$ (early-exit optimization used in robotics).

### The Relaxation Operation

For a directed edge $(u,v)$ with weight $w(u,v)$, the relaxation step is:

$$d[v] \leftarrow \min\Big(d[v],\; d[u] + w(u,v)\Big)$$

And if $d[u] + w(u,v) < d[v]$, we additionally update:

$$\pi[v] \leftarrow u$$

This single equation is the **heart** of every shortest-path algorithm in graph theory. It must be memorized verbatim for the KTU board.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Exam Recall:** *"DIJK = **D**istance **I**nit **J**ust **K**eep going"* — Initialize $\infty$, Just Keep relaxing until Q is empty.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Dijkstra's algorithm is the **algorithmic backbone** of:

- **GPS navigation systems** (Google Maps, OSRM routing engines).
- **Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)** — Interior Gateway Protocol in IP networks.
- **Robot Operating System (ROS) `navfn` global planner** — uses Dijkstra over a costmap grid.
- **Traffic-aware urban mobility** and **logistics dispatch**.
- **VLSI wire routing** in chip design.
- **Roadmap-based motion planning** in autonomous vehicles (combined with RRT for hybrid planning).

In **mobile robotics**, Dijkstra is preferred over A* when:
1. No informative heuristic is available.
2. The cost function is irregular (e.g., energy or risk) and Euclidean distance is misleading.
3. The **entire shortest-path tree** is required, not just a single goal.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Full Hand-Traced Iteration

Consider the following weighted undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ representing a robot's discretized workspace with **5 nodes** $V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and **7 edges**:

| Edge | Weight |
|------|--------|
| $A \leftrightarrow B$ | **4** |
| $A \leftrightarrow C$ | **2** |
| $B \leftrightarrow C$ | **1** |
| $B \leftrightarrow D$ | **5** |
| $C \leftrightarrow D$ | **8** |
| $C \leftrightarrow E$ | **10** |
| $D \leftrightarrow E$ | **2** |

**Source node:** $A$  **Target node:** $E$

> [!NOTE]
> All weights are non-negative integers (in meters of traversal cost). The graph is undirected, so each edge contributes two directed arcs.

---

### Initialization (Iteration 0)

$$d[A] = 0, \quad d[B] = d[C] = d[D] = d[E] = \infty$$
$$S = \emptyset, \quad Q = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$$

The source node has zero self-distance by definition. All others are unreachable, hence $\infty$.

---

### Iteration 1 — Extract Minimum from Q

**Extract:** $u = A$ (since $d[A] = 0$ is minimum).

$$S = \{A\}$$

**Relax all edges emanating from $A$:**

- Edge $(A, B)$ with $w = 4$:

$$d[B] \leftarrow \min\big(\infty,\; 0 + 4\big) = 4, \quad \pi[B] \leftarrow A$$

- Edge $(A, C)$ with $w = 2$:

$$d[C] \leftarrow \min\big(\infty,\; 0 + 2\big) = 2, \quad \pi[C] \leftarrow A$$

State after Iteration 1:

| Node | $d$ | $\pi$ |
|------|-----|-------|
| A | **0** | — |
| B | 4 | A |
| C | **2** | A |
| D | $\infty$ | — |
| E | $\infty$ | — |

---

### Iteration 2 — Extract Minimum from Q

**Extract:** $u = C$ (since $d[C] = 2$ is the new minimum).

$$S = \{A, C\}$$

**Relax all edges emanating from $C$:**

- Edge $(C, A)$ with $w = 2$: $d[A] + w = 0 + 2 = 2$. But $A \in S$, so skip (already finalized).
- Edge $(C, B)$ with $w = 1$:

$$d[B] \leftarrow \min\big(4,\; 2 + 1\big) = 3, \quad \pi[B] \leftarrow C$$

(The old route $A \to B$ cost 4; the new route $A \to C \to B$ costs 3. Improvement!)

- Edge $(C, D)$ with $w = 8$:

$$d[D] \leftarrow \min\big(\infty,\; 2 + 8\big) = 10, \quad \pi[D] \leftarrow C$$

- Edge $(C, E)$ with $w = 10$:

$$d[E] \leftarrow \min\big(\infty,\; 2 + 10\big) = 12, \quad \pi[E] \leftarrow C$$

State after Iteration 2:

| Node | $d$ | $\pi$ |
|------|-----|-------|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | **3** | C |
| C | 2 | A |
| D | 10 | C |
| E | 12 | C |

---

### Iteration 3 — Extract Minimum from Q

**Extract:** $u = B$ (since $d[B] = 3$).

$$S = \{A, C, B\}$$

**Relax all edges emanating from $B$:**

- Edge $(B, D)$ with $w = 5$:

$$d[D] \leftarrow \min\big(10,\; 3 + 5\big) = 8, \quad \pi[D] \leftarrow B$$

(New path $A \to C \to B \to D$ costs 8 vs old 10. Improvement!)

- Edge $(B, A)$: $A \in S$, skip.
- Edge $(B, C)$: $C \in S$, skip.

State after Iteration 3:

| Node | $d$ | $\pi$ |
|------|-----|-------|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | 3 | C |
| C | 2 | A |
| D | **8** | B |
| E | 12 | C |

---

### Iteration 4 — Extract Minimum from Q

**Extract:** $u = D$ (since $d[D] = 8$).

$$S = \{A, C, B, D\}$$

**Relax all edges emanating from $D$:**

- Edge $(D, E)$ with $w = 2$:

$$d[E] \leftarrow \min\big(12,\; 8 + 2\big) = 10, \quad \pi[E] \leftarrow D$$

(New path $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$ costs 10 vs old 12. Improvement!)

- Other neighbours already in $S$, skip.

State after Iteration 4:

| Node | $d$ | $\pi$ |
|------|-----|-------|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | 3 | C |
| C | 2 | A |
| D | 8 | B |
| E | **10** | D |

---

### Iteration 5 — Goal Reached (Early Termination)

**Extract:** $u = E$ (since $d[E] = 10$). $E = q_{goal}$, so we may **terminate** if only the goal distance is required.

$$S = \{A, C, B, D, E\}$$

---

## 3.2 Path Reconstruction Using $\pi$

To reconstruct the path $A \to E$, backtrack from $E$ via $\pi$:

$$\pi[E] = D, \quad \pi[D] = B, \quad \pi[B] = C, \quad \pi[C] = A$$

Reversed path: $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$.

**Verification by direct summation:**

$$w(A,C) + w(C,B) + w(B,D) + w(D,E) = 2 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 10 \;\; \checkmark$$

This matches $d[E] = 10$ from the algorithm.

---

## 3.3 Closed-Form Bound on Iterations

In a graph with $n = \vert V \vert$ nodes, Dijkstra performs **exactly $n$ extractions** from the priority queue (or array) in the worst case, with each extraction triggering up to $\deg(v)$ relaxation operations.

$$T(n) = \sum_{v \in V} \Big[\text{ExtractMin}(v) + \sum_{(v,u) \in E} \text{Relax}(v,u)\Big]$$

For a binary heap implementation:

$$T(n) = n \cdot \mathcal{O}(\log n) + \vert E \vert \cdot \mathcal{O}(\log n) = \mathcal{O}\big((n + \vert E \vert) \log n\big)$$

This is the **expected board answer** when asked about complexity.

---

## 3.4 Proof Sketch of Optimality (Why Dijkstra is Correct)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greedy-Exchange Argument (commonly asked as 7-mark theory question):**

Assume $S$ contains finalized nodes and the next extracted node is $u$ with $d[u]$ minimum in $Q$. Suppose, for contradiction, that there exists an alternative path $P$ to $u$ that is shorter than $d[u]$. That alternative path must exit $S$ via some edge $(x, y)$ where $x \in S$, $y \notin S$. By the relaxation invariant, when $x$ was added to $S$, edge $(x,y)$ would have set $d[y] \leq d[x] + w(x,y)$. By induction on the order of extraction, $d[y] \leq d[u]$, contradicting that $u$ is the minimum unvisited node. Hence no shorter path exists, and $d[u]$ is final.

**Boundary condition for validity:** $w(u,v) \geq 0$ for all edges. Negative weights would invalidate the inductive argument because the path through $S$ could later become cheaper.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Initialize]) --> nodeB[Set d of source s to 0]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Set d of all other nodes to infinity]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Initialize predecessor pi of all nodes to NIL]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Insert all nodes into priority queue Q]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Is Q empty or goal reached}
    nodeF -- No --> nodeG[Extract node u with minimum d of u from Q]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Add u to visited set S]
    nodeH --> nodeI[For each neighbor v of u in E]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Is d of u plus w of uv less than d of v}
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeK[Update d of v to d of u plus w of uv]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Set pi of v to u]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Decrease key of v in Q]
    nodeM --> nodeI
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeN{All neighbors processed}
    nodeN -- No --> nodeI
    nodeN -- Yes --> nodeF
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeO[Reconstruct path by backtracking from goal via pi]
    nodeO --> nodeP([End: Return shortest path and distances])
```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the conditions under which Dijkstra's algorithm guarantees optimality. Why does it fail when edge weights are negative?**

**Model Answer:**

Dijkstra's algorithm guarantees an optimal (shortest) path under the following **mandatory conditions**:

1. **The graph must be finite** — i.e., $\vert V \vert$ and $\vert E \vert$ are finite integers.
2. **All edge weights must be non-negative** — that is, $w(u,v) \geq 0$ for every $(u,v) \in E$.
3. **The source node must be specified and valid** — i.e., $s \in V$.

The algorithm **fails** when edge weights are negative because its **greedy optimality argument** relies on the monotonicity property: once a node is extracted from the priority queue and added to the visited set $S$, its distance is finalized. This finalization holds only if no path passing through a later (not-yet-finalized) node can become shorter. Negative weights break this assumption because a future edge could *reduce* the path cost after a node has already been committed to $S$.

For graphs with negative weights, **Bellman–Ford** must be used instead.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare Dijkstra's algorithm and A* search algorithm in terms of heuristic usage, optimality, and computational efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Dijkstra's Algorithm | A* Algorithm |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Heuristic $h(v)$** | Not used (or $h(v) = 0$ for all $v$) | Admissible heuristic guides search toward goal |
| **Cost Function** | $f(v) = g(v)$ (only actual cost from start) | $f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$ (actual + estimated) |
| **Optimality** | Optimal for non-negative weights | Optimal if $h(v)$ is admissible & consistent |
| **Search Direction** | Uniform, expands in all directions | Goal-directed, focuses exploration |
| **Time Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ | Often faster in practice due to pruning |
| **Completeness** | Complete in finite graphs | Complete in finite graphs |
| **Use Case** | When no heuristic exists or full shortest-path tree needed | When heuristic is available and only goal path is required |

In summary, A* can be viewed as a **goal-directed generalization** of Dijkstra where $h(v) = 0$ for all $v$ reduces A* to plain Dijkstra.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step execution of Dijkstra's algorithm using a suitable pseudocode representation. Clearly state the role of the priority queue, the relaxation step, and the visited set. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the graph given below, with $A$ as the source, apply Dijkstra's algorithm to compute the shortest distance to every node. Show the contents of the distance array $d[\;]$ and the predecessor array $\pi[\;]$ after each iteration. Also reconstruct the shortest path from $A$ to $F$. **(7 marks)**

**Edges and weights:**
- $A \to B = 6$, $A \to D = 1$
- $B \to C = 5$, $B \to E = 2$
- $C \to F = 3$
- $D \to B = 2$, $D \to E = 4$
- $E \to F = 1$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Data Structures Required:**

- **Distance Array $d[\;]$:** Holds the tentative shortest distance from source $s$ to every node $v$.
- **Predecessor Array $\pi[\;]$:** Stores the node preceding $v$ on the shortest path; used for backtracking.
- **Priority Queue $Q$:** A min-heap keyed on $d[v]$, allowing $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ extraction of the closest unvisited node.
- **Visited Set $S$:** Boolean marker indicating finalized nodes whose distances cannot be improved.

**Step 2 — Algorithmic Pseudocode (Structured Description, language-agnostic):**

```
FUNCTION Dijkstra(G, s):
    FOR each vertex v in V:
        d[v]  := INFINITY
        pi[v] := NIL
    END FOR
    d[s] := 0
    Q := priority queue containing all vertices keyed on d[v]

    WHILE Q is not empty:
        u := EXTRACT_MIN(Q)        // greedy selection
        ADD u TO S

        FOR each neighbor v of u in E:
            IF v not in S AND d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]:
                d[v]  := d[u] + w(u, v)    // relaxation
                pi[v] := u
                DECREASE_KEY(Q, v, d[v])
            END IF
        END FOR
    END WHILE
END FUNCTION
```

**Step 3 — Explanation of Critical Steps:**

- **Initialization:** Distances set to $\infty$ except $d[s]=0$ ensures the source is the first extracted node.
- **Greedy Selection:** `EXTRACT_MIN` always returns the unvisited node with the smallest known distance. Because edge weights are non-negative, this node's distance is provably final.
- **Relaxation:** The condition $d[u] + w(u,v) < d[v]$ is the *only* place where distances improve; it embodies the recurrence:

$$d[v] = \min_{(u,v) \in E} \big(d[u] + w(u,v)\big)$$

- **Visited Set:** Once in $S$, a node is never re-extracted, guaranteeing each vertex is processed exactly once.

**Step 4 — Termination:** The loop terminates when $Q$ is empty, meaning every reachable node has been finalized.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Initialization:**

$$d[A]=0,\; d[B]=d[C]=d[D]=d[E]=d[F]=\infty, \quad \pi[\cdot]=\text{NIL}$$

---

**Iteration 1 — Extract $u = A$ ($d = 0$):**

Relax edges from $A$:

- $A \to B$: $d[B] = \min(\infty, 0+6) = 6$, $\pi[B] = A$.
- $A \to D$: $d[D] = \min(\infty, 0+1) = 1$, $\pi[D] = A$.

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | **0** | 6 | $\infty$ | 1 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | — | A | — | A | — | — |

---

**Iteration 2 — Extract $u = D$ ($d = 1$):**

Relax edges from $D$:

- $D \to B$: $d[B] = \min(6, 1+2) = 3$, $\pi[B] = D$. (Improvement)
- $D \to E$: $d[E] = \min(\infty, 1+4) = 5$, $\pi[E] = D$.

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | **3** | $\infty$ | 1 | 5 | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | — | D | — | A | D | — |

---

**Iteration 3 — Extract $u = B$ ($d = 3$):**

Relax edges from $B$:

- $B \to C$: $d[C] = \min(\infty, 3+5) = 8$, $\pi[C] = B$.
- $B \to E$: $d[E] = \min(5, 3+2) = 5$, $\pi[E] = B$. (Tie with 5, choose predecessor as $B$).

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | 3 | 8 | 1 | **5** | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | — | D | B | A | B | — |

---

**Iteration 4 — Extract $u = E$ ($d = 5$):**

Relax edges from $E$:

- $E \to F$: $d[F] = \min(\infty, 5+1) = 6$, $\pi[F] = E$.

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | 3 | 8 | 1 | 5 | **6** |
| $\pi$ | — | D | B | A | B | E |

---

**Iteration 5 — Extract $u = F$ ($d = 6$):** Goal reached.

---

**Final Shortest Distances from $A$:**

$$d[A]=0,\; d[B]=3,\; d[C]=8,\; d[D]=1,\; d[E]=5,\; d[F]=6$$

**Path Reconstruction (Backtrack from $F$):**

$$\pi[F] = E, \quad \pi[E] = B, \quad \pi[B] = D, \quad \pi[D] = A$$

**Reverse path:** $A \to D \to B \to E \to F$.

**Verification:** $1 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 6 = d[F]$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain why Dijkstra's algorithm is classified as a *greedy algorithm*. Justify using the **greedy-choice property** and **optimal substructure** that the algorithm satisfies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **time and space complexity** of Dijkstra's algorithm for the following implementations:
  (i) Adjacency matrix with linear array
  (ii) Adjacency list with binary min-heap
  (iii) Adjacency list with Fibonacci heap

Identify which implementation is most suitable for large-scale robotic grid maps and justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Greedy Classification:**
Dijkstra's algorithm is termed *greedy* because at every iteration it makes the **locally optimal choice** — extracting the unvisited node with the *current minimum* tentative distance — and irrevocably commits to that choice, never reconsidering it later.

**Two Required Properties for Greedy Optimality:**

**1. Greedy-Choice Property:**
A globally optimal solution can be reached by repeatedly making locally optimal (greedy) choices. In Dijkstra's context, the local choice is:

$$u^* = \arg\min_{v \in Q} d[v]$$

This choice is globally correct: once $u^*$ is extracted, its distance $d[u^*]$ equals the true shortest-path distance $d(s, u^*)$ and cannot be improved by any alternative path. This is provable via the exchange argument — any other path to $u^*$ must first pass through some node still in $Q$ with $d \geq d[u^*]$, and since edge weights are non-negative, that alternative cannot be strictly shorter.

**2. Optimal Substructure:**
The shortest path from $s$ to any node $v$ contains within it the shortest path from $s$ to every intermediate node on that route. Formally, if $s \to \cdots \to u \to v$ is a shortest path to $v$, then $s \to \cdots \to u$ is a shortest path to $u$. This recursive property enables the relaxation equation:

$$d[v] = d[u] + w(u,v), \quad \text{where } d[u] \text{ is itself optimal.}$$

**Conclusion:** Because Dijkstra satisfies both properties — and additionally benefits from non-negative edge weights to lock in greedy decisions — its greedy approach is provably optimal, not merely heuristic.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Complexity Comparison Table:**

| Implementation | Extract-Min | Decrease-Key | Total Time |
|----------------|-------------|--------------|------------|
| (i) Adj. matrix + linear array | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ |
| (ii) Adj. list + binary heap | $\mathcal{O}(\log \vert V \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log \vert V \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}\big((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert\big)$ |
| (iii) Adj. list + Fibonacci heap | $\mathcal{O}(\log \vert V \vert)$ amortized | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized | $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ |

**Space complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for the adjacency list representations; $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ for the matrix.

**Suitability for Robotic Grid Maps:**

For typical robotic occupancy grids, $\vert V \vert$ can range from $10^4$ (small room) to $10^6$ (large warehouse), and $\vert E \vert \approx 4 \vert V \vert$ (4-connected grid) to $8 \vert V \vert$ (8-connected).

**Recommendation:** The **adjacency list + binary min-heap** implementation (option ii) is most practically suitable because:

1. It avoids the $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ explosion of dense matrix storage.
2. Binary heaps are simple to implement and have excellent cache behavior.
3. Fibonacci heaps, while asymptotically superior, have high constant factors and complex implementations that rarely pay off in dense grid scenarios where $\vert E \vert \sim \vert V \vert$.
4. In practice, modern planners (e.g., ROS `navfn`) use grid-based Dijkstra with binary heaps for global planning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to initialize $\pi[\;]$** alongside $d[\;]$ — the predecessor array is essential for path reconstruction.
> 2. **Using $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ as a universal answer** without specifying the implementation (heap vs array).
> 3. **Failing to mention the non-negativity assumption** when justifying optimality — this is a 2-mark deduction guaranteed.
> 4. **Confusing Dijkstra with BFS** — BFS applies to unweighted graphs only.
> 5. **Skipping the relaxation equation** in pseudocode questions; the inequality $d[u] + w(u,v) < d[v]$ is mandatory.
> 6. **Not showing all iterations** in worked examples — the KTU examiner expects every iteration in tabular form.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Dijkstra's algorithm** computes the **single-source shortest path** for all nodes in a weighted graph with $w \geq 0$.
- It uses a **greedy strategy** selecting the unvisited node with the **minimum tentative distance** at every step.
- **Three data structures** are mandatory: distance array $d[\;]$, predecessor array $\pi[\;]$, and a priority queue (min-heap).
- The **relaxation equation** is:

$$d[v] \leftarrow \min\big(d[v],\; d[u] + w(u,v)\big)$$

- **Optimality proof** relies on the **greedy-choice property + optimal substructure + non-negativity**.
- **Fails on negative edge weights** — use Bellman–Ford instead.
- **Time complexities:**
  - Array: $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$
  - Binary heap: $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$
  - Fibonacci heap: $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert\log \vert V \vert)$
- **Space complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for list-based implementations.
- In **mobile robotics**, Dijkstra serves as a **global planner** in known static environments; it is preferred when no informative heuristic exists or when the **entire shortest-path tree** is required.
- **Difference from A*:** A* adds a heuristic $h(v)$ to bias search toward the goal; A* reduces to Dijkstra when $h(v) = 0$.
- **Termination conditions:** $Q$ empty (full search) or goal extracted (early-exit variant).
- **Reconstruct the optimal path** by backtracking through $\pi[\;]$ from the goal to the source, then reversing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Best First Search - A and A* Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Best First Search - A and A* Algorithm ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Best-First Search (BFS)** is a class of informed (heuristic) search strategies in mobile robot path planning that expands the most promising node on the frontier of the search graph, where "most promising" is determined by an **evaluation function** $f(n)$. Unlike uninformed search (BFS, DFS, UCS) that explores blindly, BFS exploits domain knowledge via a heuristic estimate to direct the search toward the goal. The family of Best-First Search includes two cornerstone algorithms: - **A Algorithm (Hart, Nilsson & Raphael, 1968):** A best-first search that uses the evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ where $g(n)$ is the exact cost from the start to $n$, and $h(n)$ is an *arbitrary* heuristic estimate from $n$ to the goal. **A guarantees finding *a* solution, but not necessarily the optimal (least-cost) one.** - **A* Algorithm:** A special case of A that uses an **admissible heuristic** — that is, $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ where $h^*(n)$ is the true minimum cost to the goal. Under admissibility (and optionally consistency), A* is **optimally efficient**: no other algorithm using the same heuristic can expand fewer nodes while still guaranteeing an optimal path. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** > - A = $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ with *any* heuristic → finds a solution > - A* = A with **admissible** heuristic → finds the *optimal* solution > - A* with **consistent** heuristic → optimal AND optimally efficient (no node re-expansion) --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Rescue Rover" Imagine a **search-and-rescue rover** dropped on hilly terrain at point $S$ and tasked with reaching an injured hiker at point $G$. The rover has: 1. A **GPS odometer** → tells it exactly how far it has already driven from $S$. This is $g(n)$. 2. A **binocular rangefinder** → estimates the straight-line distance to the hiker. This is $h(n)$. 3. A **decision-making computer** → combines both into a single "expected total effort" score. This is $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. At every junction, the rover asks: *"Of all the next possible paths, which one has the lowest $f$ score — i.e., the lowest expected total effort?"* It then commits to that path first, expanding the most promising frontier. This is precisely **A\***. - If the rover ignored $g(n)$ and followed only the ranger's distance estimate → that is **pure Greedy Best-First Search**. - If it ignored $h(n)$ and only counted the odometer → that degenerates into **Uniform Cost Search (Dijkstra)**. - A* is the elegant middle ground that balances *the cost incurred so far* with *the cost estimated to remain*. --- ### 1.3 Terminology Quick-Reference | Symbol | Meaning | Domain | |:---:|:---|:---| | $g(n)$ | Actual cost from **start** $S$ to current node $n$ | Path planning, robotics | | $h(n)$ | Heuristic estimate from $n$ to **goal** $G$ | Map / domain knowledge | | $f(n)$ | Estimated total cost via $n$: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Decision / ranking function | | $h^*(n)$ | True optimal cost from $n$ to $G$ | Oracle / ideal value | | $c(n, n')$ | Edge cost between adjacent nodes $n, n'$ | Terrain, energy, time | | $\epsilon$ | Heuristic error margin | $h(n) = h^*(n) - \epsilon$ | > [!NOTE] > **Two crucial data structures** every A* implementation maintains: > 1. **OPEN list (priority queue / fringe):** Nodes discovered but not yet expanded. Ordered by ascending $f(n)$. > 2. **CLOSED list (explored set):** Nodes already expanded. Avoids re-processing. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Evaluation Function — Heart of A* The cornerstone equation governing both A and A* is: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$ - $g(n)$ — **Backward cost:** the cheapest known path cost from $S$ to $n$. Computed exactly by summation along the path. - $h(n)$ — **Forward cost (heuristic):** an estimate of the cheapest cost from $n$ to $G$. It is the only place where "intelligence" or domain knowledge enters the algorithm. - $f(n)$ — **Total estimated cost** of a solution passing through $n$. By always expanding the OPEN node with the smallest $f$, A* balances two competing pressures: completing the path cheaply so far, and getting close to the goal. --- ### 2.2 Heuristic Properties — The KTU Exam Favourites #### 2.2.1 Admissibility A heuristic $h$ is **admissible** if it **never overestimates** the true cost to the goal: $$0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n) \quad \forall\, n$$ > An admissible heuristic is **optimistic** — it believes reaching the goal costs less than (or equal to) reality. #### 2.2.2 Consistency (Monotonicity) A heuristic is **consistent** if for every edge $(n, n')$, the triangle inequality holds: $$h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$$ A consistent heuristic is automatically admissible. Consistency guarantees that when a node $n$ is first expanded, $g(n) = g^*(n)$ (its $g$-cost is already optimal), so we never need to re-expand nodes — a critical property for runtime efficiency in mobile robots. #### 2.2.3 Dominance Given two admissible heuristics $h_1$ and $h_2$, we say $h_2$ **dominates** $h_1$ if: $$h_1(n) \le h_2(n) \le h^*(n) \quad \forall\, n$$ A* with the dominating heuristic $h_2$ expands **fewer or equal** nodes than A* with $h_1$, while preserving optimality. --- ### 2.3 Common Heuristics for Mobile Robots (Grid Maps) For a robot on a **2-D grid** with cells of unit size, the most-used heuristics are: | Heuristic | Formula | Admissible? | Use Case | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | **Manhattan distance** $h_{\text{man}}$ | $\vert x_G - x_n \vert + \vert y_G - y_n \vert$ | ✅ Yes (4-connected grid) | Differential-drive on axis-aligned lattice | | **Euclidean distance** $h_{\text{euc}}$ | $\sqrt{(x_G - x_n)^2 + (y_G - y_n)^2}$ | ✅ Yes (any graph) | Omnidirectional / holonomic robots | | **Chebyshev distance** $h_{\text{cheb}}$ | $\max(\vert x_G - x_n \vert, \vert y_G - y_n \vert)$ | ✅ Yes (8-connected grid) | Robots that move in 8 directions | | **Zero heuristic** $h(n)=0$ | — | Trivially admissible | Reduces A* → Uniform Cost Search | | **Octile distance** | $\min(\Delta x, \Delta y)\sqrt{2} + \vert \Delta x - \Delta y \vert$ | ✅ Yes (8-connected w/ $\sqrt{2}$ diagonals) | Realistic cost for diagonal motion | --- ### 2.4 Optimality & Completeness Theorems (Board-Exam Gold) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem 1 — Completeness of A\*:** A* on a finite graph with positive edge costs terminates (finds a goal if one exists). > **Theorem 2 — Optimality (Admissibility condition):** If $h$ is admissible, A* returns an optimal (minimum-cost) path. > **Theorem 3 — Optimal Efficiency (Consistency condition):** If $h$ is consistent, A* is optimally efficient — among all algorithms that find an optimal path using the same heuristic, A* expands the fewest nodes. > **Theorem 4 — f-value monotonicity:** Under a consistent heuristic, the sequence of $f$-values of expanded nodes is non-decreasing. This is why we can stop as soon as a goal is dequeued from OPEN. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of A* in Mobile Robotics | |:---|:---| | **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva, GreyOrange)** | Compute optimal pick-and-drop routes around dynamic shelving | | **Autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla)** | Lane-level path planning within behavioural layer | | **Drone delivery (Zipline, Wing)** | Obstacle-aware 3-D trajectory planning in urban airspace | | **Service robots (Temi, Pepper)** | Indoor navigation between waypoints, room-to-room traversal | | **Mars rovers (Perseverance, Curiosity)** | Long-baseline path planning with traversability heuristics | | **Search & rescue UGVs** | Time-critical navigation through partially known rubble maps | --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This!) | # | Equation / Concept | Statement | Validity / Unit | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Total estimated cost via $n$ | Cost units (m, s, J, etc.) | | 2 | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | Admissibility — heuristic never overshoots | Real number | | 3 | $h(n) \le c(n,n') + h(n')$ | Consistency — triangle inequality | Real number | | 4 | $g(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(n_{i-1}, n_i)$ | Path cost accumulation | Cost units | | 5 | $h_{\text{man}} = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | Manhattan distance on 4-grid | Cells | | 6 | $h_{\text{euc}} = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Euclidean / straight-line | Map units | | 7 | $f_{\text{goal}} \le f^*$ at termination | A* optimality condition | $f^*$ = optimal cost | | 8 | $N = O(b^{\epsilon d})$ | Node expansion exponential bound | $b$ = branching, $d$ = depth | | 9 | $\epsilon = (f^* - h(S)) / f^*$ | Effective branching heuristic error | $\epsilon \ge 0$ | | 10 | $h_{\text{octile}}(n) = (D_1 + D_2) \cdot 1 + (D_2 - D_1) \cdot (\sqrt{2}-1)$ | Octile distance ($D_1 \le D_2$) | Map units | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example ### 3.1 Canonical A* Algorithm — Operational Walkthrough We will solve a **representative exam-style problem** that examiners frequently set. > **Problem Statement:** > A differential-drive robot navigates the following weighted graph. Each edge is labelled with its cost. The robot must travel from **Start $S$** to **Goal $G$**. The admissible heuristic $h(n)$ for each node is given in the table. Use **A\* search** to find the optimal path. Show all OPEN/CLOSED list states and step-by-step node expansions. **Graph topology:** ``` (A) --- 1 --- (B) | \ | 4 3 2 | \ | (S) -- 5 -- (C) - 2 - (D) | | 7 3 | | (E) ---------- 4 ---------- (G) ``` **Heuristic table:** | Node | $S$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ | $G$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $h(n)$ | 7 | 6 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 0 | **Step-by-step A\* execution:** **Initial State:** - OPEN = $\{(S, f=0+7=7)\}$, $g(S)=0$, $h(S)=7$, $f(S)=7$ - CLOSED = $\emptyset$ --- **Iteration 1:** Pop node with lowest $f$: **$S$ (f = 7)**. Expand. Compute successors of $S$: - Successor $A$: $g(A) = g(S) + c(S,A) = 0 + 4 = 4$, $h(A) = 6$, $f(A) = 10$ - Successor $C$: $g(C) = 0 + 5 = 5$, $h(C) = 4$, $f(C) = 9$ - Successor $E$: $g(E) = 0 + 7 = 7$, $h(E) = 2$, $f(E) = 9$ - OPEN = $\{(C,9), (E,9), (A,10)\}$ — ordered by $f$ then $h$ - CLOSED = $\{S\}$ --- **Iteration 2:** Pop node with lowest $f$: **$C$ (f = 9)** (ties broken by lower $h$, here $C$ and $E$ both have $f=9$ but we pick $C$ by convention or alphabetically; the *other* path is still in OPEN). Expand. Compute successors of $C$: - Successor $A$ via $C$: $g(A)_{\text{new}} = 5 + 3 = 8$. But $A$ is in OPEN with $g(A)=4$, and $8 > 4$. **Discard** (OPEN already has a better path). - Successor $B$ via $C$: $g(B) = 5 + 2 = 7$, $h(B) = 4$, $f(B) = 11$ - Successor $D$ via $C$: $g(D) = 5 + 2 = 7$, $h(D) = 3$, $f(D) = 10$ - OPEN = $\{(E,9), (A,10), (D,10), (B,11)\}$ - CLOSED = $\{S, C\}$ --- **Iteration 3:** Pop node with lowest $f$: **$E$ (f = 9)**. Expand. Compute successors of $E$: - Successor $G$ via $E$: $g(G) = 7 + 4 = 11$, $h(G) = 0$, $f(G) = 11$ - OPEN = $\{(A,10), (D,10), (B,11), (G,11)\}$ - CLOSED = $\{S, C, E\}$ --- **Iteration 4:** Pop node with lowest $f$: **$A$ (f = 10)**. Expand. Compute successors of $A$: - Successor $B$ via $A$: $g(B)_{\text{new}} = 4 + 1 = 5$. $B$ is in OPEN with $g(B)=7$. Since $5 < 7$, **update** $B$ in OPEN: $g(B) = 5$, $f(B) = 5 + 4 = 9$. - OPEN = $\{(B,9), (D,10), (G,11)\}$ - CLOSED = $\{S, C, E, A\}$ --- **Iteration 5:** Pop node with lowest $f$: **$B$ (f = 9)**. Expand. Compute successors of $B$: - Successor $D$ via $B$: $g(D)_{\text{new}} = 5 + 2 = 7$. $D$ in OPEN has $g(D)=7$. Equal — keep existing (or tie-break). No update. - OPEN = $\{(D,10), (G,11)\}$ - CLOSED = $\{S, C, E, A, B\}$ --- **Iteration 6:** Pop node with lowest $f$: **$D$ (f = 10)**. Expand. Compute successors of $D$: - Successor $G$ via $D$: $g(G)_{\text{new}} = 7 + 3 = 10$. $G$ in OPEN has $g(G)=11$. Since $10 < 11$, **update** $G$: $g(G)=10, f(G)=10$. - OPEN = $\{(G,10)\}$ - CLOSED = $\{S, C, E, A, B, D\}$ --- **Iteration 7:** Pop node with lowest $f$: **$G$ (f = 10)**. Goal reached! **Traceback parents:** $G \leftarrow D \leftarrow C \leftarrow S$ **Optimal path: $S \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$** with total cost $g(G) = 10$. > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** The alternative path $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$ has cost $4+1+2+3 = 10$ — also optimal. This is a common property: multiple optimal paths can exist; A* returns the one it discovers first. --- ### 3.2 Admissibility Proof Sketch (For Theory Marks) We need to prove: *if $h$ is admissible, A\* returns an optimal path.* **Setup:** - Let $G^*$ be an optimal goal node with cost $f^* = g(G^*) = h^*(S)$ (true cost from $S$ to any optimal goal). - Let $n$ be any unexpanded node on OPEN. Its $f$-value is $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. **Lemma 1 (Bounding unexpanded f-values):** For every unexpanded $n$ on OPEN, $f(n) \le f^*$. *Proof of Lemma 1:* Consider the path along the optimal path from $S$ to $G^*$. At some point, an unexpanded node $n$ on this optimal path exists. We have: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \le g(n) + h^*(n) = g(G^*) = f^*$$ The inequality uses **admissibility**: $h(n) \le h^*(n)$. The equality $g(n) + h^*(n) = g(G^*)$ holds because $n$ lies on the optimal path, so the true remaining cost to $G^*$ is $h^*(n) = g(G^*) - g(n)$. Substituting: $$g(n) + (g(G^*) - g(n)) = g(G^*) = f^*$$ Hence $f(n) \le f^*$. $\blacksquare$ **Lemma 2 (f-monotonicity under consistency):** If $h$ is consistent, $f$ is non-decreasing along any path: $f(n') \ge f(n)$ whenever $n'$ is a successor of $n$. *Proof:* By consistency, $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$, so: $$f(n') = g(n) + c(n,n') + h(n') \ge g(n) + h(n) = f(n)$$ Hence $f$ is monotonically non-decreasing. $\blacksquare$ **Theorem (A* Optimality):** When A* terminates by selecting goal $G$ from OPEN, $g(G) = f^*$. *Proof:* By Lemma 1, every unexpanded node $n$ satisfies $f(n) \le f^*$. In particular, if there were another goal $G'$ on OPEN with $f(G') < f(G)$, A* would have popped it first. Since $G$ was popped and $h(G) = 0$ (heuristic at goal is conventionally zero), $g(G) = f(G) \le f^*$. But $g(G) \ge f^*$ trivially (no path can beat the optimum). Therefore $g(G) = f^*$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.3 Comparison Matrix — A vs A* vs UCS vs Greedy | Criterion | Uniform Cost Search | Greedy Best-First | **A** (any $h$) | **A*** (admissible $h$) | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Uses $g(n)$? | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Uses $h(n)$? | ❌ (set $h=0$) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Complete? | ✅ (positive costs) | ❌ (can get stuck) | ✅ (positive costs) | ✅ | | Optimal? | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (unless $h$ admissible) | ✅ | | Optimal efficiency? | ❌ (wastes effort) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (if $h$ consistent) | | Typical $f(n)$ | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $g(n)+h(n)$ | $g(n)+h(n)$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 A* Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start]) --> initA[Initialize OPEN with start node S
g of S equals 0
h of S given
f of S equals g of S plus h of S] initA --> initB[CLOSED equals empty set] initB --> checkEmpty{OPEN empty?} checkEmpty -- Yes --> failA([Failure: No path exists]) checkEmpty -- No --> popA[Pop node n with lowest f from OPEN] popA --> goalChk{n equals Goal?} goalChk -- Yes --> successA([Success: Reconstruct path via parent pointers]) goalChk -- No --> expandA[Generate successors of n
For each successor m of n] expandA --> gcalc[g of m equals g of n plus cost of edge n to m] gcalc --> hcalc[h of m from heuristic table] hcalc --> fcalc[f of m equals g of m plus h of m] fcalc --> dupChk{m already in CLOSED
with lower g?} dupChk -- Yes --> skipA[Skip m
Continue to next successor] dupChk -- No --> openChk{m in OPEN
with lower g?} openChk -- Yes --> skipB[Skip m
Continue to next successor] openChk -- No --> addA[Insert m into OPEN
Set parent of m to n] addA --> nextA{More successors?} skipA --> nextA skipB --> nextA nextA -- Yes --> gcalc nextA -- No --> closeA[Add n to CLOSED] closeA --> checkEmpty ``` ### 4.2 Robot Path-Planning Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PER["PERCEPTION LAYER"] L1[LiDAR Scan] L2[IMU Odometry] L3[Camera Obstacle Map] end subgraph MAP["OCCUPANCY GRID BUILDER"] M1[Probabilistic Grid
update via Bayes filter] M2[Cost Map
traversability scoring] end subgraph PLAN["A STAR PLANNER"] P1[Heuristic Function
h of n - Euclidean or Manhattan] P2[OPEN Priority Queue
min heap on f] P3[CLOSED Set
hash table] P4[Path Reconstructor
parent pointer traceback] end subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER"] C1[Pure Pursuit or DWA
trajectory tracker] C2[Motor Command
linear and angular velocity] end L1 --> M1 L2 --> M1 L3 --> M1 M1 --> M2 M2 --> P1 M2 --> P2 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> L2 ``` ### 4.3 OPEN / CLOSED List State Evolution (Worked Example) ```mermaid flowchart TD st0["Step 0
OPEN: S f=7
CLOSED: empty"] st1["Step 1 - expand S
OPEN: C f=9, E f=9, A f=10
CLOSED: S"] st2["Step 2 - expand C
OPEN: E f=9, A f=10, D f=10, B f=11
CLOSED: S, C"] st3["Step 3 - expand E
OPEN: A f=10, D f=10, B f=11, G f=11
CLOSED: S, C, E"] st4["Step 4 - expand A
OPEN: B f=9, D f=10, G f=11
CLOSED: S, C, E, A"] st5["Step 5 - expand B
OPEN: D f=10, G f=11
CLOSED: S, C, E, A, B"] st6["Step 6 - expand D
OPEN: G f=10
CLOSED: S, C, E, A, B, D"] st7["Step 7 - expand G GOAL
Path: S to C to D to G cost 10
CLOSED: S, C, E, A, B, D, G"] st0 --> st1 --> st2 --> st3 --> st4 --> st5 --> st6 --> st7 ``` ### 4.4 Heuristic Function Block — Intuition ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Current Node n
position xn, yn] --> B[Compute delta x equals xG minus xn] A --> C[Compute delta y equals yG minus yn] B --> D{Choose Heuristic Type} C --> D D -- Manhattan --> E["h equals |dx| plus |dy|
Best for 4-connected grid"] D -- Euclidean --> F["h equals sqrt of dx squared plus dy squared
Best for any-angle motion"] D -- Chebyshev --> G["h equals max of |dx| and |dy|
Best for 8-connected grid"] D -- Octile --> H["h equals min plus min times sqrt2 minus 1
Best for 8-connected with diag cost sqrt2"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** *Define the A\* search algorithm. What is the significance of the heuristic function $h(n)$ in A\*?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A\* (A-star) is an informed graph-search algorithm that finds the **shortest path** from a start node $S$ to a goal node $G$ by combining: - $g(n)$: the actual cost from $S$ to the current node $n$, and - $h(n)$: a heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to $G$. The evaluation function is $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, and A\* always expands the unexpanded node with the smallest $f$-value. **Significance of $h(n)$:** The heuristic $h(n)$ encodes *domain knowledge* about the problem (e.g., straight-line distance in 2-D grid navigation). When $h(n)$ is **admissible** ($h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$), A\* is guaranteed to return an *optimal* path. When $h(n)$ is also **consistent**, A\* is optimally efficient and never re-expands a node. Thus $h(n)$ directly controls both correctness and search effort. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Distinguish between Best-First Search and A\* Algorithm.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Best-First Search (Greedy) | A* Algorithm | |:---|:---|:---| | Evaluation function | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | | Considers past cost $g(n)$? | No | Yes | | Optimal? | Not guaranteed | Yes (if $h$ admissible) | | Heuristic requirement | None specified | Must be admissible (and ideally consistent) | | Practical use | Fast, heuristic-only planning | Optimal path planning in robotics | **Key distinction (1 mark):** Greedy Best-First Search only considers the estimated cost *to the goal* ($h$) and ignores the cost *already incurred* ($g$), which makes it fast but suboptimal. A\* combines both via $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, balancing the two and producing optimal solutions under admissible heuristics. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The two alternatives below represent a true internal-choice pair. --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a) Solve the following graph problem using the A\* algorithm.** Start node is $S$, goal node is $G$. Use the heuristic values given. **Graph:** ``` (A) --- 2 --- (B) / \ \ 4 \ 3 / \ \ (S) --3-- (C) -- 1 -- (D) -- 2 -- (G) \ / \ / 5 -- (E) --- 2 --- (F) \ / 1 (G) ← same goal ``` **Heuristic table:** | Node | $S$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ | $F$ | $G$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $h(n)$ | 6 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 0 | Show the OPEN and CLOSED list at each step, the parent pointers, and the final optimal path with its total cost. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 0:** - OPEN = $\{(S, f=0+6=6)\}$ - CLOSED = $\emptyset$ **Step 1 — Expand $S$ (f=6):** - $A$: $g=0+4=4, h=4, f=8$ - $C$: $g=0+3=3, h=4, f=7$ - $E$: $g=0+5=5, h=5, f=10$ - OPEN = $\{(C,7), (A,8), (E,10)\}$, CLOSED = $\{S\}$ **Step 2 — Expand $C$ (f=7):** - $A$ via $C$: $g=3+4=7 > 4$ → discard (OPEN has $A$ with $g=4$) - $D$ via $C$: $g=3+1=4, h=2, f=6$ - $E$ via $C$: $g=3+?$ → no direct edge $C \to E$ from given topology; skip - OPEN = $\{(D,6), (A,8), (E,10)\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, C\}$ **Step 3 — Expand $D$ (f=6):** - $B$ via $D$: $g=4+3=7, h=5, f=12$ - $G$ via $D$: $g=4+2=6, h=0, f=6$ - $F$ via $D$: $g=4+2=6, h=1, f=7$ - OPEN = $\{(G,6), (F,7), (A,8), (E,10), (B,12)\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, C, D\}$ **Step 4 — Expand $G$ (f=6):** **GOAL REACHED!** **Traceback parents:** $G \leftarrow D \leftarrow C \leftarrow S$ **Optimal path:** $S \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$ with total cost $g(G) = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$. **[7 marks]** --- **(b) Prove that if the heuristic $h(n)$ is admissible, then A\* returns an optimal solution. Also state the role of a *consistent* heuristic. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** *Admissibility definition:* $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true minimum cost from $n$ to $G$. *Proof of Optimality:* Let $G^*$ be an optimal goal with $f^* = g(G^*)$. For any unexpanded node $n$ on OPEN lying on the optimal path from $S$ to $G^*$: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \le g(n) + h^*(n) = g(G^*) = f^*$$ where the inequality is by **admissibility** and the equality uses the fact that the true remaining cost from $n$ along the optimal path is $h^*(n) = f^* - g(n)$. When A* terminates by selecting goal $G$ from OPEN, $h(G)=0$ so $g(G) = f(G)$. Since every other unexpanded node $n'$ satisfies $f(n') \le f^*$, we must have $f(G) \le f^*$. But by definition $g(G) \ge f^*$ (no path can be cheaper than the optimum). Therefore: $$g(G) = f^* \quad \blacksquare$$ *Role of Consistent Heuristic:* A heuristic $h$ is consistent if for every edge $(n, n')$: $$h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$$ **Implications:** 1. Every consistent $h$ is admissible. 2. The $f$-cost is **monotonically non-decreasing** along any path: $f(n') \ge f(n)$. 3. When a node is first expanded, its $g$-cost is already optimal, eliminating the need to re-expand nodes. This makes A* **optimally efficient**. 4. Ensures that the first goal popped from OPEN is the optimal one, allowing early termination. **[7 marks]** --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative* **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Restructured for 2024 Scheme]** **(a) Describe the A\* algorithm in detail. Explain the role of the evaluation function $f(n)$, the path cost $g(n)$, and the heuristic $h(n)$. List any two admissible heuristics used in mobile robot grid maps. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **A\* Algorithm — Description:** A\* (A-star) is a best-first, graph-based search algorithm that uses both the actual cost from the start and a heuristic estimate of the cost to the goal to determine the order of node expansion. It maintains two lists: - **OPEN list** — priority queue of frontier nodes, ordered by $f(n)$. - **CLOSED list** — set of already-expanded nodes. The algorithm: 1. Insert start $S$ into OPEN with $f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + h(S)$. 2. Repeat: - If OPEN is empty → fail. - Pop node $n$ with minimum $f$ from OPEN. - If $n$ is goal → reconstruct path; return success. - Add $n$ to CLOSED. - For each successor $m$ of $n$: - Compute $g(m) = g(n) + c(n,m)$. - Compute $f(m) = g(m) + h(m)$. - If $m$ is in CLOSED with lower $g$ → skip. - If $m$ is in OPEN with lower $g$ → skip. - Otherwise update/insert $m$ in OPEN with parent pointer to $n$. **Role of each component (4 marks):** - $g(n)$: tracks the **actual cost incurred so far** from start to $n$. Provides the "look-back" intelligence — the algorithm won't waste effort on nodes that have already been reached expensively. - $h(n)$: provides a **forward-looking estimate** to the goal. Encodes domain knowledge; this is the *informed* part of the algorithm. - $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$: the **ranking score** that combines past and future cost. A\* always expands the node with the smallest $f$ — a strategy proven to yield the optimal path under admissibility. **Two admissible heuristics for grid maps (2 marks):** 1. **Manhattan distance:** $h_{\text{man}}(n) = \vert x_G - x_n \vert + \vert y_G - y_n \vert$ — admissible for 4-connected grids. 2. **Euclidean distance:** $h_{\text{euc}}(n) = \sqrt{(x_G - x_n)^2 + (y_G - y_n)^2}$ — admissible for any-angle motion, never overestimates straight-line distance. **(Bonus, 1 mark for octile/Chebyshev):** Octile distance $= (D_1 + D_2) + (D_2 - D_1)(\sqrt{2} - 1)$ where $D_1 = \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$, $D_2 = \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ — admissible for 8-connected grids with diagonal cost $\sqrt{2}$. **[7 marks]** --- **(b) Apply A\* to the following 5×5 grid navigation problem. The robot moves in 4 directions (up, down, left, right) with unit cost per move. Start = $S = (0,0)$, Goal = $G = (4,4)$. Cells shaded 'X' are obstacles. Use the Manhattan distance as the heuristic. Find the first optimal path A\* discovers. **[7 marks]** **Grid (S at top-left, G at bottom-right; X = obstacle):** ``` Row 0: S . . X . Row 1: . . . X . Row 2: . X . . . Row 3: . X X X . Row 4: . . . . G ``` **Model Solution:** **Heuristic calculation (Manhattan):** $h(x,y) = \vert 4 - x \vert + \vert 4 - y \vert$ **Step-by-step expansion (showing $f$, $g$, $h$ and parent pointer):** | Step | Pop from OPEN | $g$ | $h$ | $f$ | Action / Successors | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | 0 | $(0,0)$ | 0 | 8 | 8 | Expand: insert $(1,0),(0,1)$ | | 1 | $(1,0)$ | 1 | 7 | 8 | Expand: $(2,0)$ f=8; $(1,1)$ blocked by X at (0,1)? No — wait, check topology | | 2 | $(2,0)$ | 2 | 6 | 8 | Expand: $(3,0)$ f=8; $(2,1)$ blocked | | 3 | $(3,0)$ | 3 | 5 | 8 | Expand: $(4,0)$ f=8; $(3,1)$ blocked | | 4 | $(4,0)$ | 4 | 4 | 8 | Expand: $(4,1)$ f=8; obstacle at (4,-1) doesn't exist | | 5 | $(4,1)$ | 5 | 3 | 8 | Expand: $(4,2)$ f=8 | | 6 | $(4,2)$ | 6 | 2 | 8 | Expand: $(3,2)$ f=8 | | 7 | $(3,2)$ | 7 | 1 | 8 | Expand: $(2,2)$ f=8 | | 8 | $(2,2)$ | 8 | 0 | 8 | $(2,2)$ is not $G=(4,4)$ — Expand: $(2,3)$ blocked; $(1,2)$ X; $(3,3)$ blocked; $G$ not yet | | 9 | $(4,3)$? | 7 | 1 | 8 | $(4,3)$ from $(4,2)$: $g=7$ — Expand: $(4,4)=G$ found with $g=8$ | **Refined answer:** After expanding $(4,2)$ with $f=8$, both $(3,2)$ and $(4,3)$ are in OPEN with $f=8$. Pop $(4,3)$ (lower h or tie-break) and expand to find $G$ at $(4,4)$ with $g=8, h=0, f=8$. **Path reconstruction:** $G(4,4) \leftarrow (4,3) \leftarrow (4,2) \leftarrow (4,1) \leftarrow (4,0) \leftarrow (3,0) \leftarrow (2,0) \leftarrow (1,0) \leftarrow S(0,0)$ **Optimal path:** $S \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (4,0) \to (4,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to G$ with total cost $g(G) = 8$. **[7 marks]** --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks on A\* questions:** > > 1. **Confusing A with A\*** (loses 1–2 marks): A uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ with *any* heuristic and is **not** guaranteed optimal. A\* specifically uses an **admissible** heuristic.
