Examiners will deduct marks if you claim A is always optimal. > > 2. **Wrong $h(G)$ value (loses 1 mark):** The heuristic at the goal is **always 0** by definition: $h(G) = 0$. Forgetting this yields a non-zero $f(G)$ and breaks optimality proofs. > > 3. **Skipping tie-breaking (loses 1 mark):** When two nodes share the same $f$, examiners expect a stated tie-breaker (lowest $h$, or alphabetical, or insertion order). State it explicitly. > > 4. **Forgetting to update parent pointer on re-relaxation (loses 2 marks):** When a node in OPEN is found via a *better* path (lower $g$), you must **update** $g$, $f$, and the parent pointer — not just discard. This is the *relaxation step* of Dijkstra-style A\*. > > 5. **Not showing OPEN/CLOSED at every step (loses 2–3 marks):** Step-by-step tabular display of $(OPEN, CLOSED, f, g, h)$ is *expected* in KTU 2024 scheme answers. Omitting this loses nearly half the marks. > > 6. **Mixing $h(n)$ with $h^*(n)$ (loses 1 mark):** $h(n)$ is the *heuristic* (an estimate); $h^*(n)$ is the *true cost* (an oracle). The admissibility inequality is $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ — examiners are very strict on this notation. > > 7. **Skipping the admissibility check in exam answers (loses 1 mark):** When asked to "use A\* to solve...", confirm in one line that your $h$ is admissible. E.g., "Manhattan distance is admissible for 4-connected grids as it never overestimates the number of moves." --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Best-First Search, A and A\* Algorithm** - **Best-First Search** uses an evaluation function to always expand the most promising frontier node. - **Greedy Best-First** uses $f(n) = h(n)$ — fast but not optimal. - **A Algorithm** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ with any heuristic — finds *a* solution, not necessarily optimal. - **A\* Algorithm** = A + **admissible** heuristic → finds the **optimal** solution. - **Admissibility:** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ — heuristic never overestimates. - **Consistency:** $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ — implies admissibility + $f$-monotonicity. - **Dominance:** Heuristic that gives higher $h$-values (closer to $h^*$) expands fewer nodes. - **Two key data structures:** OPEN (priority queue on $f$), CLOSED (explored set). - **Stop condition:** Pop goal $G$ from OPEN — its $g(G)$ equals optimal cost. - **Manhattan heuristic** is admissible for 4-connected grids; **Euclidean** for any-angle; **Octile** for 8-connected with diagonal cost $\sqrt{2}$. - **Optimal efficiency:** A\* with a consistent heuristic expands the fewest possible nodes. - **Heuristic at the goal is always zero:** $h(G) = 0$. - **Re-relaxation:** Always update parent and $g$ when a cheaper path to a node is found. - **Real-world use:** AGV routing, autonomous cars, drone delivery, Mars rover navigation. - **Limitation:** A\* is exponential in worst case — for very large maps, use weighted A\*, ARA\*, D\*, or theta\*.

topics.title: Searching a Continuous State Space - Potential Fields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Searching a Continuous State Space - Potential Fields

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Artificial Potential Field (APF) method** is a reactive, gradient-based path-planning technique in which the mobile robot is treated as a charged particle moving through a scalar potential field $\mathcal{U}(q)$ defined over the continuous robot configuration space $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$ (typically $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ for a planar mobile robot).

The configuration space potential is constructed as the **superposition** of an **attractive potential** $U_{att}(q)$ that pulls the robot toward the goal configuration $q_{goal}$, and a **repulsive potential** $U_{rep}(q)$ that pushes the robot away from obstacles $\mathcal{O}_i$. The total artificial force is the negative gradient:

$$F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

The robot follows the trajectory produced by the **steepest descent** of this combined potential, conceptually analogous to a ball rolling downhill toward the goal while avoiding hills formed over obstacles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Potential fields fall under the umbrella of **searching a continuous state space** because, unlike discrete grid searches (BFS, A*, D*), the configuration $q = (x, y, \theta)$ is a real-valued vector. The search proceeds by continuous gradient descent rather than node expansion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Charged Ball in a Hilly Landscape"

Imagine a smooth metal sheet shaped like a landscape. The goal is at the **bottom of a valley** (a paraboloid bowl), and every obstacle is replaced by a **steep hill** (a Gaussian-shaped bump). A tiny steel ball — representing the robot — is placed at the start. Gravity pulls it down the slope, so it naturally rolls **toward the goal** while the **hills deflect it sideways**, avoiding the obstacles. That gravity field is the *attractive potential*, and the obstacle hills are the *repulsive potentials*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Potential field methods convert a *geometric path planning* problem into a *differential equation* problem: solve $\dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$ until $q \approx q_{goal}$.

## 1.3 Why "Potential Fields" in Continuous Spaces?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Smooth, dense coverage** | Unlike graph-based search, the field exists at *every* configuration in $\mathcal{C}$. |
| **Real-time reactive control** | The next control input $u = F(q)$ is computed in $O(k)$ time, where $k$ is the number of obstacles, making it ideal for dynamic environments. |
| **Continuous trajectory** | Path is $C^1$ smooth (no sharp turns), suitable for non-holonomic robots with curvature constraints. |
| **Mathematical elegance** | Couples naturally with Lyapunov stability theory. |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love framing potential fields as a *trade-off* between computational efficiency and completeness — never lose this framing in your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Potential Field

The total potential at configuration $q$ is a scalar sum of independent contributions:

$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} U_{rep}^{(i)}(q)$$

### 2.1.1 Attractive Potential (Goal Pull)

The goal $q_{goal}$ is a paraboloid well. Two standard forms are accepted by KTU examiners.

**Form A — Quadratic (Convergent) Attractor:** (used when the robot is *near* the goal)

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \, \rho_{goal}^{2}(q), \quad \rho_{goal}(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$$

Corresponding attractive **force**:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att} \left( q - q_{goal} \right)$$

This force grows linearly with distance → strong pull far away, weak near goal.

**Form B — Conic (Constant Magnitude) Attractor:** (used to prevent vanishing control near the goal)

$$U_{att}(q) = k_{att} \, \rho_{goal}(q)$$

$$F_{att}(q) = -k_{att} \, \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert}, \quad q \neq q_{goal}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The conic form produces a **constant-magnitude force vector** of magnitude $k_{att}$ pointing at the goal. Many KTU textbook answers mention the quadratic form is preferred for stability analysis because $\nabla^2 U_{att} = k_{att} I \succ 0$ (positive definite Hessian).

### 2.1.2 Repulsive Potential (Obstacle Push)

Let $q^*$ denote the point on obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$ that is **closest** to the robot. The distance function is:

$$\rho_i(q) = \Vert q - q^* \Vert = \min_{q' \in \mathcal{O}_i} \Vert q - q' \Vert$$

Define the influence radius $d^* > 0$ such that the repulsive field acts only when the robot is *within* $d^*$ of an obstacle.

**Khatib's Standard Repulsive Potential (1986):**

$$U_{rep}^{(i)}(q) = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{2} \, k_{rep} \, \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho_i(q)} - \dfrac{1}{d^*} \right)^{2}, & \rho_i(q) \le d^* \\[8pt]
0, & \rho_i(q) > d^*
\end{cases}
$$

Repulsive force (acts **away** from the obstacle along the line $q \to q^*$):

$$F_{rep}^{(i)}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}^{(i)}(q) = 
\begin{cases}
k_{rep} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho_i(q)} - \dfrac{1}{d^*} \right) \dfrac{1}{\rho_i^{2}(q)} \, \nabla \rho_i(q), & \rho_i(q) \le d^* \\[8pt]
0, & \rho_i(q) > d^*
\end{cases}
$$

where $\nabla \rho_i(q) = \dfrac{q - q^*}{\Vert q - q^* \Vert}$ is the unit vector from the closest obstacle point to the robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The repulsive force **diverges to infinity** as $\rho_i(q) \to 0$ (collision), guaranteeing $F_{rep} \to \infty$ — a *safety guarantee* but also a numerical instability near obstacles.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units / Values |
|---|---|---|
| $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Robot configuration (pose) | $(x, y)$ or $(x, y, \theta)$ |
| $q_{goal}$ | Goal configuration | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| $k_{att}$ | Attractive gain | $> 0$, dimensionless or N/m |
| $k_{rep}$ | Repulsive gain | $> 0$, dimensionless or N·m |
| $d^*$ | Obstacle influence radius | metres; $0.5$–$3$ m typical |
| $\rho_{goal}(q)$ | $\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ | metres |
| $\rho_i(q)$ | Distance to $i$-th obstacle | metres |
| $U_{att}(q)$ | Attractive potential | energy units (J) |
| $U_{rep}^{(i)}(q)$ | Repulsive potential (obstacle $i$) | energy units (J) |
| $F(q)$ | Net artificial force | Newtons (N) |
| $\nabla U(q)$ | Gradient of total potential | N |
| $\epsilon$ / $\Delta t$ | Discrete step size for descent | seconds |

### 2.2.1 Gradient Descent Update Law (Discrete Implementation)

$$q_{t+1} = q_t + \epsilon \, F(q_t) = q_t - \epsilon \, \nabla U(q_t), \quad \epsilon > 0$$

Equivalently, the continuous-time closed-loop system is:

$$\dot{q}(t) = -\nabla U(q(t))$$

### 2.2.2 Equilibrium Condition

A configuration $q_{eq}$ is a **critical point** of the field if:

$$\nabla U(q_{eq}) = 0 \iff F_{att}(q_{eq}) = -F_{rep}(q_{eq})$$

The goal is *one* such critical point (stable). Other critical points are **local minima** (unstable for path planning) — the central failure mode of APF.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Potential Fields |
|---|---|
| **AGV warehouses (Amazon Kiva)** | Local obstacle avoidance layered atop A* global plan |
| **Autonomous cars** | Lane-keeping + pedestrian repulsion (as supplement to MPC) |
| **Drone swarms** | Inter-agent repulsion → collision-free flocking |
| **Surgical robots** | Force-reflection haptic feedback to surgeon |
| **Underwater AUVs** | Real-time seafloor obstacle avoidance where SLAM is noisy |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask *"Give two real-world limitations of APF."* Always mention **(1) local minima trap** and **(2) oscillations in narrow corridors** for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Attractive Force from Quadratic Potential

**Goal:** Show that $F_{att}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$ for the quadratic attractive potential.

**Step 1.** Start with the potential definition.

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 2.** Expand the squared norm using the dot product.

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \left( q - q_{goal} \right)^{T} \left( q - q_{goal} \right)$$

**Step 3.** Apply the product rule for gradients: $\nabla_x (x^T x) = 2x$.

$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot 2 \left( q - q_{goal} \right) = k_{att} \left( q - q_{goal} \right)$$

**Step 4.** Force is the negative gradient.

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att} \left( q - q_{goal} \right) = k_{att} \left( q_{goal} - q \right)$$

**Step 5.** Magnitude of attractive force.

$$\Vert F_{att}(q) \Vert = k_{att} \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert = k_{att} \, \rho_{goal}(q)$$

The force is a vector pointing from $q$ to $q_{goal}$ with magnitude proportional to distance — exactly the behaviour of a Hookean spring anchored at the goal.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Repulsive Force from Khatib's Potential

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for $F_{rep}(q)$ in 2-D.

**Step 1.** Let the closest point on obstacle $i$ be $q^* = (x^*, y^*)$, and the robot be at $q = (x, y)$.

$$\rho_i(q) = \sqrt{(x - x^*)^2 + (y - y^*)^2}$$

**Step 2.** For $\rho_i(q) \le d^*$, write the potential.

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{d^*} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3.** Apply chain rule. Let $g(\rho) = \dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{d^*}$. Then $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} g^2$.

$$\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep} \, g(\rho) \cdot \nabla g(\rho)$$

**Step 4.** Compute $\nabla g(\rho)$ using $\nabla \rho_i = \dfrac{q - q^*}{\rho_i}$.

$$\nabla \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} \right) = -\frac{1}{\rho_i^{2}} \nabla \rho_i = -\frac{1}{\rho_i^{2}} \cdot \frac{q - q^*}{\rho_i} = -\frac{q - q^*}{\rho_i^{3}}$$

$$\nabla \left( \frac{1}{d^*} \right) = 0 \quad \text{(constant)}$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla g(\rho) = -\frac{q - q^*}{\rho_i^{3}}$$

**Step 5.** Combine.

$$\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{d^*} \right) \left( -\frac{q - q^*}{\rho_i^{3}} \right)$$

**Step 6.** Apply the force sign convention $F_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}$.

$$F_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{d^*} \right) \frac{q - q^*}{\rho_i^{3}}$$

**Step 7.** Identify the unit vector $\hat{n} = \dfrac{q - q^*}{\Vert q - q^* \Vert} = \dfrac{q - q^*}{\rho_i}$.

$$F_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{d^*} \right) \frac{1}{\rho_i^{2}} \hat{n}$$

**Step 8.** Verify the **divergence to infinity** at contact.

$$\lim_{\rho_i \to 0^{+}} F_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \cdot \infty \cdot \infty = \infty \quad \checkmark$$

The robot receives an unbounded push the instant it touches the obstacle — guaranteed safety at the cost of unbounded control effort.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Force and Stability Margin

**Goal:** Show that the goal is a stable equilibrium of the closed-loop dynamics $\dot q = -\nabla U$.

**Step 1.** Total potential.

$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} U_{rep}^{(i)}(q)$$

**Step 2.** Lyapunov candidate: $V(q) = U(q)$. Since $V \ge 0$ and $V(q_{goal}) = 0$, $V$ is positive semi-definite.

**Step 3.** Time derivative along the trajectory.

$$\dot V(q) = \nabla U(q)^{T} \dot q = \nabla U(q)^{T} \left( -\nabla U(q) \right) = -\Vert \nabla U(q) \Vert^{2} \le 0$$

**Step 4.** By **LaSalle's invariance principle**, trajectories converge to the largest invariant set where $\dot V = 0$, i.e., $\nabla U(q) = 0$.

**Step 5.** If the goal is the **unique** critical point, the system converges there globally. Otherwise, convergence to a *local minimum* is possible — the fundamental incompleteness of APF.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

A planar point robot at $q_s = (0, 0)$ must reach $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$. A circular obstacle of radius $r = 1$ m is centred at $(5, 0)$. Parameters: $k_{att} = 1$, $k_{rep} = 5$, $d^* = 3$ m.

**Step 1.** Compute attractive force.

$$F_{att} = -k_{att} (q_s - q_{goal}) = -(1)((0,0) - (10,0)) = (10, 0) \text{ N}$$

**Step 2.** Distance to obstacle.

$$\rho = \Vert (0,0) - (5,0) \Vert = 5 \text{ m}$$

Since $\rho = 5 > d^* = 3$, the obstacle is outside the influence region.

**Step 3.** Total force.

$$F_{total} = (10, 0) \text{ N}$$

**Step 4.** Iterate gradient descent with step $\epsilon = 0.1$ s. The robot moves to $(1, 0)$ and continues. Once the robot enters $d^*$, the repulsive force pushes it above or below the obstacle (sign depending on $\epsilon$ updates).

This is the textbook *obstacle avoidance dance* you must describe in a 14-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an APF Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Pose q from Odometry] --> B[Compute Distance to Goal rho_goal]
    A --> C[For Each Obstacle i Compute Closest Point q_star_i]
    C --> D[Compute Distance rho_i and Unit Vector n_i]
    B --> E[Compute Attractive Force F_att]
    D --> F[Compute Repulsive Force F_rep_i]
    E --> G[Sum Forces F_total = F_att plus Sum F_rep]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Local Minima Detector}
    H -->|Stable| I[Apply Escape Heuristic: Random Walk or Virtual Obstacle]
    H -->|Normal| J[Gradient Descent Update q_new = q plus epsilon F_total]
    J --> K[Velocity Controller]
    I --> J
    K --> L[Wheel Motor Commands]
    L --> M[Robot Actuators]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 State Diagram — APF Convergence Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StartPose
    StartPose --> Descending: Apply F_att
    Descending --> AvoidObstacle: rho_i less than d_star
    AvoidObstacle --> Descending: rho_i greater than d_star
    Descending --> GoalReached: norm q minus q_goal less than tolerance
    Descending --> LocalMinima: gradient U equals zero but not at goal
    LocalMinima --> EscapeHeuristic: Trigger recovery
    EscapeHeuristic --> Descending: Resume descent
    GoalReached --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Superposition of Potentials

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Attractive Potential U_att: Paraboloid centered at Goal] --> C[Total Potential U = U_att plus Sum U_rep]
    B[Repulsive Potential U_rep: Sum of inverted cones over Obstacles] --> C
    C --> D[Gradient Operator nabla]
    D --> E[Artificial Force F = minus nabla U]
    E --> F[Gradient Descent Loop: q dot = F]
    F --> G[Trajectory in Configuration Space]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Local Minima Geometry (KTU-Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FreeSpace [Free Space]
        Start[Start q_s] -->|descent| LM[Local Minimum q_lm: F_att = F_rep]
        LM -->|trapped| Trap((Oscillation or Stalling))
    end
    subgraph GoalRegion [Goal Region]
        Goal[Goal q_g: U is minimum] -->|unique attractor| Done((Converged))
    end
    LM -.->|escape via virtual obstacle or random walk| Done
    style Trap fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style Done fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **local minima** is a configuration where $\nabla U_{att}$ and $\sum \nabla U_{rep}$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction — a *mechanical equilibrium* that is not the goal. This is the central failure mode of APF and is heavily tested in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Artificial Potential Field method. State two advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**
Artificial Potential Field (APF) is a path planning technique in which the robot is treated as a particle moving under the influence of a scalar potential $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum U_{rep}^{(i)}(q)$, where $U_{att}$ is an attractive well at the goal and $U_{rep}^{(i)}$ are repulsive hills at obstacles. The robot follows the negative gradient $F = -\nabla U$.

**Advantages:** (i) Computationally efficient, $O(k)$ per step for $k$ obstacles, suitable for real-time use. (ii) Produces smooth, continuous trajectories.

**Disadvantage:** Susceptible to **local minima** traps where the robot stalls before reaching the goal.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the gradient of the repulsive potential set to zero outside the obstacle influence radius $d^*$?**

**Model Answer:**
Outside $d^*$, the robot is sufficiently far from the obstacle that obstacle avoidance is unnecessary. Defining $U_{rep} = 0$ for $\rho > d^*$ ensures the repulsive force is identically zero beyond $d^*$, eliminating unwanted influence on the global attractive trajectory and reducing computational load. This also guarantees $C^1$ smoothness of $U$ at the boundary $\rho = d^*$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the attractive and repulsive force expressions for a point robot navigating in a 2-D workspace with one circular obstacle. Clearly state the assumptions and the role of the influence radius $d^*$.

(b) **[7 Marks]** For a point robot at $q = (2, 2)$ with goal $q_{goal} = (10, 10)$, and a single circular obstacle of radius $r = 1$ m centred at $(6, 6)$, compute the **total artificial force** using $k_{att} = 1$, $k_{rep} = 5$, and $d^* = 4$ m. State whether the robot enters the repulsive region.

**Model Solution (a):**

Assume a 2-D Euclidean configuration space $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$, with the robot as a point mass (configuration $q = (x, y)$). Obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ is a closed disc of radius $r$ centred at $q_c = (x_c, y_c)$.

**Attractive force derivation:** Starting from $U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2$, applying the gradient operator and the negative sign:

$$F_{att}(q) = -k_{att} (q - q_{goal}) = k_{att} (q_{goal} - q)$$

This is a Hookean spring force of stiffness $k_{att}$ anchored at the goal.

**Repulsive force derivation:** The closest point on a circular obstacle is $q^* = q_c + r \cdot \hat{n}$ where $\hat{n} = (q - q_c)/\Vert q - q_c \Vert$. The distance is $\rho = \Vert q - q_c \Vert - r$. For $\rho \le d^*$:

$$F_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{d^*} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \hat{n}$$

**Role of $d^*$:** The influence radius defines the *spatial extent* over which the obstacle exerts a repulsive force. It must be large enough to provide early avoidance but small enough to avoid interference with the global attractive trajectory. A typical choice is $d^* \ge 2 \cdot v_{max} \cdot \Delta t$ to allow sufficient reaction time.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1.** Compute attractive force.

$$q - q_{goal} = (2 - 10, \, 2 - 10) = (-8, -8)$$

$$F_{att} = -k_{att} (q - q_{goal}) = -(1)(-8, -8) = (8, 8) \text{ N}$$

$$\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \sqrt{64 + 64} = 8\sqrt{2} \approx 11.31 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2.** Compute distance to obstacle.

$$\rho = \Vert q - q_c \Vert - r = \Vert (2 - 6, 2 - 6) \Vert - 1 = \sqrt{32} - 1 \approx 4.66 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3.** Compare with $d^*$.

$$\rho \approx 4.66 \text{ m} > d^* = 4 \text{ m}$$

Therefore the robot is **outside** the influence region. The repulsive force is **zero**.

**Step 4.** Total force.

$$F_{total} = (8, 8) \text{ N}, \quad \Vert F_{total} \Vert \approx 11.31 \text{ N}$$

The robot proceeds directly toward the goal without obstacle deflection at this instant. The next iteration will reduce $\rho$ until the robot enters the influence region at $\rho = 4$ m, after which deflection begins.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **local minima problem** in Artificial Potential Field methods. With neat diagrams, describe two recovery strategies used in practice.

(b) **[7 Marks]** Compare Artificial Potential Field method with the Wavefront / Navigation Function approach in terms of completeness, computational cost, and trajectory smoothness.

**Model Solution (a):**

A **local minimum** of the potential $U(q)$ is a configuration $q_{lm} \neq q_{goal}$ such that $\nabla U(q_{lm}) = 0$ and $U(q_{lm}) \le U(q)$ in a neighbourhood. Geometrically, it occurs when the attractive force pulling toward the goal is exactly balanced by the combined repulsive forces from one or more obstacles. The robot halts because $F_{total} = 0$ even though it has not reached $q_{goal}$. Local minima are a fundamental *incompleteness* of APF.

**Two recovery strategies:**

1. **Random Walk / Brownian Escape:** When the robot detects stalling (e.g., velocity below threshold for $N$ steps), it executes a randomized walk to perturb out of the local basin. Once $\nabla U \neq 0$ resumes, normal descent continues.

2. **Virtual Obstacle / Wall-Following:** Inject a small artificial repulsive bump at $q_{lm}$ to break the symmetry, or follow the obstacle boundary (tangential motion along the iso-potential contour) until the attractive field resumes dominance.

**Model Solution (b):**

| Criterion | Artificial Potential Field | Wavefront / Navigation Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Completeness** | Not complete (local minima exist) | Complete (unique global minimum at goal) |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(k)$ per step; very low | $O(N^2)$ pre-computation of grid; high |
| **Trajectory Smoothness** | $C^1$ smooth, continuous gradients | Piecewise smooth; grid-aligned, requires post-smoothing |
| **Reactivity to Dynamic Obstacles** | Excellent — just update $U_{rep}$ | Poor — must re-run wavefront propagation |
| **Memory Footprint** | Stores only obstacle parameters $k$ | Stores full $N \times N$ potential grid |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign:** Force is $F = -\nabla U$, not $+\nabla U$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for sign errors.
> 2. **Ignoring the condition $\rho \le d^*$:** Students often write the repulsive force without the piecewise definition. Always specify the region of validity.
> 3. **Conflating attractive and repulsive gradients:** $\nabla \rho$ and $\nabla \rho_{goal}$ use the same notation but refer to different distances. Label them clearly.
> 4. **Skipping the influence-radius explanation:** In 7-mark sub-parts, KTU examiners expect a 2–3 sentence justification of *why* $d^*$ is chosen. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **No mention of local minima:** Any APF answer without addressing local minima is graded at most 80% of full marks. Treat it as a *mandatory* talking point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **APF Definition:** $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} U_{rep}^{(i)}(q)$, with $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$.
- **Quadratic attractor:** $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \rho_{goal}^{2}$ → linear attractive force $-k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$.
- **Conic attractor:** $U_{att} = k_{att} \rho_{goal}$ → constant-magnitude attractive force, useful near goal.
- **Khatib repulsive:** $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} (1/\rho - 1/d^*)^2$ for $\rho \le d^*$, else $0$.
- **Repulsive force direction:** along $\hat{n} = (q - q^*)/\rho$, pointing **away** from the closest obstacle point.
- **Discrete update:** $q_{t+1} = q_t + \epsilon F(q_t)$; choose $\epsilon$ small enough for stability.
- **Lyapunov stability:** $V(q) = U(q)$ is a valid Lyapunov function with $\dot V = -\Vert \nabla U \Vert^2 \le 0$.
- **Local minima:** occur when attractive and repulsive forces balance away from goal — central failure mode.
- **Recovery heuristics:** random walk, virtual obstacles, wall-following, harmonic potential functions.
- **Influence radius $d^*$:** tradeoff between early avoidance and global path optimality; rule of thumb $d^* \ge 2 v_{max} \Delta t$.
- **Practical applications:** AGV warehouses, autonomous cars, drone swarms, surgical robots, AUVs.
- **Gains tuning:** higher $k_{att}$ → goal-aggressive; higher $k_{rep}$ → safety-cautious; balance needed to prevent oscillations.
- **Comparison with grid search:** APF is reactive and continuous; A* / Wavefront are complete but require discretization.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mnemonic for KTU viva:** *"Attract by spring, repel by hill, descend by gradient, watch for traps."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Field Histogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vector Field Histogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** is a real-time, reactive obstacle-avoidance algorithm developed by **Borenstein and Koren (1991)** for mobile robot navigation. It produces a one-dimensional polar histogram representing the obstacle density around the robot as a function of the angular direction. The algorithm compresses the two-dimensional **Cartesian Certainty Grid** (occupancy grid) into a 1-D polar density distribution by mapping each occupied cell into an angular sector of width $\alpha$, and then selects a safe **steering direction** based on candidate *valleys* (low-density sectors) aligned with the target heading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** VFH is listed under *Module 3 – Robot Perception & Local Navigation*. You must know the three-stage pipeline — **Grid Map → Polar Histogram → Steering Decision** — for both 3-mark definitions and 14-mark derivations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are blindfolded in a room. You sweep a long stick (your sonar) around you, and for every direction you feel an *obstacle*, you mentally “record” how close it is. After one full sweep, you build a mental dial where each tick mark represents a direction and the *height* of the tick represents how *dangerous* that direction is. Now, instead of walking toward the closest danger, you walk through the **gap (valley)** that points most closely to where you want to go (the door).

That mental dial is the **Polar Histogram**, and the rule "walk through the valley pointing to the door" is the **Steering Decision** in VFH.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Decades of Evolution:** The original VFH (1991) evolved into VFH+ (1998) and VFH* (2000). For KTU 2024 Module 3, focus on the **classical VFH** unless the question explicitly mentions VFH+ or VFH*.

### 1.3 Key Terms and Metrics

- **Certainty Value (C):** Probability that a cell is occupied, range **[0, 1]**.
- **Active Region / Window:** A square of side **$2 \cdot W_s + 1$** cells centred on the robot; cells outside are ignored.
- **Sector Angle ($\alpha$):** Angular width of one polar histogram bin, typically **$5°$** (giving **72 sectors** for 360°).
- **Threshold $H_s$ (Smoothing):** Used to smooth raw density spikes.
- **Threshold $H_t$ (Valley threshold):** Density value below which a sector is considered *traversable*.
- **Steering Gain $\mu_1$, $\mu_2$, $\mu_3$:** Weights balancing *heading alignment, wheel alignment,* and *distance to goal*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Operational Pipeline

The VFH algorithm executes in **three sequential stages** every control cycle (typically every **100 ms**).

**Stage 1 — Cartesian Certainty Grid Update**
- Each sonar/IR return updates the cell $(i, j)$ of the certainty grid.
- The cell certainty value is incremented using the discrete Bayesian update:

$$C^{*} = C + \Delta C \cdot (1 - C)$$

where $\Delta C$ is a positive increment constant (often **0.05 to 0.3** depending on sensor confidence).

**Stage 2 — Construction of the 1-D Polar Histogram**
- For every *active* cell inside the **Window Size $W_s$** (e.g., 33 cells ≈ 1.5 m radius on a 5 cm grid), the algorithm computes:
  1. The **direction angle** $\beta_{i,j}$ from robot to the cell.
  2. The **obstacle strength** $m_{i,j}$ as a function of certainty and distance.
  3. The **angular sector index** $k$ that the cell projects into.
- All cells contributing to sector $k$ are summed to form the **Polar Density** $H_k$.

**Stage 3 — Steering Decision (Valley Selection)**
- The polar histogram is **smoothed** to eliminate narrow spurious spikes.
- A threshold $H_t$ is applied; consecutive sectors below $H_t$ form a **candidate valley**.
- A **cost function** is evaluated at the *center* of each candidate valley; the direction with the **minimum cost** is selected as the next steering command.

### 2.2 Why VFH Works (The Engineering Logic)

- **Computational Efficiency:** Compressing a 2-D grid (e.g., 60 × 60 = 3600 cells) into 72 scalar bins reduces decision complexity by ~98%.
- **Reactive Speed:** Purely sensor-driven, no global path planner needed ⇒ sub-millisecond response.
- **Tunable Safety:** $W_s$ and $H_t$ directly trade-off *aggressiveness vs. clearance*.
- **Why Polar?** Human drivers and animal navigation naturally reason in *angular* terms (left/right), not Cartesian (x, y).

> [!TIP]
> **Why a "Histogram" not a "Map"?** The polar histogram *discards* x-y positional information and keeps only the **angular distribution of danger**. This is the *key insight* that makes VFH fast.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Cell certainty update | $C^{*} = C + \Delta C \cdot (1 - C)$ | Bayesian increment, dimensionless |
| 2 | Direction to cell | $\beta_{i,j} = \arctan\!\big(\dfrac{y_j - y_r}{x_i - x_r}\big)$ | Angle from robot to cell, **radians / degrees** |
| 3 | Distance to cell | $d_{i,j} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_j - y_r)^2}$ | Euclidean, **cells or metres** |
| 4 | Obstacle strength | $m_{i,j} = (C_{i,j})^2 \cdot (a - d_{i,j})$ | $a$ = positive constant, dimensionless |
| 5 | Sector index | $k = \text{INT}\!\left(\dfrac{\beta_{i,j}}{\alpha}\right)$ | $\alpha = 5°$ typically, $k \in [0, 71]$ |
| 6 | Polar density | $H_k = \sum_{i,j} m_{i,j}$ | Total obstacle magnitude in sector $k$ |
| 7 | Smoothing | $H'_k = \dfrac{H_{k-s} + H_{k-s+1} + \cdots + H_{k+s}}{2s + 1}$ | Window of $2s+1$ sectors, dimensionless |
| 8 | Valley condition | $H'_k < H_t$ for all $k \in [k_1, k_2]$ | Defines a candidate valley |
| 9 | Steering cost | $g = \mu_1 \cdot \Delta\theta + \mu_2 \cdot \Delta P + \mu_3 \cdot \Delta d$ | Weighted sum, **radians / cells** |
| 10 | Sector angular width | $\alpha = \dfrac{360°}{n}$ | $n$ = total sectors, default **72** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** In the steering cost function $g$, students often forget the **normalisation** term for $\Delta P$ (polar density difference). Without it, $\mu_1$ and $\mu_3$ dominate because $\Delta P$ can be orders of magnitude larger. KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this oversight.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Amazon Kiva robots use VFH-family algorithms for aisle navigation.
- **AGVs in Manufacturing:** Toyota, KUKA, and Swisslog platforms use polar histogram variants.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots:** Early Roomba models employed VFH-like reactive fields.
- **Disaster Response Robots:** PackBot and Talon use VFH+ in rubble-strewn environments.
- **Service Robots in Hospitals:** Tug robots navigate crowded corridors via histogram-based local planners.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Certainty Grid to Polar Histogram

We will derive the **polar density value $H_k$** for a generic sector $k$ given a **single active cell** at position $(i, j)$ with certainty $C_{i,j}$.

---

**Step 1 — Establish the Robot Frame**

Place the robot at the origin of a local Cartesian frame. The robot's heading is along the **+x axis**. An active cell has coordinates:

$$x_c = x_i - x_r \qquad y_c = y_j - y_r$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Direction Angle $\beta_{i,j}$**

The angle from the robot's heading to the line-of-sight to the cell is:

$$\beta_{i,j} = \text{atan2}(y_c, x_c)$$

The two-argument arctangent is essential to correctly resolve all four quadrants.

**Step 3 — Compute the Euclidean Distance $d_{i,j}$**

$$d_{i,j} = \sqrt{x_c^{2} + y_c^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Map Cell to Sector Index $k$**

The number of sectors spanning $360°$ is $n$ (commonly 72). The angular width of each sector is:

$$\alpha = \frac{2\pi}{n} \quad \text{radians} \quad \text{or} \quad \alpha = \frac{360°}{n} \quad \text{degrees}$$

The sector index that the cell projects into is computed as:

$$k = \text{INT}\!\left(\frac{\beta_{i,j}}{\alpha}\right) \pmod{n}$$

The modulo operation ensures wrap-around at $\pm 180°$.

**Step 5 — Compute the Obstacle Strength $m_{i,j}$**

The strength is a function of *both* the certainty and the proximity (closer & more certain = stronger):

$$m_{i,j} = (C_{i,j})^{2} \cdot (a - d_{i,j})$$

The squared certainty makes near-certain cells dominate; the linear distance term makes near cells dominate.

**Step 6 — Accumulate Density into the Polar Histogram**

For each sector $k$, sum the contributions of *all* active cells that map to it:

$$H_k = \sum_{(i,j) : k(i,j)=k} m_{i,j}$$

This is the canonical 1-D polar histogram value.

**Step 7 — Apply Smoothing to Reduce Noise**

A moving-average filter of width $2s+1$ is applied:

$$H^{\prime}_k = \frac{1}{2s+1} \sum_{j=-s}^{s} H_{k+j}$$

Typical $s = 2$, giving a 5-sector smoothing window.

**Step 8 — Identify Candidate Valleys**

A candidate valley is a maximal contiguous arc $[k_1, k_2]$ such that:

$$H^{\prime}_k \le H_t \quad \forall \, k \in [k_1, k_2]$$

The width of the valley must exceed a minimum to be traversable by the robot's body.

**Step 9 — Compute Steering Cost for Each Valley**

For each candidate valley with center direction $\theta_c$, the cost is:

$$g = \mu_1 \cdot \Delta\theta + \mu_2 \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{P_{\max}} + \mu_3 \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d_{\max}}$$

where:
- $\Delta\theta = \vert \theta_{\text{target}} - \theta_c \vert$ (heading alignment)
- $\Delta P = H^{\prime}_{k_c} - H_t$ (clearance from threshold)
- $\Delta d = \vert d_{\text{goal}} - d_c \vert$ (progress toward goal)

**Step 10 — Select the Minimum-Cost Direction**

$$\theta_{\text{steer}} = \arg\min_c \, g(c)$$

This direction is sent to the low-level motion controller.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):**
> A mobile robot with a 1.65 m × 1.65 m certainty grid (33 × 33 cells of 5 cm) detects **two obstacles** at the following robot-relative coordinates (in cells): $(4, 2)$ with $C = 0.9$ and $(-2, 3)$ with $C = 0.7$. Compute the sector indices they project into for $n = 72$ ($\alpha = 5°$) and the resulting polar density $H_k$ (assume $a = 15$).

**Solution:**

**For cell (4, 2):**

Compute the angle:
$$\beta_1 = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2}{4}\right) = \arctan(0.5) = 26.57°$$

Compute the sector index:
$$k_1 = \text{INT}\!\left(\frac{26.57°}{5°}\right) = \text{INT}(5.31) = 5$$

Compute the distance:
$$d_1 = \sqrt{4^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{20} = 4.47 \text{ cells}$$

Compute the obstacle strength:
$$m_1 = (0.9)^2 \cdot (15 - 4.47) = 0.81 \cdot 10.53 = 8.53$$

**For cell (−2, 3):**

Compute the angle (note: atan2 is used because x is negative):
$$\beta_2 = \text{atan2}(3, -2) = 180° - 56.31° = 123.69°$$

Compute the sector index:
$$k_2 = \text{INT}\!\left(\frac{123.69°}{5°}\right) = \text{INT}(24.74) = 24$$

Compute the distance:
$$d_2 = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{13} = 3.61 \text{ cells}$$

Compute the obstacle strength:
$$m_2 = (0.7)^2 \cdot (15 - 3.61) = 0.49 \cdot 11.39 = 5.58$$

**Final polar histogram (only non-zero entries):**

$$H_5 = 8.53 \quad \text{(from cell 1)}$$
$$H_{24} = 5.58 \quad \text{(from cell 2)}$$

All other $H_k = 0$ for $k \neq 5, 24$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Parameter Trade-offs

| Parameter | Symbol | Small Value Effect | Large Value Effect | Typical Value |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Window size | $W_s$ | Misses far obstacles | Slow, may freeze | **15–20 cells** |
| Sector width | $\alpha$ | High resolution, noisy | Coarse, unstable | **5°** (72 sectors) |
| Certainty increment | $\Delta C$ | Slow learning | History saturated | **0.1** |
| Smoothing radius | $s$ | Sharp, jittery | Blurred, slow response | **2 sectors** |
| Valley threshold | $H_t$ | Conservative (cluttered) | Aggressive (risky) | **30–50** |
| Heading weight | $\mu_1$ | Ignores goal | Goal-seeking dominant | **5** |
| Clearance weight | $\mu_2$ | Cuts close | Wide berth | **2** |
| Distance weight | $\mu_3$ | No goal pull | Goal-pulls always | **1** |

> [!NOTE]
> The above weights ($\mu_1 = 5, \mu_2 = 2, \mu_3 = 1$) are the **canonical Borenstein defaults** cited in his 1991 IEEE T-RA paper. KTU answers quoting these defaults score full credit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture of VFH

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sonar and IR Sensor Array] --> B[Certainty Grid Update Stage]
    B --> C[Window Filter of Size 2Ws plus 1]
    C --> D[Active Cell Enumeration Loop]
    D --> E[Compute Angle beta and Distance d for each cell]
    E --> F[Map cell to Sector Index k]
    F --> G[Compute Obstacle Strength mij]
    G --> H[Accumulate into Polar Histogram Hk]
    H --> I[Smoothing Filter with window 2s plus 1]
    I --> J[Valley Threshold Detection Ht]
    J --> K{Valleys Found}
    K -- Yes --> L[Evaluate Cost Function g for each valley]
    K -- No --> M[Stop and Rotate in place]
    L --> N[Select Minimum Cost Direction]
    N --> O[Send Steering Command to Low Level Controller]
    O --> P[Robot Moves and Loop Repeats every 100 ms]
    P --> A
    M --> P
```

### 4.2 State Machine: VFH Decision Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitGrid
    InitGridGrid --> ReadSensors : Sonar Scan
    ReadSensors --> UpdateCertainty : Bayesian Increment
    UpdateCertainty --> BuildPolar : Sum Active Cells
    BuildPolar --> Smooth : Moving Average
    Smooth --> DetectValleys : Threshold Ht
    DetectValleys --> CostEval : Valleys Present
    DetectValleys --> Recover : No Valley
    CostEval --> Steer : Argmin g
    Recover --> Steer : Rotate 90 deg
    Steer --> ReadSensors : Next Cycle
    Steer --> [*] : Goal Reached
```

### 4.3 Data Flow Schematic (Certainty Grid → Polar Density)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cartesian Certainty Grid 33 by 33] --> B[Window of Active Cells]
    B --> C1[Cell A at 4 2 cells with C equals 0.9]
    B --> C2[Cell B at minus 2 3 cells with C equals 0.7]
    B --> C3[Cell C at 0 6 cells with C equals 0.5]
    C1 --> D1[Sector 5 with strength 8.53]
    C2 --> D2[Sector 24 with strength 5.58]
    C3 --> D3[Sector 30 with strength 3.20]
    D1 --> E[Polar Histogram Hk 72 bins]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Smoothed Histogram H prime k]
    F --> G[Threshold Ht equals 40]
    G --> H[Two Candidate Valleys]
    H --> I[Steering Decision theta steer]
```

### 4.4 Cost Function Visualisation (Bar Chart Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SectorIndex0To71
    direction LR
    S0[Sector 0] --- S1[Sector 1] --- S2[Sector 2] --- S3[Sector 3] --- S4[Sector 4] --- S5[Sector 5 Obstacle Peak H equals 8.53] --- S6[Sector 6] --- S7[Sector 7] --- S8[Sector 8] --- S9[Sector 9] --- S10[Sector 10] --- S11[Sector 11] --- S12[Sector 12] --- S13[Sector 13] --- S14[Sector 14] --- S15[Sector 15] --- S16[Sector 16] --- S17[Sector 17] --- S18[Sector 18] --- S19[Sector 19] --- S20[Sector 20] --- S21[Sector 21] --- S22[Sector 22] --- S23[Sector 23] --- S24[Sector 24 Obstacle Peak H equals 5.58] --- S25[Sector 25] --- S26[Sector 26] --- S27[Sector 27] --- S28[Sector 28] --- S29[Sector 29] --- S30[Sector 30 Small Peak 3.20] --- S31[Sector 31] --- S32[Sector 32] --- ... --- S71[Sector 71]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*Define the Vector Field Histogram (VFH) method of obstacle avoidance. List the three main processing stages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** is a real-time, sensor-based obstacle avoidance technique that compresses a 2-D certainty grid into a 1-D polar histogram of obstacle density versus angle, then selects a safe steering direction through the lowest-density *valley*. The three processing stages are:

1. **Grid Update Stage:** Each sensor return updates a certainty value in the 2-D occupancy grid.
2. **Polar Histogram Stage:** Active cells within a window are projected onto angular sectors to build a 1-D density distribution $H_k$.
3. **Steering Decision Stage:** The histogram is smoothed and thresholded; the cost function $g = \mu_1 \Delta\theta + \mu_2 \Delta P + \mu_3 \Delta d$ is minimised to select the steering direction.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three stages — 3 marks total.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
*What is a "candidate valley" in VFH? Why is smoothing applied before valley detection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **candidate valley** is a maximal contiguous arc of polar-histogram sectors whose smoothed density $H'_k$ lies below a threshold $H_t$ and whose angular width is at least the **minimum traversal width** needed to fit the robot's body.

**Smoothing** is applied to:
- Suppress **spurious narrow spikes** caused by single noisy sonar returns.
- Ensure the robot does not commit to a **one-sector false gap** that would cause collisions.
- Make the histogram **monotonically reasonable** for robust valley boundary detection.

*(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for the role of smoothing.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following. Each carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): VFH — Architecture, Formulation, and Worked Example**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the three stages of the **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** algorithm. Clearly state the role of the certainty grid, polar histogram, and candidate valley selection. (7 marks)

**(b)** A mobile robot with 72 polar sectors ($\alpha = 5°$) detects three obstacles at robot-relative polar coordinates: $(r, \beta) = (2.5 \text{ m}, 20°), (3.0 \text{ m}, 90°), (4.0 \text{ m}, 175°)$ with certainty values 0.8, 0.6, and 0.9 respectively. Given $a = 10$, compute the polar density $H_k$ in each sector. The robot's target is at $30°$. Identify the candidate valleys if $H_t = 20$ and the minimum valley width is 3 sectors. Recommend the steering direction. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The VFH algorithm operates in three sequential stages, as shown in the figure below.

**Stage 1 — Certainty Grid Construction (2 marks):**
The certainty grid is a 2-D Cartesian array of cells, each holding a probability $C \in [0,1]$ that the cell is occupied. Sensor readings (sonar, IR, lidar) update the relevant cells using:

$$C^{*} = C + \Delta C (1 - C)$$

The robot is at the centre of the grid. A square **active window** of side $2W_s + 1$ is applied; cells outside are not considered for obstacle avoidance, reducing computation.

**Stage 2 — Polar Histogram Construction (3 marks):**
For each active cell $(i,j)$ with $C_{i,j} > 0$, compute:
- The direction $\beta_{i,j} = \arctan\!\big(\frac{y_j - y_r}{x_i - x_r}\big)$
- The distance $d_{i,j} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_j - y_r)^2}$
- The obstacle strength $m_{i,j} = (C_{i,j})^2 (a - d_{i,j})$
- The sector index $k = \text{INT}(\beta_{i,j}/\alpha) \bmod n$

The polar density of sector $k$ is the sum:
$$H_k = \sum m_{i,j}$$

**Stage 3 — Smoothing and Steering Decision (2 marks):**
A moving-average smoothing filter is applied: $H'_k = \frac{1}{2s+1}\sum_{j=-s}^{s} H_{k+j}$. A threshold $H_t$ identifies candidate valleys. The cost function $g = \mu_1 \Delta\theta + \mu_2 \Delta P + \mu_3 \Delta d$ is evaluated at the centre of each valley, and the minimum-cost direction is selected.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute sector indices and obstacle strengths (3 marks):**

| Obstacle | $\beta$ | $k = \text{INT}(\beta/5°)$ | $d$ (m) | $C$ | $m = C^2 (10 - d)$ |
|----------|---------|----------------------------|---------|-----|---------------------|
| 1 | 20° | $k = 4$ | 2.5 | 0.8 | $0.64 \times 7.5 = 4.80$ |
| 2 | 90° | $k = 18$ | 3.0 | 0.6 | $0.36 \times 7.0 = 2.52$ |
| 3 | 175° | $k = 35$ | 4.0 | 0.9 | $0.81 \times 6.0 = 4.86$ |

**Step 2 — Write the polar histogram (1 mark):**

$$H_4 = 4.80, \quad H_{18} = 2.52, \quad H_{35} = 4.86, \quad H_k = 0 \text{ otherwise}$$

**Step 3 — Identify candidate valleys (2 marks):**
Since $H_t = 20$ and *all* $H_k < 20$, the histogram is **entirely below threshold**. The minimum valley width is 3 sectors. The candidate valleys are continuous arcs of width ≥ 3 that exclude the three occupied sectors $\{4, 18, 35\}$. This gives candidate valleys:
- Valley 1: sectors $[0, 3]$ (centre at 1.5)
- Valley 2: sectors $[5, 17]$ (centre at 11)
- Valley 3: sectors $[19, 34]$ (centre at 26.5)
- Valley 4: sectors $[36, 71]$ (centre at 53.5)

**Step 4 — Pick the steering direction (1 mark):**
The target is at $30°$ (sector 6). Valley 2 is the closest and its centre ($11 \times 5° = 55°$) gives a heading error of $\vert 55° - 30° \vert = 25°$. The other valleys have larger heading errors. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\theta_{\text{steer}} \approx 55°}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Comparative Study and Parameter Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Compare the **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** with the **Virtual Force Field (VFF)** method of obstacle avoidance. Discuss at least **four** points of contrast with diagrams. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable formula, derive the **steering cost function** used in VFH. Explain the role of each weight ($\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3$) and state what happens if any weight is set to zero. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| # | Aspect | VFF (Virtual Force Field) | VFH (Vector Field Histogram) |
|---|--------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Representation** | 2-D grid of pressure forces summed vectorially | 1-D polar histogram of density per angle |
| 2 | **Computation** | Force vector addition at every cell | Discrete binning + sum per sector |
| 3 | **Steering Output** | Direct resultant force vector | Argmin of cost function over candidate valleys |
| 4 | **Local Minima** | Yes — robot can get stuck in force dead-zones | Largely avoided by valley-following logic |
| 5 | **Speed / Efficiency** | O($W^2$) per cycle | O($W^2$) but with massive constant-factor reduction |
| 6 | **Output Stability** | Oscillates in symmetric corridors | Smoothing gives smoother, monotonic outputs |
| 7 | **Parameter Tuning** | $F_{rr}, F_{ro}, F_{ra}$ | $\alpha, H_s, H_t, \mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3$ |

**Diagrammatic justification (1 mark each for two diagrams):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[VFF Grid with Force Vectors] --> A2[Sum Forces to get Resultant]
    A2 --> A3[Steer along Resultant]
    B1[VFH Certainty Grid] --> B2[Reduce to Polar Histogram Hk]
    B2 --> B3[Smooth and Threshold Ht]
    B3 --> B4[Find Candidate Valleys]
    B4 --> B5[Argmin of Cost Function]
```

*(4 marks for tabular contrast + 1 mark per diagram × 2 = 2 marks + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation of the steering cost function (4 marks):**

For a candidate valley with central sector $k_c$, the direction is:

$$\theta_c = k_c \cdot \alpha$$

Three competing objectives must be balanced:
1. **Heading alignment** — the steering should be close to the target $\theta_t$:
   $$\Delta\theta = \vert \theta_t - \theta_c \vert$$
2. **Clearance from threshold** — the chosen sector should have low density:
   $$\Delta P = H^{\prime}_{k_c} - H_t$$
3. **Progress toward goal** — minimise the remaining distance to the goal:
   $$\Delta d = \vert d_{\text{goal}} - d_{k_c} \vert$$

The weighted sum gives the canonical Borenstein cost:

$$g = \mu_1 \cdot \Delta\theta + \mu_2 \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{P_{\max}} + \mu_3 \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d_{\max}}$$

**Role of each weight (2 marks):**

- **$\mu_1$ (heading weight):** If $\mu_1 = 0$, the robot **ignores the goal direction** and wanders through the nearest valley. If very large, the robot **ignores obstacles** in pursuit of the goal.
- **$\mu_2$ (clearance weight):** If $\mu_2 = 0$, the robot **cuts arbitrarily close to obstacles** (dangerous). If very large, the robot **over-avoids** and may stop in cluttered environments.
- **$\mu_3$ (distance weight):** If $\mu_3 = 0$, the robot has **no preference** between equally-costed valleys; only heading and clearance decide. If large, the robot **always steers toward the goal** even if it has to pass through near-obstacle sectors.

**Effect of zero weights (1 mark):**
Setting any weight to zero disables the corresponding criterion entirely. The robot's behaviour becomes **degenerate** and may not reach the goal. The defaults $\mu_1 = 5, \mu_2 = 2, \mu_3 = 1$ provide a balanced trade-off.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in VFH Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `atan2`:** Many students use `atan(y/x)` which fails in Quadrants II and III. KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing VFH with VFH+:** VFH+ adds *look-ahead verification* and robot *width compensation*; classical VFH does not. Mixing them up loses **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the modulo operation** when computing $k$ — leads to out-of-range indices and **0 marks** in the example.
> 4. **Not normalising $\Delta P$ and $\Delta d$** in the cost function — examiners specifically check this since the original Borenstein paper emphasises it.
> 5. **Omitting the active window** — describing a global polar histogram will be marked as conceptually wrong; the window is the *defining* feature of VFH.
> 6. **Drawing VFF vectors instead of VFH histogram** in diagrams — instant **2-mark penalty** for mislabelled concept.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **VFH = Real-time, reactive, 2-D → 1-D compression algorithm** for mobile robot obstacle avoidance.
- **Three stages:** Certainty Grid Update → Polar Histogram Construction → Smoothing + Valley-Based Steering.
- **Key formula set:** $C^{*} = C + \Delta C(1-C)$, $\beta = \text{atan2}(y_c, x_c)$, $m = C^2(a - d)$, $H_k = \sum m$, $H'_k = $ smoothed, $g = \mu_1 \Delta\theta + \mu_2 \Delta P + \mu_3 \Delta d$.
- **Default parameters:** $\alpha = 5°$, $n = 72$, $s = 2$, $\mu_1 = 5, \mu_2 = 2, \mu_3 = 1$.
- **Valleys = contiguous sectors with $H'_k < H_t$** and width ≥ robot footprint.
- **Steering = $\arg\min_c g(c)$** evaluated at valley centres.
- **VFH+ adds:** Robot width compensation, look-ahead threshold, and cost-look-ahead.
- **VFH* adds:** A* global planner wrapped around VFH+ for optimality.
- **Advantages:** O(1) decision, low memory, real-time on 8-bit MCUs, robust to noise after smoothing.
- **Disadvantages:** Local minima possible, no global optimality, no map persistence.
- **Real-world users:** Kiva/Amazon warehouse robots, Roomba (early models), PackBot military robots.
- **Originators:** **Borenstein & Koren, 1991** — IEEE Transactions on Robotics and Automation.
- **Originators of evolution:** Ulrich & Borenstein (VFH+, 1998) and Ulrich & Borenstein (VFH*, 2000).
- **Exam mantra:** *"Grid → Histogram → Valley → Cost → Steer."* Memorise this 5-word chain.
- **Smoothing is mandatory**; without it, sonar noise creates phantom valleys.
- **Active window $W_s$** is the *physical* embodiment of "look-ahead distance."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bug 1 and 2 and Tangent Bug Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bug 1 and 2 and Tangent Bug Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Bug Algorithms?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Bug algorithms are a class of reactive, sensor-based local path planning algorithms that guide a mobile robot from a start position to a goal position by directly using sensory feedback about the environment. The robot either moves toward the goal (motion-to-goal) or follows obstacle boundaries (boundary-following) based on its current sensor measurements, without requiring a prior global map.*

The Bug family of algorithms belongs to the **Bug family of sensor-based planners** introduced by **Vladimir Lumelsky and Alexander Stepanov in 1987**. They are classified as *complete* algorithms in the sense that they are guaranteed to reach the goal (or report failure) given a *finite* number of obstacles, assuming perfect sensing and a point-sized robot.

| Algorithm | Year | Sensor Type | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bug 1 | 1987 | Tactile (Contact) | Exhaustive boundary exploration |
| Bug 2 | 1987 | Tactile (Contact) | M-line based boundary following |
| Tangent Bug | 1998 | Range (Distance) | Continuous distance minimization |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Maze Walker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: "Walking in a Dark Room"**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in an unfamiliar room and you must reach a *known* point (a glowing light you can feel the warmth of). You walk straight toward it. The instant your outstretched hand touches a wall, you stop and begin following the wall with your right hand, keeping it gently touching the surface. As you walk along the wall, you keep "feeling" the warmth of the light.
> 
> - **Bug 1** = You walk around the *entire* wall, remembering the point where the warmth was strongest, then return there and walk toward the light again.
> - **Bug 2** = You keep your hand on the wall *only until* you are directly across from the light on the original line you would have taken. Then you let go and head for the light.
> - **Tangent Bug** = You have a *ruler* that can tell you the exact distance to the wall in any direction. You use this ruler to always move toward the closest part of the wall, achieving the most efficient path.

This **reactive, feedback-driven** nature is what distinguishes Bug algorithms from deliberative planners (A*, D*), which require prior environmental knowledge.

---

## 1.3 The Foundational Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Assumptions for Bug Algorithm Analysis (KTU Board Examiner's Expectation):**
> 
> 1. The robot is modeled as a **point** in a **2D planar workspace**.
> 2. The workspace is **bounded** and contains a **finite** number of obstacles.
> 3. Each obstacle is **simply connected** and has a **piecewise-analytic boundary**.
> 4. The robot is capable of **perfect localization** (knows its own position at all times).
> 5. The robot has either **contact sensors** (Bug 1, Bug 2) or **omnidirectional range sensors** (Tangent Bug).
> 6. The goal position is **known** in global coordinates.

---

## 1.4 The M-Line — A Critical Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition: M-Line (Motion Line)**
> The **M-line** (or **start-goal line**) is the straight line segment connecting the robot's **start position** $q_{start}$ to the **goal position** $q_{goal}$. This imaginary line plays a central role in both Bug 1 and Bug 2, as it represents the *shortest possible path* in a completely obstacle-free world.

Mathematically, the M-line is the geometric locus:

$$M\text{-line} = \{(1-\lambda)\cdot q_{start} + \lambda\cdot q_{goal} \mid \lambda \in [0, 1]\}$$

The M-line serves as the *reference geometry* against which the robot measures progress and decides when to "leave" an obstacle's boundary.

---

## 1.5 Why are Bug Algorithms Important in Mobile Robotics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> 1. **Simplicity** — Implementable on low-cost microcontrollers with minimal memory.
> 2. **Completeness** — Guaranteed to find a solution (if one exists) without a pre-built map.
> 3. **Real-time operation** — Decisions are made in O(1) per sensor update.
> 4. **Robustness** — Can handle dynamically changing environments when combined with reactive control.
> 5. **Benchmark standard** — Used to compare against more advanced planners in research.

In KTU's industry-aligned curriculum, Bug algorithms form the **conceptual bridge** between classical AI search (A*, Dijkstra) and modern reactive control (DWA, MPC).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Common State Machine Framework

All three Bug algorithms operate on a **two-state core** that toggles based on environmental feedback:

> [!NOTE]
> **State 0 — Motion-to-Goal (MTG):**
> The robot moves along a straight line toward the goal. It continues until either:
> - It **reaches the goal** → SUCCESS (terminate)
> - It **detects an obstacle** → Transition to State 1 (boundary following)
> 
> **State 1 — Boundary-Following (BF):**
> The robot moves along the obstacle's perimeter in a predetermined direction (typically right-hand rule). It continues until a *leave condition* is met → Transition to State 0.

The **critical difference** between Bug 1, Bug 2, and Tangent Bug lies in the *leave condition* evaluated while in State 1.

---

## 2.2 BUG 1 Algorithm — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bug 1 — Complete Algorithm Description:**
> 1. From start, head straight toward the goal. This defines the **m-line**.
> 2. Upon hitting an obstacle at point $H_i$ (the **hit point**), record the location.
> 3. Switch to boundary-following mode (typically clockwise or counter-clockwise — let us adopt **clockwise**).
> 4. Traverse the *entire* obstacle boundary back to the hit point.
> 5. As you traverse, continuously record the point $L_i$ that is *closest to the goal* (minimum Euclidean distance to $q_{goal}$). This is the **leave point candidate**.
> 6. After completing the full loop, return to the recorded leave point $L_i$ via the shortest boundary arc.
> 7. At $L_i$, switch back to motion-to-goal.
> 8. Repeat until the goal is reached.

### 2.2.2 Leave Condition for Bug 1

$$\text{Leave Bug 1} \iff \text{Robot has returned to } H_i \text{ and finished a complete loop}$$

The leave point $L_i$ is defined as:

$$L_i = \arg\min_{q \in \partial O_i} \;\vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2$$

where $\partial O_i$ is the boundary of the $i$-th encountered obstacle.

### 2.2.3 Performance Bound

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Result (Lumelsky-Stepanov 1987):**
> The path length produced by Bug 1, denoted $L_{Bug1}$, is bounded by:
> $$L_{Bug1} \leq D + \sum_{i=1}^{N} 1.5 \cdot P_i$$
> where:
> - $D$ = straight-line distance from start to goal $= \vert\vert q_{start} - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2$
> - $P_i$ = perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle
> - $N$ = total number of obstacles encountered

The factor of **1.5** arises because the worst case requires traversing half the perimeter to find $L_i$, returning to $H_i$ (half perimeter), and then to $L_i$ (half perimeter).

### 2.2.4 Strengths & Weaknesses

| Aspect | Assessment |
|---|---|
| Simplicity | ★★★★★ |
| Optimality | ★☆☆☆☆ (often produces long detours) |
| Sensor requirement | Tactile only |
| Completeness | Guaranteed |
| Anytime behavior | Poor (must finish full loop) |

---

## 2.3 BUG 2 Algorithm — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bug 2 — Complete Algorithm Description:**
> 1. From start, head straight toward the goal along the **m-line**.
> 2. Upon hitting an obstacle at point $H_i$, switch to boundary-following.
> 3. While following the boundary, continuously check whether the robot has **re-crossed the m-line** at a point $q$ that is *closer to the goal* than $H_i$.
> 4. If yes, this is the **leave point** $L_i$ — immediately switch to motion-to-goal.
> 5. If no re-crossing yields a closer point, follow the entire boundary until returning to $H_i$. If even after a full loop no qualifying point is found, the goal is **unreachable** → FAILURE.

### 2.3.2 Leave Condition for Bug 2

$$\text{Leave Bug 2} \iff \text{Intersection with m-line found at } q \text{ where } d(q, q_{goal}) < d(H_i, q_{goal})$$

Equivalently, if we parameterize the m-line as $q(\lambda) = (1-\lambda)q_{start} + \lambda q_{goal}$ for $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$d(q, q_{goal}) < d(H_i, q_{goal}) \iff \lambda(q) > \lambda(H_i)$$

### 2.3.3 Performance Bound

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:**
> The path length of Bug 2 is bounded by:
> $$L_{Bug2} \leq D + \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \cdot \frac{P_i}{2}$$
> where $n_i$ is the number of times the robot crosses the m-line on the boundary of obstacle $i$ (worst case $n_i = 1$ in simple environments, but can be higher in cluttered spaces).

In the best case (single intersection, single obstacle), Bug 2 produces a path **at most half the obstacle's perimeter longer** than the optimal path.

### 2.3.4 Strengths & Weaknesses

| Aspect | Assessment |
|---|---|
| Simplicity | ★★★★★ |
| Optimality | ★★★☆☆ (better than Bug 1) |
| Sensor requirement | Tactile only |
| Completeness | Guaranteed |
| Robustness | ★★★☆☆ (can fail in m-loop traps) |

---

## 2.4 TANGENT BUG Algorithm — Detailed Analysis

### 2.4.1 Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tangent Bug — Complete Algorithm Description (Kamon, Rimon, Rivlin, 1998):**
> Unlike Bug 1 and Bug 2, the **Tangent Bug** uses **range sensors** that can perceive obstacle geometry at a *distance*. The algorithm maintains a continuous function $d_{reach}(\theta)$ representing the distance to the obstacle in direction $\theta$.
> 
> 1. **Motion-to-Goal State:**
>    - Compute the heading that **minimizes the distance to the goal** among all directions that are not blocked by obstacles.
>    - If a direction is found, move along it. Otherwise, transition to boundary-following.
> 2. **Boundary-Following State:**
>    - Follow the obstacle boundary in the direction that locally **reduces $d(q, q_{goal})$** the fastest.
>    - This corresponds to following along the **visible boundary** until a *local minimum* of $d(q, q_{goal})$ is detected.

### 2.4.2 The Key Distance Function

> [!NOTE]
> **Tangent Bug's $d_{reach}$ Function:**
> For a robot at position $q$ with heading $\theta$, define:
> $$d_{reach}(\theta) = \begin{cases} d(q + v \cdot R(\theta), q_{goal}) & \text{if no obstacle in direction } \theta \text{ within range } R \\ d(q + v \cdot R(\theta), q_{goal}) & \text{where } v \text{ is the first obstacle point hit along ray } \theta \end{cases}$$
> 
> The algorithm continuously minimizes $d_{reach}(\theta)$ over all $\theta$ to determine the optimal local direction.

### 2.4.3 Leave Condition for Tangent Bug

$$\text{Leave Tangent Bug} \iff d_{reach}(\theta_{min}) < d(q, q_{goal})$$

That is, the robot can "see" a direction $\theta$ along which, when it moves, it gets **strictly closer** to the goal than it currently is.

### 2.4.4 Performance Bound

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:**
> The Tangent Bug's path length is bounded by:
> $$L_{Tangent} \leq D + \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$$
> This is **tighter** than Bug 1's $1.5 P_i$ bound because Tangent Bug uses *anticipatory* information from range sensors.

### 2.4.5 The M-Loop Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition: M-Loop**
> An **m-loop** occurs when the robot's boundary-following trajectory **loops back** and crosses the m-line at a point *farther* from the goal than $H_i$ before it can find a closer intersection. Both Bug 2 and Tangent Bug can be trapped in m-loops in pathological environments, but Tangent Bug's range-sensing makes it more resilient.

---

## 2.5 Comprehensive KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the most-likely-asked equations in KTU ESE.**

| **Concept** | **Formula / Condition** | **Variables** | **KTU Marks Weightage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| M-line parameterization | $q(\lambda) = (1-\lambda)q_{start} + \lambda q_{goal}$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | 2 marks |
| Straight-line distance | $D = \vert\vert q_{start} - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2$ | Euclidean norm | 2 marks |
| Bug 1 leave point | $L_i = \arg\min_{q \in \partial O_i} \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2$ | $\partial O_i$ = obstacle boundary | 3 marks |
| Bug 1 path bound | $L_{Bug1} \leq D + 1.5 \sum P_i$ | $P_i$ = perimeter | 3 marks |
| Bug 2 leave condition | $d(L_i, q_{goal}) < d(H_i, q_{goal})$ | $H_i$ = hit point | 4 marks |
| Bug 2 path bound | $L_{Bug2} \leq D + 0.5 \sum n_i P_i$ | $n_i$ = crossings | 3 marks |
| Tangent Bug $d_{reach}$ | $d_{reach}(\theta) = d(q + R \cdot u_\theta, q_{goal})$ | $u_\theta$ = unit direction | 4 marks |
| Tangent Bug path bound | $L_{Tangent} \leq D + \sum P_i$ | Range sensor bound | 3 marks |
| Tangent Bug leave | $d_{reach}(\theta_{min}) < d(q, q_{goal})$ | Local minimum condition | 4 marks |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Use Cases of Bug Algorithms:**
> 1. **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) navigation** in warehouses with sparse, dynamic obstacles.
> 2. **Floor-cleaning robots** (e.g., early-generation Roomba variants) — when mapping fails, fall back to Bug-like behavior.
> 3. **Search and rescue robots** in unknown, GPS-denied environments.
> 4. **Planetary rovers** (NASA's Mars rovers use reactive planners inspired by Tangent Bug).
> 5. **Agricultural robots** navigating between crop rows with intermittent obstacles.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bug 1's Path Length Bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the upper bound $L_{Bug1} \leq D + 1.5 \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$ from first principles.

**Step 1: Decompose the total path into segments.**

The path $\gamma$ produced by Bug 1 consists of:
- $N$ straight-line segments (motion-to-goal phases) of lengths $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N$
- $N$ boundary-following segments of lengths $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_N$

$$L_{Bug1} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i$$

**Step 2: Bound the sum of straight-line segments.**

The straight-line segments must connect the start, all leave points $L_i$, and the goal. By the triangle inequality applied repeatedly:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i \leq \vert\vert q_{start} - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert\vert L_i - L_{i+1} \vert\vert_2$$

But since $L_i$ is the *closest point to goal* on the $i$-th obstacle boundary, $\vert\vert L_i - L_{i+1} \vert\vert_2$ is bounded by the path through obstacles. In the *loosest* bound:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i \leq D + \text{(path connecting } L_i \text{ via obstacles)}$$

**Step 3: Bound each boundary-following segment.**

For the $i$-th obstacle, the worst case is:
- Traverse from $H_i$ to $L_i$ along boundary: $\leq P_i / 2$
- Complete the full loop to return to $H_i$: exactly $P_i$ (full perimeter)
- Re-traverse from $H_i$ to $L_i$ along the shorter arc: $\leq P_i / 2$

$$b_i \leq \frac{P_i}{2} + P_i = \frac{3 P_i}{2}$$

Wait — careful. Bug 1 actually follows the *entire boundary once*, identifies $L_i$, then returns to $L_i$. The total traversal is:

$$b_i = \underbrace{P_i}_{\text{full loop}} + \underbrace{\text{shorter arc from } H_i \text{ to } L_i}_{\leq P_i / 2} - \underbrace{(\text{progress during first loop})}_{=0 \text{ on return}}$$

**Step 4: Final assembly.**

The boundary traversal in the worst case is:

$$b_i \leq P_i + \frac{P_i}{2} = \frac{3 P_i}{2}$$

The straight-line segments in the *worst* case (where the goal-direction is completely blocked except for a single re-entry point) are bounded by $D$ plus the distances between successive leave points, which are themselves bounded by the obstacle crossings. Combining:

$$L_{Bug1} \leq D + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{3 P_i}{2} = D + 1.5 \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$$

$$\boxed{L_{Bug1} \leq D + 1.5 \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **QED: The factor 1.5 = 3/2 arises from "one full perimeter + one half-perimeter return."**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Bug 2's Path Length Bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the upper bound $L_{Bug2} \leq D + 0.5 \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i P_i$.

**Step 1: Identify the worst-case m-line crossings.**

For each obstacle $i$, the robot may cross the m-line $n_i$ times while following the boundary. The **leave point** is the first crossing where $d(q, q_{goal}) < d(H_i, q_{goal})$.

**Step 2: Bound the boundary-following length for obstacle $i$.**

The robot begins at $H_i$ and ends at $L_i$ (the chosen leave point). In the worst case, $L_i$ is reached after traversing the *entire boundary*:

$$b_i \leq n_i \cdot \frac{P_i}{2}$$

The factor of $n_i$ accounts for the worst case where the robot makes $n_i$ "passes" around the obstacle before finding an acceptable leave point. The factor of $1/2$ comes from the fact that *on average*, the leave point lies within half a perimeter traversal.

**Step 3: Sum the boundary lengths.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i \leq \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \cdot \frac{P_i}{2}$$

**Step 4: Combine with the motion-to-goal segments.**

The straight-line motion-to-goal segments, by the triangle inequality and the fact that each $L_i$ is on the m-line with $d(L_i, q_{goal}) < d(H_i, q_{goal})$, sum to at most:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i \leq D + \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} (d(H_{i+1}, q_{goal}) - d(L_i, q_{goal}))$$

In the worst case, this telescopes to $D + \text{boundary related terms}$, but the **dominant** term is the boundary:

$$\boxed{L_{Bug2} \leq D + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i P_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **QED: Bug 2 saves up to 2/3 of Bug 1's detour in environments with convex or near-convex obstacles (n_i = 1).**

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Tangent Bug's Reachability Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Show that Tangent Bug can always reach a *local minimum* of $d(\cdot, q_{goal})$ along the obstacle boundary.

**Step 1: Setup the distance function along the boundary.**

Let $\gamma : [0, T] \to \partial O$ be a parameterization of the obstacle boundary that the robot follows. Define:

$$f(t) = \vert\vert \gamma(t) - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2$$

**Step 2: Show that $f(t)$ achieves a minimum on a closed boundary.**

Since $\partial O$ is **compact** (closed and bounded) and $f$ is **continuous**, by the **Extreme Value Theorem**:

$$\exists\; t^* \in [0, T] : f(t^*) = \min_{t \in [0,T]} f(t)$$

**Step 3: Characterize the leave condition.**

At the local minimum $t^*$, the gradient of $f$ projected onto the boundary is zero. The Tangent Bug detects this when the **$d_{reach}$ function** evaluated along the visible boundary becomes greater than $f(t^*)$:

$$d_{reach}(\theta) > f(t^*) \quad \forall \theta \text{ visible}$$

Equivalently, **no direction $\theta$ offers a path that gets closer to the goal than the current boundary-following point.** This is the **stopping condition** for the boundary-following state.

**Step 4: Show completeness under the bounded range assumption.**

Under the assumption that the range sensor has maximum range $R$, and all obstacles are detected within $R$:

$$L_{Tangent} \leq D + \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$$

The factor of 1 (vs. 1.5 in Bug 1) is due to the **anticipatory boundary following** enabled by range sensing.

$$\boxed{L_{Tangent} \leq D + \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Bug 1 in a Rectangular Environment

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A point robot starts at $q_{start} = (0, 0)$ and must reach $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$. There is one rectangular obstacle with vertices at $(4, -1), (6, -1), (6, 1), (4, 1)$ (width 2, height 2). Compute the Bug 1 path length.

**Step 1: Compute the straight-line distance.**

$$D = \sqrt{(10-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 10$$

**Step 2: Identify the hit point.**

The robot moves along the x-axis (y = 0) toward (10, 0). The obstacle spans $x \in [4, 6]$. The first contact is at:

$$H_1 = (4, 0)$$

**Step 3: Compute the obstacle perimeter.**

$$P_1 = 2 \times (2 + 2) = 8$$

**Step 4: Identify the leave point (closest to goal on boundary).**

Among the four vertices, the one closest to (10, 0) is (6, 0) — but wait, (6, 0) is the rightmost point of the obstacle *on* the x-axis. Distance from (10, 0) to (6, 0) is **4**.

Other vertices: $(4, 1), (4, -1), (6, 1), (6, -1)$ all have distance $\sqrt{16 + 1} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.12$.

So $L_1 = (6, 0)$.

**Step 5: Compute the actual path length under Bug 1.**

- Motion-to-goal from (0,0) to $H_1$ = (4,0): length **4**
- Full boundary loop from (4,0) back to (4,0): length **8**
- Shortest arc from (4,0) to (6,0) along boundary: half-perimeter = **4**

$$L_{Bug1} = 4 + 8 + 4 = 16$$

**Step 6: Verify against the bound.**

$$L_{Bug1} \leq D + 1.5 P_1 = 10 + 1.5 \times 8 = 10 + 12 = 22$$

The actual length 16 satisfies this bound. ✓

**Step 7: Compute the Bug 2 path length for comparison.**

Bug 2 leaves at the first m-line crossing closer to goal. The robot follows the boundary from (4, 0). After traversing, it crosses the m-line at (6, 0) which is at distance 4 from goal — closer than $H_1$ at distance 6. **Leave point = (6, 0).**

- Motion-to-goal (0,0) → (4,0): length **4**
- Boundary from (4,0) to (6,0) (right-hand side of rectangle): traverse the lower edge (2) + right edge (2) + upper edge (2) = **6**
- Motion-to-goal (6,0) → (10,0): length **4**

$$L_{Bug2} = 4 + 6 + 4 = 14$$

**Comparison:** Bug 1 produces path 16, Bug 2 produces path 14. Bug 2 is **12.5% more efficient** in this example.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Tangent Bug's $d_{reach}$ Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A robot at $q = (0, 0)$ has a range sensor with $R = 5$ and detects an obstacle edge at angle $\theta = 30°$ at distance 4. The goal is at $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$. Compute $d_{reach}(30°)$.

**Step 1: Compute the obstacle contact point along the ray $\theta = 30°$.**

$$q_{obs} = q + R_{contact} \cdot (\cos\theta, \sin\theta) = (0,0) + 4 \cdot (\cos 30°, \sin 30°)$$

$$q_{obs} = (4 \times 0.866, 4 \times 0.5) = (3.464, 2.0)$$

**Step 2: Compute $d_{reach}(30°)$.**

$$d_{reach}(30°) = \vert\vert q_{obs} - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2 = \sqrt{(10 - 3.464)^2 + (0 - 2.0)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(6.536)^2 + (2.0)^2} = \sqrt{42.72 + 4.0} = \sqrt{46.72}$$

$$d_{reach}(30°) \approx 6.835$$

**Step 3: Compare to current distance to goal.**

$$d(q, q_{goal}) = \vert\vert (0,0) - (10,0) \vert\vert_2 = 10$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

Since $d_{reach}(30°) = 6.835 < 10 = d(q, q_{goal})$, the leave condition is **satisfied** — the robot should transition to motion-to-goal in the direction $30°$.

> [!NOTE]
> **This example illustrates how Tangent Bug uses *anticipatory* sensing to choose a path that gets closer to the goal even if it cannot see the entire way.**
