# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Machine — Bug 1 Algorithm

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MTG : Start
    MTG : Motion-to-Goal
    MTG --> BF : Hit obstacle at H_i
    MTG --> GOAL : Reached q_goal
    BF : Boundary-Following
    BF --> BF : Traverse boundary, track L_i (min d to goal)
    BF --> RECORD : Complete full loop, return to H_i
    RECORD : Record closest point L_i
    RECORD --> MTG : Resume motion-to-goal from L_i
    BF --> GOAL : Reached q_goal during boundary
    MTG --> MTG : Move toward goal (no obstacle)
    GOAL : Goal Reached
    GOAL --> [*] : Terminate SUCCESS
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid State Machine — Bug 2 Algorithm

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MTG : Start, define m-line
    MTG : Motion-to-Goal
    MTG --> BF : Hit obstacle at H_i on m-line
    MTG --> GOAL : Reached q_goal
    BF : Boundary-Following
    BF --> CHECK : Test m-line intersection
    CHECK : d(q, q_goal) less than d(H_i, q_goal)?
    CHECK --> LEAVE : YES
    CHECK --> BF : NO, continue following
    BF --> CHECK : Continue traversal
    LEAVE : Transition to MTG
    LEAVE --> MTG : Resume motion-to-goal
    BF --> FAIL : Full loop, no closer m-line crossing
    FAIL : Goal Unreachable
    FAIL --> [*] : Terminate FAILURE
    GOAL : Goal Reached
    GOAL --> [*] : Terminate SUCCESS
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Machine — Tangent Bug Algorithm

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MTG : Start, scan 360 degrees
    MTG : Motion-to-Goal
    MTG --> MTG : Move along theta_min that minimizes d_reach
    MTG --> BF : All directions blocked OR local minimum
    MTG --> GOAL : Reached q_goal
    BF : Boundary-Following
    BF --> SCAN : Update sensor scan
    SCAN : Compute d_reach(theta) for all theta
    SCAN --> LEAVE_CHECK : Find theta_min
    LEAVE_CHECK : d_reach(theta_min) less than d(q, q_goal)?
    LEAVE_CHECK --> MTG : YES, leave obstacle
    LEAVE_CHECK --> BF : NO, continue along boundary
    BF --> SCAN : Continuous scan during motion
    MTG --> MTG : Sensor update, recompute theta_min
    GOAL : Goal Reached
    GOAL --> [*] : Terminate SUCCESS
```

## PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **KTU University Exam - Dec 2023** | [Model Q1]

**Q1.1.** *Differentiate between Bug 1 and Bug 2 algorithms in terms of the leave condition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Bug 1 Algorithm (1.5 marks):**
> The robot follows the *entire* obstacle boundary upon hitting an obstacle. After completing the full loop and returning to the hit point $H_i$, it identifies the point $L_i$ on the boundary that is *closest to the goal*. The robot then returns to $L_i$ and resumes motion-to-goal.
> 
> **Bug 2 Algorithm (1.5 marks):**
> The robot follows the obstacle boundary *only* until it crosses the m-line at a point $q$ such that $d(q, q_{goal}) < d(H_i, q_{goal})$. At this point, it immediately resumes motion-to-goal. Bug 2 does not need to complete the full loop.
> 
> **Key Distinction:** Bug 1 requires a complete boundary traversal before deciding to leave; Bug 2 leaves as soon as a better m-line crossing is found. Consequently, Bug 2 is more efficient but may be trapped in m-loops.

---

> **KTU University Exam - May 2024** | [Model Q2]

**Q1.2.** *What is the M-line in Bug algorithms? State its significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The M-line (Motion line) is the imaginary straight line segment connecting the start position $q_{start}$ to the goal position $q_{goal}$ in the robot's workspace.
> 
> **Mathematical Form (1 mark):** $q(\lambda) = (1-\lambda)q_{start} + \lambda q_{goal}$ for $\lambda \in [0,1]$.
> 
> **Significance (1 mark):** The M-line serves as the *reference geometry* for Bug 2's leave condition — the robot leaves an obstacle when it re-crosses the M-line at a point closer to the goal than its entry point. The M-line also represents the *shortest possible obstacle-free path* in an empty workspace.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> **KTU University Exam - Dec 2023** | Module 3 | 14 Marks

**Q.A.(a)** *Explain the Bug 1 algorithm in detail with its state diagram. Derive the upper bound on its path length.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Algorithm Description (3 marks):**
Bug 1 is a tactile-sensor-based, reactive path planning algorithm by Lumelsky and Stepanov (1987). The robot operates in two states:

- **State 0 — Motion-to-Goal (MTG):** The robot moves in a straight line from $q_{start}$ toward $q_{goal}$. If an obstacle is encountered, the robot records the **hit point** $H_i$ and transitions to State 1.
- **State 1 — Boundary-Following:** The robot follows the obstacle boundary in a fixed direction (e.g., clockwise) using its contact sensor. While following, it continuously records the point $L_i$ that minimizes the distance to the goal: $L_i = \arg\min_{q \in \partial O_i} \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2$. After completing the full loop and returning to $H_i$, the robot traverses the shortest boundary arc back to $L_i$ and transitions to State 0.

**(ii) State Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MTG
    MTG : Motion-to-Goal
    MTG --> BF : Hit obstacle
    BF : Boundary-Following
    BF --> BF : Track min distance point
    BF --> MTG : Return to L_i after full loop
    MTG --> GOAL : Reach q_goal
    GOAL --> [*]
```

**(iii) Path Length Derivation (2 marks):**

$$L_{Bug1} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i$$

For the boundary component, the robot must traverse the *entire* perimeter $P_i$ to identify $L_i$, and then return to $L_i$ from $H_i$ via the shorter arc, which in the worst case is $P_i / 2$:

$$b_i \leq P_i + \frac{P_i}{2} = 1.5 P_i$$

For the straight-line component, by the triangle inequality:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i \leq D + \text{intermediate boundary effects}$$

Combining:

$$\boxed{L_{Bug1} \leq D + 1.5 \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i}$$

---

**Q.A.(b)** *A point robot starts at (0,0) and must reach (12, 0). A circular obstacle of radius 2 is centered at (6, 0). Compute the path length produced by Bug 1. Compare with Bug 2.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Compute the straight-line distance (1 mark):**

$$D = \sqrt{(12-0)^2 + 0^2} = 12$$

**(ii) Compute the perimeter and hit point (1 mark):**

$$P_1 = 2\pi r = 2\pi (2) = 4\pi \approx 12.566$$

The robot travels along the x-axis. The circle intersects the x-axis at $(6-2, 0) = (4, 0)$ and $(6+2, 0) = (8, 0)$. The first contact is at:

$$H_1 = (4, 0)$$

**(iii) Bug 1 path length (3 marks):**

- **Motion-to-goal phase 1:** (0,0) → (4,0), length = 4
- **Full boundary loop:** (4,0) → (8,0) around the circle, length = $P_1 = 4\pi$
- **Shortest arc from H₁ to L₁:** The leave point is the point on the circle closest to (12, 0), which is $(8, 0)$ (rightmost point of the circle). The arc from (4,0) to (8,0) over the top is half the perimeter = $2\pi$.
- **Motion-to-goal phase 2:** (8,0) → (12,0), length = 4

$$L_{Bug1} = 4 + 4\pi + 2\pi + 4 = 8 + 6\pi \approx 8 + 18.85 = 26.85$$

**(iv) Bug 2 path length (2 marks):**

- **Motion-to-goal phase 1:** (0,0) → (4,0), length = 4
- **Boundary following:** The m-line is the x-axis. The robot follows the boundary from (4,0). It first crosses the m-line again at (8,0). Since $d((8,0), (12,0)) = 4 < d((4,0), (12,0)) = 8$, this is the leave point.
- **Boundary arc from (4,0) to (8,0):** The robot traverses the upper half of the circle (assuming clockwise), length = $2\pi$.
- **Motion-to-goal phase 2:** (8,0) → (12,0), length = 4

$$L_{Bug2} = 4 + 2\pi + 4 = 8 + 2\pi \approx 8 + 6.28 = 14.28$$

**(v) Comparison:**

$$L_{Bug1} - L_{Bug2} = 4\pi \approx 12.57$$

Bug 2 produces a path **46.8% shorter** than Bug 1 in this circular obstacle scenario. Bug 2 saves the full-perimeter traversal by leveraging the m-line re-crossing.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

> **KTU University Exam - May 2024** | Module 3 | 14 Marks

**Q.B.(a)** *Explain the Tangent Bug algorithm in detail. How does it differ from Bug 2 in terms of sensor requirements and performance?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tangent Bug Overview (2 marks):**

The Tangent Bug, proposed by Kamon, Rimon, and Rivlin (1998), is an extension of Bug 2 that uses **omnidirectional range sensors** instead of tactile sensors. The key innovation is the **$d_{reach}$ function**:

$$d_{reach}(\theta) = d(q + R(\theta) \cdot u_\theta, q_{goal})$$

where $R(\theta)$ is the distance to the nearest obstacle along direction $\theta$, and $u_\theta$ is the unit vector in that direction. If no obstacle is detected in direction $\theta$, $R(\theta)$ is set to the sensor's maximum range.

**(ii) Two States of Tangent Bug (2 marks):**

- **Motion-to-Goal State:** The robot moves along the direction $\theta_{min}$ that minimizes $d_{reach}$. The transition to boundary-following occurs when either (a) all directions are blocked, or (b) the robot detects a local minimum of $d(q, q_{goal})$.
- **Boundary-Following State:** The robot follows the obstacle boundary in the direction that locally *reduces* $d(q, q_{goal})$ the fastest. The leave condition is $d_{reach}(\theta_{min}) < d(q, q_{goal})$.

**(iii) Comparison with Bug 2 (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Bug 2** | **Tangent Bug** |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor | Tactile (contact) | Range (distance) |
| Leave condition | M-line re-crossing | $d_{reach}$ minimization |
| Anticipatory sensing | No (only detects obstacles on contact) | Yes (detects obstacles at distance) |
| Path length bound | $D + 0.5 \sum n_i P_i$ | $D + \sum P_i$ |
| M-loop resistance | Vulnerable | More resilient |
| Implementation cost | Low | Higher |

The Tangent Bug's **anticipatory sensing** allows it to choose *better* leave points and *shorter* boundary segments, even though its theoretical bound has a factor of 1 (vs. 0.5 in Bug 2's formula), in practice it often produces shorter paths because the leave point is more optimally chosen.

---

**Q.B.(b)** *A robot at (0, 0) with a range sensor of maximum range $R = 8$ detects an obstacle at angles $\theta_1 = 0°$ (distance 5) and $\theta_2 = 45°$ (distance 6). The goal is at (15, 0). Determine the direction the Tangent Bug will choose to move, and verify the leave condition.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Compute the obstacle contact points (2 marks):**

For $\theta_1 = 0°$ at distance 5:
$$q_{obs,1} = (0,0) + 5 \cdot (\cos 0°, \sin 0°) = (5, 0)$$

For $\theta_2 = 45°$ at distance 6:
$$q_{obs,2} = (0,0) + 6 \cdot (\cos 45°, \sin 45°) = (6 \times 0.7071, 6 \times 0.7071) = (4.243, 4.243)$$

**(ii) Compute $d_{reach}$ for each direction (2 marks):**

$$d_{reach}(0°) = \vert\vert (5, 0) - (15, 0) \vert\vert_2 = \sqrt{100 + 0} = 10$$

$$d_{reach}(45°) = \vert\vert (4.243, 4.243) - (15, 0) \vert\vert_2 = \sqrt{(10.757)^2 + (4.243)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{115.71 + 18.0} = \sqrt{133.71} \approx 11.56$$

**(iii) Determine the optimal direction (1 mark):**

$$\theta_{min} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \{0°, 45°\}} d_{reach}(\theta) = 0°$$

The robot will move in the direction $\theta = 0°$ (along the positive x-axis), as this gives the smallest $d_{reach}$.

**(iv) Verify the leave condition (2 marks):**

Current distance to goal:
$$d(q, q_{goal}) = \vert\vert (0,0) - (15,0) \vert\vert_2 = 15$$

Since $d_{reach}(0°) = 10 < 15 = d(q, q_{goal})$, the leave condition $d_{reach}(\theta_{min}) < d(q, q_{goal})$ is **satisfied**.

Therefore, the robot should move in the direction $0°$ along the x-axis, toward the obstacle at (5, 0), and upon reaching it, will transition to boundary-following mode.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Bug Algorithms**

### Foundational Concepts
- ✅ Bug algorithms are **reactive, sensor-based local planners** that do NOT require a pre-built map.
- ✅ The robot is modeled as a **point** in a **2D bounded workspace** with **finite** obstacles.
- ✅ All Bug algorithms have a **two-state core**: Motion-to-Goal (MTG) and Boundary-Following (BF).
- ✅ The **M-line** is the straight line from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$, defined as $q(\lambda) = (1-\lambda)q_{start} + \lambda q_{goal}$ for $\lambda \in [0,1]$.
- ✅ Bug 1 and Bug 2 use **tactile sensors**; Tangent Bug uses **range sensors**.

### Bug 1 Essentials
- ✅ **Leave condition:** Complete full boundary loop, return to $H_i$, then go to $L_i$ (closest point to goal).
- ✅ **Leave point:** $L_i = \arg\min_{q \in \partial O_i} \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert_2$.
- ✅ **Path length bound:** $L_{Bug1} \leq D + 1.5 \sum P_i$ (factor 1.5 = full loop + half-perimeter return).
- ✅ **Weakness:** Inefficient — must always complete the full loop.

### Bug 2 Essentials
- ✅ **Leave condition:** M-line re-crossing at point $q$ with $d(q, q_{goal}) < d(H_i, q_{goal})$.
- ✅ **Path length bound:** $L_{Bug2} \leq D + 0.5 \sum n_i P_i$ where $n_i$ = m-line crossings.
- ✅ **Weakness:** M-loops in pathological environments.
- ✅ **Efficiency gain:** Up to 2/3 less detour than Bug 1 in convex obstacle cases.

### Tangent Bug Essentials
- ✅ **Key function:** $d_{reach}(\theta) = d(q + R(\theta) \cdot u_\theta, q_{goal})$.
- ✅ **Leave condition:** $d_{reach}(\theta_{min}) < d(q, q_{goal})$.
- ✅ **Path length bound:** $L_{Tangent} \leq D + \sum P_i$.
- ✅ **Strength:** Anticipatory sensing — sees obstacles at a distance.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Higher sensor cost vs. better path quality.

### Examiner's Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Do NOT confuse the leave conditions across the three algorithms.
- ❌ Do NOT forget the **boundary sensor assumption** (tactile vs. range).
- ❌ Do NOT omit the **constants (1.5, 0.5, 1)** in path length bounds.
- ❌ Do NOT skip the **assumptions** at the start of derivations.
- ❌ Do NOT forget to **draw the M-line** in numerical problems.
- ❌ Do NOT confuse **hit point ($H_i$)** with **leave point ($L_i$)**.

### Memory Aid — The Bug Family Mantra
> **Bug 1 = "Explore Completely"**
> **Bug 2 = "M-Line Master"**
> **Tangent Bug = "Sense Anticipatorily"**

### High-Yield Comparison for KTU Viva / Oral Exams
| **Q** | **Answer** |
|---|---|
| Which is most efficient? | Tangent Bug (in practice) |
| Which is simplest to implement? | Bug 1 |
| Which uses M-line explicitly? | Bug 2 (and Bug 1 implicitly) |
| Which uses range sensors? | Tangent Bug |
| Which is guaranteed complete? | All three (under assumptions) |
| Which is best for a 6-DOF robotic arm? | None directly — Bug algorithms are for *mobile* (locomotion-based) robots |
| What is the worst-case bound for Bug 1? | $D + 1.5 \sum P_i$ |
| What is the worst-case bound for Bug 2? | $D + 0.5 \sum n_i P_i$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, questions on Bug algorithms are typically asked as either:
> 1. A 7-mark "Explain with state diagram" question, OR
> 2. A 7-mark numerical problem involving a specific environment.
> 
> Always **state the assumptions**, **draw the state diagram or M-line**, **show the derivation steps**, and **mention the path length bound with the correct constant factor**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Bug 1, Bug 2, and Tangent Bug Algorithms | KTU PBRAT604 Module 3**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Path Planning - RPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Path Planning - RPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Probabilistic Path Planning (RPP)** is a sampling-based paradigm of motion planning that solves the navigation problem in high-dimensional configuration spaces ($\mathcal{C}$-space) by randomly sampling collision-free configurations and connecting them with simple local planners to form a **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)**. The classical RPP framework (Kavraki, Svestka, Latombe, Overmars, 1994–1996) operates in two decoupled phases:

1. **Learning (Preprocessing) Phase** — A roadmap graph $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ is constructed by stochastically sampling the free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ and retaining only those configurations that are reachable from a small neighborhood of "nearby" samples via a deterministic local planner.
2. **Query Phase** — For any given start $q_{\text{start}}$ and goal $q_{\text{goal}}$, both endpoints are connected into the precomputed roadmap, and a conventional graph search (Dijkstra, A*) retrieves an admissible path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probabilistic Completeness (Kavraki's Theorem):** As the number of samples $n \to \infty$, the probability that RPP returns a feasible path (if one exists) approaches **1.0**. However, RPP provides **no optimality guarantee** — path quality depends purely on sampling density. This is the central trade-off that distinguishes RPP from combinatorial planners.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Cartographer

Imagine a **blindfolded cartographer** dropped into a pitch-black cave system with only a flashlight. He cannot see the whole map, but he is allowed to:

1. **Throw a thousand rubber balls** randomly across the cave. The balls that land on solid rock are discarded; the balls that land in the open tunnels are pinned to the floor.
2. From every pinned ball, he **shines his flashlight in straight lines** to the nearest 5–10 pins. If the beam of light can travel unobstructed (no rock wall in between), he draws a string between them.
3. Once the network of strings is built, **any traveler entering the cave** only needs to be tied to the nearest pin from his entrance point, and from there, he just follows the strings to the exit.

The cartographer never knows the *complete* shape of the cave, but if he throws **enough** balls, the string network will be dense enough to span every reachable region. This is *exactly* the philosophy of RPP — **density replaces omniscience**.

### 1.3 Why RPP Exists — The Motivation

| Classical Limitation | RPP Solution |
|---|---|
| Explicit $\mathcal{C}$-space representation is exponential in DOF | Random sampling reduces complexity to polynomial |
| Cell decomposition fails in narrow passages | Probabilistic sampling is uniform over measure |
| Exact algorithms have super-exponential runtime | RPP is **anytime** — returns best-so-far solution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics in RPP (per KTU 2024 syllabus):**
> - **Voronoi Bias:** Probability of selecting a node is proportional to the volume of its Voronoi cell, ensuring frontier exploration.
> - **Connection Radius** $\mathbf{r}$: Maximum distance for connecting a new sample to existing nodes (typically $r \propto (\log n / n)^{1/d}$).
> - **Path Length Ratio:** Actual path length $\div$ optimal length (heuristic quality metric).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Configuration Space Foundation

A robot with $n$ degrees of freedom is described by a configuration $q \in \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$. The space is partitioned into:

- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ — collision-free configurations
- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ — configurations in collision

RPP samples exclusively from $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ using a **collision checker** $\Delta: \mathcal{C} \to \{\text{FREE}, \text{OBS}\}$.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Learning Phase Steps:**

1. **Sampling Step:** Generate $n$ random configurations $\{q_i\}_{i=1}^{n} \sim U(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$ using a pseudo-random sequence (Halton, Sobol, or PRNG).
2. **Filtering Step:** Discard any $q_i \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ via the collision checker $\Delta(q_i)$.
3. **Neighborhood Step:** For each valid $q_i$, identify the $k$-nearest neighbors (or all samples within radius $r(n)$) — denoted $\mathcal{N}(q_i)$.
4. **Edge Construction Step:** For every $q_j \in \mathcal{N}(q_i)$, invoke the local planner $\text{LP}(q_i, q_j)$:
   - If the local path is collision-free $\Rightarrow$ add edge $(q_i, q_j)$ to $\mathcal{E}$.
5. **Component Connection Step (Lazy PRM / Visibility PRM):** Identify and merge disconnected connected components to enhance connectivity.

**Query Phase Steps:**

1. Connect $q_{\text{start}}$ and $q_{\text{goal}}$ to the roadmap using the same local planner.
2. Run Dijkstra (weighted) or A* (with Euclidean heuristic) on $\mathcal{G}$.
3. Smooth the path using shortcutting or elastic band techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Voronoi Bias Phenomenon:** Because uniform sampling distributes nodes with a probability density proportional to the volume of their Voronoi regions, the nearest neighbor of a random query is *most likely* the node whose Voronoi cell is largest. This is the theoretical foundation of the **RRT (Rapidly Exploring Random Tree)** algorithm, which extends RPP to single-query planning by exploiting the same principle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Interpretation | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Roadmap graph | $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ | Nodes + Edges | Discrete structure |
| 2 | Sample set size | $n$ | Total nodes in roadmap | $\mathbb{Z}^{+}$ |
| 3 | Connection radius (asymptotic optimal) | $r(n) = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$ | $d$ = $\mathcal{C}$-space dimension; $\gamma > \gamma_{\text{PRM}^*}$ | meters/radians |
| 4 | $k$-nearest neighbor count | $k = e \cdot (1 + 1/d) \log n$ | $e$ = Euler's number | dimensionless |
| 5 | Path existence probability | $P(\text{success}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda n}$ | $\lambda$ = clearance-dependent rate | $\in [0,1]$ |
| 6 | Cost of a path | $c(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} \vert\vert q_{i+1} - q_i \vert\vert$ | Sum of Euclidean segment lengths | meters/radians |
| 7 | Voronoi cell volume (expected) | $\mathbb{E}[V_i] = \frac{\text{vol}(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})}{n}$ | Uniform distribution | $\text{vol}^d$ |
| 8 | Local planner validity | $\text{LP}(q_i, q_j) = \bigwedge_{s \in [0,1]} \Delta(s \cdot q_i + (1-s) \cdot q_j)$ | Discrete check on interpolated path | Boolean |
| 9 | Path length ratio (quality) | $\rho = \frac{c(\pi_{\text{RPP}})}{c(\pi^*)}$ | $\pi^*$ = optimal path | $\rho \geq 1$ |
| 10 | Sampling density | $\rho_{\text{sample}} = \frac{n}{\text{vol}(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})}$ | Samples per unit volume | $\text{samples}/\text{vol}^d$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** RPP underpins modern industrial robotics — autonomous vehicles (lane-change planning), warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), surgical robots (da Vinci path planning), and planetary rovers (Mars Curiosity/Pathfinder) all use RPP-derived algorithms. Any engineering field with **high-DOF motion under uncertainty** is a candidate domain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Asymptotic Optimal Connection Radius

The connection radius is the **most critical parameter** in RPP. We derive the minimum $r(n)$ that guarantees asymptotic optimality (as in **PRM\*** by Karaman & Frazzoli, 2011).

**Step 1 — Define the Slender $\varepsilon$-ball Condition:**

For a configuration $q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, consider an $\varepsilon$-ball $\mathcal{B}(q, \varepsilon)$. The path planner is **asymptotically optimal** if it can find paths of cost approaching the optimum as $n \to \infty$. The condition requires the roadmap to cover all such balls densely.

**Step 2 — Apply Covering Theory:**

The number of $r$-balls required to cover $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ of $d$-dimensional volume $V$ scales as:

$$
N_{\text{cover}} = \frac{V}{\text{vol}(\mathcal{B}(0, r))} = \frac{V \cdot \Gamma(\tfrac{d}{2}+1)}{\pi^{d/2} \cdot r^d}
$$

**Step 3 — Require the Roadmap to be a Net:**

For the $n$ sampled nodes to form an $r$-net of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, the maximum empty ball radius must shrink:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[\max_{q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}} \min_{i} \vert\vert q - q_i \vert\vert \right] \to 0
$$

Setting this to $\varepsilon$ and solving via the **coupon collector / extreme value** argument yields:

$$
r(n) = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}
$$

**Step 4 — Threshold for Optimality (Karaman-Frazzoli Condition):**

$$
\gamma > \gamma_{\text{PRM}^*} = 2 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{d} \right)^{1/d} \left( \frac{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})}{\zeta_d} \right)^{1/d}
$$

where $\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$ is the Lebesgue measure of the free space and $\zeta_d$ is the volume of the $d$-dimensional unit ball.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the $\log n$ factor?** Sampling theory (Cover & Efron) shows that for $n$ uniform samples in $d$ dimensions, the *expected* nearest-neighbor distance is $O(n^{-1/d})$. The $\log n$ factor is the **correction term** needed to guarantee that the union of neighborhoods covers the entire space with high probability $1 - O(1/n)$, not just in expectation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Probability of Finding a Path

Assume a feasible path $\pi^*$ exists in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ of length $L$. Let $\pi^*$ be thickened to a $\delta$-tube of volume $V_{\text{tube}}$. A sample lands in the tube with probability $p = V_{\text{tube}} / V_{\text{free}}$.

**Step 1 — Probability that a single sample misses the tube:**

$$
P(\text{miss}) = 1 - p
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that all $n$ samples miss:**

$$
P(\text{all miss}) = (1 - p)^n
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\lim_{x \to 0} (1-x)^n \approx e^{-nx}$:**

$$
P(\text{all miss}) = (1 - p)^n \approx e^{-pn}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of success (at least one sample in the tube):**

$$
P(\text{success}) = 1 - e^{-pn} = 1 - e^{-\lambda n}
$$

where $\lambda = p = V_{\text{tube}} / V_{\text{free}}$.

**Step 5 — Convergence in $n$:**

For $P(\text{success}) \geq 1 - \varepsilon$, we require:

$$
e^{-\lambda n} \leq \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \geq \frac{1}{\lambda} \ln\left( \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even when a path exists, RPP may fail for finite $n$. The number of samples needed grows **only logarithmically** in the failure tolerance $\varepsilon$ — this is the source of the *exponential speedup* over exhaustive enumeration in high dimensions.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voronoi Bias and Frontier Selection

The probability that a new sample $q_{\text{new}}$ has $v_i$ as its nearest existing node equals the volume of $v_i$'s Voronoi region $\text{Vor}(v_i)$:

$$
P(q_{\text{new}} \to v_i) = \frac{\text{vol}(\text{Vor}(v_i))}{\text{vol}(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})}
$$

**Why this is critical for RRT (and for RPP growth):**

A node located on the *boundary* of the current explored region has a larger Voronoi cell (extending into unexplored territory). Therefore, the next sample is most likely to attach to a **frontier node**, which causes the tree/roadmap to expand outward — the **"Rapidly Exploring"** property.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — RPP vs. Classical Planners

| Property | Visibility Graph | Cell Decomposition | Potential Field | **RPP / PRM** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Optimality | Yes (shortest) | Approximate | Local minima prone | **Probabilistic** |
| Completeness | Yes (full) | Resolution-complete | No | **Probabilistically complete** |
| DOF Scalability | O(2^n) | O(k^n) | O(n) | **Polynomial in $n$** |
| Narrow Passage Handling | Poor | Poor | Fails | **Probabilistic (slow)** |
| Anytime | No | No | No | **Yes (Lazy PRM)** |
| Query Reuse | No | No | No | **Yes (multi-query)** |
| Implementation Complexity | High | Very High | Low | **Moderate** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RPP Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef input fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef process fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef decision fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef output fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef storage fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#1A1A1A

    Cfree["C-free Configuration Space"]:::input
    Robot["Robot Geometry and DOF"]:::input
    Map["Static Map / Obstacles"]:::input

    Sampler["Random Sampler - Halton or Sobol"]:::process
    Checker["Collision Checker - Delta q"]:::process
    Filter{"Is q collision free?"}:::decision
    KNN["K-Nearest Neighbour Search"]:::process
    LocalPl["Local Planner - Straight Line"]:::process
    Graph["Roadmap Graph - Vertices and Edges"]:::storage
    Smooth["Path Smoother"]:::process

    Start["q-start"]:::input
    Goal["q-goal"]:::input
    ConnectS{"Connect to Roadmap?"}:::decision
    AStar["A-star or Dijkstra Search"]:::process
    Result["Feasible Path Pi"]:::output

    Cfree --> Sampler
    Map --> Checker
    Sampler --> Checker
    Checker --> Filter
    Filter -->|Yes| KNN
    Filter -->|No| Sampler
    KNN --> LocalPl
    LocalPl -->|Collision Free| Graph
    LocalPl -->|Collision| Filter
    Graph --> ConnectS
    Start --> ConnectS
    Goal --> ConnectS
    ConnectS -->|Yes| AStar
    ConnectS -->|No| Sampler
    AStar --> Smooth
    Smooth --> Result

    Robot -.-> Checker
    Robot -.-> LocalPl
```

### 4.2 RPP Algorithm Phases — Learning vs Query

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef learn fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef query fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef shared fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#1A1A1A

    subgraph L["LEARNING PHASE - Offline Preprocessing"]
        L1["Step 1: Generate n random samples"]:::learn
        L2["Step 2: Collision check each sample"]:::learn
        L3["Step 3: For each valid q find k nearest"]:::learn
        L4["Step 4: Test local connection"]:::learn
        L5["Step 5: Add valid edges to roadmap"]:::learn
        L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    end

    L5 --> R["ROADMAP GRAPH - Reusable Asset"]:::shared

    subgraph Q["QUERY PHASE - Online Per Request"]
        Q1["Step 1: Connect q-start to roadmap"]:::query
        Q2["Step 2: Connect q-goal to roadmap"]:::query
        Q3["Step 3: Run A-star on graph"]:::query
        Q4["Step 4: Smooth output path"]:::query
        R --> Q1
        R --> Q2
        Q1 --> Q3
        Q2 --> Q3
        Q3 --> Q4
    end

    Q4 --> OUT["FINAL PATH - Output to Controller"]:::shared
```

### 4.3 RPP Variant Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef root fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef v1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef v2 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef v3 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef v4 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#1A1A1A

    RPP["Probabilistic Path Planning - RPP"]:::root

    RPP --> PRM["Classic PRM - Multi-Query"]:::v1
    RPP --> RRT["RRT - Single-Query Tree"]:::v1
    RPP --> Lazy["Lazy PRM - Delayed Collision Check"]:::v1
    RPP --> OB["Obstacle-Based PRM - Narrow Passage Boost"]:::v1

    PRM --> PRMS["PRM Star - Asymptotically Optimal"]:::v2
    PRM --> VPRM["Visibility PRM - Guard Nodes Only"]:::v2

    RRT --> RRTS["RRT Star - Optimality Convergence"]:::v3
    RRT --> RRTCon["RRT Connect - Bidirectional Growth"]:::v3
    RRT --> Infor["Informed RRT Star - Heuristic Sampling"]:::v3

    Lazy --> LazyCol["Lazy Collision Checking"]:::v4
    OB --> Gaussian["Gaussian Bridge Test"]:::v4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Green blocks (Learning Phase) execute **once** during preprocessing. Orange blocks (Query Phase) execute **every time** the robot is asked to plan a new path. The Roadmap Graph is the **persistent interface** between the two phases — this is the architectural elegance that makes RPP a *multi-query* planner.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define **Probabilistic Completeness** in the context of RPP. How does it differ from classical completeness?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Probabilistic completeness states that if a feasible path exists in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, the probability that RPP finds it approaches **1** as the number of samples $n \to \infty$. Formally, $P(\text{success} \mid n) \to 1$.
>
> **Difference from classical completeness:** Classical (exact) planners guarantee finding a path *in finite time* (or correctly report infeasibility) for *every* problem instance. Probabilistic completeness only guarantees success *asymptotically* and *in probability* — for any finite $n$, RPP may fail even when a path exists, especially in narrow passages.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the **Voronoi Bias** property of uniform sampling in RPP, and why is it important for the RRT algorithm?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> When samples are drawn uniformly from $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, the probability that a new sample's nearest neighbor is node $v_i$ is proportional to the volume of $v_i$'s **Voronoi cell** $\text{Vor}(v_i)$. Frontier nodes (those on the boundary of the explored region) have disproportionately large Voronoi cells extending into unexplored space.
>
> **Importance for RRT:** This causes the tree to grow preferentially toward unexplored regions, giving RRT its "rapidly exploring" character — the algorithm efficiently biases growth toward the goal rather than expanding uniformly.

---

### 📗 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. They are split into (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the two phases of the Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM) algorithm with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the local planner and the connection radius $r(n)$ in determining path quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the asymptotic expression for the connection radius $r(n) = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$ for a $d$-dimensional configuration space. Show that choosing $\gamma$ below the critical threshold $\gamma_{\text{PRM}^*}$ leads to loss of asymptotic optimality. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Phase 1: Learning Phase (Preprocessing):**
1. Generate $n$ random samples $\{q_i\}$ uniformly in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ using Halton/Sobol sequences.
2. Discard collision samples via the binary collision checker $\Delta(q)$.
3. For each retained $q_i$, identify its $k$-nearest neighbors or all nodes within radius $r(n)$.
4. Invoke the **local planner** $\text{LP}(q_i, q_j)$ — typically a straight-line interpolation checked at discrete resolutions — to verify that the line segment between $q_i$ and $q_j$ lies entirely in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
5. If the segment is valid, add the undirected edge $(q_i, q_j)$ to graph $\mathcal{G}$.

**Phase 2: Query Phase (Online):**
1. Connect $q_{\text{start}}$ and $q_{\text{goal}}$ to $\mathcal{G}$ using the same local planner.
2. Run A* (with Euclidean heuristic) on $\mathcal{G}$ to retrieve a feasible path.
3. Apply post-processing smoothing (e.g., random shortcutting).

**Role of the Local Planner:**
- Determines *what* "close" means geometrically.
- Trade-off: Aggressive planner (large $r$) finds long edges $\Rightarrow$ fewer nodes needed, but increases collision-check cost per edge.
- Conservative planner (small $r$, $k$ fixed) is more robust but produces a denser graph.

**Role of $r(n)$:**
- Controls the **sparsity of the roadmap** and the maximum edge length.
- If $r(n) \sim n^{-1/d}$ (no $\log$): connectivity is fragile — disconnected components arise.
- If $r(n) \sim (\log n / n)^{1/d}$ with $\gamma > \gamma_{\text{PRM}^*}$: roadmap is asymptotically optimal (PRM*).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation:**

Consider $n$ i.i.d. uniform samples in a $d$-dimensional free space of volume $V_{\text{free}}$. The probability that a specific point $q$ is **not covered** by any $r$-ball around the samples is:

$$
P(\text{uncovered}) = \left( 1 - \frac{V_r}{V_{\text{free}}} \right)^n \approx e^{-n V_r / V_{\text{free}}}
$$

where $V_r = \zeta_d r^d$ is the volume of the $r$-ball and $\zeta_d = \pi^{d/2} / \Gamma(d/2 + 1)$.

Using the union bound over a covering net of size $N_{\text{net}} = V_{\text{free}} / V_r$:

$$
P(\text{any uncovered}) \leq N_{\text{net}} \cdot e^{-n V_r / V_{\text{free}}} = \frac{V_{\text{free}}}{V_r} e^{-n V_r / V_{\text{free}}}
$$

Setting $x = n V_r / V_{\text{free}}$ and requiring $P \to 0$:

$$
\frac{V_{\text{free}}}{V_r} e^{-n V_r / V_{\text{free}}} \to 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^x / x \to 0
$$

The minimum $r$ that satisfies this is obtained by setting $x = \log n$:

$$
\frac{n V_r}{V_{\text{free}}} = \log n \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{n \zeta_d r^d}{V_{\text{free}}} = \log n
$$

Solving for $r$:

$$
r^d = \frac{V_{\text{free}} \log n}{n \zeta_d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r(n) = \left( \frac{V_{\text{free}}}{\zeta_d} \right)^{1/d} \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}
$$

Identifying $\gamma = \left( V_{\text{free}} / \zeta_d \right)^{1/d} \cdot \left( 1 + 1/d \right)^{1/d} \cdot 2$, the **PRM\* critical threshold** is:

$$
\gamma_{\text{PRM}^*} = 2 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{d} \right)^{1/d} \left( \frac{V_{\text{free}}}{\zeta_d} \right)^{1/d}
$$

If $\gamma < \gamma_{\text{PRM}^*}$, the $r$-balls do not form a covering net in the limit $n \to \infty$, and the planner loses asymptotic optimality — paths produced have a cost ratio $\rho > 1$ that does not converge to 1.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **PRM** and **RRT** algorithms. Under what conditions is PRM preferred over RRT? Illustrate with a block diagram of the RRT growth procedure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DOF manipulator operates in a 3D workspace of volume $V = 8 \text{ m}^3$ with $V_{\text{free}} = 5 \text{ m}^3$. The path requires clearance of $\delta = 0.05$ m. Compute:
   (i) the number of samples $n$ needed for a success probability of 0.99,
   (ii) the optimal connection radius $r(n)$ using $d = 6$.
   **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | PRM | RRT |
|---|---|---|
| Query type | Multi-query | Single-query |
| Structure | Graph (roadmap) | Tree rooted at $q_{\text{start}}$ |
| Connection direction | Bidirectional | Unidirectional (toward sample) |
| Optimality variant | PRM\* | RRT\* |
| Reusability | Yes | No (one tree per query) |
| Memory | Higher | Lower |

**When PRM is preferred:**
- Multiple queries must be served from the same environment (warehouse robots, CAD verification).
- The roadmap preprocessing cost can be amortized.
- Path optimality is desired (with PRM\*).

**RRT Growth Procedure (Block Diagram):**
- Start: $q_{\text{start}}$ is the tree root.
- Sample: Draw $q_{\text{rand}} \sim U(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$.
- Nearest: Find $q_{\text{near}} = \arg \min_{q \in \mathcal{T}} \vert\vert q - q_{\text{rand}} \vert\vert$.
- Steer: Move from $q_{\text{near}}$ toward $q_{\text{rand}}$ by a fixed step $\varepsilon$ to obtain $q_{\text{new}}$.
- Collision-Check: If $\Delta(q_{\text{new}}) = \text{FREE}$ and the segment $q_{\text{near}} \to q_{\text{new}}$ is collision-free, add $q_{\text{new}}$ to $\mathcal{T}$ with parent $q_{\text{near}}$.
- Repeat until $q_{\text{goal}}$ is reached or $n$ samples are drawn.

**Voronoi Bias ensures:** the tree grows preferentially into unexplored Voronoi cells — yielding rapid frontier coverage.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $d = 6$ (configuration space dimension)
- $V_{\text{free}} = 5 \text{ m}^3$ (in joint space, this is the *measure* in the joint-angle space — treated as effective volume)
- Clearance $\delta = 0.05$ m
- Tube radius approximation: $V_{\text{tube}} \approx \pi \delta^2 \cdot L_{\text{path}}$. For unit-length path, $V_{\text{tube}} \approx \pi (0.05)^2 = 0.00785 \text{ m}^3$.
- Target $P(\text{success}) = 0.99$.

**Step (i) — Number of samples:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{V_{\text{tube}}}{V_{\text{free}}} = \frac{0.00785}{5} = 0.00157
$$

$$
P(\text{success}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda n} = 0.99 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-\lambda n} = 0.01
$$

$$
\lambda n = \ln(100) = 4.605
$$

$$
n = \frac{4.605}{0.00157} = 2933 \text{ samples}
$$

**Step (ii) — Connection radius:**

$$
r(n) = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}, \quad \gamma \approx 2 \left( \frac{V_{\text{free}}}{\zeta_6} \right)^{1/6}
$$

For $d = 6$, $\zeta_6 = \pi^3 / 6 \approx 5.168$. Assuming $\gamma \approx 1.5$ (for scaled joint space):

$$
\log(2933) \approx 7.98
$$

$$
\frac{\log n}{n} = \frac{7.98}{2933} = 0.00272
$$

$$
\left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/6} = (0.00272)^{1/6} \approx 0.397
$$

$$
r(n) \approx 1.5 \times 0.397 \approx 0.596 \text{ (in joint-radians)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\log n$ factor:** Writing $r(n) \propto n^{-1/d}$ instead of $(\log n/n)^{1/d}$ loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing PRM with RRT:** A common error is describing the RRT extension step as a "PRM edge construction" — they are *fundamentally different* (RRT uses a steered extension, PRM uses bidirectional straight-line connection).
> 3. **Ignoring units in $V_{\text{free}}$:** Joint-space "volume" is measured in $\text{rad}^d$, not meters — students often write $V_{\text{free}}$ in $\text{m}^3$ for a manipulator.
> 4. **No smoothing step in query phase:** Examiners explicitly test whether you remember that RPP paths are *graph-optimal*, not *configuration-optimal* — smoothing (shortcutting) is required.
> 5. **Probability mis-formulation:** Writing $P(\text{success}) = 1 - (1-p)$ (single-sample) instead of $1 - (1-p)^n$ (multi-sample) — a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Omitting the collision checker role:** Many students describe the sampling step but forget that every node *and every edge* must be validated by $\Delta$.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **RPP = Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM) family** — sampling-based, probabilistically complete, multi-query capable.
- [x] **Two phases:** Learning (roadmap construction) + Query (graph search + smoothing).
- [x] **Voronoi Bias** is the theoretical pillar that makes uniform sampling "exploratory" — exploited fully by RRT.
- [x] **Connection radius** $r(n) = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$ — the $\log n$ term is **non-negotiable** for optimality.
- [x] **Probabilistic completeness:** $P(\text{success}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda n}$, requires only $O(\log(1/\varepsilon))$ samples.
- [x] **No optimality guarantee in classical RPP** — only in **PRM\*** (asymptotic) and **RRT\*** (anytime).
- [x] **Narrow passage problem** is the Achilles' heel — solutions include **Gaussian Bridge Test** and **Obstacle-Based PRM**.
- [x] **Variants taxonomy:** PRM, PRM\*, RRT, RRT\*, RRT-Connect, Informed-RRT\*, Lazy PRM, Visibility PRM.
- [x] **DOF Scalability:** $O(n)$ samples in $d$ dimensions — polynomial in DOF (vs. exponential for exact methods).
- [x] **Always cite the collision checker $\Delta$** in the algorithm description — examiners test for it.
- [x] **Real-world systems using RPP:** Amazon Kiva AMRs, Mars rovers, autonomous cars, da Vinci surgical robots, humanoid locomotion planners (Atlas, ASIMO).
- [x] **Difference between RPP and RRT** must be crystal clear in the exam — one is *multi-query roadmap*, the other is *single-query tree*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horizontal Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Horizontal Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Horizontal Decomposition** is a classical **cell decomposition** technique used in mobile robot motion planning that subdivides the planar free space $C_{free}$ of a robot's configuration space into a finite set of convex polygonal cells by sweeping a horizontal line across the workspace. As the line sweeps, it stops at every vertex (corner point) of every polygonal obstacle, generating a partition composed of **trapezoidal and triangular cells**.

This method belongs to the family of **exact motion planning algorithms** (also called *combinatorial planning methods*), and is formally introduced in the foundational works on cell decomposition by **Schwartz and Sharir (1983)** and refined by **Latombe (1991)** in the context of algorithmic motion planning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** This topic falls under **Module 4 – Robot Motion Planning** of PBRAT604. It is a high-yield topic for Part B (14-mark) questions and frequently appears in KTU ESE examinations as a sub-part of cell decomposition or roadmap-based path planning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Sliced Loaf of Bread"** 🥖
>
> Imagine you have a rectangular piece of paper (the workspace) with some irregular shapes (obstacles) glued onto it. Now, take a sharp knife and make **horizontal cuts** through the paper. But — and this is the key — you only cut at the *exact heights* where the corners of the obstacles sit. Each cut produces a strip. The obstacle corners act as "anchor points" determining where cuts go.
>
> - If an obstacle has its top-left corner at height $h_1$ and top-right corner at height $h_2$ (different heights), the strip between $h_1$ and $h_2$ will be a **trapezoid** (because the obstacle's edge is slanted).
> - If both corners are at the *same* height, you get a **rectangle**.
> - The narrow pointy bits that taper to zero width become **triangles**.
>
> Each resulting trapezoid/triangle is called a **cell**. A robot can move freely inside a cell, but to move from one cell to another, it must cross the shared boundary. The collection of cells and their shared boundaries forms a **roadmap graph**, and finding a path becomes a simple graph-search problem (like BFS or Dijkstra).

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Sweep Line** | A virtual horizontal line $y = c$ that scans the workspace from $y = -\infty$ to $y = +\infty$ (or top to bottom). |
| **Critical Point / Vertex** | Any corner (vertex) of a polygonal obstacle at coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$. |
| **Cell** | A maximal connected region of free space between two consecutive critical $y$-levels. |
| **Trapezoid** | A cell with two parallel horizontal edges and two non-horizontal (slanted) edges. |
| **Slab** | The horizontal strip between two adjacent $y$-levels of vertices. |
| **Roadmap / Connectivity Graph** | A graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = cells, $E$ = shared boundaries between cells. |
| **Slit** | A vertical edge (or portion thereof) that separates two cells in the same slab. |
| **$C_{free}$** | The free configuration space where the robot can move without collision. |
| **$C_{obs}$** | The obstacle region in the configuration space. |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in the Method

- **Total number of vertices in workspace:** $n$
- **Number of cells generated:** $O(n)$
- **Time complexity of decomposition:** $O(n \log n)$
- **Time complexity of graph search:** $O(n)$ (for BFS/DFS) or $O(n \log n)$ (for $A^*$)
- **Space complexity:** $O(n)$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

The horizontal decomposition algorithm proceeds through the following structured sequence:

1. **Preprocessing — Vertex Extraction:**
   Extract the set of all vertices $V_{obs} = \{(x_i, y_i) : i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ from all polygonal obstacles in the workspace. Each vertex is a *critical event* for the sweep line.

2. **Sorting:**
   Sort all $y$-coordinates of vertices: $y_{(1)} \le y_{(2)} \le \ldots \le y_{(n)}$.

3. **Sweep-Line Construction:**
   Imagine a horizontal line $\ell(y) : Y = c$ that moves from $y = -\infty$ to $y = +\infty$. The line is "frozen" (stops / changes region count) at every $y_{(i)}$.

4. **Slab Generation:**
   Each interval $S_k = [y_{(k)}, y_{(k+1)}]$ (plus outer half-strips $S_0$ and $S_{n}$) is a **slab**. Inside each slab, the obstacle topology *does not change* — only obstacle edges that cross the slab are present.

5. **Trapezoid Decomposition within Each Slab:**
   Within a slab, drop a vertical line (a *slit*) from each vertex that lies on the slab boundary downward, until it hits the next obstacle edge or the slab floor. Each region of free space enclosed is a **trapezoid** (with triangles as degenerate trapezoids).

6. **Cell Labeling:**
   Assign a unique ID $C_j$ to each generated trapezoid.

7. **Connectivity Graph Construction:**
   Build a graph $G = (V_G, E_G)$:
   - $V_G = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ — one node per cell.
   - $E_G = \{(C_i, C_j) : C_i \text{ and } C_j \text{ share a slit of positive length}\}$.

8. **Path Query:**
   Given start configuration $q_{start}$ and goal configuration $q_{goal}$:
   - Locate the cells $C_{start}$ and $C_{goal}$ containing each.
   - Run **BFS / Dijkstra / A$^*$** on $G$.
   - For each consecutive pair of cells in the path, compute a straight-line or "go-through-slit" path segment.

9. **Path Smoothing (Optional):**
   Shortcut the path by removing redundant waypoints.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Deep Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "horizontal" and not "vertical"?**
> The choice of axis is arbitrary. Horizontal decomposition is preferred in scenarios where obstacles are *elongated horizontally* (e.g., corridors, rivers, conveyor belts). For vertically-elongated environments, **vertical decomposition** (the dual method) is used. The algorithmic complexity is identical; only the sweep direction differs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this exact and complete?**
> The sweep line guarantees that *every* cell of $C_{free}$ is visited, and the slit construction guarantees that *every* connectivity between cells is captured. Therefore, if a path exists in $C_{free}$, the roadmap $G$ will contain it — this is the **completeness property**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Concept | Definition / Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of obstacle vertices | $n$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Number of cells | $m = O(n)$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Sorting complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | operations |
| 4 | Sweep + slit complexity | $O(n)$ | operations |
| 5 | Graph search complexity (BFS) | $O(m + \vert E_G \vert) = O(n)$ | operations |
| 6 | Graph search complexity (A$^*$) | $O(n \log n)$ | with admissible heuristic |
| 7 | Total preprocessing time | $O(n \log n)$ | operations |
| 8 | Path query time | $O(n \log n)$ | operations |
| 9 | Path length lower bound | $L^* \le L_{path} \le 2L^*$ | $\le$ **2-approximation** for shortest path in $C_{free}$ |
| 10 | Workspace area | $A = (x_{max} - x_{min})(y_{max} - y_{min})$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 11 | Cell area | $A_{cell} = \frac{1}{2}(b_1 + b_2)h$ | $\text{m}^2$ (trapezoid) |
| 12 | Slit length | $s = y_{next} - y_{current}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 13 | Trapezoid height | $h = y_{(k+1)} - y_{(k)}$ | $\text{m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** Memorize the **$O(n \log n)$ total complexity** — this is the single most asked metric in the 14-mark questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Decompose the floor plan into strips to plan pickup-and-deliver routes for robots like Amazon Kiva.
- **Autonomous cars in parking lots:** Static parked cars form vertices; the parking algorithm sweeps across to identify drivable corridors.
- **Surgical robotics:** Decomposing the abdominal cavity into cells for tool-path planning in laparoscopic surgery.
- **2D Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM):** Tool-path generation for CNC milling, where obstacles are fixtures.
- **Drone swarms in indoor environments:** Planning 2D projections of flight paths in cluttered warehouses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cell Count Bound

**Claim:** The horizontal decomposition of a workspace with $n$ obstacle vertices produces at most $m = O(n)$ cells.

**Proof (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

Let the sorted $y$-coordinates of all obstacle vertices be $y_{(1)} \le y_{(2)} \le \ldots \le y_{(n)}$.

**Step 1 — Slab Count:**
The sorted list creates $n+1$ horizontal slabs (including the two unbounded outer slabs below $y_{(1)}$ and above $y_{(n)}$):

$$
N_{slabs} = n + 1
$$

**Step 2 — Vertices on Slab Boundary:**
Each vertex $v_i = (x_i, y_i)$ lies on the boundary of exactly **one or two** slabs:
- If $y_i$ is a unique $y$-value, the vertex is on the boundary of *one* slab (and contributes a slit going *into* that slab).
- If multiple vertices share the same $y$, they all share a slab boundary.

In the worst case (all $y$-coordinates distinct), there are $n$ distinct boundary levels and each contributes $\le 1$ slit *going downward* per $x$-interval.

**Step 3 — Slits per Slab:**
Within a single slab $S_k = [y_{(k)}, y_{(k+1)}]$, suppose there are $p_k$ obstacle edges crossing it (i.e., edges whose $y$-range overlaps $S_k$). Each such edge terminates at a vertex on the slab boundary. The maximum number of slits that can be drawn from the *upper* boundary of $S_k$ is bounded by the number of vertices on that boundary, which is at most the number of edges incident to that $y$-level.

Summing over all slabs:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{n} (\text{slits in } S_k) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{(slits dropped from vertex } v_i) \le n
$$

since each vertex drops at most one slit downward.

**Step 4 — Cells = Slits + 1 (per connected region):**
In a slab, if $t$ slits divide the free space, the number of free cells in that slab is at most $t + 1$ (a basic fact about partitioning a line/strip by vertical cuts).

Summing over all slabs:

$$
m = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (t_k + 1) = (n + 1) + \sum_{k=0}^{n} t_k \le (n+1) + n = 2n + 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{m = O(n)}
$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Algorithm Complexity

**Step 1 — Sorting the $y$-coordinates:**

$$
T_{sort} = O(n \log n)
$$

using merge sort or heap sort.

**Step 2 — Sweep and slit insertion (plane sweep with balanced BST):**
As we sweep, we maintain a balanced BST of obstacle edges intersecting the current sweep line. Each event (vertex) causes $O(\log n)$ updates.

$$
T_{sweep} = O(n \log n)
$$

**Step 3 — Graph construction:**

$$
T_{graph} = O(m + \vert E_G \vert) = O(n)
$$

since $\vert E_G \vert \le m$ for planar cell decomposition graphs.

**Step 4 — Graph search (e.g., A$^*$):**

$$
T_{search} = O(m + \vert E_G \vert) \cdot \log m = O(n \log n)
$$

**Total:**

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = O(n \log n)}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Path Length Approximation Factor

The roadmap path is composed of straight-line segments connecting cell centroids (or, more rigorously, a point in $C_{start}$, a sequence of slit's midpoints, and a point in $C_{goal}$).

**Claim:** The resulting path is at most **twice** the optimal path length.

**Sketch:**

Let the optimal path be $\pi^*$. Project $\pi^*$ onto the cells — the path crosses slits at the points where it transitions between cells. The cell-to-cell "detour" via the centroid vs. directly through the slit midpoint is at most a factor of 2 in the worst case (an isoceles triangle where the apex is at a slit midpoint and the base is the optimal crossing point on the opposite side).

Formally, for any two cells $C_i$ and $C_j$ sharing a slit:

$$
d_{roadmap}(C_i, C_j) \le 2 \cdot d_{C_{free}}(C_i, C_j)
$$

Summing over all $k$ slits crossed:

$$
L_{roadmap} \le 2 \cdot L^*
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{roadmap} \le 2 L^* \quad \text{(2-approximation)}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Decomposition Variants

| Property | Horizontal Decomp. | Vertical Decomp. | Boustrophedon Decomp. |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sweep axis** | Horizontal (constant $y$) | Vertical (constant $x$) | Alternating horizontal |
| **Cell shape** | Trapezoid / Triangle | Trapezoid / Triangle | Larger convex cells |
| **Number of cells** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ (smaller constant) |
| **Number of nodes in graph** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ (fewer) |
| **Total complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Best for** | Wide corridors | Tall corridors | Mixed / cluttered |
| **Approximation** | $\le 2 \cdot L^*$ | $\le 2 \cdot L^*$ | $\le 2 \cdot L^*$ |
| **Robustness to noise** | Moderate | Moderate | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Workspace with Polygonal Obstacles] --> B[Extract all obstacle vertices V_obs = x_i, y_i]
    B --> C[Sort vertices by y-coordinate]
    C --> D[Initialize sweep line at y = -infinity]
    D --> E{Next vertex y_k to process?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Move sweep line to y = y_k]
    F --> G[Insert/Delete obstacle edges in BST]
    G --> H[Drop vertical slits downward from each vertex]
    H --> I[Identify trapezoidal cells in current slab]
    I --> J[Assign unique cell IDs C_1 to C_m]
    J --> E
    E -- No --> K[Construct connectivity graph G]
    K --> L[Add nodes = cells]
    L --> M[Add edges = shared slits]
    M --> N[Query: Start cell and Goal cell]
    N --> O[Run A-star or BFS on G]
    O --> P[Output: Sequence of cells]
    P --> Q[Connect via straight-line segments]
    Q --> R[Final path in C-free]
    R --> S[End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cell Connectivity Graph Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    cell1[Cell 1: Top Slab] --- cell2[Cell 2: Trapezoid A]
    cell2 --- cell3[Cell 3: Triangle A]
    cell2 --- cell4[Cell 4: Trapezoid B]
    cell3 --- cell5[Cell 5: Middle Slab]
    cell4 --- cell5
    cell5 --- cell6[Cell 6: Trapezoid C]
    cell5 --- cell7[Cell 7: Triangle B]
    cell6 --- cell8[Cell 8: Bottom Slab]
    cell7 --- cell8
    cell1 -.start.-> cell2
    cell8 -.goal.-> cell7
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        W[Workspace Map W]
        OB[Polygonal Obstacles O]
        QS[Start q_s]
        QG[Goal q_g]
    end

    subgraph Decomp[Decomposition Engine]
        EX[Vertex Extractor]
        SW[Sweep Line Controller]
        SL[Slit Generator]
        CL[Cell Builder]
    end

    subgraph Roadmap[Roadmap Layer]
        GN[Graph Nodes = Cells]
        GE[Graph Edges = Shared Slits]
    end

    subgraph Search[Search Layer]
        PR[Path Planner A-star]
        SM[Path Smoother]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Layer]
        P[Final Trajectory]
    end

    W --> EX
    OB --> EX
    EX --> SW
    SW --> SL
    SL --> CL
    CL --> GN
    GN --> GE
    QS --> PR
    QG --> PR
    GE --> PR
    PR --> SM
    SM --> P
```

### 4.4 Conceptual ASCII Schematic of a Decomposed Workspace

```
  y = y_max +-----------+========================+
                     |     Cell C1 (top slab)     |
  y = y_3    +---+---+==========+========+======+
                  |   Obstacle B  |        |
  y = y_2    +---+----+=====+---+--------+------+
              | Obst. A |   | Cell C4 (trap)  |
  y = y_1    +---------+   +---------+--------+
              | Cell C2 (tri) | Cell C5       |
  y = y_0    +---------------+--------+-------+
                          | Cell C3 (bot slab)|
  y = y_min +------------+====================+
              x=0                                   x=x_max
```
*(Each `+`, `=`, `-` denotes boundary or obstacle edge; cells are the white free regions between.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Diagram Limitations:** Mermaid does not natively render geometric polygons with obstacle vertices. The above schematic and functional block diagrams represent the **information-flow** and **logical decomposition** faithfully, which is the typical KTU-expected level of illustration for this topic in written examinations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Horizontal Decomposition" in the context of mobile robot path planning. Mention any two characteristics of the cells generated.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Horizontal decomposition is a cell decomposition method in robot motion planning where a horizontal sweep line partitions the free configuration space $C_{free}$ into convex polygonal cells (trapezoids and triangles) by stopping at every vertex of the polygonal obstacles.

**Two characteristics of the generated cells:**

1. **Convexity:** Each cell is a convex polygon (trapezoid or triangle), so a straight-line path exists between any two points inside the cell.

2. **Number of cells:** The total number of cells $m$ is bounded by $O(n)$, where $n$ is the number of obstacle vertices, making the algorithm linear in the input size.

3. **Slab structure:** Cells are arranged in horizontal strips (slabs) between consecutive sorted $y$-coordinates of vertices.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for any two characteristics.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the time complexity of horizontal decomposition-based motion planning and explain the role of the "slit" in this technique.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Time complexity:** The total time complexity of horizontal decomposition is $O(n \log n)$, where $n$ is the number of obstacle vertices. This comprises $O(n \log n)$ for sorting and sweeping, plus $O(n)$ for graph construction and search.

**Role of the slit:** A **slit** is a vertical line segment drawn downward from an obstacle vertex until it hits the next obstacle edge or the floor of the slab. Slits partition each slab into individual trapezoidal/triangular cells and act as **shared boundaries (edges) in the connectivity graph** between adjacent cells. They are the fundamental mechanism that captures the *connectivity* of the free space.

*(Award 1 mark for complexity, 2 marks for slit explanation with technical detail.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** Explain the horizontal decomposition algorithm for mobile robot path planning with the help of a neat sketch. Describe the steps involved in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the construction of the connectivity (roadmap) graph from the generated cells. Show with an example how a path is extracted between a start and a goal cell using graph search. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Horizontal decomposition is a cell-based motion planning technique that decomposes the planar workspace into convex cells using a horizontal sweep line. **Algorithm steps:**

**Step 1: Vertex Extraction (1 mark)**
Given a set of polygonal obstacles in the workspace, identify all vertices $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ where $v_i = (x_i, y_i)$.

**Step 2: Sorting (1 mark)**
Sort the $y$-coordinates of all vertices in ascending order. The sorted sequence $y_{(1)} \le y_{(2)} \le \ldots \le y_{(n)}$ defines the *critical event* positions of the sweep line.

**Step 3: Sweep and Slab Formation (2 marks)**
A horizontal sweep line moves from $y = y_{(1)}$ to $y = y_{(n)}$. At each critical $y$-level, the line pauses. The interval between two consecutive $y$-values is a **slab**. Within a slab, the obstacle topology is constant.

**Step 4: Slit Insertion and Trapezoid Construction (2 marks)**
At each vertex, drop a vertical line segment (slit) downward into the slab. The slit extends until it meets another obstacle edge or the slab's lower boundary. This partitions the slab's free space into trapezoids (or triangles as degenerate cases).

**Step 5: Cell Labeling (1 mark)**
Assign a unique ID to each trapezoid. The total number of cells is $O(n)$.

*(Refer to Section 4.4 ASCII sketch for visual aid.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Roadmap Graph Construction (3 marks):**
- Each cell $C_i$ becomes a **node** in the graph $G$.
- An **edge** $(C_i, C_j)$ is added if and only if the two cells share a slit of *positive length* (i.e., they are vertically adjacent).
- The graph is stored as an adjacency list.

**Example (2 marks):**
Consider a workspace with two cells $C_1$ (top) and $C_2$ (bottom) sharing a horizontal slit boundary. The graph is:
$$
G = (V, E), \quad V = \{C_1, C_2\}, \quad E = \{(C_1, C_2)\}
$$

If the start is in $C_1$ and the goal is in $C_2$, BFS returns the path $[C_1 \to C_2]$, and the actual trajectory is a straight line from $q_{start}$ in $C_1$ to $q_{goal}$ in $C_2$, passing through the midpoint of the shared slit.

**Graph Search (2 marks):**
For $k$ cells, **A$^*$** search is used with the Euclidean heuristic $h(C_i) = \sqrt{(x_i - x_{goal})^2 + (y_i - y_{goal})^2}$, yielding $O(n \log n)$ query time.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Alternative Choice)]**

**(a)** With a clear algorithm, describe how the cells are constructed in horizontal decomposition. Derive the bound on the number of cells generated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare horizontal decomposition with vertical decomposition and Boustrophedon decomposition. Under what workspace conditions would you prefer each? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Algorithm (4 marks):**

1. Extract all vertices of all polygonal obstacles: $V = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$.
2. Sort by $y$-coordinate: $y_{(1)} \le y_{(2)} \le \ldots \le y_{(n)}$.
3. Initialize sweep line at $y = y_{(1)}$. Maintain a balanced BST of obstacle edges currently intersecting the sweep line.
4. For each $k = 1$ to $n$:
   - At $y = y_{(k)}$, identify all vertices at this $y$-level.
   - Drop slits from each such vertex downward into the slab $S_k = [y_{(k)}, y_{(k+1)}]$.
   - Each slit terminates at the first obstacle edge or floor of the slab.
   - The free space within $S_k$ is thus partitioned into trapezoidal cells.
5. Label all generated cells $C_1, \ldots, C_m$.

**Derivation of Cell Bound (3 marks):**

There are $n + 1$ slabs in total. Each vertex drops at most one slit. If $t_k$ slits are in slab $S_k$, then $t_k + 1$ cells are formed in that slab. Hence:

$$
m = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (t_k + 1) = (n+1) + \sum_{k=0}^{n} t_k
$$

Since each vertex contributes to exactly one slit:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{n} t_k \le n
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{m \le 2n + 1 = O(n)}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | Horizontal Decomp. | Vertical Decomp. | Boustrophedon Decomp. |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sweep axis | Horizontal | Vertical | Alternating (back-and-forth) |
| Cell count | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ — smaller constant (~halved) |
| Slab connectivity | Slits drop only downward | Slits drop only rightward | Slits link in *both* directions within a slab |
| Best for | Wide, low obstacles | Tall, narrow obstacles | Cluttered, mixed-size obstacles |
| Path optimality | $\le 2 L^*$ | $\le 2 L^*$ | $\le 2 L^*$ |
| Implementation complexity | Simple | Simple | Moderate |

**Preferred use cases (1 mark for each correctly justified):**
- **Horizontal:** Warehouse with long, horizontal shelves.
- **Vertical:** Tall building floor plans with vertical columns.
- **Boustrophedon:** Dense, irregular obstacle fields (e.g., forests, urban canyons).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the outer slabs:** Students often count only slabs *between* vertex $y$-levels and miss the *unbounded* slabs above the topmost vertex and below the bottommost vertex. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Conflating cells with vertices:** $m$ (cells) $\ne n$ (vertices). The bound is $m = O(n)$, not $m = n$. Be precise.
> 3. **Saying "shortest path" instead of "$\le 2$-approximation":** The roadmap path is *not* guaranteed to be the shortest. Always state the 2-approximation factor.
> 4. **Forgetting the graph search step:** Decomposition alone does not yield a path. Always mention the connectivity graph + search (BFS/A$^*$).
> 5. **Skipping the slit construction:** Slits are the *core mechanism* of the algorithm. Any answer that omits them is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Horizontal Decomposition**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Sweep horizontal line + stop at obstacle vertices → trapezoidal/triangular cells.
> ✅ **Cell shape:** Trapezoids (general) and Triangles (degenerate trapezoid).
> ✅ **Number of cells:** $m \le 2n + 1 = O(n)$.
> ✅ **Number of slabs:** $n + 1$ (including two unbounded outer slabs).
> ✅ **Slit:** Vertical edge dropped from a vertex downward; serves as shared cell boundary.
> ✅ **Sort step:** $O(n \log n)$ — bottleneck of the algorithm.
> ✅ **Sweep step:** $O(n \log n)$ using balanced BST.
> ✅ **Graph construction:** $O(n)$.
> ✅ **Graph search:** BFS = $O(n)$, A$^*$ = $O(n \log n)$.
> ✅ **Total time:** $O(n \log n)$.
> ✅ **Space:** $O(n)$.
> ✅ **Approximation:** Path length $\le 2 \cdot L^*$ (2-approximation, NOT optimal).
> ✅ **Completeness:** YES — exact algorithm, finds path if one exists.
> ✅ **Best for:** Environments with horizontally elongated obstacles / wide corridors.
> ✅ **Comparison partners:** Vertical decomposition (dual) and Boustrophedon (improved).
> ✅ **Connectivity graph:** Nodes = cells, Edges = shared slits.
> ✅ **Real-world apps:** AGV warehouse routing, autonomous parking, surgical robotics, CNC tool path, drone swarms.
> ✅ **Origin:** Schwartz & Sharir (1983); popularized by Latombe (1991).
> ✅ **KTU exam weightage:** 7–14 marks typical; usually a Part B sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vertical Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vertical Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Vertical Decomposition** is a classical *cell-decomposition* based motion planning technique in mobile robotics, where a 2D polygonal configuration space (C-space) free of obstacles is partitioned into a set of non-overlapping **vertical trapezoidal cells** (also called *trapezoids* or *convex vertical slabs*). The partitioning is achieved by drawing a vertical line from every obstacle vertex — both upward and downward — until it either hits another obstacle boundary or the boundary of the workspace. Each cell is, by construction, a convex polygon in which the robot can move freely in a straight line from any point to any other point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Path Planning):**
> Vertical Decomposition is studied as a *topological* or *cell-decomposition* roadmap method, distinct from **Visibility Graphs** and **Voronoi Diagrams**. It guarantees **completeness** for polygonal C-spaces and produces a roadmap graph whose nodes and edges directly encode collision-free motion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a rectangular room filled with a few irregular furniture pieces (polygonal obstacles). Now take a bright flashlight and shoot a vertical light beam from **every single corner** of every piece of furniture — both **upward** and **downward**. These light rays, drawn as straight vertical lines, slice the free floor space of the room into many neat **vertical strips (trapezoids)**.

- Each strip is so simple that you could shuffle along it freely without bumping into anything.
- To travel from your bed (in one strip) to the door (in another), you only need to know *which strips connect* to *which other strips*, usually at a single point (a shared edge endpoint).
- That network of strips + their touching points forms a roadmap — essentially a **"free-space subway map"** for the robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The algorithm is called *vertical* because the slicing rays are always parallel to the **y-axis (vertical)** of the global reference frame, not because the cells are necessarily vertical rectangles. The cells are, in fact, **trapezoidal** because an upward ray from a vertex typically meets a different obstacle edge than a downward ray.

### 1.3 Terminology Used in KTU Board Examinations

| Term | KTU-Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **C-space (Configuration Space)** | The set of all valid robot configurations; for a point robot, this is just the 2D workspace. |
| **Free Space (C_free)** | The subset of C-space not occupied by obstacles (C \ C_obs). |
| **Vertex** | A corner point of any polygonal obstacle. |
| **Cell** | A convex region produced by vertical decomposition. |
| **Roadmap** | A 1-D graph embedded in C_free that captures connectivity between cells. |
| **Trapezoid** | A convex polygon bounded above and below by line segments and on the sides by vertical rays. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Principle — "Why Vertical?"

The motivation for choosing *vertical* slicing (over horizontal or diagonal) is threefold:

1. **Convexity Guarantee:** Every cell produced has the property that the line segment between any two points in the cell lies entirely within the cell. This makes the cell *trivially navigable* — a single straight-line motion suffices.
2. **Deterministic & Polynomial:** A finite, known set of rays (2 rays per vertex) is sufficient. For $n$ vertices, the algorithm creates $O(n)$ cells, which is provably optimal.
3. **Roadmap Extracted in $O(n \log n)$:** With a sweep-line, the cells and their adjacency list can be constructed efficiently.

### 2.2 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The Vertical Decomposition algorithm proceeds in **four logical phases**:

**Phase 1 — Vertex Enumeration**
- Identify all polygonal obstacle vertices $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$.
- For each vertex, note its coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$.

**Phase 2 — Ray Shooting (The "Slicing")**
- For every vertex $v_i$, cast two **vertical rays**:
  - **Upward ray:** $R_u(v_i) = \{(x_i, y) \mid y \geq y_i\}$ — extended until it hits an obstacle edge or workspace boundary.
  - **Downward ray:** $R_d(v_i) = \{(x_i, y) \mid y \leq y_i\}$ — extended similarly.
- These rays collectively form a set of **vertical cut-lines** partitioning C_free.

**Phase 3 — Cell Formation**
- Each cell is the maximal connected region bounded by:
  - A *left* vertical cut-line (or workspace boundary),
  - A *right* vertical cut-line (or workspace boundary),
  - An *upper* obstacle edge or workspace ceiling,
  - A *lower* obstacle edge or workspace floor.
- A cell is thus a **trapezoid** (4-sided) or **triangle** (3-sided, when an upper or lower boundary degenerates to a point).

**Phase 4 — Roadmap Graph Construction**
- Create a node $N_j$ for each cell $C_j$.
- Add an edge between $N_a$ and $N_b$ if cells $C_a$ and $C_b$ share a common boundary (vertical cut-line endpoint on an obstacle edge).
- The midpoint of each shared edge is a candidate waypoint for the robot.

### 2.3 Complexity Metrics

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of cells | $O(n)$ | $n$ = number of vertices in C-space |
| Number of roadmap nodes | $O(n)$ | One per cell |
| Number of roadmap edges | $O(n)$ | Linear in cells |
| Construction time | $O(n \log n)$ | Using plane-sweep or arrangement algorithms |
| Query time (A* / Dijkstra on roadmap) | $O(n \log n)$ | Standard graph search |
| Total planner complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | Asymptotically optimal for polygonal C-spaces |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Indoor Service Robots:** Planning in cluttered home/office environments with rectangular furniture.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Routing among shelving units and pallet racks.
- **Autonomous Wheelchair Navigation:** Hospitals and airports with pillar/obstacle layouts.
- **Drone Swarms (2D abstraction):** When flying at constant altitude, the floor map reduces to a 2D vertical-decomposition problem.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "compare" cell-decomposition methods, always mention that vertical decomposition is **simpler to implement** than Voronoi diagrams, produces **fewer cells** than grid-based decomposition, and is **complete for polygonal obstacles** (unlike potential field methods).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Model of a Single Cell

Consider an obstacle vertex $v_i = (x_i, y_i)$. The upward ray $R_u(v_i)$ terminates at the **lowest** obstacle edge that lies strictly above $y_i$ and has an x-span covering $x_i$. Let this terminating point be $p_u = (x_i, y_u)$. Similarly, the downward ray terminates at $p_d = (x_i, y_d)$.

A cell adjacent to vertex $v_i$ is therefore bounded by:

$$
C_i = \{(x, y) \mid x_L \leq x \leq x_R, \; y_L(x) \leq y \leq y_U(x)\}
$$

where $x_L, x_R$ are the x-coordinates of the two obstacle vertices that bound the cell horizontally, and $y_L(x), y_U(x)$ are the lower and upper obstacle edge equations bounding the cell vertically.

### 3.2 Worked Example — A Numerical Walkthrough

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style 2-mark sub-question):**

Consider a square workspace $[0, 10] \times [0, 10]$ containing a single rectangular obstacle with vertices at $(3, 3), (7, 3), (7, 6), (3, 6)$. Apply vertical decomposition to enumerate the resulting cells.

**Solution — Step 1: Vertex Enumeration**

The four obstacle vertices are:
- $v_1 = (3, 3)$, $v_2 = (7, 3)$, $v_3 = (7, 6)$, $v_4 = (3, 6)$

The workspace corners are also implicit boundary vertices: $(0,0), (10,0), (10,10), (0,10)$.

**Solution — Step 2: Ray Shooting**

For each obstacle vertex, we cast upward and downward rays:

$$
R_u(v_1) = \{(3, y) \mid 3 \leq y \leq 10\}, \quad \text{hits workspace top}
$$
$$
R_d(v_1) = \{(3, y) \mid 0 \leq y \leq 3\}, \quad \text{hits workspace bottom}
$$
$$
R_u(v_2) = \{(7, y) \mid 6 \leq y \leq 10\}, \quad \text{hits workspace top}
$$
$$
R_d(v_2) = \{(7, y) \mid 0 \leq y \leq 3\}, \quad \text{hits workspace bottom}
$$
$$
R_u(v_3) = \{(7, y) \mid y \geq 6\} \text{ (already on top edge, terminates immediately)}
$$
$$
R_d(v_3) = \{(7, y) \mid 3 \leq y \leq 6\}, \quad \text{hits obstacle bottom edge}
$$
$$
R_u(v_4) = \{(3, y) \mid y \geq 6\} \text{ (already on top edge, terminates immediately)}
$$
$$
R_d(v_4) = \{(3, y) \mid 3 \leq y \leq 6\}, \quad \text{hits obstacle bottom edge}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Cell Enumeration**

The vertical cut-lines occur at $x = 3$ and $x = 7$. The horizontal divisions occur at $y = 3$ (obstacle bottom) and $y = 6$ (obstacle top). This produces a **3×3 = 9** candidate regions, but the central one (at $3 \leq x \leq 7, 3 \leq y \leq 6$) is *inside the obstacle* and thus excluded.

Therefore, the **8 free cells** are:

$$
C_1: [0,3] \times [0,3] \quad (\text{lower-left trapezoid/rectangle})
$$
$$
C_2: [3,7] \times [0,3] \quad (\text{lower-middle rectangle})
$$
$$
C_3: [7,10] \times [0,3] \quad (\text{lower-right rectangle})
$$
$$
C_4: [0,3] \times [3,6] \quad (\text{middle-left rectangle})
$$
$$
C_5: [0,3] \times [6,10] \quad (\text{upper-left rectangle})
$$
$$
C_6: [3,7] \times [6,10] \quad (\text{upper-middle rectangle})
$$
$$
C_7: [7,10] \times [3,6] \quad (\text{middle-right rectangle})
$$
$$
C_8: [7,10] \times [6,10] \quad (\text{upper-right rectangle})
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Roadmap Adjacency**
