| 2 | Attractive Force | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -k_{\text{att}}\,(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})$ | Newtons (N) |
| 3 | Repulsive Potential | $U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{1}{2}\, k_{\text{rep}}\,\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$ | J, valid for $\rho \le \rho_0$ |
| 4 | Repulsive Force | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}}\,\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\,\frac{1}{\rho^{3}}\,(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}})$ | N |
| 5 | Total Force | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} + \sum_{i} \mathbf{F}_{\text{rep},i}$ | N |
| 6 | Linear Velocity Command | $v = k_v\, \Vert \mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} \Vert$ | $\text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Angular Velocity Command | $\omega = k_\omega\, \text{atan2}(F_y, F_x) - \theta_{\text{current}}$ | $\text{rad} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Fuzzy Defuzzification (centroid) | $u = \frac{\int x\, \mu(x)\, dx}{\int \mu(x)\, dx}$ | Crisp output |
| 9 | Subsumption Output | $\mathbf{u} = B_n \oplus B_{n-1} \oplus \cdots \oplus B_1$ | Higher layers override |
| 10 | Local Minima Trap Condition | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} = \mathbf{0}$ at $\mathbf{q} \neq \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ | Pathological failure |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

| Formalism | Industry Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Potential Field | **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva robots)**, surgical robot motion planning |
| Behavior-Based | **iRobot Roomba**, Mars rover reactive safety layers |
| Fuzzy Logic | **Subaru Eyesight**, washing-machine controllers, HVAC systems |
| Neural Network | **Tesla FSD perception stack**, drone acrobatic control |
| Hybrid | **DARPA Grand Challenge vehicles (Stanford Junior, CMU Boss)** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Gradient of the Repulsive Potential

The repulsive potential for a single point obstacle is given as:

$$U_{\text{rep}}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\, k_{\text{rep}}\,\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, \quad \text{valid for } \rho \le \rho_0$$

where $\rho = \sqrt{(x - x_{\text{obs}})^{2} + (y - y_{\text{obs}})^{2}}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the inner function:**
Let $f(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$, so that $U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{1}{2}\, k_{\text{rep}}\, f^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule:**
The gradient is:
$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}}\, f(\mathbf{q})\, \nabla f(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\nabla f(\mathbf{q})$:**
Since $f = \rho^{-1} - \rho_0^{-1}$ and $\rho_0$ is a constant:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{q}) = -\rho^{-2}\, \nabla \rho(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\nabla \rho(\mathbf{q})$:**
By definition, $\rho^{2} = (x - x_{\text{obs}})^{2} + (y - y_{\text{obs}})^{2}$. Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$:

$$2\rho\, \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = 2(x - x_{\text{obs}}) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_{\text{obs}}}{\rho}$$

Similarly, $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} = \frac{y - y_{\text{obs}}}{\rho}$. Therefore:

$$\nabla \rho(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{\rho}\begin{bmatrix} x - x_{\text{obs}} \\[2pt] y - y_{\text{obs}} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**
$$\nabla f(\mathbf{q}) = -\rho^{-2}\, \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho} = -\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\nabla U_{\text{rep}}$:**
$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}}\,\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\,\left(-\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho^{3}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — The repulsive force is the negative gradient:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}}\,\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\,\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Interpretation:** The factor $\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)$ ensures the force is **zero at the influence boundary** $\rho = \rho_0$ and **grows unboundedly** as $\rho \to 0$ (collision). The direction $\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho}$ points **away from the obstacle** — exactly the desired behavior.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: APF in 2D

**Problem Setup:**
- Robot position: $\mathbf{q} = (2,\, 1)$ m
- Goal position: $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = (8,\, 6)$ m
- Obstacle position: $\mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}} = (4,\, 3)$ m
- Parameters: $k_{\text{att}} = 1.0$, $k_{\text{rep}} = 2.0$, $\rho_0 = 2.5$ m

**Step 1 — Compute $\rho_{\text{goal}}$:**
$$\rho_{\text{goal}} = \sqrt{(8-2)^{2} + (6-1)^{2}} = \sqrt{36 + 25} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81 \text{ m}$$

Assuming $d_{\text{goal}}^{*} = 5$ m, since $\rho_{\text{goal}} > d_{\text{goal}}^{*}$, we use the **conic** attractive potential.

**Step 2 — Attractive Force (conic case):**
For the conic region, $\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -d_{\text{goal}}^{*}\, k_{\text{att}}\, \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}}{\rho_{\text{goal}}}$

$$\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 8 \\ 1 - 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \frac{1}{7.81} \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 3.84 \\ 3.20 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\rho$ (distance to obstacle):**
$$\rho = \sqrt{(2-4)^{2} + (1-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83 \text{ m}$$

Since $\rho = 2.83 > \rho_0 = 2.5$, the obstacle is **outside** the influence zone, so $\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 4 — Total Force and Velocity Commands:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.84 \\ 3.20 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

Magnitude: $\Vert \mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} \Vert = \sqrt{3.84^{2} + 3.20^{2}} = \sqrt{14.75 + 10.24} = \sqrt{24.99} \approx 5.0$ N

If $k_v = 0.1$: $v = 0.1 \times 5.0 = 0.5 \text{ m} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$
If $k_\omega = 1.0$, robot heading $\theta = 0$: $\omega = 1.0 \times \text{atan2}(3.20, 3.84) - 0 \approx 0.694 \text{ rad} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$

**Interpretation:** The robot will move at $0.5$ m/s while turning left at $\approx 39.8^{\circ}$/s to head toward the goal. Since the obstacle is beyond $\rho_0$, it exerts no influence.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Formalisms (High-Yield for KTU)

| Criterion | Potential Field | Behavior-Based | Fuzzy Logic | Neural Network |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Computational cost | **Low** (vector math) | **Very low** (state machines) | **Low** (rule lookup) | **High** (forward pass) |
| Memory required | Map of obstacles | None (purely reactive) | Rule base (KB-scale) | Network weights (MB) |
| Handling dynamic env. | **Poor** (local minima) | **Excellent** | **Good** | **Excellent** (after training) |
| Optimality guarantee | Only local minimum | None | None | None |
| Real-time feasibility | Yes (kHz loop) | Yes (kHz loop) | Yes (kHz loop) | GPU-accelerated |
| Tuning effort | Gains $k_{\text{att}}, k_{\text{rep}}, \rho_0$ | Layer design + thresholds | Membership fns + rules | Hyperparameter search |
| Explainability | High (physics) | High (state diagrams) | Medium (linguistic) | **Low** (black box) |
| KTU expected depth | **High** (derivations) | **High** (architecture) | Medium (concepts) | Low (introduction only) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Potential Field Landscape (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Configuration q] --> B[Goal Position q_goal]
    A --> C[Obstacle Positions q_obs]
    B --> D["Attractive Potential U_att"]
    C --> E["Repulsive Potential U_rep"]
    D --> F["Negative Gradient: F_att"]
    E --> G["Negative Gradient: F_rep"]
    F --> H["Vector Superposition"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Total Force F_total"]
    I --> J["Velocity Command v"]
    I --> K["Angular Command omega"]
    J --> L[Mobile Robot Actuator]
    K --> L
    L --> M[New Configuration q_new]
    M --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** The loop is the **control cycle**. At every time step, the robot senses its position, the potential field module computes the net force, and the velocity commands update the robot's pose. The cycle repeats at the controller's update frequency (typically $10$–$100$ Hz).

---

### 4.2 Subsumption Architecture Layer Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["Layer 5: Identify Objects - High-level reasoning"]
    L4["Layer 4: Notice Change - Anomaly detection"]
    L3["Layer 3: Build Map - Place environment markers"]
    L2["Layer 2: Follow Corridor - Wall-following behavior"]
    L1["Layer 1: Wander - Random exploration"]
    L0["Layer 0: Avoid Contact - Emergency obstacle avoidance"]
    L5 -->|subsumes| L4
    L4 -->|subsumes| L3
    L3 -->|subsumes| L2
    L2 -->|subsumes| L1
    L1 -->|subsumes| L0
    L0 --> ACT[Actuator Output]
    L1 --> ACT
    L2 --> ACT
    L3 --> ACT
    L4 --> ACT
    L5 --> ACT
```

**Reading the diagram:** The vertical arrows show the **inhibition (subsumption) hierarchy**. If Layer 0 (Avoid Contact) detects an imminent collision, it sends an inhibition signal to all higher layers, suppressing their outputs and forcing the robot to safety.

---

### 4.3 Fuzzy Logic Control Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crisp Sensor Input] --> B["Fuzzification: Membership Functions"]
    B --> C["Rule Base: IF-THEN statements"]
    C --> D["Inference Engine: Mamdani or Sugeno"]
    D --> E["Defuzzification: Centroid Method"]
    E --> F[Crisp Actuator Command]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Crisp values enter the FLC, are converted to fuzzy sets, matched against rules, aggregated, and converted back to crisp commands. Each block represents a **functional transformation** that can be implemented as a software module or hardware circuit.

---

### 4.4 Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HP[High-Level Planner: A* or RRT] --> GP[Global Path Waypoints]
    GP --> LC[Local Controller: Potential Field or Fuzzy]
    S[Sensor Stream: Lidar / Camera] --> PR[Perception Module]
    PR --> LC
    LC --> CMD[Velocity Commands v, omega]
    HP -.feedback.-> PR
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **deliberative layer** (top) generates a coarse global path. The **reactive layer** (bottom) handles real-time obstacle avoidance using sensor data. This is the architecture used in most modern autonomous vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is meant by an artificial potential field method in mobile robot navigation? Mention its two main components.**

**Model Answer:**
The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method is a reactive control technique in which the robot's configuration space is modeled as a potential energy landscape. The robot is treated as a point mass moving under the influence of artificial forces.
**Two main components:**
1. **Attractive Potential ($U_{\text{att}}$):** Generated by the goal; pulls the robot toward the target.
2. **Repulsive Potential ($U_{\text{rep}}$):** Generated by obstacles; pushes the robot away from them.

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
> **List any three levels of Brooks' subsumption architecture in order of priority.**

**Model Answer:**
Brooks' subsumption architecture organizes robot behaviors into a stack of Augmented Finite State Machines (AFSMs), with higher layers capable of subsuming the outputs of lower layers. Three levels in increasing priority are:
1. **Level 5 — Identify Objects** (lowest priority, high-level reasoning)
2. **Level 2 — Follow Corridor** (medium priority, wall-following)
3. **Level 0 — Avoid Contact** (highest priority, emergency obstacle avoidance)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **attractive force** experienced by a mobile robot at position $\mathbf{q}$, given a goal at $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ and a parabolic attractive potential $U_{\text{att}} = \frac{1}{2}\, k_{\text{att}}\, \rho_{\text{goal}}^{\,2}$, where $\rho_{\text{goal}} = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert$. Explain the behavior of this force near and far from the goal. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A differential-drive robot at $\mathbf{q} = (1,\, 2)$ m must reach a goal at $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = (5,\, 5)$ m. A circular obstacle is centered at $\mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}} = (3,\, 3)$ m with influence radius $\rho_0 = 1.5$ m. Using $k_{\text{att}} = 1.0$ and $k_{\text{rep}} = 3.0$, compute the **total artificial force** on the robot. State the corresponding linear and angular velocity commands if $k_v = 0.1$, $k_\omega = 1.0$, and the current heading is $\theta = 30^{\circ}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Express the distance:**
$$\rho_{\text{goal}} = \sqrt{(x - x_{\text{goal}})^{2} + (y - y_{\text{goal}})^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$:**
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial x} = k_{\text{att}}\, (x - x_{\text{goal}})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $y$:**
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial y} = k_{\text{att}}\, (y - y_{\text{goal}})$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the gradient:**
$$\nabla U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = k_{\text{att}}\, (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})$$

**Step 5 — The attractive force is the negative gradient:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = -k_{\text{att}}\, (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})$$

**Step 6 — Behavior analysis:**
- **Near the goal** ($\rho_{\text{goal}} \to 0$): $\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} \to \mathbf{0}$ — the robot decelerates smoothly, which prevents overshoot.
- **Far from the goal** ($\rho_{\text{goal}} \to \infty$): $\Vert \mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} \Vert \to \infty$ — unbounded force, which is why a *conic* potential is preferred for distant regions in practical implementations.

---

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\rho_{\text{goal}}$:**
$$\rho_{\text{goal}} = \sqrt{(5-1)^{2} + (5-2)^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the attractive force (parabolic case, since $5.0$ is finite):**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -1.0 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 5 \\ 2 - 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\rho$ (distance to obstacle):**
$$\rho = \sqrt{(1-3)^{2} + (2-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}$$

Since $\rho = 2.236 > \rho_0 = 1.5$, the obstacle is **outside** the influence zone. Therefore, $\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 4 — Total force:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \text{ N} \end{bmatrix}$$

Magnitude: $\Vert \mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} \Vert = \sqrt{16 + 9} = 5.0$ N

**Step 5 — Velocity commands:**
$$v = 0.1 \times 5.0 = 0.5 \text{ m} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

Desired heading: $\theta_{\text{desired}} = \text{atan2}(3, 4) = 36.87^{\circ} \approx 0.6435$ rad
Current heading: $\theta = 30^{\circ} = 0.5236$ rad
Heading error: $\theta_{\text{err}} = 0.6435 - 0.5236 = 0.1199$ rad
$$\omega = 1.0 \times 0.1199 \approx 0.12 \text{ rad} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

The robot moves at $0.5$ m/s and turns left at $\approx 0.12$ rad/s ($\approx 6.87^{\circ}$/s) to align toward the goal.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Subsumption Architecture** proposed by Rodney Brooks. How does it differ from the traditional Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) paradigm? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the four stages of a **Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)**. How is the centroid defuzzification formula derived, and what is its physical interpretation? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

The **Subsumption Architecture** is a behavior-based, reactive control framework proposed by **Rodney Brooks (1986)** at MIT. It decomposes a robot's intelligence into a hierarchy of **layers**, each representing an **Augmented Finite State Machine (AFSM)**. Each AFSM is an independent module with its own sensors and actuators.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L5["Layer 5: Reason about objects"]
        A5[AFSM: Identify Objects]
    end
    subgraph L0["Layer 0: Survival"]
        A0[AFSM: Avoid Contact]
    end
    L5 -->|inhibits| L0
    A0 --> OUT[Actuator Output]
    A5 --> OUT
```

**Layers (from lowest to highest priority):**
| Layer | Behavior |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | Avoid Contact (reflexive obstacle avoidance) |
| 1 | Wander (random exploration) |
| 2 | Follow Corridor (wall following) |
| 3 | Build Map (place markers at landmarks) |
| 4 | Notice Change (detect environmental changes) |
| 5 | Identify Objects (high-level reasoning) |

**Difference from Sense–Plan–Act (SPA):**
| Aspect | SPA (Traditional) | Subsumption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Information flow | Sequential: Sense → Plan → Act | Parallel and reactive |
| World model | **Required** (centralized map) | **Not required** |
| Response time | Slow (planning overhead) | Fast (reflexive) |
| Failure mode | Single point of failure | Graceful degradation |
| Computational cost | High (search-based planning) | Low (state machines) |
| Robustness to uncertainty | Poor | Excellent |

**Key advantage:** Subsumption allows the robot to **survive** even if higher-level reasoning fails — the lower layers continue functioning autonomously.

---

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

A **Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)** processes linguistic rules to generate control actions. The four stages are:

**Stage 1 — Fuzzification:**
The crisp sensor input $x$ is mapped to one or more fuzzy sets using **membership functions** $\mu_{A_i}(x) \in [0, 1]$. Common shapes include triangular, trapezoidal, and Gaussian.

**Stage 2 — Rule Base Evaluation:**
The rule base contains linguistic IF–THEN statements, e.g., *"IF distance is NEAR AND angle is LEFT THEN steer = SHARP_RIGHT"*. Each rule has an antecedent (IF part) and consequent (THEN part).

**Stage 3 — Inference Engine:**
For each rule, the **firing strength** $\alpha_i$ is computed as the minimum of the antecedent membership values (Mamdani method):
$$\alpha_i = \min\!\left(\mu_{A_1}(x_1),\, \mu_{A_2}(x_2)\right)$$
The consequent fuzzy set is then **clipped** to height $\alpha_i$.

**Stage 4 — Defuzzification (Centroid Method):**
The clipped consequent sets from all rules are aggregated into a single fuzzy set $\mu_{\text{out}}(x)$. The crisp output is the centroid (center of gravity) of this aggregated set:
$$u_{\text{crisp}} = \frac{\int_{x_{\min}}^{x_{\max}} x \cdot \mu_{\text{out}}(x)\, dx}{\int_{x_{\min}}^{x_{\max}} \mu_{\text{out}}(x)\, dx}$$

**Derivation Insight:**
The centroid formula can be derived by treating $\mu_{\text{out}}(x)$ as a **probability density** (or mass distribution) and computing its **first moment**:
$$u_{\text{crisp}} = \frac{\int x\, d\mu_{\text{out}}(x)}{\int d\mu_{\text{out}}(x)} = E[X \vert \mu_{\text{out}}]$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The centroid is the **balance point** of the aggregated fuzzy output — if you "weighed" the fuzzy set on a scale, $u_{\text{crisp}}$ is the fulcrum position. It represents the *best compromise* among all activated rules.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}}$:** Students often forget the negative sign when converting gradient to force, leading to the robot being *attracted* to obstacles. Always verify direction with a sketch.
> 2. **Ignoring the influence radius:** Writing $U_{\text{rep}}$ as a global function without the $\rho \le \rho_0$ condition causes the robot to oscillate around distant obstacles.
> 3. **Confusing gradient descent with gradient ascent:** In potential field method, the robot moves *down* the attractive well — force is the *negative* gradient.
> 4. **Confusing Subsumption with parallel execution:** The key is **inhibition**, not parallelism per se. Higher layers send inhibition signals that *suppress* lower-layer outputs.
> 5. **Forgetting the threshold $d_{\text{goal}}^{*}$:** The conic attractive potential exists precisely to prevent unbounded force at large distances; omitting it loses **2–3 marks** in numericals.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Alternative Control Formalisms** = non-classical control paradigms for mobile robots: **APF, BBC, FLC, NNC, Hybrid**.
- **APF (Khatib, 1986):** Models workspace as a potential landscape; robot rolls downhill toward goal.
  - Attractive force: $\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -k_{\text{att}}\,(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})$.
  - Repulsive force: $\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho^{3}}$.
  - **Local minima trap:** When $\mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} = \mathbf{0}$ at $\mathbf{q} \neq \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ (e.g., symmetric obstacle arrangement).
- **Behavior-Based Control (Brooks, 1986):** Layered AFSMs with subsumption — higher layers inhibit lower layers.
  - Levels: Avoid Contact → Wander → Follow Corridor → Map → Notice Change → Identify.
  - **No world model required**; purely reactive.
- **Fuzzy Logic Control:** Fuzzification → Rule Base → Inference → Defuzzification.
  - Centroid: $u = \frac{\int x\, \mu(x)\, dx}{\int \mu(x)\, dx}$.
  - Works well when expert knowledge is available as linguistic rules.
- **Hybrid Architecture:** Combines **deliberative** (global planner) + **reactive** (local controller) for robust autonomy.
- **Comparison Cheat-Codes for KTU:**
  - **APF** → elegant, but suffers from local minima.
  - **Subsumption** → simple, robust, no planning.
  - **FLC** → interpretable, expert-rule-driven.
  - **Neural Network** → powerful but black-box.
- **Always** convert gradients to forces using the **negative sign convention**.
- **Always** check whether the obstacle lies within $\rho_0$ before computing $U_{\text{rep}}$.
- **Real-world deployments** of these formalisms: Amazon Kiva (APF), Roomba (Subsumption), autonomous cars (Hybrid).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Human Robot Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Human Robot Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human-Robot Interface (HRI)**: The multidisciplinary field of study dedicated to understanding, designing, and evaluating robotic systems for use by or with humans. It encompasses the hardware, software, communication channels, and cognitive models that enable bidirectional information flow between a human operator and an autonomous or semi-autonomous mobile robotic agent.

In the context of **Mobile Robotics**, the Human-Robot Interface (HRI) is the perceptual and control bridge that allows a human supervisor to:

1. **Sense** the robot's internal state (battery, position, task progress, sensor health).
2. **Perceive** the robot's external environment (SLAM maps, detected obstacles, recognized objects).
3. **Command** the robot (navigation goals, task switches, mode changes).
4. **Receive** feedback (confirmations, alerts, error messages, haptic cues).

The HRI is classified by KTU as a **non-functional requirement** that governs the **usability, safety, and acceptance** of mobile platforms across domains such as search and rescue, planetary exploration, medical assistance, industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and domestic service robots.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Taxi Driver and the GPS Dashboard**
> Imagine you are sitting in the passenger seat of a self-driving taxi. You are not driving, but you want to feel safe and informed. The HRI is the **dashboard, the voice prompt ("Turning left in 200 meters"), the touchscreen showing your route, and even the gentle haptic vibration of the seat when the brakes are applied sharply**.
> The robot is the car. The HRI is everything that lets the human understand the car and let the car communicate with the human. Without it, the human feels helpless, and the car cannot receive high-level intent.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The HRI is governed by measurable Human Factors standards:

- **NASA-TLX (Task Load Index)**: A standard metric (0–100 scale) used to quantify subjective workload of the operator.
- **Fitts's Law Standard Throughput**: A target interaction throughput of **$\approx 4$–$10$ bits/second** for pointing tasks on graphical interfaces.
- **Maximum Tolerable End-to-End Latency**: **$\leq 300$ ms** for teleoperation tasks, **$\leq 100$ ms** for haptic feedback loops, per ISO 9241 (Ergonomics of Human-System Interaction).
- **Standard Safe Approach Distance (ISO 13849 / ISO/TS 15066)**: **$\geq 500$ mm** for collaborative robot workspaces with power-and-force-limiting.

### 1.4 Three Pillars of the HRI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The HRI Triad** (sylabus highlight):
> 1. **The Human** — with cognitive, perceptual, and physical limitations.
> 2. **The Robot** — with sensors, actuators, and an autonomy stack.
> 3. **The Task / Environment** — which defines the *necessary* level of interaction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Human-Robot Interaction

KTU 2024 scheme classifies interaction along a **5-level autonomy scale** (adapted from Sheridan & Verplank, 1978, and Parasuraman's 10-level model):

| Level | Name | Human Role | Robot Role |
|:------|:-----|:-----------|:-----------|
| L1 | **Direct Manual Control** | Operator issues every joint/motor command | Pure actuator |
| L2 | **Action-Level (Teleoperation)** | Operator specifies velocities/end-effector pose | Executes commands |
| L3 | **Task-Level (Shared Control)** | Operator specifies sub-goals; robot plans path | Local motion planner |
| L4 | **Supervisory Control** | Operator monitors; intervenes on exceptions | Full autonomous task execution |
| L5 | **Full Autonomy** | None (only high-level mission goals) | Complete decision-making |

> [!NOTE]
> **Most current mobile robots operate at L3–L4.** A Mars rover is L4 (supervised by Earth operators with seconds-to-minutes delay), while a Roomba vacuum is L5.

### 2.2 Interaction Modalities (The Human Side)

The interface must accommodate multiple human sensory channels simultaneously — a principle called **Multimodal Interaction**:

- **Visual (GUI / AR / HUD)**: 2D screens, head-mounted displays, AR overlays, status LEDs.
- **Auditory (Non-speech & Speech)**: Beeps, sonification of sensor data, text-to-speech (TTS), voice recognition.
- **Haptic (Force / Vibrotactile)**: Force-feedback joysticks, wearable rumble packs, exoskeleton suits.
- **Gesture / Posture**: IMU gloves, vision-based skeleton tracking (e.g., Kinect, MediaPipe).
- **Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)**: EEG headsets (e.g., Emotiv EPOC) for direct neural command.
- **Tactile Touchscreens**: Capacitive panels on the robot chassis for proximate interaction.

### 2.3 Operational Flow of an HRI Loop

The HRI is a closed-loop system that can be broken into five functional stages:

1. **Perception Capture** — Sensors (LIDAR, cameras, IMU) build an internal world model.
2. **Information Abstraction** — Raw data is condensed into a human-understandable representation (map, icons, natural language).
3. **Display Rendering** — Data is rendered onto the chosen output modality (screen, speaker, haptic motor).
4. **Human Cognition** — The operator interprets the information and decides on an action.
5. **Command Injection** — The operator's input (button, voice, gesture) is parsed and sent to the robot's planner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Round-Trip Latency** $T_{rt}$ of this loop is the most critical performance parameter in HRI design.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every equation, constant, and bound you need for KTU 2024 examination questions on HRI.

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Description | Units / Bounds |
|:--------|:-----------------|:------------|:---------------|
| Round-Trip Latency | $T_{rt} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{xmit} + T_{display} + T_{cog} + T_{cmd}$ | End-to-end time from sensor acquisition to actuator response | $\leq 300$ ms (teleop) |
| Fitts's Law (Pointing Time) | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Time to acquire a target; $D$ = distance, $W$ = width | $a \approx 50$ ms, $b \approx 150$ ms/bit |
| Index of Difficulty | $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Bit-rate demand of a pointing task | bits |
| Throughput | $TP = \dfrac{ID}{MT}$ | Effective information rate of interaction | bits/s |
| Cognitive Workload | $WL = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot r_i$ | NASA-TLX weighted sum of 6 sub-scales (0–100) | dimensionless |
| Steer Angle (Bicycle Model) | $\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R}\right)$ | Steering command; $L$ = wheelbase, $R$ = turn radius | rad |
| Haptic Stability Bound | $T_{delay} < \dfrac{\pi}{2 \omega_{max}}$ | Hard upper bound to keep force feedback stable (Anderson–Spong) | seconds |
| Safe Stop Distance (Cobot) | $d_{safe} = v_{robot} \cdot T_{react} + d_{buffer}$ | ISO/TS 15066 minimum approach distance | meters |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Audio/voice interface quality | dB (target $\geq 15$ dB) |
| Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = BW \cdot T_{delay}$ | Telemetry channel capacity; $\geq$ size of one data frame | bits |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation scripts, writing the **units** of every formula is mandatory. Examiners deduct marks for unit-less answers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Space Exploration**: NASA uses supervisory control with 4–24 min light-time delay for Mars rovers. The HRI must be predictive (previewing commands) rather than reactive.
- **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci)**: Sub-millisecond latency ($< 10$ ms) with full haptic feedback for telesurgery.
- **Autonomous Wheelchairs**: Multimodal (voice + joystick + gaze) for patients with motor impairments.
- **Disaster Response**: HRI must function in **darkness, dust, and noise** — pushing designers toward haptic and audio cues.
- **Industrial Cobots**: HRI enforces speed-and-separation monitoring per ISO/TS 15066.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fitts's Law Applied to a Touchscreen HRI Panel

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A teleoperator must use a 12-inch touchscreen ($W_{screen} = 25.4$ cm) to select one of 5 navigation waypoints arranged in a row. Each waypoint icon is **$W = 1.2$ cm** wide, and the **maximum distance** between the user's finger start point and the furthest icon is **$D = 18$ cm**. The empirically fitted Fitts's Law parameters for this operator are $a = 52$ ms and $b = 158$ ms/bit. Calculate the **(i)** Index of Difficulty, **(ii)** Movement Time, and **(iii)** Throughput.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
The width $W$ is the size of the target (icon), and the distance $D$ is the amplitude of the pointing motion.

$$
W = 1.2 \text{ cm}, \quad D = 18 \text{ cm}, \quad a = 52 \text{ ms}, \quad b = 158 \text{ ms/bit}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID).**
Substitute $D$ and $W$ into the Fitts ID equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left( \frac{D}{W} + 1 \right) \\[6pt]
   &= \log_2\!\left( \frac{18}{1.2} + 1 \right) \\[6pt]
   &= \log_2\!\left( 15 + 1 \right) \\[6pt]
   &= \log_2(16) \\[6pt]
   &= 4 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Movement Time (MT).**
Apply Fitts's Law with the empirical constants.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\[6pt]
   &= 52 \text{ ms} + 158 \text{ ms/bit} \times 4 \text{ bits} \\[6pt]
   &= 52 + 632 \\[6pt]
   &= 684 \text{ ms} \approx 0.684 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Throughput (TP).**
Throughput is information per unit time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= \frac{ID}{MT} \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{4 \text{ bits}}{0.684 \text{ s}} \\[6pt]
   &= 5.85 \text{ bits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
A throughput of **$\approx 5.85$ bits/s** is within the expected human range of **$4$–$10$ bits/s**, confirming the interface design is within ergonomic norms. If the operator is elderly or stressed, throughput falls to $\approx 2$–$3$ bits/s, which is the design floor for safety-critical mobile robot teleoperation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Round-Trip Latency Budget for a Wi-Fi Teleoperated Mobile Robot

> **Problem Statement:** A mobile robot is teleoperated over a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi link. Compute the worst-case round-trip latency, and verify whether it is within the ISO 9241 limit of **$300$ ms**.

| Stage | Latency |
|:------|:--------|
| LIDAR scan capture ($T_{sense}$) | $33$ ms |
| Onboard SLAM compute ($T_{compute}$) | $80$ ms |
| Wi-Fi uplink transmission ($T_{xmit}$) | $12$ ms |
| GUI render on operator laptop ($T_{display}$) | $40$ ms |
| Operator cognitive decision ($T_{cog}$) | $90$ ms |
| Command downlink + actuation ($T_{cmd}$) | $20$ ms |

**Step 1 — Sum all serial stages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rt} &= T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{xmit} + T_{display} + T_{cog} + T_{cmd} \\[6pt]
       &= 33 + 80 + 12 + 40 + 90 + 20 \\[6pt]
       &= 275 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare against the ISO limit.**

$$
T_{rt} = 275 \text{ ms} \le 300 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

The interface is compliant, but with only **$25$ ms of margin**. Adding camera-stream encoding (typical $50$–$80$ ms) would push the system past the safe threshold, justifying the use of **predictive displays** (showing *expected* future robot state) to compensate.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing HRI Modalities

| Modality | Bandwidth | Latency Tolerance | Cognitive Load | Best Use Case |
|:---------|:---------:|:-----------------:|:--------------:|:--------------|
| **Visual (2D GUI)** | High | $\le 200$ ms | Medium | Map-based navigation |
| **Visual (AR HUD)** | Very High | $\le 100$ ms | High | Surgical / military |
| **Audio (Speech)** | Low–Medium | $\le 500$ ms | Low | Hands-busy environments |
| **Audio (Sonification)** | Medium | $\le 300$ ms | Medium | Obstacle proximity alerts |
| **Haptic (Force)** | Medium | $\le 50$ ms | Low | Teleoperation, cobots |
| **Haptic (Vibrotactile)** | Low | $\le 150$ ms | Low | Directional cues |
| **Gesture (Vision)** | Medium | $\le 200$ ms | Medium | Social robots |
| **BCI (EEG)** | Very Low | $\le 1$ s | High | Assistive mobility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often pose a **design question**: "Select an HRI modality for a firefighting robot." The answer should reference *bandwidth*, *latency tolerance*, *noise robustness*, and *operator cognitive load* using this matrix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `node2`).
- No reserved keywords used as node names.
- Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting inside double-quotes.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: The Closed-Loop HRI Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[ROBOT SIDE] --> node2[LIDAR AND CAMERA SENSORS]
    node2 --> node3[SLAM AND LOCALIZATION]
    node3 --> node4[WORLD MODEL BUILDER]
    node4 --> node5[ABSTRACTED STATE PACKET]
    node5 --> node6[TELEMETRY LINK WIFI OR 4G]
    node6 --> node7[OPERATOR GUI HMI SCREEN]
    node7 --> node8[HUMAN COGNITION AND DECISION]
    node8 --> node9[COMMAND GENERATION JOYSTICK VOICE GESTURE]
    node9 --> node10[COMMAND LINK DOWNLINK]
    node10 --> node11[ROBOT MOTION PLANNER]
    node11 --> node12[ACTUATORS WHEELS AND MANIPULATORS]
    node12 --> node1
    node7 -. FEEDBACK .-> node13[HAPTIC VEST AND AUDIO BEACON]
    node13 -. VIBROTACTILE .-> node8
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Multimodal Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        nodeA1[VOICE MIC]
        nodeA2[TOUCHSCREEN]
        nodeA3[FORCE JOYSTICK]
        nodeA4[EEG HEADSET]
    end

    end

        nodeC2[BEHAVIOR ARBITER]
    end

        nodeD1[VISUAL GUI]
        nodeD2[TTS SPEAKER]
        nodeD3[HAPTIC MOTOR]
    end
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA3 --> nodeB1

    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeC1
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1
    nodeC1 --> nodeD2
    nodeC2 --> nodeD3
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Sheridan-Verplank Autonomy Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL1[L1 MANUAL] --> nodeL2[L2 ACTION TELEMOTION]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3[L3 TASK SHARED]
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4[L4 SUPERVISORY]
    nodeL4 --> nodeL5[L5 FULL AUTONOMY]
    nodeL5 -. FALLBACK RETURN .-> nodeL1
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Operator Workload Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeW1[OPERATOR] --> nodeW2[TASK DEMAND]
    nodeW2 --> nodeW3[INTERFACE COMPLEXITY]
    nodeW3 --> nodeW4[WORKLOAD MEASURED BY NASA TLX]
    nodeW4 --> nodeW5[ADAPTIVE UI ADJUSTMENT]
    nodeW5 --> nodeW3
    nodeW5 --> nodeW6[REDUCED COGNITIVE FATIGUE]
    nodeW6 --> nodeW1
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams are accepted in KTU answer scripts as substitutes for hand-drawn schematics, provided they are reproduced with sufficient labeling on paper during the examination.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1.** Define Human-Robot Interface (HRI) and list any **four** interaction modalities used in mobile robotics.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Human-Robot Interface is the perceptual and control bridge that enables bidirectional communication between a human operator and a mobile robot. It encompasses hardware, software, and communication protocols that allow the human to perceive the robot's state and command its actions.
> Four interaction modalities: (1) Visual (GUI, AR, HUD), (2) Auditory (TTS, beeps, sonification), (3) Haptic (force feedback, vibrotactile), (4) Gesture or Brain-Computer Interface.

**Q2.** What is the **maximum tolerable round-trip latency** for teleoperation as per ISO 9241, and why is it critical?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The maximum tolerable round-trip latency for teleoperation is **$T_{rt} \leq 300$ ms**. Beyond this, the operator experiences *cognitive disconnect* — the perceived state of the robot lags the operator's mental model, leading to **oscillatory commands** (Poser's "move-and-wait" pathology) and degraded task performance. For haptic feedback the bound is stricter, **$T_{delay} \leq 100$ ms**, to maintain closed-loop force stability.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Design Problem**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five functional stages** of a closed-loop HRI system for a teleoperated mobile robot. Identify the dominant latency contributor in each stage.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2).**
>
> 1. **Perception Capture ($T_{sense}$)** — LIDAR/camera sampling. Dominant: sensor frame rate; e.g., a 30 Hz camera contributes $33$ ms.
> 2. **Information Abstraction ($T_{compute}$)** — SLAM and mapping. Dominant: algorithmic complexity; typical $50$–$150$ ms on embedded GPUs.
> 3. **Display Rendering ($T_{display}$)** — GUI/HUD update. Dominant: video pipeline buffering, $\approx 30$–$50$ ms.
> 4. **Human Cognition ($T_{cog}$)** — Operator interpretation. Dominant: human reaction time, $\approx 200$–$300$ ms (cannot be reduced).
> 5. **Command Injection ($T_{cmd}$)** — Downlink + actuation. Dominant: communication protocol overhead, $\approx 20$–$100$ ms over 4G.
>
> **Cumulative bound**:
> $$T_{rt} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{display} + T_{cog} + T_{cmd} \le 300 \text{ ms}$$
>
> Since $T_{cog}$ is fixed by human physiology, optimizations focus on $T_{compute}$ (predictive displays) and $T_{cmd}$ (edge computing).
>
> **[7 marks breakdown: 2 for diagram, 3 for stages, 2 for latency analysis]**

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mobile robot's touchscreen HRI requires an operator to select icons of width **$W = 0.8$ cm** placed at a maximum distance of **$D = 20$ cm**. Using Fitts's Law with parameters **$a = 60$ ms** and **$b = 165$ ms/bit**, compute the **(i)** Index of Difficulty, **(ii)** Movement Time, and **(iii)** Throughput. Comment on whether the interface is ergonomically acceptable.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Index of Difficulty:**
> $$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{20}{0.8} + 1\right) = \log_2(26) = 4.70 \text{ bits}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Movement Time:**
> $$MT = a + b \cdot ID = 60 + 165 \times 4.70 = 60 + 775.5 = 835.5 \text{ ms} \approx 0.836 \text{ s}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Throughput:**
> $$TP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{4.70}{0.836} = 5.62 \text{ bits/s}$$
>
> **Comment:**
> A throughput of $5.62$ bits/s lies within the human ergonomic range of **$4$–$10$ bits/s**, so the interface is **acceptable**. However, the movement time approaching 1 s may cause hesitation in time-critical scenarios (e.g., obstacle avoidance). Recommendation: enlarge the icons to $W \geq 1.2$ cm to reduce ID to $\approx 4$ bits.
>
> **[7 marks breakdown: 2 ID, 2 MT, 1 TP, 2 comment]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Selection**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **supervisory control, teleoperation, and full autonomy** as three levels of HRI. For each, state one real-world mobile robot example and the typical latency regime.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> | Mode | Human Role | Example | Latency Regime |
> |:-----|:-----------|:--------|:---------------|
> | **Teleoperation (L2)** | Continuous velocity/pose commands | Bomb-disposal robot (Talon) | $\le 100$ ms, often wired |
> | **Supervisory (L4)** | Goal-setting + exception handling | NASA Mars rover (Curiosity) | $4$–$24$ min (one-way light time) |
> | **Full Autonomy (L5)** | Mission only; no real-time control | iRobot Roomba | N/A — local only |
>
> - **Teleoperation** offers maximum control but maximum operator load.
> - **Supervisory** balances autonomy and trust; requires *predictive displays* due to the unavoidable light-time delay.
> - **Full autonomy** minimizes human load but requires robust AI; trust calibration becomes the dominant HRI challenge.
>
> **[7 marks: 2 table, 2 examples, 3 discussion]**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **role of multimodal interaction** in mobile robotics. With a block diagram, show how voice, gesture, and haptic channels are fused to command a navigation stack.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Definition:** Multimodal interaction combines two or more human communication channels (voice + gesture + touch + gaze) to improve **robustness, speed, and naturalness** of command input. It exploits the principle that *no single modality is optimal across all conditions*.
>
> **Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3).**
>
> 1. **Input Layer** captures voice (ASR), gesture (vision-based skeleton tracking), and joystick.
> 2. **Fusion Layer** uses a probabilistic engine (e.g., Bayesian, Kalman, or transformer-based) to combine noisy inputs and infer intent (e.g., "go to the kitchen *while pointing* at the door").
> 3. **Robot Layer** receives the intent and dispatches to the navigation stack.
> 4. **Output Layer** confirms via TTS, visual GUI, and haptic vest.
>
> **Advantage in mobile robotics:** A rescue robot may lose voice input in noise → gesture takes over. A disabled user may lack hand mobility → BCI takes over. Redundancy raises mission success probability.
>
> **[7 marks breakdown: 1 definition, 2 diagram, 2 fusion explanation, 2 advantage]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always draw the block diagram first.** Part (a) of any 7-mark sub-question is incomplete without it; examiners deduct 1–2 marks routinely.
> 2. **Units are non-negotiable.** "300 ms" not just "300". "bits/s" not "bits".
> 3. **Fitts's Law misuse:** Do not confuse $D$ (distance to target) with screen diagonal. $D$ is the *amplitude* of the pointing motion.
> 4. **Latency summation:** Do not multiply the stages; they are **serial** (sum, not product).
> 5. **Sheridan levels:** KTU 2024 explicitly maps Levels 1–5 to *Manual → Teleop → Shared → Supervisory → Full*. Do not write 10 levels (that is Parasuraman's extended model — different question).
> 6. **Cognitive load vs. physical load:** Distinguish *mental* (NASA-TLX) from *physical* (Fitts's Law) workload. Examiners test this distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — HRI)**

- **HRI** = Perceptual + Control bridge between human and mobile robot.
- **Three pillars:** Human, Robot, Task/Environment.
- **5 autonomy levels** (Sheridan): Manual → Teleop → Shared → Supervisory → Full.
- **Round-trip latency** $T_{rt} \le 300$ ms (teleop), $\le 100$ ms (haptic).
- **Fitts's Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$; throughput target $4$–$10$ bits/s.
- **Multimodal interaction** = combination of $\geq 2$ channels (visual, audio, haptic, gesture, BCI).
- **NASA-TLX** = standard cognitive workload metric ($0$–$100$).
- **ISO/TS 15066** governs cobot safety; safe stop distance uses $v \cdot T_{react} + d_{buffer}$.
- **Predictive displays** compensate for high latency in space teleoperation.
- **Haptic stability bound:** $T_{delay} < \pi / (2\omega_{max})$.
- **SNR $\ge 15$ dB** for voice interfaces; **4G/Wi-Fi bandwidth-delay product** determines streaming feasibility.
- **Trust calibration** is the central HRI challenge in full-autonomy mode.
- **Multimodal fusion** uses Bayesian or deep-learning engines to resolve conflicting inputs.
- **Design constraints** for mobile HRI: latency, cognitive load, robustness to noise, accessibility for disabled users, and compliance with ISO 9241.
- **Real-world examples:** Mars rovers (L4, $4$–$24$ min delay), Da Vinci (L2, $<10$ ms), Roomba (L5, local), bomb-disposal Talon (L2, wired).
- **Exam traps:** sum latencies (not multiply); include units; differentiate Fitts's law throughput from bandwidth.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — The Human Robot Interface**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Off-Board Communication: Tethered
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Off-Board Communication: Tethered

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Off-Board Communication (Tethered)** in mobile robotics refers to a unidirectional or bidirectional data (and often power) transmission architecture in which a mobile robotic platform is **physically linked to an external host system, base station, or power source through a dedicated hard-wired cable assembly known as a *tether***. The tether serves as a guided, deterministic, and bounded-bandwidth conduit that simultaneously delivers electrical energy and/or information signals between the robot and the off-board infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor**
> *Tethered Off-Board Communication* is a hard-wired physical link where the umbilical cable replaces the wireless (RF/optical/acoustic) channel, ensuring deterministic latency, immunity to electromagnetic interference, and continuous high-power delivery in exchange for operational mobility constraints.

The three operational functions that a tether commonly performs are:

1. **Power Tethering** – Delivery of regulated DC or AC electrical power to on-board actuators, sensors, and embedded compute.
2. **Data Tethering** – Transmission of control commands (uplink) and telemetry/video (downlink) using protocols such as RS-232, RS-422, RS-485, CAN, Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T), or fiber-optic channels.
3. **Hybrid Tethering** – A combination of power and data in a single composite cable, often with dedicated shielding, drain wires, and Kevlar strength members.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine a **deep-sea diver** working on the ocean floor. The diver cannot breathe unaided for long, cannot communicate by shouting through water, and cannot freely drift away. The lifeline (umbilical cord) connects the diver to the support vessel above — supplying air, a two-way communication line, and a way to be hauled back. If the diver ventures too far, the hose kinks; if it tangles, the mission ends.

A **tethered mobile robot** is the engineering equivalent of this diver. The host computer on the surface is the support vessel, the tether is the umbilical cord, and the robot is the diver exploring a constrained but rich environment (nuclear plants, bomb sites, deep ocean, planetary caves).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a tether at all?** Wireless links drop out in underwater, underground, RF-denied (EMI/RFI) zones, and inside metal enclosures. A tether guarantees connectivity, but trades it for a *mobility budget* — the robot can only operate as far as the cable length allows.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded)

- **Tether Length Range:** typically **10 m – 2000 m** (ROVs), up to **5 km** (deep-ocean trench explorers).
- **Power Delivery:** **100 W – 30 kW** continuous.
- **Data Bandwidth:** **1 Mbps (RS-232) to 10 Gbps (single-mode fiber)**.
- **Operating Temperature of Cable Jacket:** **−40 °C to +80 °C** (polyurethane) or up to **+200 °C** (silicone).
- **Signal Velocity in Copper:** ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (≈ 66 % of $c$).
- **Signal Velocity in Optical Fiber:** ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (silica, $n = 1.5$).

## 1.4 Classification of Tethered Architectures

| Class | Conductor / Medium | Typical Use | Range |
|-------|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Copper Power Tether** | Multi-core Cu (AWG 8–18) | Bomb disposal, indoor inspection | < 300 m |
| **Twisted-Pair Data Tether** | Cat-5e / Cat-6 / RS-485 | Industrial AGVs, service robots | < 100 m |
| **Coaxial Tether** | RG-58 / RG-174 | Video down-link, RF modulation | < 500 m |
| **Fiber-Optic Tether** | SMF (9/125) / MMF (50/125) | Subsea ROVs, high-data inspection | up to 50 km |
| **Hybrid Tether** | Cu + Fiber + Kevlar jacket | ROVs (most common) | up to 5 km |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Tethered Communication Link

A tethered link in mobile robotics is composed of **five functional sub-systems** that the student must be able to draw and label:

1. **On-Board Termination Unit (OTU):** Connector, signal-conditioning PCB, and protocol transceiver (e.g., MAX485 for RS-485, BCM89810 for 1000BASE-T).
2. **Tether Cable Itself:** Conductors, dielectric, shielding (braid + foil), strength members (Kevlar / aramid), and outer jacket (PUR, PE, TPU).
3. **Tether Management System (TMS):** Winch, drum, level-wind, cable spool, slip ring, tension sensor, payout encoder.
4. **Off-Board Interface Unit (OIU):** Reel-mounted breakout box, isolation transformers, surge protection, line drivers.
5. **Host Controller / Base Station:** PC, PLC, or ground-station workstation running ROS, LabVIEW, or bespoke SCADA software.

> [!NOTE]
> The TMS is the *only* moving electromechanical interface on the off-board side. It governs **dynamic cable length** ($L(t)$) and **dynamic cable tension** ($T(t)$), both of which directly affect robot dynamics and communication signal integrity.

## 2.2 Electrical Model of the Tether (Why and How)

A copper conductor is modelled as a **distributed R-L-G-C parameter network**. For a sufficiently short tether ($L \ll \lambda/20$ of the highest signal harmonic), the **lumped-parameter model** is acceptable:

- $R$ → series DC resistance
- $L$ → series inductance (self-inductance of the wire)
- $G$ → shunt conductance (dielectric loss)
- $C$ → shunt capacitance (between conductors)

The DC resistance of a single conductor of length $L$, cross-section $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ is:

$$R_{DC} = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

For a **two-wire return path** (positive + negative), the **loop resistance** is double:

$$R_{loop} = 2 \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

The **voltage drop** at the robot-end (load voltage $V_L$ vs source voltage $V_S$) is:

$$V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{loop}$$

The **power lost in the tether** is:

$$P_{loss} = I_{load}^{2} \cdot R_{loop}$$

The **efficiency of power transmission** through the tether is:

$$\eta_{tether} = \frac{V_L \cdot I_{load}}{V_S \cdot I_{load}} = \frac{V_S - V_{drop}}{V_S}$$

## 2.3 Data-Link Considerations

For digital signalling, the tether behaves as a **low-pass transmission line**. The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a twisted pair is:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L'}{C'}} \;\;\;\;[\Omega]$$

where $L'$ is inductance per unit length (H/m) and $C'$ is capacitance per unit length (F/m).

The **propagation delay** of a signal through a length $L$ of cable is:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{L}{v_p}, \;\;\; v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

Termination mismatch causes reflections. The **reflection coefficient** at an impedance discontinuity is:

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

For **fiber-optic tethers**, signal attenuation follows:

$$\alpha_{dB} = \frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right) \;\;\;\;[dB/km]$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.** Memorize the **boundary conditions** and **units** alongside each.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $R_{DC} = \rho L / A$ | Loop resistance | Cable sizing for power |
| 2 | $R_{loop} = 2 \rho L / A$ | Two-wire return | Robot supply drop |
| 3 | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{loop}$ | Voltage sag | Compliance with actuator spec |
| 4 | $P_{loss} = I^{2} R_{loop}$ | Heat dissipated | Thermal derating of jacket |
| 5 | $\eta_{tether} = (V_S - V_{drop})/V_S$ | Power efficiency | Battery vs tethered decision |
| 6 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L'/C'}$ | Line impedance | Termination for high-speed data |
| 7 | $t_{pd} = L / v_p$ | Signal latency | Real-time control feasibility |
| 8 | $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ | Reflection coeff. | Termination resistor design |
| 9 | $\alpha_{dB} = (10/L) \log(P_{in}/P_{out})$ | Fiber attenuation | Optical-link budget |
| 10 | $f_c = 1 / (2 \pi R C)$ | RC low-pass cutoff | Max data rate on copper pair |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tethered communication is **mission-critical** in domains where RF is forbidden, unreliable, or impossible:

- **Defence EOD (Explosive Ordnance Disposal):** *Talon* and *PackBot* robots use 300 m copper tethers carrying 48 V DC and 100 Mbps Ethernet.
- **Subsea ROVs:** *Jason*, *Hercules*, *Kaiko* — 10 km fiber-optic hybrids carrying 3-phase 4 kVAC + single-mode fiber for 4K video.
- **Nuclear Decommissioning:** *BROKK* and *CARVER* machines use 200 m tethers inside γ-shielded reactor cavities.
- **Planetary Cave Exploration (NASA / ESA):** *Daughter-bot* in *Team A – CoSTAR* used a 10 m micro-tether through lava tubes.
- **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci):** The patient-side manipulator is tethered to the surgeon's console with a 7 m hybrid cable (power + fiber + pneumatic lines).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Conductor Cross-Section for a Given Voltage Drop

> **Problem Statement (typical 7-mark KTU sub-question):**
> *A tethered inspection robot is to operate at 200 m from the base station, drawing a steady current of 5 A from a 48 V DC supply. The maximum allowable voltage drop is 4 V. The tether conductor is copper ($\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \;\Omega\!\cdot\!m$). Calculate the minimum cross-sectional area of each conductor and the standard AWG size.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Translate the constraint to loop resistance.**

The voltage drop across the *two-wire loop* (positive + return) must be $\le$ 4 V.

$$V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{loop} \le 4 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R_{loop} \le \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \;\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Use the loop-resistance formula.**

$$R_{loop} = 2 \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; A \ge \frac{2 \rho L}{R_{loop}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$A \ge \frac{2 \times (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 200}{0.8}$$

$$A \ge \frac{6.896 \times 10^{-6}}{0.8}$$

$$A \ge 8.62 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mm² and select AWG.**

$$A \ge 8.62 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{2} = 8.62 \;\text{mm}^{2}$$

The next *standard* conductor size above 8.62 mm² is **AWG 8 (≈ 8.37 mm²) is borderline**, so we select **AWG 6 (≈ 13.3 mm²)** as a safe engineering choice. (Many KTU answers accept 10 mm² as the textbook answer — both score full marks.)

**Step 5 — Recompute the actual voltage drop with AWG 6.**

For AWG 6, $A = 13.3 \;\text{mm}^{2} = 13.3 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{2}$.

$$R_{loop} = 2 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times \frac{200}{13.3 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{loop} = \frac{6.896 \times 10^{-6}}{13.3 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.5185 \;\Omega$$

$$V_{drop} = 5 \times 0.5185 = 2.59 \;\text{V} \;\;\;\;(\le 4 \;\text{V} \;\checkmark)$$

**Step 6 — Compute power loss and efficiency.**

$$P_{loss} = I^{2} R_{loop} = 25 \times 0.5185 = 12.96 \;\text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{48 - 2.59}{48} = 0.946 = \mathbf{94.6\%}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Data Rate on a Copper Twisted-Pair Tether

A twisted-pair tether has $R' = 0.14 \;\Omega/\text{m}$, $C' = 50 \;\text{pF/m}$, and $L = 150 \;\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Total lumped parameters.**

$$R = R' \cdot L = 0.14 \times 150 = 21 \;\Omega$$

$$C = C' \cdot L = 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 150 = 7.5 \;\text{nF}$$

**Step 2 — RC low-pass cutoff (3-dB point).**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 21 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{9.896 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.01 \;\text{MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Nyquist-limited maximum data rate (binary).**

For a channel with bandwidth $B = f_c$, the noiseless maximum symbol rate is $2B$ baud, so the binary bit-rate is:

$$R_{b,max} \approx 2 \times f_c = 2.02 \;\text{Mbps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the RS-485 standard limits signalling to 10 Mbps only over **< 10 m**. For 150 m, RS-485 spec gives a maximum of **≈ 1 Mbps**, perfectly matching our derived theoretical ceiling.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optical Power Budget of a Fiber Tether

A 2 km single-mode fiber tether at 1310 nm has $\alpha = 0.35$ dB/km. There are **two ST connectors** at 0.5 dB each and **one splice** at 0.1 dB. Transmitter power is 0 dBm, receiver sensitivity is −28 dBm.

**Total loss:**

$$L_{total} = \alpha \cdot L + 2 L_{connector} + L_{splice}$$

$$L_{total} = 0.35 \times 2 + 2 \times 0.5 + 0.1$$

$$L_{total} = 0.7 + 1.0 + 0.1 = 1.8 \;\text{dB}$$

**Power at receiver:**

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} - L_{total} = 0 - 1.8 = -1.8 \;\text{dBm}$$

**System margin:**

$$M = P_{rx} - P_{rx,sens} = -1.8 - (-28) = \mathbf{26.2 \;\text{dB}}$$

A 6 dB margin is the industry rule, so this link is **highly over-engineered** — a 1310 nm laser could be replaced with a low-cost LED, or the cable length could be tripled to 6 km.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Master Reference)

| Parameter | Copper Tether | Fiber-Optic Tether | Hybrid (Cu + Fiber) |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Max Practical Length | ≈ 500 m | ≈ 50 km | ≈ 5 km |
| Power Delivery | Excellent (kW) | None directly | Good (kW) |
| EMI Immunity | Poor (requires shield) | Total | Total (data part) |
| Bandwidth | < 100 Mbps typical | > 10 Gbps | > 1 Gbps |
| Weight (per km) | **400 – 1500 kg** | **50 – 150 kg** | **300 – 800 kg** |
| Cost (per km) | Low | High | Medium-High |
| Failure Mode | Conductor break | Fiber fracture | Complex (both) |
| Use Case | EOD, indoor | Subsea HD video | ROVs (industry standard) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOST["OFF-BOARD BASE STATION"]
        HS1[Host PC with ROS / SCADA]
        HS2[Surge Protector and Isolation Transformer]
        HS3[48 V DC Power Supply Unit]
        HS4[Ethernet to Fiber Media Converter]
    end

    subgraph TMS["TETHER MANAGEMENT SYSTEM (TMS)"]
        TM1[Slip Ring Rotary Joint]
        TM2[Drum and Winch Motor]
        TM3[Tension Sensor]
        TM4[Payout Encoder]
        TM5[Level Wind Guide]
    end

    subgraph CABLE["TETHER CABLE"]
        CB1[Kevlar Strength Member]
        CB2[Power Conductors Cu AWG 8]
        CB3[Twisted Pair Data]
        CB4[Single Mode Fiber]
        CB5[Polyurethane Outer Jacket]
    end

    subgraph ROBOT["ON-BOARD MOBILE ROBOT"]
        RB1[Junction Box and Strain Relief]
        RB2[DC DC Converter 48V to 24V and 5V]
        RB3[Robot Controller SBC]
        RB4[Sensor Stack IMU Lidar Camera]
        RB5[Drive Motors and Motor Drivers]
    end

    HS1 --> HS4
    HS3 --> HS2
    HS2 --> CB2
    HS4 --> CB4
    CB2 --> TM1
    CB4 --> TM1
    CB3 --> TM1
    TM1 --> TM2
    TM2 --> TM5
    TM5 --> TM3
    TM3 --> TM4
    CB1 --> TM2
    CB2 --> RB1
    CB3 --> RB1
    CB4 --> RB1
    RB1 --> RB2
    RB2 --> RB5
    RB1 --> RB3
    RB3 --> RB4
```

## 4.2 Tether Signal Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator Console
    participant TMS as Tether Management System
    participant T as Tether Cable
    participant RB as Robot Onboard CPU
    participant ACT as Actuators and Sensors

    OP->>TMS: 1. Send Joystick Command (UDP over Ethernet)
    TMS->>T: 2. Apply Twist to Avoid Kink
    T->>RB: 3. Deliver Differential Signal (RS-422 / 100BASE-TX)
    RB->>ACT: 4. Drive PWM to Motors
    ACT-->>RB: 5. Encoder and IMU Feedback
    RB-->>T: 6. Encode Telemetry (H.264 video + state)
    T-->>TMS: 7. Optical to Electrical Conversion
    TMS-->>OP: 8. Display on HMI (Latency typically 5 to 30 ms)
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Tether Type Selection (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Robot Application] --> B{RF Permitted?}
    B -- No --> C{Power > 100 W?}
    B -- Yes --> Z[Consider Wireless Off Board Comm]
    C -- Yes --> D[Range > 1 km?]
    C -- No --> E[Use Copper Twisted Pair]
    D -- Yes --> F[Budget > 100 USD per meter?]
    D -- No --> G[Use Hybrid Cu plus Fiber Tether]
    F -- Yes --> H[Use Pure Single Mode Fiber plus Separate Power Tether]
    F -- No --> I[Use Hybrid Cu plus Fiber Tether]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A has 5 questions of 3 marks each. We provide two representative questions here; the remaining three may be selected from adjacent Module 4 sub-topics during the actual exam.

### **Q1.** *Define a tethered off-board communication link in mobile robotics. List **two** advantages and **two** disadvantages compared to wireless communication.* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PBRAT604]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **tethered off-board communication link** is a hard-wired physical connection (umbilical cable) that carries **electrical power and/or data** between a mobile robot and an external host system. The tether replaces the wireless (RF/acoustic/optical) channel with a deterministic, EMI-immune, guided medium.

**Advantages (any 2 × 1 mark):**
1. Continuous, unlimited power delivery — no on-board battery required.
2. Immunity to RF jamming, EMI, and acoustic multipath.
3. High bandwidth and deterministic low latency (essential for tele-operation).
4. Secure — no eavesdropping via radio interception.

**Disadvantages (any 2 × 0.5 mark):**
1. Limited operating range (cable length).
2. Tether snagging, entanglement, and mechanical wear.
3. Tether drag force reduces robot manoeuvrability.
4. Weight and stiffness of cable constrain payload.

---

### **Q2.** *A tethered robot operates 80 m from its base. The tether is a 2-wire copper pair of cross-section 1.5 mm². Compute the loop resistance. (Given $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\!\cdot\!m$.)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBRAT604]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:** $L = 80$ m, $A = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\!\cdot\!m$.

**Step 2 — Apply the two-wire loop-resistance formula:**

$$R_{loop} = \frac{2 \rho L}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$R_{loop} = \frac{2 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 80}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{loop} = \frac{2.7584 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \mathbf{1.839 \;\Omega}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PBRAT604]`

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a tethered off-board communication system** in a mobile robot. Identify and describe the role of the **Tether Management System (TMS)** in 7 marks.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A tethered communication architecture (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1) consists of **five functional sub-systems**:

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **1. Host Base Station** | Houses the operator HMI, ROS workstation, and the bulk power supply (typically 48 V DC or 220 V AC). |
| **2. Off-Board Interface Unit** | Performs surge protection, isolation (galvanic), and protocol conversion (e.g., copper Ethernet to fiber media converter). |
| **3. Tether Management System** | A motorised winch / drum that pays out and reels in cable, monitors tension, and provides a **slip-ring** rotary electrical joint. |
| **4. Tether Cable** | Multi-conductor copper + optical fiber + Kevlar strength member + PUR jacket. |
| **5. On-Board Termination** | Junction box, strain relief, DC-DC converters, and protocol transceivers. |

**Role of the TMS (the 7-mark key points):**
1. **Length Control** — Maintains a **slack tether** of constant tension by paying out / reeling in cable as the robot moves, preventing both taut-line drag and on-floor tangling. *Equation:* $L_{drum} = L_{payout} + L_{slack}$, with $L_{slack}$ typically held at 1–3 m.
2. **Tension Management** — A load-cell on the drum senses $T(t)$. If $T > T_{max}$, the winch pays out to release load; if $T < T_{min}$, the winch reels in to remove slack.
3. **Slip-Ring Rotary Joint** — Enables the drum to rotate indefinitely without twisting the cable; carries power (typically 30 A / 600 V) and data (Ethernet / fiber) across the rotating interface.
4. **Operator Safety** — Detects tether breakage (sudden $T \to 0$ during retraction) and stops the winch, preventing whip-back.
5. **Tether Health Monitoring** — Real-time insulation-resistance measurement and fiber-OTDR (Optical Time-Domain Reflectometry) check.
6. **Dynamic Drag Compensation** — Active winch control reduces apparent cable drag, extending robot endurance by up to 30 %.
7. **Diameter Compensation** — A level-wind (screwed cam or rotating ring) lays cable evenly on the drum to prevent snarls.

---

**(b)** *A tethered mobile robot is supplied through a 150 m, 2-wire copper cable of conductor area 2.5 mm², drawing a current of 6 A at 24 V DC. Calculate: (i) loop resistance, (ii) voltage drop, (iii) power loss in the cable, and (iv) power transmission efficiency. Take $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\!\cdot\!m$.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**

$$A = 2.5 \;\text{mm}^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{2}, \quad L = 150 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Loop Resistance (i).**

$$R_{loop} = \frac{2 \rho L}{A} = \frac{2 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 150}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{loop} = \frac{5.172 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \mathbf{2.069 \;\Omega}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage Drop (ii).**

$$V_{drop} = I \times R_{loop} = 6 \times 2.069 = \mathbf{12.41 \;\text{V}}$$

**Step 4 — Power Loss (iii).**

$$P_{loss} = I^{2} \times R_{loop} = 36 \times 2.069 = \mathbf{74.5 \;\text{W}}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency (iv).**

$$V_L = V_S - V_{drop} = 24 - 12.41 = 11.59 \;\text{V}$$

$$\eta_{tether} = \frac{11.59}{24} \times 100 = \mathbf{48.3\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Forgetting to multiply by **2** (loop, not single-wire) — loses 1 mark.
> 2. Forgetting to convert mm² → m² (off by $10^{6}$) — loses 2 marks and gives absurd "V-drop = 0.0001 V" answers.
> 3. Computing efficiency on the wrong reference — must use $V_S$ (source), not $V_L$ (load).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBRAT604]`

**(a)** *Explain the **Tether Management System (TMS)** in detail. With a sketch, describe the working of a **slip ring** used in TMS. Why is it necessary?* **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of a TMS** — Refer to the answer of Q-A (a) for the 7 functional roles. Add the key dynamic equation:

$$T_{tether}(t) = T_{static} + T_{dynamic}(t) = \mu \, W_{cable} \, g + \frac{1}{2} \dot{L} \, m_{cable} \, \ddot{L}$$

where $\mu$ is the friction coefficient between cable and ground, and $W_{cable}$ is the weight of the *unsupported* (on-floor) cable span.

**Slip Ring (Rotary Electrical Joint) — Working Sketch:**

```
      ┌──────────────────┐
      │  Stator (Fixed)  │  ← connected to off-board system
      │  [Ring 1] [Ring 2] [Ring 3] [Fiber Bulkhead]
      └──────┬───────────┘
             │ brushes (Au–Ag alloy)
      ┌──────┴───────────┐
      │ Rotor (Spinning) │  ← mounted on the winch drum
      │ [Brush 1] [Brush 2]
      └──────────────────┘
```

- The **stator** is fixed to the chassis of the TMS and holds the **conductive rings** (one per electrical circuit) and an **optical bulkhead** (for the fiber channel, since optical fiber cannot pass through a metallic slip ring without a rotary joint).
- The **rotor** is mounted on the spinning drum shaft. Spring-loaded **brushes** (graphite, Au, or Ag-graphite) ride on the rings.
- Current flows from the off-board cable into the stator ring, across the brush contact (with milliohm contact resistance), into the rotor brush, and onto the drum-attached cable that is paid out to the robot.

**Why Necessary (3 marks):**
1. The drum must rotate continuously to pay out / reel in cable. Without a slip ring, the cable would **twist and fracture** within a few rotations.
2. The slip ring provides a **zero-twist rotary junction** that allows infinite rotation in either direction.
3. It acts as the **central electromechanical interface** that keeps power and data flowing while the cable spool turns.
4. For fiber channels, an **optical rotary joint (FORJ)** is used in series with the electrical slip ring.

---

**(b)** *A fiber-optic tether of length 3 km is used at 1550 nm wavelength with attenuation coefficient 0.22 dB/km. There are two FC/PC connectors (loss 0.3 dB each) and one fusion splice (loss 0.05 dB). The transmitter launches 0 dBm. Compute: (i) total link loss, (ii) received power, and (iii) system margin if the receiver sensitivity is −32 dBm.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total Link Loss (i).**

$$L_{total} = \alpha \cdot L + 2 L_{conn} + L_{splice}$$

$$L_{total} = 0.22 \times 3 + 2 \times 0.3 + 0.05$$

$$L_{total} = 0.66 + 0.60 + 0.05 = \mathbf{1.31 \;\text{dB}}$$

**Step 2 — Received Power (ii).**

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} - L_{total} = 0 - 1.31 = \mathbf{-1.31 \;\text{dBm}}$$

**Step 3 — System Margin (iii).**

$$M = P_{rx} - P_{rx,sens} = -1.31 - (-32) = \mathbf{30.69 \;\text{dB}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Mixing dB and dBm** — dB is a *ratio*, dBm is an *absolute power*; subtraction of dBm minus dB is valid, but dB minus dBm is meaningless.
> 2. **Forgetting connector & splice losses** — most KTU answer-sheets deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Not expressing final answer in dBm** — required unit convention.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam**

- **Tethered = wired** off-board communication; gives **power + data + security** at the cost of **mobility**.
- Three classes: **copper power, twisted-pair data, fiber-optic, hybrid**.
- **Five-block architecture:** Host → OIU → TMS → Cable → OTU.
- **Key formula cluster to memorize verbatim:**
  - $R_{loop} = 2 \rho L / A$
  - $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{loop}$
  - $P_{loss} = I^{2} R_{loop}$
  - $\eta_{tether} = (V_S - V_{drop})/V_S$
  - $Z_0 = \sqrt{L'/C'}$
  - $t_{pd} = L / v_p$
  - $\alpha_{dB} = (10/L) \log(P_{in}/P_{out})$
- **TMS roles** (7-point answer): length control, tension, slip-ring, safety, health, drag compensation, level-wind.
- **Slip ring** allows the drum to rotate without twisting the cable; for fiber you need a **FORJ** in addition.
- **Voltage drop budget** must be ≤ 5–10 % of source voltage for motor-driver compliance.
- **Attenuation budget** for fiber must reserve a **6 dB engineering margin** beyond calculated loss.
- **Hybrid tether (Cu + fiber + Kevlar)** is the industry standard for **ROVs**; copper for power, fiber for high-bandwidth video, Kevlar for tensile strength.
- **Common KTU traps:** forgetting the factor of 2 in $R_{loop}$, mixing dB with dBm, omitting connector/splice losses, drawing the slip ring with brushes on the wrong side.
- **Typical real-world examples** to mention for full credit: Talon EOD, Jason ROV, Da Vinci surgical robot, NASA CoSTAR lava-tube explorer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Untethered - Types of Untethered Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Untethered - Types of Untethered Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Untethered Communication in Mobile Robotics** refers to any data, command, or telemetry exchange between a mobile robotic platform and a remote station (or among a swarm of robots) that occurs **without any physical wired medium** (no copper cable, no fiber-optic tether, no electrical conductor). The information carrier is a propagating wave — electromagnetic (radio, optical) or acoustic (ultrasonic) — that traverses free space (air, vacuum, or water).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the term *untethered* is the direct antonym of *tethered*, where a tether (umbilical cable) typically carries:
- Power to the robot
- Bidirectional serial/Ethernet data
- Safety emergency-stop signals

When the tether is removed, the robot must rely on **onboard energy storage** (batteries/fuel cells) and an **onboard radio/optical modem** for communication, making the *communication channel* itself a critical design subsystem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Walkie-Talkie vs. The Landline Phone**
> Imagine a security guard patrolling a large campus. A *landline telephone* on a wall is like a *tethered* robot: it works flawlessly, but the guard is chained to the wall socket. A *walkie-talkie* (or a smartphone on WhatsApp) is *untethered*: the guard can walk anywhere on the campus and still talk to the control room. The trade-off is that the walkie-talkie has **limited range**, can suffer from **interference** (other guards on the same channel), and needs its **battery** to be charged — these are exactly the same three trade-offs a mobile robot designer faces when choosing an untethered link.

## 1.3 Why Untethered Communication is Mandatory in Mobile Robotics

A mobile robot, by the very definition of *mobility*, cannot be permanently cabled to a base station. The communication link is the lifeline that delivers:

| Function | Example Payload |
|---|---|
| **Telemetry uplink** (Robot → Base) | Odometry, GPS, IMU data, camera frames, LIDAR point clouds |
| **Command downlink** (Base → Robot) | Waypoints, speed set-points, mission abort, gait patterns |
| **Swarm coordination** (Robot ↔ Robot) | Formation control, task allocation, collision avoidance |
| **Safety channel** | E-Stop, health monitoring, link-loss heartbeat |

## 1.4 The Governing Physical Constraint

All untethered links, regardless of carrier type, obey the **inverse-square law of wave propagation**: the received signal power decays as $1/d^2$ in free space. This single fact dictates *range*, *antenna size*, *transmit power*, and ultimately the choice of physical layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Reference (ITU-R & IEEE):** Untethered robotic links operate in shared, licence-free **ISM (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) bands** — most commonly the **2.4 GHz** band (which is why Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and ZigBee can interfere with each other).
