# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Untethered Communication

Untethered links can be classified along **four orthogonal axes**. A KTU examiner typically asks the student to compare technologies on at least two of these axes.

### Axis 1 — By Physical Carrier
1. **Electromagnetic (EM) Wave** — Radio Frequency (RF) and Optical
2. **Acoustic** — Ultrasonic, Underwater acoustic
3. **Magnetic Induction** — Near-field (NFC, RFID)

### Axis 2 — By Range (the most common KTU comparison axis)
- **PAN** (Personal Area Network): < **10 m** — Bluetooth, ZigBee, NFC
- **LAN** (Local Area Network): 10 – **100 m** — Wi-Fi, ZigBee
- **MAN** (Metropolitan): 100 m – **10 km** — WiMAX
- **WAN** (Wide Area): > **10 km** — Cellular (GSM/4G/5G), Satellite

### Axis 3 — By Topology
- **Point-to-Point** (one base, one robot)
- **Star** (one base/AP, many robots)
- **Mesh / Ad-hoc** (robots relay for each other — ZigBee, MANET)
- **Broadcast** (satellite, cellular)

### Axis 4 — By Directionality
- **Simplex** (one-way — e.g., RF remote control)
- **Half-Duplex** (walkie-talkie — one talks at a time)
- **Full-Duplex** (phone call — both talk simultaneously)

## 2.2 Detailed Study of Each Type

### 2.2.1 Radio Frequency (RF) Communication
The umbrella category. Uses **electromagnetic waves in the 30 kHz – 300 GHz** range. Sub-divided into:
- **Low Frequency (LF, 30–300 kHz):** RFID tags
- **High Frequency (HF, 3–30 MHz):** Long-range telemetry, amateur radio
- **Ultra-High Frequency (UHF, 300 MHz – 3 GHz):** GPS (1.575 GHz), cellular
- **Super/Extremely High Frequency (SHF/EHF, 3–300 GHz):** Wi-Fi (5/6 GHz), 5G mmWave, satellite

**Utility:** Backbone of all mobile robot teleoperation. Used in UAVs, UGVs, search-and-rescue.

### 2.2.2 Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)
- **Bands:** **2.4 GHz** and **5 GHz** (Wi-Fi 6E adds **6 GHz**)
- **Range:** 30 – 100 m indoor, up to 250 m line-of-sight outdoor
- **Data Rate:** 11 Mbps (802.11b) → 9.6 Gbps (802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6)
- **Topology:** Star (via Access Point)
- **Power:** 100 mW – 1 W
- **Pros:** High bandwidth → ideal for streaming **video** from a robot
- **Cons:** High power drain, short range, congested ISM band

### 2.2.3 Bluetooth & BLE (IEEE 802.15.1 / 802.15.4 derivative)
- **Band:** **2.4 GHz** ISM, uses FHSS (Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum)
- **Range:** 1 m (Class 3), 10 m (Class 2), 100 m (Class 1)
- **Data Rate:** 1 Mbps (classic) / 2 Mbps (BLE 5.0)
- **Pros:** Ultra-low power, perfect for **sensor beacons** on a robot
- **Cons:** Limited bandwidth, pairing overhead

### 2.2.4 ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)
- **Bands:** 868 MHz (Europe), 915 MHz (USA), **2.4 GHz** (global)
- **Data Rate:** 20 – 250 kbps
- **Topology:** **Mesh** — a critical feature for multi-robot swarms because each robot can act as a router
- **Power:** ~30 mW → multi-year battery life
- **Use case:** Industrial robot fleets, warehouse AGVs, swarm robotics

### 2.2.5 Infrared (IR) Communication
- **Carrier:** Near-IR light (typically 870 nm – 940 nm wavelength)
- **Range:** < **10 m**, requires **line-of-sight** (LoS)
- **Modulation:** Pulse-coded, typically 38 kHz carrier
- **Pros:** Zero RF interference, very cheap, secure (cannot penetrate walls)
- **Cons:** Blocked by walls, sunlight can swamp the receiver
- **Use case:** Indoor robot docking, short-range remote control, optocoupler-like links between modules

### 2.2.6 Ultrasonic / Acoustic Communication
- **Carrier:** Mechanical pressure waves at **> 20 kHz**
- **Range:** 1 – 100 m in air; kilometers in water
- **Data Rate:** Very low (bits per second to a few kbps)
- **Use case:** **Underwater robots (AUVs/ROVs)** where EM waves are absorbed by water; short-range indoor beacon localization
- **Trade-off:** Speed of sound in air ≈ **343 m/s** introduces high latency

### 2.2.7 Cellular (GSM / LTE / 5G)
- **Bands:** Licensed spectrum — 700 MHz – 6 GHz for sub-6 GHz 5G; 24–40 GHz for mmWave
- **Range:** 1 – 10 km (cell tower range)
- **Data Rate:** Up to 10 Gbps (5G mmWave theoretical)
- **Use case:** Outdoor autonomous vehicles, drones beyond visual line of sight (BVLOS), agricultural robots in remote fields
- **Trade-off:** Requires SIM/subscription, latency variability

### 2.2.8 Satellite Communication
- **Bands:** L-band (1–2 GHz, Iridium), S-band, Ku-band (12–18 GHz), Ka-band (26–40 GHz)
- **Range:** Global
- **Data Rate:** 1 kbps – 100 Mbps (LEO constellations like Starlink)
- **Use case:** Space rovers, ocean-crossing AUVs, disaster-zone robots

### 2.2.9 Li-Fi / Optical Wireless (IEEE 802.11bb)
- **Carrier:** Visible or near-IR light from LEDs
- **Data Rate:** Up to 10 Gbps
- **Range:** 1 – 10 m, line-of-sight
- **Use case:** Hospitals, EMI-sensitive zones, secure indoor robots

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Every formula below has been used in previous KTU University Examinations. Memorize the **physical meaning** of each symbol, not just the equation.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-Space Path Loss with **d in km**, **f in MHz**. Doubling distance → **+6 dB** loss. Doubling frequency → **+6 dB** loss. |
| 2 | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^2$ | **Friis transmission equation.** Links transmit power, antenna gains, and wavelength to received power. |
| 3 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon–Hartley theorem.** Maximum error-free bit rate C (bps) over bandwidth B (Hz) at given S/N ratio. |
| 4 | $d_{\max} \propto \sqrt{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \, \lambda^2}$ | Derived from Friis equation. Range scales as $\sqrt{P_t}$. |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength–frequency relation. **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.** |
| 6 | $T_{\text{propagation}} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ (RF) or $\dfrac{d}{v_{\text{sound}}}$ (acoustic) | One-way time delay. Critical for real-time teleoperation. At **300 m** RF link, $T = 1\ \mu\text{s}$ (negligible). At **300 m** acoustic, $T \approx 0.87\ \text{s}$ (severe). |
| 7 | $E_b/N_0 = \dfrac{S/N}{R/B}$ | Bit-energy to noise-density ratio; used to compute link budget. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Swarm Robotics (Kilobots, Crazyflie drones):** Use **ZigBee** mesh — 30 mW per node, multihop routing, multi-day operation.
- **Search & Rescue (e.g., DARPA SubT):** Use **multi-modal** — Wi-Fi (video), mesh radio (command), acoustic (cave mapping).
- **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Tesla):** Use **cellular C-V2X** + **DSRC** (802.11p at 5.9 GHz) for vehicle-to-everything.
- **Mars Rovers (Perseverance):** Use **UHF/X-band** direct-to-Earth and **UHF relay via orbiters** — only untethered option over 200 million km.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

### 3.1.1 Start with the Isotropic Radiator

An **isotropic antenna** radiates power $P_t$ uniformly in all directions. The power flux density (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is therefore:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Reasoning:** The power is spread over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$, whose area is $4\pi d^2$. As $d$ grows, the same power is diluted over a larger area — hence the $1/d^2$ law.

### 3.1.2 Account for Directional Gain

If the transmit antenna is not isotropic but has gain $G_t$ (compared to isotropic), the flux along its main beam becomes:

$$
S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

### 3.1.3 Capture with the Receive Antenna

A receive antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures power $P_r = S \cdot A_e$, where the aperture is related to gain and wavelength by:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

### 3.1.4 Combine

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

This is the **Friis transmission equation**:

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

### 3.1.5 Extract the Free-Space Path Loss

Rearranging for the loss factor $L = P_t / P_r$ (assuming $G_t = G_r = 1$):

$$
L = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

Converting to decibels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{dB}} &= 10\log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2\right] \\
&= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\lambda = c/f$ and converting to convenient units (d in km, f in MHz):

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(d_{\text{km}}\right) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(f_{\text{MHz}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (KTU frequent):** What is the FSPL at **d = 1 km** and **f = 2400 MHz** (Wi-Fi)?
> $\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(1) + 20\log_{10}(2400) = 32.44 + 0 + 67.60 = \mathbf{100.04\ dB}$
> This is why Wi-Fi is limited to a few hundred metres — a 20 dBm (100 mW) transmitter can only deliver about $-80$ dBm, just at the receiver's noise floor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Capacity for a Robotic Data Link

The robot transmits sensor data (e.g., LIDAR @ 10 Mbps) over a channel of bandwidth $B = 20$ MHz. The measured SNR is **30 dB** (i.e., $S/N = 1000$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
&= 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
&= 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1001) \\
&\approx 20 \times 10^6 \times 9.97 \\
&\approx 199.4\ \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

The channel can support up to ~200 Mbps, comfortably carrying the 10 Mbps LIDAR stream with margin.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Range from a Given Transmit Power

Rearranging the Friis equation for $d$, given a minimum receivable power $P_{r,\min}$ (the receiver sensitivity):

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r}{P_{r,\min}}}
$$

For a **ZigBee** link at 2.4 GHz with $P_t = 10\ \text{mW}$ (10 dBm), $G_t = G_r = 2$ (small chip antenna), receiver sensitivity $P_{r,\min} = -100\ \text{dBm}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125\ \text{m} \\
P_t/P_{r,\min} &= 10\ \text{mW}/10^{-13}\ \text{W} = 10^8 \\
d_{\max} &= \frac{0.125}{4\pi}\sqrt{2 \cdot 2 \cdot 10^8} \\
&= 0.00995 \times \sqrt{4 \times 10^8} \\
&= 0.00995 \times 2 \times 10^4 \\
&\approx 199\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the empirical outdoor range of ZigBee (100–200 m).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2-Mark Comparison Favorite)

| Parameter | Wi-Fi (802.11n) | Bluetooth (BLE) | ZigBee (802.15.4) | Cellular (4G LTE) | Satellite (Iridium) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Band** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 0.7 – 2.6 GHz | 1.6 GHz (L-band) |
| **Range** | 100 m | 10 m | 100 m (mesh) | 10 km | Global |
| **Data Rate** | 300 Mbps | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 100 Mbps | 2.4 kbps |
| **Tx Power** | 200 mW | 10 mW | 30 mW | 1 W | 5 W |
| **Topology** | Star | P2P / Piconet | Star / Mesh / Tree | Star (cell) | Star (bent-pipe) |
| **Latency** | ~10 ms | ~3 ms | ~15 ms | ~50 ms | ~600 ms |
| **Best Robotic Use** | Video uplink | Wearable sensors | Swarm mesh | Outdoor UGVs | Space rovers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice the **inverse relationship** between range and data rate — long-range links (satellite) are slow; short-range links (Wi-Fi) are fast. This is a direct consequence of the Shannon–Hartley theorem combined with practical antenna and regulatory limits.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem

**Q:** A mobile robot uses a 2.4 GHz radio with $P_t = 100$ mW, isotropic antennas, and a receiver sensitivity of $-90$ dBm. Find the maximum communication range in free space.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{r,\min} &= -90\ \text{dBm} = 10^{-90/10}\ \text{mW} = 10^{-9}\ \text{mW} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W} \\
P_t &= 100\ \text{mW} = 0.1\ \text{W} \\
\text{FSPL}_{\max} &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{r,\min}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.1}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(10^{11}) = 110\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the FSPL formula with $f = 2400$ MHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
110 &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(2400) \\
110 - 32.44 - 67.60 &= 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) \\
9.96 &= 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) \\
d_{\text{km}} &= 10^{9.96/20} = 10^{0.498} \approx 3.15\ \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is the **free-space, line-of-sight** maximum. In a real cluttered environment, walls, foliage, and multipath fading typically reduce this by **20–40 dB**, i.e., a 10× to 100× range reduction. KTU expects students to mention this.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define untethered communication in the context of mobile robotics. Mention two advantages over tethered systems.**

**Model Answer:**

Untethered communication refers to the wireless transfer of command, telemetry, and sensor data between a mobile robot and a remote station (or between robots) without any physical cable connection. The information carrier is an electromagnetic or acoustic wave propagating through free space.

**Two advantages over tethered systems:**
1. **Unrestricted mobility** — the robot can traverse any terrain (stairs, rough ground, water, air) without the tether snagging or limiting range.
2. **Reduced mechanical complexity and weight** — no cable management system (spools, slip rings) is required, increasing payload capacity and reliability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write only *“wireless”* and stop. KTU expects the second part of the answer — explicit *comparison* with tethered. You lose **1 mark** if you do not name advantages.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List any three differences between Bluetooth and ZigBee protocols used in mobile robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Bluetooth (BLE) | ZigBee (802.15.4) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data rate up to **2 Mbps** | Data rate only **250 kbps** |
| 2 | Supports only **star / piconet** (max 7 slaves) | Supports **star, tree, and full mesh** (thousands of nodes) |
| 3 | **Higher power** (~10 mW), battery life days–weeks | **Lower power** (~30 mW with sleep), battery life months–years |

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks  [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an untethered communication link for a mobile robot. Identify the role of each block. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Compare Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and ZigBee under the headings: frequency band, range, data rate, topology, and typical robotic application. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A typical untethered robotic radio link consists of the following functional blocks (transmitter chain, free-space channel, receiver chain):

**Transmitter (on the robot):**
1. **Source Encoder** — Compresses sensor data (e.g., H.264 for video, deflate for LIDAR). Reduces bandwidth.
2. **Channel Encoder** — Adds redundancy using Forward Error Correction (FEC, e.g., Reed–Solomon, LDPC) so the receiver can correct bit errors caused by noise.
3. **Modulator** — Maps bits to symbols on a carrier; common schemes are BPSK, QPSK, OFDM (in Wi-Fi 802.11n).
4. **Up-converter & RF Front-End** — Shifts the baseband signal to the carrier frequency (e.g., 2.4 GHz) and filters out-of-band emissions.
5. **Power Amplifier (PA)** — Boosts signal to 10 mW – 1 W depending on the standard and regulatory limits.
6. **Transmit Antenna** — Radiates the EM wave. Small chip antennas have gain $\approx 2$ dBi; directional patch antennas $\approx 8$–12 dBi.

**Free-Space Channel:**
- The EM wave propagates at $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- Suffers **Free-Space Path Loss** $\propto 1/d^2$, plus multipath fading, shadowing, and interference.

**Receiver (on the base station):**
1. **Receive Antenna** — Captures a tiny fraction of the radiated power (often $< 10^{-9}$ of $P_t$).
2. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)** — Amplifies the weak signal while adding as little noise as possible (noise figure $\approx 2$–3 dB).
3. **Down-converter** — Mixes the RF signal back to baseband using a local oscillator.
4. **Channel Decoder** — Applies the inverse of the channel encoder to correct errors.
5. **Source Decoder** — Decompresses the data back to its original form for the base station CPU.

**Why this architecture matters in robotics:** The block ordering reflects a hierarchy of impairments — source coding removes redundancy, channel coding adds controlled redundancy, modulation chooses the right trade-off between data rate and robustness, and the RF front-end finally delivers the signal over the air. A KTU answer that simply lists the blocks without explaining *why* each block exists loses 2–3 marks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | Wi-Fi (802.11) | Bluetooth (BLE) | ZigBee (802.15.4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Band** | 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz, 915 MHz, 868 MHz |
| **Range** | 30 – 100 m indoor | 10 m (Class 2) | 10 – 100 m |
| **Data Rate** | 54 Mbps – 9.6 Gbps | 1 – 2 Mbps | 20 – 250 kbps |
| **Topology** | Star (via AP) | Piconet / Scatternet | Star, Tree, **Mesh** |
| **Modulation** | OFDM, DSSS | FHSS, GFSK | BPSK, OQPSK |
| **Power** | 200 mW – 1 W | 1 – 10 mW | 30 mW (with aggressive sleep) |
| **Robotic Use** | Video streaming from UGV/UAV | Wearable sensor / docking beacon | Swarm robotics, warehouse AGV mesh |

**Conclusion:** For a single robot streaming high-resolution video, **Wi-Fi** is best. For a single robot exchanging status with a phone, **BLE** is best. For a swarm of dozens of robots, **ZigBee mesh** is best.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss:** Students forget to mention **modulation technique** and **typical application**. Both are worth 1 mark each.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks  [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Derive the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) formula starting from the isotropic radiator assumption and show that for a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi link, the FSPL at 100 m is approximately **80 dB**. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Explain the role of **ZigBee mesh networking** in multi-robot coordination. State any two reasons why ZigBee is preferred over Wi-Fi for swarm robotics. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation (recap from Section 3.1):**

Starting from the isotropic source:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

With transmit gain $G_t$ and effective receive aperture $A_e = G_r \lambda^2 / 4\pi$:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
L = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

In decibels:

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda)
$$

For the numerical part — f = 2.4 GHz, d = 100 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125\ \text{m} \\
\text{FSPL} &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 100}{0.125}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}(10053) \\
&\approx 20 \times 4.002 \\
&\approx 80.04\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common error:** Students forget to convert **d in km** when using the short form FSPL = $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$. With d = 0.1 km and f = 2400 MHz: $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.1) + 20\log_{10}(2400) = 32.44 - 20 + 67.60 = 80.04$ dB. Both methods must give the same answer.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**ZigBee Mesh Networking in Multi-Robot Coordination:**

In a **mesh topology**, every ZigBee node (robot) can act as both a *router* and an *end device*. When the direct path to the base station is broken (by an obstacle, RF shadow, or distance), a robot's data packet is *multi-hopped* through neighbouring robots until it reaches the destination. This is implemented via protocols like **Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector (AODV)** routing.

**Co-ordination use cases:**
1. **Formation control** — robots exchange relative positions over the mesh; one robot's GPS-denied location is computed via ranging to its mesh neighbours.
2. **Task allocation** — a central planner broadcasts a job via the mesh; the nearest available robot acknowledges.
3. **Resilience** — if one robot fails, packets are automatically rerouted; the swarm does not collapse.

**Why ZigBee is preferred over Wi-Fi for swarms:**

1. **Power efficiency** — ZigBee transceivers consume ~30 mW and spend >99% of time in deep sleep, enabling multi-day operation on a coin cell. Wi-Fi radios consume 200 mW – 1 W and are continuously active, draining a mobile robot's battery in hours.
2. **Mesh scalability** — ZigBee supports thousands of nodes in a single PAN; Wi-Fi (infrastructure mode) is limited to ~32 clients per AP, requiring many APs.
3. **No licensing overhead** — ZigBee's 250 kbps is sufficient for command/telemetry, freeing expensive Wi-Fi bandwidth for sensor/video streams.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pet Peeve:** Students write *“ZigBee uses mesh, Wi-Fi does not”* without explaining *what mesh buys you*. Always link the technology choice to the **engineering requirement** (range extension, fault tolerance, scalability). Lose 1 mark for generic comparison.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Untethered Communication in Mobile Robotics**

- ☐ **Untethered = no physical cable**; carrier is EM wave, acoustic wave, or magnetic field.
- ☐ **ISM band 2.4 GHz** is shared by Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee → cross-interference is a real engineering issue.
- ☐ **FSPL** is the master equation: $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$.
- ☐ **Friis equation** links $P_r$ to $P_t$, gains, wavelength, distance — basis of every link budget.
- ☐ **Shannon's capacity** $C = B\log_2(1 + S/N)$ is the *theoretical* ceiling; real protocols reach 30–70% of it.
- ☐ **Range vs. Bandwidth trade-off:** satellite = global but slow; Wi-Fi = fast but local; this is not a coincidence, it is antenna-physics.
- ☐ **Bluetooth** = personal area, low power, point-to-point, ~10 m.
- ☐ **ZigBee** = mesh, ultra-low power, ideal for **swarm** robotics.
- ☐ **Wi-Fi** = high bandwidth, ideal for **video** and **LIDAR** streaming.
- ☐ **Cellular (4G/5G)** = long-range outdoor, needs subscription.
- ☐ **Satellite** = global, high latency, used in space and ocean robotics.
- ☐ **Acoustic** = only option for **underwater** communication (EM is absorbed in water).
- ☐ **Infrared / Li-Fi** = secure, line-of-sight, no RF interference, indoor use.
- ☐ For every KTU 14-mark question, **draw the block diagram first**, then list comparisons in a **table**, then derive or compute.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Untethered Communication. All the best for your KTU exams!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestion on Project Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestion on Project Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (Honors / Elective) Project Methodology**, a *Suggestion on Project Topics* in Mobile Robotics refers to a **curated framework of academically rigorous, industry-aligned, and technically feasible research-and-development themes** that guide a student or project team in identifying, scoping, and formulating a capstone-level problem statement within the interdisciplinary domain of **perception, cognition, navigation, control, and actuation of autonomous mobile platforms**.

A project topic in this domain is not merely a "title" — it is a **bounded engineering problem** characterized by:
- A defined **mobile agent** (UGV, UAV, AUV, legged, wheeled, aerial, marine, or swarm)
- A constrained **operational environment** (indoor, outdoor, GPS-denied, dynamic, structured, unstructured)
- A measurable **performance metric** (accuracy, latency, energy efficiency, success rate, RMSE, F1-score)
- A verifiable **methodology** (algorithmic, model-based, learning-based, or hybrid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Project Definition (Per Course Outcome CO5 / CO6):** A valid mobile robotics project must demonstrate a closed-loop **Sense → Plan → Act** architecture, irrespective of whether the implementation is hardware, simulation-based (Gazebo, Webots, CoppeliaSim, Isaac Sim), or hybrid Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **medical student choosing a surgical specialty**. They do not pick randomly; they evaluate:
- Their aptitude (interest in cardiology vs. neurology)
- Available hospital infrastructure (a cardiac catheterization lab?)
- Duration of residency (4 years vs. 7 years)
- Patient impact and novelty of contribution

**Selecting a Mobile Robotics project topic is identical.** You are essentially choosing a *specialty surgery* for your engineering "patient" — the autonomous robot. A project on *autonomous SLAM in a GPS-denied mine* is the robotic equivalent of *neurosurgery*: highly specialized, infrastructure-heavy, but immensely rewarding. Conversely, a project on *line-follower optimization* is the equivalent of *general practice* — important, but limited in novelty.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Topic Selection:**
> 1. **Feasibility Triangle** — intersects at the point of (Skill × Resources × Time)
> 2. **Novelty Quotient** — how different from existing literature / GitHub repositories
> 3. **Societal / Industrial Utility** — does the solution address a real-world pain point?

---

### 1.3 The KTU 2024 Recommended Project Topology

KTU explicitly encourages projects that integrate **at least two of the following verticals**:

| Vertical | Weightage in Evaluation | Example Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware Realization (PCB / Chassis / Sensors) | 20% | Custom-designed 4-wheel UGV with STM32 |
| Algorithm / Software Innovation (ROS 2, Python, C++) | 30% | A* vs. RRT* comparison under dynamic obstacles |
| Simulation-to-Real (Sim2Real) Transfer | 15% | Training in Isaac Sim, deploying on Jetson Nano |
| AI / ML Integration (CNN, RL, Transformer) | 20% | Vision-based pothole detection with YOLOv8 |
| Documentation / Publication Readiness | 15% | IEEE-format paper + GitHub repo + demo video |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric (for Project Evaluation):** Every project must declare a **Primary KPI (Key Performance Indicator)** such as *mean localization error (m)*, *path optimality ratio (%)*, *detection mAP@0.5*, *energy per meter (J/m)*, or *success rate over N trials*. Projects without a quantitative KPI typically lose **15–20 marks** in the final external evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Project Topic Selection Framework (PTSF) — A Structured Methodology

A robust project topic in Mobile Robotics is selected through a **five-stage funnel**:

1. **Domain Identification** — Which sub-field? (Perception, Planning, Control, SLAM, Swarm, HRI)
2. **Problem Statement Crystallization** — Convert a vague idea into a falsifiable hypothesis
3. **Feasibility Audit** — Skill matrix, budget matrix, time matrix
4. **Methodology Lock-in** — Algorithmic vs. learning-based vs. hybrid
5. **Validation Strategy** — Simulation benchmarks + real-world trials

---

### 2.2 High-Yield Analytical Categories for Mobile Robotics Projects

#### A. Perception-Centric Projects
Projects focused on how the robot "sees" the world.

- **Visual SLAM (vSLAM)** with monocular / stereo / RGB-D cameras (ORB-SLAM3, RTAB-Map)
- **LiDAR-Inertial Odometry (LIO-SAM, FAST-LIO2)**
- **Semantic Segmentation** for off-road autonomous navigation
- **3D Object Detection** for autonomous driving (PointPillars, CenterPoint)
- **Event Camera-based perception** for high-speed drones

#### B. Planning & Decision-Making Projects
Projects focused on how the robot "thinks" and decides its path.

- **Sampling-based planners** (RRT, RRT*, Informed-RRT*) in dynamic environments
- **Optimization-based planners** (MPC, Trajectory Optimization)
- **Behavior Trees** for complex mission planning
- **Reinforcement Learning** (PPO, SAC, DQN) for navigation
- **Bio-inspired planners** (Ant Colony, Particle Swarm)

#### C. Control-Centric Projects
Projects focused on how the robot "moves" precisely.

- **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** for Ackermann-steered UGVs
- **Adaptive Control** for quadrotor UAVs under payload variation
- **Whole-Body Control** for legged robots (quadrupeds)
- **PID / LQR / Sliding Mode** tuning on differential drive platforms

#### D. Multi-Agent & Swarm Projects
Projects focused on coordination.

- **Multi-Robot SLAM** with inter-robot loop closure
- **Swarm Coverage Path Planning**
- **Cooperative Transportation** with heterogeneous agents
- **Consensus Algorithms** for formation control

#### E. Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) Projects
- **Gesture-controlled drones** using MediaPipe + ROS 2
- **Natural Language Waypoint Specification** using LLMs
- **Social Navigation** in pedestrian-rich environments

#### F. Embedded & Edge-AI Projects
- **Jetson Nano / Raspberry Pi + Coral TPU** deployment
- **TinyML** for keyword-spotted robot activation
- **FPGA-accelerated SLAM front-ends**

#### G. Soft & Bio-Inspired Robotics
- **Soft grippers** for delicate manipulation
- **Bio-inspired locomotion** (snake, fish, insect)
- **Morphological computation** in soft bodies

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Project Evaluation Quantification

| Metric | Formula | Units | Typical Threshold (KTU) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Path Optimality Ratio** $\eta_{opt}$ | $\eta_{opt} = \dfrac{L_{actual}}{L_{optimal}}$ | dimensionless | $\eta_{opt} \leq 1.15$ |
| **Localization RMSE** | $RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2}$ | meters | $\leq 0.15$ m (indoor) |
| **Detection mAP@0.5** | $mAP = \dfrac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$ | ratio [0,1] | $\geq 0.70$ |
| **Energy Efficiency** | $E_m = \dfrac{E_{total}}{d_{travelled}}$ | J/m | project-defined baseline |
| **Success Rate** | $S_r = \dfrac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | $\geq 90\%$ |
| **CPU/GPU Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{total}}$ | ratio | $\leq 0.85$ (real-time) |
| **Real-time Factor (RTF)** | $RTF = \dfrac{T_{sim}}{T_{wall}}$ | dimensionless | $\geq 1.0$ (faster than real-time) |
| **F1-Score (perception)** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | ratio | $\geq 0.80$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Markdown:** In the table above, the absolute value and division bars are represented as $\dfrac{\cdot}{\cdot}$ (LaTeX display fraction) to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. In your project reports, always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value and $\dfrac{a}{b}$ for divisions.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Each project category maps to a high-impact industrial vertical:

- **Perception (vSLAM, LiDAR)** → Autonomous vehicles (Tesla, Waymo, Mobileye), AR/VR (Meta Quest), warehouse robotics (Amazon Kiva)
- **Planning (MPC, RL)** → Drone delivery (Zipline, Skydio), agricultural robotics (John Deere), defense (autonomous convoys)
- **Control (Whole-Body)** → Humanoid robotics (Tesla Optimus, Figure 01, Agility Digit), quadrupeds (Boston Dynamics Spot, Unitree)
- **Swarm** → Search & rescue, environmental monitoring, light shows (Verity Studios)
- **Soft Robotics** → Medical (surgical assistants), food handling, underwater exploration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU External Examiner Heuristic:** A project that **maps cleanly to a commercial product or a published research paper** automatically scores higher on the "Industrial Relevance" and "Research Depth" axes of the KTU evaluation rubric.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — The Project Topic Selection Derivation

### 3.1 Derivation: From "Idea" to "Valid Project Topic" — The Funnel Reduction

A raw project idea must be progressively refined. Let us derive the **mathematical reduction** of an idea into a well-scoped topic using the **Feasibility-Adjusted Novelty Index (FANI)**.

---

#### Step 1: Define the Idea Universe

Let $\mathcal{I} = \{I_1, I_2, I_3, \dots, I_n\}$ be the set of all candidate ideas. For Mobile Robotics, $n$ is typically large (often $> 50$ after brainstorming).

**Example student-generated set:**
- $I_1$: "Make a self-driving car"
- $I_2$: "Use drone to detect potholes"
- $I_3$: "Build a robot that maps a building"
- $I_4$: "Quadruped that climbs stairs"

---

#### Step 2: Apply the Novelty Filter

Novelty is a continuous value. Assign a **Novelty Score** $N(I_i) \in [0, 1]$ based on:
- Absence of direct GitHub replica
- Novelty in sensor combination
- Novelty in environment
- Novelty in evaluation metric

$$
N(I_i) = w_1 \cdot N_{repo} + w_2 \cdot N_{sensor} + w_3 \cdot N_{env} + w_4 \cdot N_{metric}
$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$.

**Example weights:** $w_1 = 0.3, w_2 = 0.25, w_3 = 0.25, w_4 = 0.20$

**Example scoring for $I_2$ (pothole drone):**
- $N_{repo} = 0.5$ (many similar repos exist)
- $N_{sensor} = 0.7$ (RGB + thermal fusion is less common)
- $N_{env} = 0.8$ (Kerala monsoon roads are a niche)
- $N_{metric} = 0.6$ (using mAP + depth-error composite)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(I_2) &= (0.3 \times 0.5) + (0.25 \times 0.7) + (0.25 \times 0.8) + (0.20 \times 0.6) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.175 + 0.20 + 0.12 \\
&= 0.645
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 3: Apply the Feasibility Filter

Feasibility $F(I_i)$ is a product of three normalized sub-scores:

$$
F(I_i) = S_{skill}(I_i) \cdot R_{resource}(I_i) \cdot T_{time}(I_i)
$$

where:
- $S_{skill} \in [0,1]$ — team's proficiency in required tools
- $R_{resource} \in [0,1]$ — availability of hardware/budget
- $T_{time} \in [0,1]$ — time-to-completion feasibility

**Example for $I_1$ (self-driving car) for a typical 4-member B.Tech team:**
- $S_{skill} = 0.3$ (team lacks deep learning experience)
- $R_{resource} = 0.2$ (no GPU cluster, no full-scale vehicle)
- $T_{time} = 0.4$ (infeasible in 6 months)

$$
F(I_1) = 0.3 \times 0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.024
$$

**Example for $I_2$ (pothole drone):**
- $S_{skill} = 0.7$ (some CV experience)
- $R_{resource} = 0.6$ (DJI Tello + laptop GPU)
- $T_{time} = 0.8$ (achievable in 4 months)

$$
F(I_2) = 0.7 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 = 0.336
$$

---

#### Step 4: Compute the FANI

$$
\text{FANI}(I_i) = N(I_i) \cdot F(I_i)
$$

**For $I_1$:** $\text{FANI} = 0.4 \times 0.024 = 0.0096$ ❌ (Eliminated)

**For $I_2$:** $\text{FANI} = 0.645 \times 0.336 = 0.2167$ ✅ (Selected)

> [!NOTE]
> The topic with the **highest FANI** is the recommended project topic. A threshold of $\text{FANI} \geq 0.15$ is suggested for KTU-level projects.

---

#### Step 5: Refine into a Falsifiable Statement

Convert $I_2$ into a valid problem statement:

> **"Design and implement a UAV-based pothole detection and geotagging system using RGB-thermal sensor fusion and a lightweight YOLOv8-nano model, validated on Kerala urban roads with a target mAP@0.5 $\geq 0.80$ and geolocation error $\leq 2$ m."**

This statement is:
- **Bounded** (UAV, pothole, urban road)
- **Falsifiable** (mAP and geolocation error are measurable)
- **Methodologically defined** (sensor fusion + YOLOv8)
- **Time-bound** (validated on real roads)

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sample Project Topics with FANI

| ID | Topic (Mobile Robotics) | N | F | FANI | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | Vision-based autonomous wheelchair with LiDAR safety layer | 0.72 | 0.42 | 0.302 | ✅ Excellent |
| $T_2$ | Multi-UAV cooperative search using PSO in a Gazebo wind field | 0.81 | 0.35 | 0.284 | ✅ Excellent |
| $T_3$ | SLAM comparison (ORB-SLAM3 vs RTAB-Map) on TurtleBot4 in KTU corridor | 0.40 | 0.85 | 0.340 | ✅ Strong |
| $T_4$ | Deep RL navigation in Webots with curriculum learning | 0.78 | 0.50 | 0.390 | ✅ Top |
| $T_5$ | Self-balancing two-wheel robot with PID vs LQR | 0.45 | 0.92 | 0.414 | ✅ Solid |
| $T_6$ | Full-scale autonomous car on public road | 0.85 | 0.04 | 0.034 | ❌ Reject |
| $T_7$ | Gesture-controlled quadcopter using MediaPipe + MAVROS | 0.65 | 0.65 | 0.423 | ✅ Top |
| $T_8$ | Underwater AUV pipeline inspection with sonar SLAM | 0.88 | 0.18 | 0.158 | ⚠ Borderline |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Project Topic Selection — Domain Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    MR[Mobile Robotics Project Domain]
    MR --> PERC[Perception Stack]
    MR --> PLAN[Planning and Decision]
    MR --> CTRL[Control Systems]
    MR --> SWRM[Swarm and Multi-Agent]
    MR --> HRI[Human Robot Interaction]
    MR --> EDGE[Edge AI and Embedded]
    MR --> SOFT[Soft and Bio-Inspired]

    PERC --> P1[Visual SLAM]
    PERC --> P2[LiDAR Odometry]
    PERC --> P3[3D Object Detection]
    PERC --> P4[Event Camera Vision]

    PLAN --> PL1[Sampling Based Planners]
    PLAN --> PL2[Optimization Based MPC]
    PLAN --> PL3[Reinforcement Learning]
    PLAN --> PL4[Bio Inspired Planners]

    CTRL --> C1[PID and LQR Tuning]
    CTRL --> C2[Whole Body Control]
    CTRL --> C3[Adaptive Control]
    CTRL --> C4[Sliding Mode Control]

    SWRM --> S1[Multi Robot SLAM]
    SWRM --> S2[Swarm Coverage]
    SWRM --> S3[Formation Control]

    HRI --> H1[Gesture Control]
    HRI --> H2[LLM based Waypoints]
    HRI --> H3[Social Navigation]

    EDGE --> E1[Jetson Nano Deployment]
    EDGE --> E2[TinyML Activation]
    EDGE --> E3[FPGA Acceleration]

    SOFT --> SF1[Soft Grippers]
    SOFT --> SF2[Bio Locomotion]
    SOFT --> SF3[Morphological Computing]
```

### 4.2 The Five-Stage Project Topic Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Domain Brainstorm] --> B[Stage 2: Novelty Filter N]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Feasibility Audit F]
    C --> D[Stage 4: FANI Computation]
    D --> E{FANI greater than 0.15?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Stage 5: Falsifiable Refinement]
    E -->|No| A
    F --> G[Final Project Topic]
```

### 4.3 The Sense-Plan-Act Architecture (Mandatory in Every Project)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Sensors: Camera, LiDAR, IMU, GPS, Ultrasonic] --> PROC[Perception: Preprocessing, Feature Extraction, SLAM, Detection]
    PROC --> DEC[Planning: Path Planning, Behavior Tree, MPC, RL Policy]
    DEC --> ACT[Actuators: Motors, Servos, ESCs, Wheels, Propellers]
    ACT --> ENV[Environment: Indoor, Outdoor, GPS Denied, Dynamic]
    ENV -.Feedback.-> SENS
    ACT -.Feedback.-> SENS
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Sample Project — Autonomous Quadruped Stair Climbing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input
        A1[RGB-D Camera]
        A2[IMU 6-Axis]
        A3[Joint Encoders]
    end

    subgraph Perception Module
        B1[Stair Detection CNN]
        B2[Depth Estimation]
        B3[State Estimation EKF]
    end

    subgraph Planning Module
        C1[Footstep Planner]
        C2[Trajectory Optimizer]
        C3[Balance Controller MPC]
    end

    subgraph Control Module
        D1[Whole Body Controller]
        D2[Joint Torque Commands]
    end

    subgraph Actuation
        E1[12x Servo Motors]
        E2[Quadruped Chassis]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
```

### 4.5 Project Execution Timeline (KTU 6-Month Standard)

```mermaid
gantt
    title KTU Mobile Robotics Project Timeline (6 Months)
    dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD
    section Phase 1
    Literature Survey and Topic Finalization :a1, 2024-07-01, 30d
    section Phase 2
    System Design and Hardware Procurement :a2, after a1, 30d
    section Phase 3
    Implementation and Simulation :a3, after a2, 60d
    section Phase 4
    Real World Testing and Iteration :a4, after a3, 45d
    section Phase 5
    Documentation Report and Demo Prep :a5, after a4, 15d
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a "valid Mobile Robotics project topic" as per the KTU 2024 evaluation rubric. List any three mandatory components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A valid Mobile Robotics project topic is a **bounded, falsifiable engineering problem** that demonstrates a closed-loop **Sense → Plan → Act** architecture. The three mandatory components are:
1. A defined **mobile agent** (UGV, UAV, AUV, legged, etc.) with clear kinematics/dynamics.
2. A measurable **performance metric** (e.g., RMSE, mAP, success rate, energy per meter).
3. A **validation strategy** combining simulation benchmarks and at least one real-world trial.

*(1 mark each — Marks awarded: Definition 1, List 1 mark for all three components + 1 mark for closing statement.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*What is the "Feasibility-Adjusted Novelty Index (FANI)"? State its formula and threshold.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
FANI is a scalar metric that combines the **Novelty Score (N)** and the **Feasibility Score (F)** of a project idea to enable quantitative comparison among candidate topics.

$$
\text{FANI}(I_i) = N(I_i) \times F(I_i)
$$

where $N \in [0,1]$ is the weighted novelty score and $F = S_{skill} \cdot R_{resource} \cdot T_{time}$ is the product of skill, resource, and time feasibility sub-scores. A threshold of $\text{FANI} \geq 0.15$ is recommended for KTU-level projects.

*(Formula 2 marks, definition + threshold 1 mark.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided — hence **Question A** and **Question B** are offered as alternatives.

---

#### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *A team of four B.Tech students is considering the following project topics:*
- *T1: Autonomous wheelchair for hospital navigation using ROS 2 and a depth camera.*
- *T2: Full-scale self-driving car on Kerala public roads.*
- *T3: Quadruped robot stair-climbing using deep reinforcement learning.*

*Apply the FANI methodology to evaluate and rank these topics. Show all sub-score calculations. Justify your final ranking using a quantitative threshold.*

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assign Novelty Scores (N):**
- $N(T_1) = 0.40$ (limited novelty — hospital AMR is a mature field)
- $N(T_2) = 0.85$ (high novelty — full-scale autonomy in mixed Kerala traffic)
- $N(T_3) = 0.78$ (high novelty — DRL for legged locomotion is active research)

**Step 2 — Assign Feasibility Sub-scores (S, R, T):**

| Topic | $S_{skill}$ | $R_{resource}$ | $T_{time}$ | $F$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 0.80 | 0.75 | 0.85 | 0.510 |
| $T_2$ | 0.30 | 0.05 | 0.20 | 0.003 |
| $T_3$ | 0.55 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.110 |

**Step 3 — Compute FANI:**

$$
\text{FANI}(T_1) = 0.40 \times 0.510 = 0.204 \quad ✅
$$

$$
\text{FANI}(T_2) = 0.85 \times 0.003 = 0.0026 \quad ❌
$$

$$
\text{FANI}(T_3) = 0.78 \times 0.110 = 0.0858 \quad ⚠
$$

**Step 4 — Final Ranking using threshold $\text{FANI} \geq 0.15$:**

**Final Rank:** $T_1 > T_3 > T_2$

Only **T1** clears the threshold and is recommended. T3 is borderline (could be salvaged with simulation-only scope). T2 must be rejected due to infeasibility despite high novelty.

*(Scoring: Sub-score table 3 marks, FANI calculations 3 marks, final ranking with justification 1 mark.)*

---

**(b)** *Refine T1 (Autonomous wheelchair) into a falsifiable problem statement suitable for KTU submission. Identify the **primary KPI**, the **operational environment**, and propose a **validation strategy** combining simulation and real-world trials.*

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Refined Problem Statement:**

> *"Design and implement a depth-camera-based autonomous wheelchair navigation system using ROS 2 Humble on a Jetson Orin Nano, capable of obstacle-aware point-to-point navigation in a multi-floor hospital corridor (50 m × 3 m), achieving a success rate $\geq 90\%$ over 20 trials with a mean deviation from optimal path $\leq 0.20$ m."*

**Primary KPI:** Success Rate $S_r \geq 90\%$ (with secondary KPI: Path Optimality Ratio $\eta_{opt} \leq 1.20$).

**Operational Environment:** Indoor hospital corridor — controlled lighting, dynamic obstacles (patients, stretchers), 2D planar navigation, multiple doorway crossings.

**Validation Strategy (2 stages):**

| Stage | Environment | Tool | Trials |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Simulation | Gazebo + Hospital World Plugin | ROS 2 + Nav2 Stack | 100 trials |
| 2. Real-world | Actual KTU / Govt. Medical College corridor (with IRB approval) | Hardware deployment | 20 trials |

**Hardware Stack:**
- **Compute:** NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano
- **Sensors:** Intel RealSense D435i (RGB-D), 2D LiDAR (RPLidar A1)
- **Drive:** Differential drive with wheelchair-motor interface (relay + H-bridge)
- **Software:** ROS 2 Humble, Nav2, RTAB-Map for localization

*(Scoring: Problem statement 2 marks, KPI 2 marks, Validation strategy 2 marks, Hardware stack 1 mark.)*

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Compare and contrast the following four project domains for a KTU 2024 Mobile Robotics capstone: (i) Visual SLAM, (ii) Reinforcement Learning-based Navigation, (iii) Multi-Robot Swarm Coverage, (iv) Edge-AI Embedded Deployment. Use the dimensions: novelty, hardware cost, computational demand, and required expertise.*

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | Visual SLAM | RL Navigation | Swarm Coverage | Edge-AI Embedded |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Novelty** | Medium (mature field, room for niche improvements) | High (active research) | High (less explored) | Medium-High (deployment-focused) |
| **Hardware Cost** | Medium (camera + GPU laptop) | Medium-High (training GPUs) | High (multiple robots) | Low-Medium (Jetson/RPi + Coral) |
| **Computational Demand** | Medium (real-time on GPU) | Very High (training phase) | Medium (distributed) | Low (must fit edge) |
| **Required Expertise** | CV, linear algebra, optimization | Deep learning, RL theory, simulation | Distributed systems, graph theory | Embedded C++, quantization, ROS 2 |
| **Publication Potential** | Medium | High | High | Medium |

**Recommended for KTU 2024 cohorts with limited budget and time:** Visual SLAM or Edge-AI deployment.

**Recommended for ambitious research-oriented students with GPU access:** RL Navigation or Swarm Coverage.

*(Scoring: 4 domains × 1 dimension per mark allocation; balance 4 marks for table, 3 marks for recommendation.)*

---

**(b)** *A team chooses the topic: "Multi-UAV cooperative package delivery in a wind-disturbed urban environment." Identify three major risks and propose one mitigation strategy per risk. Also declare the FANI-relevant sub-scores and decide whether the topic is feasible.*

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Three Major Risks:**

1. **Wind-disturbance modeling complexity** — Real wind is stochastic; Gaussian models are insufficient. *Mitigation:* Use a **Dryden wind turbulence model** in Gazebo with $\sigma_u = 0.5$ m/s and validate with real anemometer data.
2. **Inter-UAV communication latency** — Wi-Fi handoffs in urban areas cause packet loss. *Mitigation:* Use **ROS 2 DDS with QoS reliability settings** and a **fallback consensus protocol** (e.g., RAFT) when communication drops.
3. **Payload variation affecting flight dynamics** — Different package weights change mass and inertia. *Mitigation:* Implement an **adaptive MPC controller** with online mass estimation using an **onboard force sensor**.

**FANI Sub-scores:**
- $N = 0.82$ (high novelty in cooperative urban UAV)
- $S_{skill} = 0.45$ (team needs RL + multi-agent training)
- $R_{resource} = 0.55$ (multiple UAVs + wind tunnel or Gazebo plugin)
- $T_{time} = 0.50$ (achievable in simulation + scaled real test)

**Feasibility Calculation:**

$$
F = 0.45 \times 0.55 \times 0.50 = 0.124
$$

$$
\text{FANI} = 0.82 \times 0.124 = 0.1017
$$

**Verdict:** **Borderline (FANI < 0.15).** The team should either:
- **Scope-down** to a **single-UAV variant with wind robustness**, or
- **Augment skills** (3 weeks of ROS 2 + multi-agent training) to push $S_{skill} \geq 0.65$, raising FANI to $\geq 0.15$.

*(Scoring: 3 risks with mitigations = 3 marks, FANI sub-scores 2 marks, verdict + scope-down 2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Reasons for Mark Deduction:**
> 1. **No quantitative KPI declared** → Lose up to **3 marks** in the final evaluation.
> 2. **Pure simulation project without any hardware intent** → Lose up to **5 marks**; KTU 2024 prefers at least a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) component.
> 3. **Overly broad problem statement** ("Build a self-driving car") without bounds → Lose **2–3 marks** for lack of falsifiability.
> 4. **FANI-style evaluation with arithmetic errors** → Always show explicit multiplication steps; do not skip the product computation.
> 5. **Ignoring Novelty — replicating an existing GitHub project verbatim** → Lose marks for lack of innovation.
> 6. **Skipping the Sense → Plan → Act flow in the architecture diagram** → Lose up to **2 marks** in design evaluation.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A valid **Mobile Robotics project topic** must have a **mobile agent**, an **operational environment**, a **measurable KPI**, and a **validation strategy**.
- ✅ Every project must implement the closed-loop **Sense → Plan → Act** architecture.
- ✅ The **FANI (Feasibility-Adjusted Novelty Index)** quantifies project quality: $\text{FANI} = N \times F$ where $F = S_{skill} \cdot R_{resource} \cdot T_{time}$.
- ✅ Recommended **FANI threshold** for KTU projects: $\geq 0.15$.
- ✅ KTU 2024 encourages integration of **hardware + algorithm + simulation + AI/ML + documentation**.
- ✅ Always declare a **Primary KPI** in the project proposal (RMSE, mAP, success rate, energy/m, F1-score).
- ✅ High-impact project domains: **Visual/LiDAR SLAM, RL-based navigation, MPC control, swarm robotics, HRI, edge-AI deployment, soft robotics**.
- ✅ Real-world validation is **mandatory** for top marks — pure simulation projects lose 4–5 marks.
- ✅ Use **ROS 2 Humble** as the default middleware for all 2024 KTU Mobile Robotics projects.
- ✅ Simulation tools: **Gazebo (Ignition), Webots, CoppeliaSim, NVIDIA Isaac Sim, MATLAB Simulink**.
- ✅ Hardware kits: **TurtleBot 4, JetBot, Unitree Go1, DJI Tello, Crazyflie, Pixhawk, RPLidar, Intel RealSense**.
- ✅ KTU evaluation weightage: **Hardware 20% + Algorithm 30% + Sim2Real 15% + AI/ML 20% + Documentation 15%**.
- ✅ When in doubt, **scope-down** the project (narrower environment, simpler benchmark) rather than overpromise.
- ✅ Maintain a **GitHub repository, IEEE-format report, and a 3-minute demo video** — these are the KTU 2024 documentation trifecta.
- ✅ **Pitfalls to avoid:** vague problem statements, no KPI, no real-world test, GitHub replication, and arithmetic errors in FANI calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Students May Be Given Projects on Modelling to Kinematics, Localization and Path Planning of Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT604 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Students May Be Given Projects on Modelling to Kinematics, Localization and Path Planning of Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — PROJECT TOPIC: KINEMATICS, LOCALIZATION & PATH PLANNING OF MOBILE ROBOTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBRAT604 – Mobile Robotics**, Module 4 is a **project-based learning (PBL) module** in which students are expected to model, simulate, and analyze the complete autonomy stack of a wheeled mobile robot (WMR). The module formally integrates three foundational pillars of mobile robotics:

1. **Kinematic Modelling** – Deriving the geometric and time-rate relationships between the robot's actuator inputs (wheel velocities) and its resulting motion in the world frame (pose $\{x, y, \theta\}$).
2. **Localization** – Estimating the robot's pose relative to a global reference frame using sensor data, odometry, and probabilistic filters.
3. **Path Planning** – Computing a collision-free trajectory from a start pose to a goal pose in a known (or partially known) environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Module 4 is assessed primarily through a *mini-project report* and *viva-voce*. The project must demonstrate end-to-end integration: a student is expected to mathematically model a chosen WMR (e.g., differential drive, Ackermann, or omnidirectional), implement at least one localization algorithm (e.g., dead reckoning or Kalman filter), and execute a path planner (e.g., A* or RRT) — preferably in a simulator like **MATLAB, Gazebo, or ROS/Python** with a clear results section.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are blindfolded in a large, unfamiliar shopping mall and given a list of items to find:

- **Kinematic Modelling** is like understanding how your legs and wheels move — knowing that turning the left wheel forward while keeping the right wheel stationary will make you pivot clockwise. Without this, you cannot *predict* where you will end up.
- **Localization** is like knowing *where you currently are* on the mall map. You use clues (the smell of a coffee shop, the echo of a fountain, your phone's Wi-Fi) and combine them with memory of how you walked (odometry) to update your position belief.
- **Path Planning** is like deciding the optimal route through the mall: avoiding the closed food court, choosing the shortest queue-free corridor, and reaching the bookstore efficiently.

A mobile robot in an unknown environment faces **exactly** the same three problems — and Module 4 asks you to build all three for a simulated (or real) robot.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
The following constants and metrics are universally adopted in KTU board examinations and standard textbooks (Siegwart, Thrun, Choset):

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | $\mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$ |
| $R$ | Wheel radius | **metres (m)** |
| $L$ or $b$ | Wheelbase / axle length | **metres (m)** |
| $d$ | Caster offset | **metres (m)** |
| $v$ | Linear velocity of robot | $\mathbf{m/s}$ |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity of robot | $\mathbf{rad/s}$ |
| $\xi$ | Robot pose vector | $[x, y, \theta]^T$ |
| $\Sigma$ | Pose covariance matrix | $3 \times 3$ symmetric positive definite |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Pose Convention:** A mobile robot's configuration in a 2D plane is always given as $\xi = (x, y, \theta)$ where $(x,y)$ is the position of the robot's reference point in the world frame, and $\theta$ is the heading angle measured counter-clockwise from the positive X-axis.

### Scope of the Module 4 Project
A typical KTU Module 4 project proposal must explicitly cover the following workflow:

- **Robot Selection:** Choose a kinematic configuration (differential drive is the most common in KTU projects due to simplicity).
- **Environment:** 2D occupancy grid or continuous workspace with known obstacles.
- **Sensors:** Encoders (for odometry), range finders / LiDAR (for localization), GPS (for ground-truth comparison).
- **Algorithms:** Minimum one from each pillar — Kinematics, Localization, Path Planning.
- **Validation:** Comparison of planned vs. actual trajectory, RMSE in pose estimation, and computational time analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kinematic Modelling of Wheeled Mobile Robots

A mobile robot's motion is **non-holonomic** for most practical wheel configurations — meaning it cannot move instantaneously in any direction in its configuration space. This constraint makes kinematic modelling both essential and mathematically interesting.

### 2.1.1 The General Kinematic Model
For a robot moving on a 2D plane, the pose evolves according to the unicycle model:

$$\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

This can be compactly written as $\dot{\xi} = f(\xi, u)$ where $u = [v, \omega]^T$ is the control input vector.

### 2.1.2 The Differential Drive Robot (DDR)
The most common WMR in KTU projects. It has two independently driven wheels on a common axle and a caster (or passive) wheel for balance.

- **Why this model?** Two motor inputs → two outputs (linear and angular velocity), making the system fully controllable in the planar sense.
- **How it works:** Each wheel's angular velocity $\omega_i$ and radius $R_i$ produce a linear velocity $v_i = R_i \omega_i$ at the contact point. The difference between left and right wheel velocities creates rotation.

For a symmetric DDR with $R_L = R_R = R$ and axle length $2b$ (wheel-to-wheel distance) or sometimes denoted $L$:

$$v = \frac{R}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

$$\omega = \frac{R}{2b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

The **forward kinematic equation** in the world frame is then:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{R}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{R}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{R}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{R}{2}\sin\theta \\ -\frac{R}{2b} & \frac{R}{2b} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.1.3 The Ackermann Steering Model (Car-Like Robot)
Used for car-like robots (Autonomous vehicles — popular KTU project choice).

- **Why this model?** Realistic modelling of a car where the inner and outer wheels turn at different angles to avoid tire slip.
- **How it works:** The steering angle $\phi$ of the front wheels and the linear velocity $v$ determine the Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR) at distance $R_{ICR} = L / \tan\phi$ from the rear axle.

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$

### 2.1.4 Omnidirectional / Mecanum Wheels
For robots that can translate in any direction without reorientation. The Jacobian relates each wheel's velocity to the robot's body twist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In the board exam, when asked to "model a mobile robot," the student MUST clearly state the **non-holonomic constraint** equation, e.g., for a DDR:
> $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$
> This is a Pfaffian constraint that reduces the *Degrees of Freedom (DOF)* of configuration space motion.

## 2.2 Localization — Probabilistic Pose Estimation

### 2.2.1 Why Probability?
Sensor measurements are **noisy**, actuator commands are **inexact**, and environments have **ambiguities** (e.g., two identical corridors). Localization is therefore formulated as estimating the posterior probability distribution:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

over the robot's pose $x_t$ given all sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and all control inputs $u_{1:t}$ up to time $t$.

### 2.2.2 Bayes Filter — The Theoretical Backbone
The recursive Bayes filter is the foundation of ALL modern localization algorithms:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

where:
- $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ = **motion model** (prediction)
- $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ = **measurement model** (correction)
- $\eta$ = normalization constant

### 2.2.3 Three Practical Implementations

**(a) Dead Reckoning (Open-Loop Odometry)**
The simplest form: integrate wheel encoder readings.

$$x_{t} = x_{t-1} + v\Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1}$$
$$y_{t} = y_{t-1} + v\Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1}$$
$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Dead reckoning is open-loop — errors accumulate **unboundedly** with time. KTU examiners frequently test this concept by asking "Why does odometry drift?"

**(b) Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization**
Linearizes the non-linear motion and measurement models using Jacobians.

Prediction step:
$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$
$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

Update step:
$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$
$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$$
$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

where $G_t = \partial g / \partial x$ and $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$ are the Jacobians.

**(c) Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)**
Represents the belief by a set of $M$ weighted samples $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$. Resampling concentrates particles in high-likelihood regions.

## 2.3 Path Planning

### 2.3.1 Problem Formulation
Given:
- A map $M$ (occupancy grid, polygon, or topological graph)
- Start pose $x_{start}$
- Goal pose $x_{goal}$
- A robot footprint $C$

Find a continuous, collision-free path $\tau: [0,1] \to C_{free}$ such that $\tau(0) = x_{start}$ and $\tau(1) = x_{goal}$.

### 2.3.2 Configuration Space ($C_{space}$)
The space of all possible robot configurations. For a 2D mobile robot, $C_{space} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times [0, 2\pi)$, partitioned into $C_{free}$ (free space) and $C_{obs}$ (obstacle space).

### 2.3.3 Graph Search Planners

**A\* Algorithm** (most common in KTU projects — admissible and optimal with consistent heuristic):

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where $g(n)$ is the cost from start to $n$ and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal. Common heuristic: **Euclidean distance** or **Octile distance** for grid maps.

**Dijkstra's Algorithm:** Same as A* but with $h(n) = 0$ — guaranteed optimal but explores more nodes.

### 2.3.4 Sampling-Based Planners

**Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT):**
A randomized incremental algorithm that builds a tree $T$ by sampling random configurations in $C_{free}$ and extending the nearest vertex toward the sample.

```
RRT_BUILD(q_init, K, Δq):
    T.init(q_init)
    for k = 1 to K:
        q_rand  ← RANDOM_CONFIG()
        q_near  ← NEAREST_NEIGHBOR(T, q_rand)
        q_new   ← STEER(q_near, q_rand, Δq)
        if COLLISION_FREE(q_near, q_new):
            T.add_vertex(q_new)
            T.add_edge(q_near, q_new)
    return T
```

### 2.3.5 Reactive / Local Planners

**Dynamic Window Approach (DWA):**
Samples admissible velocities, simulates short trajectories, and selects the velocity tuple $(v, \omega)$ that maximizes an objective combining heading alignment, clearance, and velocity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Domain** | **Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unicycle Model | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Generic 2-DOF planar motion | m/s, rad/s |
| DDR Linear Velocity | $v = \frac{R}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Average wheel speed | m/s |
| DDR Angular Velocity | $\omega = \frac{R}{2b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Differential speed | rad/s |
| Ackermann Turning Radius | $R_{ICR} = L/\tan\phi$ | ICR distance from rear axle | m |
| Non-holonomic Constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | DDR cannot slide sideways | dimensionless |
| Odometry Update | $x_t = x_{t-1} + v\Delta t\cos\theta_{t-1}$ | Dead reckoning integration | m |
| Bayes Filter Prediction | $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\text{bel}(x_{t-1})dx$ | Convolution of prior with motion model | probability |
| Bayes Filter Update | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t)\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | Sensor correction step | probability |
| EKF Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Optimal linear fusion | matrix |
| A* Evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Path cost + heuristic | cost units |
| Euclidean Heuristic | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ | Admissible for any map | distance |
| Octile Heuristic | $h(n) = (|dx| + |dy|) + (\sqrt{2} - 2)\min(|dx|,|dy|)$ | Admissible for 8-connected grid | distance |
| Robot Reachability | $\dot{\xi} = J(q)\dot{q}$ | Joint-to-task-space mapping | m/s, rad/s |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the rigorous mathematical derivations that a KTU student must master for both the project report and the viva-voce. Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Drive Forward Kinematics

### Step 1 — Set Up the Coordinate Frames
Define two frames:
- **World frame $\{W\}$** — fixed inertial frame on the ground.
- **Robot frame $\{R\}$** — attached to the robot's centre, with X-axis along the forward direction.

Let the robot's pose in $\{W\}$ be $\xi = (x, y, \theta)^T$ where $\theta$ is the orientation of $\{R\}$ relative to $\{W\}$.

### Step 2 — Express Each Wheel's Contribution
Each wheel $i \in \{L, R\}$ has an angular velocity $\omega_i$ (rad/s) and radius $R$. The linear velocity of the wheel's contact point with the ground is:

$$v_i = R \cdot \omega_i$$

This is the **no-slip rolling constraint**.

### Step 3 — Compute the Robot's Centre Velocity
The two contact points are on opposite sides of the robot centre, separated by a total wheelbase of $2b$. If both wheels spin at the same velocity, the robot translates forward. If they differ, the robot rotates.

The **linear velocity** at the robot's centre is the *average* of the two wheel velocities (since the centre is the midpoint of the axle):

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{R(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Angular Velocity
The angular velocity of the rigid body (the robot frame rotating about the ICR) is derived from the difference in wheel velocities divided by the wheelbase:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2b} = \frac{R(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{2b}$$

### Step 5 — Transform to the World Frame
The robot's body-frame velocity $[v, 0, \omega]^T$ must be rotated into the world frame using the rotation matrix $R_z(\theta)$:

$$R_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying this to the body velocity vector:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the DDR Expressions
Combining Steps 3, 4, and 5:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= \frac{R}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= \frac{R}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \frac{R}{2b}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Write in Matrix Form
The control input is $u = [\omega_L, \omega_R]^T$. The complete forward kinematic map is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \frac{R}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{R}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{R}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{R}{2}\sin\theta \\ -\frac{R}{2b} & \frac{R}{2b} \end{bmatrix}}_{J(\xi)} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Step 7 Explanation:** The matrix $J(\xi)$ is the **configuration-dependent Jacobian** of the DDR. It explicitly depends on $\theta$, confirming the system is non-linear. For a stationary configuration, the determinant of the $2 \times 2$ lower-right submatrix $\left[\begin{smallmatrix} -R/2b & R/2b \end{smallmatrix}\right]$ is **non-zero** iff $b \neq 0$, ensuring controllability.

## 3.2 Derivation: Dead Reckoning Update Equations

### Step 1 — Discretize Time
Sample encoder data every $\Delta t$ seconds. The encoder reports incremental wheel rotations $\Delta\phi_L$ and $\Delta\phi_R$.

### Step 2 — Compute Incremental Wheel Displacements
$$d_L = R \cdot \Delta\phi_L, \quad d_R = R \cdot \Delta\phi_R$$

### Step 3 — Compute Incremental Robot Motion
Average displacement (translation):

$$D_c = \frac{d_R + d_L}{2}$$

Incremental orientation change (rotation):

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{d_R - d_L}{2b}$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Robot's Reference Pose
The pose update uses the robot's *current* orientation $\theta_{t-1}$ (or, for higher accuracy, the *midpoint* orientation $\theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta/2$). The midpoint correction is called the **Exact Mileage Method** and is required for large $\Delta\theta$.

### Step 5 — Update Pose Using Mid-Point Orientation
$$\begin{aligned} x_t &= x_{t-1} + D_c \cos\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\ y_t &= y_{t-1} + D_c \sin\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\ \theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta \end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Simplified Euler Integration (Small $\Delta t$)
For sufficiently small $\Delta t$ where $\Delta\theta$ is negligible:

$$\begin{aligned} x_t &= x_{t-1} + v\Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ y_t &= y_{t-1} + v\Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ \theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Step 6 Explanation:** The Euler approximation is the first-order Taylor expansion of the continuous-time unicycle model. It is computationally cheap but introduces a discretization error of order $O(\Delta t^2)$ per step, which compounds over time and contributes to odometry drift.

## 3.3 Derivation: EKF Localization Linearization

### Step 1 — Start with the Non-Linear System
The motion model is $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \epsilon_t$ and the measurement model is $z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ and $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.

### Step 2 — Linearize $g$ around the Current Estimate
Use a first-order Taylor expansion at $x_{t-1} = \mu_{t-1}$:

$$g(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$

where $G_t = \left.\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\right|_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$ is the **motion Jacobian**.

### Step 3 — Linearize $h$ around the Predicted State
$$h(x_t) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)$$

where $H_t = \left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right|_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ is the **measurement Jacobian**.

### Step 4 — Compute the Predicted Mean and Covariance
$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Step 4 Explanation:** The covariance update captures two effects: the previous uncertainty is propagated forward through the linearized dynamics ($G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T$), and the *new* process noise $R_t$ is added. The matrix $G_t$ amplifies the prior uncertainty in directions that the system is sensitive to.

### Step 5 — Compute the Innovation and Kalman Gain
The **innovation** (measurement residual) is:

$$\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$$

The **innovation covariance** (uncertainty in the predicted measurement) is:

$$S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$$

The **Kalman gain** (optimal blending factor) is:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$$

### Step 6 — Update the Mean and Covariance
$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Kalman Gain Looks Like This:** $K_t$ minimizes the *a posteriori* covariance $\Sigma_t$. Intuitively, if the sensor is very accurate (small $Q_t$), $S_t$ is small and $K_t$ becomes large — meaning we trust the measurement more than the prediction. If the prediction is very accurate (small $\bar{\Sigma}_t$), $K_t$ becomes small — meaning we trust the prediction more.

## 3.4 Derivation: A* Optimality from Admissibility

### Step 1 — Definitions
- **Admissible heuristic:** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ where $h^*(n)$ is the true cost-to-goal.
- **Consistent (monotone) heuristic:** $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every neighbour $n'$.

### Step 2 — Admissibility Implies Optimality
If $h$ is admissible, A* is guaranteed to return an optimal path because it expands nodes in non-decreasing order of $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. When a goal node is popped from the open list, $f(\text{goal}) \le f(\text{any unexpanded node}) \le f(\text{any other goal}) = g^*(\text{goal})$.

### Step 3 — A* with Euclidean Heuristic on a Grid
For a 2D grid with cardinal cost 1 and diagonal cost $\sqrt{2}$, the **octile distance** is the tightest admissible heuristic:

$$h_{oct}(n) = (|dx| + |dy|) + (\sqrt{2} - 2)\min(|dx|, |dy|)$$

where $dx = x_n - x_g$ and $dy = y_n - y_g$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The formula separates the path into $(\min(|dx|,|dy|))$ diagonal steps and $(|dx| - \min) + (|dy| - \min)$ straight steps. Since $\sqrt{2} - 2 \approx -0.586 < 0$, this gives $h_{oct} \le$ Manhattan distance, ensuring admissibility.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection for KTU Projects

| **Algorithm** | **Computational Complexity** | **Optimal?** | **Complete?** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra | $O(\vert V \vert \log\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Yes (uniform cost) | Yes | Small grids, uniform cost |
| A* (Euclidean) | $O(b^d)$, $b$ = branching, $d$ = depth | Yes (admissible $h$) | Yes | Known grid maps, KTU default |
| Weighted A* ($\epsilon > 1$) | $O(\log W)$ with bound $W$ | No ($\epsilon$-suboptimal) | Yes | Time-critical real-time planning |
| RRT | $O(n \log n)$ for $n$ samples | No (probabilistically complete) | Probabilistically | High-dimensional spaces |
| RRT* | $O(n \log n)$ | Asymptotically optimal | Probabilistically | High-DOF manipulators |
| DWA | $O(N_v \cdot N_\omega \cdot T_{sim})$ | No (local minima possible) | Local | Dynamic obstacle avoidance |
| Particle Filter (MCL) | $O(M)$ for $M$ particles | Approximate (converges as $M \to \infty$) | Yes (global) | Global localization / Kidnapped Robot |
