# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the non-holonomic constraint** for a DDR — the examiner will deduct **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Confusing inverse kinematics with a mobile robot** — a WMR's inverse kinematics is a *kinematic constraint satisfaction problem*, not a closed-form inverse like in manipulators. Acknowledge this distinction.
> 3. **Not specifying frame conventions** — Always state whether velocities are in body frame or world frame.
> 4. **Using Manhattan distance on a 4-connected grid as the A* heuristic** — admissible but **inadmissible** on an 8-connected grid (since diagonals cost $\sqrt 2$). Use Euclidean or Octile.
> 5. **Stating "EKF is optimal"** — EKF is only optimal for *linear* systems with Gaussian noise. For non-linear systems, it is a *first-order approximation*.
> 6. **Plotting odometry without ground truth** — The examiner expects a comparison plot with GPS or motion-capture truth, plus an RMSE numerical value.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the configuration space of a mobile robot and explain its importance in path planning. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **configuration space** ($C_{space}$) is the set of all possible configurations (poses) that a robot can attain. For a 2D differential-drive robot, $C_{space} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times [0, 2\pi)$ since the pose $\xi = (x, y, \theta)$ includes 2 translational and 1 rotational DOF. (1 mark)
- $C_{space}$ is partitioned into two disjoint subsets: $C_{free}$ (collision-free configurations) and $C_{obs}$ (configurations in collision with obstacles). (1 mark)
- **Importance:** Path planning is formulated as finding a continuous path in $C_{free}$ from $\xi_{start}$ to $\xi_{goal}$. The non-holonomic constraint of a DDR (i.e., $\dot x \sin\theta - \dot y \cos\theta = 0$) restricts feasible paths to manifolds within $C_{free}$. (1 mark)

### Question 2: Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic mobile robots with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **holonomic** robot can instantaneously move in *any* direction in its configuration space. The controllable DOFs equal the total DOFs. Example: an **omnidirectional robot with mecanum wheels** — it can translate in X, Y, and rotate independently. (1.5 marks)
- A **non-holonomic** robot has *fewer controllable DOFs* than total DOFs due to kinematic constraints. It cannot move sideways instantaneously. Example: a **differential drive robot** constrained by $\dot x \sin\theta - \dot y \cos\theta = 0$. (1.5 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a **differential drive robot (DDR)** with wheel radius $R$ and wheelbase $2b$. Express the result in matrix form. Clearly state the non-holonomic constraint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $R = 0.05$ m, wheelbase $2b = 0.30$ m, and initial pose $\xi_0 = (0, 0, 0)^T$. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s. Compute the linear and angular velocity of the robot and update the pose after $\Delta t = 1$ s using Euler integration. Plot (describe) the expected trajectory shape. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** State the wheel rolling constraints: $v_L = R\omega_L$, $v_R = R\omega_R$.
- **(1 mark)** Derive $v = \frac{R}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ and $\omega = \frac{R}{2b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- **(1 mark)** State the transformation from body to world frame using the rotation matrix $R_z(\theta)$.
- **(1 mark)** Write the final three scalar equations $\dot x = v\cos\theta$, $\dot y = v\sin\theta$, $\dot \theta = \omega$ and substitute the DDR expressions.
- **(1 mark)** Express in matrix form as a $3 \times 2$ Jacobian.
- **(1 mark)** State the non-holonomic constraint: $\dot x \sin\theta - \dot y \cos\theta = 0$ (no lateral slip).
- **(1 mark)** Conclude with controllability comment: the system is controllable despite the constraint, with the reachable set forming a Reeds-Shepp car-like manifold.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute linear velocity:**

$$v = \frac{R}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{2}(6 + 4) = 0.05 \times 5 = 0.25 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{R}{2b}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{0.30}(6 - 4) = 0.1667 \times 2 = 0.333 \ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler integration for pose update:**

$$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= x_0 + v\Delta t\cos\theta_0 = 0 + 0.25 \times 1 \times \cos 0 = 0.25 \ \text{m} \\ y_1 &= y_0 + v\Delta t\sin\theta_0 = 0 + 0.25 \times 1 \times \sin 0 = 0 \ \text{m} \\ \theta_1 &= \theta_0 + \omega \Delta t = 0 + 0.333 \times 1 = 0.333 \ \text{rad} \approx 19.1^\circ \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Trajectory shape:** (2 marks for the qualitative description)

The robot moves in a **circular arc** (since both $v$ and $\omega$ are non-zero and constant). The radius of the circular arc is:

$$R_{arc} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.25}{0.333} = 0.75 \ \text{m}$$

The trajectory is a left-turning (counter-clockwise) circular arc of radius **0.75 m** subtending an angle of 0.333 rad over 1 second.

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Bayes filter** for mobile robot localization. Derive the prediction and update steps and discuss two practical implementations (dead reckoning vs. EKF). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot uses an EKF to localize in a 1D corridor. The motion model is $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ and $R = 0.04$ m². The measurement model is $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$ with $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ and $Q = 0.09$ m². The prior is $\mu_0 = 0$, $\sigma_0^2 = 1$. The robot takes a step $u_1 = 0.5$ m and receives measurement $z_1 = 0.4$ m. Compute the posterior mean and variance after one EKF update. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** Define the belief $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ and the prediction $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$.
- **(1.5 marks)** Derive the prediction step:
$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\text{bel}(x_{t-1})dx_{t-1}$$
- **(1.5 marks)** Derive the update step with Bayes' rule and normalization:
$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t)\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$
- **(1 mark)** Discuss Dead Reckoning: open-loop, integrates encoder data, unbounded error growth $\sigma \propto \sqrt{t}$, no correction.
- **(1 mark)** Discuss EKF: closed-loop, linearizes non-linear models via Jacobians, fuses prediction and measurement via Kalman gain, bounded error under correct noise assumptions.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Prediction (Mean):**

$$\bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = 0 + 0.5 = 0.5 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Prediction (Variance):**

For 1D linear motion, the motion Jacobian is $G = \partial g / \partial x = 1$. So:

$$\bar{\sigma}_1^2 = G^2 \sigma_0^2 + R = 1^2 \times 1 + 0.04 = 1.04 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Innovation:**

$$\nu_1 = z_1 - \bar{\mu}_1 = 0.4 - 0.5 = -0.1 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Innovation Covariance:**

For 1D linear measurement, the measurement Jacobian is $H = 1$. So:

$$S_1 = H^2 \bar{\sigma}_1^2 + Q = 1 \times 1.04 + 0.09 = 1.13 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute Kalman Gain:**

$$K_1 = \bar{\sigma}_1^2 H / S_1 = 1.04 \times 1 / 1.13 = 0.920$$

**Step 6 — Posterior Mean:**

$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \nu_1 = 0.5 + 0.920 \times (-0.1) = 0.5 - 0.092 = 0.408 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Posterior Variance:**

$$\sigma_1^2 = (1 - K_1 H)\bar{\sigma}_1^2 = (1 - 0.920) \times 1.04 = 0.080 \times 1.04 = 0.0832 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\mu_1 = 0.408 \ \text{m}, \quad \sigma_1^2 = 0.0832 \ \text{m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The measurement ($Q = 0.09$ m²) is noisier than the process ($R = 0.04$ m²), so the Kalman gain $K_1 = 0.920$ is close to 1, meaning the posterior leans heavily toward the measurement. The posterior variance is significantly lower than the prior (1.0 → 0.0832), demonstrating successful information fusion.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 4 Project & Exam**

### Kinematics
- ☐ **Unicycle model:** $\dot x = v\cos\theta$, $\dot y = v\sin\theta$, $\dot \theta = \omega$ — applicable to ALL planar WMRs.
- ☐ **DDR Jacobian** depends on $\theta$, making the system non-linear.
- ☐ **Ackermann** uses $\phi$ (steering angle) and $L$ (wheelbase) with $R_{ICR} = L/\tan\phi$.
- ☐ **Non-holonomic constraint** for DDR: $\dot x \sin\theta - \dot y \cos\theta = 0$.
- ☐ **Inverse kinematics** for WMR is a velocity-level constraint satisfaction, not a closed-form inverse.

### Localization
- ☐ **Bayes filter** is the theoretical foundation; EKF and particle filter are practical implementations.
- ☐ **Dead reckoning** drifts unboundedly — never use it alone for long missions.
- ☐ **EKF** linearizes using Jacobians $G_t$ and $H_t$ — first-order approximation only.
- ☐ **Kalman gain** is small when the prediction is accurate, large when the sensor is accurate.
- ☐ **Particle filter** is required for **global localization** (kidnapped robot problem).

### Path Planning
- ☐ **A\*** = Dijkstra + heuristic; optimal if heuristic is admissible.
- ☐ **Octile distance** is the tightest admissible heuristic for 8-connected grids.
- ☐ **RRT** is probabilistically complete but **not optimal**; **RRT\*** is asymptotically optimal.
- ☐ **DWA** is for *local* reactive planning — vulnerable to local minima in U-shaped traps.
- ☐ **Configuration space** is essential: $C_{free} = C_{space} \setminus C_{obs}$.

### Project Execution Tips
- ☐ Choose a **differential drive robot** unless your team has strong mechatronics background.
- ☐ Use **MATLAB + Robotics Toolbox** OR **Python + ROS** for fastest prototyping.
- ☐ Always include **ground truth comparison** (GPS, motion capture, or known landmarks).
- ☐ Report **RMSE** for localization accuracy and **path length** for planner efficiency.
- ☐ In the viva, be ready to **defend your choice of algorithm** with complexity/accuracy trade-offs.
